articles.details: An oral dose fractionation study in P. falciparum-infected humanized mice evaluated berzosertib's antimalarial pharmacokinetics, showing activity strongly dependent on time above minimum parasiticidal concentration (MPC). Using PBPK modeling, the predicted effective human dose is six daily intravenous doses of 75 mg to maintain concentrations above MPC, comparatively lower than the clinical maximum tolerated dose for cancer therapy (440 mg IV twice weekly over 21 days).

articles.headline: Puromycin combinations achieve complete P. falciparum inhibition in vitro
articles.title_original: Repositioning Antibiotics Against Plasmodium falciparum RAD5 and WD11 as Novel Antimalarial Targets.
articles.authors: Albaqami AS, Oyewole OA, Adam KM
articles.details: A computational and experimental study screened 216 antibiotics against P. falciparum RAD5 and WD11 proteins. In vitro testing showed puromycin achieved 85% inhibition at 100 μg/mL alone. The triple combination (puromycin + raltegravir + dicloxacillin) achieved complete inhibition, with puromycin + chloroquine exhibiting synergistic effects. qPCR confirmed downregulation of both target genes with puromycin-based treatments.

articles.headline: Calcifer stabilizes basal body during Plasmodium exflagellation
articles.title_original: Mechanoregulation of basal body integrity during Plasmodium exflagellation.
articles.authors: Guan J, Gong Y, Liang W, Cui H, Yuan J
articles.details: Parasites lacking Calcifer form intact axonemes but show rapid axoneme disintegration during exflagellation. Calcifer directly stabilizes basal body integrity under mechanical stress from axoneme beating by forming a wrapping structure at the axoneme minus end. This conserved Plasmodium protein is essential for successful male gamete formation and mosquito transmission.

articles.headline: Malaria test positivity declined from 33% to 11% in Malakand
articles.title_original: Spatial Distribution, seasonal dynamics and molecular confirmation of malaria in District Malakand, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Rabia R, Ullah W
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Analysis of 41,863 tests from 2011–2017 identified 6,246 positive cases (14.92% test positivity rate). The annual test positive rate declined from 32.97% in 2011 to 10.77% in 2017. Males comprised 58.89% of cases, with highest proportion in ages 1–20 years. Autumn had the highest seasonal proportion (46.73%) versus winter (7.09%). Gharhi, Dargai, and Haryankot were identified as high-burden areas.

articles.headline: Artemisinin partial resistance confirmed in four African countries
articles.title_original: Africa cannot afford to wait for malaria treatment failure.
articles.authors: Pindolia D, Martinez-Vega R, Uzakbayeva G, Ishengoma D, Ngou O, Gosling R
articles.details: For over two decades, artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) have served as the backbone of malaria treatment across Africa. Artemisinin partial resistance (ART-R) has now been detected across eastern Africa, the Horn of Africa, and southern Africa, with confirmation in four countries: Rwanda, Uganda, Eritrea, and Tanzania, with additional signals reported in neighbouring countries.

articles.headline: Clinical malaria hotspots show stable and expanding patterns in Mali
articles.title_original: Analysis of clinical malaria hotspots according to transmission levels in two health areas of the Kolondieba health district, Mali.
articles.authors: Berthé I, Cissoko M, Diarra M, Mbaga DS, Koné M, Boly A, Togola OB, Haidara B, Théra I, Ongoiba A, Togola T, Mathias Dolo AD, Amaguiré Sy EHI, Konaté A, Coulibaly Y, Sissoko L, Bougoudogo I, Niangado B, Togola A, Traore D, Rabarijaona LP, Keita K, Traoré B, Tidiane Traoré CA, Sagara I
articles.details: A retrospective cross-sectional analysis of 35,934 confirmed malaria cases from 2019–2021 in two health areas identified distinct hotspot dynamics. In Kadiana (high transmission), clusters remained stable with persistent hotspots (RR: 1.8–6.3), while in Kolondieba Central (moderate transmission), hotspots evolved from localized configurations toward spatial expansion (RR: 1.7–2.2). Kolondieba town remained a persistent hotspot.

articles.headline: Culex restuans strongly associated with avian Plasmodium prevalence
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous distribution of avian malaria parasites across mosquito species: disproportionate involvement of Culex restuans in the Midwestern United States.
articles.authors: Ciloglu A, Mackay AJ, Cara C, Kim CH, Marguccio A, Yan J, Stone CM
articles.details: Analysis of 233 pools from 6,170 mosquitoes across seven species in Illinois identified 44 pools (18.9%) positive for avian haemosporidians, with 11 lineages detected (9 Plasmodium, 2 Haemoproteus; 1 novel Plasmodium lineage). Culex restuans harbored the highest lineage diversity and infection rate among mosquito species. Mosquito species was a significant predictor of avian Plasmodium prevalence, with Cx. restuans most strongly associated.

articles.headline: Cis-regulatory elements mapped in An. gambiae germline
articles.title_original: Decoding cis-regulatory elements in the germline of the human malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Chesters E, Ravenscroft L, Thompson TA, Sylla L, Tembely B, Kerr J, Hasenauer J, Page N, Winny T, Nolan T, Sharakhov I, Tonge D, Galizi R
articles.details: A systematic in silico analysis identified hundreds of putative cis-regulatory elements (CREs) significantly correlated with germline gene expression in Anopheles gambiae, linked to distinct sperm developmental stages and chromosomal locations. Validation of a top-ranked testis-enriched CRE using in vivo dual-reporter assay showed that mutation of conserved nucleotides drastically altered male germline expression, providing the first nucleotide-resolution regulatory genome annotation for this malaria vector.

articles.headline: Conflict disrupts malaria surveillance data completeness across Sudan
articles.title_original: Impact of the 2023-armed conflict on routine malaria imapact indicators in Sudan (January 2020-March 2025): a mixed-methods analysis of surveillance data.
articles.authors: Alnaeem KF, Saha UR, Ahmed HF, Mergenthaler C
articles.details: Mixed-methods analysis examined DHIS2 surveillance data from 17 Sudanese states (January 2020-March 2025) to assess conflict impact on malaria reporting. After April 2023, data completeness declined substantially with geographic variation. Interrupted time series analysis showed a statistically significant decline in quarterly reported malaria case rates at conflict quarter (p=0.02). Health facility destruction, workforce shortages, and communication failures were identified as major barriers.

articles.headline: Alpha-mangostin nanoparticles synergize with chloroquine against Plasmodium berghei
articles.title_original: Synergistic Antimalarial Activity of Alpha-Mangostin Chitosan Alginate Nanoparticles with Chloroquine in Mice Infected with Plasmodium berghei: The Potential of a New Antimalarial Drug Combination.
articles.authors: Tjahjani S, Hermanto F, Muchtaridi M, Aulifa DL, Haq FA
articles.details: In P. berghei-infected mice, three chloroquine-ACAN combinations were tested using a 4-day suppressive test. Ratio-1 (189:1 weight ratio), ratio-2 (63:1), and ratio-3 (567:1) achieved ED50s of 9.196, 7.626, and 82.13 mg/Kg body weight, respectively. All three combinations showed synergistic activity (∑FED50 values of 0.069, 0.113, and 0.414, all below 1), with ratio-1 demonstrating the strongest effect.

articles.headline: Altitude influences malaria transmission patterns in Pakistan
articles.title_original: A Topographic Analysis of Malaria Transmission - Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, 2019-2022.
articles.authors: Haq IU
articles.details: Study examined malaria transmission across different topographic regions of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province, Pakistan (2019-2022). High-altitude regions experienced low transmission rates, while low-altitude regions showed high transmission rates. The findings suggest that considering topography in malaria control strategies may enable more targeted and effective prevention measures in the region.

articles.headline: World Malaria Day 2026 theme: Driven to End Malaria
articles.title_original: World Malaria Day 2026: Driven to End Malaria.
articles.authors: Zhou X, Chen J, Xia Z, Li S
articles.details: World Malaria Day 2026 campaign with the theme 'Driven to End Malaria' addressing global malaria elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Intravenous artesunate is first-line treatment for cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: [Cerebral Malaria].
articles.authors: Ohnishi K
articles.details: Cerebral malaria occurs when Plasmodium-infected red blood cells adhere to endothelial cells in brain microvasculature, obstructing blood flow. Although the exact pathophysiology remains incompletely understood, intravenous artesunate is recommended as immediate treatment when cerebral malaria is suspected. The condition presents as impaired consciousness in malaria patients.

articles.headline: PfARMA vaccine candidate shows limited protection despite antibody response
articles.title_original: The N-terminal region of malaria vaccine candidate asparagine-rich merozoite antigen is immunodominant and targeted by polyreactive antibodies.
articles.authors: Garza R, Marchioni JM, Honeycutt JD, Hurlburt NK, Torres C, Garcia A, Neog S, Loranc E, Yemington E, Towers D, Ssewanyana I, Pancera M, Lavinder JJ, Jagannathan P, Greenhouse B, Bol S, Bunnik EM
articles.details: In P. falciparum-exposed individuals, B cell and antibody responses to the vaccine candidate asparagine-rich merozoite antigen (PfARMA) were dominated by IgM+ B cells and IgG targeting the N-terminal intrinsically disordered region (IDR1). Although PfARMA-specific plasma IgM and IgG levels increased with P. falciparum exposure, these antibody responses did not correlate with protection in age- and exposure-matched children. Approximately 30% of unexposed individuals also possessed polyreactive IgG targeting IDR1, suggesting these responses may reflect recent exposure rather than functional immunity.

articles.headline: Host genetic diversity does not predict avian malaria infection risk
articles.title_original: Avian Malaria Infections Are Not Associated With Host Genetic Diversity: A Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Podmokła E, Pluciński B, Dubiec A
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 35 studies (27 bird species, 85 effect sizes) examined the relationship between individual genetic diversity and avian malaria infections caused by Plasmodium, Haemoproteus, and Leucocytozoon. No significant association was found between genetic diversity and infection prevalence or intensity, regardless of genetic marker type. Meta-regression analyses found no significant influence of parasite genus, infection assessment method, host migratory behavior, latitude, or MHC class, suggesting broad genetic diversity measures may not reliably predict infection outcomes.

articles.headline: Digital molecular diagnostics advance malaria detection strategies
articles.title_original: How can the use of digital molecular diagnostic tools help to tackle malaria?
articles.authors: Chamai M, Jebreel WMA, Kabuya JB, Rakotomalala Robinson D, Cunnington AJ
articles.details: Commentary on the role of digital molecular diagnostic tools in improving malaria diagnosis and control programs.

articles.headline: High Pfdhps mutation frequency indicates sulfadoxine resistance in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Polymorphisms of the plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase gene among patients attending LEPI and ADLUCEM hospitals in the west region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Edna GDM, Nadia NAC, Cedric Y, Raoul SNS, Christian NO, Pacome TK, Sidiki NNA, Sandra TNJ, Merveille TT, Line GAP, Yval KT, Hu H, Choukem SP
articles.details: In a cross-sectional study from two health facilities in Cameroon, blood samples from 286 individuals (198 women, 88 men, mean age 29±20 years) were tested for P. falciparum and Pfdhps mutations. P. falciparum was present in 87 samples (30.41%), with mean parasite densities of 2343±1240 parasites/μL in males and 2283±1483 parasites/μL in females. The A581G mutant allele frequency was 67.60% and A437G was 45.07%, indicating substantial sulfadoxine resistance pressure.

articles.headline: Human and primate malaria parasites share genetic lineages in Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: Zoonotic and Anthroponotic Plasmodium spp. Circulation between Wild Primates and Indigenous Community, Peruvian Amazon, 2007-2020.
articles.authors: Ulloa GM, Greenwood AD, Cornejo OE, Alonso H, Santolalla Robles ML, Montero S, Lescano AG, Mayor P
articles.details: Molecular survey examined Plasmodium spp. in 141 Indigenous community members and 341 nonhuman primates (10 species) in the Peruvian Amazon during 2007-2020 using PCR and sequencing. Plasmodium was detected in 43.3% of humans and 51.9% of primates; P. vivax/simium predominated in humans (42.1%) while P. brasilianum/malariae predominated in primates (24.6%). Bayesian phylogenies revealed >99.9% genetic identity between human and primate lineages, indicating shared Plasmodium circulation. One-third of human infections were asymptomatic.

articles.headline: Meteorological drivers predict malaria incidence in South Africa
articles.title_original: Understanding and modeling meteorological drivers of the incidence and spread of malaria in South Africa.
articles.authors: Wright CY, Kapwata T, Naidoo N, Maharaj R, Aleksandrov N, Tošić M, Blesić S
articles.details: Study examined associations between malaria hospital admissions and meteorological variables (temperature, rainfall, evapotranspiration) across five district municipalities in Limpopo province, South Africa. Wavelet transform spectral analysis identified characteristic time lags for malaria development. A regression model was developed incorporating meteorological variables to predict admission cases and identify critical values that initiate disease spread. Malaria projections were made for 2021-2050 and 2051-2080 using climate scenarios RCP2.6 and RCP8.5.

articles.headline: Long-acting ivermectin injection shows promise for residual malaria control
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of ivermectin-based optimal strategies on malaria control: the role of formulation, coverage, and mosquitocidal efficacy timing.
articles.authors: Dongmo FJ, Porciani A, Moiroux N, Pooda HS, Dabiré RK, Beneteau S, Le Lamer-Déchamps S, Wondji CS, Mouline K, Djidjou-Demasse R
articles.details: Mathematical model compared three ivermectin regimens for malaria control: monthly oral IVM (3×300 μg/kg for 4 months), monthly oral IVM (1×400 μg/kg for 3 months), and long-acting injectable BEPO® (0.6–1.0 mg/kg). Under 50-90% target population coverage, oral regimens reduced total cases by 7-38% over one year, depending on the regimen and setting. A single long-acting injectable campaign reduced cases by 16-29%, increasing to 33-57%, 48-74%, and 59-78% with two, three, and four campaigns, respectively.

articles.headline: State-of-the-art malaria pharmacotherapy in infants
articles.title_original: The impact of state-of-the-art pharmacotherapy for malaria in infants: a breakthrough?
articles.authors: Grobusch MP, Adegbite BR, Hanscheid T, Gupta N
articles.details: The article examines the impact of state-of-the-art pharmacotherapy for malaria in infants. The title questions whether this represents a breakthrough, but specific findings are not available in the title alone.

articles.headline: Molecular detection of avian Plasmodium in Malaysian poultry
articles.title_original: Molecular detection and genetic diversity of avian haemosporidia (Plasmodium and Haemoproteus) infecting red jungle fowls (Gallus gallus spadiceus) and domestic poultry in peninsular, Malaysia.
articles.authors: Fufa Ido G, Sharma RSK, Zakaria A, Lin LC, Ola-Fadunsin SD, Abdullah DA, Babjee SMA, Sani RA
articles.details: Molecular detection study of Plasmodium and Haemoproteus in 713 poultry samples from peninsular Malaysia, including red jungle fowls, village chickens, and broilers. Plasmodium prevalence was 4.6% by microscopy and 30.6% by PCR; Haemoproteus was 1.1% and 10.7% respectively. Species identified included P. gallinaceum, P. parasitense, and Haemoproteus sp.

articles.headline: Plasmodium huffi documented in Brazilian vultures and falcons
articles.title_original: No host too tough: New records of Plasmodium huffi in competent avian hosts, including Brazilian vultures and falcons.
articles.authors: Vieira LMC, Braga ÉM
articles.details: Molecular and morphological study describing new hosts of Plasmodium huffi in Brazil: black vultures (n=29) and yellow-headed caracara (n=6), as well as red-legged seriemas (n=14). P. huffi demonstrated high host plasticity and was found across diverse bird orders with gametocytes present in peripheral blood.

articles.headline: Agricultural insecticides for malaria vector control and resistance
articles.title_original: Malaria vector control: Can repurposed agricultural insecticides offer solutions to resistance issues?
articles.authors: Malone D, Zahouli JZB, Ekra AK, Lucas JR, Van Leeuwen T, Koudou BG
articles.details: Review evaluating repurposing of agricultural insecticides for malaria vector control in the context of insecticide resistance. The review examined neonicotinoids, pyrroles, broflanilide, meta-diamides, growth regulators, ivermectin, methoxyfenozide, and halofenozide, which demonstrated efficacy against Anopheles, Aedes, and Culex mosquitoes at both adult and larval stages. Some compounds affect parasite development, offering dual-action potential for malaria transmission reduction.

articles.headline: Developing a new class of malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Eizo Takashima-developing a new class of malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Kirby T
articles.details: The article discusses vaccine development by Eizo Takashima, focusing on a new class of malaria vaccines. Specific details and findings are not available in the title alone.

articles.headline: COVID-19 and malaria co-infection outcomes in ambulatory patients
articles.title_original: Uncomplicated malaria as a risk factor for COVID-19 duration and severity in western Kenya and Burkina Faso (MALCOV): a prospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Barsosio HC, Tangara B, Marlais T, Kaboré JMT, Tiono AB, Otieno K, Wanjiku M, Achieng M, Onyango ED, Ondieki ED, Aura H, Odawo T, Allen DJ, Hannan L, Tetteh KKA, Soulama I, Ouedraogo A, Serme SS, Soulama BI, Barry A, Badoum ES, Matthewman J, Brazal-Monzó H, Canizales J, Drabko A, Wu W, Kariuki S, Lesosky M, Sirima SB, Drakeley C, Ter Kuile FO
articles.details: Prospective cohort study of 742 COVID-19 patients (151 with P. falciparum co-infection) in western Kenya and Burkina Faso. Time to symptom resolution was similar (median 9 vs 10 days; aHR 1.14, p=0.26); hospitalization and deaths occurred in 2% and 1% respectively. SARS-CoV-2 clearance was slower with co-infection by day 7 (aHR 0.69, p=0.017) but similar by day 28. Previous malaria exposure associated with faster clearance.

articles.headline: Malaria and COVID-19 clinical relationship explored
articles.title_original: The fascinating malaria-COVID-19 relationship.
articles.authors: Awandare GA, Morang'a CM
articles.details: Perspective discussing the relationship between malaria and COVID-19 across clinical scenarios. Specific findings are not available in the title alone.

articles.headline: P. falciparum genomics under intensive malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Impact of intensive control on malaria population genomics under elimination settings in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Li X, Arya GA, Thu AM, Landier J, Parker DM, Delmas G, Reyes A, Lwin KM, Sriprawat K, Nosten F, Anderson TJC
articles.details: Analysis of 2,270 genome-sequenced P. falciparum infections from 283 malaria posts in Kayin State (Myanmar), sampled over 58 months (2015–2020), during intensive control efforts that reduced transmission by 97%. Effective population size decreased over the period; artemisinin resistance allele frequency remained minimal until 2020, when one predominant genotype carrying kelch13-R561H became dominant. Mass drug administration produced parasite founder effects with sustained localized transmission chains.

articles.headline: Gametocyte membrane protein complexes in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Deciphering Membrane Protein Complexes in Plasmodium falciparum Gametocytes via Integrative Structural Systems Biology.
articles.authors: Mostafavi S, Maurer V, Ruwolt M, Schwartz J, Stein F, Niedermüller K, Gilberger TW, Liu F, Seuring C, Witt S, Kosinski J, Löw C, Filarsky M
articles.details: Structural systems biology study of P. falciparum gametocyte membrane proteins using cross-linking and cofractionation mass spectrometry combined with AlphaFold and AF3x modeling. Identified host-pathogen and pathogen-pathogen membrane-associated complexes distributed across parasite and host cell compartments. Results demonstrate gametocyte remodeling mechanisms, relevant to transmission-blocking interventions since gametocytes are not susceptible to most antimalarials.

articles.headline: Proteomics for understanding host-parasite interactions in malaria
articles.title_original: Integrative proteomics to illuminate host-parasite interactions in malaria.
articles.authors: Attallah N, Srivastava H, Carlton JM, Idaghdour Y
articles.details: Review article proposing proteomics as a core component of malaria systems biology, capable of quantifying protein abundance, post-translational modifications, interaction networks, localization, and turnover in host and parasite. Proteomics can help identify mechanisms of cytoadhesion, endothelial dysfunction, immune modulation, tissue-specific pathology, and therapeutic response variability. Advanced technologies including single-cell and spatial proteomics combined with AI pipelines are highlighted.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge and preventive practices in mining workers
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes and practices of malaria control and prevention in a high-exposure occupational group: A cross-sectional survey of mining workers in Haut-Katanga Province in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Liang C, Du Y, Frumence G, Ke X, Kasombo PM, Mukolo JM, Gu Y, Zhou H, Zhu G, Huang J, Cao J
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 485 mining workers in the Democratic Republic of Congo. 75.3% possessed adequate knowledge; middle school education and early/evening shifts were barriers (adjusted OR 0.37–0.46). Most reported willingness for insecticide-treated nets (99.3%) and indoor spraying (94.7%), but a significant knowledge-practice gap exists.

articles.headline: Sudanese migrants' pathways to malaria care in Cairo
articles.title_original: Sudanese migrants' pathways to malaria care in Egypt: An in-depth qualitative study.
articles.authors: Elafef E, Edris H, Abdelrahman S, Elnahry W, Hadjiabduli S, Abdelkhalek A, Adam A, El-Ghitany E, Hilali M, Hamed Arisha A, Bani I, Seedat F, Hargreaves S, Evangelidou S, Requena-Méndez A
articles.details: Qualitative study of 21 Sudanese migrants in Cairo (90.5% female) with prior malaria experience. About 24% developed symptoms upon arrival; others within six months. 90.5% initially used traditional remedies or self-medication without testing. Barriers included lack of trust, stigma, quarantine fears, and medication concerns; system-level factors involved affordability and perceived quality.

articles.headline: Global malaria burden from child growth failure, 1990-2021
articles.title_original: The unequal malaria burden attributable to child growth failure: a global analysis among children under five from 1990 to 2021.
articles.authors: Deng JJ, Cheng XE, Li S, Chang YW, Wang P, Tao SS, Pan HF
articles.details: Global Burden of Disease study analyzed malaria burden attributable to child growth failure in children under 5 from 1990-2021. Overall burden declined (AAPC -1.26) but increased after 2019 (APC 3.66). Highest burdens in males (ASMR 17.59/100,000), infants aged 6-11 months (31.63/100,000), and West/Central Africa (88.09/100,000). Lower socio-demographic index correlated with higher burden.

articles.headline: Dual-antigen malaria vaccine shows high protection in challenge studies
articles.title_original: Robust Bioconjugated Antigens Induce Immune Responses Preventing Malaria Infection and its Transmission.
articles.authors: Sivakumaran N, Flores-Garcia Y, Naar CM, Chen S, Mehta M, van Leeuwen FWB, Tripathi A, Murugan R, Roestenberg M, Zavala F, Rehm BHA
articles.details: Dual-antigen Pfs47-RTS,S-BP vaccine combining pre-erythrocytic and sexual-stage antigens induced RTS,S-specific antibodies 5.3-fold higher than protective thresholds of current RTS,S/AS01 vaccine. Demonstrated 80.4% protection against mosquito bite challenge and 68.15% transmission-reducing activity. Thermostable formulation retained antigenicity after storage at 37°C, with potential to overcome cold-chain limitations.

articles.headline: Potent selective inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum aminopeptidase P discovered
articles.title_original: Hydroxamate-based inhibitors reveal structural determinants of selectivity for Plasmodium falciparum aminopeptidase P.
articles.authors: Mills BJ, Gregory KS, Cozier GE, Taweechai S, Loughlan E, McConkey GA, Isaac RE, Foster R, Acharya KR
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship studies identified hydroxamate-based inhibitors of PfAPP with markedly improved potency over apstatin (Ki 16 μM): compound 6d achieved Ki of 685 nM, further improved by compound 6e (Ki 24 nM). Crystal structure showed binding at active site with coordination to di-metal center. P4' phenylalanine moiety drives selectivity for PfAPP over human APP1.

articles.headline: Over half of febrile travelers return without diagnosis despite malaria testing
articles.title_original: High rate of undiagnosed cases of fever among returning travelers from tropical areas tested for malaria: a single-center retrospective study.
articles.authors: Caronni S, Pieruzzi ME, Salari F, Behring AH, Rizzo A, Poloni A, Grosso S, Mancon A, Galimberti L, Oreni L, Casalini G, Mileto D, Gori A, Dolci A, Giacomelli A, Antinori S
articles.details: Retrospective study of 778 returning febrile travelers found 446 (57%) remained undiagnosed. Arboviral infections identified in 107 (13.6%), with dengue most frequent (95 cases, 12.2%), followed by malaria (72 cases, 9.3%) and pneumonia (42 cases, 5.4%). Diagnostic capacity improved over time: definite diagnoses rose from 29.4% in 2018 to 57.8% in 2024. Travelers from Americas and Western Pacific showed lower odds of remaining undiagnosed versus Africa.

articles.headline: Point-of-care G6PD test performance for malaria patients: systematic review
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of Point-of-Care Devices for Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency Detection Among Malaria Patients: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Unnikrishnan G, Singh A
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 13 studies evaluated CareStart and STANDARD G6PD Biosensor against spectrophotometry. At 30% activity threshold, CareStart showed sensitivity 0.82 (95% CI 0.55-0.95) and specificity 0.96 (95% CI 0.93-0.98); at 70% threshold sensitivity decreased to 0.54 (95% CI 0.31-0.76). STANDARD G6PD Biosensor demonstrated superior performance with sensitivity 0.99 at 30% and 0.96 at 70%, maintaining high specificity.

articles.headline: Natural compound brazilein kills Plasmodium falciparum via iron disruption
articles.title_original: Brazilin and its oxidized form "brazilein", the natural fluorescently traceable agents, toxify Plasmodium falciparum to pyknotic death via disrupting its iron homeostasis.
articles.authors: Kulaphisit M, Jaikamlue W, Jaitan N, Sirirak J, Tamdee P, Pomlok K, Chumchanchira C, Changkhong S, Wipasa J, Saenjum C, Lithanatudom P
articles.details: Caesalpinia sappan L. heartwood decoction showed anti-plasmodial activity, with brazilein identified as the active oxidized form of major compound brazilin. Brazilein binds Fe2+ and disrupts iron homeostasis in parasites and red blood cells. This disruption blocks hemoglobin digestion and erythrocyte invasion via binding to AMA1 active site, inducing pyknotic death detectable by holotomography.

articles.headline: DENV-1, DENV-3, DENV-4 circulation with dengue-malaria co-infection in febrile children
articles.title_original: Molecular evidence of co-circulation of different dengue virus serotypes associated with malaria co-infection among febrile children in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, Fritz M, Kombila Koumavor CA, Ndong Ngomo JM, Moussavou Mabika DA, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Agbanrin AA, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Leroy EM, Bouyou-Akotet MK
articles.details: Molecular analysis of 151 febrile children identified DENV RNA in 18.5%, confirming DENV-1 and DENV-3 circulation with possible DENV-4. Malaria prevalence was 56.9%, with 9.3% co-infected with dengue. Children 5-10 years showed higher malaria odds (OR 2.61, 95% CI 1.07-7.02, p=0.03) and co-infection odds (OR 16.28, 95% CI 1.85-79.40, p=0.002). Severe anemia most common in malaria; thrombocytopenia most marked in co-infected patients.

articles.headline: Tetrandrine targets Plasmodium heme detoxification protein with high affinity
articles.title_original: Atomistic simulations identify the Tetrandrine as potent anti-malarial drug candidate against Plasmodium falciparum targeting Heme Detoxification Protein (HDP).
articles.authors: Bux K, Abbasi U, Hasan KA, Hussain A, Malik F, Herwig R
articles.details: Computational screening identified tetrandrine, a bis-benzyl-isoquinoline alkaloid, as inhibitor of heme detoxification protein (HDP) that parasite uses to convert toxic heme from hemoglobin digestion. Tetrandrine showed highest affinity (-13.0 kJ/mol) versus Bisnordihydrotoxiferine (-10.2 kJ/mol), Neferine (-9.01 kJ/mol), and Oxytetracycline (-10.9 kJ/mol). Molecular dynamics simulations confirmed complex stability; MM-PBSA calculations showed favorable binding energy of -30 kcal/mol.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax proteins detected in patient urine via mass spectrometry
articles.title_original: Discovery of parasite proteins in the urine of vivax malaria patients through mass spectrometry.
articles.authors: Tripathi H, Kar PP, Babu AP, Srivastava A, Pachori G, Bhatt TK
articles.details: Mass spectrometry analysis of urine from P. vivax-infected patients identified 252 human proteins unique to infected samples. Twenty-eight P. vivax proteins were detected including rifin-like protein, single-stranded DNA-binding protein, and profilin. Gene ontology analysis revealed detected proteins involved in key biological pathways relevant to both host and parasite. These urine biomarkers could enable non-invasive diagnosis.

articles.headline: Reconsidering identity and interpretation of malaria-derived particles
articles.title_original: "What are we measuring?" reconsidering the biological identity and clinical interpretation of malaria-derived particles.
articles.authors: Lei Z
articles.details: This work critically examines the biological identity and clinical interpretation of malaria-derived particles, reconsidering measurement methodologies.

articles.headline: Spatial repellent emanators reduce malaria incidence in humanitarian crisis camps
articles.title_original: The effectiveness of long-lasting spatial repellent emanators against malaria in humanitarian crisis settings in northern Nigeria: a two-arm pragmatic, open-label, controlled trial.
articles.authors: Allan RJ, Scherrer R, Estecha-Querol S, Weetman D, Paris L, Ba'abba Goni U, Idris Abdulhamid F, Nfornuh Alenwi B, Ahmad S, Cross CL, Ashir GM, Waziri M, Ntadom G, Messenger LA
articles.details: Two-arm pragmatic trial across 24 internally displaced person camps evaluated spatial repellent emanators over 6 months in 1655 children aged 6-10 years (842 intervention, 813 control). Emanators significantly reduced malaria infection incidence (IRR 0.713, 95% CI 0.584-0.869, p=0.001) with 22.5% protective efficacy against first-time infection (HR 0.775, 95% CI 0.637-0.943, p=0.011). Also reduced pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles gambiae sensu lato density and blood feeding.

articles.headline: Cyclic peptides inhibit Plasmodium oocyst rupture protein dimerization
articles.title_original: Targeting the histone-fold dimerization interface of oocyst rupture proteins from Plasmodium berghei for antimalarial inhibitor discovery.
articles.authors: Ballabio F, Bertaso C, Villa M, Livero O, Del Cont Bernard A, Russo R, Gessmann R, Siden-Kiamos I, Preira CMF, Kumawat A, Gabrieli P, Camilloni C, Currà C, Masiero S, Nardini M, Gourlay LJ
articles.details: X-ray crystallographic analysis resolved the histone-fold domain heterodimer structure of Plasmodium berghei oocyst rupture proteins at 3.1 Å resolution. Dimerization between ORP1 and ORP2 is essential for oocyst rupture in mosquito vectors. Yeast two-hybrid screening identified six cyclic peptide candidates that inhibit dimerization in vitro, with molecular docking confirming binding at the dimer interface.

articles.headline: Flow cytometry method quantifies malaria-derived particles in blood
articles.title_original: A novel flow cytometry approach to quantify malaria-derived particles.
articles.authors: Palasuwan A, Sophondilok K, Ampawong S, Srisutham S, Noulsri E, Palasuwan D
articles.details: Study optimized flow cytometry using propidium iodide staining to enumerate malaria-derived particles (MDPs) per microliter. Samples from P. falciparum and P. vivax infections showed significant MDP increases compared to controls (<1 particle/μL). MDP counts correlated strongly with infected red blood cell percentage (R²=0.94) and showed parallel correlation with parasite density across both species (P. falciparum R²=0.94, P. vivax R²=0.91). The approach is reliable for routine laboratory diagnosis and treatment monitoring.

articles.headline: Multistage malaria vaccine demonstrates efficacy against infection and transmission
articles.title_original: First efficacy evidence for a multistage malaria vaccine targeting infection and transmission.
articles.authors: Moncunill G, Dobaño C
articles.details: Title indicates clinical trial of a multistage malaria vaccine that targets both parasite infection and transmission stages with reported efficacy.

articles.headline: Multistage vaccine ProC6C achieves 76% efficacy against malaria infection
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ProC6C-AlOH/Matrix-M against Plasmodium falciparum infection and mosquito transmission: a phase 2, randomised, controlled human malaria infection study.
articles.authors: Kone M, Plieskatt J, Thienta M, Kamate B, Ofori EA, Gmeiner M, Haidara B, Kanoute MB, Traore B, Poudiouguo OA, Bayogo HS, Diarra I, Bengaly K, Zeguime A, Sylla D, Sacko A, Hoffman SL, McCall MBB, Theisen M, Sagara I
articles.details: Phase 2 randomized controlled trial in healthy malaria-experienced adults using controlled human malaria infection with 22,500 P. falciparum sporozoites. Participants received three doses of ProC6C-AlOH/MM vaccine (n=17) or Verorab control (n=17) four weeks apart. By day 28 post-infection, 6/16 vaccinated and 13/16 control participants developed parasitemia; median time to parasitemia was delayed by 2 days in vaccine group (p=0.049). Vaccine efficacy was 76% (95% CI 36-91, p=0.0022) by time-to-event analysis and 54% (95% CI 9-77, p=0.029) by proportional analysis.

articles.headline: LSA3 protein essential for final stages of malaria merozoite invasion
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Liver Stage Antigen 3 plays a role in completing the final stages of merozoite invasion of red blood cells.
articles.authors: Morita M, Jennison C, O'Neill MT, Kanoi BN, Ishino T, Tsuboi T, Boddey JA, Takashima E
articles.details: In vitro study of P. falciparum identified Liver Stage Antigen 3 (LSA3) as a merozoite dense granule protein critical for late-stage invasion. LSA3 localized to posterior end of invading merozoites; antigen-specific antibodies inhibited parasite growth by 24%. Lsa3 knockout parasites showed 65% reduction in normal invasion completion compared to wild-type and high frequency of aberrant accolé-form morphology. LSA3 represents a novel blood-stage vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Food vacuole protein shows potential allosteric regulation by antimalarials
articles.title_original: Computational characterization of a HotDog domain-containing hypothetical protein from the food vacuole of Plasmodium falciparum reveals potential allosteric regulation by antimalarials.
articles.authors: Gupta P, Kaushik NK, Sachdeva C, Mathur K, Pai SS, Saha B, Dhir S, Sharma A
articles.details: Computational structural biology study characterized hypothetical P. falciparum food vacuole-associated protein PfHDDCP with HotDog fold domain. Molecular docking and dynamics simulations predicted two ligand-binding sites: one for canonical thioesterase substrates (Acetyl-CoA, Acyl Carrier Protein) and a second distinct pocket where antimalarial compounds bind, suggesting possible allosteric modulation. Over 90% of model residues were in favored Ramachandran regions with good model quality. Results are preliminary and require experimental validation.

articles.headline: P. falciparum and P. vivax endemic in Ethiopian highlands despite rare travel
articles.title_original: High-Altitude Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Reservoirs in Ethiopia Are Not Linked to Recent Travel.
articles.authors: Ewnetu Y, Kayode TA, Oduma CO, Adeyemi-Kayode TM, Zemene A, Lemma W, Berhane N, Koepfli C
articles.details: Cross-sectional surveys in 1241 subclinical and 1775 clinical individuals across altitudes 1850-2850 m in Ethiopian highlands. P. falciparum prevalence by qPCR decreased from 67% at 1850 m to 16% at 2850 m; P. vivax ranged 10-34% with no clear altitude trend. Only 20.3% of subclinical individuals reported recent travel, which did not affect infection risk, suggesting sustained local transmission rather than imported cases. LDH-based RDT detected nearly twice as many subclinical infections as HRP2-based diagnosis.

articles.headline: P. falciparum antimalarial resistance genotyping in Zambian children survey
articles.title_original: National genomic profiling of Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance in Zambian children participating in the 2018 malaria indicator survey.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Ciubotariu II, Dorman J, Mwenda MC, Mambwe B, Mulube C, Kasaro R, Hawela MB, Hamainza B, Miller JM, Bailey JA, Moss WJ, Bridges DJ, Carpi G
articles.details: National genomic profiling study of antimalarial resistance markers in P. falciparum parasites from Zambian children participating in the 2018 malaria indicator survey.

articles.headline: Vector molecular characterization and transmission indices in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Molecular Characterization and transmission indices of malaria vector in a rural community in Osun State south-west Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oyewusi RA, Surakat OA, Abdulsalam MM, Yusuff YO, Igenoza AJ, Iwalewa OZ, Ganiu OD, Busari LO, Adeleke MA
articles.details: Study characterizing malaria vector species, molecular markers, and transmission indices in rural Nigeria community.

articles.headline: Ethiopia malaria test-and-treat guideline fidelity during resurgence
articles.title_original: Implementation fidelity of Ethiopia's Malaria test-and-treat guideline amid a resurgence in Amhara Region: A mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Gelaye KA, Asemahagn MA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Mixed-methods study assessed implementation fidelity of Ethiopia's malaria test-and-treat guideline in 53 health facilities (38 public, 15 private) in Amhara Region. Overall mean fidelity was 64.3% (SD 12.1), with 40% achieving high fidelity (≥75%) and 13% scoring <50%. Public facilities had higher median fidelity than private (67% vs 63%, p=0.041). Fidelity was significantly associated with higher provider responsiveness (β=3.4), stronger facilitation strategies (β=2.8), and lower perceived intervention complexity (β=2.1, all p<0.001).

articles.headline: Vector density high near dam but infection rates higher in distant villages
articles.title_original: Characteristics and dynamics of malaria vectors around the Soum dam in Nanoro Health District, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ilboudo H, Sanon EDG, Hien DFS, Traoré F, Hansson H, Kaboré B, Tahita MC, Derra K, Rouamba E, Bouda I, Rouamba T, Alifrangis M, Sorgho H, Magnussen P, Tinto H
articles.details: Entomological survey of malaria vectors in 5 villages around Soum dam in Burkina Faso from March 2022 to February 2023 collected 11,378 Anopheles mosquitoes. An. gambiae s.l. was most abundant (86.5%), with An. coluzzii predominant within complex (91.5%). Vector density was highest in Soum village (49.7%) and decreased with distance from dam, but sporozoite infection rates were paradoxically higher in distant villages (Seguedin 9.5%, Soala 8.7% vs overall 6.2%). Blood meal analysis: 34.7% human, 14.2% animal, 23.6% mixed.

articles.headline: Novel parasite entry mechanism identified in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Plasmodium gets inside through the back door.
articles.authors: Jajosky RP, Stowell SR
articles.details: Title indicates discussion of alternative mechanism of Plasmodium entry into host cells.

articles.headline: P. vivax relapse cluster in Korean Peninsula service members
articles.title_original: To Break a Fever: Relapse of Long-Latency Plasmodium vivax Malaria After Service in the Korean Peninsula.
articles.authors: Robben PM, Woo NW, Merk AR, Jansen NK, Mody RM
articles.details: Case series of 6 male military service members (mean age 23 years, range 20-27) who developed P. vivax malaria with long latency after exposure in Dagmar North training area, Gyeonggi province in 2021. Mean time from symptom onset to diagnosis was 57 days (range 8-121 days). All had uncomplicated malaria with <1% parasitemia; hemoglobin 8.1-14.2 g/dL, platelets 40-171 × 10³/µL. Treated with artemether/lumefantrine plus primaquine with parasitemia clearance on peripheral smear in average 2.8 days (range 2-4 days).

articles.headline: P. knowlesi adapts to Duffy-negative RBCs via chimeric dbpαγ gene
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi can adapt to infect Duffy-negative erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Zinga M, Ibrahim A, Mohring F, Chainarin S, Jonsdottir TK, Ngernna S, Amabilino-Perez B, Pholcharee T, Turkiewicz A, Campino S, Clark TG, Miao J, Cui L, Roobsoong W, Sattabongkot J, Moon RW, Nguitragool W
articles.details: In vitro study of P. knowlesi invasion adaptation showed parasites stably invaded Duffy-negative erythrocytes independent of DARC binding. Genomic analysis identified a recombination event creating chimeric dbpαγ gene. CRISPR-Cas9 reversion demonstrated dbpαγ is essential for Fy- RBC invasion, revealing unexpected invasion plasticity with implications for parasite spread beyond Southeast Asia.

articles.headline: Heme metabolism architecture offers nested targets for antimalarial therapy
articles.title_original: Russian Dolls of Heme Metabolism in Malaria-Infected Red Blood Cells: Nested Vulnerabilities and Therapeutic Opportunities.
articles.authors: Adapa SR, Siddiqui FA, Jiang RHY
articles.details: Review of compartmentalized heme metabolism in malaria-infected RBCs from three sources: host erythrocyte biosynthesis, parasite synthesis, and hemoglobin digestion. Exogenous 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) can increase protoporphyrin IX and sensitize parasites to oxidative stress as potential therapeutic approach, though effects depend on parasite stage and exposure duration.

articles.headline: P. falciparum genetic metrics link complexity of infection to transmission intensity
articles.title_original: Genetic metrics decode Plasmodium falciparum diversity: complexity of infection, parasite connectivity, and transmission intensity in Mainland Tanzania's diverse regions.
articles.authors: Pereus D, Fola AA, Bakari C, Seth MD, DeFeo R, Lyimo BM, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Mbata D, Popkin-Hall ZR, Moshi R, Mbwambo RB, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Giesbrecht DJ, Kulohoma B, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Juma G, Mobegi VA, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 7,199 P. falciparum samples from 100 Tanzanian health facilities (February–July 2021) sequenced targeting 1,832 SNPs across 14 chromosomes. Mean complexity of infection (COI) 1.5 with 59.6% monoclonal infections; COI significantly higher in high and moderate transmission regions (p<0.001); low parasite genetic differentiation (FST 0–0.006) with weak identity-by-descent relationships. Findings support integration of genetic metrics into surveillance to monitor transmission trends.

articles.headline: CellaVision DM9600 misclassifies P. vivax as artefactual cells
articles.title_original: Classification of Plasmodium vivax as artefactual cells by the CellaVision DM9600: a system limitation in a non-endemic area.
articles.authors: Chien LC, Wu FT, Hsu SF, Su YD, Lai HY, Hsu HC, Hsiao CT, Cheng MY, Hsueh PR
articles.details: Case report of 46-year-old returning from Indonesia with fever and thrombocytopenia; automated cellular morphology analyzer failed to identify parasites, flagging artefactual cells instead. Blood smear and PCR confirmed P. vivax. Patient treated with artemether-lumefantrine and primaquine with rapid recovery and no relapse, illustrating diagnostic limitations of automated systems in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Swiss travel medicine maps support evidence-based malaria prevention advice
articles.title_original: [Travel and malaria prevention : what lies behind the maps].
articles.authors: Veit O, Eperon G, Staehelin C, Chappuis F
articles.details: Commentary on Swiss travel medicine resource (www.healthytravel.ch) using transparent, harmonized methodology developed collaboratively with European countries. Maps classify malaria risk areas based on epidemiological data and present consistent, evidence-based preventive measures to support travel medicine experts and primary care physicians in providing standardized advice.

articles.headline: P. vivax seroprevalence among Myanmar migrants links exposure to demographics and mobility
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and behavioral determinants of Plasmodium vivax-specific antibody responses among short-term Myanmar migrants in Thailand.
articles.authors: Sripoorote P, Inthitanon N, Wattanagoon Y, Lim PS, Cui L, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Nguitragool W, Chotirat S, Longley RJ, Aung PL
articles.details: Cross-sectional sero-survey (March–April 2025) of 300 Myanmar migrants in Tak Province, Thailand assessing IgG responses to 8 P. vivax antigens. Among 294 PCR-negative participants, 35.4% (104) predicted as recently exposed. Age 30–39 (aOR 3.11), secondary education (aOR 3.28), and Karen ethnicity (aOR 3.68) increased exposure odds; protective: seasonal work (aOR 0.28), temporary work (aOR 0.26), higher income (aOR 0.17). Findings support sero-surveillance for border elimination strategies.

articles.headline: ITN use and student residence drive malaria and anemia in Ghanaian adolescents
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic malaria parasite carriage and anaemia among senior high school students in Tempane District of the Upper East Region of Ghana: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Awindago JA, Captain-Esoah M, Motey GA, Angyiereyiri ED, Azameti MK, Adjei MR, Donkor MN, Frempong KK, Dassah SD
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 290 Ghanaian secondary students (age 15–22) found 23.1% malaria prevalence and 49.0% anemia. Day students had 59.2% malaria vs 15.8% in boarders (p<0.001); non-ITN users 39.7% infected vs 5.0% in users (p<0.001). Non-ITN use (aOR 15.97) and day student status (aOR 5.65) predicted infection. Malaria-infected students had lower hemoglobin (11.50 vs 12.56 g/dL, p<0.001); infection associated with anemia (aOR 9.36).

articles.headline: S. mansoni infection intensity linked to gut inflammation independent of malaria
articles.title_original: Schistosoma mansoni Infections Are Associated With Fecal Calprotectin Markers of Gut Inflammation After Accounting for HIV, Hepatitis B, and Malaria.
articles.authors: Wilburn L, Bui HL, Akampurira P, Ddamba R, Oguttu DW, Nabatte B, Kabatereine NB, Chami GF
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (2024) of 640 Ugandan participants (age 6–85) with 49.1% S. mansoni prevalence found 74.4% detectable fecal calprotectin (fCal). S. mansoni infection intensity positively associated with fCal ≥100 µg/g (OR 1.11) and >250 µg/g (OR 1.26). HIV co-infection associated with elevated fCal ≥100 (OR 2.52); malaria was uninformative for fCal outcomes. Findings suggest persistent gut inflammation from schistosomiasis independent of malaria status.

articles.headline: Outcrossed P. falciparum oocysts mature faster, indicating fitness advantage
articles.title_original: Genetic crosses using fluorescently labelled malaria parasites indicate advantages of outcrossing in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li X, Jutzeler K, Abatiyow B, Rezakhani N, Haile M, Leeb A, Patel H, Kappe S, Nosten F, Cheeseman IH, Ferdig M, Anderson TJC, Vaughan A, Kumar S
articles.details: Study of P. falciparum mating dynamics using fluorescently labeled African (NF54-mCherry) and Thai (NHP4026-GFP) clones crossed in mosquitoes. Genotyping of 8,540 oocysts from 435 mosquitoes (7–14 days post-infection) showed decreasing outcrossed and increasing selfed oocysts over time, consistent with faster outcrossed oocyst maturation reflecting fitness advantage via removal of deleterious mutations. Selfed NF54 oocysts were larger, potentially affecting sporozoite production and transmission.

articles.headline: CD4 T cell responses show species-dependent differences in falciparum vs vivax
articles.title_original: Comparison of CD4 T-Cell Response in Plasmodium falciparum and vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Nalubega M, Soon MSF, Andrew D, Ortega-Pajares A, Canning J, Dooley N, Loughland JR, Engwerda C, Kenangalem E, Price RN, Minigo G, Anstey NM, Oyong DA, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Comparative study of CD4 T cell responses in adults with P. falciparum or P. vivax found largely comparable activation and proliferation profiles between species. Peripheral T follicular helper (pTfh) compartment showed species-dependent differences with increased pTfh1 in P. falciparum. P. falciparum infection associated with increased IL-10 production, including in IL-21-producing cells, potentially affecting antibody development pathways.

articles.headline: FcγRIIa mediates antibody-dependent neutrophil phagocytosis of infected RBCs
articles.title_original: Antibody-Dependent Neutrophil Phagocytosis of Plasmodium falciparum-Infected Erythrocytes Is Mediated by FcγRIIa.
articles.authors: Saeed M, Aitken EH, Wines BD, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: In vitro study using TNF and phosphatidylinositol phospholipase C (PIPLC) to deplete FcγRIIIb showed FcγRIII decreased 79% while FcγRIIa increased 82%. Antibody-dependent neutrophil phagocytosis (ADNP) by FcγRIIIb-depleted neutrophils increased 585% (p=0.042); FcγRIIa blocking reduced ADNP >5-fold (~17%, p<0.05). Results suggest FcγRIIa is the primary phagocytic receptor while FcγRIIIb acts as a decoy receptor.

articles.headline: Travel importation accounts for 14–30% of malaria in low-transmission Uganda zones
articles.title_original: Quantifying the Role of Importation on Sustained Malaria Transmission in a Low-to-Moderate Burden Region of Southwest Uganda.
articles.authors: Epstein A, Aramanzan O, Nabende I, Kayondo TM, Obbo M, Tumwesigye R, Wiringilimaana I, Mbabazi M, Kagurusi BA, Tukwasibwe S, Ssewanyana I, Routledge I, Briggs J, Wesolowski A, Greenhouse B, Dorsey G, Arinaitwe E, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study (14 months) of 1,918 individuals from 400 Southwest Ugandan households recorded 283 infections (244 symptomatic, 39 asymptomatic). Overnight travel to higher-incidence areas associated with infection in lower-transmission villages (OR 4.38, 95% CI 1.80–10.64) and during low-transmission periods. Population attributable fraction analysis: travel accounted for 14% of cases overall in low-transmission villages, rising to 30% during low-transmission seasons, primarily from short-distance trips.

articles.headline: Dobinin K inhibits chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum via redox disruption
articles.title_original: Antimalarial Effects of Dobinin K against Chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium falciparum Through Disruption of the Parasite's Redox System.
articles.authors: He LF, Sun H, Cang LQ, Wang JD, Xiao CJ, Jiang B, Shen L
articles.details: Tested Dobinin K against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum Dd2. Compound showed concentration- and time-dependent antiplasmodial activity affecting all parasite developmental stages by disrupting redox homeostasis: increased oxidative products, elevated GSSG/GSH ratio, inhibited antioxidant enzymes (PfGR, PfTrxR, PfSOD), decreased NADP+/NADPH ratio, disrupted mitochondrial membrane potential, and induced apoptosis.

articles.headline: LAMP assay feasible for malaria xenomonitoring in field conditions
articles.title_original: Assessment of automated loop-mediated isothermal amplification-(LAMP-)based xenomonitoring for Plasmodium spp. in Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Eisenbarth A, Adegnika AA, Boussougou-Sambe ST, Glyschewski L, Jacobs T, Kann S, Krüger A, Hagen RM, Ramharter M, Mehmel E, Ndalembouly AGD, Frickmann H
articles.details: Assessed automated LAMP assay for xenomonitoring Plasmodium spp. in Anopheles mosquitoes against real-time PCR. With artificial exposure to P. berghei, LAMP showed 34.9% sensitivity (15/43) versus 51.2% for PCR (22/43). With field-infected mosquitoes, comparable rates: 7.0% (LAMP) vs 7.6% (PCR) for 172 pools. Overall infection rate with human parasites (P. falciparum, P. malariae, P. ovale) was 1.2%.

articles.headline: Controlled human malaria infection reveals immunity on virulence genes
articles.title_original: Correction: Controlled human malaria infection with Plasmodium falciparum demonstrates impact of naturally acquired immunity on virulence gene expression.
articles.authors: Bachmann A, Bruske E, Krumkamp R, Turner L, Wichers JS, Petter M, Held J, Duffy MF, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Kremsner PG, Lell B, Lavstsen T, Frank M, Mordmüller B, Tannich E
articles.details: Correction to article on controlled human malaria infection with Plasmodium falciparum examining impact of naturally acquired immunity on virulence gene expression.

articles.headline: Wastewater-based surveillance feasible in endemic African countries
articles.title_original: Modeling and Laboratory Experiments to Assess Detection of Plasmodium falciparum in Wastewater: Insights for Wastewater-Based Surveillance of Malaria.
articles.authors: Chen W, Ochomo E, Muok E, Bibby K
articles.details: Modeled P. falciparum detection in wastewater with estimated shedding of 5.31 log10 genome copies/day per infection. In non-endemic countries, case rates would require >1000-fold increase for 50% detection probability using 8 PCR replicates at ideal process limit of 3.0 log10 GC/L. Approximately 84% of endemic African countries could achieve this detection probability. Laboratory experiments demonstrated first-order wastewater decay (0.035-0.11 h-1 at 4-35°C).

articles.headline: CRISPR/Cas9pyrR enables marker-free gene editing in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: A heterologous marker-free selection approach for CRISPR/Cas9-based gene editing in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Carrington E, Ballmer D, Niederwieser I, Thommen BT, Brancucci NMB, Voss TS
articles.details: Developed heterologous marker-free CRISPR/Cas9 strategy using simultaneous pyrimethamine resistance mutations in pfdhfr-ts gene. Generated transgenic parasite lines expressing GFP-tagged proteins: PfGEX1 marks nuclear envelope specifically in gametocytes but not asexual stages, and PfNUP116 localizes to perinuclear region in early gametocytes. Demonstrated sequential genetic engineering by additionally tagging PfAP2-G and PfNUP313.

articles.headline: Iron supplementation safe but modest effect on malaria anemia
articles.title_original: Effect of Iron-Containing Micronutrient Powders on Growth and Malaria-Induced Anaemia Among Preschool Children in Ghana: A Cluster-Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Tchum EKY, Koto JE, Kanyoke F, Opoku O, Ussher F, Dassah SD, Amoani B, Tchum SK, Nyarko E
articles.details: Secondary analysis of cluster-randomized trial with 1,806 children (6-35 months) receiving daily micronutrient powder with 12.5mg elemental iron or placebo for 5 months. Iron-containing MNP had no significant effect on growth: height-for-age z-score (β=0.026, p=0.609), weight-for-age z-score (β=-0.015, p=0.719), or weight-for-height z-score (β=-0.035, p=0.463). However, MNP reduced risk of malaria-induced anaemia (risk difference 0.050, 95% CI 0.004-0.096; p=0.032).

articles.headline: Novel invasion inhibitors target parasite sterol metabolism
articles.title_original: Unpacking boxes: identification of novel inhibitors of malaria parasite invasion.
articles.authors: Barnes CB, Schneider MP, Ventura OD, Razook Z, McCann K, Boulet C, Literski AH, Barry AE, Sleebs BE, Crabb BS, Gilson PR, Bullen HE
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Screened 400 compounds for parasite invasion and egress inhibitors. Identified 20 invasion inhibitors and 2 partial egress inhibitors at 1 μM. Six compounds showed invasion as likely mechanism (similar growth and invasion IC50s). Two compounds (MMV006931, MMV024850) directly blocked merozoite invasion. Resistance selection and whole-genome sequencing of MMV006931-resistant parasites identified mutations in PfNCR1 (sterol exporter).

articles.headline: Malaria increases anemia risk in HIV-infected children on ART
articles.title_original: Anemia in HIV-Infected Children on ART in Uganda: High Risk Associations with Malaria, Opportunistic Infections, and Poor Adherence.
articles.authors: Anyelwisye CL, Ogwal JK, Kadogosa SE, Timuzigu R, Anyetaba C, Wagubi R, Olong C, John EA, Muwanguzi E, Okongo B
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 293 HIV-positive children. Anaemia prevalence was 6.14% (95% CI 3.4-8.9%), predominantly normocytic normochromic type (72.2%). Multivariate analysis identified independent factors associated with anaemia: recent malaria episode (AOR=9.61, 95% CI 3.26-28.34), opportunistic infection (AOR=6.78, 95% CI 1.28-35.83), and poor ART adherence (AOR=14.75, 95% CI 1.31-165.65). Findings highlight need for integrated malaria control and enhanced adherence support.

articles.headline: Humidity drives seasonal malaria transmission in refugee camps
articles.title_original: Seasonal Climate Drivers and Immunohematological Profiles of Malaria in Rohingya Refugee Camps, Bangladesh: A 4-Year Repeated Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Islam A, Akhi MM, Hossain A, Palit PK, Rahman N, Baidya S, Prome TM, Rahman M, Sultana A, Chowdhury MMH, Alam MS
articles.details: 4-year repeated cross-sectional study (2021-2024) of 582 participants with 345 confirmed malaria cases. Peak transmission occurred during monsoon season (June-September), with P. falciparum showing earlier and sharper peaks than P. vivax. Relative humidity showed strongest correlation with malaria incidence (r=0.724-0.77), followed by rainfall and temperature. P. falciparum-infected individuals exhibited significantly higher antibody titers and more pronounced hematological alterations (anemia, thrombocytopenia, elevated ESR).

articles.headline: Spatial heterogeneity of malaria in pregnancy linked to antenatal care
articles.title_original: Mapping inequity: spatial clustering of malaria in pregnancy in the Amhara region, Northwest Ethiopia (2018-2024).
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Asemahagn MA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Retrospective spatial analysis of 53,478 confirmed malaria cases among pregnant women (2018-2024), with annual incidence of 9.2 per 1,000 populations. Identified 7 significant high-risk spatial clusters. Spatial regression revealed malaria incidence negatively associated with ANC4 utilization (IRR=0.92, 95% CI 0.91-0.94), soil moisture, API, and temperature (IRR=0.65, 95% CI 0.58-0.73), but positively associated with ANC1 utilization (IRR=1.07, 95% CI 1.06-1.08).

articles.headline: Malaria-bacterial coinfection in febrile children with seizures
articles.title_original: Malaria and bacteremia in febrile children presenting at a teaching hospital in Kumasi, Ghana.
articles.authors: Gyasi WA, Boampong K, Sylverken J, Badu K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 270 febrile children at teaching hospital. Malaria prevalence 33%, bacteremia 31%, with coinfection in 10.4% (±3.6%, 95% CI). Seizures independently associated with increased odds of malaria-bacterial coinfection (aOR=3.57, 95% CI 1.28-9.95, p=0.015). Most common bacterial isolates: Staphylococcus aureus (43.8%) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (6.3%), with multi-drug resistance in 34.5% of isolates.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccines complement chemoprophylaxis and vector control
articles.title_original: Malaria Prevention: Progress to Date.
articles.authors: Grobusch MP, Schnyder JL, Schlagenhauf P, de Jong HK, Hanscheid T
articles.details: Review of malaria prevention strategies combining chemoprophylaxis, seasonal preventive treatment, and vector control. WHO-endorsed vaccines RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix) and R21/Matrix-M provide moderate protection in young children while significantly reducing severe disease and death, demonstrating safety in endemic areas. Monoclonal antibodies in development as preventive treatment to protect immunocompromised individuals. Integration of drug-based prevention, vaccines, and vector control essential for reducing disease burden.

articles.headline: Trimeric NF-Y-like complex required for sporozoite release from oocysts
articles.title_original: Sporozoite egress from Plasmodium oocysts requires a trimeric NF-Y-like complex.
articles.authors: Preira CMF, Deligianni E, Andreadaki M, Tzagkaraki S, Kokkas E, Kołakowska A, Gessmann R, Bertaso C, Masiero S, Russo R, Nardini M, Siden-Kiamos I, Gourlay LJ, Currà C
articles.details: Identified ORP3 as third subunit of ORP complex essential for oocyst rupture and sporozoite egress in mosquitoes. ORP3 contains conserved NF-YA helix A1 required for trimerization with ORP1/ORP2 dimer, as confirmed by in vitro Yeast-3-hybrid assays. orp3(-) mutant showed defect in oocyst opening similar to orp1(-) and orp2(-) mutants. Sporozoites from orp(-) mutants retained motility but were unable to develop into exoerythrocytic forms after injection into mice.

articles.headline: Single CSP epitope sufficient for protection against sporozoites
articles.title_original: Relevance of the antibody Fc fragment and epitope valency in protection against malaria sporozoites.
articles.authors: Flores-Garcia Y, Aguirre-Botero MC, Tripathi P, Locke E, Park M, Mathis-Torres S, Salgado-Jimenez B, Herrera-Arevalo S, Gladston A, Asady B, Coppens I, Amino R, Zavala F
articles.details: Evaluated protective efficacy of antibody Fab fragments and Fc-mutated antibodies against Plasmodium berghei sporozoites expressing different numbers of (NPNA)3 circumsporozoite protein epitopes. Fab fragments and Fc-mutated antibodies showed protective efficacy comparable to intact antibodies, indicating limited or no role for Fc functions in sporozoite protection. Binding to single (NPNA)3 epitope was sufficient to reduce liver burden, while sterile protection required antibody binding to two separate epitopes.

articles.headline: Anopheles maculatus confirmed as malaria vector with flexible feeding
articles.title_original: Anopheles maculatus s.l as potential malaria vectors with opportunistic feeding in Meghalaya, Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sihag AK, Rajkonwar J, Kharbisnop B, Sangma DM, Budnah C, Acharya P, Chellappan S, Hazarika G, Talukder P, Pebam R, Baruah K, Bhattacharyya DR, Hajong SR, Kaur H, Narain K, Bhowmick IP
articles.details: Molecular survey of 215 Anopheles specimens in Khasi Hills and Garo Hills regions, Meghalaya identified 177 (82.32%) An. maculatus sensu stricto, 33 (15.34%) An. pseudowillmori, and 5 (2.32%) An. willmori. Plasmodium falciparum naturally infected 49 (27.68%) of 177 An. maculatus s.s. and 16 (48.48%) of 33 An. pseudowillmori specimens. Blood meal analysis revealed opportunistic feeding: 15% human, 16% cattle, 17% both. FST analysis showed significant genetic differentiation between populations from the two regions.

articles.headline: CIS43 junctional epitope vaccine shows comparable efficacy to R21
articles.title_original: Protective immunity against malaria by a nanoparticle CIS43-based junctional vaccine alone or in combination with R21.
articles.authors: Tripathi P, Koo JH, Chen X, Silva Pereira LD, Dillon M, Zhang B, Lofgren M, Nguyen KT, Teng IT, Bonilla B, Kerscher S, Kong WP, Henry AR, Stephens T, Tsybovsky Y, Weldon SR, Douek DC, Pierson TC, Batista FD, Idris AH, Seder RA, Kwong PD, Zhou T
articles.details: Developed nanoparticle-formatted vaccine displaying tandem repeats of CIS43 junctional epitope of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein and compared to R21 vaccine. At 1 μg dose, comparable reduction in liver burden was observed with best junctional vaccine and R21. At 0.25 μg dose, junctional vaccine showed modest reduction versus R21. Combining junctional and R21 vaccines did not yield substantial improvement. Junctional vaccine elicited antibodies against both junctional and major repeat regions, while R21 elicited primarily against major repeats.

articles.headline: Low chikungunya seroprevalence with concurrent malaria in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Seroprevalence of chikungunya virus infection and co-occurrence with malaria among patients with febrile illness in Oguma, the middle belt region of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Daudu MJ, Omatola CA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 200 febrile patients in Oguma, Nigeria screened serologically for chikungunya and microscopically for malaria. Anti-chikungunya antibodies detected in 9 (4.50%) [4 (2.0%) IgM, 5 (2.5%) IgG]. Three (1.5%) patients with active chikungunya were co-infected with malaria, 1 (1.0%) had chikungunya mono-infection, and 60 (30%) had malaria alone. Active chikungunya cases occurred only in males [4 (4.3%) vs 0%], with rash and back pain frequently observed (p<0.05).

articles.headline: Mass drug administration requires integration with vaccines and monitoring
articles.title_original: Application Strategy and Research Progress of Large-Scale Population Drug Intervention in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Cao Z, Gu Y, Li G, Deng C
articles.details: Review of mass drug administration (MDA) strategy for malaria elimination. MDA plays important role but faces core limitations including long-term drug resistance risks, insufficient implementation sustainability, and high failure rate of regional adaptation. Challenges include control of multiple malaria species and newly discovered Plasmodium knowlesi. Authors propose 'MDA+' collaborative strategy integrating vaccines, digital monitoring, and cross-border cooperation to optimize resource allocation, achieve full parasite coverage, and advance global elimination.

articles.headline: Piperaquine prevents malaria in pregnancy but reduces infant birthweight
articles.title_original: Effect mechanisms of different malaria chemoprevention regimens in pregnancy on infant growth outcomes: causal mediation analysis of a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Nguyen AT, Nankabirwa JI, Kakuru A, Roh ME, Aguti M, Adrama H, Kizza J, Olwoch P, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Jagannathan P, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial (NCT04336189) with N=761 infant follow-up comparing monthly intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), or DP+SP. IPTp-DP+SP versus IPTp-SP decreased mean weight-for-length z-score by 0.18 [95% CI -0.03, 0.39] between 1-3 months and 0.28 [95% CI 0.07, 0.49] between 4-6 months, with largest differences among primigravidae. Lower active placental malaria in DP+SP provided partial benefit (+0.06 WLZ [95% CI 0.02, 0.10]). DP+SP had 0.28 lower length-for-age z-score versus DP at 7-13 months.

articles.headline: PBO-LLINs show reduced efficacy against resistant vectors in Kenya
articles.title_original: Reduced Efficacy of Piperonyl Butoxide Based Long-Lasting Insecticide-Treated Nets Against Malaria Vectors in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Kahindi S, Abel L, Kirwa E, Amunga M, Kimachas E, Omondi E, Cherono M, Mangeni J, Obala A, Markwalter C, O'Meara WP
articles.details: Evaluated susceptibility of 2,020 wild-collected and susceptible-strain Anopheles in Bungoma County, western Kenya to piperonyl butoxide (PBO)-based long-lasting insecticide-treated nets using WHO bioassays. Mortality after exposure to new PBO-LLINs was 68% in wild-collected mosquitoes and only 34% with 24-month-old used nets. Very low mortality observed with permethrin with or without PBO in susceptibility tests. Anopheles arabiensis was main surviving species. Reduced sensitivity attributed to net aging and very high resistance levels in the local vector population.

articles.headline: L9LS monoclonal antibody achieves 42.7% efficacy against perennial malaria
articles.title_original: Safety and efficacy of the monoclonal antibody L9LS for malaria prevention in children exposed to perennial malaria transmission in Kenya: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial.
articles.authors: Steinhardt LC, Kwambai TK, Oneko M, Ouma E, Njoroge R, Callier V, Hu Z, Gutman JR, Yego R, Otieno K, Onoka K, Otieno L, Oduol K, Serebryannyy L, Lin BC, Adams W, Hickman S, Preston AC, Carlton K, Holdsworth M, Xiao Y, O Ter Kuile F, Odongo W, Murphy SC, Tran TM, Kariuki S, Crompton PD, Seder RA
articles.details: Phase 2 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in Siaya county, western Kenya. Parts 1a-1b enrolled 96 children (72 L9LS, 24 placebo) at doses 5-40 mg/kg for safety assessment. Part 2 randomized 324 children aged 5-59 months to two L9LS doses 10-20 mg/kg (n=106), one L9LS dose (n=108), or placebo (n=110). Grade 3+ treatment-related adverse events occurred in 1% each of L9LS and placebo injections. In part 2, 66% of two-dose L9LS group had P. falciparum infection during 12 months versus 83% of placebo (protective efficacy 42.7%, 95% CI 22.5-57.7, p=0.0003).

articles.headline: Antibody-based approach to malaria prevention in perennial transmission
articles.title_original: Antibody-based malaria prevention in an intense perennial transmission setting.
articles.authors: Engwerda CR, Haque A
articles.details: Study examining antibody-based malaria prevention strategies in settings with intense perennial transmission. Specific details regarding study design, population characteristics, and quantitative results cannot be determined from available title information.

articles.headline: Safety monitoring methods for RTS,S/AS01 vaccine implementation compared
articles.title_original: Assessing safety in the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine implementation programme: an overview and comparison of methods.
articles.authors: Schellenberg D, Hamel M, Njuguna P, Furrer E, Praet N, Balakrishnan MR, Jain S, Maiga D, Schuerman L, Whitney CG
articles.details: Overview of safety monitoring methods used in RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine implementation program. Described complementary approaches including sentinel hospital and community-based mortality surveillance to evaluate safety signals from phase 3 trial, strengthened pharmacovigilance to identify rare unanticipated signals related to vaccination, and cohort event monitoring to identify rare conditions of special interest. Article summarizes strengths and weaknesses of these safety-monitoring approaches.

articles.headline: Malaria research priorities amid control stagnation and resource constraints
articles.title_original: Prioritising in a crisis: what next for malaria research?
articles.authors: Chestnutt EG, Afolabi MO, D'Alessandro U, Delves M, Gosling R, Jagoe G, Lasry E, Maiteki C, Moon RW, Oriero E, Pitt C, Tadesse F, Tibenderana JK, Rao VB
articles.details: Perspective arguing malaria research must protect lives now while preparing for future threats amid funding shortfalls, parasite/vector resistance, and changing epidemiology. Recommends three priorities: (1) pragmatic, equity-focused implementation and health-systems research to optimize effectiveness and coverage of existing interventions; (2) sustained investment in next-generation chemoprevention, vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, vector control, and digital/AI-enabled surveillance; (3) research on sustainable financing and how data, economic evidence, and political economy analysis can inform national and global decision-making.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M vaccine awareness low among Nigerian caregivers
articles.title_original: Awareness, acceptability and determinants of the new malaria vaccine among caregivers of under-five children in Asaba, Nigeria: a multi-centre cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ilikannu CO, Uwadia OM, Ilikannu SO, Agenu P, Mbachu CNP, Okoye NP, Uwaezuoke AC, Arinze B, Akinkunmi I, Jideije JA, Onwuasoeze C, Dan-Nwankwo C, Aladegbehingbe OE, Igboejesi PC, Onomuighokpo HO, Chukwu N, Igwe E
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 358 caregivers at health facilities in Asaba, Nigeria (January-June 2025). Only 36.3% were aware of R21/Matrix-M, though 85.8% were willing to vaccinate. Main reasons for reluctance: fear of adverse reactions (45.1%), lack of interest (19.6%), spousal disagreement (13.7%). Female caregivers and civil servants were significantly more willing to accept vaccination (p=0.006 and p=0.018).

articles.headline: Global malaria control progress: regional experts discuss 2030 targets
articles.title_original: Unite against Malaria: challenges and achievements in malaria control around the globe.
articles.details: Perspective article marking World Malaria Day 2026, themed "Driven to End Malaria: Now We Can. Now We Must." Three regional experts discuss challenges in malaria control: molecular surveillance and vector genomics in Peruvian Amazon region, molecular epidemiology in Horn of Africa, and epidemiology and implementation research in Asian-Pacific region. Reflects on WHO Global Technical Strategy 2016-2030 goals.

articles.headline: Multiple genetic pathways drive artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Insights into the factors contributing to artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Sharma P, Verma K, Tyagi S, Singh K, Bharti PK
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Narrative review of molecular mechanisms underlying artemisinin resistance (ARTR). While Kelch13 mutations (particularly propeller domain) are linked to ARTR, in vitro and field studies indicate additional genetic determinants and pathways contribute: reduced hemoglobin endocytosis, increased phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase levels, unfolded protein response activation, and polymorphism in other genes.

articles.headline: Structural biology advances blood-stage malaria vaccine development
articles.title_original: Atomic clarity: how structural biology is shaping blood-stage malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Nema S, Rathore S, Banerjee M, Zhou ZH, Mohmmed A, Malhotra P
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Review of structural biology approaches to malaria vaccine design, particularly cryogenic electron microscopy (cryoEM) for analyzing blood-stage parasites and their multiprotein surface complexes essential for host cell invasion. Recent advances in AI and deep learning have improved cryoEM data analysis and structural modeling, accelerating rational design of broadly protective malaria vaccines.

articles.headline: PBO nets and IRS reduce malaria incidence in adaptive control trial
articles.title_original: Adaptive intervention strategies for malaria vector control in western Kenya: a cluster, sequential, multiple-assignment, randomised, open-label trial.
articles.authors: Zhou G, Wang X, Lee MC, Machani MG, Kidwell KM, Atieli HE, Kazura JW, Githure JI, Zhong D, Yan G
articles.details: Cluster RCT with 47,614 participants comparing vector control strategies in Kisumu County, Kenya. PBO-treated nets significantly reduced malaria incidence vs standard LLINs at 1-6 months (aIRR 0.75, p<0.0001) and 7-12 months (aIRR 0.87, p=0.0012). Stage 2 tested adaptive strategies in non-responding clusters. Overall, all four adaptive intervention strategies significantly reduced clinical malaria incidence vs standard LLINs (p<0.01).

articles.headline: Tinospora crispa extract shows synergistic antimalarial effect with artesunate
articles.title_original: Antimalarial Potency of Tinospora crispa Stem Extract and Artesunate Against Plasmodium berghei: A Study on Combination Therapy.
articles.authors: Ounjaijean S, Somsak V
articles.details: Preclinical study in mice infected with Plasmodium berghei evaluating combination antimalarial therapy. Artesunate ED50 was 200.31 mg/kg; Tinospora crispa extract ED50 was 1.96 mg/kg. Combination therapy demonstrated synergistic effects (combination index <1 at ED50, ED50/2, ED50/4) with significantly higher parasitemia inhibition, enhanced protection against hematocrit reduction and weight loss, and prolonged mean survival time.

articles.headline: Malaria treatment in infants <6 months: evidence gaps and needs
articles.title_original: Malaria in children aged <6 months: a narrative review of current evidence, recommendations and practice gaps.
articles.authors: Dalsuco J, Bassat Q, Ahmed H, Pfeffer S, Winnips C, Boulton C, Otsyula N, D'Alessandro U
articles.details: Narrative review of malaria epidemiology and management in neonates and young infants. Clinical malaria is rare but often misdiagnosed due to symptom overlap with other conditions. Evidence on safety and efficacy of antimalarial therapies in infants <5 kg remains limited. Until recently, no antimalarials were licensed for neonates or infants <4.5 kg, highlighting urgent need for harmonized treatment guidelines.

articles.headline: Spatial hotspot mapping and forecasting guide malaria control in Uganda
articles.title_original: Hotspot Mapping, Spatiotemporal Clustering and Forecasting of Malaria Incidence in Uganda, 2014-2023: Strategies to Inform Targeted Public Health Interventions.
articles.authors: Paasi G, Alunyo JP, Nakiyemba A, Ocen GG, Pande S, Alaroker F, Waako PJ, Olupot-Olupot P
articles.details: Ecological study analyzing district-level malaria surveillance data from Uganda 2014-2023. Spatial heterogeneity analysis identified hotspots in West Nile, Acholi, and Lango. ARIMA(2,1,1) time-series model projected monthly malaria cases at approximately 1.0-1.2 million through 2030 with seasonal peaks April-June. Findings support intensified, geographically-targeted control in high-burden districts and adaptive planning.
