articles.headline: Systematic review assesses malaria RDT accuracy in endemic regions
articles.title_original: Are rapid diagnostic tests reliable for the detection of malaria in the emergency department?
articles.authors: Moran C, Coyne S
articles.details: Systematic review of diagnostic accuracy for malaria rapid diagnostic tests in febrile adults from endemic regions. Database search of MEDLINE, EMBASE, Cochrane, and Google Scholar identified 138 papers; 6 met inclusion criteria. Limited quantitative results provided in this summary.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria alters gut microbiota composition in Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Gut microbiome alterations among Ghanaian children with asymptomatic malaria infections.
articles.authors: Larbi AA, Etsey M, Brew O, Koduah B, Mawuenyega RE, Amewu EKA, Essilfie NK, Wireko S, Kwarteng A, Gyan BA
articles.details: 16S rRNA sequencing study of gut microbiota in children with asymptomatic malaria (predominantly mixed P. falciparum and P. malariae). Infected individuals showed decreased Firmicutes (52.5% to 44.0%), increased Bacteroidetes (34.7% to 45.6%), increased Proteobacteria (3.0% to 4.8%), and decreased Faecalibacterium. Beta-diversity analysis showed malaria status significantly affected microbial composition (PERMANOVA p<0.05), explaining approximately 19.1% of variation.

articles.headline: Tfh2 cells drive high-titer anti-PvDBPII antibody responses in vivax malaria
articles.title_original: A functional crosstalk between circulating follicular helper 2 T cells and memory B cells drives anti-Plasmodium vivax antibodies.
articles.authors: Salsabila ZZ, Kochayoo P, Khunsri T, Tianpothong P, Leepiyasakulchai C, Thawornpan P, Hafalla JCR, Adams JH, Chootong P
articles.details: Study of 40 subjects with acute P. vivax malaria (23 seropositive for anti-PvDBPII antibodies; 3 High Responders produced inhibitory antibodies). Circulating Tfh2 (cTfh2) cells showed expansion in seropositive subjects, with higher ICOS co-stimulatory molecule expression in High Responders. PvDBPII antigen specifically stimulated cTfh2 activation. cTfh2-memory B cell co-cultures promoted differentiation into antibody-secreting cells producing anti-PvDBPII IgG.

articles.headline: Maternal postpartum ACT reduces infant malaria incidence in coastal PNG
articles.title_original: Malaria in Papua New Guinean infants: a prespecified analysis of a randomised postpartum antimalarial treatment trial.
articles.authors: Tesine P, Woon SA, Laman M, Yadi G, Kasian B, Lorry L, Salman S, Pomat W, Manning L, Chih H, Moore BR, Davis TME
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: RCT with 151 infants in Madang Province, PNG, followed 6 months. Mothers randomized to no treatment or ACT (artemether-lumefantrine or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine). Malaria incidence: 2 slide-positive cases (1.3%, 95% CI 0.2-5.2); 3 PCR-detected infections; overall 3.3% (95% CI 1.2-8.0). Four of five cases occurred in no-treatment group. No significant differences in infant growth or development.

articles.headline: Combining livestock insecticide treatment and nets for vector control
articles.title_original: Integrating zooprophylaxis, insecticide-treated livestock and mosquito nets for malaria control: a One Health proposal.
articles.authors: Otolorin GR, Adegboye OA, Dishon CH, Dunka HI, Adanu AW, Ihieri CO, McBryde ES
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This review proposes an integrated vector management strategy combining insecticide-treated livestock (ITL), zooprophylaxis, and insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) to improve malaria control. ITL has been demonstrated to significantly reduce mosquito survival, and the combined approach could improve control for vector species that feed outdoors and prefer animal hosts.

articles.headline: Malaria transmission risk shaped ancient human habitat choice
articles.title_original: Malaria shaped human spatial organization for the past 74 thousand years.
articles.authors: Colucci M, Leonardi M, Blinkhorn J, Irish SR, Padilla-Iglesias C, Kaboth-Bar S, Gosling WD, Snow RW, Manica A, Scerri EML
articles.details: Using species distribution models of mosquito complexes and palaeoclimatic data, researchers estimated Plasmodium falciparum malaria transmission risk in sub-Saharan Africa from 74,000 to 5,000 years ago and correlated it with reconstructed human habitat niches. Results demonstrated that humans avoided or were unsuccessful in establishing populations in potential malaria hotspots, suggesting disease was a key selective force in human spatial organization.

articles.headline: China establishes standard for Plasmodium antigen rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: [Interpretation of a health industry standard Detection of Plasmodium spp. Immune-chromatographic test (WS/T 10029-2025)].
articles.authors: Xu S, Zhu G, Cao J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: This article interprets a new Chinese health industry standard (WS/T 10029-2025) for detection of Plasmodium species using immunochromatographic rapid diagnostic tests. The standard provides technical guidelines and operational procedures covering testing principles, sample collection, detection procedures, and result interpretation to standardize malaria diagnosis across medical and disease control institutions.

articles.headline: Placental malaria vaccine shows high cost-effectiveness in primigravidae
articles.title_original: Modelling the Cost-Effectiveness of a Placental Malaria Vaccine in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Chinkhumba J, Manda-Taylor L, D'Alessio F, Madanitsa M
articles.details: A decision-analytic model compared vaccinating women of childbearing age against placental malaria versus no vaccination. Under baseline assumptions (40% vaccine efficacy, 30% uptake, $5 dose price), the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio was $321 per perinatal DALY averted for primigravidae versus $4444 for multigravidae, with best-case scenarios yielding $225 and $3148 respectively.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria diagnosis and management challenges in resource-limited settings
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria in resource-limited African regions: Diagnostic and therapeutic challenges.
articles.authors: Akintunde JK, Oluwajembola AM, Oladosu MA, Akintunde DG, Akintunde OG, Ojo AE, Golagha R
articles.details: This review discusses cerebral malaria in resource-limited African healthcare settings, where it remains associated with mortality of 15%-30% and long-term neurological sequelae in 10%-30% of survivors. Major diagnostic and management barriers include widespread misdiagnosis (up to 23% of clinically diagnosed cases), lack of fundoscopic examination and neuroimaging, delayed presentation, inadequate supportive care infrastructure, and limited rehabilitation services.

articles.headline: LLIN ownership 71.5% but utilization only 59.9% in Ethiopian communities
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Net (LLIN) Ownership, Utilization, and Associated Barriers in Malaria-Endemic Communities of Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Waldetensai A, Tasew G, Yewhalaw D, Takie H, Gidey B, Kinde S, Gemechu F, Yirga S, Kinfe E, Hailemariam A, Tadesse H, Solomon H, Assefa G, Dilu D, Bashaye S, Wuletaw Y, Abdulatif B, Kebede T, Tadiwos S, Gebrewold G, Hailu S, Tesfaye F, Tollera G, Hailu M, Guiyun Y, Eukubay A, Gebresillassie A
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 9,222 households across Ethiopia found long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) ownership of 71.5% but overall utilization of only 59.9%, with higher rates in rural areas (72.7%) and among vulnerable groups (pregnant women 78.5%, children under five 67.2%). Major barriers to use included 60.5% lacking confidence in hanging nets and 64.1% misconceiving that malaria risk is restricted to rainy season.

articles.headline: Guillain-Barré syndrome with asymmetric presentation after childhood malaria
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome After Malaria: A Case Report of a 7-Year-Old Child With Asymmetric Onset of Acute Motor Axonal Neuropathy.
articles.authors: Abusaada MIM, Ali MMI, Adam MMA, Ali MHO, Baraka MAM, Bakheit AAM, Mohamed Ahmed KAH, Mohmed IYK
articles.details: A case report describes a 7-year-old child who developed asymmetric acute motor axonal neuropathy after malaria with relapsing weakness, highlighting that atypical Guillain-Barré variants can present unusually in children and emphasizing the importance of early recognition and treatment in malaria-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Severe childhood malaria associated with long-term cognitive deficits
articles.title_original: Malaria may cause long-term brain deficits, study shows.
articles.authors: Vogel G
articles.details: This study reports that Ugandan children who survived severe malaria infection performed worse on cognitive tests in later years, demonstrating long-term neurological effects from severe malaria infection in childhood.

articles.headline: Malaria exposure not associated with SARS-CoV-2 seropositivity
articles.title_original: Life Course Malaria Exposure and SARS-CoV-2 Seroepidemiology in Ugandan Adolescents: A Longitudinal Study Nested in a Birth Cohort.
articles.authors: Zirimenya L, Nayebare D, Baine C, Nassuuna J, Namutebi M, Zziwa C, Nsubuga D, Kiwudhu F, Akello F, Tumusiime J, Oduru G, Kabami G, Akurut H, Nakyesige R, Natukunda A, Wajja A, Lubyayi L, Kimuda S, Nkurunungi G, Serwanga J, Doores KJ, Webb EL, Elliott AM
articles.details: A longitudinal study of 476 Ugandan adolescents (mean age 16.4 years) assessed associations between life-course malaria exposure (clinical malaria history, asymptomatic parasitaemia, and antibody responses to P. falciparum merozoite surface protein 2 and apical membrane antigen 1) and SARS-CoV-2 seropositivity. SARS-CoV-2 seropositivity increased from 63% at baseline to 89.3% at 6 months and 94.9% at 12 months, but multivariable analyses showed no consistent association between malaria exposure and seropositivity or antibody levels.

articles.headline: Abbott RDT shows cross-reactivity with non-falciparum Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: Strong Cross-Reactivity of Anti-Plasmodium falciparum pLDH Monoclonal Antibodies With Plasmodium malariae pLDH in the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.f/P.v Rapid Diagnostic Test.
articles.authors: Montoia G, Sanchez MCA, Lima-Cooper G, Farinas MLRN, Inoue J, Di Santi SM
articles.details: Testing the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test on 40 samples revealed accurate detection of P. falciparum and P. vivax. However, 12 of 14 P. malariae samples and 4 P. ovale samples yielded positive results on the T2 line intended for Pf-specific detection, with specificity of anti-Pf-pLDH antibodies against P. malariae being only 14.29% (p < 0.05).

articles.headline: Selective Plasmodium proteasome inhibitors show nanomolar antiparasitic activity
articles.title_original: An Optimized Route to the Syringolin Natural Products Enables Combinatorial Synthesis of Selective, Bioactive Inhibitors of the Plasmodium falciparum 20S Proteasome.
articles.authors: Gu X, Fajtova P, Yan NL, Saxena A, Fei F, Zhu J, Tse E, Melo A, Yoo E, Buchwald NH, Southworth DR, Bogyo M, Derisi JL, Gestwicki JE, O'Donoghue AJ, Sello JK
articles.details: Researchers developed an improved synthetic route to syringolin natural products and generated a combinatorial library of selective inhibitors of the Plasmodium falciparum proteasome. Compounds identified showed high second-order rate constants for Plasmodium proteasome inhibition and nanomolar antiparasitic activity with selectivity over the human proteasome, as confirmed by cryo-EM structures of inhibitor-proteasome complexes.

articles.headline: Mefloquine-platinum complexes enhance antiparasitic activity in vivo
articles.title_original: Examination of Coligands in Mefloquine-Metal Complexes Reveals the Structural Determinants of Activity against Plasmodium falciparum and Schistosoma mansoni.
articles.authors: Villarreal W, Quadros HC, Colina-Vegas L, Aboagye SY, Dziwornu GA, Ribeiro GH, Barros AI, Kucharski DJ, Rahbari M, Brandstädter C, D'Alessandro S, Basilico N, Masike K, Mehlala N, Nobrega JA, Deflon VM, Navarro M, Boratyński PJ, Chibale K, Williams DL, Batista AA, Moreira DRM
articles.details: Researchers synthesized mefloquine-metal complexes with platinum or palladium centers and evaluated them against Plasmodium falciparum. The metal center enhanced the antiparasitic property of mefloquine through thioredoxin/glutathione reductase targeting. Mefloquine-Pt complexes demonstrated high in vivo efficacy through intracellular metal accumulation that disrupted essential parasite biochemical pathways while sparing mammalian cells.

articles.headline: Malaria surveillance barriers in sub-Saharan pharmacy sector
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers to malaria surveillance in community pharmacies and over-the-counter medicine sellers in sub-Saharan Africa's health sector: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Bonful HA, Owiredu D, Annor RB, Opoku-Mireku M, Abokyi LN, Vroom BD, Kenu E, Kretchy IA, Koduah A
articles.details: Protocol for scoping review of facilitators and barriers to malaria surveillance in community pharmacies and over-the-counter medicine sellers in sub-Saharan Africa. Will search PubMed, CINAHL, Scopus, Web of Science, and Hinari through June 15, 2026, with English-language full-text inclusion. Data will be synthesized narratively and reported following PRISMA guidelines.

articles.headline: Archaeological evidence of human-malaria interaction in East Asia
articles.title_original: Historical texts and archaeological evidence for malaria and its interactions with humans from East to South Asia.
articles.authors: Li G, Yuan X, Wang T
articles.details: Synthesizes archaeological, biological, and ecological evidence exploring human-malaria interaction in East, Southeast, and South Asia. Reviews genetic analysis of Plasmodium species, ancient Chinese historical/medical documents, and skeletal markers. Examines evolution of Plasmodium and humans in this context, highlighting malaria's role in human demographic shifts and biological resilience, noting that rice cultivation inadvertently created suitable environments for malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Oocyst stage biology and transmission-blocking malaria research
articles.title_original: Oocyst: knowns and unknowns about the lengthiest life stage of the malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Bailey AJ, Vlachou D, Christophides GK
articles.details: Review synthesizing knowledge of Plasmodium oocyst biology, the longest and least-explored parasite life stage. The oocyst occurs in mosquito midgut and undergoes prolonged development before differentiating into thousands of sporozoites. Major gaps persist in understanding molecular/cellular processes including transcriptional regulation, nutrient acquisition, metabolic remodeling, and mosquito immune interactions. Highlights how deeper insight could inform next-generation transmission-blocking strategies and malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Plasmodium knowlesi human blood-stage infection model development
articles.title_original: Development of an experimental human blood-stage model for studying Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Gower JSE, Webster R, Westaway JAF, Tickner JA, Wang CYT, Peters JM, Andrew D, Tan AF, Webb L, Potter AJ, Woo R, Mathison S, Sahai N, Chen N, Cheng Q, Boyle MJ, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Woodford J, Jennings HE, Amante FH, Barber BE
articles.details: Developed P. knowlesi YH1 parasite bank cultured in human serum and evaluated in induced blood-stage malaria (IBSM) model with four adults using dose escalation (∼2,800 to ∼280,000 parasites). Two of four participants developed detectable parasitaemia: Participant 2 at ∼2,800 parasites showed ∼6 parasites/mL on day 21; Participant 3 at ∼280,000 parasites peaked at 202 parasites/mL with parasite multiplication rate (PMR32) of 2.33 (95% CI 1.66–3.25). All received artemether-lumefantrine.

articles.headline: China-Africa malaria control cooperation in people-to-people exchanges
articles.title_original: [Practice and prospect of China's participation in international cooperation for malaria control from the perspective of the "China-Africa Year of People-to-People Exchanges"].
articles.authors: Liang C, Zhu G, Cao J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Discusses strategic significance and practical pathways for China's participation in international malaria control cooperation, particularly with African countries in context of the 2026 "China-Africa Year of People-to-People Exchanges." Proposes policy recommendations for optimizing bilateral cooperation models to accelerate progress toward global malaria elimination and building a China-Africa health community.

articles.headline: Magneto-optical microscopy quantifies malaria parasites in blood
articles.title_original: Magneto-optical microscopy platform for quantitative imaging of hemozoin in blood for malaria diagnosis.
articles.authors: Kinyua DM, Kotar J, Cicuta P
articles.details: Presents magneto-optical (MO) imaging integrating into conventional polarizing microscope for label-free, quantitative detection of malaria infection via hemozoin crystals. External magnetic field induces torque on paramagnetic hemozoin, aligning them and modulating optical anisotropy to produce field-dependent change in transmitted polarization contrast. Integrates ratiometric intensity analysis and threshold-based segmentation metrics establishing quantitative correlation between MO signal strength and hemozoin concentration. Enables automated malaria diagnostics suitable for endemic regions with established microscopy expertise.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum MKT1 protein dispensable for gametogenesis
articles.title_original: An MKT1 domain protein is dispensable for erythrocytic stages of plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Chakraborty B, McGee SM, Kumar S
articles.details: Reports that P. falciparum MKT1 (PfMKT1), a posttranscriptional gene regulation protein, was expressed in asexual and sexual stages. P. falciparum with MKT1 gene deletion (Pfmkt1¯) proliferated asexually similar to wildtype NF54, differentiated into mature male and female gametocytes, underwent gametogenesis forming male and female gametes, and showed no apparent defect in flagellar gamete formation. Identifies that MKT1-like protein is dispensable during asexual and sexual stage development.

articles.headline: Malaria serology identifies surveillance blind spots in low transmission
articles.title_original: Malaria antibody responses augment surveillance in low-transmission settings in the Upper River Region, the Gambia.
articles.authors: Aydin RA, Keller T, Dabira ED, Mohammed N, Erhart A, Drakeley C, D'Alessandro U, Stresman G
articles.details: Evaluated whether antibody responses to P. falciparum antigens could identify populations with low vs. high P(Detect) (proportion of infections captured by surveillance) among 5,300 seropositive individuals across 32 villages in The Gambia following mass drug administration. Random forest models using 19 antigens achieved PR-AUCs of 0.92–0.93 in <5% PCR prevalence stratum and 0.89–0.90 in <10% stratum with reduced 8-antigen panel. PfSEA, PfMSP1-19, gSG6, and Etramp4.Ag2 were consistently important predictors.

articles.headline: Rac1 promoter molecular link to hypertension and malaria
articles.title_original: The Rac1 Promoter as a Molecular Nexus for Antihypertensive Therapy Resistance and Enhanced Malaria Susceptibility.
articles.authors: Ameke SL, Fosu K, Amenga-Etego L, Sarpong KAN, Kwofie SK
articles.details: In silico analysis of human Rac1 promoter region (-2000 to +500 bp) using biomaRt, BSgenome, JASPAR2022, and GRCh38/hg38 reference genome. Found 2501 bp promoter is GC-rich (57.34%) containing 164 CpG sites (observed/expected ratio 0.75, canonical CpG island). Identified binding motifs for mineralocorticoid receptor, hypoxia-inducible factors, inflammatory mediators, and GATA-1. Proposes Rac1 promoter as molecular nexus potentially linking mineralocorticoid receptor-mediated resistance to ACEI/ARB with enhanced malaria susceptibility via erythrocyte remodeling.

articles.headline: Cost-efficiency framework for Zambia malaria surveillance data audits
articles.title_original: A framework for identifying cost efficiencies in malaria routine data quality audits: methodology and application in Zambia.
articles.authors: Nguyen A, Malama P, Lungu C, Mwanza M, Phiri D, Silumbe K, Chiwaula J, Hainsworth M, Das S, Roca-Feltrer A
articles.details: Developed systematic framework identifying cost efficiencies in routine data quality audits (RDQA) using health economic evaluation and micro-costing, applied to nine RDQA interventions by four projects in Zambia (2022–2024) covering 10 provinces, 91 districts, 1,189 health facility catchment areas. Estimated average economic costs of $24,938 per intervention ($205 per HFCA), with personnel (64%) and transportation (25%) as primary drivers. Optimized scenarios incorporating virtual orientation, streamlined teams, and harmonized allowances reduced costs by 41%; digital RDQA tools projected additional savings of 52%.

articles.headline: Baseline malaria burden and RTS,S vaccine acceptability Kenya
articles.title_original: The Malaria Vaccine Programme Evaluation in Kenya: results of a baseline household survey prior to the introduction of the RTS,S/AS01 vaccine.
articles.authors: Westercamp N, Nyangau I, Kwambai TK, Seda B, Akach D, Okanda I, Marube E, Siambe P, Radiro E, Wafula F, Ondiegi B, Shah MP, Seffren V, Moore KA, Fogelson A, Milligan P, Kariuki S, Samuels AM
articles.details: Baseline household survey conducted July–October 2019 in 46 MVIP sub-counties in western Kenya prior to RTS,S/AS01E vaccine introduction, enrolling 4,065 households with 4,948 children aged 5–48 months. Overall P. falciparum prevalence was 22% (95% CI 19–26), varying substantially (1–71%) by sub-county. Insecticide-treated net ownership 93%, use 87%. Thirty-eight percent of children had fever in prior two weeks; 70% sought care. Only 36% of caregivers had heard of malaria vaccine before introduction, yet willingness to vaccinate exceeded 98%.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic burden of malaria and artemether-lumefantrine benefit
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic Impact of Artemether-Lumefantrine in Malaria Treatment across Sub-Saharan Africa: A Model-Based Historical Analysis with 2022 as Reference Year.
articles.authors: Atitallah A, Peristeris P, Lovera D, Branner J, Shah R, Gandhi P, Gruenberger JB
articles.details: De novo Microsoft Excel-based model assessing socioeconomic burden of malaria and benefit of artemether-lumefantrine (AL, Coartem®) treatment in sub-Saharan Africa using 2022 as reference year. Model estimated 30.8 million malaria-related DALYs in individuals ≥15 years old, corresponding to socioeconomic burden of approximately $82.8 billion. Total socioeconomic benefit of AL compared with nontreatment scenario was $24.2 billion, reflecting avoided productivity losses from prevented deaths and reduced caregiving days.

articles.headline: NK cell activation transiently enhanced during acute malaria
articles.title_original: NK cell cytotoxicity is transiently enhanced during acute malaria and modulated by the host microenvironment.
articles.authors: Xi P, Sandoz PA, Lautenbach MJ, Bilev E, Önfelt B, Färnert A, Hammer Q, Sundling C
articles.details: Longitudinal study in malaria patients found transient activation of NK cell cytotoxicity during acute infection, characterized by rapid granzyme B-mediated killing and elevated cytotoxicity genes (PRF1, GZMB, GZMA). Plasma granzymes and proinflammatory cytokines enhanced NK cell function in vitro. However, plasma also contained inhibitory mediators including soluble cytokine receptors that dampened NK responses. The host microenvironment orchestrated tight regulation of NK cell activity that potentiated cytotoxicity during acute infection and downmodulated it after treatment.

articles.headline: High-affinity aptamers for PfLDH point-of-care malaria diagnostics
articles.title_original: Engineering High-Performance Aptamers via Optimized SELEX and Structure-Guided Dimer Assembly for Clinical Malaria Diagnostics.
articles.authors: Xiong D, Ren Y, Gu J, Wu B, Dong X, Salena BJ, Zhang Z, Li Y
articles.details: Developed aptamers targeting Plasmodium falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (PfLDH) via hybrid SELEX (bead-based followed by gel-based isolation) and structure-guided engineering into symmetric inverted dimeric aptamers. Dimeric aptamers achieved picomolar affinity (>800-fold higher than existing aptamers) with enhanced nuclease resistance from 3'-3' linkage. Clinical evaluation of 52 human blood samples demonstrated 81.8% sensitivity and 100% specificity with limit of detection of 3.3 ng/mL.

articles.headline: 2-bromopalmitate inhibits P. falciparum replication and gametocyte formation
articles.title_original: [Role of protein palmitoylation modification in the proliferation and gametogenesis of Plasmodium falciparum].
articles.authors: Zhang M, Zhang M, Yang T, Zhu G, Tang J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Measured palmitoyltransferase (PATs) activity during P. falciparum development using click chemistry. Treatment with 2-bromopalmitate (2-BP, 10 μmol/L) to inhibit PATs significantly reduced parasitemia (AUC 8.370±1.751 vs control 25.700±0.696, p<0.001), delayed asexual development, reduced merozoite numbers per schizont (14.020±4.320 vs 18.050±4.362, p<0.001), and severely blocked gametocyte formation and maturation (AUC 7.507±0.201 vs 18.900±0.384, p<0.001).

articles.headline: Malaria in Saudi Arabia predominantly affects young expatriates
articles.title_original: Clinical Characteristics and Outcomes of Malaria Patients in the Aseer Region, Saudi Arabia: A Retrospective Study (2022-2025).
articles.authors: Alshehri FI, Alkhosafi DA, Al Asmari EA, Saeed AB, Zarbah AM, Algarni SA, Ahmed MG, Mohamed MA, Mady FA, Albakri SMZ, Ghazy RM
articles.details: Retrospective study of 311 malaria patients (2022-2025) at a Saudi hospital: 90% male, mean age 28.8 years; 48.2% Ethiopian, 16.4% Saudi, 15.1% Yemeni nationals. Plasmodium vivax predominant (51.1%) versus P. falciparum (40.2%). Severe malaria (≥1 WHO criterion) in 43.7%, with severe anemia (26.0%) and jaundice (23.2%) most frequent. Independent predictors of severe malaria: higher parasitemia (aOR=1.70 per 1% increase, p<0.001) and non-Saudi nationality (aOR=2.40, p=0.027). 84% of cases occurred 2024-2025.

articles.headline: Low ITN ownership and use among Fulani pregnant women in Ghana
articles.title_original: Factors Associated With Insecticide-Treated Bed Net Possession and Utilization in Malaria Prevention Among Fulani Pregnant Women in the Savannah Hinterlands of Ghana: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Salifu Y, Lasong J, Wobi EB, Salifu T, Nantomah B, Dandeebo G
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 159 Fulani pregnant women (April-June 2022) in West Gonja Municipality. Only 54.7% owned insecticide-treated nets (ITNs), and among owners, 52.9% actually used them. Awareness that ITNs prevent malaria (AOR=3.45, 95% CI 1.33-8.94) and prior ITN counseling (AOR=4.52, 95% CI 1.53-13.34) were associated with ownership. Among net owners, utilization was lower with knowledge of malaria symptoms (AOR=0.18) and positively associated with owning multiple nets (AOR=0.07).

articles.headline: Plant capsules from A. indica and K. senegalensis show antiplasmodial activity
articles.title_original: Formulation and Evaluation of Capsules From Azadirachta indica and Khaya senegalensis for Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Adoley WN, Johnson R, Boakye-Gyasi ME, Owusu FWA, Obeng LM, Osei-Asibey A, Asamoa Mensa KA, Zoiku FK
articles.details: Formulated plant decoctions into capsules: Azadirachta indica (35 mg/dose kaolin) and Khaya senegalensis (50 mg/dose bentonite) with disintegration times of 10.01±0.34 and 4.48±0.23 minutes, respectively. Cumulative drug release was 78.13±0.37% and 95.63±1.00%. A. indica (IC50 2.74±0.70 μg/mL) and K. senegalensis (IC50 24.37±4.19 μg/mL) both demonstrated good to moderate antiplasmodial activity (IC50 ≤50 μg/mL) with selectivity for Plasmodium parasites over human cells.

articles.headline: Convulsions and delayed care predict child malaria mortality in Uganda
articles.title_original: Risk factors for malaria-related mortality among children under five at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda, 2020-2024: A case-control study.
articles.authors: Kwizera P, Migisha R, Mutesi C, Rukundo G, Kabwama SN, Kwesiga B, Bulage L, Ario AR
articles.details: Case-control study (100 fatal cases, 200 non-fatal controls) of severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria in children ≤59 months (2020-2024). 73% of fatal cases aged <24 months. Independent mortality predictors: convulsions (aOR=17, 95% CI 4.2-71), loss of consciousness (aOR=14, 95% CI 1.4-113), severe anemia (aOR=3.4, 95% CI 1.4-8.2), vomiting (aOR=3.1, 95% CI 1.4-6.9), and care-seeking delay >24 hours (aOR=8.8, 95% CI 2.3-34).

articles.headline: Maternal antimalarial effects may differ for infants
articles.title_original: Malaria in pregnancy: what's good for the goose may not be good for the goslings.
articles.authors: Rogerson SJ, Unger HW
articles.details: Title suggests that antimalarial interventions or malaria effects in pregnancy may have different outcomes for mothers versus their infants.

articles.headline: IPTp-DP similar efficacy to IPTp-SP for infant malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Impact of intermittent preventive treatment for malaria in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and their combination on infant outcomes: A randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Nankabirwa JI, Kakuru A, Nguyen AT, Roh ME, Aguti M, Adrama H, Kizza J, Olwoch P, Camanag K, Benjamin-Chung J, Rosenthal PJ, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Double-blind RCT (n=871 infants) comparing monthly intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) regimens: sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (IPTp-DP), or IPTp-DP+SP. Clinical malaria incidence in infants from IPTp-DP mothers (IRR=1.04, 95% CI 0.78-1.38) and IPTp-DP+SP mothers (IRR=0.93, 95% CI 0.68-1.26) was similar compared to IPTp-SP. Parasite prevalence overall 21.8% with similar rates between groups. Wasting at birth was transiently higher with DP-containing regimens but resolved by 3-6 months.

articles.headline: Malaria-driven mechanisms may increase cancer risk in Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria driven mechanisms shaping cancer risk and aggressiveness in African populations.
articles.authors: Udobi ME, Bella-Omunagbe M, Effiong PE, Afolabi IS, Chinedu SN
articles.details: Narrative review examining intersections between endemic malaria and cancer in sub-Saharan Africa. Identified five mechanistic axes: malaria-induced oxidative stress and chronic inflammation causing genomic instability; gut microbiome dysbiosis altering systemic immunity; exploitation of shared molecular targets (EPCR, oncofetal chondroitin sulfate) by Plasmodium parasites and cancer cells; cooperation between malaria and oncogenic viruses in lymphomagenesis; and malaria-associated vitamin D deficiency impairing immune surveillance. Antimalarial drugs (artemisinin, chloroquine, quinacrine) possess anticancer properties.

articles.headline: P. vivax GBS causation requires exclusion of other triggers
articles.title_original: Plasmodium Vivax Can Only Be Responsible for GBS if All Other Triggers Have Been Ruled Out.
articles.authors: Finsterer J
articles.details: Title suggests that Plasmodium vivax should only be considered responsible for Guillain-Barré Syndrome after other potential triggers have been ruled out.

articles.headline: Cultural and historical data on malaria in Hungary
articles.title_original: [Data for cultural history of malaria in Hungary].
articles.authors: Kiss L
articles.details: Article presents data on the historical and cultural aspects of malaria in Hungary.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccination strategies for pregnancy prevention discussed
articles.title_original: Immunization strategies to prevent malaria in pregnancy - a multistakeholder workshop.
articles.authors: D'Alessio F, Honkpehedji J, Sirima S, Gamain B, Morten N, Ndam N, Mordmüller B, Dicko A, Adechina R, Dossou A, Chinkhumba J, Kaur M, Houngnihin R, LaMontagne DS, Menendez C, Amani A, Mayani T, Hamel MJ, Carrió E, Sayeh A, Adegnika AA
articles.details: This workshop summary discusses development and implementation strategies for vaccines targeting pregnancy-associated malaria and placental malaria. VAR2CSA antigen candidates (PRIMVAC, PAMVAC) are in development, and existing malaria vaccines are being repurposed. An estimated 12.4 million pregnant women were affected in 2023. The workshop emphasized strengthened pharmacovigilance, community engagement strategies, clinical trial efficacy endpoints, and reduced-dose schedules to support vaccine implementation.

articles.headline: P. vivax sequencing assay distinguishes relapse from reinfection
articles.title_original: Understanding Plasmodium vivax recurrent infections using an amplicon deep sequencing assay, identity-by-descent and model-based classification.
articles.authors: Rosado J, Han J, Obadia T, Munro J, Traore Z, Schoffer K, Brewster J, Bourke C, Vinetz JM, Taylor AR, White M, Bahlo M, Gamboa D, Mueller I, Ruybal-Pesántez S
articles.details: Researchers developed PvAmpSeq, an amplicon sequencing assay targeting 11 SNP-rich genomic regions, to distinguish P. vivax relapse, reinfection, and recrudescence. In a Solomon Islands trial, approximately 50% of recurrent infections showed >50% identity-by-descent relatedness to baseline parasites and were classified as probable relapses/recrudescences. In a Peru cohort, 68% of recurrences showed <25% relatedness, suggesting reinfection. The assay provides high-resolution genotyping for characterizing treatment outcomes and transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Long non-coding RNAs regulate P. falciparum gametocyte development
articles.title_original: Dynamic regulation of long non-coding RNAs across asexual and gametocyte development in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Grünebast J, Singhal R, Olson S, Bromley R, Kanatani S, Ko K, Dunning Hotopp J, Sinnis P, Llinás M, Serre D
articles.details: Using Oxford Nanopore long-read RNA sequencing, ribosome profiling, and single-cell transcriptomics, researchers characterized P. falciparum long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) in asexual and sexual blood stages. Most lncRNAs exhibited pronounced stage-specific expression, particularly abundant in mature gametocytes, with differential expression between female and male gametocytes. Many lncRNAs are located antisense to protein-coding genes and co-expressed with sense mRNAs, suggesting roles in controlling gene expression during gametocytogenesis and transmission.

articles.headline: Mosquito density varies most by household, species by village
articles.title_original: Variance partitioning reveals contrasting random effect contributions to the density and species composition of malaria-transmitting mosquitoes in western Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Hui TJ, Epopa PS, Millogo AA, Yao FA, Koulmaga D, Noulin F, Diabate A, Burt A
articles.details: Over 60 months of intensive pyrethroid spray catch sampling (2012-2019) in four villages in Burkina Faso, between-house variation was the largest variance component for An. gambiae s.l. density (after accounting for seasonality and covariates), followed by between-year and between-village variation. Density increased with household inhabitants but was uncorrelated with bed net presence. For An. coluzzii and An. gambiae composition, between-village variance contributed 76% of total random variation. These variance components help parameterize field trials for vector control and monitoring.

articles.headline: P. falciparum predominates in Niger, P. vivax detected in hypo-endemic areas
articles.title_original: Prevalence and spatial distribution of malaria-causing Plasmodium infections in the Niger Republic.
articles.authors: Sanoussi MK, Issa I, Mahaman Moustapha L, Idrissa Y, Hachimou I, Wilfried H, Seini HI, Abdoulaye D, Abou Y, Mounkaila B, Ibrahim ML
articles.details: Cross-sectional study during high transmission (September–November 2022) in Niger tested 5,251 patients; PCR analysis of 304 samples found 55% were Plasmodium-positive. P. falciparum represented 90% (149/166 positive samples), with P. ovale 5% (9/166), P. malariae 3% (5/166), and P. vivax 2% (3/166). P. vivax was identified only in a hypo-endemic area. Findings support adaptation of diagnosis and management strategies across Niger's epidemiological zones.

articles.headline: Protein-protein interaction networks as antimalarial drug targets reviewed
articles.title_original: Targeting protein-protein interactions in Plasmodium: from asexual replication to sexual development.
articles.authors: Tang J, Tang W, Xie Y, Yang W, Luo X, Yu Y, He B, Liu C, Li Z
articles.details: This review synthesizes advances in Plasmodium protein-protein interactions (PPIs) as next-generation drug targets, emphasizing that core biological processes governed by conserved PPIs require cooperative mutations for resistance emergence, offering higher species selectivity than conventional enzyme-targeted drugs. The review integrates cryo-electron microscopy, proximity-dependent biotinylation, and genetic manipulation findings to delineate PPI networks in merozoite invasion, nutrient uptake, metabolic homeostasis, transcriptional regulation, and sexual development. Emerging strategies include PPI-targeted neutralizing antibodies, vaccine candidates, and small-molecule inhibitors.

articles.headline: Mosquito Shield transfluthrin emanator shows 43% protective efficacy in Benin
articles.title_original: Experimental hut evaluation of mosquito shield, a transfluthrin spatial emanator, for control of wild pyrethroid resistant malaria vectors in Benin.
articles.authors: N'dombidjé B, Syme T, Ahoga J, Pearce B, Yadouleton A, Yamadjako M, Ngufor C
articles.details: Experimental hut trial in Benin against pyrethroid-resistant An. gambiae s.l. evaluated Mosquito Shield™, a passive transfluthrin-based emanator, over two 32-day product life cycles. Against An. gambiae s.l., it provided 43.0% protective efficacy via human landing catches (95% CI: 24.0–57.0; p<0.001) and 64% personal protection via aspiration (95% CI: 21.9–81.8; p<0.001), with 49.0% mosquito mortality and 48.5% deterrence. Against Mansonia africana, 38.0% protective efficacy and >96% mortality were observed. Results support WHO prequalification.

articles.headline: Most DRC malaria patients experience RDT misdiagnosis over 7-year period
articles.title_original: Challenges in malaria diagnosis and treatment in Kinshasa Province, Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: François-Zafka R, Westreich D, Banek K, Kashamuka MM, White SJ, Bala JA, Nkalani M, Kihuma G, Atibu J, Mahilu GE, Thwai KL, Kokolomami J, Emch M, Edwards J, Juliano JJ, Tshefu A, Parr JB
articles.details: In a 7-year cohort study (2015–2022) of 2,269 participants in Kinshasa Province, DRC, one-year cumulative incidences of false-positive and false-negative RDT results were 23.1% and 17.8% respectively; by year seven, these rose to 77.7% and 83.4%. Misdiagnosis was associated with age, recent treatment, transmission intensity, and parasite density. About 58% of RDT-negative participants received antimalarial treatment at clinic visits, while 4% of RDT-positive participants did not, indicating significant diagnostic and treatment guideline gaps.

articles.headline: Dual-active ITNs reduce malaria cases 30–40% in Ghana region
articles.title_original: Reductions in malaria cases after deployment of dual-active ingredient insecticide-treated nets in Ghana-a Bayesian interrupted time series analysis.
articles.authors: Oppong SK, Owusu-Akrofi O, Atta-Obeng C, Coleman S, Amratia P, Symons TL, Alene KA, Peprah NY, Gething PW, Malm KL
articles.details: Bayesian interrupted time series analysis of monthly confirmed malaria cases (2018–2023) in Western North Region of Ghana showed Interceptor® G2 dual-active ingredient ITNs (distributed 2021) reduced cases by 30% immediately post-distribution (OR 0.696; 95% CrI 0.623–0.778), with sustained reductions of 26% at 6 months (OR 0.739; 95% CrI 0.653–0.837) and 40% at 30 months (OR 0.594; 95% CrI 0.492–0.718) after controlling for climate covariates. The intervention was strongly associated with reductions in 7 of 9 districts.

articles.headline: 81% of DRC healthcare workers accept Artemisia annua tea for malaria
articles.title_original: Public Health Perspectives on Integrating Artemisia annua Tea for Uncomplicated Malaria Treatment: A Cross-Sectional Study of Perceptions and Acceptability Among Healthcare Workers in Kalima District, Maniema, DRC.
articles.authors: Munyangi Wa Nkola J, Akilimali Zalagile P, Lukuke Mbutshu H, Kabala Munyemo S, Ramazani Bin Eradi I, Camara A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 337 healthcare professionals in Kalima district, DRC found 81.0% clinical acceptability of Artemisia annua tea integration for malaria treatment, with 82.8% perceiving it as effective. Rural residency was the strongest predictor of adherence (AOR 6.847; p=0.003). However, 49.0% cited lack of clinical evidence as a barrier and 91.4% demanded formal training on standardized dosage. Ongoing genomic surveillance is comparing PfKelch13 mutation prevalence in Artemisia tea users versus ACT-treated patients.

articles.headline: Research focus on Culex mosquitoes lower in malaria-endemic regions
articles.title_original: Forgotten in the tropics: research on Culex mosquitoes is overshadowed in malaria and dengue-endemic regions.
articles.authors: Abdi AA, Almeida R, Harris T, Magalhaes T, Juarez JG, Hamer GL
articles.details: Systematic literature review of 1,389 mosquito-related publications from 2010 and 2020 found publications including Culex spp. data were significantly lower in malaria-endemic countries (30.2%) and dengue-endemic countries (34.2%) compared to non-endemic countries (64.5%). Over the decade, Aedes spp. reporting increased while Anopheles reporting decreased, reflecting changing research emphasis including the 2015–2017 Zika emergence. Authors advocate for comprehensive mosquito community reporting in endemic-region vector studies.

articles.headline: 76% of Ethiopian households perceive malaria risk with geographic clustering
articles.title_original: Household malaria risk perception in Ethiopia: multilevel and spatial analysis using the 2022-2023 National Health Equity Survey.
articles.authors: Leul Y, Ararso D, Girmay AM, Zeru A, Taye G, Bahre W, Berhanu K, Lemlem A, Getachew T, Achamyeleh H, Shibiru Y, Alemu A, Tadele A, Wasie A, Molla G, Getachew F, Tadele W, Beshah SA, Tesfaye H, Zelalem D, Sori SA, Ayele S, Dagne T, Kabeta T, Belay YA, Terefe B, Fekadu L, Tiru G, Tollera G
articles.details: Analysis of 9,157 households from the 2022–2023 Ethiopian National Health Equity Survey found 76.4% reported perceiving malaria risk (95% CI: 74.9–77.9), with higher odds among rural households (AOR 1.54; 95% CI: 1.22–1.95) and those with higher education (AOR 1.89; 95% CI: 1.36–2.63), but lower odds among older household heads (AOR 0.49; 95% CI: 0.35–0.67) and those without phones or bank accounts (AOR 0.59). Approximately 38% of perception variability occurred between woredas with significant spatial autocorrelation (Global Moran's I = 0.42, p<0.001).

articles.headline: Regional malaria parasite diversity and G6PD variants in Sudan conflict zones
articles.title_original: Characterisation of malaria and glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency in conflict-affected zones of southern and eastern Sudan.
articles.authors: Jebreel WMA, Abdel Hamid MM, Mustafa SA, Awad F, Chamai M, Malpartida-Cardenas K, Boadu EA, Amoah LE, Rodriguez-Manzano J, Cunnington AJ, Mohamed AO
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 717 malaria-suspected patients in 2023-2024 Kassala and Kosti, Sudan. Malaria confirmed by microscopy and qPCR; G6PD genotypes determined by PCR-RFLP. Malaria prevalence was 62.6% (95% CI 58.2-67.0%) in Kassala and 52% (95% CI 46.6-59.0%) in Kosti; P. vivax dominated in Kassala (54%, 95% CI 48.2-59.7%) while P. falciparum predominated in Kosti (97.7%). Submicroscopic infections occurred in 8.7% overall. G6PD B variant predominated (94.2% in Kassala, 79.4% in Kosti); the African A− variant was newly identified in 3.5% of Kosti subjects.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury prevalence and risk factors in malaria patients
articles.title_original: Prevalence of acute kidney injury and its associated factors among malaria patients admitted to public hospitals in the Ilu Aba Bora and Bedelle Zones of Oromia region, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Befkadu Z, Teferi S, Endalu T, Mitiku A, Barkesa B, Darge T
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 307 adult malaria patients in Southwest Ethiopia (Feb-Apr 2024). AKI, defined as serum creatinine increase ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours, occurred in 28.3% (87/307). Risk factors included male sex (AOR 1.98, 95% CI 1.12-3.48), age ≥60 years (AOR 4.46, 95% CI 1.38-14.38), dehydration (AOR 1.74, 95% CI 1.01-3.02), P. falciparum infection (AOR 2.73, 95% CI 1.51-4.95), mixed malaria infection (AOR 3.30, 95% CI 1.44-7.55), and high parasitaemia (AOR 2.71, 95% CI 1.23-5.94).

articles.headline: Neonicotinoid IRS associated with increased malaria incidence in Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence rose following the introduction of neonicotinoid-based IRS in selected districts in northern Ghana: An observational analysis.
articles.authors: Coleman S, Atta-Obeng C, Nkolokosa C, Mohammed AG, Akrofi OO, Isaka I, Mohammed W, Peprah NY, Asiedu S, Dadzie SK, Dery DB, Tangena JA, Epstein A, Malm K
articles.details: Observational analysis of monthly district-level malaria incidence (2015-2022) in 22 Ghanaian districts transitioned from organophosphate to neonicotinoid indoor residual spraying in 2018-2019. Using interrupted time series modeling adjusted for rainfall, temperature, vegetation, and seasonality, observed incidence exceeded counterfactual estimates after the switch. Overall, malaria incidence was 26% higher under neonicotinoid IRS (IRR 1.26, 95% CI 1.07-2.08), with significant regional increases in Northern (IRR 1.76, 95% CI 1.04-2.74), North East (IRR 1.83, 95% CI 1.08-3.40), and Upper East regions (IRR 4.26, 95% CI 2.17-8.50).

articles.headline: Computational analysis of P. falciparum merozoite invasion mechanics
articles.title_original: In silico analysis of the invasion mechanics and invasiveness of the Plasmodium falciparum merozoite.
articles.authors: Msosa C, Abdalrahman T, Franz T
articles.details: Computational study using finite element models to investigate P. falciparum merozoite invasion mechanics and the role of erythrocyte morphology and membrane damage. Merozoite invasiveness decreased with increased erythrocyte sphericity (as seen in hereditary spherocytosis) and was greater when membrane damage occurred at early invasion stages. The minimum force required for merozoite invasion was predicted to be 11 piconewtons (pN). Nanoindentation simulations suggested potential for mechanically assessing erythrocyte membrane damage as a screening approach for novel invasion-blocking antimalarial drugs.

articles.headline: Free healthcare and differential diagnosis reduce malaria burden in Guinea
articles.title_original: Model-Based Assessment of the Effects of Selective Free Healthcare and Differentiated Diagnosis on Malaria Burden in the Republic of Guinea.
articles.authors: Diallo EM, Loum MA, Diallo OO, Gning LD, Ndiaye K, Ly AY, Bakhoum LN, Diallo A, Gerbaud L, Camara A
articles.details: Modeling study using EMOD software to simulate 2025-2030 effects of selective free healthcare and differentiated diagnosis on malaria burden across ecological archetypes in Guinea. Reducing financial and geographical barriers raised ACT access from 0.60 to 0.81, with more pronounced effects in low-incidence areas (incidence dropping below 50 cases per 1000 by 2028). The combined strategy was projected to prevent 13,428 complicated paediatric malaria cases (95% CI 7768-19,088). Results highlight the effectiveness of age-targeted interventions, particularly for children under five.

articles.headline: Cytokines and metabolism regulate γδ T cell responses in placental malaria
articles.title_original: Ex vivo cytokine responses and metabolic reprogramming are associated with γδ T cell differentiation in Plasmodium falciparum placental malaria.
articles.authors: Nana Mbianda CM, Kwanou Tchakounte BD, Bitye Zambo BM, Ntankheuh Tchinkou J, Atchombat Nyasse D, Fogang B, Argüello RJ, Ayong L, Megnekou R
articles.details: Case-control study of 50 women with and without placental malaria (21 PM+, 29 PM-) analyzing γδ T cell differentiation, cytokine responses, and metabolic reprogramming. Flow cytometry revealed compartment-specific memory γδ T cell phenotypes, with naive and central memory cells in cord blood and effector/terminal memory cells in peripheral and placental blood. IL-8 was associated with down-regulation of exhaustion markers TIM-3 and PD-1, while IL-8 and IL-33 promoted terminal memory differentiation (TEMRA) in peripheral blood. Metabolic profiling showed enhanced mitochondrial activity in terminally differentiated γδ T cells versus glycolytic activity in non-differentiated cells.

articles.headline: Chloroquine sensitivity re-emerging in P. falciparum in Gabon
articles.title_original: Reversal of Chloroquine Resistance in Plasmodium falciparum in Gabon: A Phenotype-Genotype Relationship over the Last 20 Years.
articles.authors: Inoue J, Rodi M, Bohissou FET, de Carvalho LP, Lohse K, Koehne E, Agnandji ST, Mombo-Ngoma G, Kremsner PG, Kreidenweiss A, Held J
articles.details: Study tracking ex vivo chloroquine susceptibility and drug-resistance genotypes in P. falciparum from Lambaréné, Gabon over 20 years (2004-2024). Median chloroquine IC50 decreased stepwise from 113.8 nM (2004) to 2.1 nM (2024), indicating restoration of susceptibility. The chloroquine-sensitive pfcrt haplotype CVMNK increased from 3.6% (2009) to 98.2% (2024), and wild-type pfmdr1 N86 from 23.1% to 100% (2024), demonstrating phenotype-genotype concordance.

articles.headline: Malaria control progress partial in Kinshasa, DRC (2020-2023)
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Performance Indicators for Malaria Control in Kinshasa from 2020 to 2023, the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Lutumbu BB, Mbanzulu KM, Kapour GK, Ndona MM, Zanga J, Mukendi JPK, Kayembe H, Mbangama A, Wumba R
articles.details: Cross-sectional evaluation of malaria control performance indicators from 23 health zones in Kinshasa, DRC (2020-2023). Malaria incidence was 30% with an upward trend peaking in 2022. LLIN distribution to pregnant women varied by year (45-88%), and 83.1% of suspected cases received RDT diagnosis. The study found that objectives of the 2020-2023 strategic plan were only partially achieved, with no health zone reaching 100% RDT diagnostic coverage and only four reaching at least 95%.

articles.headline: Novel 2-piperazino-pyrimidine targets P. falciparum plasmepsin X
articles.title_original: A novel 2-piperazino-pyrimidine compound exhibits asexual antimalarial activity by targeting Plasmodium falciparum plasmepsin X.
articles.authors: Hong J, Sakura T, Asada N, Fuchino K, Kuwana D, Kaki R, Ishii R, Kato T, Kita K, Matsumura K, Inaoka DK
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Identification of a novel 2-piperazino-pyrimidine compound (FPSA) with antimalarial activity against P. falciparum through targeting plasmepsin X (PMX) and blocking merozoite egress. Whole-genome sequencing of FPSA-resistant parasites identified substitutions (I252V, E55G) in the PMX-encoding gene and genomic duplications, with genome editing confirming causative mutations. FPSA inhibited recombinant PMX proteolytic activity and substrate cleavage in parasites, with transcriptomic profiles consistent with the validated PMX/PMIX dual inhibitor WM382.

articles.headline: Computational screening identifies NDH2 inhibitors with selectivity challenges
articles.title_original: Comparative Structural and Functional Analysis of Plasmodium Type II Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide: Quinone Oxidoreductase and its Human Homologue, Apoptosis-Inducing Factor, Mitochondrion-associated 1 (AIF-M1).
articles.authors: Baye BC, Moses V, Ugbaja SC, Lobb KA
articles.details: Comparative computational study of Type II NADH quinone oxidoreductase (NDH2) from five Plasmodium species against human AIF-M1 homologue. Virtual screening of the South African Natural Compounds Database identified compounds (SANC00101, SANC00344, SANC00418) with strong binding to P. falciparum NDH2 (-8.6 to -10.0 kcal/mol). However, molecular dynamics simulations revealed these compounds also stabilized human AIF-M1, identifying Phe482 as a critical off-target anchor. Species-specific analysis showed variable stability profiles, and effective pan-Plasmodium inhibition requires simultaneous optimization against human AIF-M1.

articles.headline: Gut microbiota regulates cerebral malaria protection and severity in mice
articles.title_original: Gut microbiota bidirectionally influences protection and severity in cerebral malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Taniguchi T, Miyauchi E, Kawabata-Iwakawa R, Nishiyama M, Umemiya-Shirafuji R, Toma H, Sasaki N, Hisaeda H, Tomita H, Ohno H, Kishimoto H, Suzuki H
articles.details: Experimental study in mice showing that antibiotic-induced microbiota modification prevented experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) in approximately 80% of mice, with reduced blood-brain barrier disruption and lymphocyte brain infiltration. 16S rRNA gene sequencing identified specific bacterial species that either protected against or exacerbated ECM. Mono-colonization experiments confirmed that distinct bacterial species could independently attenuate or exacerbate ECM symptoms, demonstrating bidirectional microbiota influence on cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: High malaria misdiagnosis rates detected by LAMP re-evaluation in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Unveiling the overlooked burden of malaria misdiagnosis using lamp-based re-evaluation of routine malaria diagnosis in health centers: implications for public health and clinical practice in Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Alula MT, Tegegne B, Setegn A, Yimer M, Tefera MW, Gedfie S, Ashagre A, Abere A, Tamir M, Mezgebu B, Abebe AD, Almaw A, Pillai DR, Lemma W, Eshetu T
articles.details: Health facility-based cross-sectional study of 384 malaria-suspected patients in Northwest Ethiopia (Jul-Aug 2024), comparing routine microscopy diagnosis with LAMP molecular assay. Thirty percent of microscopy results were discordant with LAMP, comprising false positives (7.6%), false negatives (18.8%), and species misidentification (3.6%), with statistical significance (p < 0.001). The findings reveal substantial diagnostic error in routine microscopy and underscore the need for strengthening microscopy accuracy through training and quality assurance.

articles.headline: Mixed Plasmodium infections increase mortality in severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium ovale and Plasmodium malariae Mixed Infections and Associated Mortality in Children with Severe Falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Lima-Cooper G, Datta D, Bond C, Mellencamp KA, Namazzi R, Gupta M, Conroy AL, Opoka RO, John CC
articles.details: In 440 hospitalized children with severe malaria, nPCR detected P. falciparum mono-infection in 329 (74.8%), mixed P. falciparum and P. ovale in 100 (22.8%), and triple infections (P. falciparum, P. ovale, P. malariae) in 9 (2.1%). Triple infections were associated with 33% mortality versus 7% in mono-infection (OR=7.0, 95% CI 1.8-28; p=0.005), and 67% experienced respiratory distress versus 35% in mono-infection (p=0.08).

articles.headline: Machine learning classifies malaria var genes with 92% accuracy
articles.title_original: upsML: A high-accuracy machine learning classifier for predicting Plasmodium falciparum var gene upstream groups.
articles.authors: Pangilinan EA, Quenu M, Claessens A, Otto TD
articles.details: upsML, a machine-learning classifier trained on 2,530 var genes, assigns PfEMP1-encoding var genes to upstream groups (upsA, upsB, upsC, upsE) with 83% accuracy for DBLα-tag sequences and 92% for full-length sequences, significantly outperforming existing tools. Application to global P. falciparum genomes revealed higher frequency of internal var genes in Asia.

articles.headline: Rapid genomic sequencing identifies drug resistance in cryptic malaria cases
articles.title_original: Rapid whole genome sequencing of Plasmodium DNA from cryptic malaria cases in UK travellers provides insights into infection origins, transmission, and antimalarial resistance.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, Billows N, Nolder D, Moss S, Phelan JE, Thorpe J, Edgeworth JL, Sutherland CJ, Chiodini PL, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 9 Plasmodium isolates using Illumina and Oxford Nanopore platforms, analyzed with Malaria-Profiler for drug-resistance prediction and AI-based geographic origin inference using a reference database of over 15,000 genomes, successfully profiled 4 cryptic P. falciparum cases and characterized transmission clusters in P. ovale and P. falciparum family-related cases.

articles.headline: Reply on ivermectin for malaria control trial
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to Control Malaria - A Cluster-Randomized Trial. Reply.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Rudd M
articles.details: No abstract available; this is a response article regarding a cluster-randomized trial of ivermectin for malaria control.

articles.headline: Ivermectin for malaria control: cluster-randomized trial
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to Control Malaria - A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Last A, Hayes R, Bradley J
articles.details: No abstract available; title indicates a cluster-randomized trial of ivermectin for malaria control.

articles.headline: Reply on permethrin-treated wraps for malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria in Children. Reply.
articles.authors: Boyce RM, Shook-Sa BE, Mulogo EM
articles.details: No abstract available; this is a response article regarding permethrin-treated baby wraps for malaria prevention in children.

articles.headline: Permethrin-treated wraps for malaria prevention in children
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria in Children.
articles.authors: Javelle E, de Santi VP, Briolant S
articles.details: No abstract available; title indicates a study of permethrin-treated baby wraps as a malaria prevention strategy in children.

articles.headline: CRISPR mutagenesis reveals CLAG3 helix 44 role in nutrient uptake
articles.title_original: A CRISPR-Based Mutagenesis Strategy for Examining CLAG3 Helix 44 Contribution to Malaria Parasite Nutrient Uptake Channels.
articles.authors: Gonzalez-Chavez Z, Siddiqui MA, Ganesan S, Desai SA
articles.details: Using CRISPR-based mutagenesis, researchers examined the α-helix 44 (α-H44) domain of CLAG3, a component of the plasmodial surface anion channel (PSAC). CLAG3 truncation lacking α-H44 phenocopied knockout, and restoration with recodonized α-H44 fully restored PSAC activity. Two engineered mutants exhibited distinct PSAC phenotypes and failed to expand in medium approximating in vivo nutrient availability, supporting α-H44's role in channel permeation.

articles.headline: Deep learning achieves 96.35% accuracy in malaria smear classification
articles.title_original: CT-Malaria Detection via Adaptive-Weighted Deep Learning Models.
articles.authors: Gasmi K, Krichen M, Alanazi A, Almenwer S, Almaghrabi S, Yahyaoui S
articles.details: A two-track deep learning architecture combining classical machine learning with convolutional neural networks for malaria smear analysis achieved 96.35% accuracy on held-out test data. The method uses real-time augmentation, feature extraction, and weighted ensemble selection to reduce false negatives for subtle parasites and false positives from staining artifacts.

articles.headline: P. vivax peptide library reveals distinct CD4+ T cell responses
articles.title_original: Different clinical forms of Plasmodium vivax infection result in unique T cell epitope-specific immune responses.
articles.authors: Costa CM, Almeida GG, Lindestam Arlehamn CS, Queiroz-Glauss CP, Gunderman VHG, Pereira MMM, Amaral LRD, Pereira DB, Tada MS, Martins-Filho OA, Gazzinelli RT, Antonelli LRDV
articles.details: A 310-peptide library (MPv310) was used to evaluate CD4+ T cell responses in P. vivax-infected individuals with distinct clinical phenotypes. Symptomatic before treatment (SY-I) and post-treatment (SY-T) patients showed increased CD154+ and CD69+CD154+ frequencies, while asymptomatic individuals exhibited higher frequencies of IFN-γ+TNF+ CD4+ T cells and IFN-γ+ effector memory cells, revealing distinct antigen-specific patterns.

articles.headline: Congenital and neonatal malaria underrecognized in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Congenital and Neonatal Malaria in Ethiopia: A Narrative Review.
articles.authors: Leake Y, Hagos HH, Reta BK
articles.details: A narrative review of 10 Ethiopian studies reported 46 cases of congenital and neonatal malaria (40 congenital, 6 neonatal). P. falciparum was the predominant species (35 cases), followed by P. vivax (8 cases) and mixed infections (3 cases). Most data came from case reports or case series, indicating these conditions are underrecognized despite Ethiopia's substantial malaria burden.

articles.headline: G6PD variants protect against Plasmodium knowlesi infection
articles.title_original: G6PD variants but not Filipino beta-thalassemia are associated with reduced risk of Plasmodium knowlesi infection in Sabah, Malaysia.
articles.authors: Nuin NA, Rajahram GS, Barber BE, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Chua TH
articles.details: In a case-control study of 106 P. knowlesi cases and 89 controls, G6PD deficiency variants were significantly less prevalent in cases (3.8%, 95% CI 1.0-9.4%) versus controls (12.4%, 95% CI 6.3-21.0%; OR=0.28, 95% CI 0.09-0.91; p=0.034), with Viangchan (871G>A) being the predominant variant. Filipino β-thalassemia showed no protective association, suggesting G6PD deficiency confers substantial protection against P. knowlesi susceptibility.

articles.headline: E3 ligase complex regulates P. falciparum gametocyte development
articles.title_original: GID/CTLH E3 ligase complex control cell fate programs for sexual development of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Marapana DS, Lopaticki S, Balan B, Reaksudsan K, Cobbold SA, Geoghegan ND, Khurana S, Scally SW, Hickey P, Jayakrishnan N, Vaibhav V, Spall S, Yousef JM, Dagley LF, Goodman CD, Cozijnsen A, McFadden GI, Rogers KL, Jex A, Komander D, McCarthy JS, Tonkin CJ, Dixon MWA, Cowman AF
articles.details: Mechanistic study demonstrated that the PfGID E3 ubiquitin ligase complex regulates gametocyte cell fate programs through targeted ubiquitination of key proteins. Deletion of PfGID complex components led to arrest in gametocyte development and loss of transmission to mosquitoes. PfGID fine-tunes protein levels of GD1 (ZFP36 family RNA-binding protein) and PfDPL (cryptochrome-like protein), which govern gametocyte maturation; PfDPL regulates male-specific proteins essential for gametogenesis, while GD1 maintains translational repression of critical developmental transcripts.

articles.headline: Entomological surveillance reveals high vector density in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of malaria in Tanzania: Baseline surveillance of vectors that drive persistent malaria transmission in regions with varying transmission intensities.
articles.authors: Derua YA, Batengana BM, Francis F, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Seth MD, Challe DP, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in five Tanzanian districts (Aug-Oct 2023) collected 19,898 mosquitoes indoors and outdoors using CDC light and Furvela tent traps, identifying vectors and screening for P. falciparum infection. An. funestus was the predominant vector (95.3% of all vectors; 96.3% An. funestus s.s.), with 1,799 vectors tested revealing 0.5% (9/1799) P. falciparum infection. Annual entomological inoculation rates for An. funestus ranged from 13.0 to 242.6 infectious bites per person per year across three districts, with Kyerwa showing the highest transmission intensity.

articles.headline: Integrated malaria services underutilized in Ethiopian antenatal care
articles.title_original: Integrated malaria service delivery and its determinants among pregnant women in Ethiopia: Multi level analysis of 2021/22 National Service Provision Assessment Survey.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Asemahagn MA, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Secondary analysis of Ethiopia Service Provision Assessment Survey 2021/22 examined 4,273 pregnant women across 662 health facilities to assess integrated malaria service delivery during antenatal care. Only 7.9% of pregnant women attended ANC visits where all integrated malaria service components were delivered concurrently. Independent factors associated with service delivery included malaria-trained providers (AOR 4.24, 95% CI: 1.80-10.00), insecticide-treated net receipt during ANC (AOR 2.81, 95% CI: 1.29-6.12), ≥3 ANC visits (AOR 1.58, 95% CI: 1.04-2.40), and rural facility attendance (AOR 2.73, 95% CI: 1.04-7.19).

articles.headline: Epigenetic and chromatin mechanisms regulate VAR2CSA expression
articles.title_original: Epigenetics and chromatin structure regulate var2csa expression and the placental-binding phenotype in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Lenz T, Sirel M, Hoppe H, Shafeeq S, Le Roch KG, Ribacke U
articles.details: Mechanistic study used phenotypic and systems biology assays to elucidate var2csa transcriptional regulation in phenotypically homogenous VAR2CSA-expressing P. falciparum parasites, which exclusively transcribed var2csa with all other var genes silenced. Transcriptional activation was strongly associated with depletion of repressive H3K9me3 marks and significant repositioning of var2csa relative to heterochromatic telomeric clusters. 5-methylcytosine occupancy was present in all var genes but was independent of transcriptional repression and switching.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Menkin-Smith L, Koval K, Samandari T
articles.details: No abstract available. Title indicates this is an article on P. vivax malaria.

articles.headline: Historical account of placental malaria in nineteenth-century Scotland
articles.title_original: Placental malaria in nineteenth-century Scotland.
articles.authors: Brabin BJ
articles.details: Historical paper describes Scottish obstetrician James Young Simpson's 1835 discovery of malaria pigmentation in a placental sample from 4 months gestation, representing the first documented case of placental malaria pathology. Simpson presented this observation in his inaugural lecture as President of the Royal Edinburgh Medical Society, pioneering understanding of placental inflammation and infection in the context of malaria in pregnancy during the early 19th century.

articles.headline: Chloroquine resistance patterns drive drug susceptibility variation
articles.title_original: Chloroquine resistance transporter drives divergent multi-locus drug resistance genetic backgrounds and susceptibility in Gambian Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Drammeh NF, Bojang F, Nganyewo NN, Jawara AS, Manneh B, Correa S, Amambua-Ngwa BN, Mbye H, Oriero EC, Sutherland CJ, D'Alessandro U, Amambua-Ngwa A
articles.details: Amplicon sequencing of 364 P. falciparum isolates from The Gambia assessed drug-resistance genotypes at pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, pfdhps, and pfkelch13 loci; in vitro IC50s determined for 68 isolates to chloroquine, dihydroartemisinin, lumefantrine, mefloquine, piperaquine, and amodiaquine. Two main multi-locus drug-resistance backgrounds were identified: CVIET haplotype (62% prevalence) showed chloroquine resistance with highest IC50s when combined with pfmdr1 NFD (31.7% of infections); CVMNK was less sensitive to mefloquine and dihydroartemisinin. No validated pfkelch13 resistance mutations detected.

articles.headline: Novel acridone T111 active across all parasite life stages
articles.title_original: Potent acridone antimalarial against all three life stages of Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Kancharla P, Dodean RA, Li Y, Zhang X, Kelly S, Charlton J, Binauhan AJ, Navarrete K, Garcia L, Mu J, Lu J, Caridha D, Madejczyk MS, Jin X, Dennis WE, Kudyba K, McEnearney S, Lee PJ, Blount C, DeLuca J, Vuong C, Pannone K, Dinh HT, Mdaki K, Leed S, Martin ML, Pybus BS, Chin GC, Chim-Ong A, Cui L, Qahash T, Shoue DA, Ferdig MT, Mockenhaupt FP, van Loon W, Ndoli JM, Kudyba H, Vega-Rodriguez J, Okitwi M, Kreutzfeld O, Docan C, Green C, Liu Y, Agulay A, Mirsalis J, Farrell SN, Probst AS, Nilsen A, Alday PH, Stone Doggett J, McFadden GI, Goodman CD, Catteruccia F, Rosenthal PJ, Riscoe MK, Cooper RA, Roth A, Kelly JX
articles.details: Development of acridone antimalarial T111 demonstrated potent in vitro activity against cultured parasites and ex vivo activity against clinical isolates, with oral single-dose cure in asexual blood-stage rodent models. T111 inhibited sexual blood-stage parasites, showed activity against relapsing parasites in non-human primate liver cells, and prevented parasite development in mosquitoes, with synergy observed when combined with tafenoquine. The compound acts via mitochondrial electron transport chain inhibition with a favorable safety profile including no hemolytic toxicity and low genotoxicity/cardiotoxicity potential.

articles.headline: Point-of-care biosensors for malaria diagnosis: recent advances
articles.title_original: Potential biomarkers and their recent applications ofr point-of-care biosensors for the diagnosis of malaria.
articles.authors: Karadurmus L, Cetinkaya A, Kocabay P, Jaballah MB, Baldrich E, Ozkan SA
articles.details: Review of 2022-2025 advances in biosensor engineering, nanomaterials, and microfluidic automation for malaria point-of-care diagnostics, providing comparative and mechanistic evaluation of electrochemical, optical, and piezoelectric biosensor approaches. The review emphasizes signal transduction mechanisms, analytical trade-offs, microfluidic integration strategies, and real-world deployability considerations. Key challenges identified include asymptomatic carriers, antimalarial and insecticide resistance, and the need for affordable, practical diagnostics deployable in resource-limited settings without requiring skilled professionals.

articles.headline: Multiplexed isothermal assay for falciparum and T. vaginalis detection
articles.title_original: Multiplexed Isothermal Amplification Assay for the Detection of Plasmodium falciparum and Trichomonas vaginalis in Urine.
articles.authors: Dupaty J, Shiluli C, Schaus B, Lolabattu SR, Gitaka J, Klapperich C
articles.details: Development of multiplexed isothermal nucleic acid amplification assay (iso-IMRS) for simultaneous detection of P. falciparum and T. vaginalis in urine as a noninvasive diagnostic approach for malaria in pregnancy, where placental sequestration complicates conventional microscopy or rapid diagnostic test detection. The assay detected both pathogens at 100 copies/μL sensitivity in processed human urine. While neat urine samples require a processing step that may limit point-of-care use, the authors note their Kenya clinics process samples locally with turnaround within a typical prenatal visit.

articles.headline: Integrated serosurveillance reveals malaria and NTD exposure patterns
articles.title_original: Hotspot-based integrated serosurveillance of neglected tropical diseases, malaria, and vaccine-preventable diseases in urban Guyana.
articles.authors: Niles-Robin RA, Sampson A, Valz O, Scott O, Dickson KE, Escalada R, Nicholson D, Woolford J, Ade MP, Carvalho Scholte R, Luciañez A, Bravo P, Rey-Benito G, Ibarra-Ozcariz S, Morice A, Saboyá-Díaz MI
articles.details: Hotspot-based integrated serosurveillance in urban Guyana assessed exposure to lymphatic filariasis, other neglected tropical diseases, and malaria using multiplex bead assay analysis of dried blood spots from 300 individuals aged ≥6 years per hotspot across four coastal regions. Serological testing included malaria antigens pvMSP1-19 and pfMSP1-19. Seropositivity to P. falciparum and P. vivax was higher among adults, while reactivity to filaria antigens (Wb123, Bm14) was consistent with previous reports of elevated transmission in these regions.

articles.headline: Protocol for core outcome set in severe malaria treatment trials
articles.title_original: Synthesis and Standardization of Outcomes in Severe Malaria Treatment Trials: Protocol for the Development of a Core Outcome Set (the COSSMaT Study).
articles.authors: Asamoah GD, Love SB, Gibb DM, Maitland K, George EC
articles.details: Protocol describes development of the first Core Outcome Set (COS) for severe malaria treatment trials following COMET Initiative guidance. Methodology includes updated systematic review of outcomes in severe malaria treatment randomized controlled trials, qualitative interviews with parents/caregivers of severe malaria patients (26 participants interviewed to date), and a 2-round Delphi consensus process targeting 60-80 multistakeholder participants. Preliminary systematic review identified 326 records with 5 eligible RCTs from 2020-2024 in Africa and Asia; final consensus meeting and outcome measurement instrument selection anticipated by September 2026.

articles.headline: Minor Plasmodium species rebound after malaria interventions pause
articles.title_original: The dynamics of asymptomatic Plasmodium spp. infections following 10 years of malaria control interventions in Northern Sahelian Ghana.
articles.authors: Rios-Teran CA, Tiedje KE, Bangre O, Deed SL, Argyropoulos DC, Koram KA, Oduro AR, Ansah PO, Day KP
articles.details: Study using interrupted time-series design with ~2,000 participants per survey tracked P. malariae and P. ovale from 2012-2022 in Ghana. Following indoor residual spraying (IRS) in 2015, P. malariae prevalence dropped from 13.7% to 1.4% and P. ovale from 5.7% to 0.4%. After IRS ended and seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) began (2017), P. malariae rebounded to 2.9% (~2-fold increase) and P. ovale to 4.0% (~10-fold increase). By 2020-2022, P. malariae reached 7.4-5.8% while P. ovale declined to 2.6-1.3%, suggesting sustained intervention deployment is necessary.

articles.headline: PfRESA3 hijacks human chaperone for parasite cytoskeletal remodeling
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum ring-infected erythrocyte surface antigen 3 (PfRESA3) is a cytoskeleton-interacting protein and potential co-chaperone.
articles.authors: Mallick NR, Sahu W, Ojha DK, Mund S, Das A, Pati AK, Elangovan S, Reddy KS
articles.details: Using recombinant protein and antibody approaches, researchers identified PfRESA3 as a P. falciparum protein exported early to the erythrocyte inner membrane where it associates with the cytoskeleton through PRESAN and DnaJ-X interactions. The protein stimulated ATPase activity of human HsHSPA8 chaperone and enhanced protein refolding, suggesting PfRESA3 functions as a co-chaperone that recruits host cell machinery to support parasite development and cytoskeletal remodeling in infected erythrocytes.

articles.headline: Review of human immune responses to blood-stage Plasmodium infection
articles.title_original: Cellular and molecular interactions underlying the peripheral immune responses to blood-stage malaria infections.
articles.authors: Tebben K, Asawa R, Serre D, Lyke KE
articles.details: Comprehensive review examining cellular and molecular interactions in human immune responses to blood-stage malaria parasites and Plasmodium immune evasion mechanisms. Covers the complex interactions between parasite life cycle, human immune defenses, and coevolved evasion strategies that allow parasites to persist in human hosts.

articles.headline: Military-civilian malaria coordination in Thailand requires formalization
articles.title_original: Combatting malaria together: key findings from a rapid formative assessment on Royal Thai Army malaria practices and military-civilian collaboration in Sisaket Province, Thailand.
articles.authors: Dantzer E, Tabprasit S, Suwannarong K, Jongsakul K, Kana K, Dharmarak P, Feldman M, Sudathip P, Chaiwan J, Yokani W, Kramyoo M, Thammasutti K, Cotter C
articles.details: Mixed-methods assessment combined 5-year retrospective analysis (2016-2020) with qualitative data (Dec 2021-Jan 2022, n=116 respondents) in Sisaket Province, Thailand. Military cases represented 18% of provincial malaria cases (432/2425 total), all male with median age 28 years; 41% were indigenous and 40% imported infections. Despite 96% diagnosed at health ministry facilities, military-civilian coordination needed strengthening in patient follow-up, border control, vector control, and military medic capacitation.

articles.headline: White rice substrate optimizes Beauveria bassiana fungus production
articles.title_original: Effect of solid substrates on the production and germination of Beauveria bassiana (Balsamo) Vuillemin, and their infectivity against the malaria vector, Anopheles stephensi (Diptera: Culicidae).
articles.authors: Siddaramegowda R, Chalageri VH, Eapen A
articles.details: Researchers tested 11 solid substrates for culturing entomopathogenic fungus PDBC-Bb5a against Anopheles stephensi. White rice achieved highest conidial production (6.8 × 10^5 CFU/g) and germination rates (37% at 8 hours, 54.3% at 16 hours). Conidia from white rice caused 90% and 86.25% mortality of mosquitoes on cement and mud panels respectively with 5-7 day median survival time, substantially exceeding other substrates like barley (51.25-47.50% mortality).

articles.headline: IPTp-SP coverage remains suboptimal in pregnancy despite availability
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria during pregnancy and coverage of intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine in urban Amboasary-Sud, Southern Madagascar.
articles.authors: Zafindraibe NJ, Razafindrakoto AC, Zemey CB, Rakotozandrindrainy R, Rasamindrakotroka A, Razanakolona LRS
articles.details: Retrospective study of 1262 pregnant women from antenatal care in Amboasary-Sud, Madagascar found 64.9% received at least one IPTp-SP dose but only 49.9% completed the recommended ≥3 doses. Malaria RDT positivity was 21.1% among 289 symptomatic women tested, representing 4.8% confirmed symptomatic malaria overall.
