Adequate IPTp-SP coverage (≥3 doses) was independently associated with living <5 km from a health facility (aOR=1.54, 95% CI 1.14-2.08) and attending ≥4 ANC visits (aOR=2.38, 95% CI 1.76-3.21).

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus expands larval habitat use during rainy season
articles.title_original: Seasonal variation in aquatic habitat availability and use by the malaria vector, Anopheles funestus.
articles.authors: Kahamba NF, Kifungo K, Jumanne M, Trujillano F, Finda MF, Msugupakulya B, Baldini F, Nelli L, Okumu FO, Ferguson HM
articles.details: Cross-sectional surveys of aquatic habitats in south-eastern Tanzania during dry season (Sep-Nov 2021) and rainy season (Feb-May 2022) identified 2824 total habitats, 27% positive for An. funestus larvae. Larval occurrence and density varied significantly by habitat type, village, season, and physicochemical factors including water depth, vegetation, algae, and clarity. An. funestus consistently occupied river streams, ground pools, and ditches across both seasons but expanded into spring-fed wells, rice fields, and dug pits during wet season.

articles.headline: Cabamiquine shows promise against non-falciparum Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: Antimalarial cabamiquine inhibits blood-stage of different field isolates of Plasmodium species.
articles.authors: Dembele L, Aniweh Y, Soulama A, Sogore F, Diakité O, Diallo F, Dao F, Amenga-Etego LN, Demarta-Gatsi C, Spangenberg T, Djimde AA
articles.details: Ex vivo drug susceptibility testing from field isolates in Ghana and Mali compared cabamiquine activity against P. ovale, P. malariae, and P. falciparum. P. ovale showed similar cabamiquine susceptibility to P. falciparum (median IC50 = 0.9441 nM, no significant difference), while P. malariae was less susceptible (median IC50 = 3.915 nM, p=0.001). Both non-falciparum species were overall susceptible to cabamiquine; cabamiquine-resistant parasites showed limited cross-resistance to other tested antimalarials.

articles.headline: Malaria incidence, risk factors, and hematological-immunological assessment
articles.title_original: Incidence and risk factors of malaria with hematological and immunological evaluation and integrable analysis of a fractional mathematical model.
articles.authors: Murad MA, Murad SA, Abdeljawad T, Alqudah MA
articles.details: Study examining incidence and risk factors of malaria with evaluation of hematological and immunological parameters, incorporating a fractional mathematical model for integrated analysis.

articles.headline: Sex distorter male drive enables resistance-resilient vector suppression
articles.title_original: Sex distorter male drive for resistance-resilient population control of the human malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Grilli S, Vertsimakha O, Marston L, Gonzalez IA, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: Genetic engineering study in Anopheles gambiae characterized two germline-specific promoters, spo11 and vasa1, with distinct temporal and spatial expression patterns. Researchers developed Sex Distorter Male Drive (SDMD) technology that generates highly male-biased offspring spreading through super-Mendelian inheritance. This approach improves genetic stability and resistance resilience compared to previous designs by reducing target-site resistance development risk.

articles.headline: Severe malarial anemia and bleeding predict patient referral
articles.title_original: "Referral patterns and associated factors among adult patients admitted with Severe Malaria at Shedeho Meket Primary Hospital, Northeast Ethiopia".
articles.authors: Addis GT, Addis MT, Mihret Y, Addis ET, Tadesse TY
articles.details: Retrospective cross-sectional study at a primary hospital in Northeast Ethiopia reviewed 165 patients admitted with severe malaria from April 2023 to April 2025 (94 males, 71 females, mean age 34.44 years). Overall 91.5% recovered (151/165); 13.94% required referral (23/165) with zero deaths. Significant associations with referral included hyperparasitemia (aOR=2.083, 95% CI 0.019-7.803), severe malarial anemia (aOR=3.052, 95% CI 2.066-13.232), and significant bleeding (aOR=8.012, 95% CI 2.066-21.232).

articles.headline: Dissecting malaria protective immunity from natural infection vaccination
articles.title_original: Editorial: Dissecting malaria protective immunity: acquired by natural infection and/or vaccination.
articles.authors: Kanoi BN, Nguitragool W, Palacpac NMQ, Tsuboi T
articles.details: Editorial commentary addressing mechanisms of malaria protective immunity acquired through natural Plasmodium infection and vaccination approaches.

articles.headline: Plasmodium sporozoites retained in spleen during liver transit
articles.title_original: Retention and survival of Plasmodium sporozoites in the spleen.
articles.authors: Celli S, Thiberge JM, Formaglio P, Martins RM, Snounou G, Amino R
articles.details: Mouse models infected intravenously with P. yoelii and P. berghei showed GFP-expressing sporozoites present in splenic parenchyma for at least 3 hours post-inoculation, suggesting the spleen plays a functional role beyond trapping and destroying sporozoites during their journey from skin to liver.

articles.headline: P. yoelii impairs lung dendritic cell autophagy, worsening inflammatory injury
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection induces lung injury by modulating type 2 conventional dendritic cells autophagy via the STAT3-IRF4 signaling.
articles.authors: Hong C, Deng G, Jiang Z, Lu Y, Wu Q, Huang C, Tang J, Zhou H, Liu Q, Luo X, Zhao Y, Qi Y, Li Q, Ma X, Yang Q
articles.details: P. yoelii-infected mice showed increased lung cDC2 accumulation with decreased phagocytosis through JAK/STAT3/IRF4 signaling-mediated autophagy suppression, driving Th1-biased CD4+ T cell differentiation and exacerbating lung injury. STAT3 inhibition or IRF4 knockout restored autophagy, phagocytic function, and reduced inflammatory responses.

articles.headline: Malaria exposure expands cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cells in lymphoid organoids
articles.title_original: Cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cell subsets expand in response to malaria in human tonsil and spleen organoids.
articles.authors: Press KD, Bach F, Sola E, Camanag K, Dooley NL, Ivanawati A, Oyong D, Nalubega M, Kakuru A, Matsiko S, Nankya F, Musinguzi K, Nalwoga A, Nansubuga E, Ategeka J, Ebusu C, Odongo B, Kashiwagi C, Ji X, Miranda M, Tachiwa-Appiah J, Sandhu K, Kamalyan L, van der Ploeg K, Boyle M, Wagar LE, Davis MM, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Human tonsil and spleen organoids exposed to P. falciparum-infected RBCs showed significantly increased Vδ2+ γδ T cell frequencies with clonal expansion, activated cytotoxic phenotypes including upregulated granzymes and interferon genes. Validation in Ugandan children with asymptomatic parasitemia confirmed expanded Vδ2+ T cells with enhanced cytotoxic potential.

articles.headline: PRMT inhibitors show multistage activity against P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: Protein Arginine Methyltransferase Inhibitors Target Multiple Stages of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites In Vitro.
articles.authors: Opperman D, Rabie T, Maré M, Naude M, van der Watt M, Thibaud JL, Shannon M, Sanders N, Bolscher JM, van der Laak R, Willemsen R, Bronkhorst A, Boonyalai N, Lee MCS, Birkholtz LM
articles.details: Chemical repositioning identified TC-E 5003 as a potent protein arginine methyltransferase (PRMT) inhibitor active across multiple P. falciparum developmental stages. Structure-activity analysis of TC-E 5003 analogs revealed distinct pharmacophore features and proposed PfPRMT1 as the primary target.

articles.headline: P. malariae and P. ovale frequently misidentified as P. vivax in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: The hidden malaria: Misidentification of Plasmodium malariae and Plasmodium ovale in low-transmission setting targeted for elimination in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getachew H, Wang C, Habtamu K, Demissew A, Degefa T, Birhanie SK, Kazura JW, King CL, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (September 2019–July 2022) screened 3,590 febrile patients; 323 tested positive by microscopy/RDT; qPCR of 245 samples revealed: 215 (87.7%) P. vivax, 9 (3.7%) P. falciparum, 8 (3.3%) P. malariae, 6 (2.4%) P. ovale, and 7 (2.8%) mixed infections. RDTs showed 8.6% false-negative rate.

articles.headline: AL and ASAQ remain efficacious against P. falciparum in Sierra Leone children
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children 6-59 months in three sentinel sites of Sierra Leone, 2021-2022.
articles.authors: Cavros I, Abanyie F, Kamara ARY, Chalobah F, Fofana N, Ahmed J, Larbi K, Kennedy C, Horton B, Bajic M, Richardson M, Matlock B, McCaffery J, Kanu S, Schnabel D, Labor TSA, Cash S, Plucinski M, Komeh JP, Sillah-Kanu M
articles.details: 28-day in vivo efficacy study in children 6–59 months at three sites. Kaplan-Meier uncorrected efficacy: artemether-lumefantrine 89.5% (Bo), 96.1% (Bombali), 93.2% (Kenema); artesunate-amodiaquine 92.0% (Bo), 94.5% (Bombali). PCR-corrected efficacy 100% for all AL arms and ASAQ in Bo; 99.1% for ASAQ in Bombali. No Pfk13 mutations detected.

articles.headline: Malaria microscopy shows variable accuracy across rural Kenyan health facilities
articles.title_original: Malaria microscopy evaluation and quality assurance in rural clinics of Rarieda and Alego Usonga sub-counties of Siaya County, western Kenya.
articles.authors: Schultz JS, Towett O, Obiet K, Seda B, Otieno K, Omondi D, Odongo W, Seffren V, Wafula P, Oreri E, McDermott DP, Staedke SG, Kwambai TK, Kariuki S, Gutman JR
articles.details: Quality assessment in 29 health facilities (January–July 2024) evaluated 1,494 blood smears. Concordance between routine and expert microscopy was 91% (1,289/1,414; range 55–100% by facility). Sensitivity 76%, specificity 92%. Parasitemia 1–100 p/μL had lower diagnostic odds (OR=0.12, 95% CI 0.05–0.29, p<0.001); >10,000 p/μL had higher odds (OR=6.08, 95% CI 2.22–25.1, p=0.002).

articles.headline: Socioeconomic factors drive late malaria treatment in under-five children
articles.title_original: Determinants of late malaria treatment among under-five children in West Africa.
articles.authors: Shittu SB, Shittu RO, Adedini SA
articles.details: Secondary analysis of Malaria Indicator Survey data from Mali, Niger, Nigeria, and Senegal identified treatment-delay determinants by country. Senegal: child sex (AOR=0.41), maternal wealth (AOR=0.037), residence (AOR=1.82). Nigeria: maternal wealth (AOR=0.54), maternal education (AOR=0.49). Mali: maternal age (AOR=0.6). Prevalence of treatment delay highest in Mali.

articles.headline: PfVPS4 protein essential for parasite replication and sexual conversion
articles.title_original: PfVPS4, an ESCRT AAA-ATPase, is essential for asexual proliferation and gametocyte sexual conversion in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Yang W, Wu J, Zhou Y, Yu Z, Shang X, Huang J
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9 conditional knockdown in P. falciparum (Pf3D7 and PfNF54 strains) showed PfVPS4 knockdown reduced asexual progeny 84%, decreased merozoite numbers, and reduced gametocyte conversion 46%. ATPase activity optimal at pH 7.5 and 37°C with Vmax 2.23±0.053 U/mg and Km 0.086 mM. PfVta1 cofactor significantly enhanced activity.

articles.headline: Public health media campaigns improved WWII soldier malaria compliance
articles.title_original: Attention for Compliance: Visual Media in Second World War Anti-Malaria Efforts.
articles.authors: Marble WS, Shanks GD
articles.details: Historical account of extensive media campaign conducted during WWII to improve soldier compliance with antimalarial measures. Multiple communication approaches included humorous education, asserting command authority, linking malaria control to military success, and promising accelerated return home. Contributions from later-famous artists including Dr. Seuss.

articles.headline: Border population mobility and system constraints hinder malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Challenges and responses of malaria elimination in a high-endemic area along the Thai-Myanmar border: A health systems perspective.
articles.authors: Saita S, Parker DM, Suk-Uam K, Phuanukoonnon S, Pooseesod K
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in Tha Song Yang District applied WHO Six Building Blocks framework. Household survey (n=388) showed 75.77% malaria knowledge and 94.59% positive prevention attitudes. Adherence to 1-3-7 strategy: Day 1 and Day 3 reached 96% and 100%; Day 7 declined to 69% by 2022. Key barriers: short-term migrants, workforce shortages, reduced funding, logistical barriers.

articles.headline: Mini chamber test enables high-throughput chlorfenapyr net efficacy assessment
articles.title_original: The mini chamber test: a novel bioassay for bioefficacy testing of chlorfenapyr treated nets used in malaria control.
articles.authors: Lewis A, Jones-Brown R, Harvey G, Praulins G, Reid E, Lugenge AG, Mechan F, Gleave K, Lees RS
articles.details: Novel high-throughput bioassay optimized for chlorfenapyr-pyrethroid dual-active-ingredient nets using pyrethroid-susceptible and -resistant mosquito strains. Overnight exposure yielded 95% 72-hour mortality in mini chamber versus 70% in tunnel test. Mosquito number (10–30) and chamber orientation had no significant effect (p=0.46 and p=0.35). Six-hour exposure showed correlation between chemical content and mortality in durability-tested nets.

articles.headline: Widespread pvmdr1 amplification in Plasmodium vivax from Brazil
articles.title_original: Hidden Infections, Emerging Resistance: Underdiagnosed Plasmodium vivax and multidrug resistance-1 (pvmdr1) gene Amplification in the Guiana Shield.
articles.authors: Puça MCSB, Pereira Mascarenhas ME, Huguet QB, Salazar YEAR, Nogueira IR, Louzada J, de Pina-Costa A, de Siqueira AM, de Silva MO, Pereira DB, Ladeia-Andrade S, Ferreira MU, de Oliveira Ferreira J, Gil JP, de Sousa TN
articles.details: Study of 727 malaria cases from Roraima and other Brazilian states revealed misclassification of P. vivax/P. falciparum mixed infections due to microscopy limitations. Molecular diagnosis showed pvmdr1 copy number variation with geographic heterogeneity: 60% of Roraima isolates carried amplification, rising to over 70% after 2020, while western Amazon isolates had single copies. Findings emphasize the role of underdiagnosis in selecting drug-resistant parasite strains.

articles.headline: P. falciparum prevalence and seroprevalence across Nigeria, 2018
articles.title_original: Risk factors and geospatial associations with Plasmodium falciparum malaria infection and IgG seroprevalence: Nigeria, 2018.
articles.authors: Jaramillo-Underwood A, Ogunniyi A, Zhou H, Uhomoibhi P, Mba N, Ndodo N, Dawurung AB, Abubakar AG, Okoye MI, Iriemenam NC, Maire M, Steinhardt L, Rogier E, Ihekweazu C
articles.details: Nationwide 2018 study with blood samples tested for Pf antigenemia and IgG seroprevalence against multiple antigens. Current/recent Pf antigenemia prevalence was 38.3% (95% CI: 37.2–39.5%), with seroprevalence ranging 48–87% across five-antigen panel. Age 5–14 years showed highest odds of active infection; wealthiest category showed 5-fold reduction vs poorest; urban residence was protective (30% reduction in infection, 60% in exposure). Geospatial analysis revealed heterogeneous but hyperendemic malaria epidemiology.

articles.headline: Imported malaria at German university hospital, 2012–2023
articles.title_original: Malaria: 11-Year Experience With Imported Cases at a German University Hospital and Epidemiological Trends Amid the COVID-19 Pandemic.
articles.authors: Mester P, Kunst C, Schmid S, Zimmermann K, Bülow S, Rennert J, Lampl BMJ, Schilling T, Müller M, Pavel V
articles.details: Retrospective study of 58 malaria patients treated 2012–2023 at University Hospital Regensburg, Germany. Median age 37.5 years; 96% caused by P. falciparum; 31.6% required ICU care with 98% survival rate. Only 17% had taken prophylaxis; thrombocytopenia was the primary laboratory abnormality. COVID-19 pandemic led to decrease in imported cases across Europe.

articles.headline: Climate extremes, migration, and malaria risk in South Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria at the margins: climatic extremes, migration, and urban informal settlement risk in South Africa.
articles.authors: Matamanda SH, Musakwa W, Dzomba A
articles.details: Analysis of 2000–2024 climate data (temperature, rainfall, humidity) from three South African endemic provinces linked with laboratory-confirmed malaria cases and in-migration data. Same-year humidity positively correlated with malaria incidence in KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga; lag-1 rainfall associated with incidence in Limpopo reflecting hydrological carry-over. Shock events (2022 KwaZulu-Natal floods, 2017 Limpopo cyclone) showed compound climatic anomalies. Post-flood migration aligned with malaria tail years, indicating climate-migration-transmission co-movement.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasite biology and host specificity in birds
articles.title_original: New experimental insights into the biology of avian malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Valkiūnas G, Iezhova TA, Chagas CRF, Tchoumbou M, Himmel T, Petrone ME, Duc M
articles.details: Experimental transfer of three avian Plasmodium species (P. circumflexum, P. vaughani, P. relictum) from naturally infected wild birds to Eurasian siskins showed all recipients susceptible. Long-lasting persistence resulted from erythrocytic merogony rather than tissue stages; pCOLL7 infections consisted almost exclusively of gametocytes. First-time detection of Matryoshka RNA virus (MaRNAV-9) associated with P. circumflexum, opening new research into MaRNAV diversity and haemosporidian relationships.

articles.headline: HTM investments averted 6.9 billion PHC visits, $135 billion costs
articles.title_original: The benefits of investments to combat HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria for primary healthcare from 2000 to 2023: An economic modeling analysis.
articles.authors: Su JS, Stover J, Pretorius C, Winskill P, Sweeney S, Hallett TB, Menzies NA
articles.details: Economic modeling in 108 low- and middle-income countries 2000–2023 compared actual malaria control scale-up to counterfactual scenario maintaining 2000 coverage levels. Scale-up averted 6.9 billion outpatient visits and 3.9 billion inpatient bed-days (US$135 billion in costs) with greatest impact in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia/Pacific. These reductions represented median 4.4% of hospital bed capacity and 1.6% of government health spending, rising to 22.9% and 5.1% in low-income countries.

articles.headline: MosQNet-SA: deep learning for mosquito classification, 99.42% accuracy
articles.title_original: MosQNet-SA: Explainable convolutional-attention network for mosquito classification with application as a RESTful API for dengue and malaria risk mapping.
articles.authors: Masud MA, Akter S, Sultana N, Islam MS, Abu Yousuf M, Noori FM, Uddin MZ
articles.details: Novel convolutional-attention network (MosQNet-SA) for automated mosquito species classification achieved 99.42% accuracy on 1,000 images (Aedes, Anopheles, Culex) with 10-fold fewer parameters than existing CNNs. Model incorporates spatial attention and depthwise separable convolutions for computational efficiency. Explainability enhanced through Saliency, GradCAM, LIME, and Kernel SHAP methods. RESTful API enables real-time mosquito classification for dengue and malaria risk mapping in public health surveillance.

articles.headline: R561H and C580Y kelch13 mutations confer artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Functional assessment of prevalent kelch13 mutations reveals high-level artemisinin resistance potential in Bangladeshi Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nima MK, Bhattacharyya N, Shoue D, Park J, Phru CS, Sazed SA, Padmanabhan PK, Kumar S, Alam MS, Ferdig M, Mukherjee A
articles.details: Using CRISPR-Cas9 editing in two Bangladeshi P. falciparum isolates, researchers functionally assessed three globally prevalent kelch13 mutations (C580Y, F446I, R561H). C580Y and R561H both increased artemisinin resistance substantially in both strains; R561H in the moderately resistant background achieved extreme resistance (ring-stage survival assay 30.09% ± 1.9%) with asexual growth advantage of 5.1% per generation. Findings demonstrate Bangladeshi parasites are genetically primed to sustain high-level artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: IFN-γ signaling in T cells regulates malaria-associated lung pathology
articles.title_original: Comprehensive immune profiling reveals IFN-γ signaling in T cells mediates parasite phagocytosis in a rodent malaria model.
articles.authors: Chen S-s, Yang Q, Zhong Y, Liu D, Zhou L, Wei H-c, Li C-l, Song J, Wang Y-h, Hu X, Chen L, Lin J-w
articles.details: Using rodent malaria model and transcriptomics, study identified IFN-γ signaling in T cells as key mediator of malaria-associated lung pathology. Disruption of IFN-γ signaling reduced parasite load in lungs and attenuated pathology through enhanced T cell–monocyte interaction. CD8+ Ly6C+ proinflammatory monocytes showed elevated phagocytic capacity. Findings highlight complex immune network in malaria-associated pulmonary manifestation.

articles.headline: Novel pfk13 and pfubp1 variants confer artemisinin-lumefantrine resistance
articles.title_original: Novel Pfk13 and Pfubp1 genotypes in African Plasmodium falciparum isolates exhibiting reduced susceptibility to the antimalarials artemisinin and lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Pratt S, van Schalkwyk DA, Stewart L, Hocke EF, Mannan S, Berry C, Muwanguzi-Karugaba J, Nolder D, Rogers C, Chiodini PL, Henrici RC, Moss S, Billows N, Phelan J, Clark TG, Campino S, Sutherland CJ
articles.details: In vitro phenotyping and genotyping of 50 P. falciparum isolates from UK travelers to Africa revealed novel and established pfk13 variants (propeller and non-propeller domains) conferring partial artemisinin resistance from Kenya, Uganda, Zambia, and Namibia. Additional resistance-associated variants identified in pfap2μ, pfcoronin, and pfubp1. Among 39 evaluable isolates from 15 countries, reduced lumefantrine susceptibility emerged in multiple African settings, with six of ten least-susceptible isolates from Uganda-exposed individuals.

articles.headline: P. falciparum csp haplotype diversity in pre-vaccine Cameroon
articles.title_original: Haplotype diversity of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein prior to malaria vaccine implementation in Kaelé Health District, Cameroon, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Ali IM, Vasquez Martinez B, Tchuenkam VPK, Sadler JM, Gorman CC, Nsango SE, Junior VV, Ateba JM, Wanda GY, Bailey JA, Dinglasan RR, Lin JT, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Molecular survey of 100 P. falciparum infections from Cameroon 2022–2023 prior to RTS,S vaccine rollout sequenced circumsporozoite protein (csp), apical membrane antigen 1 (ama1), and other vaccine-relevant antigens. csp genotyping (35% success) identified 22 haplotypes with high diversity (heterozygosity 0.908, nucleotide diversity 0.021); vaccine-matched haplotype present in 20% of genotyped infections. Balancing selection detected in T-cell epitope regions. Mean within-host haplotype counts: ama1=2.8, sera2=2.1, csp=1.3, trap=1.2.

articles.headline: IgG magnitude, not avidity, predicts functional vaccine response
articles.title_original: Relationship between antibody avidity, Fc-mediated functional activity and longevity of malaria vaccine responses in clinical trials.
articles.authors: Horton JL, Chan JA, Kurtovic L, Reiling L, Feng G, Persson KEM, Sacarlal J, Anders RF, McCarthy JS, Dobaño C, Boyle MJ, Beeson JG
articles.details: Analysis of two malaria vaccine trials (phase 1 merozoite surface protein 2 in malaria-naïve adults, phase 2b RTS,S in African children) evaluated relationships between antibody avidity, Fc-mediated functions (complement fixation, Fcγ-receptor binding, opsonic phagocytosis), and longevity. IgG magnitude correlated strongly with avidity but was stronger predictor of Fc-mediated functional activity than avidity alone. Avidity showed only weak, antigen-specific association with antibody longevity, suggesting vaccines targeting avidity improvement alone may not enhance efficacy or duration.

articles.headline: PvDBP gene amplification enables P. vivax immune evasion
articles.title_original: PvDBP gene amplification is associated with functional immune evasion by Plasmodium vivax in vivo.
articles.authors: Baldor L, Seng D, Heng S, Khim N, Hor S, Thin S, Salinas N, Tolia N, Chitnis C, Mueller I, Foroughi S, King C, Lo E, Witkowski B, Flamand C, Feufack-Donfack L, Popovici J
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort (2019-2020) from 9 villages in Eastern Cambodia with 657 participants analyzed using PCR and flow cytometry. Multi-copy pvdbp parasites ranged 35% in low-prevalence to 47% in high-prevalence villages (p=0.0246). Among individuals with high binding-inhibitory antibodies, 87% (40/46) had multi-copy infections vs 38% (193/514) without antibodies (p<0.0001). Multi-copy parasites were more frequent in asymptomatic carriers, indicating pvdbp duplication helps parasites evade anti-PvDBP immunity.

articles.headline: Hospitals treating severe malaria cases mapped in US
articles.title_original: Mapping Hospitals That Treat Severe Malaria Cases in the United States: Evidence to Inform Health Systems Strategies for Intravenous Artesunate Supply.
articles.authors: Ravishankar K, Mace KE, Koiso S, Ridpath AD, Gulbas E, Thwing J, LaRocque RC, Williams S, Ryan ET, Brown TS, Hyle EP
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 1,466 hospitalized severe malaria cases reported to CDC during 2012-2018. Cases occurred in 292 of 3,142 US counties across 46 states, concentrated in New York, Maryland, Texas, District of Columbia, and Pennsylvania. Of 1,458 cases in the contiguous US, 95.8% were hospitalized within 2 hours driving time of a level-1 trauma center, identifying priority locations for intravenous artesunate supply distribution.

articles.headline: Artesunate treatment of severe malaria in non-endemic countries
articles.title_original: Artesunate Treatment of Severe Malaria in Nonendemic Countries: When, Where, How, and What Now?
articles.authors: White AC Jr
articles.details: No abstract provided; based on title, appears to address clinical guidance for artesunate treatment of severe malaria among travelers and patients in non-endemic countries.

articles.headline: Pharmacies provide guideline-concordant malaria care for Kenyan children
articles.title_original: Guideline-concordant care and outcomes for pediatric malaria cases: descriptive evidence from pharmacy-based fever management in Kenya.
articles.authors: Dieci M, Nekesa C, Kayaro NI, Vernon J, Graetz I, Winskell K, Cummings JR
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort at 39 private pharmacies in Kenya: among 239 febrile children under five, 69% received diagnostic testing; of tested children, 59% were malaria-positive; 78% of malaria-positive children received appropriate antimalarial treatment; 3% of malaria-negative received antimalarials; 88% of treated malaria-positive children fully recovered at follow-up. Diagnostic testing predicted by caregiver education and provider knowledge; appropriate treatment more likely for older, male children with experienced providers.

articles.headline: Dak Lak malaria elimination progress despite ongoing transmission
articles.title_original: Malaria situation in a highly endemic province in the Central Highlands of Vietnam: a retrospective analysis.
articles.authors: Nguyen-Tien T, Tran V, Nguyen TM, Hoang PH, Le DM, Maude RJ
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 2,147 malaria cases in Dak Lak province, Vietnam 2017-2022: 58.2% indigenous and 39.1% imported. P. falciparum dominant. Cases declined dramatically, approaching zero since 2021. Cases peaked in October-November. Gender, ethnicity, and high endemicity residence associated with Pf and Pv infection. Temperature and rainfall most strongly associated with case numbers. Cold spots identified in southwest province, no hotspots.

articles.headline: Renal biomarkers predict severe malaria complications and mortality
articles.title_original: Kidney and endothelial injury biomarkers as predictors of complications and mortality in patients with malaria: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Nasserala JCL, Leitão FNC, Dos Santos AVR, Martins AMC, Meneses GC, de Souza RN, da Silva Junior GB, De Francesco Daher E, de Deus Morais MJ
articles.details: Systematic review per PRISMA 2020 guidelines, registered in PROSPERO, searching PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science. Studies evaluated using Newcastle-Ottawa Scale and MINORS. Combined evaluation of renal and endothelial injury biomarkers with clinical and laboratory parameters allows improved characterization of malaria-associated acute kidney injury. Elevated biomarker levels consistently associated with increased risk of renal complications and adverse outcomes.

articles.headline: Mass drug administration campaign achieves operational targets in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Operational performance of a programmatic mass drug administration campaign for malaria in southern Mozambique: a comprehensive mixed-methods evaluation of implementation outcomes.
articles.authors: Fuente-Soro L, Tusell M, Vecchio J, Nhangave A, Bapu K, Riley C, Materrula F, Dimene M, Sibindy S, Candrinho B, Aide P, Guinovart C
articles.details: Mixed-methods evaluation of two rounds of programmatic MDA in Chidenguele, Gaza province, Mozambique (December 2022-February 2023) using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, target population ~59,271. Among 631 eligible round 2 participants, 96.2% (607/631) accepted. Programmatic coverage 73.9% (569/770), operational coverage 90.2% (569/631). Community acceptance 96.2%, health staff commitment 86-96%, but reaching 80% coverage target remains challenging despite strong operational performance.

articles.headline: Dengue fever masked as malaria in western Kenya children
articles.title_original: Unmasking non-malarial mosquito-borne infections among febrile children in malaria-endemic regions of western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Karanja S, Ayodo G, Akinyi I, Onguru D, Angira C, Menya D, Mangeni J
articles.details: Screening of febrile children under 5 years at health facilities in Busia and Kisumu Counties: 1,004 screened, 380 met criteria. Results: 215 (21.4%) tested positive for P. falciparum alone, 90 (8.9%) for dengue alone, 75 (7.5%) for co-infections. Busia had highest P. falciparum-only rate (23.4%), Kisumu highest dengue-only (12.6%), revealing dengue as major contributor to non-malarial febrile illness in children.

articles.headline: African genetic variants protect against severe malaria and bacteremia
articles.title_original: African-specific genetic loci determine iron status and risk of severe malaria and bacteremia in African children.
articles.authors: Muriuki JM, Mentzer AJ, Band G, Chong AY, Macharia AW, Mogire RM, Abuga KM, Mitchell R, Gilchrist JJ, Webb EL, Ndungu FM, Raffield LM, Ekunwe L, Bentley AR, Sirima SB, Madhi SA, Hill AVS, Prentice AM, Bejon P, Hemani G, Smith GD, Sandhu MS, Elliott AM, Williams TN, Adeyemo A, Atkinson SH
articles.details: Genome-wide association studies of six iron-related biomarkers in 3,928 African children across 5 sites with replication in 2,868 African American adults. Identified loci at GTF3C5 (transferrin), CHCHD7/SDR16C5 (hepcidin), and DUP4 haplotype (Dantu blood group). Variants at GTF3C5 and DUP4 confer protection against severe malaria and bacteremia. CHCHD7/SDR16C5 variant increases hepcidin and reduces K. pneumoniae and S. aureus bacteremia risk. European polygenic risk scores show limited African transferability.

articles.headline: Public education critical for malaria vaccine rollout in Sudan
articles.title_original: Public awareness and education: key to the success of the malaria vaccine rollout in Sudan.
articles.authors: Mohammed AAEA, Elagib HKE, Ibrahim MAA, Rafai MAA, Ali ODMH, Yaseen OYM
articles.details: No abstract provided; based on title, discusses importance of public awareness and education for successful malaria vaccine implementation in Sudan.

articles.headline: Biological control offers alternative malaria vector management strategies
articles.title_original: Exploring the Scope and Status of Biological Interventions in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Kumari S, Siddaramegowda R, Beeranthbail A, Shankar G, Kumar M, Sinha S, Anvikar A, Chalageri VH, Tripathi PK, Marinaik SB
articles.details: Narrative review of biological control methods for malaria vector management as alternatives to traditional approaches (indoor residual spraying, insecticide-treated bed nets). Covers viruses, fungi, microbial pathogens, natural predators, alkaloids, essential oils, and plants with larvicidal properties. Discusses genetic techniques including sterile insect technique and genetically modified mosquitoes. Highlights entomopathogenic fungi as particularly promising for mosquito control.

articles.headline: Infections reduce childhood growth velocity in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Associations of Malaria, Diarrhea, and Respiratory Infections with Growth Velocity of Children in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Hiruy HN, Liu E, Worku A, Manji KP, Duggan CP, Berhane Y, Fawzi WW
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 2,397 Tanzanian children in RCT, 18-month follow-up (30,079 monthly visits). Average height velocity 1.58 cm/month, weight velocity 358 g/month. Malaria associated with -0.14 cm/month height reduction (95% CI -0.23 to -0.05), not significant for weight. Respiratory tract infections associated with -0.10 cm/month height and -37 g/month weight reductions. Diarrhea associated with -0.26 cm/month height and -120 g/month weight reductions.

articles.headline: Chloroquine transport in malaria parasites depends on redox state
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Chloroquine Resistance Transporter (PfCRT) Is a Redox-Dependent Drug Transporter.
articles.authors: Chernitsky-Hamd D, Roy R, Schemm J, Schall A, Petros M, Willems A, Roepe PD
articles.details: Mechanistic study using site-specific mutagenesis, maleimide labeling, and molecular dynamics showing that chloroquine transport by PfCRT is regulated by redox potential. Disulfide bonds at C289/C312 and C301/C309 pairs are required for full transport activity. Additional cysteines (C101, C139, C171, C328) modulate transport capacity.

articles.headline: Liver-stage vaccine shows high immunogenicity but fails to prevent infection
articles.title_original: Phase I/IIa study to assess the safety, immunogenicity and efficacy of ChAdOx1-MVA vectored vaccines expressing a novel liver-stage malaria dual antigen LS2 by sporozoite challenge in malaria-naïve adults.
articles.authors: Silman D, Flaxman A, Datoo M, Edwards NJ, Ramos-Lopez F, Mitton C, Mair C, Bellamy D, Bowyer G, Morter R, Halbroth B, Venkatraman N, Folegatti PM, Marshall J, Poulton I, Bajer A, Salman AM, Berrie E, Baum J, Blagborough AM, Crocker W, Roberts R, Lawrie AM, Spencer AJ, Gilbert SC, Ewer KJ, Hill AVS
articles.details: Phase I/IIa study of ChAdOx1 LS2 prime and MVA LS2 boost in UK adults (n=10) with controlled human malaria infection by mosquito bite, compared to unvaccinated controls (n=6). Vaccination was well tolerated with high median IFN-γ response of 4473 SFC/10^6 PBMC. All vaccinated volunteers became infected with median time to diagnosis of 13 days, similar to controls.

articles.headline: Multiple insecticide resistance in Indian malaria vectors widespread across Odisha
articles.title_original: Mapping the Insecticide Resistance Landscape of Malaria Vectors in Odisha, India (1993-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Barik TK, Velamuri PS, Bhuyan KB, Sabar S, Mohapatra P, Sahu B, Das MK, Pati S, Dash AP, Raghavendra K
articles.details: Scoping review of insecticide resistance in Anopheles species across Odisha from 1993-2024, compiling data from 28 published sources. An. culicifacies showed most resistance: DDT resistance in 26 districts, malathion in 17 districts, deltamethrin in 10 districts. Multiple insecticide resistance emergence signals need for novel control strategies and integrated vector management.

articles.headline: Malaria-HIV co-infection in sub-Saharan Africa requires integrated services
articles.title_original: Insights into Malaria and HIV Co-infection: Trends and Impact in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Ayoola AO, Okanlawon BM, Idowu EM, Adedokun TT, Ige AA, Olorunfemi AB, Ojurongbe OD
articles.details: Review of malaria and HIV co-infection epidemiology in sub-Saharan Africa examining evidence on risk factors, health outcomes, diagnostic limitations, and treatment interactions. Authors emphasize that closer coordination of malaria and HIV services may improve outcomes and efficiency, and identify persistent gaps in surveillance, clinical management, and integrated control strategies.

articles.headline: Climatic gradients drive malaria vector adaptation through chromosomal inversions
articles.title_original: Spatial eco-climatic gradients drive adaptive 2La chromosomal inversion polymorphism in Anopheles coluzzii, a major malaria vector in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Adeogun AO, Adetunji O, Babalola AS, Izekor RT, Oyeniyi TA, Adesoye O, Wahedi J, Busari LO, Aniekeme G, Ajayi S, Chabi J, Awolola ST
articles.details: Molecular population genetics study of 2La chromosomal inversion in An. coluzzii across Nigeria's ecological zones from larvae sampled in 12 states. Geographic structuring showed 2La/2La homokaryotype predominant in hot/arid northern zones, 2La+/2La+ in humid southern zones, and heterokaryotype at intermediate frequencies in central Nigeria, with pronounced latitudinal cline.

articles.headline: Housing modifications combined with behavior change reduce disease transmission
articles.title_original: Household and community-based approaches to malaria and cholera prevention: A narrative review with case studies from Uganda and Mozambique.
articles.authors: Guido G, Totaro V, Lochoro P, Ictho J, Occa E, Chhaganlal K, Segala FV, Dall'Oglio G, Mechendura BF, Gomonda E, Putoto G, Penco F, Asaduzzaman M, Nicastri E, Farkas FB, Iatta R, Lakatos B, Ali RW, Saracino A, Di Gennaro F
articles.details: Narrative review with case studies from Uganda and Mozambique examining housing interventions for malaria and cholera prevention. Structural modifications (screened windows/doors, sealed eaves, elevated flooring, insecticide-treated barriers) combined with improved sanitation are low-cost, community-acceptable, easily implementable strategies that can complement existing large-scale control programs.

articles.headline: Urbanization reduces avian malaria transmission in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Urbanization and Vector Species Shape Avian Malaria Prevalence in Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Parra P, Veiga J, Garrigós M, Panisse G, Garrido M, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: Cross-sectional assessment of avian malaria prevalence in two mosquito species in urban and peri-urban habitats. No infections detected in either species in urban areas, while 2.53% prevalence was found in native Culex pipiens from peri-urban sites, highlighting urbanization's influence on parasite transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Ivermectin precipitates encephalopathy in malaria patient with Loa loa
articles.title_original: Probable Loa loa-associated encephalopathy following ivermectin administration in a patient with malaria infection: A case report from the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Posite CM, Findama JK, Vwiravwahali MK, Molo IM, Bunduki GK
articles.details: Case report of 37-year-old woman from DRC with concurrent malaria and loiasis presenting with altered mental status after ivermectin administration. Blood smear revealed Plasmodium trophozoites and Loa loa microfilariae. Within 24 hours of ivermectin, she developed meningeal signs; cerebrospinal fluid contained Loa loa microfilariae without bacterial or fungal infection. Complete clinical recovery occurred after albendazole and supportive care.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy in conflict zones requires urgent integrated action
articles.title_original: Time to Act; Let Us Unite to Eliminate Malaria Among Pregnant Women in War Zone From Eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo: A Review.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Mehmood L, Njazi PI, Ali B, Maqbool S, Younas M, Sagide M, Rizvi SVZ, Munsab R, Rizvi SUA, Fatima S, Alhassan A, Wei CR, Langat AK
articles.details: Narrative review (2007-2025) of malaria in pregnancy in eastern DRC conflict zones. Malaria prevalence 17-40% among pregnant women with 26% asymptomatic pooled estimate. Early-stage parasitemia associated with reduced umbilical artery resistance and fetal growth restriction. Armed conflict has decimated health infrastructure causing critical shortages of insecticide-treated nets and intermittent preventive treatment.

articles.headline: Broad protection strategies superior to targeted high-intensity malaria control
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal agent-based modelling of malaria.
articles.authors: Walker CR, Anwar MN, Bräuninger L, Richards J, Ataide R, Thang ND, Thang NX, Canavati S, Flegg JA
articles.details: Stochastic spatiotemporal agent-based model integrating mosquito climatic suitability, household locations, and spatially distributed interventions to assess malaria transmission in Vietnam. Model demonstrates that providing some level of protection to a wide range of households reduces malaria prevalence more effectively than providing strong protection to a limited number of households.

articles.headline: Malaria-schistosomiasis coinfection affects 10-13% of African children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum coinfection with Schistosoma haematobium and Schistosoma mansoni among children in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Hassan U, Siddiqa A, Zhu Y, Zhao J
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 21 observational studies estimating pooled prevalence of P. falciparum coinfection in sub-Saharan African children aged 0-17 years. P. falciparum-S. haematobium coinfection prevalence 10.0% (95% CI 6.0-14.0%) with regional variation (West Africa 13%, Central Africa 9%, East Africa 3%). P. falciparum-S. mansoni coinfection 13.0% (95% CI 6.0-24.0%) with significant regional variation (East Africa 25%, Central Africa 3%, West Africa 2%).

articles.headline: Malaria-associated respiratory distress involves microvascular obstruction, not acidosis
articles.title_original: Malarial pneumonopathy in Malawian children with cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Wynkoop HJ, Lintner-Rivera M, Taylor T, Seydel KB, Pensulo P, Ayamie M, Chetcuti K, Vidal LLM, O'Brien NF
articles.details: Prospective observational study of 97 Malawian children (6 months to 12 years) with cerebral malaria. Respiratory distress occurred in 28% (n=27); despite elevated lactate (median 7.0 mmol/L), median pH was 7.41 with metabolic acidosis as sole acid-base disturbance in only 12%. Lung ultrasound revealed subcentimeter consolidations in 88%, suggesting pulmonary involvement from microvascular obstruction and inflammation rather than metabolic acidosis alone.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine rollout safety monitoring in three African countries
articles.title_original: Safety monitoring of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine: experiences and lessons from routine pharmacovigilance in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi.
articles.authors: Ashie A, Mandale M, Ndalama A, Darko D, Seaneke S, Boateng EK, Sabblah G, Asamoa-Amoakohene A, Amponsa-Achiano K, Mohammed NT, Jalang'o R, Nkansah E, Adu-Boahen Y, Siyoi F, Toroitich A, Khaemba C, Nambwa P, Jain S, Okine RNA, Mssusa AK
articles.details: This pharmacovigilance study monitored RTS,S/AS01E vaccine rollout safety in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi through strengthened routine systems. Causality assessment found no new safety concerns or confirmation of Phase 3 safety signals. Main challenges included underreporting of adverse events following immunization (AEFIs), delays in data submission and investigation, and poor report quality despite targeted investments.

articles.headline: Spatial malaria hotspots identified through vector-incidence modeling in Benin
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria hotspots in southwestern Benin through spatial joint modelling of malaria incidence and vector abundance.
articles.authors: Monteiro GM, Aïkpon RY, Dandonougbo C, Sedda L, Djogbenou LS
articles.details: This Bayesian spatial modeling study in southwestern Benin identified malaria hotspots by jointly analyzing Anopheles gambiae s.l. and An. funestus abundance with human malaria incidence. An. funestus showed stronger spatial correlation with incidence (119.95 km²) than An. gambiae s.l. (89.90 km²), with 67.29 km² showing synergistic effects. Wind speed, mid-infrared reflectance, leaf area index, and land surface temperature were key ecological drivers.

articles.headline: Pediatric malaria severity independent of prior exposure in Europe
articles.title_original: Pediatric malaria in a non-endemic European setting: clinical patterns, laboratory markers, and the ambiguous role of immunity.
articles.authors: Caiazzo R, Panero B, Carello M, Coppola C, Di Tonno R, Markowich AH, Stracuzzi M, Valentino MS, Grosso S, Gigantiello A, Giacomet V
articles.details: This retrospective study of 74 pediatric malaria cases found severe malaria in 23% with no significant difference between presumed-immune (52.7%) and non-immune (47.3%) groups (p=0.783). P. falciparum (62.2%) and P. vivax (27%) predominated. Hyponatremia inversely associated with hospital stay (ρ=-0.284, p=0.020); CRP correlated with fever duration (ρ=0.256, p=0.033). Intravenous artesunate replaced quinine post-2015.

articles.headline: Uric acid reflects inflammation but not severe malaria outcomes
articles.title_original: Serum Uric Acid as a Biomarker of Disease Severity in Children With Complicated Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study From Eastern India.
articles.authors: Kumar M, Jha S, Kumar H, Sarangam B, Munda PK, Garg V, Mudgal SK
articles.details: In a hospital-based cross-sectional study of 102 Indian children with P. falciparum complicated malaria, hyperuricemia occurred in 80.4% (82), with elevated liver enzymes (SGOT 72.5%, SGPT 83.3%) and bilirubin (65.7%). Adverse outcomes in 29.4% (30) were significantly associated with abnormal SGOT (p=0.011), SGPT (p=0.004), and bilirubin (p=0.004), but serum uric acid was not significantly associated with outcomes (p=0.115).

articles.headline: Global malaria burden review with emerging control challenges
articles.title_original: Malaria in the 21st Century: Global Disease Burden, Epidemiological Insights, and Strategic Control Approaches.
articles.authors: Alharbi BF, Ahmed MA
articles.details: This review summarizes epidemiological data on global malaria burden from WHO World Malaria Report 2024 and Global Burden of Disease estimates, covering regional trends, determinants, and control strategies. Key challenges include drug resistance, climate change, and limited funding. It emphasizes multifactorial, locally-tailored approaches and policy priorities to achieve WHO Global Technical Strategy malaria elimination goals by 2030.

articles.headline: P. falciparum prevalence dropped 53% in Rwanda despite drug resistance
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of Falciparum malaria in Rwanda: Shifts in parasite prevalence and risk factors between the 2014-15 and 2019-20 Rwanda Demographics and Health Surveys.
articles.authors: Zuromski J, Young NW, Gashema P, Iradukunda V, Bosco NJ, Sadler JM, Gaither C, Munyaneza T, Connelly SV, Lee L, Goel V, Muvunyi CM, Dieu Butera J, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA
articles.details: This study of 7,127 adult dried blood spot samples from 2019-20 Rwanda DHS using ultra-sensitive qRT-PCR found national P. falciparum infection prevalence of 7.7% (95%CI [6.8%, 8.7%]) with predominantly low-density infections (median parasitemia 7.3 parasites/uL). Male sex, lower household wealth, lower education, and lower elevation were significant predictors. National prevalence decreased 53% compared to 2014-15, despite reports of increasing artemisinin-resistance-associated mutations.

articles.headline: Novel K13 double mutations confer ozonide resistance in parasites
articles.title_original: Double Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 drive resistance to next-generation artemisinin derivatives in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Bower-Lepts C, Ward KE, Wittlin S, Stokes BH, Yeo T, Qahash T, Small-Saunders JL, Park H, Uhlemann AC, Llinás M, Fidock DA, Mok S
articles.details: This in vitro study of Cambodian K13 mutant parasites subjected to increasing artefenomel (OZ439) pressure identified novel K13 A212T mutation conferring resistance. K13 double mutation R539T+A212T determined OZ439 resistance with accelerated parasite recrudescence following OZ439 or OZ277 exposure. Metabolomic analysis showed increased glutathione, nucleotide, and aspartate-glutamate metabolism, suggesting enhanced redox regulation as the resistance mechanism rather than altered drug activation.

articles.headline: Imported malaria comprises 89% of cases in Asia Pacific elimination countries
articles.title_original: Estimating the burden of imported malaria in the Asia Pacific Region: A Systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Penjor K, Thapa B, Tsheten T, Mazumdar S, Bagheri N, Kotepui M, Wangdi K
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 42 studies from January 2010–December 2024 in 9 Asia Pacific countries (435,454 cases) found pooled prevalence of imported malaria 89.0% (95% CI: 75.9–95.5). Males accounted for 94% of imported cases (95% CI: 92.0–96.0), P. falciparum 57.0% (95% CI: 47.2–65.5), and cases from Africa 64.3% (95% CI: 43.1–81.0). Pre-elimination phase countries: 84.6% imported malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria burden in reproductive-age women declined in East Africa 1990-2023
articles.title_original: Fatal and non-fatal health outcomes of malaria among reproductive age women in Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa, from 1990 to 2023.
articles.authors: Tadesse S, Korma W, Sherfa A, Bedewi J, Alemseged EA, Angaw Y, Assefa L, Fekadu Y, Ashenafi G, Tsegaye D, Gemechu T
articles.details: This Global Burden of Disease 2023 study estimated malaria in reproductive-age women across Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa. Highest prevalence in Mozambique (19,627 per 100,000 WRA; 95% UI: 12,347–27,601); highest incidence in Mozambique (16,076 per 100,000 WRA; 95% UI: 11,890–21,714). Substantial reductions in mortality, years of life lost (YLLs), and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) occurred region-wide from 1990 to 2023, except in Djibouti and Ethiopia.

articles.headline: Baseline malaria and pyrethroid resistance in Tanzania trial site
articles.title_original: Baseline malaria burden and pyrethroid resistance in Muheza, Tanzania informing a cluster randomized trial of the 3D window screens.
articles.authors: Kathet S, Bwana VM, Fereji MA, Mhando JB, Magogo FS, Mwingira V, Kisinza W, Meri S, Khattab A
articles.details: Baseline study in 20 hamlet clusters (Muheza District, Tanzania) in 778 children assessed malaria burden and vector resistance. Malaria prevalence 40.2%, anaemia 55.2%. Of 14,263 mosquitoes, An. funestus s.l. (70.2%) and An. gambiae s.l. (29.8%) had annual EIRs of 14.2 and 13.5 per person per night. Pyrethroid resistance high (56.5% to permethrin, 52.8% to deltamethrin); kdr-East allele frequency 0.45. Children ≥5 years: 3-fold higher infection odds (aOR=3.03, 95% CI: 2.09–4.40).

articles.headline: Risk-based reactive case detection increased infection yield in Indonesia
articles.title_original: Adapting reactive case detection strategies for high-risk populations to increase malaria infection detection in Aceh Province, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Bennett A, Cotter C, Zarlinda I, Silaen MG, Lina RN, Cueto C, Elyazar I, Jacobson JO, Ekawati L, Hsiang MS, Noviyanti R, Smith JL, Coutrier FN
articles.details: Feasibility study in Aceh Province, Indonesia compared household-based reactive case detection (RACD) with risk-based RACD targeting forest-exposed contacts in 47 index cases (28 P. knowlesi). Standard household RACD tested 847 contacts from 34 cases, yielding 1 LAMP/PCR-confirmed infection (0.1%; 95% CI 0.0–0.7%). Risk-based RACD tested 179 forest contacts from 32 cases, yielding 8 LAMP-positive infections (4.5%; 95% CI 1.9–8.6%), with 3 PCR-confirmed (1.7%; 95% CI 0.3–4.8%).

articles.headline: Correction: mosquito biting behavior and intervention timing study
articles.title_original: Correction: A matter of timing: Biting by malaria-infected Anopheles mosquitoes and the use of interventions during the night in rural south-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Namango IH, Moore SJ, Marshall C, Saddler A, Kaftan D, Tenywa FC, Makungwa N, Limwagu AJ, Mapua S, Odufuwa OG, Ligema G, Ngonyani H, Matanila I, Bharmal J, Moore J, Finda M, Okumu F, Hetzel MW, Ross A
articles.details: Correction notice for an article on the timing of Anopheles mosquito biting behavior and intervention use in rural southeastern Tanzania.

articles.headline: Climate and net distribution reduce child malaria mortality
articles.title_original: Assessing the role of interventions and climate on malaria mortality among children under five years of age: insights from two decades of data from the Health Demographic Surveillance System of Nouna, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traoré N, Zabré P, Millogo O, Sié A, Vounatsou P
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of malaria mortality data from Burkina Faso (2002-2021) used wavelet analysis and Bayesian models (2013-2021) to examine effects of interventions and climate. Rainfall positively associated with mortality (MRR=1.59, 95% BCI 1.18-1.95), lower land surface temperature reduced mortality (MRR=0.68, 95% BCI 0.52-0.86), and insecticide-treated nets reduced mortality (MRR=0.59, 95% BCI 0.42-0.79); artemisinin-based combination therapies were not statistically significant. ITN coverage was more effective than temperature in reducing mortality, but rainfall had the greater opposing impact.

articles.headline: Inflammation does not mediate BMI-vitamin A relationships
articles.title_original: Inflammation Does Not Mediate Relationships between BMI/BMI-for-Age Z-Score and Retinol or Retinol-Binding Protein in Adult Women or Young Children without Underweight, Wasting, or Malaria: An Analysis of 24 Surveys.
articles.authors: Davis JN, Williams A, Arnold CD, Addo Y, Oaks BM, Wirth JP, Suchdev PS, Young MF, Engle-Stone R
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 24 surveys (16,771 women, 24,707 children) from the BRINDA project examined whether inflammatory biomarkers mediated relationships between BMI and vitamin A status (retinol/RBP), excluding observations with underweight, wasting, or malaria. Among women, BMI was positively associated with retinol/RBP in 5 of 13 surveys, with partial mediation in 3 of 13. Among children, BAZ was positively associated with retinol/RBP in 3 of 22 surveys with partial mediation in 1 of 22. Inflammation associated with overweight/obesity does not appear to impact vitamin A assessment in individuals without undernutrition or malaria.

articles.headline: Social protection gaps in Sub-Saharan malaria policies
articles.title_original: Assessing the integration of social protection into infectious disease policies: a multi-country analysis of TB, HIV, and malaria strategies in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Munyangaju I, Osório D, Nacarapa E
articles.details: Structured policy and document review of 111 documents from Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Nigeria, and Zambia (2015-2025) assessed integration of social protection into TB, HIV, and malaria programs using a five-domain assessment matrix. Overall readiness ranged from 2.6 to 2.9 out of 3, with Malawi (2.9) and Zambia (2.8) scoring highest; implementation/coordination was the strongest domain (3.0), while financial protection was weakest (2.1). Although national policies increasingly acknowledge social protection, significant gaps persist in catastrophic-cost monitoring, budgeting, and accountability.

articles.headline: PfEMP1 antibody domains protect young children from malaria
articles.title_original: IgG and IgM responses to PfEMP1 domains associated with semi-immunity to clinical malaria in Burkinabe children under five.
articles.authors: Dankyi BO, Yuguchi T, Rojrung R, Nagaoka H, Kanoi BN, Tiono AB, Nebie I, Ouedraogo A, Tanaka K, Miyake Y, Miura K, Sattabongkot J, Sirima SB, Tsuboi T, Takashima E
articles.details: Study measured IgG and IgM antibody responses to 271 P. falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP1) domains in 80 children (aged 0-60 months) from Burkina Faso collected before and during peak transmission. IgG responses to 132 domains (49%) were significantly associated with reduced risk of clinical malaria after FDR correction; only 5 IgM responses were nominally protective and none remained significant. Opsonic phagocytosis activity correlated with protection for only one antigen, suggesting PfEMP1-specific antibodies mediate protection through mechanisms beyond phagocytosis.

articles.headline: Genetically diverse P. vivax relapses transmit efficiently
articles.title_original: Frequent and genetically diverse Plasmodium vivax relapses contribute to malaria transmission in Cambodia.
articles.authors: Seng D, Ko K, Orban A, Heng S, Ea M, Tat B, Feufack Donfack LB, Eng V, Tebben K, Khim N, Lefèvre T, Cohuet A, Sommen C, Flamand C, Serre D, Popovici J
articles.details: Cohort of 59 Cambodian P. vivax patients treated with artesunate and relocated to a malaria-free area, followed every 2 days for 90 days with qPCR, microscopy, and genome sequencing. Relapses occurred in 81% within 90 days (up to 4 consecutive relapses, median 20 days to first relapse), with relapsing parasites showing genetic identity, sibling relationships, or no relatedness to initial infection. Polyclonal infections at enrollment experienced significantly more relapses. Despite low parasitemia, relapse infections were frequently infectious to mosquitoes, highlighting the role of complex hypnozoite reservoirs in transmission.

articles.headline: Hemoglobin AA variant increases asymptomatic malaria odds
articles.title_original: The Role of Haemoglobin Variants and Red Blood Cell Polymorphisms in Asymptomatic Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Boatey CA, Deku JG, Boakye AA, Nyarkotey E, Larbi G, Acheampong DO
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of community members in Ghana recruited without clinical malaria signs and screened for P. falciparum HRP2, with ABO blood group and hemoglobin phenotyping via alkaline electrophoresis. Hemoglobin variants (p=0.021) and red cell polymorphisms (p<0.001) were significantly associated with asymptomatic malaria. Individuals with hemoglobin AA had significantly higher odds of asymptomatic malaria (AOR=2.1, p=0.02), while individuals with O Rh D-positive, O/HbAA, and B/HbAS showed higher odds that were not statistically significant.

articles.headline: Novel hydroxamate inhibitors show promise against PfA-M1
articles.title_original: In silico targeting of plasmodium falciparum metallo-aminopeptidases using hydroxamate inhibitors.
articles.authors: Sodum N, Rao V, Chhabra V, Kumar N, Rao CM
articles.details: In-silico molecular modeling study evaluated cinnamoyl sulfonamide hydroxamate derivatives (NMJ 1-8) against P. falciparum metallo-aminopeptidase PfA-M1 using extra-precision docking, MMGBSA binding energy analysis, and molecular dynamics simulations. NMJ-2 and NMJ-3 showed the strongest binding affinities with induced-fit docking scores from -1984.16 to -1989.93 kcal/mol and formed stable complexes with persistent interactions at key residues. Both compounds satisfied ADME pharmacokinetic parameters and showed interaction patterns overlapping with reference inhibitor tosedostat.

articles.headline: Duffy-negative individuals can harbor P. vivax infections
articles.title_original: Genetic variation in the Duffy blood group among vivax malaria patients and its impact on disease susceptibility.
articles.authors: Tsigie M, Baldor L, Feufack-Donfack LB, Shenkutie TT, Khim N, Melese D, Abera A, Girmachew F, Feleke SM, Hailemariam A, Tasew G, Tollera G, Hailu M, Popovici J, Abebe A, Lo E
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 485 microscopy-confirmed P. vivax-infected samples from Ethiopia (January-June 2024) used molecular screening and Duffy genotyping via TaqMan-based qPCR. Among samples, 93.8% were mono-P. vivax and 6.2% mixed; 75.1% (364/485) were heterozygous Duffy-positive, 24.5% (119/485) homozygous, and 0.4% (2/485) Duffy-negative with mixed infections and low parasitemia. Duffy negativity was not an absolute barrier to P. vivax infection, suggesting an alternative invasion pathway exists.

articles.headline: Vector composition and outdoor biting threaten bed net effectiveness
articles.title_original: Mosquito vector composition and biting behavior of malaria vectors in forest versus humid savanna eco-epidemiological settings of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Kiam CB, Aabate L, Bayibeki AN, Ibrahima I, Nanssong-Vomo CT, Tumamo BF, Onguene C, Awono-Ambéné PH, Ayong LS, Moukoko CEE, Morlais I, Nsango SE
articles.details: Entomological survey collected 3,651 Anopheles mosquitoes (1,817 from forest site Mfou, 1,834 from savanna site Tibati in Cameroon) April-July 2019 using human landing catches indoors and outdoors, with molecular identification and qPCR for Plasmodium. An. gambiae s.l. predominated in Mfou (68.96%), while An. funestus s.l. dominated in Tibati (49.95%). Entomological inoculation rate was higher in Mfou (indoors 1.47, outdoors 1.00 ib/p/n) than Tibati (indoors 0.25, outdoors 0.23), with substantial outdoor biting and peak aggressiveness 4-5am.

articles.headline: Malaria burden high in Ugandan infants despite maternal immunity
articles.title_original: Natural History and the Burden of Malaria During the First Year of Life in the High-Transmission Setting of Uganda.
articles.authors: Aguti M, Nankabirwa JI, Kizza J, Kakuru A, Ssemukuye T, Adrama H, Olwoch P, Opira B, Odongo B, Camanag K, Nakalembe M, Clark T, Rosenthal PJ, Dorsey G, Jaganathan P, Kamya MR
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 855 infants in Uganda enrolled at 4-8 weeks age and followed to 1 year (2021-2025) with active and passive case detection via microscopy and qPCR. Over 706.7 person-years of follow-up, 662 malaria episodes occurred; microscopic parasitemia prevalence was 7.9% and combined microscopic and submicroscopic parasitemia was 21.8%. Sickle cell trait conferred 39% protection against symptomatic malaria but was not associated with parasitemia risk; modern housing and higher maternal education were independently associated with reduced risk. District-wide LLIN distribution in October 2023 was followed by 80% reduction in incidence.

articles.headline: SARIMA forecasts rising malaria cases in Nigerian states
articles.title_original: Exploring the past and forecasting the future of malaria in selected Nigerian states: A time series modelling approach using wavelet and SARIMA.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Mogbojuri OA, Bakare DA, Odewusi OJ
articles.details: Time series analysis of confirmed uncomplicated malaria cases from Nasarawa, Kwara, and Zamfara States, Nigeria (January 2015-December 2024) used wavelet analysis and Seasonal Auto-Regressive Integrated Moving Average modeling to reveal patterns and forecast 2025-2026 cases. Wavelet analysis showed annual seasonal cycles in Nasarawa (peaks September 70%) and Zamfara with synchronization between these states, while Kwara showed more variable patterns. SARIMA forecasts for 2025-2026 were: Nasarawa 51,482 (95% CI 23,899-116,073), Kwara 20,850 (95% CI 3,381-202,511), and Zamfara 67,463 (95% CI 27,317-171,418).

articles.headline: Duffy selection may have reduced P. vivax prevalence in Oman
articles.title_original: Adaptive admixture at ACKR1, the Duffy blood group locus, may have shaped Plasmodium vivax prevalence in Oman.
articles.authors: Haffener PE, Al-Riyami AZ, Al-Zadjali S, Busby GBJ, Al-Mahdhuri S, Al-Rawahi M, Al-Hosni S, Al-Marhoobi A, Al-Sheriyani A, Leffler EM
articles.details: Whole-genome sequence analysis of 100 Omani individuals examined genetic ancestry and selection at the Duffy locus, where the FyES null allele confers resistance to P. vivax. Genetic ancestry inference showed 15.6% (95% CI 10.8-20.3%) Omani ancestry from African populations, but at the Duffy locus African ancestry reached 76%. Estimated selection coefficient at FyES was 0.038 (95% CI 0.027-0.051) with likely introduction at least 59 generations ago. No enriched African ancestry for P. falciparum-protective alleles, indicating lack of adaptive admixture driven by P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Computational epitope prioritization framework for malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: TEpiNom: A computational framework integrating population data to prioritize Plasmodium falciparum T cell epitopes.
articles.authors: Laurenson AJ, Pierce BG, Takala-Harrison S, Laurens MB
articles.details: Developed TEpiNom computational framework integrating P. falciparum sequence diversity, HLA polymorphisms, and African population HLA frequencies to prioritize T cell epitopes for vaccine development. Analyzed 42 proteins from 18 African countries and identified 2265 MHC I and 1992 MHC II conserved epitopes. Selected pre-erythrocytic epitopes (FabZ, FabG, p36, PKG) achieved 100% predicted inter-locus MHC I coverage.

articles.headline: Caregiver perception of malaria risk linked to vaccination hesitancy
articles.title_original: Impact of caregivers' perceptions of malaria on their hesitancy toward childhood malaria vaccination.
articles.authors: Kretchy IA, Bonful HA, Opoku-Mireku M, Koduah A, Opare-Addo M, Marfo AFA, Atobrah D, Klobodu HK, Alhassan Y, Kretchy JP, Opuni KFM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 2590 adults in six Ghana regions measuring malaria vaccine hesitancy (44.8% prevalence) using adapted Oxford COVID-19 hesitancy scale and Brief Illness Perception Questionnaire. Greater perceived control and understanding of malaria associated with increased hesitancy (aPR = 1.15; 95% CI: 1.09-1.21; p < 0.001). Other factors included region, marital status, ethnicity, housing, employment, and health conditions.

articles.headline: Nanoparticles enhance malaria vaccine antibody durability in mice
articles.title_original: Iron-oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) enhance malaria vaccine antibody response.
articles.authors: Al-Abboodi A, Falih IQ, Al-Asadi M, Hussein BA, Al-Saady MAAJ, Abdullah TA, Al-Saady M, Nhat T, Le ND, Le PC
articles.details: Tested carboxylated superparamagnetic iron-oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4-COOH SPIONs) as carrier for PfMSP-1 (25 μg) in BALB/c mice (n=8/group). Single-dose SPION-PfMSP-1 generated delayed but steadily rising IgG response; three-dose SPION-PfMSP-1 schedule produced highest and most durable anti-PfMSP-1 titers throughout observation period, superior to soluble antigen alone.

articles.headline: Plasmodium juxtanucleare prevalence in Thai poultry assessed molecularly
articles.title_original: Molecular prevalence and phylogenetic characterization of Plasmodium juxtanucleare in Thai native chickens and fighting cocks across Kalasin Province, Northeast Thailand.
articles.authors: Insee J, Thanasuwan S
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 181 blood samples (112 Thai native chickens, 69 fighting cocks) across Kalasin Province. Overall P. spp. molecular prevalence was 62.98% (114/181); higher in native chickens (67.85%) versus fighting cocks (55.07%), difference not statistically significant (p > 0.05). Prevalence varied significantly by district (p < 0.05), ranging 20-100%. Sequencing revealed two Plasmodium juxtanucleare haplotypes with 99-100% nucleotide identity.

articles.headline: Widespread sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance in Indian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Genetic profiling of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance markers in Indian Plasmodium falciparum field isolates.
articles.authors: Narang G, Jakhan J, Gupta D, Singh V
articles.details: Analyzed 307 P. falciparum isolates from 14 Indian states; successfully sequenced 243 Pfdhfr and 241 Pfdhps isolates. Most frequent Pfdhfr mutations: S108N (77.8%) and C59R (61.7%); Pfdhps: A437G (31.9%) and K540E/N (4.98%). Combined haplotype analysis revealed 83% of isolates carried resistant alleles, with double and triple mutants predominating. Weak positive selection at Pfdhfr 51 and Pfdhps 436, 540 codons.

articles.headline: Quality assurance frameworks for malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Quality assurance in rapid diagnostic tests for accurate diagnosis of malaria.
articles.authors: Ahmed N, Yadav A, Ranjha R, Anvikar AR, Bharti PK
articles.details: Review of quality assurance measures for ensuring reliability of malaria RDTs. Discusses how inaccurate RDT performance leads to misdiagnosis, inappropriate treatment, increased mortality, and potential emergence of drug-resistant strains. Emphasizes strengthening quality assurance protocols to maintain diagnostic accuracy, enhance disease surveillance, and support global malaria elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Parasite antigen mediates splenic fibroblast adhesion via vimentin
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 mediates infected erythrocyte adhesion to splenic fibroblasts via binding to vimentin.
articles.authors: Zhu Y, Zhang H, Sun Y, Han S, Cheng Y
articles.details: Using P. yoelii model, PyTRAg7 shown to interact with vimentin on mouse splenic fibroblasts, activating NF-κB p65 signaling and increasing ICAM-1 and integrin β1 expression. PyTRAg7 deletion reduced infected RBC adhesion to fibroblasts, lowered splenic parasite burden, delayed parasitemia onset, and prolonged host survival. Preserved splenic architecture with reduced hemozoin deposition.

articles.headline: Comparative assessment of P. falciparum parasite synchronization methods
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of synchronization methods for Plasmodium falciparum: Efficiency, practicality and experimental applicability.
articles.authors: Deng Y, Yin Y, Li Y, Xu J, Han JH, Liu Y, Lu F
articles.details: Evaluated 4 synchronization methods for P. falciparum 3D7 culture: 5% D-sorbitol, Percoll-Sorbitol 60%, discontinuous Percoll-Sorbitol 70%-40%, and Plasmion. Using Giemsa microscopy and flow cytometry with SYBR Green I and 20nm polystyrene fluorescent microspheres (PFMs20). Results: D-sorbitol achieved 88.37% ring-stage purity; 60% PS showed 87.47% late-stage enrichment; 70%-40% PS showed 88.12% binding efficiency; Plasmion yielded 73.37% late-stage binding.

articles.headline: Severe malaria complicated by post-infectious neurological syndrome
articles.title_original: Opsoclonus myoclonus syndrome in an HIV patient, following plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Dominique ETH, Caroline K, Daniel GM, Tchokote SE, Mélanie MA, Laura N, Gishlen I, Sandrine MD, Sandra DGN, Mahaman SAT, Raissa MTJ, Yacouba MN
articles.details: 47-year-old HIV+ woman on HAART presented 2 weeks after severe P. falciparum malaria treatment with multidirectional ocular saccades, trunk myoclonus, aggressive behavior, sleep disturbances, and severe ataxia—diagnosed as post-infectious opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome. High-dose IV methylprednisolone caused partial improvement; subsequent rituximab addition led to complete symptom resolution and restoration of functional autonomy.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine rollout should reduce deaths but mortality rising
articles.title_original: Vaccines mean malaria deaths should be falling - not rising.
articles.details: No abstract provided. Article discusses apparent paradox that malaria vaccine introduction has not led to expected mortality reductions despite widespread vaccine deployment.

articles.headline: Platform enables P. vivax merozoite isolation and immune study
articles.title_original: Accessing invasion-competent Plasmodium vivax merozoites reveals antibody-dependent NK cell activation.
articles.authors: Salvador J, Guselli L, Yeat R, Lay S, Thol T, Ya N, Feufack-Donfack L, Heng S, Bohaud C, Orban A, Baldor L, Tat B, Chitnis C, Cantaert T, Popovici J
articles.details: Established platform for synchronized release and high-yield isolation of invasion-competent P. vivax merozoites from ex vivo cultures using ML10 (cGMP-dependent protein kinase inhibitor) for egress inhibition and liposome-based mechanical extrusion. Achieved >75% merozoite recovery with preserved invasive capacity. Direct evidence that antibody-opsonized P. vivax merozoites activate human natural killer cells via antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance markers absent but multidrug resistance widespread
articles.title_original: Border Region Surveillance of Malaria Drug Resistance, Northern Burundi, 2023-2024.
articles.authors: Niyomwungere D, Sinarinzi P, Caspar E, Thiebaut L, Strubel PE, Tibiri YNG, Ma L, Rurihafi J, Ndizeyimana P, Hakizimana P, Niyizompa N, Witonze A, Ndayiragije V, Ndayisenga F, Ndikumukiza M, Niyonkuru O, Niyonsaba JC, Bangirinama C, Bigirimana JL, Ntahondi N, Mugisha L, Nibakire M, Nzoyikorera N, Sinzinkayo D, Menard D, Nyandwi J
articles.details: During December 2023-June 2024, studied 423 children <5 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum in 8 northern Burundi health facilities. After artemether/lumefantrine (first dose directly observed), 4.5% remained parasitemic on day 3. No pfkelch13 mutations detected. Multidrug resistance widespread: dhfr triple mutant N51I/C59R/S108N nearly fixed (92%), dhps double/triple mutants common (41%/47%), pfcrt CVIET predominant (84%).

articles.headline: Spatial malaria distribution varies with bed net use across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Spatial epidemiology of malaria by bed net utilization in 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a DHS-based study (2013-2023).
articles.authors: Yitageasu G, Demoze L
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study analyzing 126,424 individuals across 11,594 survey clusters in 19 sub-Saharan African countries using DHS data. Pooled malaria prevalence 24.0% (95% CI 18-30%), with regional variation: Central Africa 26%, West Africa 25%, East Africa 20%. Malaria prevalence by bed net use: no net 32.75%, untreated net 28.91%, ITN users 13.23%. Spatial analysis detected significant clustering (Global Moran's I = 0.159, Z-score = 239.06, p ≤ 0.001) with hotspots in Benin, Burkina Faso, Togo, Uganda, Rwanda, and parts of Republic of Congo and Mozambique.

articles.headline: AMA1 haplotypes not associated with malaria symptoms in Bandiagara
articles.title_original: Distribution of Plasmodium falciparum apical membrane antigen-1 cluster one loop haplotypes and their association with malaria symptoms in Bandiagara, Mali.
articles.authors: Ouattara A, Adams M, Niangaly A, Coulibaly D, Kone AK, Traore K, Cissoko Y, Kouriba B, Diallo DA, Plowe CV, Doumbo OK, Djimdé A, Laurens MB, Takala-Harrison S, Thera MA
articles.details: Analysis of ama1 gene sequences from 100 children during a three-year malaria incidence study in Bandiagara, Mali examined whether cluster one loop (c1L) haplotypes were associated with clinical malaria phenotypes. Clinical malaria defined as temperature >37.5°C and parasitemia ≥2,500 parasites/μL. After adjusting for study participant location, specific c1L haplotypes were not associated with malaria symptoms or disease phenotypes. Findings suggest malaria symptoms may be driven by infections with new alleles rather than alleles previously seen by an individual.

articles.headline: African countries integrate climate data for adaptive malaria control strategies
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria control in Africa: country experiences and strategic responses.
articles.authors: Yahouedo AG, Merle CS, Diallo I, Ndiop M, Muhire A, Mukashema C, Mutabazi A, Hakizimana E, Muteba C, Kaseba AN, Tanoh MA, Aimain AS, Coulibaly KA, Razanakotomalala V, Diar MSI, Djaskano MI, Kodindo I, Ayala D, Caminade C, Diouf I, de Chevigny T, Ngou O, Pothin E, Girond F, Tchouatieu AM, Fleutelot E, Noseda V, Deuve JL
articles.details: Synthesis of experiences from six African countries (Chad, Côte d'Ivoire, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Madagascar, Rwanda, Senegal) describing how climate variability has influenced malaria epidemiology. National malaria control programs are adopting adaptive strategies including integrating climate and health data, strengthening surveillance and early warning systems, engaging communities, and promoting multisectoral collaboration. Study identifies systemic barriers: limited data integration capacity, infrastructural deficits, behavioral factors, and financial constraints, emphasizing need for sustained political commitment and long-term investment in climate-resilient health systems.

articles.headline: ZY19489 resistance mechanism creates piperaquine hypersusceptibility in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.authors: Okombo J, Ndiaye T, Qahash T, Moura IMR, Gil-Iturbe E, Hagenah LM, Bridgford JL, Bonatto V, Ward KE, Yeo T, Narwal SK, Orta LV, Anderson I, Dhingra SK, Pasaje CFA, Park H, Kim J, Guido RVC, Jiménez-Diaz MB, Angulo-Barturen I, Niles JC, Mancia F, Uhlemann AC, Mok S, Quick M, Winzeler EA, Leroy D, Llinás M, Thathy V, Fidock DA
articles.details: Characterization of ZY19489, a triaminopyrimidine in Phase Ib clinical trial, reveals blood-stage parasites develop low-grade resistance through novel PfCRT mutation with substantial fitness cost. ZY19489-resistant parasites lose chloroquine resistance and become hypersusceptible to piperaquine (PPQ). Mechanistic studies show ZY19489 blocks mutant PfCRT-mediated efflux of both PPQ and chloroquine, suggesting pairing ZY19489 with PPQ could generate a resistance-refractory combination therapy through an evolutionary trap mechanism.

articles.headline: Scoping review protocol: African stakeholder perspectives on gene drive mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions on gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control among African stakeholders: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Sogodogo A, Diarra S, Keita S, Diallo I, Hartley S, Telly N, Dolo H, Sanogo D, Kané F, Tangara CO, Diakité M, Sangho O, Kayentao K, Fritz H, Winch PJ, Doumbia S
articles.details: Protocol for scoping review of scientific articles and gray literature exploring African stakeholders' knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions regarding gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control. Search strategy spans PubMed, Embase, Science Direct, Cochrane, and Google Scholar with documents selected through four-stage process (identification, deduplication, title/abstract screening, full-text review).
