Findings highlight strain- and pathogenesis-dependent effects of omega-3 supplementation, contrasting prior results in P. berghei ANKA infection and emphasizing the importance of parasite-strain diversity when investigating nutritional interventions in malaria.

articles.headline: Severe malaria and co-infections drive prolonged hospitalization in rural Madagascar
articles.title_original: Clinical Characteristics and Outcomes of Hospitalized Malaria Patients in Rural Madagascar.
articles.authors: Kasprowicz D, Korzeniewski K, Wilczyńska W
articles.details: Three-year retrospective study of 134 hospitalized malaria patients in Madagascar (median age 15 years, 52.2% female): 94.0% P. falciparum, 20.1% classified as severe malaria including 10.4% with cerebral malaria. Co-infections occurred in 52.2% (most common: Schistosoma haematobium 14.2%, typhoid 12.7%). Median hospital stay 2 days (IQR 2-3), with 12.7% prolonged (≥5 days). Prolonged stay significantly associated with cerebral malaria, high parasitemia (≥5%), blood transfusion, and age <15 years (all p≤0.034).

articles.headline: Floodable vegetation and microclimate shape malaria vector density in Amazonian communities
articles.title_original: Reframing vector control: land use and climate shape malaria risk in Amazonian communities.
articles.authors: Acosta AL, Bergo ES, Figueira EAG, Conn JE, Sallum MAM
articles.details: Human landing catches at 70 sites across four Amazonian ecoregions recorded 14,689 mosquito bites. Nyssorhynchus darlingi human biting rate (HBR) was highest in areas with intermediate forest degradation, positively associated with temperature, and negatively with humidity. Floodable vegetation was the strongest land-cover predictor at 500-m scale. Intrazonal variation exceeded interzonal differences. Findings emphasize local environmental conditions and microclimate influence on vector activity, supporting climate-sensitive, spatially targeted vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Multiple malaria control interventions reduce severe disease; single interventions show modest benefit
articles.title_original: Progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria among children in settings receiving different combinations of malaria control interventions in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Okek EJ, Jehopio J, Sande OJ, Musinguzi B, Awor S, Asio J, Le DK, Lutwama J, Alison AK, Bongomin F, Ocan M, Kayondo J
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 studies (9,283,160 individuals) examined progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria under different intervention combinations. Simultaneous deployment of multiple malaria control interventions significantly lowered odds of severe malaria (OR=0.41, 95% CI 0.30–0.56, p<0.001), parasitaemia (OR=0.52, 95% CI 0.38–0.70, p<0.001), uncomplicated malaria (OR=0.64, 95% CI 0.43–0.95, p=0.003), and gametocytes (OR=0.41, 95% CI 0.28–0.60, p<0.001). No significant difference in progression odds between single versus no interventions (OR=0.35, 95% CI 0.11–1.13).

articles.headline: Dengue-3 circulating in Haiti detected via discarded malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.
articles.authors: Matulis GA, Smith HP, Hall G, Katich RS, Delp KL, Douglas CE, Williams J, Hirsch S, Kumar R, Pshea-Smith I, Lilak AA, Okech BA, Ricks K, Stefan CP, Existe A, Kugelman JR, Sutherland I, Dunford J, Boncy J, von Fricken ME, Koehler JW
articles.details: Nucleic acid extracted from discarded malaria RDTs collected 2021–2023 from febrile patients in Haiti (Sud and Ouest Departments): 52 tested positive for flavivirus, with 68 samples ultimately positive for DENV-3 (all collected in 2023). NGS sequencing showed high similarity to Caribbean sequences from 2022–2023. No samples tested positive for alphavirus (0/517) or Oropouche virus (0/293). Demonstrates utility of discarded malaria RDTs for remote arbovirus surveillance among febrile patients, with findings concordant with 2023 DENV-3 outbreak in the Americas.

articles.headline: Malaria parasitemia associates with liver dysfunction and dyslipidemia in adults
articles.title_original: Malaria parasitemia and its association with liver function parameters, and lipid profile among malaria-infected adult patients in western Ethiopia: A comparative cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Checkole N, Cheneke W, Ibrahim T, Bekele S, Teklay B, Tefera M, Gebretsadik A, Fikadu B, Kassahun GB
articles.details: Comparative cross-sectional study in Ethiopia (December 2021–March 2022) of 302 participants (151 malaria patients, 151 controls): 70.86% infected with P. falciparum with parasitemia stratified as low 30.46%, moderate 46.36%, high 23.18%. Mean aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, alkaline phosphatase, and serum bilirubin were significantly higher in malaria patients (p<0.001). Mean total cholesterol, high-density lipoprotein, and low-density lipoprotein were significantly lower in malaria patients (p<0.05).

articles.headline: In silico bispecific antibody design targets Plasmodium invasion pathway
articles.title_original: Computational epitope discovery and in silico design of a bispecific antibody model (COMED hook) to block Plasmodium invasion into erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Köseoğlu AE, Gül N, Duran T, Öztürk C, Hekim N
articles.details: Computational analysis identified 123 antigenic P. falciparum B-cell epitopes, with 58 conserved across species. Two highly conserved Plasmodium epitopes (KHVETWTQRVQNM, LHSNFYIKRF) predicted favorable Fab binding. Of 92 non-conserved human erythrocyte epitopes identified, 12 were selected as host-targeting domains for a bispecific antibody model (COMED hook). Designed antibodies exhibited computationally suggested sequence and docking profiles compatible with dual-targeting construction. Study presents in silico framework for bispecific antibody targeting both Plasmodium and erythrocytes; requires experimental validation.

articles.headline: Flow cytometry detects non-fluorescent Plasmodium parasites in hepatic infection
articles.title_original: Flow cytometry-based evaluation of hepatic infection by non-fluorescent Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Teixeira B, Nunes-Cabaço H, Mota MM, Fontinha D, Prudêncio M
articles.details: Novel flow cytometry method enables quantitative assessment of hepatic cell infection by non-fluorescent Plasmodium parasites using two parasite proteins: heat shock protein 70 (HSP70, parasite cytoplasm) and upregulated in infectious sporozoites 4 (UIS4, parasitophorous vacuole membrane). Method facilitates rapid, cost-effective quantification of hepatic infection and intracellular parasite development without fluorescent reporter genes. Addresses regulatory and technical challenges for evaluating reporter-free whole-sporozoite vaccine candidates and serves as versatile tool for broader malaria research.

articles.headline: Thalassemia shows protective trend against malaria but substantial heterogeneity remains
articles.title_original: Exploring the protective effects of thalassemia against malaria in Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Donkor AB, Nyarko SB, Asare KK, Asa-Atiemo CKM, Kusi KA, Afrane YA, Amoah LE
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 African studies (2013–2025) with eight providing data for meta-analysis: thalassemic individuals had lower odds (OR: 0.856) of developing malaria than non-thalassemic individuals, though association was not statistically significant and characterized by substantial heterogeneity. Alpha thalassemia mechanistically reduces cytoadhesion and rosette formation, enhances immune clearance, with favorable interactions with haptoglobin phenotype (Hp2-1). Findings underscore evolutionary link between hemoglobinopathies and malaria resistance; further research needed on molecular pathways.

articles.headline: Population pharmacokinetics of fosmidomycin-clindamycin-artesunate in Gabonese children and adults
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of fosmidomycin and clindamycin in combination with artesunate for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Gabonese children and adults.
articles.authors: Pfaffendorf C, Dejon-Agobé JC, Edoa JR, Maïga-Ascofaré O, Ahenkan E, Adegnika AA, Ramharter M, Wicha SG, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Open-label randomized trial in Lambaréné, Gabon of 40 patients (aged 3–57 years) with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria receiving oral artesunate (2 mg/kg), fosmidomycin (30 mg/kg), and clindamycin (10 mg/kg) every 12 h for 3 days. One-compartment models best described both drugs. Body weight allometric scaling significantly influenced clearance and volume of distribution for both drugs; body temperature affected fosmidomycin clearance. Weight-based fosmidomycin dosing yielded consistent exposure across age groups, but clindamycin exposure was substantially lower in children <12 years. Dose simulation suggests 12 mg/kg clindamycin in children <35 kg would equalize exposure.

articles.headline: MERISCREEN RDT achieves 94.8% accuracy in Ghanaian children under five
articles.title_original: Evaluation of malaria rapid diagnostic test kit performance in a high-transmission setting: a facility-based cross-sectional study in rural Ghana.
articles.authors: Boadu S, Axame WK, Abanga WA, Saeed FU, Adjei MR, Kubio C
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study in Nandom Municipality, Ghana (July 2024) of 404 children under five: MERISCREEN Malaria Pf HRP-II Ag® RDT sensitivity 98.1% (95% CI 92.6–99.7), specificity 92.6% (95% CI 88.9–95.2), positive predictive value 82.4% (95% CI 75.8–87.5), negative predictive value 99.1% (95% CI 96.6–99.8), overall accuracy 94.8%. Real-world performance marginally lower than manufacturer-reported values. Findings support reliability of prequalified RDT kits for guiding malaria treatment in areas lacking microscopy services, with emphasis on strict storage adherence and periodic quality assessment.

articles.headline: Biocredit RDTs outperform conventional malaria diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Evaluation of NxTek™ Eliminate Malaria-PF test and biocredit rapid diagnostic tests for detecting Plasmodium species in Southern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye Gebremeskele B, Mitiku A, Degefa T
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 384 febrile subjects evaluated three rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) against microscopy and qPCR. Biocredit Pf/Pv RDT showed superior performance with 97.9% sensitivity and 97.4% specificity for P. falciparum and 94.5% sensitivity and 97.5% specificity for P. vivax, compared to NxTek™ Eliminate (87.6% sensitivity, 91.3% specificity) and SD Bioline (51.3% sensitivity, 93.2% specificity for P. falciparum). Biocredit RDTs met WHO diagnostic performance criteria.

articles.headline: Urban malaria heterogeneity shifts across neighborhoods over decade
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal heterogeneity of urban malaria in Libreville, Gabon (2012-2023): neighborhood-level hotspot analysis for micro-stratified control.
articles.authors: Mohamed-Djawad MH, Moutombi Ditombi BC, Ndong Ngomo JM, Mbang Nguema OA, Agbanrin AA, Pongui Ngondza B, Kono HN, Nymane T, Ombago J, Migueba V, James LAJ, Moussavou Mabicka DA, Mihindou CJ, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Ndong Mouity JT, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Bouyou-Akotet MK, M'Bondoukwé NP
articles.details: Analysis of 9,507 children over 12 years (2012–2023) identified substantial neighborhood-level variation in P. falciparum prevalence, ranging from 38.0% in urban areas to 50.9% in semi-rural areas. Spatial hotspot analysis revealed shifting clusters: primarily semi-rural and semi-urban in 2012–2013, shifting toward semi-rural by 2018–2019, and re-emerging in semi-urban areas by 2023. WHO endemicity classification showed increase from mostly mesoendemic neighborhoods in 2015 to 41.5% hyperendemic neighborhoods in 2023.

articles.headline: Review of malaria drug resistance and emerging therapeutic strategies
articles.title_original: Advancing global malaria control: therapeutic strategies, drug discovery, and formulation innovations.
articles.authors: Sarma K, Sarma H, Deka J
articles.details: Review addresses challenges posed by artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) and rising Pfkelch13 resistance mutations, highlighting new drug candidates including Z439, KAF156, MMV371, MMV055/167, INE963, and GSK701. The authors emphasize that new drug regimens are urgently needed to counter partially rising artemisinin resistance. Review also discusses improvements in diagnostic tools and drug formulations as part of integrated strategies for sustained malaria control and eradication efforts.

articles.headline: Microbiota-immune axis framework for African malaria control strategy
articles.title_original: Reinventing malaria control: hierarchical intervention strategies for African settings based on the microbiota-barrier-innate immunity axis.
articles.authors: Zhu Y, Wu S, Qi Y, Wang J
articles.details: Review proposes an integrated 'microbiota-barrier-innate immunity' axis hypothesis explaining how Plasmodium infection disrupts gut microbiota homeostasis, compromises intestinal barrier function, and impairs host innate immunity, exacerbating disease pathology. The authors describe a hierarchical three-tier intervention strategy progressing from foundational nutritional regulation through targeted microbial intervention to precision bioengineering, designed specifically for implementation in resource-limited African settings to address drug resistance and vaccine shortages.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant kelch13 mutations detected in Ethiopia malaria resurgence
articles.title_original: Recurrent parasitemias with artemisinin partial resistance mutations during the 2024 Ethiopia malaria resurgence: a case series.
articles.authors: Geleta D, Brhane BG, Abera A, Belachew M, Sime H, Gebreegziaxher A, Getu M, Ali A, Tesfaye N, Habtetsion M, Kokobe B, Kebede M, Worku Z, Tasew G, Tadesse G, Tollera G, Fola AA, Bailey JA, Juliano JJ, Parr JB, Abte M, Assefa A
articles.details: Case series of 15 patients with recurrent P. falciparum malaria in central Ethiopia identified K13 P441L mutations in 3 patients (20%), a WHO-candidate artemisinin partial resistance marker. Additional drug-resistance markers (pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, pfdhps) were largely fixed in the population, with evidence of clonal expansion or dominance of a related lineage. Findings highlight emergence of ACT-resistance-associated molecular markers during Ethiopia's malaria resurgence.

articles.headline: High-density array identifies 283 immunogenic Plasmodium vivax antigens
articles.title_original: Comprehensive characterization of Plasmodium vivax antigens using a high-density peptide array.
articles.authors: Asawa RR, Hazzard B, Tebben K, Tan J, Cantaert T, Berry A, Tolia N, Popovici J, Serre D
articles.details: High-density peptide array containing 4.2 million peptides covering the complete P. vivax proteome identified 283 commonly immunogenic proteins in symptomatic individuals, including erythrocyte invasion proteins, nucleoporins, and uncharacterized proteins. Asymptomatic individuals showed a unique pattern of antibody response against PIR proteins, potentially associated with protection against clinical vivax malaria. This comprehensive characterization provides an important resource for vaccine candidate selection and development of serological markers.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax cerebral malaria in adult with full recovery
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria caused by Plasmodium vivax in an afrodescendant adult in Colombia: case report.
articles.authors: Sastre-Martínez AD, Gutiérrez MAG, Vallejo-Serna RA, Muñoz-Lombo JP
articles.details: Case report of a 43-year-old woman with P. vivax infection presenting with severe neurological symptoms consistent with cerebral malaria, confirmed by thick blood smear. She was treated with intravenous artesunate followed by chloroquine and primaquine, achieving full recovery. The case illustrates that P. vivax can cause cerebral malaria through mechanical and inflammatory mechanisms with potentially fatal outcomes without early parenteral artesunate and intensive supportive care.

articles.headline: RDT performance context-dependent on transmission intensity and parasite density
articles.title_original: Field and laboratory evaluation of Abbott-Bioline™ Malaria Ag Pf/Pv RDT performance in a high-transmission setting: contrasting results with a low-endemic area.
articles.authors: Fenomanana J, Ravoavy A, Rakotoarisoa M, Platon L, Wade A, Chala B, Tibiri YNG, Muller A, Thiebaut L, Caspar E, Strubel PE, Ménard D, Ratsimbasoa A
articles.details: Evaluation of Abbott-Bioline™ RDT in 218 febrile patients in high-transmission setting (70.2% PCR prevalence, geometric mean parasite density 10,006 parasites/µL) showed 99.1% sensitivity and 93.7% specificity against microscopy, despite identical RDT lots showing only 18% sensitivity at the Thailand-Myanmar border. Against PCR as reference, sensitivity decreased to 73.2% for Abbott-Bioline™ and 74.5% for Parascreen™. Findings demonstrate RDT performance varies substantially by local transmission setting and parasite density.

articles.headline: Plant-based antimalarial drug synthesis mechanism elucidated
articles.title_original: Botanical mystery solved: how plants make a crucial malaria drug.
articles.authors: Thompson B
articles.details: Based on title only: article reports discovery of the botanical synthesis pathway for a crucial antimalarial drug; specific details about the drug, mechanism, plant species, and quantitative findings cannot be confirmed without an abstract.

articles.headline: Malaria coinfection increases intestinal nematode fecundity and persistence
articles.title_original: Coinfection with malaria alters the fecundity and within-host persistence of an intestinal nematode.
articles.authors: Bourbon L, Dusuel A, Groetz E, Rialland M, Roche B, Faivre B, Sorci G
articles.details: Mouse model study of Plasmodium yoelii coinfection with Heligmosomoides polygyrus nematode showed increased worm egg excretion while worm biomass remained similar, reflecting a plastic fecundity response modulated by suppression of host Th2 immunity (reduced IL-4, IL-13 expression and GATA-3+ CD4+ T cell expansion). IL-13 neutralization reproduced the coinfection effect. Coinfection extended patent period with higher cumulative egg excretion up to 99 days post-infection, suggesting malaria increases environmental reservoir of transmissible nematode stages.

articles.headline: Purine salvage pathway targeting for antimalarial drug development
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium falciparum with purine antimetabolites as a therapeutic strategy.
articles.authors: Tashie W, de Koning HP, Duah-Quashie NO, Quashie NB
articles.details: Review discusses P. falciparum's absolute dependence on purine salvage pathway as a therapeutic target, highlighting Plasmodium Equilibrative Nucleoside Transporters (Pf ENTs) as conduits for delivering purine analog drugs. Purine analogs designed to enter parasitized erythrocytes via Pf ENTs and selectively activate within the parasite can disrupt purine metabolism and nucleic acid synthesis. This approach provides a framework for developing novel antimalarial therapies, particularly given rising resistance to current drug classes.

articles.headline: CRISPR-validated K13 C580Y mutation confers artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Functional validation of the Plasmodium falciparum K13 C580Y mutation in recently collected Ethiopian isolates.
articles.authors: Mukherjee A, Assefa AB, Turlo CV, Needham LC, Shoue D, Qahash T, Belachew M, Tadesse D, Kassie E, Berihun M, Brhane BG, Parr JB, Ferdig MT, Adane S, Tesfaye A, Zuromski J, Fola AA, Tasew G, Tollera G, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9 functional study introduced the K13 C580Y substitution (first detected in the Horn of Africa in Ethiopia) into two recently collected Ethiopian P. falciparum clinical isolates. Ring-stage survival assays demonstrated significantly elevated in vitro dihydroartemisinin (DHA) survival in edited parasites compared to isogenic controls, establishing that C580Y confers artemisinin tolerance in contemporary Ethiopian parasite genetic backgrounds. This represents one of the first causal assessments of C580Y in recent African isolates.

articles.headline: Vivax serological markers predict infection risk in falciparum patients
articles.title_original: Predicting risk of Plasmodium vivax microscopy-detected episodes using serological markers in patients with Plasmodium falciparum malaria: a multi-country diagnostic performance evaluation.
articles.authors: Hafidzah M, Degaga TS, Christian M, Alam MS, Hossain MS, Baird JK, Sutano I, Smith L, Argyropoulos D, Amelia AR, Noviyanti R, Robinson LJ, Price RN, Mueller I, Thriemer K, Longley RJ
articles.details: Study evaluated P. vivax serological exposure markers in 244 P. falciparum patients from Ethiopia, Indonesia, and Bangladesh (PRIMA study) to predict microscopy-detected vivax episodes over 63 days. IgG antibodies showed 82% sensitivity but only 27% specificity. A study-specific algorithm improved specificity to 68% with matching sensitivity. Authors conclude markers have limited suitability for guiding universal radical cure but may build community acceptance with strong communication.

articles.headline: Wolbachia detected in wild Anopheles funestus in southeastern Tanzania
articles.title_original: Detection of supergroup B Wolbachia strains and their co-infection with Plasmodium falciparum in wild Anopheles funestus in southeastern Tanzania: implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Mmweteni RE, Philbert AB, Mkandawile G, Abilahi F, Abbasi S, Msemo J, Milonge S, Tumbo FA, Mwalugelo Y, Muyaga LL, Msaky D, Kipekepeke AR, Okumu FO, Kaindoa EW, Baldini F
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 400 mosquitoes from 5 villages in southeastern Tanzania (March–June 2024) found Wolbachia in 46.5% by primary PCR or 70.8% by nested PCR; sequencing identified supergroup B (Dipteran-specific). P. falciparum sporozoites detected in 0.8% of mosquitoes (3/400), with only 1 co-infection with Wolbachia (0.3%). This is the first report of Wolbachia–P. falciparum co-infection in An. funestus in Tanzania.

articles.headline: Delays in malaria care-seeking linked to mortality in under-5s
articles.title_original: Delay in malaria care-seeking for children under five in Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Amoadu M, Alhassan A, Odoom E
articles.details: Systematic review following JBI and PRISMA guidelines on care-seeking delays for malaria in children <5 in sub-Saharan Africa. Prevalence of delayed care-seeking ranged from 2% to 95.8%. Key barriers included financial constraints, travel distances, reliance on traditional treatments, and perception of mildness. Delays associated with increased mortality, progression to severe malaria, and serious neurologic and hematologic complications.

articles.headline: Stochastic metapopulation model captures malaria dynamics in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Assessing the impact of climate and control interventions on spatio-temporal malaria dynamics using a stochastic metapopulation model.
articles.authors: Angelakis A, Beloconi A, Nyawanda BO, Khagayi S, Kariuki S, Munga S, Munywoki PK, Bigogo G, Vounatsou P
articles.details: Process-based stochastic metapopulation model developed for western Kenya (2008–2019) incorporating immunity, infectivity, migration, vector/host mobility, climate variability, and interventions as joint drivers. Model accurately captured small-scale heterogeneity at subpopulation level. Framework is generalizable to other endemic regions and can support localized forecasting and intervention planning under future climate scenarios.

articles.headline: LL-37 antimicrobial peptide exhibits antiplasmodial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: LL-37 selectively targets Plasmodium-infected erythrocytes and exhibits antimalarial activity.
articles.authors: He X, Zhang Y, Lou J, Wu J, Xu S, Zhu G, Tang J, Fang Y, Cao J
articles.details: Human antimicrobial peptide LL-37 demonstrated in vitro antiplasmodial activity against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant parasites by targeting infected RBCs through phosphatidylserine externalization and cholesterol depletion. Elevated plasma LL-37/CRAMP levels observed in malaria patients and infected mice. Exogenous LL-37/CRAMP in mice reduced parasitemia and improved survival, but therapeutic administration after infection establishment showed limited efficacy due to rapid peptide degradation in vivo.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria causes permanent neurologic deficits in 15% of children
articles.title_original: Prevalence and risk factors of gross neurologic deficits in children after severe malaria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Okullo AE, Ouma S, John CC, van Hensbroek MB, Ojiambo KO, Otike C, Kinengyere AA, Conroy AL, Ocan M, Obuku EA, Idro R
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 41 studies from 16 countries (Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia) involving 11,635 children. Gross neurologic deficits (motor, movement, sensory, speech) at hospital discharge: 15.2% (95%CI 11.5–18.8%) in cerebral malaria vs 2.4% (95%CI 2.0–2.8%) in broader severe malaria; prevalence decreased to 5.7% after 12 months in cerebral malaria. Sub-Saharan Africa had highest prevalence (14.2%) vs East Asia & Pacific (2.3%). Risk factors: profound coma, coma ≥48 hours, multiple convulsions, hypoglycemia, acute kidney injury.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria parasitemia increases splenic rupture risk in trauma
articles.title_original: Chronic Malaria Is Associated With Trauma-related Splenic Rupture Requiring Splenectomy.
articles.authors: Shanti PAI, Alexander K, Candrawati F, Andries B, Kambuaya NN, Rini H, Amelia AR, Puspitasari AM, Amalia R, Rahmayenti DA, Leonardo L, Prayoga P, Trianty L, Pava Z, Kenangalem E, Auburn S, Price RN, Laksanawati IS, Buffet PA, Noviyanti R, Anstey NM, Poespoprodjo JR, Kho S
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of trauma-related splenectomy patients in Timika, Papua, Indonesia (2015–2021) found PCR-detectable asymptomatic parasitemia (P. falciparum or P. vivax) in 87.9% (29/33) of splenectomy patients vs 38.6% (697/1807) in a 2013 household population survey (P<0.0001). Asymptomatic parasitemia is significantly associated with splenic rupture following trauma, suggesting chronic infection increases vulnerability to trauma-related splenic complications.

articles.headline: False-positive RDTs occur in 11% of malaria-negative children
articles.title_original: False-positive Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests are Prevalent Among Children Under 5 Years of Age in Uganda.
articles.authors: Cassidy CA, Shook-Sa BE, Boyce RM, Ciccone EJ, Gower EW, Young AM, Edwards JK
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 2018–2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey data in children <5 with paired mRDT and microscopy (n=6,786). Prevalence of false-positive mRDTs (mRDT+/microscopy−) was 10.7% (849/6,786), strongly correlated with region-level transmission intensity. Prevalence higher with recent fever (PD 17.2%), recent antimalarial use (14.7%), and comorbid anemia (8.1%); lower with recent antibiotics (−17.6%). Combined clinical/environmental/household model predicted false-positives (weighted AUC 0.79) better than individual models.

articles.headline: Duffy negativity protects vivax, but age attenuates polymorphism effects
articles.title_original: How Duffy Blood Group (FY) Polymorphism and Age Modulate Vivax Malaria Risk at the Community Level: A Population-based Retrospective Cohort Study in the Amazon.
articles.authors: Prete CA Jr, de Sousa TN, Naziazeno IM, Puça MCSB, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Johansen IC, Paula GA, Ferreira MU, Corder RM
articles.details: Retrospective cohort study of 1,737 Amazonians contributing 7,878.9 person-years of follow-up exposed to P. vivax transmission. P. vivax incidence heterogeneous (average 25.8 cases/100 person-years; range 0–11 cases per person). Fy-negativity remains a major resistance trait contributing to "structural zeroes" in infection data. Fya/Fyb polymorphism differences in infection risk attenuate with age as clinical immunity develops; Fy(b+) individuals develop clinical immunity faster than Fy(a+).

articles.headline: Praziquantel shows moderate antimalarial activity in asymptomatic malaria
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Praziquantel for Treatment of Plasmodium falciparum Infection in Asymptomatic Gabonese Adults: A Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Mischlinger J, Pechmann K, Werner J, Zinsou AH, Vogler M, Ndoumba WN, Okwu DG, von Wedel C, Kurth F, Kreidenweiss A, Bachmann A, Adegnika AA, Zoleko-Manego R, Kremsner PG, Sarpong F, Badjina L, Ramharter M, Mombo-Ngoma G
articles.details: Double-blind RCT (CORMA-MAL) in Lambaréné, Gabon of 44 semi-immune adults with asymptomatic P. falciparum parasitemia randomized to placebo or 40 mg/kg praziquantel once daily for 3 days. Praziquantel showed statistically significant hourly reduction in microscopically-determined parasitemia (β=−0.006, P<0.001) vs placebo (β=−0.0013, P=0.3). Parasite clearance by day 7: 41% (9/22) in praziquantel arm vs 27% (6/22) in placebo. Effect is only moderate compared to current first-line treatments.

articles.headline: P. vivax blood-stage antigens show high diversity under selection
articles.title_original: Extreme Genetic Diversity and Signatures of Balancing Selection in Plasmodium vivax Blood-Stage Antigens.
articles.authors: Bareng APN, Naung MT, Martin E, Mehra S, Munro JE, Takala Harrison S, Barry AE
articles.details: Analysis of 289 of 353 P. vivax whole-genome sequences from Asia, Americas, and Oceania examining genetic diversity of 14 vaccine candidate antigens. AMA1, CyRPA, RBP1a, and P41 exhibited high genetic diversity and signatures of balancing selection on both linear and 3D structures across all endemic regions, indicating immune selection. CSP, TRAP, MSP4, MSP9, MSP10, GAMA, S12, ARP, S25, and S28 showed low to intermediate diversity. Vaccine strain Sal-1 has extremely low global frequency.

articles.headline: G6PD testing and tafenoquine expansion reduces vivax costs in Brazil
articles.title_original: [Expansion of the use of technologies to reduce cases of malaria due to Plasmodium vivax in the Brazilian Amazon: a budget impact analysis].
articles.authors: Costa MGSD, Zimmermann I, Costa ACCD, Moraes AC, Pinto M
articles.details: Budget impact analysis for Brazilian SUS (5-year horizon) evaluating incorporation of tafenoquine and quantitative G6PD point-of-care testing for vivax patients <16 years and by weight, plus expanded G6PD testing for primaquine-eligible patients in the Brazilian Amazon. Incremental budget impact: BRL 12.5 million. Model predicted 9% reduction in hospitalization costs and 11% reduction in recurrence costs, with test reagent strip costs impacting results.

articles.headline: Angola P. falciparum resistance markers analyzed
articles.title_original: Molecular markers of antimalarial drug resistance in pfk13, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, and pfdhps genes of Plasmodium falciparum in a rural municipality in Cubal, Benguela Province, Angola (2022-2023).
articles.authors: Mediavilla A, García X, Martínez-Vallejo P, Molina-de la Fuente I, Febrer-Sendra B, Silgado A, Francisco K, Maturana CR, Martins JF, Nindia A, Martínez-Campreciós J, Aznar ML, Oliveira-Souto I, Molina I, Sulleiro E, Berzosa P
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 139 P. falciparum infections from Angola (2022–2023). Sequenced pfk13, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, and pfdhps resistance markers. Found 10% of isolates with pfk13 mutations but none validated for artemisinin resistance; pfmdr1 Y184F in 43%; pfdhfr/pfdhps triple mutation in 77.2%, with 58.4% "partially resistant" (IRNG) and 17.8% "fully resistant" (IRNGE) haplotypes, indicating potential risks to artemether-lumefantrine and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine efficacy.

articles.headline: pfhrp2-negative P. falciparum show distinct cytoadherence patterns
articles.title_original: Phenotypic and genotypic characterization of Plasmodium falciparum field isolates lacking pfhrp2 gene.
articles.authors: Bendezu J, Larrauri-Aguilar P, Bautista R, Villasis E, Vinetz JM, Gamboa D
articles.details: In vitro study of 5 P. falciparum field isolates from Peruvian Amazon (3 pfhrp2-positive, 2 pfhrp2-negative). pfhrp2+ isolates exhibited rosetting or chondroitin sulfate A cytoadherence; pfhrp2- isolates showed HUVEC cytoadherence. pfhrp2 gene expression was higher in positive isolates; pfhrp3 expression was similar or lower. All isolates demonstrated genomic stability over one year of culture.

articles.headline: Toxoplasma, Plasmodium, Leishmania co-infections alter immune responses
articles.title_original: Unravelling complex interactions during Toxoplasma, Plasmodium, and Leishmania co-infections in French Guiana.
articles.authors: Néron K, Fesel C, Demar M, Pied S
articles.details: Cross-sectional serological study (n=253) from French Guiana (2012–2022). Approximately 60% of patients were seropositive for ≥2 pathogens, with 2.4% having concomitant infections. Significant differences in cytokine levels, particularly IL-10, IL-17, IL-33, and IFN-α, were observed between single and co-infected patients.

articles.headline: Climate change threatens An. stephensi habitat in Iran
articles.title_original: Suitable ecological niches of invasive malaria vector under present and projected climatic conditions in South of Iran.
articles.authors: Bozorg-Omid F, Abbasi M, Yaghoobi-Ershadi MR, Hanafi-Bojd AA
articles.details: Ecological niche modeling (MaxEnt) of Anopheles stephensi distribution in Hormozgan Province, Iran, using 72 occurrence points and bioclimatic data. Model predicts significant habitat loss under future climate scenarios, with stable areas mainly in Bashagard County (AUC 0.765, TSS 0.519). Altitude and precipitation are key factors influencing species distribution.

articles.headline: Bangladesh malaria control progress with emerging resistance challenges
articles.title_original: Malaria in Bangladesh, 2000-2025: Navigating the Headwinds From Control to Elimination.
articles.authors: Mamun AA, Kayenat MSA, Islam KAMI
articles.details: Narrative review of Bangladesh malaria epidemiology (2000–2025). Country achieved >90% case reduction (2008–2020) with P. falciparum remaining dominant in Chittagong Hill Tracts. Emerging biological challenges include non-kelch13 artemisinin-partial resistance in P. falciparum and P. vivax radical cure complications amid G6PD deficiency; ecological and social challenges include outdoor-biting vectors, cross-border mobility, and asymptomatic infections.

articles.headline: Healthcare workers' vaccine acceptance in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Healthcare workers' beliefs, readiness, and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Southwest Nigeria: addressing misconceptions and uptake barriers.
articles.authors: Esan DT, Adeleke TO, Rasaq NO, Ajisafe ZA, Ramos CG
articles.details: Study examining healthcare workers' beliefs, readiness, and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Southwest Nigeria, addressing misconceptions and identifying uptake barriers.

articles.headline: RTS,S/AS01 vaccine budget impact in Uganda's immunization program
articles.title_original: The Cost and Budget Impact of Malaria Vaccine Introduction in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ochanda PN, Ekirapa-Kiracho E, Mayora C, Ssempala R, Nakimuli B, Namirembe B, Ssennyonjo A, Ssengooba F
articles.details: Budget impact analysis of RTS,S/AS01 vaccine introduction in Uganda. Treatment costs for complicated malaria in children <5 decreased from $1.1 million to $297,465. Annual vaccination cost: $29 million (24% of immunization budget). Over 5 years, total budget impact was $145.5 million with net cost of $141 million, ranging from $106.3 million at lower to $175.6 million at higher coverage levels.

articles.headline: SP chemoprevention drives dhps A581G spread in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Modeling the Impact of Malaria Chemoprevention with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine on the spread of Plasmodium falciparum dhps A581G mutation.
articles.authors: Mziray N, Okell LC, Kiware S, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Ainea N, Ishengoma DS, Nyerere N
articles.details: Deterministic age-structured mathematical model simulating sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine chemoprevention impact on dhps A581G spread. At 45% coverage, dhps A581G increased from 11% to 20.6% (three monthly cycles) or 27.8% (five monthly cycles). Coverage level explained 58% of resistance spread (ηp² = 0.58), while treatment cycle frequency explained 9.3% (ηp² = 0.093). Results highlight trade-off between immediate public health gains and long-term resistance risk.

articles.headline: Low R21/Matrix-M vaccine uptake in Sudan linked to barriers
articles.title_original: Suboptimal uptake of the malaria vaccine in Sudan: what's behind the blinds?
articles.authors: Malik EM, El-Hassan HM, Ali MAA, Alsoni KAA, Alnair NMA, Noureddin AA, Aladani ISA, Adam MAB, Saad FM, Elkhidir TA, Alshareif BA, Khalafalla M, Al-Haddad NFK, Mohammed NYE, Eltayeb DIH, Ahmed N
articles.details: Cross-sectional mixed-methods study of 416 mothers/caregivers in Sudan. R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine uptake was 36.7% (95% CI 31.1–42.8%, 94 of 256 eligible children). Barriers included limited awareness (57.7% aware of vaccine), low knowledge of dose requirements (36.7% knew number of doses, 25.7% knew starting age), vaccine stockouts, and geographic access challenges. Attitudes toward vaccination were generally positive.

articles.headline: Hemozoin-associated proteins mediate cerebral malaria barrier disruption
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum hemozoin-associated biomolecules induce brain endothelial cell barrier disruption in an in vitro model of cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Crotty KA, Clotea I, Ueberheide B, Cammer M, Sall J, Liang A, Rodriguez A
articles.details: In vitro study demonstrating that hemozoin-associated biomolecules, not hemozoin crystals themselves, cause human brain microvascular endothelial cell barrier disruption in cerebral malaria. Protease treatment inhibited the barrier-disrupting activity, indicating an important role for proteins, while nuclease treatment had no effect, suggesting proteins are the key mediators of endothelial barrier compromise.

articles.headline: RTS,S/AS01 fourth dose timing optimized for maximum impact
articles.title_original: Timing of the fourth dose of RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine in perennial settings: a modelling study.
articles.authors: Figueroa-Downing D, Kelly SL, Cavelan A, Penny MA, Malinga J
articles.details: Mathematical modeling (OpenMalaria) of RTS,S/AS01 vaccination schedules in perennial transmission settings (baseline P. falciparum prevalence 20%). Primary series averted 73–93% of uncomplicated malaria cases; fourth dose could avert additional 8–38% uncomplicated and 9–36% severe cases. Optimal timing: fourth dose at 15–21 months (6–12 month interval after third dose) achieves largest case reduction across transmission intensities.

articles.headline: Extreme weather disrupts malaria prevention in pregnancy in Kenya
articles.title_original: Extreme weather events and malaria prevention in pregnancy: a mediation analysis of antenatal care and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine use in two malaria-endemic counties of Kenya.
articles.authors: Matanda DJ, Mchenga M, Amadi JA, Nakuya Z, Okoth O, Otieno B, Obare F
articles.details: Cross-sectional mediation analysis in Kisumu and Migori counties, Kenya. Extreme weather events reduced IPTp₃+ completion (three or more sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine doses) from 58.8% (unexposed) to 49.5% (shock-exposed) primarily through reduced antenatal care (≥4 visits) attendance (coefficient −0.23, 95% CI −0.41 to −0.05). Antenatal care strongly predicted IPTp₃+ uptake (1.71, 95% CI 1.42–2.00), with 76.6% of the shock effect mediated through antenatal care disruption.

articles.headline: P. vivax-dengue coinfection causes ischemic stroke
articles.title_original: Ischemic stroke during dengue-Plasmodium vivax coinfection in a young woman: a case report.
articles.authors: Nati-Castillo HA, Saavedra-Torres JS, Cardenas JM, Gaibor-Pazmiño A, Ortiz-Prado E, Izquierdo-Condoy JS
articles.details: Case report of 23-year-old woman presenting with acute ischemic stroke (left ACA territory) following infection with Plasmodium vivax (8200 parasites/μL) and dengue virus. MRI confirmed acute infarct; no traditional stroke risk factors. Patient treated with artesunate-primaquine and supportive care; neurologic function improved with rehabilitation.

articles.headline: P. falciparum PPCS complex targeted by pantothenate analogs
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium falciparum PPCS is a unique heteromeric complex with prokaryote-like activity and is a target of pantothenate analogs.
articles.authors: Liu X, Jin R, Domingo R, Kotzé TJ, Guan J, Hamann AR, Chu A, Hart-Smith G, Spry C, Sexton AE, Crooke Z, Venkadeswaran A, Yuan X, Creek DJ, Auclair K, Corry B, Strauss E, Saliba KJ
articles.details: Biochemical study characterizing two phosphopantothenoylcysteine synthetases (PPCSs) in P. falciparum that form a unique prokaryote-like heteromeric complex. Unlike eukaryotic PPCSs, the P. falciparum complex cannot use ATP and requires CTP. Multiple pantothenate analogs inhibit this complex, offering potential for novel antimalarial drug development exploiting its unique biochemical features.

articles.headline: Stakeholder preferences for next-generation pregnancy malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Improving malaria chemoprevention coverage in pregnancy: Surveying stakeholder preferences for new product profiles and community-delivery approaches across five African countries.
articles.authors: Audibert C, Borghini-Fuhrer I, El Gaaloul M, Ramachandruni H, Tchouatieu AM, Majeres Lugand M
articles.details: Multi-country survey (5 African nations) of 254 stakeholders—pregnant women (n=75), nurses/CHWs (n=53), clinicians (n=75), policymakers (n=51)—on ideal intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) product characteristics. Safety in all trimesters rated most important, followed by short duration and fewer tablets daily. Clinicians/policymakers favored long-acting injectables (3+ months coverage) and community health worker delivery; discrete choice experiment favored products without food restrictions.

articles.headline: Systematic review protocol for Nigerian pediatric malaria burden
articles.title_original: Burden, risk factors, and clinical outcomes of pediatric malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis protocol.
articles.authors: Ibrahim OR, Abdulkareem J, Issa A, Alao MA
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis protocol pooling laboratory-confirmed pediatric malaria data across Nigeria using WHO case definitions and random-effects modeling. Primary outcome: prevalence of pediatric malaria overall and stratified by severity (severe vs. uncomplicated). Secondary outcomes: factors influencing clinical presentation and outcomes stratified by age, sex, socioeconomic status, and geography. Search spans six databases from inception through June 2025.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial gene dysregulation in P. falciparum severe malaria
articles.title_original: Differential expression of mitochondria-associated genes in clinical samples of Plasmodium falciparum showing severe manifestations.
articles.authors: Gujarati S, Singal BR, Gupta D, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: Transcriptome profiling of mitochondrial genes in 22 P. falciparum clinical isolates (uncomplicated, n=6; cerebral malaria, n=4; hepatic dysfunction, n=12) using custom microarray and strand-specific RNA-seq. Cerebral malaria and hepatic dysfunction clusters showed distinct sense and antisense transcript patterns. Cerebral malaria notably displayed upregulation of tricarboxylic acid cycle and electron transport genes despite reduced overall mitochondrial activity.

articles.headline: Global HLA coverage of P. falciparum CD8 T cell vaccine epitopes
articles.title_original: Population HLA coverage of experimentally characterized P. falciparum CD8 (+) T cell epitopes as potential components of a multi-epitope malaria vaccine construct: an in silico insight.
articles.authors: Siame C, Abdul-Mumin R, Agyekum G, Akuffo L, Afari E, Yankey CAB, Kusi KA
articles.details: In silico analysis of 16 experimentally validated CD8+ T cell epitopes from three P. falciparum antigens (CSP, AMA-1, TRAP) assessed for HLA binding across 14 geographic subregions and 78 countries. Eight epitopes showed 100% sequence conservation; CSPep5 achieved highest population coverage (>85% globally, 93.35% West Africa), while CSPep4 showed minimal coverage (<5%). Findings support designing pan-epitope malaria vaccines integrating HLA promiscuity with parasite conservation.

articles.headline: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis following P. falciparum malaria recovery
articles.title_original: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis following malaria: expanding the spectrum of post-malarial neurological syndromes.
articles.authors: Deng S, Wang W, Lu W, Wang S
articles.details: First antibody-confirmed case of anti-NMDAR encephalitis developing 11 days after confirmed P. falciparum malaria parasite clearance in a 40-year-old man. Patient presented with seizures, altered consciousness, and acute psychosis; anti-NMDAR antibodies detected in cerebrospinal fluid via immunofluorescence and cell-based assay. No concurrent infection or malignancy. Rapid clinical improvement with first-line immunotherapy, expanding the spectrum of post-malarial neurological syndromes.

articles.headline: Cytokine immune profiles associated with malaria at delivery
articles.title_original: Cytokine-Driven Immune Phenotypes at Delivery as Indicators of Malaria Infection Among Primigravidae in Burkina Faso: An Exploratory Analysis.
articles.authors: Traore O, Rouamba T, Zango SH, Sorgho H, Valea I, Traore-Coulibaly M, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: Study of 33 primigravid women (mean age 19 years) in Burkina Faso assessed at childbirth for antibody responses to P. falciparum antigens (PfCSP, PfAMA-1, EBA-175) and plasma cytokines (IL-4, IL-10, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ). Three distinct immune clusters identified: low-inflammatory, TNF-α-dominant, and highly pro-inflammatory. All women in most pro-inflammatory cluster were P. falciparum-positive at delivery (p=0.04), indicating cytokine patterns reflect concurrent infection.

articles.headline: Deep learning for automated malaria detection in blood smears
articles.title_original: Automated Malaria Ring Form Classification in Blood Smear Images Using Ensemble Parallel Neural Networks.
articles.authors: Pongpanitanont P, Suttidate N, Nuinoon M, Khampeeramao N, Laymanivong S, Janwan P
articles.details: Binary classification model using parallel neural networks (CNN branch for morphology + multi-head self-attention for global features) trained on 27,558 balanced erythrocyte image crops (128×128 pixels) from Kaggle dataset. Evaluated via 10-fold cross-validation and independent 10% hold-out test. Model#1 achieved ROC-AUC ~0.99, 95.67% cross-validation accuracy, and 97% test accuracy (macro F1-score 0.97). External validation required before clinical implementation.

articles.headline: Intravenous drug abuse with falciparum malaria and HIV
articles.title_original: Intravenous drug abuse - a concern for falciparum malaria along with HIV.
articles.authors: Makkar A, Panda PK
articles.details: No abstract available. Based on title: article addresses intravenous drug abuse as a concern in the context of falciparum malaria and concurrent HIV infection. Specific study design, population, and findings cannot be determined from title alone.

articles.headline: Community co-creation of malaria care in conflict-affected Cameroon
articles.title_original: Co-creation of community-based innovations to improve access to malaria treatment in conflict-affected regions of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Omam LA, Tanue EA, Omam EN, Metuge A, Nganmou I, Epie C, Kebam G, Ako S, Bessem ME, Hawkings H, Maxwell K, Zoungrana Y, Berryman E, Jarman E, Noukeme BM
articles.details: Participatory co-creation workshop (October 2021) in South West and Littoral regions of Cameroon developed three community-based malaria interventions informed by prior formative research across 80 conflict-affected communities. Community Health Participatory Approach replaced top-down dialogue with inclusive, participatory model. Health Voucher System provided subsidized access to malaria testing, treatment, and transport. Supportive Supervision Model enhanced community health worker performance. Concerns remain about scalability and sustainability.

articles.headline: Ethiopia's malaria control: systemic and operational gaps analysis
articles.title_original: Resurgence of malaria in Ethiopia: a national landscape analysis of systemic and operational gaps.
articles.authors: Sasie SD, Aragaw FM, Assele DD, Argaw ZM, Afele MA
articles.details: National landscape analysis assessing six operational malaria management domains combining systematic literature review (2016-2025; 24 included studies) with stakeholder consultations. Key findings: ITN ownership ~64% with lower coverage in high-burden areas; IRS in ~50% of targeted zones facing pyrethroid resistance; digital surveillance in ~80% of facilities with reporting delays and data quality issues; RDT performance compromised by stock-outs, expired tests, and pfhrp2/3 deletions affecting HRP2 sensitivity. Systemic gaps include fragmented systems and limited community engagement.

articles.headline: Malaria-helminth coinfection rare in Uganda schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Coinfection with Malaria and Intestinal Helminths among School-Aged Children in Nwoya District, Northern Uganda: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: McGowan K, Bongomin F, Otika D, O'Connell C, Matejak N, Hamer DH
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 190 school-aged children (median 13 years) in Nwoya District, Uganda used modified Kato-Katz microscopy and P. falciparum RDTs to detect parasites. Although 75.3% tested RDT-positive for P. falciparum, only 13.2% had microscopically detected parasitemia. Intestinal helminths were uncommon (3.2%): Ascaris lumbricoides 0.5%, Hymenolepis nana 1.6%, Schistosoma mansoni 1.1%. All 6 children with helminth infections were RDT-positive for malaria, but the low overall coinfection rate suggests effective mass drug administration for helminth control.

articles.headline: High P. falciparum parasitemia linked to hematological and hepatic changes
articles.title_original: Plasmodium Parasitemia and Its Relationship With Hematological, Liver, and Kidney Function of Patients With Falciparum Malaria in the Eastern Region, Ghana.
articles.authors: Badu A, Darban I, Mensah K, Antwi MH, Appeaning M, Kwakye AS, Buckman TA, Owusu VK, Mintah DT, Agyei RA, Yeboah C, Acorlor D
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 362 participants (71.8% adults, 69.3% female) in Ghana measured parasitemia, hematological parameters, and liver/kidney function. Children <5 years had higher rates of high parasitemia (51.9%). Adults with high parasitemia showed significantly lower hemoglobin, RBC count, MCV, MCH, and platelets but higher ALT and AST than those with lower parasitemia (p<0.05). No significant variations in urea or creatinine by parasitemia level. High parasitemia was associated with distinct hematological and hepatic alterations.

articles.headline: Data-driven model predicts malaria control strategies for imported cases
articles.title_original: Data-driven modeling of imported malaria in Morocco and the impact of population migration.
articles.authors: Toufga H, Benrhmach G, Lhous M, Tridane A
articles.details: Study applied three parameter-estimation methods (least-squares fit, Extended Kalman Filter, LSTM neural networks) to Morocco malaria data (1990–2023) in a compartmental transmission model accounting for human immigration. Optimal control analysis tested awareness campaigns, prophylactic treatment, infected-population treatment, and insecticide vector control. Numerical results showed significant reductions in human and vector infections through combined control strategies, emphasizing the importance of surveillance and control policies accounting for migration.

articles.headline: Pregnant women underutilize malaria prevention despite known efficacy
articles.title_original: Factors influencing the utilisation of malaria preventive services during pregnancy in Ile Ife, Nigeria: a mixed method approach.
articles.authors: Inyangudo GN, Ojo TO, Oluwabunmi F, Stanley E, Olaniyi B
articles.details: Mixed-method cross-sectional study of 200 pregnant women in Ile-Ife, Nigeria found only 34% had good knowledge of malaria preventive services. Although 62.5% owned long-lasting insecticide nets (LLINs), only 47.5% reported current use. Intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine uptake was 50.8% for at least one dose and 7% for three or more doses. Multivariable analysis showed significant associations between IPTp-SP uptake and occupation, knowledge, and gravidity. Barriers included stockouts, transportation costs, and preference for faith-based providers over antenatal care.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention with SPAQ generally safe in Ghana
articles.title_original: Incidence and trends of adverse drug reactions during seasonal malaria chemoprevention among children: evidence from Ghana's pharmacovigilance database, 2015-2023.
articles.authors: Otoo BAF, Opoku-Mireku M, Seaneke SK, Darko DM, Sabblah GT, Bonful HA
articles.details: Analysis of 2,097 adverse drug reactions (ADRs) to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine (SPAQ) reported to Ghana's FDA database (2015–2023) showed 51.2% involved male children and 60.3% occurred in those aged 13–59 months. Most common ADRs were fever (22.7%), vomiting (21.5%), and diarrhea (16.9%); serious reactions accounted for 8.6%. ADR incidence per 100,000 doses declined from 220.63 in 2015 to 0.34 in 2023. Caregivers submitted two-thirds of reports, highlighting low professional reporting despite SPAQ's overall safety profile.

articles.headline: COX1 barcoding reveals cryptic Anopheles diversity in Indonesia
articles.title_original: Malaria Vector Surveillance in Indonesia: COX1 Phylogenetic Reveals Monophyletic Clades and Cryptic Diversity in Anopheles Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Senjarini K, Hasanah LNU, Wathon S, Oktarianti R, Labes A
articles.details: Anopheles mosquitoes collected from two sites in Indonesia (Bangsring and Hargowilis) underwent morphological and COX1 molecular identification. Phylogenetic analysis using Neighbor-Joining methods revealed eight monophyletic clades with ≥99% bootstrap support for six. An. aconitus and An. minimus specimens from Hargowilis were molecularly confirmed as An. flavirostris, indicating morphological misidentification. An. sundaicus dominated Bangsring (68%), while An. flavirostris dominated Hargowilis (13%), highlighting the need for targeted vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Pfgch1 gene copy number variation correlates with antifolate resistance
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum GTP-cyclohydrolase 1 (Pfgch1) Gene Copy Number Variation and Its Implications in antimalarial resistance from Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sen A, Sharma P, Sharma S, Srivastava B, Singh K, Anvikar A, Bharti PK
articles.details: Quantitative PCR analysis of 101 P. falciparum isolates from Northeast India revealed significant variation in Pfgch1 gene copy number, with most isolates carrying a single copy. Higher copy numbers were significantly associated with mutations in Pfdhfr and Pfdhps genes (p<0.05). A statistically significant positive correlation was observed between Pfdhfr/Pfdhps mutations and increased Pfgch1 copy numbers, supporting the hypothesis that Pfgch1 amplification compensates for folate-pathway mutations and contributes to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance.

articles.headline: PbSERA3 protease essential for Plasmodium hepatic merozoite egress
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei serine repeat antigen 3 (PbSERA3) is required for hepatic merozoite egress.
articles.authors: Singh D, Patri S, Veeda N, Verma CK, Kavati A, Segireddy RR, Kolli SK, Arun Kumar K
articles.details: Using Flp/FRT conditional mutagenesis in P. berghei, Pbsera3-edited sporozoites invaded hepatocytes and developed normally in late liver stages but failed to initiate blood-stage infection, demonstrating PbSERA3's essentiality in hepatic egress. PbSERA3 is processed in ookinete and sporozoite stages yielding two proteolytic products, with localization on ookinete and sporozoite surfaces under non-permeabilized conditions. As PbSERA3 and its P. falciparum ortholog PfSERA6 are substrates for subtilisin-like protease (SUB1), inhibitors preventing PbSERA3/PfSERA6 maturation may have therapeutic implications.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance and behavioral shifts undermine malaria vector control
articles.title_original: Musings from a Vectosaur: Malaria in 2026.
articles.authors: Lluberas MF
articles.details: Opinion piece analyzing the 2025 Africa Malaria Progress Report's observation that malaria programs remain "off-course" despite two decades of reported progress. The article argues modern malaria control over-relies on two tools—long-lasting insecticide nets (LLINs) and indoor residual spraying (IRS)—neither designed for indefinite sustained use. Under sustained pressure, mosquito populations exhibit increasing insecticide resistance and shifts in feeding/resting behavior circumventing indoor interventions. Climate change and expansion of Anopheles stephensi in African urban environments add complexity; integrated vector management combining surveillance, larval source management, and coordinated adult control under unified programs is proposed.

articles.headline: Malaria and G6PD deficiency both significantly impact childhood anemia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the impact of malaria and erythrocyte G6PD deficiency on anaemia outcomes among children in Jos Northcentral Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoli CA, Wamu DI, Deffi F, Leshak PD
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 100 children aged 1–5 years in Nigeria (2023) used Giemsa microscopy to detect malaria and colorimetric assays for G6PD activity. Of 100 children, 55% had malaria parasitemia and 49% were G6PD deficient; 24 of the 55 malaria-positive children (43.6%) were also G6PD deficient. Twenty-eight children (28%) were anemic. Malaria and G6PD deficiency together had a significant impact on anemia outcomes (F=7.327, p=0.002), with 37.5% of G6PD-deficient malaria-positive children anemic.

articles.headline: Severe malaria mortality high; convulsions and anemia key predictors
articles.title_original: Treatment outcome and associated factors of severe malaria among admitted children in the Gambella Region, Southwest Ethiopia: a retrospective study design.
articles.authors: Animut A, Bekana T, Siraj SZ, Ponpetch K, Tasew G, Kinfe E, Mekonnen Z
articles.details: Retrospective study of 417 children (mean age 4.03 years, SD±2.11) admitted to Gambella General Hospital, Ethiopia with severe malaria (May 2022 to May 2024). Overall case fatality rate was 9.11%; higher fatality rates observed with severe anemia (31.8%), convulsions (27%), pulmonary edema (19.7%), coma (18.5%), acute kidney injury (18.4%), and comorbidities (16.9%). Majority (91.7%) had P. falciparum, accounting for 34 of 38 deaths (89.5%). Multivariable logistic regression identified convulsion, severe anemia, and comorbidities as significantly associated with mortality.

articles.headline: Drug pressure drives pyrimethamine resistance fixation in human malaria
articles.title_original: Contrasting evolutionary trajectories of pyrimethamine resistance in human and zoonotic malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Putaporntip C, Somkuna J, Rojrung R, Tia T, Kuamsab N, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Comparative sequencing of dhfr-ts genes in 356 human malaria parasites (P. falciparum n=241, P. vivax n=115) from Thailand and 145 zoonotic parasites (P. knowlesi n=18, P. inui n=64) revealed marked differences in evolution under drug pressure. Human P. falciparum showed high prevalence of quadruple IRNL mutant indicating strong directional selection; P. vivax showed coexistence of wild-type and resistance mutations suggesting incomplete fixation. Zoonotic parasites exhibited higher mutation counts, haplotype diversity, and excess rare variants, with elevated synonymous/nonsynonymous ratios indicating purifying selection and genetic drift, not directional selection.

articles.headline: P. falciparum iron metabolism during blood-stage infection
articles.title_original: Fifty shades of iron: Unorthodox mechanisms of iron acquisition and utilization in blood-stage Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Loveridge KM, Sigala PA
articles.details: Review synthesizing current knowledge of blood-stage P. falciparum iron management, covering acquisition, distribution, and utilization. Highlights unconventional strategies parasites employ despite lacking canonical iron-metabolism proteins and pathways, identifying key unanswered questions for understanding and therapeutically targeting parasite iron metabolism.

articles.headline: Antibody responses to P. vivax protective antigens
articles.title_original: A longitudinal study of children identifies antibody Fc-mediated functions and antigen targets of immunity to Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Opi DH, Longley RJ, Takashima E, Spelman T, Tayipto Y, Schoffer K, Brewster J, Reiling L, Wines BD, Kiniboro B, Siba P, Harbers M, Hogarth M, Tsuboi T, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Beeson JG
articles.details: Longitudinal study in Papua New Guinea children identified antigens targeted by functional antibodies to P. vivax, including Fc-receptor interactions and complement fixation. Specific antigen subsets were associated with protection from P. vivax malaria, informing development of multi-antigen vaccines eliciting multi-functional antibody responses.

articles.headline: Synthetic attractants for Anopheles in Madagascar field trials
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of efficacy and selectivity of select benzopyranone attractants for anthropophilic malaria vectors in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Ralimanana SV, Ramarosandratana NH, Andrianjafy MT, Rambala Rakotomena NAH, Duclos MC, Ravaomanarivo LH, Mavingui P, Ramiharimanana FD, Mbaye A, Métay E, Vestalys Ramanandraibe V, Lemaire M
articles.details: Field evaluation in Madagascar highlands tested benzopyranone attractants for capturing anthropophilic Anopheles. 4-hydroxycoumarin showed efficient attraction (KI=80%) with high selectivity (SI=5.1); 7-decyloxychromone was less selective (KI=69%, SI=1.6). Mass trapping with selective attractants could complement vector control programs.

articles.headline: Knowledge gaps in malaria control, North Cameroon
articles.title_original: Awareness, attitudes, and control measures regarding malaria and mosquitoes in the North Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Danga SPY, Ngadvou D, Yonki B, Ezeike AK, Djonga A, Nukenine EN, Esimone CO
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 500 households in 50 North Cameroon villages found universal malaria awareness (100%) and correct knowledge of mosquito transmission (99.6%), yet 42.4% falsely believed cold weather causes malaria. ITN ownership near-universal (99.6%; 96.2% used previous night) but 90.4% misused nets; only 19% sought treatment within 24 hours of symptom onset.

articles.headline: RDT limitations amid pfhrp2/3 deletions in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Diagnostic challenges of histidine-rich protein 2-based rapid diagnostic tests due to pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions in asymptomatic malaria in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Louis JM, Fitriana F, Syahada JH, Muh F, Chun W, Park WS, Lee SJ, Na S, Han ET, Lu F, Kidima W, Han JH
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 3489 Tanzanians (Dec 2022–Jul 2023) found RDT detected 77.6% of qPCR-positive P. falciparum (κ=0.736, 90% accuracy) vs microscopy 53.8% (κ=0.590, 86.2% accuracy). pfhrp2/3 gene deletions present in 11.8% of isolates (2.4% dual), more frequent in high-transmission areas; all dual-deletion parasites were RDT-negative.

articles.headline: Broflanilide discriminating concentration for Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Determination of the preliminary discriminating concentration of broflanilide against malaria vector mosquito Anopheles gambiae by multi-centre susceptibility testing.
articles.authors: Ando Y, Mori K, Bradley J, Snetselaar J, Small G
articles.details: Multi-center WHO bottle bioassays across 9 facilities determined preliminary discriminating concentration of broflanilide (novel IRS insecticide, VECTRON™ T500) for An. gambiae susceptibility at 18 μg/bottle. Results establish benchmark for evaluating susceptibility to this new long-lasting insecticide in sub-Saharan African populations.

articles.headline: ITN use vs IRS adoption barriers, Uganda
articles.title_original: Utilization of malaria control interventions and associated factors among women of reproductive age in Lira City, Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Apio E, Angwech H, Opio B, Okwir G, Opoke R, Malinga GM, Opiro R
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 629 women of reproductive age in Lira City, Uganda found high ITN utilization (84.1%) and moderate IPT uptake (68%), but low IRS coverage (32.8%). Despite 91.9% perceiving IRS as effective, adoption was limited by concerns about odor (51.7%), safety (21.9%), and effectiveness (17.3%).

articles.headline: Climate thresholds drive malaria incidence in Colombia
articles.title_original: Lagged effect of temperature and rainfall on malaria incidence in Colombia (2013-2023): An approach with Bayesian spatiotemporal adjustment.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez JD
articles.details: Spatiotemporal analysis of 970 Colombian municipalities (2013-2023) using Bayesian distributed lag models revealed non-linear temperature-malaria association peaking at 28°C (minimum risk exposure at 16.43°C) with lags 0-6 weeks. Lower rainfall associated with higher incidence (EMR 0.85 mm), providing climate-based early warning thresholds for targeting interventions.

articles.headline: Hexanoic acid and methyl-butanol attract Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of synthetic attractants against an important malaria vector, Anopheles stephensi (Diptera: Culicidae) mosquitoes in laboratory conditions.
articles.authors: Hosseini SZ, Basseri HR, Zaim M, Azam K, Khalaj MR, Salari E
articles.details: Laboratory olfactometer evaluation of synthetic attractants for Anopheles stephensi tested four compounds at five dilutions. Three compounds significantly attracted mosquitoes with CO2 (P<0.05): hexanoic acid (most attractive), 3-methyl-1-butanol, and L-lactic acid. Promising blend of 3-methyl-1-butanol and hexanoic acid identified for odor-baited trap development pending field evaluation.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum species missed in Senegal diagnostics
articles.title_original: Malaria misdiagnosis among febrile patients in Senegal: implications for the underestimation of Plasmodium species diversity and malaria burden.
articles.authors: Sambe BS, Diagne A, Diatta AS, Diaw SOM, Sarr I, Diatta HAM, Sané R, Guigma PYB, Senghor B, Diouf B, Sembene PM, Vigan-Womas I, Niang M
articles.details: Analysis of 948 febrile Senegalese patients found PCR detected malaria in 75.29%, with 70.25% non-falciparum infections (P. ovale, P. vivax, P. malariae). Microscopy missed 31.5% of PCR-positive infections; RDT missed 18.92%. Both methods misclassified half of non-falciparum mono-infections as P. falciparum, underestimating species diversity.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle formulation improves quinine efficacy and safety
articles.title_original: Fabrication and evaluation of quinine-loaded chitosan-polycaprolactone core-shell polymeric nanoparticles for the treatment of malaria: an in vitro and in silico study.
articles.authors: Amos Y, Montenegro ED, Rubaka C, Swai H
articles.details: In vitro study developed chitosan-PCL core-shell nanoparticles (312.9 nm diameter, 81.8% encapsulation efficiency) for quinine delivery. Against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum (FCR3), nanoparticles reduced antiplasmodial IC50 from 130.12 to 32.56 ng/mL while increasing Vero cell IC50 from 131.93 to 345.93 µg/mL, achieving ~10-fold improvement in selectivity index and 2-fold greater RBC penetration.

articles.headline: PfEF-1α vaccine elicits T-cell responses in rodents
articles.title_original: Immunological response of using emulsifying elongation factor 1 alpha of Plasmodium falciparum-based vaccines in complete Freund's adjuvant.
articles.authors: Hong H, Eom TH, Lee E, Lee E, Lee HY, Won S, Lee DS, Kim MI, Park H, Yeo SJ
articles.details: Rodent vaccine study using CFA-emulsified P. falciparum elongation factor-1 alpha achieved 50% improved survival vs control with half of vaccinated mice remaining parasitemia-negative. Vaccine elicited 16-fold higher antigen-specific IgG titers and dominant CD4+IFN-γ+ T-cell responses; epitope 410-FAIRDMRQTI-419 validated for IFN-γ induction, supporting intracellular antigen vaccine strategy.

articles.headline: SORT IT builds malaria research capacity in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Strengthening operational research to complement Global Fund grants for HIV, TB and malaria in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Kenneh S, Kamau EM, Thorson A, Halleux C, Ameh G
articles.details: The SORT IT initiative, a partnership-based program led by TDR, conducted a course in Sierra Leone that brought together 19 institutions to strengthen health research capacity. The program aims to make countries 'data rich, information rich and action rich' to improve health care delivery and outcomes, emphasizing partnerships and communities of practice.

articles.headline: RNA-seq reveals adhesion proteins in high-rosetting P. vivax parasites
articles.title_original: Transcriptomics of Plasmodium vivax rosetting.
articles.authors: Bourgard C, Ferraboli JW, Lopes SCP, Lacerda MVG, Sunnerhagen P, Albrecht L, Costa FTM
articles.details: Comparative RNA-seq analysis of 10 P. vivax field isolates from the Brazilian Amazon identified 492 differentially expressed genes between high and low rosetting parasites. Among these, 34.96% (172 genes) were conserved but of unknown function, while 65.04% (320 genes) were mapped, including adhesion proteins (10%, 53 genes), kinases (4%, 19 genes), and other proteins (50%, 248 genes). Findings highlight the importance of membrane proteins with adhesive properties, particularly PHIST proteins and 6-cysteine family proteins, in P. vivax rosetting phenotypes.

articles.headline: WWII soldiers struggled with chronic malaria relapses despite low death rates
articles.title_original: Post-infection symptoms in U.S. soldiers with malaria during the Second World War: major limitation to return to duty.
articles.authors: Shanks GD
articles.details: Over 100,000 malaria cases were reported among U.S. military personnel in the Pacific theater in 1943. Relapsing Plasmodium vivax malaria was particularly problematic, with many soldiers experiencing more than 10 relapses. While death rates were very low, thousands of soldiers required weeks to months of hospitalization and rehabilitation, with approximately 1% of malaria patients repatriated due to chronic malaria symptoms, highlighting post-infection sequelae as a major obstacle to return to duty.

articles.headline: Algae-derived silver nanoparticles show antiplasmodial activity via metabolic modulation
articles.title_original: Green synthesis of silver nanoparticles using Padina tetrastromatica against malaria and deciphering the mechanism through machine learning-driven metabolomics and network pharmacology.
articles.authors: Yaladanda N, Budige YK, Veeragoni D, Josyula JVN, Rodda R, Singh V, Sripadi P, Mutheneni SR
articles.details: Silver nanoparticles synthesized from the brown algae Padina tetrastromatica (Ag-PT) demonstrated potent antiplasmodial activity with significantly lower IC50 values than chemically synthesized counterparts. Untargeted metabolomics revealed that Ag-PT restored malaria-induced disruptions in fatty acid, arginine, and arachidonic acid metabolism, elevating specialized pro-resolving mediators (DHA, 14-HDHA, 18-HEPE). Network pharmacology and molecular docking identified COX-2 as a key hub gene, with the phytochemical eriodictyol showing strong binding, suggesting a multifaceted therapeutic mechanism combining antiparasitic and anti-inflammatory effects.

articles.headline: RALP1 essential for P. falciparum schizont maturation and host cell invasion
articles.title_original: RALP1 is essential for schizont maturation and erythrocyte invasion in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Wu J, Zhang Z, Pang J, Yang W, Ngim C, Li P, Ye J, Tian B, Cheng X, Wang F, Zhang Q, Shang X
articles.details: Conditional CRISPR-Cas9 knockdown of RALP1, a rhoptry neck-associated protein, impaired P. falciparum parasite proliferation, schizont maturation, merozoite production, and erythrocyte invasion efficiency. RNA-seq analysis revealed limited effects in ring stages but widespread downregulation of invasion-related and host-parasite interaction genes in schizonts. The protein contains extensive intrinsically disordered regions with multiple predicted epitope hotspots in its C-terminal RBC-binding domain, highlighting potential for therapeutic or vaccine targeting.

articles.headline: Extended SMC and mobile teams control malaria outbreak in flood-affected Mali
articles.title_original: Extreme rainfall, flooding and malaria in the Sahara: outbreak analysis in Kidal, Mali 2024.
articles.authors: Dolo AM, Cissoko M, Teme A, Keita K, Sanogo M, Traoré CAT, Magassa M, Koné A, Roy FA, Sagara I, Gaudart J
articles.details: A historical cohort study in Kidal, Mali analyzed malaria cases from May 2024 to February 2025 following extreme rainfall and flooding. The malaria epidemic peaked in week 39 with 1,014 cases, with an incidence of 1.688 (95% CI 1.687–1.690) cases per 1000 person-weeks, highest in children under five. Extended seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) to children under 15 years and mobile health team deployment significantly reduced morbidity, with standardized incidence ratios of 0.50 (95% CI 0.33–0.75) and 0.48 (95% CI 0.28–0.82) respectively.

articles.headline: Guava most attractive sugar source to malaria vectors in Malawi plantations
articles.title_original: Exploring the importance and preference of sugar feeding behaviour of malaria vectors in sugar plantations of southern Malawi.
articles.authors: Zembere K, Coleman S, Chirombo J, Mbewe R, Tangena JA
articles.details: A study in Chikwawa, southern Malawi evaluated sugar-feeding preferences of Anopheles gambiae. Field collections from sugar plantations revealed over 40% (n=74) of 187 collected anophelines had fed on natural sugar sources. Olfactory choice experiments with 810 reared A. gambiae s.l. demonstrated that guava was 1.97× more attractive than sugarcane (95% CI: 1.49–2.62, p<0.001), followed by banana (1.68×, 95% CI: 1.26–2.24, p<0.001), mango (1.49×), and melon (1.45×). These findings inform tailored attractive targeted sugar bait (ATSB) interventions.

articles.headline: SC83288 targets PfDNMT2 in artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum with fitness cost
articles.title_original: Mechanisms of PfDNMT2 inhibition and PfATP6-mediated resistance to the antimalarial candidate SC83288 in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Sanchez CP, Duffey M, Celada RV, Kucharski M, Hoarau M, Saeyang T, Kamchonwongpaisan S, Sohraby F, de Buhr S, Nunes Alves A, Lucky AB, Miao J, Regnault C, Dziekan JM, Bozdech Z, Barrett MP, Lanzer M
articles.details: SC83288, a novel antimalarial compound, demonstrated high efficacy against P. falciparum both in vivo and in vitro, including artemisinin- and partner-drug-resistant strains. The parasite's DNA and tRNA methyltransferase PfDNMT2 was identified as the primary molecular target, linking drug action to impaired epigenetic regulation and altered methionine metabolism. Resistance arose through mutations in the SERCA-type Ca²⁺ ATPase PfATP6, enabling compound sequestration into the endoplasmic reticulum away from nuclear targets, though this resistance mechanism carried a substantial fitness cost, limiting spread potential.

articles.headline: Encapsulated sea cucumber protects against malaria-induced organ damage in mice
articles.title_original: Hepatorenal protective role of encapsulated Holothuria atra in a mice model of Plasmodium berghei ANKA inoculation.
articles.authors: Utami PD, Setianingsih H, Sari DRT
articles.details: In a mouse model of Plasmodium berghei ANKA infection, 48 BALB/c mice were randomized to eight groups including untreated control, chloroquine monotherapy, and six combination therapies of chloroquine plus encapsulated Holothuria atra with varying gum Arabic/soy protein isolate (GA/SPI) ratios and dosages. The chloroquine-only group showed the highest renal damage scores. Combination therapy with the 4:1 GA/SPI ratio at 100 mg/kg significantly reduced both renal and hepatic damage compared to chloroquine monotherapy, approaching negative control levels.

articles.headline: Unified culture medium enables broad-spectrum antimalarial drug screening
articles.title_original: A Standardized Culture Medium for Comparative Drug Efficacy Evaluation Across Plasmodium and Babesia Species.
articles.authors: Vydyam P, Ben Mamoun C
articles.details: A standardized in vitro culture system based on DMEM/F12 supplemented with 20% fetal bovine serum (DFS20) was developed to support asexual growth of multiple Plasmodium falciparum strains (3D7, Dd2, HB3, V1/S), Babesia duncani, and other Babesia species. Parasite proliferation and morphology in DFS20 were comparable to established species-specific media, while eliminating reliance on undefined or discontinued proprietary components. This unified platform enables standardized cross-species growth inhibition assays, recently used to screen dihydrotriazines and biguanides targeting DHFR-TS enzymes and identify potent antifolate candidates with broad-spectrum activity.

articles.headline: R21 malaria vaccine more cost-effective than RTS,S in Zambia provinces
articles.title_original: Provincial stratification and equity-weighted cost-effectiveness of malaria vaccines in Zambia under climate variability.
articles.authors: Phiri AE
articles.details: A province-stratified SEIR-mosquito transmission model calibrated to routine surveillance data in Zambia evaluated RTS,S and R21 malaria vaccines over a 5-year horizon (2024–2028). RTS,S was estimated to avert a median of 9.80×10⁵ malaria cases (95% UI: 8.88×10⁵–1.08×10⁶) and approximately 1,960 deaths. R21, with higher efficacy, averted 1.09×10⁶ cases (95% UI: 9.91×10⁵–1.21×10⁶) and approximately 2,188 deaths.
