RTS,S was borderline cost-effective at current prices against Zambia's US$500/DALY willingness-to-pay threshold, while R21 was highly cost-effective and potentially dominant, with impact varying across provinces.

articles.headline: Low vaccine awareness but high acceptance among Sudanese parents despite malaria knowledge
articles.title_original: Assessment of parental knowledge, attitude, and acceptance toward malaria vaccine for children under five years in Sudan, 2025.
articles.authors: Hassan AAE, Ali NAH, Elshareef MAA, Sidahmed TSM, Elobied HES, Ali AEO, Salih DAD, Al Rahma AMM, Abdallha AWA, Hussain AMS, Hussain AMS, Mohammed EYM, Ahmed KAH, Awadallah SYM
articles.details: A descriptive cross-sectional study of 706 parents in high malaria-burden regions of Sudan (April–June 2025) assessed knowledge, attitudes, and acceptance of malaria vaccines for children under five. While 98.2% were aware of malaria, only 38.0% (268 parents) had heard of the malaria vaccine. Vaccine awareness was significantly higher among fathers versus mothers (19.0% vs 10.7%, p=0.002) and among those with postgraduate education (11.9%), public-sector employment (13.1%), and monthly incomes >500K SDG (26.9%, p=0.010). Despite limited vaccine awareness, parental acceptance was high.

articles.headline: Rare splenic rupture in mixed malaria; one emergency splenectomy survivor
articles.title_original: Malaria-associated splenic haemorrhage requiring emergency splenectomy: a rare complication managed in a resource-constrained setting.
articles.authors: Kumar A, Agatrao BA, Prakash A, Pogatyanatti B, Uppaluri D, Krishna VS, Farook AA, Sharma A, Patel A
articles.details: Two workers in South Sudan presented with mixed P. falciparum-P. vivax with hepatosplenomegaly. Both developed spontaneous splenic rupture with gross hemoperitoneum and hemodynamic instability. One patient survived following emergency laparotomy and supportive care; the other died despite emergency management.

articles.headline: P. falciparum tubulin adaptations alter microtubule structure and drug susceptibility
articles.title_original: Adaptations in Plasmodium tubulin determine distinct microtubule architectures, mechanics and drug susceptibility.
articles.authors: Bangera M, Wu J, Beckett D, Fachet D, Ferreira JL, Voth GA, Reber S, Moores CA
articles.details: Using cryo-electron microscopy on purified P. falciparum tubulin, researchers determined novel microtubule structures. Parasite-specific tubulin sequences alter dimer structure, with lateral contacts smaller but stronger than brain microtubules and a stiffer lattice, modulating drug susceptibility and polymer properties.

articles.headline: QSAR models identify potent HDAC inhibitors for malaria drug discovery
articles.title_original: Improved quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) models to predict the activity of hydroxamic acids as HDAC inhibitors against malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Dawood WA, Macdonald JR, Skinner-Adams TS, Hesping E, Gorse AJP, Andrews KT
articles.details: Researchers developed improved QSAR classification models (B5-B9, AUC 0.953-0.984) from 572 hydroxamic acid structures to predict P. falciparum HDAC inhibitor activity. External validation of model B7 achieved 81% accuracy in identifying active compounds. Virtual screening and experimental assessment identified three potent antiplasmodial hits (PfIC50 8, 14, 80 nM), two demonstrating histone H4 hyperacetylation.

articles.headline: Deep learning predicts CRISPR gene drive efficacy for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: A simulation-based deep learning framework for spatially explicit malaria modeling of CRISPR suppression gene drive mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Allegretti YH, Sun W, Champer J
articles.details: Researchers developed a deep learning model to simulate CRISPR gene drive effects on Anopheles suppression and malaria prevalence. Reducing embryo and functional resistance while increasing drive conversion efficiency enhanced suppression. The parameter space for malaria elimination was substantially larger than for mosquito elimination, suggesting imperfect drives may still achieve malaria control.

articles.headline: Declining P. vivax diversity in Cambodia suggests successful transmission control
articles.title_original: Temporal trends in Plasmodium vivax diversity in eastern Cambodia evidence declining transmission.
articles.authors: Eam R, Hoon KS, Sutanto E, Rai A, Trimarsanto H, Rumaseb A, Thin S, Hor S, Chhorvann C, Bunkea T, Price RN, Popovici J, Auburn S
articles.details: Genotyping of 182 P. vivax isolates from eastern Cambodia (2014-2023) using 93 microhaplotype markers showed declining transmission. Polyclonal infections fell from 22-48% to 5% in 2023 (p<0.05), while identity-by-descent clustering increased to 81.4% in 2023 versus 8.9-10.8% in earlier years, indicating population bottlenecking. Findings support widespread radical cure for P. vivax elimination.

articles.headline: Yellow fever ecological collapse reduces malaria in Atlantic Forest
articles.title_original: When yellow fever silenced the forest: Ecological collapse and reduced malaria transmission in the Rio de Janeiro Atlantic Forest, Brazil.
articles.authors: de Pina-Costa A, Lourenço-de-Oliveira R, Brasil P, Daniel-Ribeiro CT
articles.details: Ecological collapse and reduced malaria transmission associated with yellow fever in Rio de Janeiro Atlantic Forest, Brazil.

articles.headline: Correction to Yanomami malaria and gold mining article
articles.title_original: Correction: 'Investigating the Yanomami malaria outbreak: gold mining and malaria' (2026), by de Angeli Dutra et al.
articles.authors: de Angeli Dutra D, Fontes CJ, Braga ÉM, Mordecai EA
articles.details: Correction/erratum to previous publication investigating link between gold mining and malaria in Yanomami population.

articles.headline: Spatial climate patterns, not temporal warming, drive Amhara malaria resurgence
articles.title_original: Climate, environmental, and programmatic correlates of malaria resurgence in Amhara, Ethiopia (2018-2024): a Bayesian spatiotemporal analysis.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Asemahagn MA, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Nigussie TZ, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Bayesian spatiotemporal analysis of 13,944 district-month observations (2018-2024) across 166 Ethiopian districts documented 5,746,571 confirmed malaria cases with 5.5-fold incidence increase (1.19 to 6.53 per 1,000). Higher temperature and rainfall associated with increased incidence; IRS, ITNs, and larval source management protective. Climate-incidence associations were predominantly spatial rather than temporal, suggesting geographic suitability drives resurgence.

articles.headline: Maternal malaria parasitemia primary risk for neonatal malaria infection
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic determinants and maternal malaria: impact on neonatal parasitemia in Osun State, Southwest, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Odediji SA, Busari LO, Olawuyi AO, Amoo KO, Amoo-Adeboye BO, Surakat OA, Adeleke MA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 85 mother-infant pairs in Nigeria found neonatal parasitemia prevalence of 8.2% (7/85). All infected neonates were born to mothers with positive microscopy (7/19), while no infections occurred in offspring of parasite-negative mothers (p<0.001). No significant associations emerged between neonatal infection and maternal socioeconomic factors, ITN use, or intermittent preventive therapy.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine awareness limited; health worker trust key in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes and factors associated with the awareness of caregivers of under-five children regarding the malaria vaccine in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon: A community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Idang MA, Nsobinenyui D, Ukah CE, Yunika LK, Ngu CN, Fuanghene Wefuan R, Dang SZ, Ndip NE, Feguem MP, Shei CM, Nsagha DS
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 410 caregivers in Cameroon showed 60.7% vaccine awareness but only 26.6% adequate knowledge and 25.1% positive attitudes. Trust in health workers (AOR 3.02, 95% CI 1.83-4.99) and attendance at immunization services (AOR 3.57, 95% CI 2.27-5.60) independently associated with higher awareness, while previous malaria episodes decreased likelihood (AOR 0.55, 95% CI 0.28-0.97).

articles.headline: Correction to prolyl-tRNA synthetase and mosquito targeting article
articles.title_original: Correction to: Chemical Targeting of Prolyl-tRNA Synthetase Stalls Ovarian Development and Kills Malaria Vectors.
articles.details: Correction/erratum to article on chemical targeting of prolyl-tRNA synthetase for malaria vector control.

articles.headline: Regional drug resistance diversity in India; lumefantrine tolerance emerging
articles.title_original: Targeted Genomic Surveillance Unveils Genetic Variations Linked to Regional Malaria Drug Resistance Dynamics in India.
articles.authors: Khilari A, Sharma S, Bajpai M, Viswan K A, Chaturvedi R, Mirdha BR, Rahi M, Sharma A, Shanmugam D
articles.details: Nanopore sequencing of 238 P. falciparum samples from 6 Indian states revealed fragmented resistance landscape. Northeast showed high chloroquine resistance (Pfcrt K76T, CVIET haplotype) and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance, while Central India displayed variable chloroquine resistance and emerging lumefantrine tolerance (Pfmdr1 Y184F, Pfaat1 S258L). No artemisinin-resistance mutations detected, supporting continued artemisinin-based combination therapy efficacy.

articles.headline: Optimizing newborn sickle cell screening site selection in Uganda
articles.title_original: A maximal-coverage approach to prioritizing newborn sickle cell screening sites in Uganda using a sickle cell-malaria co-risk surface.
articles.authors: Paasi G, Ndeezi G, Kiyaga C, Namazzi R, Mupere E, Munabi IG, Kiguli S, Idro R, Olupot-Olupot P
articles.details: Study used interpolated HbS allele frequency data and 2015-2024 mean Plasmodium falciparum parasite prevalence in 2-10 year-olds (PfPR2-10, area-weighted mean 26.0%) to create a co-risk surface for sickle cell and malaria burden. A greedy algorithm placed 50 candidate screening hubs, capturing cumulative co-risk of 65.6% with an elbow in gains between 35-40 locations. Of 50 candidate hubs, 28 (56%) could be collocated to existing health facilities within 60-minute travel time, offering transparent phased expansion.

articles.headline: Widespread pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles gambiae in Benin
articles.title_original: Entomological surveillance of malaria vector: species, behaviors, and insecticide resistance in southern and central Benin.
articles.authors: Hougbe SZ, Ossé RA, Akpodji S, Ahouandjinou MJ, Koumodji KD, Affolabi ZK, DePina AJ, Oussou O, Bocco EO, Souler MI, Idrissou S, Alohoutade SV, Adjottin B, Mokuba A, Chitou S, Totin F, Ossah M, Traore I, Cisse MBM, Baba-Moussa L, Akogbeto M
articles.details: Entomological survey December 2023–October 2024 across six districts in southern and central Benin found A. gambiae s.l. as predominant vector (>98%) exhibiting both indoor and outdoor biting with site- and season-specific heterogeneity. Pyrethroid resistance was widespread with kdr allele frequencies exceeding 65% at all sites; susceptibility to pirimiphos-methyl was generally higher but reduced mortality observed in some districts. Findings support locally tailored vector control including next-generation nets and insecticide rotation.

articles.headline: AMA-1 genetic heterogeneity in Plasmodium malariae Vietnam isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism and natural selection of apical membrane antigen-1 in Plasmodium malariae isolated from Vietnam.
articles.authors: Nguyễn ĐTD, Lê HG, Võ TC, Nguyễn TH, Kang JM, Nguyễn KO, Trinh NTM, Van Khanh C, Hanh NTL, Van TTH, Trung NK, Cho M, Son TT, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Analysis of apical membrane antigen-1 (AMA-1) gene in 95 Vietnam P. malariae isolates identified 117 pmama-1 sequences grouping into 19 distinct haplotypes. Amino acid polymorphisms were predominantly in domains I and II, with most changes predicted on the protein surface. Evidence of natural selection and evolutionary trends were observed, revealing substantial genetic heterogeneity in this vaccine-candidate protein across the population.

articles.headline: Compound effector genes reduce malaria parasite transmission in gene-drive mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Compound effector genes suppress malaria parasite infections in gene-drive population modification strains of the African malaria mosquitoes, Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles coluzzii.
articles.authors: Carballar-Lejarazú R, Dong Y, Pham TB, Tushar T, Tavadia M, Dimopoulos G, James AA
articles.details: Four population modification gene-drive strains were developed in A. gambiae s.s. and A. coluzzii carrying compound effector genes TP24 and TP43, achieving 95–100% inheritance of gene-drive constructs. Life-table analysis showed mixed fitness impacts dependent on species and copy number. Challenge assays with P. falciparum demonstrated that compound effector gene complexes significantly reduced both parasite prevalence and infection intensities in both mosquito species.

articles.headline: LLIN distribution reduces malaria incidence in four Zambian provinces
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence before and after long-lasting insecticidal net distribution in four Zambian provinces, 2010-2021.
articles.authors: Mwenya RM, Ngulube ID, Richardson S
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Interrupted time series analysis of monthly malaria incidence data from January 2010–December 2021 across four Zambian provinces (Eastern, Luapula, Northern, Northwestern) with combined population 5,965,257 across 1,357 health facility catchment areas. Binomial regression showed statistically significant 35% reduction in seasonally adjusted malaria incidence following LLIN distribution (rate ratio 0.65, 95% CI 0.50–0.85; p=0.002), demonstrating essential protective benefits of nets.

articles.headline: Poor predictive value of murine cerebral malaria models for human adjuvants
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria: of mice and men.
articles.authors: Weerasekera CJ, White NJ
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Systematic review of publications since 2010 found that 149 interventions (118 adjuvants) were evaluated in C57BL/6 mice infected with P. berghei ANKA strain, with 142 (95%) reported as successful. However, only 26% were evaluated after development of murine cerebral syndrome and 65% of adjuvants tested without concomitant antimalarial treatment. In contrast, clinical research on human cerebral malaria declined with no adjuvant intervention proven effective, suggesting very poor predictive value.

articles.headline: Low amodiaquine efficacy in SMC for Plasmodium falciparum Mozambique
articles.title_original: Parasitological efficacy of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Nampula, northern Mozambique.
articles.authors: Bonnington C, Sitoe M, Pulido Tarquino IA, Enosse SM, Sararat C, Suwannasin K, Proux S, Koesukwiwat U, Tarning J, Imwong M, Theiss-Nyland K, Nosten FH, White NJ
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Pilot pharmacometric assessment of seasonal malaria chemoprevention with monthly sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine (SPAQ) in 460 healthy children in northern Mozambique showed parasitaemia prevalence decreased from 68% to 41% at first assessment and 71% to 44% at 28-day follow-up. Preventive efficacy was 97% for P. ovale but only 42% for P. falciparum. Desethylamodiaquine showed clear exposure-response relationship; amodiaquine had low parasitological efficacy and sulphadoxine/pyrimethamine did not significantly contribute to chemoprevention.

articles.headline: Limited evidence linking CYP450 polymorphisms to P. vivax treatment outcomes
articles.title_original: Polymorphisms in Cytochrome P450 genes: implications for Plasmodium vivax malaria treatment in Brazil.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Almeida MNG, Sampaio MP, Mello AGNC, Fernando DJV, Braz RP, Miceli LA, Reis NF, Guimarães RJP, Silva JRS, Storti-Melo LM, Baptista ARS, Machado RLD
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Systematic review examining host cytochrome P450 gene polymorphisms in P. vivax malaria treatment in Brazil, where P. vivax accounts for approximately 85% of annual malaria cases. Eight studies met inclusion criteria, predominantly focusing on CYP2D6 polymorphisms and associations with treatment failure. Overall findings revealed lack of consensus regarding relationship between CYP450 single nucleotide polymorphisms and antimalarial drug response, highlighting heterogeneity in pharmacogenetic effects.

articles.headline: Reactive case detection identifies subpatent malaria in migratory populations
articles.title_original: Detection of subpatent malaria among febrile mobile and migratory population individuals: a strategy for malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Kaura T, Mewara A, Devi S, Singh N, Takkar J, Kumar N, Sehgal R, Grover GS
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Case investigation with reactive active case detection (RACD) was conducted around 28 index cases among febrile mobile and migratory population (MMP) individuals in Punjab, India. Of 176 fingerprick samples collected, 11 (6.3%) infected individuals were detected: 8 by RDT, microscopy, and nPCR, and 3 (27.3%) subpatent infections by nPCR only. P. vivax comprised 93.3% of 119 total cases and P. falciparum 6.7%; no G6PDd detected in positive patients.

articles.headline: Maintaining malaria control services during COVID-19 pandemic in Uganda
articles.title_original: Ensuring continuity of malaria services in times of crisis: lessons learned in Uganda during the COVID-19 pandemic.
articles.authors: Manning EK, Magumba G, Tibenderana JK, Nuwa A
articles.details: During COVID-19 pandemic, Uganda's National Malaria Control Division (NMCD) and partners implemented updated operational protocols to maintain essential malaria control interventions. Partners supplied infection prevention and control equipment to >33,000 health workers and village health teams. Malaria Consortium provided critical support, demonstrating importance of proactive risk planning, timely data reporting, and sustainable funding for maintaining malaria services during public health crises.

articles.headline: Optimal timing and interval for mass drug administration in Senegal
articles.title_original: Mathematical modeling for analyzing mass drug administration operational factors for efficient malaria incidence reduction in southern Senegal.
articles.authors: Ndiaye K, Diop EHS, Slater HC, Loum MA, Ndiaye JA, Diallo I, NDiop M, Kaly SN
articles.details: Mathematical model based on impulsive differential equations analyzed operational factors of mass drug administration (MDA) including number of rounds, interval between rounds, campaign coverage, and campaign start date in Tambacounda health district, southeastern Senegal. Model predicted successful MDA required high coverage, multiple rounds within a year, five- to six-week intervals between rounds, and initiation in first month of transmission period. Starting campaign date and interval between rounds were highly correlated with efficacy.

articles.headline: Androgens reduce immune control of Plasmodium liver-stage infection in mice
articles.title_original: Host biological sex directs immune control of the Plasmodium parasite liver stage in mice.
articles.authors: Duncombe CJ, Poehlman AS, Conrad EN, Sen N, Ayenew A, Blyn RC, McElfresh GW, Glennon EKK, Boey K, Kalata AC, Kaushansky A, Minkah N, Shears MJ, Murphy SC
articles.details: Male and female BALB/cJ mice, including orchiectomized and androgen-treated males, were infected with P. yoelii sporozoites and assessed for hepatic parasite burden and immune responses. Androgens increased parasite survival by reducing elimination of infected hepatocytes. Females and orchiectomized males exhibited enhanced immune activity with fewer infected hepatocytes by 44 hours post-infection. Intact males had lower baseline densities of Kupffer cells, monocytes, and dendritic cells with reduced antigen presentation and blunted Type I interferon responses.

articles.headline: Age and dosing impact cellular responses to R21/RH5 vaccines
articles.title_original: B and T cell responses to pre-erythrocytic R21/Matrix-M and blood-stage RH5.1/Matrix-M malaria vaccines in endemic settings.
articles.authors: Bundi CK, Muñoz O, Kibwana E, Bellamy D, Stockdale L, Barrett JR, McHugh K, Mtaka I, Kalinga W, Kimani D, Nyamako L, Keter K, Ogwang R, Hill AVS, Bejon P, Hamaluba M, Silk SE, Minassian AM, Draper SJ, Olotu AI, Kapulu MC, Ewer KJ, Nielsen CM
articles.details: Two Phase 1b trials (Kenya, Tanzania) analyzed PBMC responses to R21/Matrix-M and RH5.1/Matrix-M vaccines against Plasmodium falciparum. For R21, infants showed higher NANP-specific memory B cells than adults, with increased response at 50μg versus 25μg Matrix-M dose. For RH5.1, delayed booster dosing (10-10-10μg at 0-1-6 months) produced highest IgG+ memory B cells correlating with antibody levels 2 years post-vaccination. Age, adjuvant dose, vaccine dose, and booster timing all influenced antigen-specific cellular responses.

articles.headline: RNAi yeast pesticides kill Anopheles stephensi in laboratory
articles.title_original: Targeting the Highly Invasive Malaria Vector Anopheles stephensi using Yeast RNAi Pesticides.
articles.authors: Njoroge TM, Hamid-Adiamoh M, Mysore K, Stewart ATM, Sun L, Akaiso DD, Duman-Scheel M
articles.details: A modified yeast RNAi pesticide (Sh.463) targeting Anopheles stephensi Shaker gene was tested against this invasive malaria vector. Treatment of larvae and adults with Sh.463-56.10R yeast achieved high laboratory mortality by silencing the Shaker gene. Female-specific RNAi yeast larvicides targeting putative female-specific genes achieved significant female mortality, resulting in higher male:female ratios in offspring.

articles.headline: Malaria vulnerability hotspots mapped in Zamfara, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Geospatial mapping of gender-sensitive malaria vulnerability and ITN usage barriers in Zamfara State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Atijosan A, Atijosan A, Salau AO
articles.details: A geospatial study integrated malaria incidence and mortality data with ITN access, maternal education, and household wealth to develop a Composite Gender-Sensitive Malaria Vulnerability Index. Hotspots identified in Gummi (index 3.86) and Bukkuyum (3.62) showed low ITN usage (82-85%), high maternal education deficits (89-91%), and poverty (85-90%), with hotspot analysis confirming significant western clustering (z-scores 2.11-4.89 at 99% confidence). Findings suggest targeting ITN distribution and socio-economic support to areas of elevated risk.

articles.headline: Structure-based DPAP-1 inhibitor shows antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Structure-based discovery of Plasmodium falciparum DPAP-1 inhibitor and its experimental validation as an antimalarial agent.
articles.authors: Verma D, Tripathi M, Rathore S, Ahmad I, Saeed M, Dwivedi VD, Tripathi RC
articles.details: Structure-based drug discovery identified a Plasmodium falciparum DPAP-1 inhibitor with experimental antimalarial activity.

articles.headline: Biomarkers predict severity in imported malaria cases
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in Portugal: A Retrospective Analysis from a Tertiary Public Hospital.
articles.authors: Valença Ferreira T, Alves Silva G, Duque V
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of 88 adult malaria patients (52.3% with prior malaria history, mostly exposed in Angola) found severe malaria in 25.0% of cases, significantly more frequent in those without prior exposure (p=0.027). Severe cases had higher creatinine (p=0.009) and lactate dehydrogenase (p=0.038) levels. Urea and parasitemia independently associated with longer hospital stay (p<0.001 and p=0.016 respectively), supporting semi-immunity hypothesis.

articles.headline: HRP2/3 gene deletions compromise malaria RDT performance
articles.title_original: Are HRP2/3 deletions silently crippling malaria rapid diagnostic tests?
articles.authors: S R S, Holla R, Rao M, Unnikrishnan B, B M S, Chalageri VH
articles.details: A systematic review of 28 studies found pfhrp2/pfhrp3 deletion rates ranging from 2.4% (Chhattisgarh, India) to 57.8% (Southern Ethiopia), with Brazil showing ~18.3% dual deletions. RDT diagnostic performance considerably declined in regions exceeding WHO's 5% deletion prevalence threshold. Alternative diagnostics (PCR, whole-genome sequencing, digital droplet PCR) are needed for effective detection and monitoring in high-deletion areas.

articles.headline: Lagged climate drought impacts malaria incidence in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Hydroclimatic extremes indexed by SPI and malaria case dynamics in Oyo State, Nigeria: evidence for lagged, site-specific effects.
articles.authors: Odjegba EE, Akanji OE, Bankole AO, Makinde AA, Aromolaran AK, Olagoke VO
articles.details: A study of monthly climate and malaria data from Saki and Sepeteri, Oyo State, Nigeria (2013-2023) found strong seasonality with peaks July-November (mean 1,134 cases/month Saki, 869 Sepeteri). In Saki, one-unit increase in 12-month SPI associated with 267% rise in cases (β=1.299, p=0.011), moderated by temperature (β=-0.049, p=0.011), with 1-2 month lagged models substantially improving prediction (ΔAIC 47-59). In Sepeteri, 3-month SPI lagged models improved fit (ΔAIC 45-50), demonstrating site-specific, non-linear drought-malaria dynamics.

articles.headline: Malaria parasites induce thymic atrophy via inflammation
articles.title_original: Thymic Atrophy Development in a Non-Lethal Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Corral-Ruiz GM, Pérez-Vega MJ, Mancilla-Herrera I, Barrios-Payán J, Fabila-Castillo L, Hernández-Pando R, Sánchez-Torres LE
articles.details: A study of Plasmodium chabaudi AS infection in mice found thymic atrophy marked by decreased weight and cellularity (especially at 20-30% parasitemia) due to DP and SP thymocyte loss and increased apoptosis. Elevated serum cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-6, CXCL9) correlated with atrophy degree, with early thymocyte release to spleen worsening cellularity loss. Post-parasitemia resolution, thymus structure and thymocyte populations recovered.

articles.headline: CCND3 enhancer variant reduces malaria risk
articles.title_original: CCND3 enhancer variant reduces malaria risk.
articles.authors: Faial T
articles.details: A genetic association study identified a CCND3 enhancer variant associated with reduced malaria risk.

articles.headline: Malarial retinopathy marks severe malaria with high specificity
articles.title_original: A Study on Prevalence of Malarial Retinopathy among Malaria Cases and to Determine Its Presence as a Marker of Severe Malaria: Observations from Eastern India.
articles.authors: Chatterjee R, Bhadra TR, Chatterjee N, Mukherjee S, Halder P, Sarkar K, Chatterjee I, Saha B, Karmakar PS, Pramanik N, Samajdar SS
articles.details: A case-control study in Eastern India of 71 malaria cases (12 severe: 8 P. vivax, 3 P. falciparum, 1 mixed; 59 uncomplicated mostly P. vivax) found malarial retinopathy in 9/12 severe cases (75%) and 2/59 uncomplicated cases (3.4%). Retinopathy features (retinal whitening, vessel changes, tramlining, hemorrhages, papilledema) showed 75% sensitivity and 96.6% specificity as a marker of severe malaria (p<0.05) with positive predictive value of 81.8%.

articles.headline: Nested-PCR optimized for Plasmodium malariae detection
articles.title_original: Optimization of Nested-PCR for the detection of Plasmodium malariae in laboratory settings in Southern Vietnam.
articles.authors: Nguyen KT, Nguyen NH, Dinh QL, Nguyen HLV, Nguyen MAT, Huynh HQ, Van Thi Tran H
articles.details: A nested-PCR protocol targeting P. malariae 18S rRNA gene was optimized using MAL1/MAL2 primers at 54°C annealing temperature with 35 cycles in the first round and 25 in the second. The MAL1/MAL2 pair demonstrated high specificity (no amplification for P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. ovale, P. knowlesi, or microbial contaminants) with limit of detection as low as 0.5 parasites/μL.

articles.headline: Climate shocks threaten malaria control in Africa
articles.title_original: Climate shocks, not just warming, threaten malaria control efforts in Africa.
articles.authors: Messina J, Carrel M
articles.details: A study examines how climate shocks pose threats to malaria control efforts. Extreme weather events, distinct from long-term warming trends, significantly impact disease control strategies across Africa.

articles.headline: Mosquito genetic responses differ to pyrethroid vs PBO nets
articles.title_original: Genetic Surveillance Reveals Differential Evolutionary Dynamic of Anopheles gambiae Under Contrasting Insecticidal Tools Used in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Njoroge HN, Namuli L, Nagi SC, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, McDermott DP, Knight E, Gonahasa S, Lynd A, Oruni A, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Opigo J, Yeka A, Katureebe A, Kyohere M, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Hemingway J, Staedke SG, Clarkson C, Miles A, Lucas ER, Donnelly MJ
articles.details: Genomic surveillance of A. gambiae during clinical trial comparing pyrethroid-only ITNs with pyrethroid-PBO nets revealed distinct evolutionary dynamics. Cyp9k1 gene duplication increased in frequency under pyrethroid-only nets but decreased under PBO-treated nets; alternative detoxification mechanism selected in 2La chromosomal region under PBO nets.

articles.headline: Malaria and antimalarial drugs impair male reproductive function
articles.title_original: Consequence of malaria and dengue on reproductive vigor. A scoping review.
articles.authors: Chatterjee PK, Chatterjee P, Dutta B, Anupama N, R M, Dhar S, Ks R, Bijoor SN, Tomar A, Tripathy A
articles.details: Scoping review (2013-2024) found malaria associated with decreased semen quality, oligozoospermia, and male infertility; increases miscarriage, intrauterine demise, preterm birth, low birth weight, neonatal and maternal deaths. Antimalarial drugs (chloroquine, quinine, quinacrine, pyrimethamine) cause male infertility.

articles.headline: Structure-guided PfCyRPA vaccine shows improved immunogenicity
articles.title_original: Structure-guided design of a PfCyRPA-based vaccine against blood-stage malaria.
articles.authors: Alam N, Wolfle C, Butkeviciute E, Quinkert D, King LDW, Higgins MK
articles.details: Structure-guided design created PfCyRPA-EM epitope mimic containing blade domains 1-2 with improved thermostability. Pre-clinical studies showed PfCyRPA-EM-elicited antibodies inhibited parasite growth at lower concentrations than wild-type PfCyRPA; improved expression as I53-50 nanoparticle fusion suitable for clinical development.

articles.headline: Zoonotic Plasmodium species prevalent in Malaysian macaques
articles.title_original: Molecular and phylogenetic analysis of zoonotic Plasmodium in long-tailed macaques: insights from wildlife-human conflict zones in Selangor, Malaysia.
articles.authors: Zamri SZ, Aziz NAA, Mazlan M, Saidu YS, Zulhisam A, Che-Amat A, Sipin Q
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 100 macaques in Selangor, Malaysia (September 2022-April 2023) found P. cynomolgi prevalence 24%, P. inui 23%, P. knowlesi 4%; 51% single infections, 18% dual infections, 2% triple infections. Phylogenetic analysis identified S-type and A-type lineages for all three species.

articles.headline: CD44 coreceptor enhances P. falciparum erythrocyte invasion
articles.title_original: With a little help from my friend: engaging CD44 facilitates Plasmodium falciparum invasion of erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Rénia L
articles.details: Study identified CD44 as host transmembrane coreceptor that, when crosslinked, significantly enhances P. falciparum erythrocyte invasion, linking parasite attachment to final invasion steps.

articles.headline: Parasite metabolic shift triggers mosquito-stage rRNA expression
articles.title_original: Shifting metabolism alters rRNA expression in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Hadjimichael E, Deitsch KW
articles.details: Study determined that altered NAD+/nicotinamide ratios from parasite metabolic shift during mosquito-stage transition initiate derepression of mosquito-specific rDNA loci.

articles.headline: Taurine suppresses symptoms in asymptomatic malaria infections
articles.title_original: How taurine keeps asymptomatic malaria infections in check.
articles.authors: Taylor TE
articles.details: Based on title only: describes taurine's role in maintaining asymptomatic state in malaria infections.

articles.headline: Ivermectin mass treatment cost-effective for malaria control
articles.title_original: Cost and cost-effectiveness of ivermectin mass drug administration for malaria control in Kwale county, Kenya: a modelling analysis of a cluster-randomised trial.
articles.authors: Xie K, Otuko R, Marathe A, Gutierrez AS, Kariuki M, Musyoka L, Yaa E, Mbarak J, Omondi I, Kasiwa L, Rabinovich R, Maia M, Chaccour C, Deng X, Rist C
articles.details: Cost-effectiveness analysis of BOHEMIA cluster-randomized trial in Kwale county, Kenya compared ivermectin MDA (monthly ×3, ~64% coverage) versus no intervention. iMDA reduced malaria incidence 26%; incremental cost-effectiveness ratio $905.23/DALY averted, below Kenya GDP threshold of $974.65. Cost-effective when reduction >23.62% in children <5 years.

articles.headline: Decision-making tools for complex malaria contexts
articles.title_original: A new tool at a complex time for malaria decision making.
articles.authors: Devine A, Kamya C
articles.details: Title only; appears to be perspective or opinion piece on tools for malaria decision-making without specific quantitative findings.

articles.headline: Taurine inhibits malaria-infected RBC adhesion via PfEMP1
articles.title_original: Longitudinal plasma metabolomics reveals a role for taurine in asymptomatic malaria and seasonal persistence.
articles.authors: Diffendall G, Millot P, Chen P, Fogang B, Dorin-Semblat D, Aprahamian F, Durand S, Gamain B, Claessens A, Scherf A
articles.details: Longitudinal plasma metabolomics of 199 individuals in Gambia found taurine levels increase during low-transmission periods. Taurine inhibited and prevented malaria-infected RBC adhesion via PfEMP1 to CD36 and endothelial protein C receptor, potentially reducing cerebral malaria and symptomatic infections.

articles.headline: Cyclin D3 reduction in erythroid cells protects malaria
articles.title_original: Reduced cyclin D3 expression in erythroid cells protects against malaria.
articles.authors: Marini MG, Mingoia M, Steri M, Tsamesidis I, Idda ML, Manca A, D'Avino C, Virdis F, Lodde V, Mulas A, Asunis I, Li X, Steiner MC, Loi A, Caria CA, Marongiu MF, Floris M, Marongiu M, Manunza L, Pitzalis M, Orrù V, Fiorillo E, Zoledziewska M, Moi P, Turrini FM, Pala M, Sidore C, Schlessinger D, Novembre J, Pantaleo A, Cucca F
articles.details: Study of rs112233623-T variant in CCND3 erythroid enhancer found it reduces CCND3 expression and inhibits G1-S cell-cycle transition. P. falciparum growth impaired in infected erythrocytes from carriers correlating with increased ROS levels; similar ROS-based mechanism as G6PD-based malaria protection.

articles.headline: Cell-cycle protein repression suppresses malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria is hindered by repression of a cell-cycle protein.
articles.authors: Band G
articles.details: Title only; appears to be editorial response to mechanistic finding about cell-cycle protein suppression in malaria.

articles.headline: Slow parasite clearance in P. vivax following artesunate in Cambodia
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance in patients with Plasmodium vivax monoinfection treated with artesunate in Cambodia: an observational secondary analysis of trial data.
articles.authors: Tebben K, Eng V, Seng D, Tat B, Feufack-Donfack LB, Orban A, Yeat R, Salvador J, Sin S, Ko K, Khim N, Flamand C, Sommen C, Lek D, Serre D, Popovici J
articles.details: Study of 161 P vivax infections from 87 patients (median age 20 years) treated with artesunate 2 mg/kg/day for 7 days. All infections cleared by day 3, but 49 (30%) displayed slow clearance, with 9 (6%) having parasite clearance slope half-life greater than 5 hours. Gene expression analysis of 31 infections showed slow-clearing parasites had delayed responses with downregulation of haemoglobin metabolism genes. Authors suggest slow clearance could allow parasites to outlast treatment and facilitate resistance emergence.

articles.headline: mRNA vaccine overcomes haemozoin-mediated vaccine failure in mice
articles.title_original: mRNA vaccination overcomes haemozoin-mediated impairment of whole-parasite malaria vaccines in mice.
articles.authors: Hassert M, Drewry LL, Pewe LL, Hancox LS, He R, Arumugam S, Mix MR, Salem AK, Harty JT
articles.details: Mouse study using P yoelii and P berghei showing previous blood-stage exposure impairs radiation-attenuated sporozoite (RAS) vaccine-induced CD8+ T cell responses via haemozoin-mediated dendritic cell dysfunction. An mRNA vaccine encoding Plasmodium CD8+ T cell epitopes restored impaired T cell responses. Combined RAS-plus-mRNA regimen enhanced liver-resident memory T cells and protection in malaria-experienced mice.

articles.headline: P falciparum development in G6PD-deficient erythroblasts unchanged in vitro
articles.title_original: Invasion and development of Plasmodium falciparum in erythroblasts of humans carrying G6PD viangchan.
articles.authors: Boonpeng K, Palasuwan A, Ketprasit N, Kulkeaw K, Palasuwan D
articles.details: In vitro study using erythroblasts from 6 subjects with normal, heterozygous, or hemizygous G6PD Viangchan (871G>A) variant. P falciparum successfully invaded and developed in polychromatic erythroblasts into mature trophozoites. No significant differences in parasitized erythroblast percentages or stages between G6PD groups. Results suggest G6PD deficiency does not provide protection in this in vitro erythroblast model.

articles.headline: ddPCR assays accurately quantify P vivax parasitemia and G6PD variants
articles.title_original: Development and implementation of droplet digital PCR assays for accurate quantification of Plasmodium vivax parasitemia and G6PD viangchan genotyping.
articles.authors: Srisutham S, Ausit K, Boonpeng K, Suksuwanont M, Kijprasong K
articles.details: Developed and validated duplex ddPCR assays for P vivax parasitemia quantification (limit of detection 3.2 parasites/µL, accuracy greater than 90%) and G6PD Viangchan genotyping (100% sensitivity and specificity). Applied to clinical samples from Kanchanaburi Province. Parasitemia ranged from 315 to 82,429 parasites/µL. Chloroquine treatment showed 99.2-99.9% reduction by day 2 with complete clearance by day 3.

articles.headline: Novel triple-drug combinations match standard artemisinin therapy efficacy
articles.title_original: Artesunate-pyronaridine-atovaquone-proguanil and artesunate-fosmidomycin-clindamycin compared with standard artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated malaria (MultiMal): a randomised, controlled, clinical, phase 2 trial in Gabon and Ghana.
articles.authors: Agobé JCD, Maïga-Ascofaré O, Adegnika AA, Pfaffendorf C, Boaheng JM, Edoa JR, Agyiri ID, Bayode Adegbite R, Bart-Plange EY, Ahenkan E, Ekoka Mbassi D, Sarpong FN, Placca E, Kanin DK, Bakari P, Loag W, Kettenbeil J, Krishna S, Lell B, Agnandji ST, Amuasi JH, Zoleko-Manego R, Mombo-Ngoma G, Kremsner PG, Wicha SG, Ramharter M, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Phase 2 randomized trial in two sites with 100 uncomplicated malaria patients (20 semi-immune adults, 40 adolescents, 40 children) comparing artesunate-pyronaridine (AP), artesunate-pyronaridine-atovaquone-proguanil (APAP), and artesunate-fosmidomycin-clindamycin (AFC, dosed 2/30/10 mg/kg twice daily). PCR-corrected day-42 ACPR: AP 87.5%, APAP 85.3%, AFC 94.4%. No differential efficacy (p=0.37). All regimens were safe with mild-to-moderate adverse events predominating.

articles.headline: Climate change projected to cause 123 million additional African malaria cases
articles.title_original: Projected impacts of climate change on malaria in Africa.
articles.authors: Symons TL, Moran A, Balzarolo A, Vargas C, Robertson M, Lubinda J, Saddler A, McPhail M, Harris J, Rozier J, Browne A, Amratia P, Bertozzi-Villa A, Bhatt S, Cameron E, Golding N, Smith DL, Noor AM, Rumisha SF, Palmer MD, Weiss DJ, Desai N, Potere D, Sukitsch N, Woods W, Gething PW
articles.details: Geotemporal model integrated 25 years of African climate, malaria, control, and socioeconomic data under SSP 2-4.5 scenario, projecting 2024-2050 impacts. Under current control levels: 123 million (range 49.5-203 million) additional cases and 532,000 (range 195,000-912,000) additional deaths. Extreme weather events were the primary driver, accounting for 79% of additional cases and 93% of additional deaths. Most increases stem from intensification in existing endemic areas rather than geographic range expansion.

articles.headline: CIK cells inhibit P falciparum parasitemia in vitro through cytolytic mechanisms
articles.title_original: Cytokine-induced killer (CIK) cells inhibit Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia through cytolytic effector activity in vitro.
articles.authors: Vaseq R, Ferkamchwi B, Weiher H, Lukacs-Kornek V, Mahleqa N, Hübner MP, Sharma A, Schmidt-Wolf IGH
articles.details: In vitro study demonstrated cytokine-induced killer (CIK) cells reduce P falciparum parasitemia within 24 hours through cytotoxic secretions including granzyme B, granulysin, and perforin, rather than cell-to-cell contact. Recombinant effectors acted synergistically; priming with P falciparum lysate-pulsed dendritic cells diminished cytotoxicity. CIK cells showed comparable efficacy to chloroquine, and slight enhancement when combined with artemisinin.

articles.headline: 27% febrile sub-microscopic malaria with 8% mixed infections in Central India
articles.title_original: High Burden of Febrile Sub-microscopic Plasmodium Mixed Infections in Central India: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Deora N, Pande V, Pal DK, Sharma H, Dube S, Sinha A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1030 febrile patients from four settings (June-November 2020) using dried blood spots and PCR. Results: 27% (280) infected with P falciparum and/or P vivax (18% mono-P falciparum, 0.5% mono-P vivax, 8% mixed). All 27% were sub-microscopic (undetected by microscopy or RDT). Nine mixed-infection patients followed 12 months showed no recurrences or clinical development. No significant differences observed between mono- and mixed infections.

articles.headline: EMAP3 identified on infected RBC surface as cytoadherence research platform
articles.title_original: Erythrocyte Membrane Protein 3 (EMAP3) Is Exposed on the Surface of the Plasmodium berghei Infected Red Blood Cell.
articles.authors: Hernandez SRC, Rashpa R, Jonsdottir TK, Paoletta MS, Ter Beek J, Díaz MR, Krol JMM, Chevalley-Maurel S, Ishizaki T, Berntsson RP, Janse CJ, Franke-Fayard B, Brochet M, Bushell ESC
articles.details: Study using P berghei-infected RBCs identified erythrocyte membrane protein 3 (EMAP3) as a newly exposed surface protein interacting with EMAP1. Parasites lacking EMAP3 showed no significant reduction in growth or sequestration, indicating EMAP3 is not a major CD36-binding protein. The outer-surface location of EMAP3 provides a scaffold for displaying P falciparum proteins to screen inhibitors of P falciparum cytoadherence in vivo.

articles.headline: Trust and reciprocal values drive community malaria intervention success
articles.title_original: The importance of values in a community-based response to malaria in Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic: results from a longitudinal, mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Keys H, Ureña K, Bardosh K, Taveras D, Desir L, Noland G
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in Santo Domingo of 489 households (cross-sectional survey) and 66 interviews with 39 key informants over two years. Trust module showed acceptable reliability (ordinal α = 0.70). Households rated institutions as very trustworthy but neighbors and neighborhood associations as somewhat trustworthy. Qualitative analysis identified two value domains: institutional trust and interpersonal solidarity/reciprocity, with both contributing to successful community-based malaria intervention.

articles.headline: Only 58.7% of P vivax patients complete 14-day primaquine course
articles.title_original: Suboptimal primaquine adherence in Plasmodium vivax malaria: Evidence from high-burden tribal districts in Odisha.
articles.authors: K AV, Thiruvengadam K, Sharma R, Perumalsamy N, Arumugam RD, Srirama S, Rahul A, Rahi M
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 269 P vivax patients (age greater than 1 year) in two high-burden tribal districts (Malkangiri, Koraput) from January-December 2024. Adherence assessed on days 7 and 14 via self-report, pill counts, and blister pack inspection. Chloroquine and artesunate-SP adherence exceeded 93%, but only 58.7% (95% CI 52.6-64.9) completed the 14-day primaquine course. Discontinuation was mainly due to symptom resolution (61.9%) and forgetfulness (21.4%); older age and household malaria history predicted better adherence.

articles.headline: Conserved Pk32 epitopes suggest blood-stage vaccine candidate for P knowlesi
articles.title_original: In silico profiling of the plasmodium knowlesi 32 kDa antigen: Diversity, epitope prediction, and structural modeling.
articles.authors: Saif A, Baruah P, Alghamdi JM, Acharyya R, Lau YL, Han JH, Ahmed MA
articles.details: In silico analysis of 39 Pk32 sequences from Malaysian Borneo showed low nucleotide diversity (π = 0.0061) and limited polymorphism. Natural selection tests provided strong evidence of purifying selection, indicating functional constraint. B-cell epitope prediction identified three common epitopes, with two (145PKERES150 and 167DIGKKQNS174) conserved in all sequences. Authors conclude limited genetic variation and stable conserved epitopes make Pk32 a compelling blood-stage vaccine candidate against P knowlesi.

articles.headline: Cold-chain-free LAMP test detects malaria parasites reliably
articles.title_original: A cold-chain free loop-mediated isothermal amplification test for detection of human malaria in point-of-care settings.
articles.authors: Rosli NN, Lau YL, Lai MY
articles.details: Developed sugar-based enzyme preservation for loop-mediated isothermal amplification assay for Plasmodium knowlesi detection without refrigeration. Sucrose-preserved LAMP reagent maintained Bst DNA polymerase activity for 12 days at room temperature, achieving analytical sensitivity of 10 copies/μL and 100% specificity against Plasmodium species and human DNA. 12-day room-temperature stability suits typical field deployments in remote clinics.

articles.headline: Clathrins regulate hemoglobin uptake in malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Clathrins Are Involved in the Endocytosis of Host Cytosol in the Malaria Parasite.
articles.authors: Miao J, Lucky AB, Ning G, Xia X, Liang X, Siddiqui F, Min H, Wang C, Li X, Zhou ZH, Cui L
articles.details: Characterized clathrin heavy chain (PfCHC) and light chain (PfCLC) localization in P. falciparum at parasite periphery and cis-Golgi. Live-cell imaging and ultrastructural studies confirmed association with AP-1 components and presence at cytostome collars. PfCHC knockdown produced abnormally long cytostome tubes and impaired hemoglobin uptake, indicating clathrins mediate cytostome-based nutrient acquisition.

articles.headline: Three new Kelch-13 mutations identified in P. falciparum from Nigeria
articles.title_original: Three new single nucleotide polymorphisms identified in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Kogi State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akoma ON, Ike AC, Ayogu EE, Agbo MC, Ezeh CC, Okoh FN, Arome SO, Ezeonu IM
articles.details: Screened 40 P. falciparum-positive blood samples from symptomatic patients for drug-resistance markers Pfmdr1 and Kelch-13. Kelch-13 sequences showed 99.88% similarity to wild-type 3D7 reference. Identified three non-synonymous mutations (K430E, W706C, Q707K) in separate isolates, none previously associated with artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: ACT quality varies across East Africa; cure rates remain high
articles.title_original: Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapies in East Africa: A Systematic Review of Quality, Clinical Efficacy, and Regulatory Progress Towards Sustainable Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Mekasha YT, Tegegne AA, Mashe T, Girma M, Hasen G, Belew S
articles.details: Systematic review of 40 articles testing 3,363 artemisinin-based combination therapy samples across four countries. Overall 11.2% failure rate for quality specifications: Uganda 33.3%, Kenya 14.1%, Tanzania 11.6%, Ethiopia 4.2%. Despite poor ACT quality, therapeutic trials reported ≥90% polymerase chain reaction-corrected cure rates. Emerging Kelch-13 mutations associated with delayed parasite clearance pose ongoing threat to ACT effectiveness.

articles.headline: Amodiaquine inhibits P. falciparum plasmepsin V efficiently
articles.title_original: Inhibiting catalytic activity of Plasmodium falciparum aspartate protease plasmepsin V: A biochemical approach to malaria intervention.
articles.authors: Prajapati AK, Kumar Y, Choudhary S
articles.details: Investigated quinoline drugs amodiaquine and clioquinol as inhibitors of P. falciparum plasmepsin V aspartate protease. Amodiaquine demonstrated moderate binding affinity and inhibited PfPlmV with IC50 0.42 ± 0.04 μM via competitive inhibition. Clioquinol showed binding but no inhibitory effect. Molecular docking revealed amodiaquine engages catalytic dyad residues Asp118 and Asp365.

articles.headline: Ilex umbellulata bark suppresses malaria parasites in mice
articles.title_original: Sub-acute toxicity studies and dynamics-based molecular insights on the in vivo antimalarial activity of Ilex umbellulata (Wall.) Loes bark in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Zothantluanga JH, Tayeng D, Zonunmawii, Rahman O, Abdalla M, Chetia D
articles.details: Tested 80% methanol extract of I. umbellulata bark in P. berghei-infected mice. Lethal dose 50% >2000 mg/kg; safe for 28 days oral administration. 200 mg/kg dose produced lowest parasitaemia (5.00 ± 0.97%), highest parasite suppression (85.65 ± 2.77%), and longest post-infection survival (15.33 ± 2.82 days). Molecular studies suggest cyanidin-3-O-rutinoside inhibits enoyl-acyl-carrier-protein reductase.

articles.headline: Three medicinal plants show strong antiplasmodial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: In vitro antiplasmodial activity, acute toxicity and phytochemical quantification of selected medicinal plants used for the management of uncomplicated malaria in eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Obakiro SB, Nabitandikwa C, Kiyimba K, Ocan M, Gavamukulya Y, Andima M, Lukwago TW, Maseruka R, Chebijira M, Opio M, Hokello JF, Kibuule D, Nabatanzi A, Orena S, Waako P, Owor RO
articles.details: Evaluated five medicinal plants for in vitro antiplasmodial activity against P. falciparum laboratory strains and clinical isolates using SYBR Green assay. Zanthoxylum chalybeum most potent (IC50 0.81–1.28 μg/ml), followed by Albizia coriaria and Entada abyssinica (IC50 10–50 μg/ml). Maytenus senegalensis showed weak activity; Kigelia africana largely inactive (IC50 >100 μg/ml).

articles.headline: IPTp-sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine reduces parasitaemia and anaemia in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Comparative effect of chemoprevention in malaria infection during pregnancy: A network meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Naing C, Ni H, Htet NH, Aung HH, Anamalai Y, Ahmed SI, Whittaker MA
articles.details: Network meta-analysis of 22 randomized controlled trials (N=14,705) comparing intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy. Moderate certainty evidence that monthly IPTp-sulphadoxine/pyrimethamine reduced maternal parasitaemia and anaemia versus placebo. Low-certainty evidence of fewer low birth weight cases with IPTp-SP than dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine combined with azithromycin. Recommends future head-to-head trials comparing various antimalarial regimens and assessment of cost-effectiveness.

articles.headline: Rare complications of severe malaria with artesunate treatment
articles.title_original: Acute Acalculous Cholecystitis and Post-artesunate Hemolytic Anemia: Two Rare Complications in Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Nunes CF, Dias JV, Nunes BS, Gouveia C, Varandas L
articles.details: No abstract provided; cannot extract specific study design, sample size, or quantitative findings from title alone.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial exonuclease essential for malaria blood-stage infection
articles.title_original: Plasmodium mitochondrial exodeoxyribonucleases, Exo(mit1) and Exo(mit2), are conserved within alveolates with Exo(mit1) essential for the establishment of blood-stage infection.
articles.authors: Mishra S, Chatterjee T, Mehra P, Gahlawat A, Pradhan S, Gupta R, Bhattacharyya MK, Mishra S, Habib S
articles.details: Identified two P. falciparum mitochondrial exonucleases (PfExomit1 and PfExomit2) with distinct DNA substrate specificities. PfExomit2 interacted with mismatch repair protein PfMutS and double-strand break repair protein PfRad51. Conditional knockout of P. berghei Exomit1 in sporozoites: parasites invaded hepatocytes and developed in liver but failed transition to blood stage, indicating mitochondrial DNA processing role in blood-stage establishment.

articles.headline: Hydrogel microarray patches improve antimalarial drug delivery
articles.title_original: Formulation and evaluation of hydrogel-forming microarray patches for transdermal primaquine and chloroquine delivery in malaria therapy.
articles.authors: Anjani QK, Volpe-Zanutto F, Miatmoko A, Adhami M, Novais JT, Gaitán XA, Hariawan BS, Cahyani DM, Octavia RT, Mubarok AS, Setyaningsih WAW, Bargieri DY, Donnelly RF
articles.details: Developed hydrogel-forming microarray patches loaded with primaquine and chloroquine using optimized lyophilized reservoirs. Reservoirs dissolved rapidly (7 seconds chloroquine, 16 seconds primaquine). In vitro delivery achieved approximately 40 mg per drug (70% efficiency) over 24 hours. Rat pharmacokinetics: Cmax increased to 1.68 ng/ml (chloroquine) and 47.20 ng/ml (primaquine) versus oral. Murine malaria model reduced parasitaemia by 99.8%.

articles.headline: Lumefantrine exposure lower in pregnant than non-pregnant women
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of lumefantrine in pregnant and non-pregnant women with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Juma E, Ding J, Ongas M, Koskei N, Onyango K, Oloo F, Aman R, Kokwaro G, Tarning J, Ogutu B
articles.details: Population pharmacokinetic study of 75 women with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria (25 non-pregnant, 30 second-trimester, 20 third-trimester) receiving artemether-lumefantrine 80/480 mg twice daily for 3 days. Pregnancy increased elimination clearance by 23% and central volume of distribution by 28%. Pregnant women showed lower area under curve (geometric mean ratio 0.76), maximum concentration (0.79), and Day 7 concentration (0.69). 28-day polymerase chain reaction-corrected cure rate 100% both groups.

articles.headline: Review of Plasmodium-Anopheles molecular interactions for control strategies
articles.title_original: Deciphering the molecular targets of Plasmodium and Anopheline interactions for malaria control.
articles.authors: Janjoter S, Kataria D, Dahiya N, Yadav M, Singh H, Garg S, Sehrawat N
articles.details: This review explores molecular interactions between Plasmodium ookinetes and the mosquito midgut, and sporozoites and salivary glands, along with mosquito immune defenses and parasite transmission genes. Understanding these interactions may inform novel vector control strategies for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Malaria-resistance genetic variants in Colombian Pacific population
articles.title_original: Prevalence of the Main Human Genetic Variants Related to Resistance to Malaria in a Population of the Colombian Pacific Coast.
articles.authors: Ortega DC, Arango MP, Cañón S, Cárdenas H, González R, Barreto G
articles.details: Study of 819 individuals found Duffy (72.2%), HbS (3.1%), HbC (2.2%), G6PD (11%), and β-thalassemia variants at combined frequencies of 4.9%. Adolescents and young adults aged 13-26 years and residents of insular zones showed highest proportions of resistance genotypes. Findings identify specific age groups and communities warranting targeted public health interventions.

articles.headline: Bacterial larvicides reduced vector density but showed limited malaria impact
articles.title_original: Systematic literature review of bacterial larvicides Bacillus thuringiensis var israelensis and Bacillus (Lysinibacillus) sphaericus for malaria control in Africa.
articles.authors: Mataba GR
articles.details: Systematic review of 54 studies found bacterial larvicides (Bti and Bs) reduced primary malaria vector larval density by 50-100% in most studies, but only 14 reported subsequent malaria incidence reductions of 31-62%. Only one large-scale routine program evaluation was found. Reviewed studies reveal serious lack of large-scale evidence and integration into routine malaria control programs.

articles.headline: Weakening TPR-MBER correlation marks progress toward malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Weakening TPR-MBER correlation: Marker of success in India's malaria elimination progress.
articles.authors: Prajapat K, Libny YS, Syed Ashish EA, Prasad P, Yalavarthi A, Das A, Chaudhary K, Jain I
articles.details: Analysis of 2020-2024 data showed progressively weakening correlation between Monthly Blood Examination Rate and Test Positivity Rate, indicating improved surveillance saturation and reduced underlying transmission. This correlation may serve as a dynamic indicator for monitoring progress toward malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 40.5% among febrile patients in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated risk factors of malaria among febrile patients at Kidist Hana Health Center Fogera District, Amhara Regional State, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Alebele S, Abate A, Sisay M, Asnake T, Alehegn M, Adisu D, Munshea A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of febrile patients found 40.5% malaria prevalence by microscopy, with P. falciparum comprising 78.4% of cases. Risk factors included age <5 years, widowed marital status, low household income, lack of insecticide-treated net ownership, stagnant water near residence, and outdoor sleeping. Rapid diagnostic test showed 95.91% sensitivity and 91.24% specificity compared to microscopy.

articles.headline: Biocredit Pf/Pv RDT shows 91% sensitivity for P. falciparum detection
articles.title_original: Diagnostic accuracy of novel Biocredit species-specific LDH-based immunochromatographic assay for Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax detection: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Gebremeskele BT, Getu E, Demeke AD, Bogale A, Churiso G
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 19 datasets from 8 studies (3923 participants) evaluated Biocredit LDH-based rapid diagnostic test. Pooled sensitivity was 91% for P. falciparum (95% CI, 77-97%) and 96% for P. vivax (95% CI, 88-99%), with pooled specificity of 99% and 100% respectively. Against microscopy, sensitivity was 95% for P. falciparum and 97% for P. vivax. The assay shows promise especially in settings with prevalent HRP2 gene deletions.

articles.headline: P. falciparum MSP genetic diversity higher in Myanmar border regions
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity in border areas of Myanmar assessed by genotyping merozoite surface protein genes.
articles.authors: Ma J, Wu Y, Zeng W, Chen X, Xiang Z, Li C, Zhang Y, Zhao H, Duan M, Liang T, Mou Y, Tang X, Liu C, Kyaw MT, Thu AN, Kyaw MP, Cui L, Yang Z
articles.details: Study of 176 P. falciparum samples found MAD20 as most frequent Msp1 allele (89% in Myitsone, 85% in Bhamo, 67% in Paletwa) followed by K1 (65% and 44%), while 3D7 predominated in Msp2. Multiplicity of infection from Msp1 was 1.87 in migrant workers, 1.33 in Bhamo residents, and 1.56 in Paletwa residents. Migrant workers had higher multi-infection rates (72%) than local residents (43-45%), suggesting lower immunity or higher transmission.

articles.headline: Heme oxygenase-1 exhibits dual protective and pathological roles in malaria
articles.title_original: Heme oxygenase-1 and malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Wakai T, Chinedu S, Afolabi I
articles.details: Review of heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) in malaria pathogenesis shows strong induction during infection. In murine models, HO-1 induction confers protection against severe complications including cerebral malaria and organ dysfunction through modulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines and vascular permeability. However, elevated HO-1 in specific immune cell populations associates with heightened inflammatory responses and severity in humans, highlighting context-dependent effects. HO-1 is discussed as potential diagnostic and therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Deep-sea metabolites show promise as novel PfDHODH inhibitor candidates
articles.title_original: Structure-Based Screening of Deep-Sea Microbial Metabolites Against Plasmodium falciparum Dihydroorotate Dehydrogenase.
articles.authors: Singh A, Rengasamy KRR, Benjakul S
articles.details: In silico screening of 1549 deep-sea microbial metabolites against P. falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase identified five lead compounds with Glide scores (-12.02 to -10.61 kcal/mol) superior to primaquine (-6.920 kcal/mol). Top compounds—(±)-puniceusine P, aspergilol F, tersaphilone C, 4-carbglyceryl-3,3'-dihydroxy-5,5'-dimethyldiphenyl ether, and 15-O-methyl ML-236A—demonstrated stable protein-ligand complexes and acceptable ADME properties. These findings highlight deep-sea metabolites as promising antimalarial candidates requiring experimental validation.

articles.headline: Malaria attributable fraction 54-64% in longitudinal Uganda cohort study
articles.title_original: Estimating malaria attributable fraction using quantitative PCR in a longitudinal cohort in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Martin AC, Wang Q, Babirye S, Arinaitwe E, Zedi M, Ssewanyana I, Namirimu FN, Nayebare P, Olwoch P, Tukwasibwe S, Jagannathan P, Nankabirwa JI, Kamya M, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Briggs J, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort of 659 individuals followed up to 3 years estimated malaria attributable fraction (MAF) from qPCR parasite densities. Overall MAF ranged 54-64%, highest in children <5 years (72%), intermediate in 5-15 year-olds (56%), and lowest in adults >16 (45%). Nearly 50% of fevers with low-moderate parasite density (10-100/μL) were attributed to malaria. MAF-corrected incidence substantially exceeded standard clinical definitions, suggesting conventional parasite density thresholds underestimate true incidence.

articles.headline: TGF-β-Tim-3 axis protects macrophages during Plasmodium yoelii infection
articles.title_original: TGF-β regulated Tim-3 sustains macrophage phagocytic function and confers protection in Plasmodium yoelii NSM-infected mice.
articles.authors: Xie X, Liang G, Chen M, Luo L, Yuan H, Chen S, Lu K, Peng W, Xu L, Xie H, Li L, Zhao S, Wei H, Pan X, Huang J
articles.details: In murine P. yoelii NSM infection, Tim-3 expression on splenic macrophages was infection-inducibly downregulated. Tim-3+ macrophages exhibited enhanced antigen presentation and proinflammatory phenotypes. In vivo Tim-3 blockade exacerbated disease severity, increased parasitemia, and impaired macrophage phagocytosis. TGF-β was identified as key upstream regulator of Tim-3; TGF-β-Tim-3 signaling potentiated macrophage phagocytosis of infected red blood cells and protected against cell death. Results reveal a protective TGF-β-Tim-3 axis maintaining macrophage immune homeostasis.

articles.headline: GIS mapping identifies 38.3% of study area highly vulnerable to malaria
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria vulnerability hotspots using multi-criteria decision analysis, GIS, and remote sensing: a case study in Abaya Woreda, West Guji Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Diriba D, Demise D, Kenate B, Gobena D, Lemi M, Teferi N, Karuppannan S, Churiso G
articles.details: Geospatial analysis using GIS and remote sensing identified malaria vulnerability hotspots. Of the mapped region, 38.3% was classified as highly vulnerable, 49.1% as moderately vulnerable, and 12.6% as low vulnerability. High vulnerability areas associated with lower elevations, wetlands, water bodies, greater distances from health facilities, and high population density. Findings provide data-driven insights for evidence-based malaria control and prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Iron deficiency protects against Plasmodium falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Host iron deficiency protects against Plasmodium infection and drives parasite molecular reprofiling.
articles.authors: Clucas D, Bennett C, Harding R, Pettikiriarachchi A, Baldi A, Randall LM, Steel R, Mellin R, Hobbs M, Caiazzo S, Mwangi MN, Fielding KL, Hickey PF, Baldwin TM, Amann-Zalcenstein D, Emery-Corbin SJ, Mzembe G, Moya E, Braat S, Jex A, Demir AY, Verhoef H, Phiri KS, Biggs BA, Tham WH, Boddey JA, Pasricha SR, Ataíde R
articles.details: Clinical and experimental study examined iron-deficiency mechanisms using 711 pregnant women from REVAMP trial, genetic mouse model, and in vitro cultures. Iron deficiency was associated with 50% reduced P. falciparum parasitemia (95% CI 30-64%, p<0.0001) and improved survival in iron-deficient mice (15.5 vs 7.0 days) with 83% vs 17% protection from cerebral malaria. Intravenous iron with malaria prevention did not increase parasitemia. Authors concluded iron deficiency protects against Plasmodium and supports WHO recommendations for iron supplementation in endemic regions with adequate prevention.

articles.headline: Elevated vascular and immune biomarkers in Plasmodium-SARS-CoV-2 coinfection
articles.title_original: Elevated ANG2/ACE2 and immune responses associated with Plasmodium falciparum and SARS-CoV-2 coinfection in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchoupe EB, Kameni MN, Eric MB, Taya JB, Kamdem SD, Numfor Nkah L, Ama Moor V, Tepa A, Neba FR, Afum-Adjei Awuah A, Amuasi JH, Netongo PM
articles.details: Study of 96 participants (COVID-19 n=28, malaria n=28, co-infection n=16, controls n=24) measured biochemical and immunological markers. Co-infected showed elevated ALT, AST, urea, creatinine, erythropoietin, IFN-γ, and IL-1β; ANG2 highest in COVID-19 and co-infection; ACE2 markedly elevated in COVID-19 (p<0.01). ACE2 demonstrated strong discriminatory power for severity (AUC 0.77 for malaria, 0.85 for COVID-19). Authors concluded vascular and immune biomarkers have diagnostic and prognostic value for early risk stratification in malaria-COVID-19 co-infection.

articles.headline: Diagnostic-resistant hrp2/3-deleted P. falciparum spread despite minimal RDT use
articles.title_original: Malaria control and the unexpected spread of diagnostic-resistant Plasmodium falciparum in Peru.
articles.authors: Gyuricza IG, Fola AA, Simkin A, Thwai KL, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Johri P, Henry CM, Cabrera-Sosa L, Porras-Laymito G, Cheng Q, Watson OJ, Gamboa D, Valdivia HO, Parr JB
articles.details: Molecular analysis of 1,215 P. falciparum samples from Peru (2003-2018) used MIP sequencing across >2,000 loci. Marked reduction in genetic diversity with increased clonality and fixation of hrp2-/3- deleted parasites. IBD analysis revealed rapid expansion of single hrp2-/3- dominant lineage in post-PAMAFRO period. Simulations indicated selection coefficient s≥0.03 required for fixation; genetic bottleneck increased fixation likelihood 4.5-17 fold. Authors concluded hrp2-/3- fixation driven by combination of demographic changes from malaria control and selective advantage unrelated to RDT use.

articles.headline: Post-transfusion malaria case reports from non-endemic African countries
articles.title_original: Post-transfusion Malaria in Morocco and Non-endemic African Countries: A Systematic Review of Reported Cases.
articles.authors: Harandou M, Nejjari MA, Naciri M, Yahyaoui A, Hami A
articles.details: Systematic review identified 20 post-transfusion malaria (PTM) cases with 6 sufficiently detailed. P. falciparum predominated; P. malariae also documented. Fever days after transfusion was most common presentation; most cases involved immunocompromised or medically complex recipients diagnosed by blood smear. Authors concluded PTM remains rare but clinically important in non-endemic countries, highlighting need for strengthened hemovigilance and refined donor risk assessment.

articles.headline: UAV dataset and deep learning for malaria vector breeding site detection
articles.title_original: High-resolution UAV dataset for identification of breeding sites for malaria and dengue vectors in rural and peri-urban Tanzania: An experimental approach with Gemma 3N E4B architecture.
articles.authors: Nyambo DG, Lyimo TM, Sauli E
articles.details: High-resolution UAV dataset from Tanzania comprised 471 aerial images with 80.5% containing useful information from 19 classes of mosquito breeding sites. Gemma 3 N E4B multimodal model training showed exceptional convergence with 96.7% reduction in loss (9.620 to 0.319) within 117 steps. Authors demonstrated the approach as effective for surveillance of potential breeding sites in rural and peri-urban settings.

articles.headline: Systematic review of gene expression, docking, and machine learning in drug discovery
articles.title_original: Gene Expression, Docking and Machine Learning in Malaria Drug Discovery: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Dangana RS, Ebhohimen IE, Malgwi SA, Ugbaja SC, Okpeku M
articles.details: PRISMA-guided systematic review of 64 studies (2014-2024) on computational methods for drug discovery. Molecular docking was dominant technique (32.37%), followed by in vitro assays (14.39%), ADMET (10.79%), gene expression (3.60%). Compounds like isorhamnetin and myricetin showed high binding affinities (ΔG < -13 kcal/mol); machine learning models achieved AUC up to 0.87; flavonoids and terpenoids identified as candidates. Authors noted great potential but identified in vivo gaps and methodology inconsistency limiting clinical translation.

articles.headline: Community awareness of climate change link to malaria in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Community perceptions on climate change and its impacts on malaria transmission in South-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Nshatsi NC, Mponzi WP, Mwalugelo YA, Msaky DS, Simbeye S, Rite E, Okumu FO, Philbert A, Angelo T, Kaindoa EW
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in Tanzania surveyed 384 community members plus 11 key informant interviews and 12 focus groups (72 participants). Results showed 86% experienced climate-related changes; 67.5% recognized climate-malaria link; 59.5% reported increased risk; 70% indicated higher malaria during rainy season. Only 26.6% received regular climate-health information despite 96.6% wanting it. Authors concluded communities demonstrated climate-impact awareness and highlighted need for integrating community perspectives into climate-adaptation and malaria-control strategies.

articles.headline: Health workers' barriers and facilitators to severe malaria guideline adherence
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers to adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines in children at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda: a health workers' perspective.
articles.authors: Chelangat SC, Nakawuki A, Orech S, Oboth P, Nekaka R, Iramiot JS, Mbwali I, Chalo RN, Epuitai J, Adongo PR, Ssenyonga LVN, Olowo S
articles.details: Qualitative study at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda interviewed 21 healthcare providers. Key facilitators: resource availability/training, working conditions, monitoring tools, patient cooperation. Barriers: resource constraints, staffing/workload issues, lab delays, low CME attendance, patient financial limitations. Authors concluded addressing resource shortages, improving staffing/workload conditions, and strengthening health system infrastructure are essential for adherence to severe malaria management guidelines.

articles.headline: Phylogenomic evidence that early hominins drove Anopheles vector anthropophily evolution
articles.title_original: Early hominin arrival in Southeast Asia triggered the evolution of major human malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Singh US, Harbach RE, Hii J, Chang MS, Somboon P, Prakash A, Sarma D, Broomfield BS, Morgan K, Albert S, Das A, Linton YM, Carlton JM, Walton C
articles.details: Phylogenomic analysis of Leucosphyrus Group Anopheles examined 2,657 genes from 40 individuals of 11 species. Molecular dating revealed monkey-feeding is ancestral (Pliocene). Anthropophily evolved once in early Pleistocene via adaptive introgression in response to early hominin arrival (~1.8 Mya). Authors concluded findings provide independent non-archaeological evidence supporting early hominin colonization in Southeast Asia.

articles.headline: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates mitotic spindle formation and identifies drug target
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.authors: Nagar A, Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Abel S, Pashley SL, Mishra A, Eze A, Rea E, Brady D, Bottrill AR, Vaughan S, Le Roch KG, Guttery DS, Holder AA, Tromer EC, Sharma P, Tewari R
articles.details: Study identified Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as key regulator of spindle formation in both Plasmodium asexual and sexual stages. Conditional ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle biogenesis, kinetochore segregation, and transmission. Interactome analysis showed ARK1 forms non-canonical chromosomal passenger complex with divergent INCENP proteins. Authors concluded ARK1-INCENP interface represents potential multistage target for antimalarial intervention.

articles.headline: Combining seasonal chemoprevention with multi-stage therapeutics improves malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Combining seasonal malaria chemoprevention with novel therapeutics for malaria prevention: a mathematical modelling study.
articles.authors: Braunack-Mayer L, Malinga J, Nekkab N, Kelly SL, Möhrle JJ, Penny MA
articles.details: Individual-based malaria transmission model evaluated combining novel therapeutics with seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) in children under five. Pre-liver stage therapeutic with >50% efficacy required >230 days protection half-life to reduce severe cases by >23% with three SMC cycles; longer protection needed for more cycles. Multi-stage therapeutics increased public health impact. Authors concluded clinical development should prioritize multi-stage therapeutics for improved malaria prevention.

articles.headline: MMV1581361 PfATP4 inhibitor shows transmission-blocking potential in mouse model
articles.title_original: Identification of Compound 15 (MMV1581361) as a PfATP4 Inhibitor with Transmission-Blocking Activity and In Vivo Efficacy in a SCID Mouse Model of Malaria.
articles.authors: Onyame HA, Brand S, Baud D, Basarab GS, Hawryluk N, Canan S, Togoh MD, Abdulai R, Eduku R, Awal M, Owoare RB, Chatterjee B, Singha Roy D, Mahapatra AKR, Bhowmik S, Putatunda P, Crespo B, Sanz L, Calvo D, Wittlin S, Tumwebaze PK, Borrows J, Amewu RK
articles.details: Structure-activity study identified compound 15 (MMV1581361) with nanomolar activity against drug-sensitive, resistant, and field P. falciparum isolates. In vivo testing showed efficacy at 25 mg/kg dose similar to chloroquine. Achieved 90% inhibition of male gamete exflagellation, 98% reduction in oocyst intensity, 88% transmission block. Mechanism-of-action studies revealed compound functions as PfATP4 inhibitor. Authors noted development was deprioritized due to resistance risk and more advanced candidates.

articles.headline: Corrigendum: PfCDPK1 interaction with Raf kinase inhibitor
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Plasmodium falciparum Raf kinase inhibitor is a lipid binding protein that interacts with and regulates the activity of PfCDPK1, an essential plant-like kinase required for red blood cell invasion" [Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 749 (2025) 151350].
articles.authors: Sharma M, Krishnan D, Singh A, Negi P, Rani K, Revikumar A, Munde M, Bansal A
articles.details: Correction notice to a previously published article describing Plasmodium falciparum Raf kinase inhibitor as a lipid-binding protein that interacts with PfCDPK1, an essential plant-like kinase required for red blood cell invasion.

articles.headline: Microscopy-PCR reveals pediatric malaria misdiagnosis in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Unmasking Malaria: Microscopy and nPCR Reveal the Hidden Misdiagnosis of Plasmodium Infections Among Febrile Pediatric in Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getie S, Mekonnen GG, Worku L, Birhanie M, Meda AL, Abere A, Noedl H
articles.details: Institution-based cross-sectional study of 302 malaria-suspected participants found 22.2% prevalence by microscopy versus 18.2% by nested PCR, with highest prevalence (25.6%) in children ≥5 years. Age, lack of bed net use, and poor awareness of malaria symptoms were significantly associated with infection (p<0.05). Results highlight the importance of improving microscopic diagnostic skills and standardized procedures to reduce misdiagnosis.

articles.headline: Anopheles cruzii comprises five cryptic species in Atlantic Forest
articles.title_original: Anopheles (Kerteszia) cruzii, the main malaria vector in the Brazilian Atlantic Forest, is a complex of at least five cryptic species.
articles.authors: Voges K, Dias GR, Dupim EG, Pitaluga AN, Vanderlinde T, Carvalho Pinto CJ, Rezende HR, Uno F, Forrester SJ, Chong J, Carvalho AB, Rona LDP
articles.details: Phylogenomic analysis of 55 Anopheles genomes identified at least five cryptic species within An. cruzii s.l. with high genetic differentiation (FST 0.4–0.7) and evidence of sympatry, and confirmed three distinct lineages within An. bellator s.l. An. homunculus appears as a single species (FST ~0.2). These findings suggest vector competence may differ among cryptic species, with implications for malaria transmission in the Atlantic Forest.

articles.headline: Host kinase phosphosignaling regulates P. vivax hypnozoite formation
articles.title_original: Host kinase regulation of Plasmodium vivax dormant and replicating liver stages.
articles.authors: Glennon EKK, Wei L, Roobsoong W, Primavera VI, van Zyl EM, Tongogara T, Yee CB, Sattabongkot J, Kaushansky A
articles.details: Kinase regression analysis identified host kinases regulating numbers of P. vivax schizonts and hypnozoites in liver stages, with variable phosphosignaling dependencies observed across patient isolates. The study reveals differences in host kinase dependencies between replicating and dormant parasite forms, suggesting a potential mechanism underlying variable relapse phenotypes in P. vivax infections.
