articles.headline: Bradykinin-mediated vasogenic edema drives cerebral malaria pathology
articles.title_original: Bradykinin Contributes to Vasogenic Edema in Murine Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Pinheiro AS, Teixeira DE, Silva-Aguiar RP, Shim YJ, Merkulova AA, Silbak S, Skomorovska-Prokvolit Y, Midem D, Ogolla S, Burckhardt BB, Gangnus T, Scharfstein J, Caruso-Neves C, McCarty OJT, Gailani D, Bader M, Rosenthal PJ, Dent AE, Janse CJ, McCrae KR, Pinheiro AAS, Kazura JW, Schmaier AH
articles.details: Study of Kenyan children and experimental mice found 40% of cerebral malaria cases had elevated cleaved high-molecular-weight kininogen (cHK, a bradykinin release marker) versus 18% with uncomplicated malaria. Genetic deletion of bradykinin receptors (Bdkrb1-/-, Bdkrb2-/-) or kininogen (Kng1-/-) protected mice from neurologic deterioration and brain edema. Adjuvant kallikrein inhibition combined with artesunate reversed neurologic deterioration and prolonged survival versus artesunate alone.

articles.headline: GFP-destabilization domain reporter monitors malaria proteasome activity
articles.title_original: Profiling 26S Proteasome Activity of Plasmodium falciparum Monitored by a Live-Cell Assay.
articles.authors: Gonçalves AF, Lima-Pinheiro A, Sampaio-Marques B, Ferreira PE
articles.details: Researchers developed a P. falciparum line expressing GFP fused to a destabilization domain (GFP-DD) to quantitatively measure proteasomal activity in live parasites. GFP-DD fluorescence inversely correlates with proteasome activity, increasing upon Shield-1-mediated stabilization or MG132 proteasome inhibition with dose-dependent response. This live-cell platform offers a practical tool for monitoring proteasome function and screening antimalarial compounds targeting this essential pathway.

articles.headline: Scoping review protocol on urban malaria in Bamako
articles.title_original: Trends in malaria indicators in urban and peri-urban areas of Bamako: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Toure M, Keita S, Keita B, Arou AZ, Diallo I, Keita M, Kane F, Sanogo D, Diallo M, Tangara CO, Kone A, Magassa MH, Sogoba N, Diakite M, Doumbia S
articles.details: This scoping review protocol will synthesize malaria epidemiology data from urban and peri-urban Bamako between 2002 and 2025, focusing on prevalence, incidence, clinical manifestations, and transmission trends influenced by rapid urbanization and emerging vectors like Anopheles stephensi. Data will be extracted from literature searches, national malaria control program reports, and academic theses to inform tailored urban malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria species widespread in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Distribution of falciparum and non-falciparum malaria among symptomatic malaria patients in Dschang, West Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchuenkam PVK, White SJ, Potlapalli V, Keming EM, Muller MS, Kaunda DBN, Kharabora O, Dinglasan RR, Parr JB, Tume CB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ, Ali IM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 431 suspected malaria cases found overall prevalence of 54.8%, with P. falciparum comprising 53.4% of infections, P. ovale 4%, and P. malariae 0.9%. Mixed infections occurred in 8.9% of cases, with 6 P. ovale curtisi and 3 P. ovale wallikeri identified among 12 genotyped P. ovale infections. Despite P. falciparum dominance, findings highlight the importance of continued surveillance for non-falciparum species.

articles.headline: Eight antenatal visits improve malaria prevention in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Impact of eight or more antenatal care visits on intermittent preventive treatment of malaria uptake during pregnancy and facility-based delivery in Ghana: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Agbi DM, Mwebesa E, Jimmy AI, Valère SK, Kerich GK
articles.details: Propensity score-matched analysis of 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey data found that adherence to WHO's recommendation of eight or more antenatal care (ANC8+) visits increased facility-based delivery by 8.2 percentage points (ATT 8.2, 95% CI 4.6–11.9) and optimal intermittent preventive treatment uptake (≥3 IPTp-SP doses) by 11.1 percentage points (ATT 11.1, 95% CI 6.8–15.4).

articles.headline: Malaria parasite RNA disrupts host immune cell splicing
articles.title_original: Nuclear import of malaria RNA rewires splicing in host immune cells.
articles.authors: Abou Karam P, Kiper E, Ziv T, Yadid S, Kozela E, Zharoni N, Nevo R, Alfandari D, Otesh H, Cruz Camacho A, Lubelsky Y, Rotkopf R, Weizman E, Cossin M, Rosenhek-Goldian I, Petrovich-Kopitman E, Porat Z, Shoshani O, Ulitsky I, Levy C, Melamed Z, Regev-Rudzki N
articles.details: Study found that Plasmodium falciparum mRNAs exported from infected red blood cells are imported into host immune cell nuclei, where they bind host RNA-binding proteins ACIN1 and PNN (key components of the splicing machinery). This interaction disrupts host splicing regulation, causing widespread misprocessing of transcripts and altered expression of immune-related proteins.

articles.headline: Review of diagnostic methods for malaria and protozoan diseases
articles.title_original: Diagnostic methods for protozoan diseases: a review focused on leishmaniasis, Chagas disease and malaria.
articles.authors: Matos ADS, Rodrigues-da-Silva RN, Toledo TS, Ataides LS, da Motta ND, Rodolphi CM, Soares IF, Ribeiro FN, Alencar ALC, Maia FM, Lima-Junior JDC, Morgado FN
articles.details: Review examines diagnostic approaches for malaria, leishmaniasis, and Chagas disease, discussing immunological and molecular diagnostic tools and their application in endemic areas. The authors emphasize the importance of integrating clinical and epidemiological information with sensitive and specific diagnostic techniques to promote early detection, monitoring, and control of infections in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Malnutrition increases malaria risk despite seasonal chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Malaria and malnutrition in children under the Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) coverage in the health district of Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Compaore EW, Sondo P, Kabore B, Rouamba T, Kouevi AFC, Bayala IMC, Kambou SAE, Millogo KS, Bouda I, Ouedraogo DF, Hien SF, Kazienga A, Derra K, Rouamba E, Gneme A, Tinto H
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 425 children aged 6–59 months found malaria incidence per 1000 child-months was substantially higher in malnourished children (974.47) than in those with adequate nutritional status (214.47). Malnourished children were 1.42 times more likely to have clinical malaria (95% CI 1.03–1.94; p=0.028) despite receiving seasonal malaria chemoprevention, suggesting that combining SMC with nutrient supplementation may optimize malaria control.

articles.headline: Malaria burden and risk factors in overseas Chinese workforce
articles.title_original: Epidemiological characteristics, prevention and control status, and factors associated with malaria infection among overseas Chinese workforce in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ji J, Gao X, Lei L, Yang T, Zhou A, Wang Y, Chen L, Cui Q, Zhu Y
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (2022-2023) of 798 Chinese workers in Uganda; 44.5% (355) reported malaria history. Risk factors included male sex (AOR=1.92, 95% CI 1.27–2.91), more Uganda visits (AOR=1.28, CI 1.06–1.53), longer residence (AOR=1.38, CI 1.19–1.61), negative prevention attitudes (AOR=2.49, CI 1.41–4.40), higher mosquito biting (AOR=1.31, CI 1.10–1.56), more cases in area (AOR=1.95, CI 1.51–2.51), and access to antimalarial drugs (AOR=1.48, CI 1.04–2.11). Protective factors: bachelor's degree or higher (AOR=0.65, CI 0.44–0.97) and living with spouse (AOR=0.57, CI 0.36–0.91).

articles.headline: DIC with thrombotic predominance in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation With a Predominant Thrombotic Phenotype in Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Gisca E, Santos MG, Costa S, Santos CR, Araújo A
articles.details: Case report of 61-year-old with severe P. falciparum malaria (parasitemia >25%) complicated by disseminated intravascular coagulation with thrombotic phenotype. Presented with thrombocytopenia, elevated D-dimer, metabolic acidosis, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction. Despite intravenous artesunate, anticoagulation, and ICU support, developed progressive peripheral ischemia requiring amputation and ultimately died. Highlights underrecognized thrombotic-predominant presentation of malaria-associated DIC.

articles.headline: Scoping review of malaria programs during COVID-19
articles.title_original: Implementation of malaria control programmes during the COVID-19 pandemic in the Southern African Development Community Elimination 8 countries: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Muzamhindo DN, Chironda G, Tsoka-Gwegweni JM
articles.journal: Afr J Prim Health Care Fam Med
articles.details: Scoping review of malaria control programs in SADC Malaria E8 countries during COVID-19 using JBI methodology. Of 658 articles retrieved, only 7 met inclusion criteria; half published in 2021, none in 2020. Limited research exists on malaria programs during the pandemic in E8 countries. Review identifies need for research on maintaining programs during pandemics and implementing coping strategies.

articles.headline: Parasite genetics predict malaria prevalence in Uganda
articles.title_original: Accuracy of Plasmodium falciparum genetic data for estimating parasite prevalence and malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiyaga S, Mbabazi M, Katairo T, Kabbale KD, Asua V, Nsengimaana B, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Mwubaha C, Nakasaanya J, Watyekele E, Ayitewala A, Tukwasibwe S, Mulondo J, Nsobya SL, Agaba B, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Kamya MR, Kateete DP, Nabende JN, Jjingo D, Mboowa G, Batte C, Ssewanyana I, Aranda-Díaz A, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ, Conrad M, Greenhouse B, Briggs J
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 3,563 symptomatic P. falciparum cases from 26 health facilities in Uganda (2023) using microhaplotype analysis. Parasite prevalence ranged 5.0–49.23% (Round 1), incidence 91–1,062 per 1,000 person-years (Round 1) and 33–1,667 per 1,000 person-years (Round 2). Complexity of infection (COI) and effective COI strongly predicted prevalence (cross-validated r=0.79), while between-host relatedness best predicted incidence (r=0.37). Genomic metrics capture transmission intensity variation but have limited ability to predict incidence alone.

articles.headline: Cross-border movement contributes to malaria importation
articles.title_original: Role of cross-border movement on the importation of malaria cases in Mudzi district, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari M
articles.details: Study of 619 malaria cases from 5 health facilities in Mudzi district, Zimbabwe (border region). Cross-border movement reported in 16.32% (101/619) of cases; 96% of cross-border cases aged 10–49 years. Highest cross-border case proportions at border-adjacent facilities. 84.33% (522/619) received treatment at health facilities versus community-based sources. 7.92% (49/619) were Mozambique residents. Findings highlight cross-border travel contribution to malaria burden despite high-performing indoor residual spraying programs.

articles.headline: Correction to severe malaria blood purification study
articles.title_original: Correction: The blood purification therapy-based strategy effectively rescued the severe falciparum malaria patient experiencing cytokine storm-driven MODS.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Huang X, Hong B, Song J, Lu Y, Ma J, Wang X, Hu W, Zhang Y, Xuan H
articles.details: Correction notice to prior article on blood purification therapy for severe falciparum malaria with cytokine storm and multiorgan dysfunction. No substantive findings reported in this correction.

articles.headline: SP-resistance mutations persist in Western Kenya
articles.title_original: Persistent and Circulating Plasmodium falciparum dhfr and dhps Mutations in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ndung'u L, Thiong'o K, Karani L, Gitahi S, Kimani F, Ngugi MP, Kiboi D
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of dhfr and dhps mutations in 66 P. falciparum isolates from Busia County, Kenya (Nov 2024–Jan 2025) related to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp). High mutation frequencies: dhfr N51I (85.2%), C59R (75.4%), S108N (32.8%), S108T (19.7%), I164L (18.0%); dhps A437G (92.3%), K540E (40%). Classical dhfr triple-mutant (N51I+C59R+S108N) with dhps A437G circulating. Highlights persistence of canonical and non-canonical haplotypes; continuous surveillance needed on functional significance of S108T and I164L.

articles.headline: Temperature-responsive chromatin regions in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Integration of ATAC-seq and RNA-seq reveals temperature-responsive regulatory regions in Plasmodium falciparum asexual stages.
articles.authors: Zhang Y, Zhao Y, Zheng G, He S, Xiao J, Ma H, Huang J, Qi Y
articles.details: ATAC-seq and RNA-seq analysis of P. falciparum ring and trophozoite stages exposed to human body temperature (37°C) versus mosquito-mimicking temperature (26°C). Low temperature induced 1,083 differentially accessible regions (DARs) in ring stage (1,081 gains, 2 losses), enriched in promoter regions, with no significant DARs in trophozoite stage. 41 genes showed concordant chromatin accessibility and gene expression changes. DAR-associated genes enriched in host cell interactions, antigenic variation, and erythrocyte aggregation pathways.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic P. vivax detected in malaria-free Argentina
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic and submicroscopic Plasmodium vivax infection: a study previous to malaria-free certification in Argentina.
articles.authors: Dantur-Juri MJ, Coello-Peralta RD, Calle-Atariguana DE, Arevalo-Bozada MM, Duque PL, González-Piñeres N, Rougeron V, Degrugillier F, Dentice-Maidana S, Liria-Salazar J, Zaidenberg MO
articles.details: Post-certification surveillance (2022) of 92 blood samples from Argentina (collected 2013–2018) using molecular PCR of cytochrome b gene. One submicroscopic, asymptomatic P. vivax infection detected in 66-year-old from La Bendición neighborhood, Salta province, with no travel history to P. vivax-endemic regions. First molecularly confirmed submicroscopic P. vivax infection in Argentina prior to malaria-free certification. Highlights risk of silent circulation in malaria-free areas and need for strengthened surveillance with highly sensitive diagnostics.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge and ITN use in pregnant Fulani women
articles.title_original: Knowledge of malaria and preventive practices among Fulani pregnant women in the savannah Region of Northern Ghana.
articles.authors: Salifu Y, Nantomah B, Lasong J
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study (April–June 2022) of 159 Fulani pregnant women in Savannah Region, Northern Ghana. 61.0% demonstrated good malaria knowledge, but 71.1% exhibited poor preventive practices. 54.7% owned ≥1 insecticide-treated net (ITN), yet only 28.9% slept under one the previous night. ANC attendance significantly associated with good malaria knowledge (AOR=0.31, 95% CI 0.12–0.83, p=0.020); number of children predicted preventive practice (AOR=0.29, 95% CI 0.10–0.90, p=0.032).

articles.headline: Climate-driven model predicts Anopheles abundance peaks
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal modelling and prediction of Anopheles mosquito abundance in Tanga and Unguja, Tanzania: climatic drivers and insights for malaria early warning and vector control strategies.
articles.authors: Njotto LL, Kulaya NB, Derua YA, Malongo BB, Francis F, Schiøler KL, Hansson H, Wang CW, Saleh F, Baraka V, Alifrangis M, van der Deure T, Senyoni W, Cronie O, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: Spatio-temporal GAMM analysis of monthly Anopheles samples (Sept 2021–Dec 2023) from 11 sites in Tanga and 4 shehias in Unguja, Tanzania. Total 4,312 mosquitoes in Tanga, 1,450 in Unguja. Region-specific climatic drivers: Tanga abundance increased with higher maximum temperatures, declined with higher minimum temperatures; Unguja peaked below 27.5°C. Precipitation effects lagged 2 months in Unguja. Models achieved strong predictive performance (correlation >0.8 between observed and predicted) and accurately forecasted Anopheles peaks in Unguja in November 2023.

articles.headline: Parasite and blood meal detection in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Molecular xenomonitoring for identifying Plasmodium parasites and blood meal sources of mosquitoes in malaria endemic villages adjacent to the southern Rift Valley Lakes, South Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Eligo N, Woldeyes D, Tamiru G, Jima B, Wudneh F, Wegayehu T, Wondale B, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: Cross-sectional entomological survey in 12 malaria-endemic kebeles near southern Rift Valley Lakes, South Ethiopia using CDC light traps from 416 houses. PCR detected P. vivax (2.0%; 9/446) and P. falciparum (0.2%; 1/446) in mosquito abdomens. Of 446 Anopheles specimens, 85% (378/446) fed on vertebrates: goat (67%; 299/446), human (23%; 102/446), cow (19%; 84/446), dog (13%; 57/446). Anopheles arabiensis predominant (85%; 380/446), followed by An. pharoensis (7%) and An. tenebrosus (3%).

articles.headline: Precipitation linked to malaria in Mali children under 5
articles.title_original: Associations between precipitation, temperature, and malaria prevalence in children under 5 in Mali.
articles.authors: Hayden NJT, Syer J, Yanow SK, Yamamoto SS
articles.details: Cross-sectional study using Demographic and Health Surveys (2012/13, 2015, 2018, 2021) examining RDT-diagnosed malaria in children 6–59 months. With 3-month lag, precipitation positively associated with malaria (OR 1.005 [95% CI 1.002, 1.009]); minimum, maximum, and average temperature negatively associated (OR 0.761 [0.621, 0.931], 0.871 [0.791, 0.958], 0.822 [0.718, 0.942]). Authors concluded precipitation is a risk factor; temperature alone not contributing to malaria odds changes.

articles.headline: Complex III serves dual roles in P. falciparum mitochondria
articles.title_original: Dual function of mitochondrial complex III in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rell RS, Shukla A, Morrisey JM, Okoye IC, Mather MW, Vaidya AB
articles.details: Mechanistic study using transgenic P. falciparum parasites. Complex III regenerates ubiquinone for pyrimidine biosynthesis and processes imported mitochondrial proteins. Mitochondrial processing peptidase α (MPPα) is essential even when electron transport is bypassed; MPPα knockdown increased hypersensitivity to proguanil. Proteomics revealed MPPα associates with multiple mitochondrial proteins beyond Complex III components, supporting dual mtETC and protein-processing functions.

articles.headline: Genome sequencing distinguishes imported from local malaria in Malaysia
articles.title_original: Whole genome sequencing to inform the epidemiology of Plasmodium falciparum malaria in the elimination setting of Malaysia.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, Billows N, Divis PCS, Daneshvar C, Edgeworth J, Singh JC, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: Analyzed 21 P. falciparum isolates (2008–2010) using nanopore sequencing and compared to 9,518 publicly available isolates. Found 13/15 cases likely originated outside Malaysia based on high multiplicity of infection; two cases showed genomic evidence inconsistent with travel histories. Identity-by-descent analysis revealed clustering in only 2 cases, indicating mostly isolated introductions rather than sustained local transmission. Genomics discerns imported from locally acquired cases, supporting malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Transmembrane proteins essential for malaria parasite daughter cell formation
articles.title_original: Transmembrane proteins mediate basal complex assembly and individual daughter cell formation in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Back PS, Wagner E, Llopis PM, de Oliveira RG, Mottinelli M, Ferrins L, Dvorin JD
articles.details: Using expansion microscopy and DNA-PAINT, authors investigated transmembrane basal complex proteins (BTP1, BTP2, BLEB) in P. falciparum. Parasites lacking BTP2 failed to separate despite being membrane-enveloped; defect isolated to specific basal complex development step. Authors propose three-step model for basal complex biogenesis, identifying BTP2's specific role and a BLEB-associated membrane region excluded from daughter cells.

articles.headline: Carbamate resistance mechanisms in malaria vector Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Mechanisms of carbamate resistance in the malaria vector: Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Akanni MH, Bajepade TI, Ajeoge JK, Zakari S
articles.details: Review synthesis of carbamate resistance in A. gambiae. Mechanisms include target-site mutations in acetylcholinesterase gene and increased detoxification via esterases and cytochrome P450s. Carbamate resistance is rising and poses significant challenges to malaria control. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for sustaining effectiveness of IRS and informing insecticide resistance management strategies in areas with pyrethroid resistance.

articles.headline: PCR more sensitive than RDT and microscopy for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Diagnostic challenges in malaria detection: A comparative diagnostic performance of HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests, microscopy, and PCR at Bichena primary hospital, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Minwuyelet A, Yewhalaw D, Atenafu G
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study (31/12/2024–28/02/2025) with 274 participants in Northwest Ethiopia. PCR detected higher prevalence (23.4%) vs microscopy (20.1%) and HRP2-RDT (19%). HRP2-RDT showed lower sensitivity (79.1%), NPV (94.1%), and accuracy (94.9%) compared to PCR. Microscopy had high specificity/PPV (100%) but sensitivity 85.9%. Moderate agreement between PCR and microscopy (κ = 0.904), weak agreement with RDTs (κ = 0.847).

articles.headline: Ecological, health system, and behavioral drivers of malaria resurgence
articles.title_original: Understanding malaria resurgence in the Amhara Region, northwestern Ethiopia: a qualitative study of perceived drivers from stakeholder and community perspectives.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Gelaye KA, Asemahagn MA, Muchie KF, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Qualitative study (1–28 February 2025) with 28 semi-structured interviews, 2 FGDs (n=20), and 12 in-depth interviews in Amhara Region. Participants perceived malaria resurgence with near year-round transmission and expansion into highlands, rising burden in children <5 and pregnant women. Perceived drivers: irregular rainfall, land use change, irrigation, water storage; health system challenges (stockouts, delayed vector control); behavioral factors (low perceived risk, traditional healers, ITN misuse).

articles.headline: Expired malaria RDTs valid up to 9 months post-expiration
articles.title_original: Test Performance of Expired Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests: A pilot diagnostic accuracy study conducted in Western Uganda.
articles.authors: Ndizeye R, Gunderson AK, Baguma E, Giandomenico DA, Ntaro M, Mulogo EM, Boyce RM
articles.details: Pilot diagnostic accuracy study in western Uganda with 200 participants (10.5% positive, 89.5% negative). Expired mRDTs (SD Bioline Ag Pf/Pan, First Response Ag pLDH/HRP2) tested in parallel with non-expired reference. All tests up to 9-months post-expiration were valid and consistent with reference tests. All tests 10–12 months post-expiration were invalid.

articles.headline: ITN use reduces malaria infection odds by 13% in Ghana
articles.title_original: Evaluating the effectiveness of insecticide-treated net use in preventing malaria among children under five: a quasi-experimental study.
articles.authors: Osei KM, Adiak AA, Han W
articles.details: Quasi-experimental study with 11,481 children using nationally representative, georeferenced Ghana survey data. Sleeping under ITN led to 13% reduction in malaria odds (aOR = 0.87; 95% CI 0.81–0.93) and 2.2% absolute risk reduction (RD = −0.022; 95% CI −0.037 to −0.006). Spatial analyses revealed misalignment between intervention coverage and malaria burden. Authors concluded ITN provides meaningful but limited protection.

articles.headline: Deep learning YOLO framework for automated Plasmodium vivax detection
articles.title_original: Enhanced YOLO-based framework and benchmarking for automated Plasmodium vivax detection.
articles.authors: Prathap VM, Yadav S
articles.details: Comparative evaluation of YOLO variants (v3, cascade v3, scaled v4, v5, v8) for automated P. vivax detection in blood smears. Proposes novel framework combining YOLOv3 with modified MobileNetV2 backbone and Transformed Convolutional Layer (TCL) for efficient feature extraction in dense thick smear images. Models assessed on precision, accuracy, F1-score, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP).

articles.headline: Fluoroquinolones inhibit P. falciparum plasmepsin II; moxifloxacin most potent
articles.title_original: Physico-Chemical Insights into the Catalytic Inhibition of Plasmodium falciparum Plasmepsin II Activity by Repurposing Fluoroquinolone Drugs.
articles.authors: Syed SF, Thakur P, Patil M, Choudhary S
articles.details: Study on repurposing fluoroquinolone drugs (ofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) to inhibit P. falciparum plasmepsin II catalytic activity. Moxifloxacin showed most potent inhibition with IC50 of 0.15 ± 0.02 μM, acting through hydrogen bonding with catalytic dyad Asp34 and Asp214 (donor-acceptor distances 2.8–3.3 Å). Methylation of NH group disrupted interactions and reduced efficacy.

articles.headline: Primaquine with chloroquine faster reduces P. vivax transmission
articles.title_original: Efficacy of primaquine-chloroquine combination on Plasmodium vivax transmission and parasite clearance in Ethiopia: a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Melaku M, Wondale B, Tamiru G, Getu R, Kumsa T, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: Open-label RCT in Ethiopia with P. vivax patients ≥15 years. PQ 0.25 mg/kg daily × 14 days + CQ vs CQ alone (n=56 completed day 3–8 feeding, n=49 completed 28-day follow-up). Before treatment: 57% (CQ) vs 82% (CQ-PQ) infectious to A. arabiensis. After 1 day: significantly higher reduction in CQ-PQ group (P < 0.0001), pronounced oocyst decline (P < 0.001), higher CSP reduction (P = 0.002), lower gametocytes (P = 0.02). No difference in asexual parasite clearance by day 3 (P = 0.42).

articles.headline: An. gambiae dominates vector species across Ethiopian study zone
articles.title_original: Species Occurrence and Seasonal Variation of Malaria Vectors in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Assemie A, Mulu D, Mekuriaw E, Muluken W
articles.details: Entomological survey collecting 721 larval samples from four villages identified An. gambiae s.l. as the predominant malaria vector species (43.82%), with An. pharoensis at 7.9%. Ditches showed the highest mean larval density (2.61 per sample) compared to swamps, riverbeds, and water pans. Larval abundance differed significantly across habitat types (p=0.012).

articles.headline: Age, rural residence, poverty drive malaria in Burkina Faso children
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and healthcare determinants of malaria infection among children under five in Burkina Faso: analysis of the 2021 Demographic and Health Survey data.
articles.authors: Kinda R, Gansane A, Traore TI, Kaboré NF, Debe S, Sore H, Nonkani WG, Guelbéogo MW, Tougri G, Tarama CW, Ilboudo SR, Sanou GS, Savadogo LGB
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 5674 children aged 6-59 months from the 2021 Burkina Faso DHS found overall malaria prevalence of 14.1% (95% CI: 13-15%), with regional variation from 5.1% to 29.3%. Compared to 6-12 month-olds, malaria risk increased with age (aPR 2.07 at 24-35 months) and was higher in rural areas (aPR 2.21), households without electricity (aPR 2.01), and poor households (aPR 1.60).

articles.headline: Plasmodium retrocopies drive parasite virulence gene diversity
articles.title_original: Comprehensive computational insights into Plasmodium retrocopies reveals potential roles in virulence-associated functions.
articles.authors: Das S, Ghosh S, Thakur S
articles.details: Computational analysis of 23 Plasmodium pathogenic genomes identified 724 high-confidence retrocopies with canonical retroposition signatures, frequently intact and transcribed during blood stages. Enrichment analyses revealed retrocopies preferentially associated with virulence processes including cytoadhesion, chromatin remodeling, and immune evasion. The study identifies retroposition as an underappreciated mechanism contributing to genomic plasticity and virulence in malaria parasites.

articles.headline: α-Thalassemia shows no protective effect against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: α-Thalassemia has no association with asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum carriage in three ecological zones of Ghana.
articles.authors: Donkor AB, Bernasko FG, Abdulai A, Eyeson NAS, Abban RL, Boadu EA, Kusi KA, Afrane YA, Amoah LE
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1401 asymptomatic individuals across three Ghanaian ecological zones found P. falciparum prevalence by PCR ranging from 23.5% (coastal) to 36.5% (forest). Despite higher odds of asexual parasites (OR=1.23) and gametocytes (OR=1.46) in homozygous α-thalassemia individuals, no statistically significant association was observed between α-thalassemia genotype and P. falciparum parasitemia (p=0.447).

articles.headline: P. knowlesi awareness remains low in endemic Thai communities
articles.title_original: Community awareness and health providers' perspectives on zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi malaria in Thailand: A mixed-methods assessment.
articles.authors: Sripoorote P, Inthitanon N, Wattanagoon Y, Cui L, Nguitragool W, Fornace K, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Aung PL
articles.details: Mixed-methods study of 300 community members and 28 healthcare providers in three P. knowlesi-endemic Thai districts found moderate overall awareness (mean score 10.7/23, SD ± 2.9). Increased awareness was associated with female sex, distance from health facilities, malaria education, and positive attitudes (p<0.05). Healthcare providers reported declining P. knowlesi incidence alongside persistent transmission in forested areas.

articles.headline: PIR genes upregulated in severe P. vivax malaria manifestations
articles.title_original: Comparative clinical transcriptome of pir genes in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Boopathi PA, Singh S, Roy P, Tahbildar S, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: Microarray analysis of parasite transcriptome from 23 patients (uncomplicated n=6, hepatic dysfunction n=12, cerebral malaria n=5) in India found 24 pir genes upregulated in cerebral malaria and 28 in hepatic dysfunction (≥50% of severe cases). Members of vir subfamily E were prominent in both phenotypes (8 genes in cerebral malaria, 9 in hepatic dysfunction), indicating differential pir gene expression in severe P. vivax presentations.

articles.headline: Folic acid with antifolate antimalarials: systematic review and meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Folic acid supplementation and malaria susceptibility and severity among people taking antifolate antimalarial drugs in endemic areas.
articles.authors: Qi YP, Crider KS, Gutman J, Centeno-Tablante E, Fothergill A, Daniels K, Yeung LF, Mai CT, Mehta S, Williams JL, Finkelstein JL
articles.details: Systematic review identified 8 trials with 3486 participants (3 prevention, 5 treatment) examining folic acid supplementation combined with antifolate antimalarials. Most treatment trials used folic acid doses above the tolerable upper limit (>1.0 mg/day). One prevention trial showed folic acid plus iron produced little difference in parasitemia compared to iron alone with antifolate antimalarials (RR 1.21, 95% CI 0.56-2.62, P=0.63, n=643).

articles.headline: Medieval Dutch malaria mapped via skeletal markers and vectors
articles.title_original: Mapping medieval malaria in the Netherlands: A bioarchaeological approach using cribra orbitalia as a proxy.
articles.authors: Schats R
articles.details: Spatial epidemiological analysis of 28 medieval Dutch archaeological sites used cribra orbitalia as a proxy for malaria burden from hemolytic anemia. Significant positive spatial autocorrelation was found, with clustering in the Southwest. Cribra orbitalia prevalence correlated significantly with historical (1938) estimates of Anopheles atroparvus density, suggesting malaria contributed to skeletal lesion prevalence in certain medieval regions.

articles.headline: Adaptive immunity imposes tradeoff on parasite transmission investment
articles.title_original: Immunity can impose a reproduction-survival tradeoff on human malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Patterson DD, Childs LM, Stopard IJ, Chitnis N, Serrato-Arroyo S, Greischar MA
articles.details: Mathematical model incorporating adaptive immunity revealed that increased transmission investment reduces infection duration and parasite fitness, with optimal transmission investment around 5%. Parasites benefit from delaying transmission investment to enable faster within-host multiplication. Adaptive immunity imposes a survival-reproduction tradeoff, with implications for understanding parasite evolution in response to malaria control.

articles.headline: P. falciparum and P. ovale show seasonal interaction patterns
articles.title_original: Seasonal Variation and Interspecies Dynamics Among Plasmodium falciparum and ovale Species in Bagamoyo, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Carey-Ewend K, Marten A, Muller J, Peter EE, Odas M, Dominick MC, Muller M, Chhetri S, Amagai K, Rutha I, Kisandu F, Beka L, Kharabora O, Popkin-Hall ZR, Bailey J, Edwards JK, Gower EW, Juliano JJ, Ngasala BE, Lin JT
articles.details: Longitudinal PCR screening of 7173 asymptomatic individuals (2018-2022) found P. falciparum prevalence of 27.4% (associated with age, sex, rainfall) and P. ovale prevalence of 11.5% (no demographic associations). P. ovale infections peaked during lower P. falciparum prevalence periods with apparent antagonism, though Pf-Po co-infections were enriched during the short wet season. Mixed Poc/Pow infections composed 23% of P. ovale-positive samples.

articles.headline: Most submicroscopic P. vivax infections clear spontaneously
articles.title_original: Spontaneous Clearance of Polymerase Chain Reaction-Positive Plasmodium vivax Parasitemia in Northwestern Thailand: An Epidemiological Perspective.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Petchvijit P, Suk-Aum K, Htwe KZZ, Lawpoolsri S, Nguitragool W, Cui L, Kaewkungwal J, Sattabongkot J
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study with 14 mass blood surveys (2018-2023) in northwestern Thailand followed 101 participants with PCR-positive submicroscopic P. vivax parasitemia. Most infections (72/101, 69.9%) cleared spontaneously within 3 subsequent surveys. Probability of persistent infections declined to 25.0% (95% CI: 4.0-100.0) by day 47 and 7.0% (95% CI: 1.0-38.0) by day 232. Clearance was not associated with demographics.

articles.headline: P. vivax source-sink dynamics impede Amazon malaria elimination
articles.title_original: The Source-Sink Dynamics of Plasmodium vivax May Undermine Malaria Elimination Efforts in the Amazon: An Epidemiological and Population Genomic Study.
articles.authors: de Albuquerque NRM, Corder RM, Johansen IC, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Ladeia-Andrade S, Silva JC, Ferreira MU
articles.details: Analysis of 5.5 million malaria case notifications (2003-2023) and whole-genome sequences from 408 P. vivax isolates revealed nearly one-third of Brazilian Amazon infections were acquired outside residents' municipalities, with only 1.7% imported from outside South America. Large cities (Manaus, Porto Velho) served as parasite sinks surrounded by high-risk source localities. Parasites >1000 km apart remained genetically connected, perpetuating transmission.

articles.headline: Chimeric malaria vaccine targets sporozoite entry proteins
articles.title_original: A Multivalent Peptide Vaccine Against Malaria, Targeting Plasmodium Circumsporozoite Protein and Mosquito AgTRIO.
articles.authors: Chuang YM, Ledizet M, Mattessich M, Fikrig E
articles.details: Developed chimeric peptide (PfAg) combining Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) and mosquito AgTRIO. Tested in C57BL/6 and CD-1 mice, generating robust humoral responses against both antigens. Vaccinated mice showed significantly improved survival compared to control animals after exposure to PfCSP-expressing P. berghei-infected mosquitoes.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic factors, water management influence malaria prevention uptake
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and household water management determinants of malaria and other vector-borne disease prevention in Urban Gujarat, India.
articles.authors: Batheja D, Samria D, Wimberly MC, Pascual M, Baharia RK, Mohanty AK, Desai V, Vaishnav K, Sharma R, Kohli V, Sharma S, Anvikar A, Murdock C, Nandi A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 4,074 households in Ahmedabad and Surat, India (September-November 2023). Reported 77 malaria, dengue, and chikungunya cases (18.90 per 1,000 households). Wealth, caste, and family size significantly associated with disease prevention adoption; households in richest wealth quintile more likely to adopt prevention measures but less likely to perceive mosquito-related risks.

articles.headline: DSM265 shows favorable safety profile in early trials
articles.title_original: Evaluating the safety, pharmacokinetics, and antiparasitic activity of DSM265, a novel antimalarial plasmodium dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor: a systematic review and meta-analysis of early clinical trials.
articles.authors: Sula I, Jihwaprani MC, Ahmad I, Khan TA, Al Abdin MG, Ali MM, Ming LC
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 7 early-phase clinical trials. Increased adverse event risk overall (RR 1.46, 95% CI 1.15-1.84). At 400 mg oral suspension dose: maximum concentration 9.3 μg/mL, time to maximum 5.4 hours, half-life 112.1 hours, AUC 1,601.9 h·μg/mL. DSM265 demonstrated greater selectivity for P. falciparum versus P. vivax, with no anti-gametocyte activity.

articles.headline: Synthetic diet sustains mosquito colonies without animal blood
articles.title_original: Effects of a bloodless diet on fitness and malaria susceptibility in Anopheles mosquitoes from Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: de Sales Hien DF, Sare I, Sib AMAP, Poda BS, Velez R, Marques J, Silveira H, Diabate A, Bilgo EM
articles.details: Compared BLOODless™ synthetic diet versus blood feeding in An. coluzzii and An. gambiae (Burkina Faso and Kisumu strains) across 7 generations. Wing size unaffected by diet across generations. By 6th generation, P. falciparum oocyst prevalence higher in blood-fed versus bloodless-fed mosquitoes, though intensity unchanged. Longevity significantly increased in bloodless-fed mosquitoes by 7th generation.

articles.headline: High prevalence of Pfdhps resistance mutations in Chad
articles.title_original: Resistance profile of the Plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase gene in three hospitals in the city of Ndjamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Cedric Y, Djakbé DL, Ngaryedji T, Nadia NAC, Pacome TK, Anaud NKJ, Esther DD, Alain DC, Ismail IA, Saleh B, Abel D, Hu H
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 220 patients with 39.54% P. falciparum prevalence. Plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase (Pfdhps) resistance gene detected in 37.93% of infected individuals. A437G mutation present in 9.09% of samples; A581G mutation in 54.54%. A581G more prevalent in males (30%) and individuals reporting Fansidar use (30%).

articles.headline: PLHIV with low CD4 develop severe malaria more frequently
articles.title_original: Clinical and biological features of complicated and asymptomatic malaria among PLHIV and HIV-negative adults: An observational study in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, Manomba Boulingui C, Mbang Nguema OA, Mihindou CJ, Pongui Ngondza B, Ndong Ngomo JM, Essomeyo Mebale M, Ntsame Owono MM, Mabicka Moussavou AD, Mayandza C, Mbondoukwé NP, Kono HN, Bouyou Akotet MK
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in Libreville, Gabon (September 2021-October 2022) of 1,192 individuals: 513 PLHIV and 599 HIV-negative. Among P. falciparum-infected participants (56 PLHIV, 66 HIV-negative), asymptomatic malaria occurred in 60.6% of HIV-negative versus 25.0% of PLHIV; severe malaria in 48.2% of PLHIV versus 12.1% of HIV-negative (p<0.01). Median CD4 count significantly lower in PLHIV with severe malaria (150 cells/µL) versus asymptomatic infection (304 cells/µL, p=0.018).

articles.headline: Correction to P. vivax population genetics study
articles.title_original: Correction: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Mehra S, Razook Z, Lautu-Gumal D, Nate E, Lee S, Kattenberg JH, Koepfli C, Kazura J, Ome-Kaius M, Laman M, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Barry AE
articles.details: Correction to article (DOI: 10.3389/fgene.2025.1621920) examining population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in Papua New Guinea endemic areas.

articles.headline: Plant extracts demonstrate antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: In Vitro Antiparasitic Activity and Phytochemical Profiling of Rhaponticum repens and Achillea millefolium Methanolic Extracts Against Plasmodium Falciparum and Leishmania major.
articles.authors: Singh K, Siyadatpanah A, Garba A, Singh J, Norouzi R, Ben Said M, Belkahia H
articles.details: In vitro testing of methanolic extracts from Achillea millefolium and Rhaponticum repens against P. falciparum and Leishmania major. A. millefolium more effective against P. falciparum: 94% inhibition at 2.0 mg/mL, IC50=0.52 mg/mL, selectivity index=26.3. R. repens showed 81% inhibition against L. major with IC50=1.18 mg/mL, SI=10.6. Both demonstrated low cytotoxicity to Vero cells.

articles.headline: Machine learning predicts antimalarial compound efficacy
articles.title_original: Hybrid Computational Framework Integrating Ensemble Learning, Molecular Docking, and Dynamics for Predicting Antimalarial Efficacy of Malaria Box Compounds.
articles.authors: Moreno M, Cuesta SA, Mora JR, Márquez Brazon EA, Paz JL, Agüero-Chapin G, Pérez-Pérez N, García-Jacas CR
articles.details: Computational framework combining ensemble machine learning, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations on Malaria Box compounds. Classification model achieved 10-fold cross-validation accuracy of 0.738 and external validation accuracy of 0.675, distinguishing 'active' and 'very active' compounds. Regression models within each class achieved Q² values of 0.810 (very active) and 0.793 (active). Identified several candidates with stable ligand-protein complexes for future evaluation.

articles.headline: Helminth co-infection modulates antimalarial antibody responses
articles.title_original: The association of helminth infection against antimalarial antibody responses in malaria infection and vaccination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Pinandyo SH, Ali S, Aurelio B, Amoah AS, Kaisar MMM
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 29 articles. Meta-analysis from 4 studies showed higher antimalarial IgG in helminth-infected groups (SMD -0.44; 95% CI -0.61 to -0.27; p<0.0001). Separate meta-analysis found marginally lower IgG with Schistosoma haematobium infection (SMD 0.40; p=0.0492), but better IgG1 and IgG3 responses (SMD -1.08; 95% CI -1.75 to -0.42 for IgG3; p=0.0015). Effects helminth-species dependent.

articles.headline: Pan-pLDH biomarker rapidly identifies severe imported malaria
articles.title_original: pLDH to identify severity in imported malaria: Implementing smartphone video analysis for rapid clinical decision-making.
articles.authors: Pedreira-Rincón J, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Villanueva G, Fleitas PE, Jimenez A, Mayor A, Muñoz J, Subirà C, Alvarez-Martinez MJ, Petrone P, Camprubí-Ferrer D, Parolo C
articles.details: Retrospective cohort of imported P. falciparum malaria and non-malarial fever cases comparing parasite biomarkers PfHRP2 and pan-pLDH. Pan-pLDH excelled at identifying severe malaria cases, outperforming PfHRP2. Smartphone video-analysis implementation reduced testing time to under 6 minutes while maintaining pan-pLDH performance in detecting severe malaria in rapid clinical decision-making contexts.

articles.headline: P. vivax and arboviruses co-circulate in border region
articles.title_original: Co-circulation of Plasmodium vivax and Arboviruses in the Brazil-French Guiana Border.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Medeiros EDS, Arouca ML, Sampaio MP, Menezes RAO, Gomes MDSM, Silva JRS, Baptista ARS, Storti-Melo LM, Machado RLD
articles.details: Serological survey of 300 individuals (102 malaria cases, 198 controls) from Brazil-French Guiana border, collected 2014-2015. High IgG reactivity observed for dengue and chikungunya viruses. Frequent co-reactivity between malaria and arbovirus antibodies noted. Zika virus detection was minimal, highlighting overlapping transmission of P. vivax and arboviruses in tropical border regions.

articles.headline: Primaquine addition to DP seasonal malaria chemoprevention simulation
articles.title_original: Agent-based simulation of seasonal malaria chemoprevention strategy in Southern Tanzania: comparing dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine with or without primaquine.
articles.authors: Mfala CT, Nyambo DG, Mwaiswelo RO, Tchuenche JM, Clemen T
articles.details: Agent-based model simulated seasonal malaria chemoprevention with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) with and without single low-dose primaquine (SLDPQ) in 2,275 under-five children over 180 days. At day 125, DP+SLDPQ reduced prevalence to 1.4% versus 4.1% with DP alone; at day 180, 3.9% versus 7.1%. SLDPQ addition provided sustained transmission reduction regardless of temperature changes. Authors conclude SLDPQ can be added to DP-SMC for malaria control.

articles.headline: Safer sex negotiation among women in Tanzanian unions
articles.title_original: Safer Sex Negotiation Among Women in Sexual Unions in Tanzania: Analysis of the 2022 Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Kessy SA, Stephano EE, Tibenderana JR, Mtoro MJ, Kessy IP, Majengo VG, Oguma ED, Mwalingo TP, Bago MH, Nyundo AA
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 2022 Tanzania DHS and Malaria Indicator Survey data found 75.8% prevalence of safer sex negotiation among women in sexual unions. Higher education, wealth, media exposure, work, STI awareness, and internet use were associated with safer sex negotiation (all APR>1.04, p<0.05). Partner education also positively associated. Geographic zones showed strong association with negotiation practices.

articles.headline: Climate change could hinder malaria eradication efforts
articles.title_original: Climate change could hinder malaria eradication efforts.
articles.authors: O'Leary K
articles.details: Title only; no abstract provided. Article examines how climate change may impede global malaria eradication efforts, but specific quantitative findings are unavailable.

articles.headline: SARIMAX forecasts seasonal surges in Nigerian malaria incidence
articles.title_original: Modelling the association of rainfall and temperature with malaria incidence in Adamawa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Dukundane D, Salako KV, Kakaï RG, Okoronkwo C, Nelson E
articles.details: Time-series ARIMA/SARIMAX modeling of monthly malaria data from January 2015–April 2024 in Adamawa State, Nigeria, incorporating lagged temperature and rainfall as drivers of Anopheles breeding. Forecasts for May 2024–December 2025 project 67,052 cases in August 2024 and peak of 80,004 cases in October 2025, with pronounced seasonal surges June–October. Authors emphasize value of climate-informed forecasting for early warning systems and seasonal preparedness.

articles.headline: Author Correction: Malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands
articles.title_original: Author Correction: Malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands track the 2000 'slowdown' in global warming.
articles.authors: Rodó X, Martinez PP, Siraj A, Pascual M
articles.details: Author correction notice; original article indicates study of malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands relative to 2000 global warming slowdown. No substantive findings provided in this correction notice.

articles.headline: Community perceptions of ivermectin for livestock-based malaria control
articles.title_original: Community perceptions and acceptance of ivermectin for malaria control on Sumba Island, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Timoria D, Dewi C, Bøgh C, Baskoro T, Nurcahyo W, Testamenti VA, von Seidlein L, Kobylinski K, Chambers M
articles.details: Qualitative study alongside the SLIM trial (November 2022–September 2023) in 4 Sumatran villages included 75 individuals in focus groups and interviews. Initial skepticism about ivermectin livestock treatment was overcome through trust-building; approximately 650 community members engaged. While interest in human mass drug administration existed, concerns about child safety were raised. Local authority approval was deemed essential for acceptance.

articles.headline: Parasite infection alters bacterial microbiota in infected birds
articles.title_original: Microbial community composition variation in relation to malaria infections in Junco hyemalis.
articles.authors: Amaya-Mejia WS, Sehgal RNM, Yeh PJ
articles.details: Comparative microbiota analysis of 8 Oregon Juncos infected with Plasmodium relictum and 8 non-infected individuals found significantly lower alpha diversity in infected birds. Beta diversity analysis revealed compositional turnover among rare taxa; Muribaculaceae, Acidobacteriales, Pseudomonas, and Escherichia-Shingella were depleted in infected birds. Co-occurrence networks showed fewer connections and missing hub taxa in infected birds, suggesting parasite-mediated dysbiosis-like changes.

articles.headline: Integrated multi-pronged strategy controlled 22-month P. vivax outbreak
articles.title_original: A six-pronged approach to manage a Plasmodium vivax outbreak in a low- and middle-income country on the road to malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Labadie-Bracho M, Baldewsingh GK, Hiwat van Laar H, Adhin MR
articles.details: Descriptive analysis of Suriname's 2019–2021 P. vivax outbreak (179 infections, 153 patients, 22 months) evaluated adaptive management combining '1-3-7' surveillance, community education, vector control, mass screen-and-treat, MDA, parasite genotyping, and 'Radical Cure.' Mass screen-and-treat was less effective than MDA; directly observed treatment achieved 95.1% efficacy by day 180. Implementation of 'Radical Cure' ended the outbreak. Authors note relapses initially fueled outbreak persistence.

articles.headline: Low RTS,S malaria vaccine coverage and timeliness in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Uptake of malaria vaccine (RTS,S/AS01) among children aged 6-24 months: A cross-sectional survey conducted in the Western region of Cameroon, 1 year following the vaccine introduction.
articles.authors: Ateudjieu J, Nana Djapou MC, Bekoa Onana BK, Tatsabong Tiomeni AA, Okoumokath Mpande GL, Kapso Nanguep D, Buh Nkum C, Ateudjieu Kenfack DW, Bissek AC
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 399 children aged 6–24 months in Cameroon 1 year after RTS,S/AS01 vaccine introduction found coverage of 31.20% (dose 1), 22.61% (dose 2), and 17.70% (dose 3). Among children receiving dose 1, only 56.49% completed the 3-dose schedule; timeliness was 50–57%. Parental perception was strongly associated with dose 1 receipt (aOR 15.54, 95%CI 12.63–19.10, p<0.001) and dose 2 completeness (aOR 9.81, 95%CI 7.78–12.36, p<0.001).

articles.headline: Gender interventions in HIV, TB, malaria show evidence gaps
articles.title_original: Gender-responsive and -transformative interventions in HIV, TB and malaria programmes: a review of evidence.
articles.authors: Bhushan A, Mishra S, Orth Z, Riha J
articles.details: Rapid systematic review of gender-responsive/-transformative interventions in HIV, TB, and malaria (2004–2024) identified 42 eligible studies from 11,844 articles; however, all 42 focused exclusively on HIV, with none on TB or malaria. Interventions addressed resilient systems (n=6), gender norms change (n=9), social empowerment (n=8), economic empowerment (n=18), and policy reform (n=1). Multi-component, multi-level socio-ecological interventions showed more sustained health and gender outcome improvements than single-component efforts.

articles.headline: Chloroquine and mefloquine resistance persist in Colombian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from the Pacific Coast of Colombia.
articles.authors: Michie K, Chiu B, Briggs C, Corredor V, Matta-Cortés L, Rayner JC, Echeverri DF
articles.details: Panel of P. falciparum isolates from Colombia's Pacific Coast collected across 23 years was assessed for resistance genotypes and phenotypes using quantitative PCR and sequencing. Resistance-associated mutations in pfdhfr/pfdhps were nearly 100% prevalent; PfDHFR I164L and PfDHPS A581G combined mutations present. Chloroquine and mefloquine resistance persisted. Crucially, no artemisinin resistance-associated PfKelch13 polymorphisms were detected. Authors conclude long-term maintenance of historical resistance argues against using chloroquine or mefloquine as artemisinin partner drugs.

articles.headline: DP more effective than SP for intermittent preventive treatment
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kizza J, Katairo T, Kakuru A, Nsengimaana B, Esilu T, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Gerlovina I, Hathaway N, Briggs J, Tukwasibwe S, Kiwuwa SM, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Randomized trial in Busia, Uganda compared monthly SP, DP, and DP+SP for IPTp in 1,377 pregnant women with asymptomatic parasitemia. Cumulative risks of recurrent parasitemia were SP 57.8%, DP 4.1%, DP+SP 3.9%; symptomatic malaria SP 9.3%, DP 1.1%, DP+SP 0.3%; recrudescence SP 40.1%, DP 2.0%, DP+SP 0.8%. In SP arm, PfDHFR I164L and PfDHPS A581G combined mutations increased from 24.9% at initiation to 35.2% post-IPTp. Primigravidae had highest risks in SP arm.

articles.headline: Chemiluminescent PRR assay reduces malaria drug screening time to 7 days
articles.title_original: Using Next Generation Chemiluminescent Probes to Improve the Plasmodium falciparum in vitro Parasite Reduction Ratio (PRR) Assay.
articles.authors: Hellingman A, Göttle NL, Walz A, Brancucci NMB, Wittlin S, Mäser P, Rottmann M
articles.details: Researchers optimized the parasite reduction ratio (PRR) assay using lacZ/β-galSENSOR chemiluminescence readout, reducing incubation from 14–28 days to 7 days while maintaining informative pharmacodynamic parameters (lag phase, parasite clearance time, PRR, maximum killing effect). The [3H]-hypoxanthine incorporation method failed to detect viable parasites reliably and consistently overestimated drug activity at the shortened timeframe. The novel assay enables laboratories without radioactive facilities to screen antimalarial compounds with greater throughput and convenience.

articles.headline: Nanotechnology-based herbal approaches for improved malaria therapy
articles.title_original: Nano-Enabled Herbal Remedies for Malaria Treatment: A Review of Recent Advances.
articles.authors: Xu C, Fatima A, Fatima M, Aqib AI, Zaheer T, Rab SO, Saeed M, Arif Z, Li K
articles.details: This review discusses limitations of conventional antimalarials (low water solubility, poor bioavailability, drug resistance) and explores nanotechnology as a promising delivery system for herbal remedies. Nanoparticles offer advantages including high drug-loading capacity, targeted delivery, improved biocompatibility, and reduced toxicity. Green nanotechnology-based antimalarial therapies demonstrated enhanced safety and cost-effectiveness compared to traditional treatments, with potential for vector control and significant commercialization prospects.

articles.headline: Mali P. falciparum drug susceptibility: current therapies potent, next-generation promising
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum field isolates drug susceptibility in Mali.
articles.authors: Maiga FO, Dembele L, Dama S, Diakite O, Diallo F, Sogore F, Maiga M, Dao F, Dara N, Alhousseini ML, Traore OB, Djimde AA
articles.details: Ex vivo susceptibility testing of 26 P. falciparum isolates from three endemic sites in Mali (Faladje, Kolle, Bougoula-Hameau) to 14 antimalarials. Current frontline drugs remained highly potent: dihydroartemisinin (median IC50 <6 nM), lumefantrine (<50 nM), and pyronaridine (<10 nM). Chemopreventive agents sulfadoxine and pyrimethamine showed high IC50 values (>1000 and >2000 nM). Next-generation compounds cabamiquine and GNF179 demonstrated consistently strong activity (IC50 <10 nM).

articles.headline: Iran reduced malaria cases dramatically, achieved zero transmission, now faces resurgence
articles.title_original: Iran's Journey Through Malaria: From Past Challenges to Future Elimination-A Narrative Review.
articles.authors: Hanifian H, Nateghpour M
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing WHO World Malaria Reports and peer-reviewed literature (2000–2025) documented Iran's elimination progress: indigenous cases fell from thousands in early 2000s to <300 annually by mid-2010s, achieving multiple consecutive years with zero transmission. COVID-19 pandemic disruptions and a 2022 resurgence associated with imported infections, operational challenges, insecticide resistance, and Anopheles stephensi spread highlighted vulnerabilities in elimination-phase systems.

articles.headline: Machine learning identifies kinase inhibitors targeting P. falciparum hemozoin and PfPKG
articles.title_original: Small Molecule Kinase Inhibitors with In Vitro β-Hematin Formation and Plasmodium falciparum Protein Kinase G Inhibitory Activity Identified Using Machine Learning.
articles.authors: Thibaud JL, Salomane N, Harries S, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Kuter D, Chibale K, de Villiers KA
articles.details: Using molecular docking and machine learning, researchers screened a kinase inhibitor library for dual activity against β-hematin formation and cGMP-dependent protein kinase (PfPKG). Derazantinib showed most potent activity with IC50 of 88 µM for β-hematin inhibition (compared to 22 µM for chloroquine) and 0.160 µM against PfPKG. Pazopanib and afatinib demonstrated more moderate activity with IC50 values of 219 µM and 234 µM for β-hematin, respectively.

articles.headline: Avian scavenger birds harbor haemosporidian parasites; novel Leucocytozoon lineage identified
articles.title_original: Unraveling Occurrence Patterns and Diversity of Avian Malaria Parasites in Iberian Obligate and Facultative Scavenger Birds.
articles.authors: Oliva-Vidal P, Muriel J, Marzal A, López Del Aguila GM, Martínez JM, Margalida A
articles.details: Active monitoring of 383 blood samples from free-living birds in Spain identified haemosporidian parasites using nested-PCR. Overall prevalence was 3.4% (13/383), with Leucocytozoon at 2.3% and Plasmodium at 1.6%. Black kites showed highest prevalence (8.3%), followed by cinereous vultures (5.3%). A novel Leucocytozoon lineage (lGYPBAR01) was detected infecting all European vultures except griffon vultures, and Plasmodium relictum pSGS1 was first confirmed in adult red kites.

articles.headline: SARIMAX forecasts increasing malaria trend in Ethiopia 2026–2030 despite variable weather
articles.title_original: Forecasting malaria incidence in a resource-limited urban setting with climate variables as exogenous regressors: time series analysis using a SARIMAX model in Bahir Dar, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Gelaw TT, Abera MA
articles.details: Researchers developed a SARIMAX model forecasting malaria incidence in Bahir Dar, Ethiopia using 90 monthly data points (January 2018–June 2025) with weather as exogenous regressors. The model SARIMAX(1,2,2)(1,2,2,12,exog) revealed an increasing trend in future predictions. None of the weather conditions showed statistically significant predictive relationship with malaria incidence (p>0.05), with mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 28.3% indicating reasonable predictive accuracy.

articles.headline: miLab™ AI malaria detection: 94.2% sensitive, 99.0% specific versus PCR reference
articles.title_original: Evaluating the diagnostic performance of miLab™ for detection of malaria parasites using nPCR as reference standard.
articles.authors: Addae EK, Awortwe-Quaicoe T, Sackey B, Ansah RO, Larbi R, Dazangapai KDE, Frimpong JO, Amoako TOO, Asamoah A, Ayisi-Boateng N, Nkrumah B, Bekoe FA, Owusu M
articles.details: Hospital-based study of 300 suspected malaria patients in Kumasi, Ghana (August 2024–June 2025) compared miLab™ (AI-assisted automated detection) versus microscopy and nested-PCR reference standard. Parasite density estimates ranged 0.95–5.34 log parasites/µL (median 3.52, IQR 2.93–3.91). Against nPCR, miLab™ demonstrated sensitivity 94.23%, specificity 98.98%, accuracy 97.33%, superior to adjudicated microscopy (85.58%, 97.96%, 93.67%).

articles.headline: Healthcare barriers delay malaria care for children in DRC Kapolowe
articles.title_original: Young children with severe malaria in the Kapolowe health zone: an analysis of their parents' health-seeking behaviors and the challenges they face.
articles.authors: Kanyamukenge DB, Maseko T, Khote FL, Makenga G, Mavoko HM, Tevuzula VM, Mulangu AM, Sompwe EM, Rietveld H, Manyando C, Van Geertruyden JP, Kakudji A, Bastiaens H
articles.details: Qualitative study using 30 focus group discussions in Kapolowe health zone villages, with 9 analyzed in-depth using the three-delays model. Mothers frequently delayed seeking care and self-medicated with modern and traditional remedies. Barriers included geographical distance, impassable roads, lack of transportation and healthcare facilities, and financial constraints from direct and indirect costs. Cultural factors were significant, with mothers preferring traditional practices and prayer.

articles.headline: SD Bioline RDT: 88.6% sensitive, 75.2% specific in Nigerian children aged 6–59
articles.title_original: Field performance of the SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. rapid diagnostic test among children under five in Nigeria: insights from the 2021 Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Olubiyi B, Alabi A, Isiko I
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2021 Nigerian Malaria Indicator Survey evaluated SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. RDT performance among 9,067 children (weighted n=9,131) versus microscopy. RDT showed sensitivity 88.6%, specificity 75.2%, positive predictive value 49.5%, negative predictive value 96.0%, accuracy 78.1%, and Cohen's kappa κ=0.496 (moderate agreement). ROC AUC was 0.816, indicating high reliability for ruling out malaria but risk of overestimating prevalence.

articles.headline: Severe malaria with cerebral involvement, anemia, thrombocytopenia; high pediatric mortality
articles.title_original: Burden of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: clinical insights from a prospective study in Jacobabad, Sindh, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Noor M, Junaid M, Furqan A
articles.details: Prospective observational study of 260 admitted malaria patients in Jacobabad, Pakistan (2024–2025); 217 (83.5%) uncomplicated, 43 (16.5%) severe. Severe malaria was significantly associated with jaundice, bleeding, pallor, respiratory distress, seizures, cerebral malaria, anemia, and thrombocytopenia. Case fatality rate was 16.2% in pediatric age group (0–15 years), with cerebral malaria as the most fatal complication.

articles.headline: CYP3A5*3 and CYP2C19*3 variants modulate chloroquine-primaquine pharmacokinetics in P. vivax
articles.title_original: Pharmacogenetic Profiling of Cytochrome P450 Enzymes in Plasmodium vivax Patients Treated with Chloroquine and Primaquine: Implications for Personalized Malaria Therapy.
articles.authors: Phompradit P, Muhamad P, Cheoymang A, Na-Bangchang K
articles.details: Pharmacogenetic analysis of 99 Burmese P. vivax patients treated with chloroquine (25 mg/kg) and primaquine (15 mg/kg/day for 14 days), genotyped for CYP polymorphisms. CYP3A5*3 was most frequent (71.7%) and significantly associated with altered desethylchloroquine levels; CYP2C19*3 was associated with faster parasite clearance. All patients achieved complete parasite clearance and fever resolution without recurrence, despite high prevalence of reduced-function CYP variants, suggesting alternative compensatory metabolic pathways.

articles.headline: Engineered symbiotic bacteria inhibit Plasmodium in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Evaluating paratransgenesis using engineered symbiotic bacteria for Plasmodium inhibition in mosquito vectors: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Kernyuy FB, Kintung IF, Yensii NG, Chick JA, Obi AMM
articles.details: Systematic review of 10 studies on paratransgenesis, using engineered bacteria (Asaia, Serratia, Pantoea, Enterobacter, Aspergillus oryzae) to deliver antiplasmodial effector proteins. Scorpine achieved up to 97.8% inhibition of P. falciparum oocysts, and multi-effector approaches surpassed 89% inhibition. Authors conclude this is a potentially important complementary malaria control tool, though ecological safety and field validation remain key obstacles.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing detects mosquito species, hosts, and parasites
articles.title_original: A multiplex assay to detect mosquito species, bloodmeal host source and Plasmodium in malaria vectors using Nanopore amplicon sequencing.
articles.authors: Rogers EA, Derilus D, Sandi C, Reimer L, Lenhart A, Impoinvil LM
articles.details: Study compared Nanopore MinION sequencing with Sanger sequencing for simultaneous identification of mosquito species, bloodmeal hosts, and P. falciparum detection. Tested 150 insectary-reared mosquitoes (9 species), 150 blood-fed mosquitoes with 5 vertebrate host sources, and 40 P. falciparum-infected samples. MinION accurately identified all 9 mosquito species, detected bloodmeal hosts up to 48 hours post feeding, and detected P. falciparum with high concordance to Sanger sequencing.

articles.headline: Genomic insecticide resistance varies geographically in Anopheles arabiensis
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal signatures of genomic insecticide resistance in the Anopheles arabiensis mosquito malaria vector from Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Eukubay A, Bennett KL, Tekie H, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Gemechu F, Miles A, Abera D, Clarkson CS, Golassa L
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of Anopheles arabiensis revealed spatial and temporal variation in insecticide resistance genes. Vgsc-L995F target-site mutation was highly prevalent in northern Ethiopia but less common at other sites; western Ethiopia showed high frequencies of cytochrome P450 (Cyp6aa/p) and carboxylesterase (Coeae2-7g) copy number variants; Central Rift Valley had high variants at Gste2 and Cyp9k1. Seasonal shifts in resistance marker frequencies occurred, with increasing Vgsc-L995F and P450 variants during transmission season.

articles.headline: Understudied Anopheles species feed on humans in low-transmission Zambia
articles.title_original: Species composition and blood feeding patterns of understudied Anopheles mosquitoes in southern Zambia, an area of low malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Mudenda T, Simubali L, Mwaanga G, Lubinda M, Katowa B, Musonda M, Hamapumbu H, Chibambo P, Chooli S, Ali RLMN, Gebhardt ME, Thuma PE, Simulundu E, Moss WJ, Saili K, Norris DE, Stevenson JC
articles.details: Entomological study in Southern Province, Zambia collected 5,697 female anopheline mosquitoes using CDC light traps at 12 households. Nine species were identified, dominated by Anopheles rufipes (26.8%) and An. pretoriensis (25.1%). Blood-meal analysis of 401 mosquitoes revealed primary feeding on goats (62.5%), with secondary feeding on humans (19.4%) and cows (10.2%), yielding a human blood index of 0.22. One An. rufipes specimen tested positive for P. falciparum sporozoites.

articles.headline: School-age children drive malaria transmission in Malawi
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic school-age children carry the majority of transmissible Plasmodium falciparum infections.
articles.authors: Buchwald AG, Vareta J, Nwagbata O, McCann RS, Sixpence A, Matengeni A, Kamzati M, Mangani C, Seydel KB, Wilson ML, Taylor TE, Mathanga DP, Valim C, Laufer MK, Cohee LM
articles.details: Year-long cohort study in rural Malawi using molecular methods to detect P. falciparum infections and gametocyte-containing parasites. Gametocytes were detected in only 23% of the population, with school-age children having significantly higher incidence of gametocyte-containing infections and high-density infections. School-age children were the key driver of gametocyte frequencies within households. Authors estimate that clearing infections from asymptomatic school-age children during the rainy season would decrease population gametocyte abundance by 67%.

articles.headline: Photonic sensors detect malaria parasites with optical discrimination
articles.title_original: Machine learning assisted malaria detection using photonic crystal fibre optical sensors.
articles.authors: Abdullah-Al-Shafi M, Sen S, Mubassera M
articles.details: Study developed a photonic crystal fiber optical sensor for malaria detection based on refractive index variations in red blood cells, discriminating between ring trophozoite and schizont stages of Plasmodium. Operating over a refractive index range of 1.373 to 1.402, the sensor achieved relative sensitivity of 97.45% for healthy cells, 96.89% for ring stage, 96.22% for trophozoite stage, and 95.45% for schizont stage, with extremely low optical confinement losses (10⁻⁸ dB/m at 2.2 THz).

articles.headline: Seasonal migration sustains malaria transmission in Ouagadougou
articles.title_original: Rural-Urban Migration and Low-Transmission Endemicity of Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Statistical Inference Approach to Uncover Possible Mechanisms.
articles.authors: Harikripal, Zabre SP, Sie A, Ouedraogo A, Danquah I, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: Study of Ouagadougou using data-driven modeling and statistical inference to examine human migration patterns in malaria transmission. Low-transmission hotspots were observed at 3–150 cases per 10,000 person-weeks during dry months. Seasonal migration most accurately explained the persistence of low-transmission endemicity in dry months and subsequent amplification into widespread rainy-season outbreaks. Authors conclude seasonal labor migration drives malaria persistence and should be incorporated into transmission models and control strategies.

articles.headline: Nanoformulation improves artemisinin efficacy against drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: Development and evaluation of novel zein-based artemisinin sustained-release formulation for treating drug-resistant malaria.
articles.authors: Wang Y, Yu X, Zhang X, He X, Tang Y, Fang L, Culleton R, Zhang Q, Dong W, Cao J
articles.details: Study developed a zein (maize protein) nanocarrier formulation of artemisinin that achieved 200-fold enhancement in water solubility and sustained drug release. In vitro assays with artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum demonstrated potent inhibitory effects in ring survival and recrudescence assays. In vivo studies in rodent and humanized erythrocyte models showed the formulation significantly prolonged artemisinin half-life, reduced parasitemia, prevented malaria recrudescence, and overcame artemisinin resistance compared to free artemisinin.

articles.headline: Acute malaria impairs Ebola vaccine efficacy through lymph node macrophages
articles.title_original: Acute malaria dysregulates specialized lymph node macrophages to suppress vaccine-elicited protection against the Ebola virus.
articles.authors: Elliff J, Grady L, O'Donnell KL, Messingham C, Rogers KJ, Akther J, Thurman A, Vijay R, Pezzulo A, Randall T, Marzi A, Butler NS, Maury W
articles.details: Study showed that acute murine Plasmodium infection at the time of rVSV/EBOV (Ebola virus) vaccination reduced vaccine-mediated protection against lethal mouse-adapted EBOV challenge. Suppression was associated with Plasmodium-induced interferon gamma elevation, which decreased rVSV/EBOV replication in lymph node macrophages and reduced primary anti-EBOV glycoprotein antibody responses. Higher vaccine doses partially overcame the antibody deficits and restored protective responses.

articles.headline: Biomarkers identify cerebral malaria among comatose children
articles.title_original: Specific Biomarkers Differentiate Cerebral Malaria from Other Causes of Coma in African children.
articles.authors: Mwangi S, Gomes C, Abdi AI, Rodriguez A
articles.details: Retrospective study in 379 Kenyan children evaluated plasma Angiopoietin-like 4 and Inhibin-βE biomarkers for distinguishing cerebral malaria, severe malarial respiratory distress, and severe malarial anemia from non-malarial causes of coma. Single biomarkers showed high specificity (AUC 0.82–0.86 for various malaria presentations), with combined biomarkers plus platelet levels achieving AUC 0.96. These biomarkers also predicted death from cerebral malaria (AUC 0.85).

articles.headline: Ivermectin kills malaria vectors through glutamate channel binding
articles.title_original: Lethal effects of ivermectin structures on malaria vectors and in silico analysis of interactions with their glutamate-gated chloride ion channels.
articles.authors: Nguyen MN, Jones AK, Hotwagner D, Khemrattrakool P, Hongsuwong T, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Jittamala P, Sriwichai P, Tarning J, Kobylinski KC
articles.details: Study evaluated lethality of ivermectin structures (full ivermectin with sugar rings, monosaccharide with one ring, aglycone with no rings) in Anopheles dirus and An. minimus. In silico structural docking models of Anopheles GluCl (glutamate-gated chloride channel) identified a hydrogen bond between the second sugar ring hydroxyl group (4″-OH) and threonine 304 (THR304) of the M2-M3 loop, a key difference from C. elegans where isoleucine 273 (ILE273) occupies this position.

articles.headline: Avian malaria transmission depends on parasitemia levels and host overlap
articles.title_original: Variation in pathogen load and the pathogen load-infectiousness relationship broaden avian malaria's distribution.
articles.authors: Seidl CM, Parise KL, Ipsaro IJ, Leach S, Hays D, Morimoto R, Banister K, Ferreira FC, Abraham EC, Foster JT, Paxton EH, Kilpatrick AM
articles.details: Experimental study in canaries quantified the relationship between Plasmodium relictum parasitemia and infectiousness to Culex quinquefasciatus mosquitoes. Using this relationship, infectiousness was estimated for 17 bird species across 11 communities in Hawai'i. Infectiousness increased with parasitemia, temperature, and time since mosquito feeding. The gradual slope at low parasitemia levels meant a wide range of parasite loads were partly infectious; substantial within-species variation resulted in extensive overlap in infectiousness among bird species.

articles.headline: Climate and seasonality predict malaria cases in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Association of Climate Variables with Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Cases in Mandoto, Madagascar: A Statistical Modeling Study.
articles.authors: Rajaonarimirana E, Ravaoarimanga M, Lockwood S, Cramer E, Rajesy M, Drakeley C, Randremanana RV, White M
articles.details: From 27 health centers in Mandoto District (2019–2024), 276,318 RDTs yielded 109,428 malaria cases (39.6% positivity). After 2021, P. falciparum was 71.5% and P. vivax 28.5%, both co-endemic. Precipitation and temperature effectively revealed seasonality and improved model accuracy, with P. falciparum showing stronger associations with climate variability. Time-series modeling combining precipitation and temperature data can predict malaria cases and support resource allocation.

articles.headline: IL-10-dominant cytokine profile linked to postpartum malaria susceptibility
articles.title_original: Postpartum cytokine shifts and IL-10-mediated immune suppression in malaria-infected primigravid women.
articles.authors: Traore O, Rouamba T, Zango SH, Sorgho H, Valea I, Traore-Coulibaly M, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: 33 Burkinabè women enrolled at delivery and followed at 1 and 3 months postpartum. At delivery, 48.5% tested positive for malaria by PCR. Malaria-infected women had significantly elevated IL-10 and decreased IL-6:IL-10 ratio versus non-infected (p=0.005), with strong correlation between IL-10 and malaria infection (σ=0.9, p<0.001). IL-10-dominant profile at delivery associated with postpartum malaria susceptibility, suggesting cytokine modulation as potential therapeutic approach.

articles.headline: Heme and hemozoin induce platelet death in vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Heme and hemozoin induce platelet cell death through UPR-induced apoptosis and ferroptosis in vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Gomes MT, Medeiros-de-Moraes IM, Vieira-de-Abreu A, de Azevedo-Quintanilha IG, Fontes TM, de Brito Carneiro A, Azambuja P, Tolley ND, Rowley JW, Carpinter BA, Ferreira MU, Gonçalves RM, Rocha VN, Rosa Nascimento AL, Carvalho WA, Scopel KKG, Campbell RA, Bozza PT, Weyrich AS, Zimmerman GA, Lacerda MVG, Castro-Faria-Neto HC, Hottz ED
articles.details: Heme from thrombocytopenic vivax malaria plasma (but not nonthrombocytopenic vivax or falciparum) induced platelet cell death ex vivo. Both heme and hemozoin activated apoptotic caspases; hemozoin triggered stronger necrotic features consistent with ferroptosis dependent on lipid peroxidation and DGAT1/2 enzymes. These regulated cell-death pathways were associated with thrombocytopenia in vivax malaria, with potential implications for other hemolytic disorders.

articles.headline: Post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis in severe P. falciparum malaria in child
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Complicated by Post-artemisinin Delayed Hemolysis in a Non-immune Pediatric Returning Traveler.
articles.authors: Galadari S, Kalsekar M, Iqbal A, Daoud Z, Balila M, Nyanga O
articles.details: 9-year-old non-immune boy with severe P. falciparum malaria from travel developed multiorgan dysfunction including thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury, hepatic dysfunction, and hypoxemic pneumonia. Treated with intravenous artesunate then oral artemether-lumefantrine with initial clinical improvement and parasite clearance. Ten days post-treatment, he re-presented with hemoglobin decline, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LDH, reticulocytosis, and low haptoglobin without parasitemia, confirming post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis (PADH). Required transfusion with full recovery.
