articles.headline: Greece's malaria elimination: from endemicity to eradication and re-emergence
articles.title_original: Malaria in Greece: From Ancient Scourge to Eradication.
articles.authors: Christodoulou MK
articles.details: Narrative review of malaria in Greece from antiquity to present. Greece successfully eliminated endemic malaria through coordinated vector control, antimalarial treatment, environmental interventions, and sustained surveillance. Following eradication, locally acquired cases re-emerged in early 21st century due to global mobility and persistent competent vectors. Greece's experience demonstrates malaria elimination is achievable through long-term public health efforts but requires continuous surveillance and rapid response.

articles.headline: Malaria in Nigerian under-fives: persistent burden despite available interventions
articles.title_original: Burden, challenges, and control of malaria among under-5s in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoro LN, Ayesiga I, Yeboah MO, Bulus NG, Gmanyami JM, Ismaila EL, Ahgu O, Otokpa EO, Edet EN, Dogo JM, Ediamu TD, Atimango L, Isiko I
articles.details: Narrative review of malaria epidemiology and control in Nigerian children under five. Northern states report highest prevalence with rural areas disproportionately affected. Gaps persist in intervention coverage (LLINs, IPT, IRS) despite demonstrated effectiveness, with diagnostic limitations, drug resistance, and suboptimal preventive measure use. Innovations in diagnostics, vaccines, and surveillance remain underutilized. Coordinated multisectoral response addressing socioeconomic vulnerabilities is essential for improving malaria control.

articles.headline: Cost-effectiveness review: LLINs and IRS highly cost-effective in Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Strategic resource allocation for malaria elimination in endemic settings: a systematic review of cost-effectiveness evidence.
articles.authors: Firdaus MH, Ho Imran DS, Puteh SEW, Zhueng TJ, Hamid MHA, Idris ZM, Manaf MRA
articles.details: Systematic review of 18 economic evaluations (2018–2025) on malaria prevention and treatment in endemic settings. LLINs and IRS were highly cost-effective with costs per DALY averted below national GDP thresholds. Combined LLIN+IRS, SMC/PDMC, IPTp, and ACT+RDT were highly cost-effective. RTS,S vaccination and RDT+microscopy were moderately cost-effective. G6PD screening with tafenoquine and traveller chemoprophylaxis were context-specific or less cost-effective.

articles.headline: No G6PD A- variant detected in P. falciparum-positive Nigerians
articles.title_original: No G6PD A- (G202A) variant detected among Plasmodium falciparum-positive patients in Awka, Southeast Nigeria: a hospital-based study.
articles.authors: Ikegbunam M, Uchechukwu N, Abone H, Grace AE, Ezeunala M, Joy N, Immaculata NC, Igwe JO, Okeke O, Nworji F, Ihekwereme P
articles.details: 64 malaria-positive participants in Awka, Nigeria screened for A376G and G202A G6PD variants via PCR and Sanger sequencing. B variant (normal) was predominant at 83%; none had the A- variant (G202A) associated with G6PD deficiency. B and A+ variants showed no significant impact on hemoglobin and parasitemia levels. Findings support absence of G202A variant in this hospital-based sample; broader community genotyping recommended before informing treatment policy.

articles.headline: Enhanced parallel IBD detection from large-scale Plasmodium genomic data
articles.title_original: hmmibd-rs: an enhanced hmmIBD implementation for parallelizable identity-by-descent detection from large-scale Plasmodium genomic data.
articles.authors: Guo B, Schaffner SF, Taylor AR, O'Connor TD, Takala-Harrison S
articles.details: Developed hmmibd-rs, enhanced multi-threaded implementation of hmmIBD for identity-by-descent (IBD) detection in P. falciparum, supporting non-uniform recombination rates. Using 128 threads shortened IBD detection from 5.2 days to 1.3 hours for 220 million pairs of simulated P. falciparum-like chromosomes (~100-fold speedup). Applied to MalariaGEN Pf7 (≈10,000 monoclonal samples), completed within hours. Tool enhances accuracy by mitigating IBD breakpoint overestimation in recombination cold spots.

articles.headline: Feasibility of community-based rectal artesunate for severe malaria in children
articles.title_original: Severe malaria treatment with rectal artesunate and artemisinin-based combination therapy in remote settings: an effectiveness-implementation hybrid type 3 study protocol (SEMA ReACT).
articles.authors: Hachizovu S, Manyando C, Nambozi M, Khondowe S, Hamainza B, Mukumbuta D, Rietveld H, Mukomena Sompwe E, Kikunda G, Muhindo-Mavoko H, Luzolo Khote F, Kanyamukenge D, Bastiaens H, Kakudji A, Maseko T, Makenga G, Baraka V, Mutombo A, Duparc S, Van Geertruyden JP
articles.details: Effectiveness-implementation hybrid Type 3 study in Nchelenge, Zambia and Kapolowe, DRC assessing feasibility of rectal artesunate (RAS) by community health workers for children 6 months–5 years. Primary outcomes: time from symptom onset to treatment, 28-day PCR-corrected cure rates following RAS+ACT or RAS+injectable artesunate+ACT, and healthcare utilization. Will provide evidence on feasibility and effectiveness of community-based pre-referral treatment for severe malaria in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: One-third of DRC children 6-59 months have malaria; wealth and ITN use key
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with malaria among children aged 6-59 months in the Democratic Republic of the Congo: a nationwide cross-sectional survey.
articles.authors: Adam J, Mukuku O, Tawi JM, Luoga P, Mutombo AM
articles.details: Secondary analysis from 2023/24 DRC Demographic and Health Survey of 10,013 children aged 6–59 months. Overall prevalence 33% (95% CI=30.2–35.6), ranging from 5% in Kinshasa to 61% in Bas-Uele. Associated factors: age 24–59 months (APR=1.25), maternal no/primary education (APR=1.15–1.13), poor/middle wealth (APR=1.52–1.42), never using ITN (APR=1.18), no household television (APR=1.73), unimproved floor/roof (APR=1.54–1.27).

articles.headline: Primaquine essential for P. vivax; AL monotherapy less effective than CQ
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether lumefantrine vs chloroquine for the treatment of Plasmodium Vivax infection in Pakistan.
articles.authors: Khan S, Muqtadir J, Abbas SA, Ayoob K, Wahab A, Zaidi SHM, Das B, Bhatti S, Vohra EA
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 354 malaria patients in Karachi, Pakistan. By Day 28, P. vivax recurrence: 10.2% (CQ), 20.2% (AL), 1.1% (CQ+PQ), 3.8% (AL+PQ). At 6 months: 15.3% (CQ), 32.2% (AL), 1.1% (CQ+PQ), 6.3% (AL+PQ); Kaplan-Meier 6-month cumulative incidence: 21.0% (CQ, 95% CI 11.0–31.0), 39.1% (AL, 95% CI 26.7–51.4), 1.5% (CQ+PQ), 10.0% (AL+PQ, 95% CI 1.6–18.4). AL monotherapy associated with higher recurrence hazard than CQ; primaquine regimens significantly reduced recurrence.

articles.headline: Longitudinal study protocol: malaria burden and vector bionomics in India
articles.title_original: Multicentric longitudinal study on malaria burden, vector bionomics and health system assessment in diverse eco-epidemiological settings in the context of malaria elimination in India: study protocol.
articles.authors: Rahul A, Thiruvengadam K, Sharma R, Jabir M, Viswan A, Jency PJ, Bharadwaj N, Muthukumaravel S, Shriram AN, Raju HK, Kannan S, Singh M, Bhuyan PJ, Jain T, Rahi M
articles.details: Study protocol for longitudinal multicentric assessment across 12 districts spanning 10 Indian states enrolling approximately 25,000 individuals. Will evaluate malaria burden, transmission determinants, health system performance, vector bionomics, and environmental drivers through baseline and monthly prevalence surveys using RDTs and microscopy, PCR detection of low-density parasitemia, behavioral surveys, health system performance evaluation, entomological surveillance, and spatiotemporal analysis of climate variables. No results presented as this is a study protocol.

articles.headline: P. falciparum STOML-FtsH complex critical for asexual development
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum stomatin-like protein forms a putative complex with a metalloprotease in distinct mitochondrial loci.
articles.authors: Verhoef JMJ, Bekkering ET, Boshoven C, Hannon M, Evers F, Proellochs NI, Spruijt CG, Kooij TWA
articles.details: Study of stomatin-like protein (STOML) in P. falciparum mitochondria. Deletion of STOML caused significant growth defect and slower asexual blood-stage development, while sexual-stage development remained unaffected. STOML localizes to punctate structures at mitochondrial branching points and forms a protein complex with FtsH zinc-dependent metalloprotease. The STOML-FtsH structure resembles bacterial vault-like complexes and appears to facilitate parasite asexual development.

articles.headline: Loiasis and malaria show no association in rural Gabon
articles.title_original: There Is No Association Between Loiasis and Malaria: Findings from a Secondary Analysis of a Cross-Sectional Survey in Rural Gabon.
articles.authors: Werner J, Zoleko-Manego R, Mombo-Ngoma G, Ramharter M, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Secondary analysis of cross-sectional survey in Gabon (2015-2016) with 947 participants examined malaria-loiasis association. Crude analysis showed apparently protective association (OR 0.67, 95% CI 0.45-1.01, p=0.0521), but adjusted analysis found no significant association (adjusted OR 1.31, 95% CI 0.81-2.11, p=0.276). Apparent crude protective effect was explained by different age distributions of the two diseases rather than true biological interaction.

articles.headline: P. falciparum predominates in imported malaria cases in Algeria
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in a Malaria-Free Region: A Retrospective Analysis of Epidemiological, Clinical, and Therapeutic Patterns in Tizi-Ouzou, Algeria (2018-2024).
articles.authors: Yousfi S, Belaid D, Salhi O, Touhami NAK, Ouchene N, Elias N, Moussaoui L, Saidani S, Silem C, Seklaoui N, Bouchaib H
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of imported malaria in Tizi-Ouzou, Algeria (2018-2024). Among 106 screened individuals, 16 were laboratory-confirmed malaria cases (15.1% positivity). P. falciparum accounted for 87.5% of cases, often with low parasitemia (<4% in 68.8%). Uncomplicated malaria represented 78.6% of cases, severe 21.4%. Main biological abnormalities: anemia (92.8%), thrombocytopenia (42.8%), hepatic cytolysis (43%). Mefloquine was most frequently prescribed treatment (71.4%).

articles.headline: Malaria cases rising in Haryana despite prior elimination progress
articles.title_original: Malaria Surge in Haryana - an early warning to relook ongoing elimination efforts.
articles.authors: Kumari A, Kant R
articles.details: Analysis of malaria epidemiology in Haryana State, India combining literature review with annual surveillance data (2018-2024). Cases decreased dramatically from 3,149 to 52 (98.34% reduction) by 2022, but subsequently increased to 270 cases in 2024, showing concerning rising trend. Study examined correlation with climatic variability and identified climate-related factors affecting mosquito proliferation and gaps in vector control implementation as drivers of recent surge.

articles.headline: P. vivax dominates imported malaria in South-West Delhi
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in South-West Delhi: Five-year Epidemiological Trends (2020-2024) from a Fever Clinic and Implications for Urban Malaria Elimination.
articles.authors: Kumar M, Pal O, Saini S, Savargaonkar D, Anvikar A, Bharti PK, Nitika N
articles.details: Analysis of 7,257 fever clinic visits (2020-2024) at ICMR-NIMR, New Delhi identified 58 malaria cases (0.8%). P. vivax accounted for 94.8% (55/58) of cases, P. falciparum 3.4% (2/58). Males comprised 70.7% and ages 18-45 comprised 60.3% of cases. Imported cases represented 51.7% (30/58), with 76.7% from neighboring Uttar Pradesh districts. Proportion of imported cases increased from 52.2% in 2020 to 66.7% in 2024.

articles.headline: ITN use gaps persist among wealthier, educated groups in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Insecticide-Treated Net Utilization Among Children and Pregnant Women: 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey Analysis.
articles.authors: Omole EB, Ndukwe OS
articles.details: Analysis of 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey included 13,727 households, 14,476 women aged 15-49, and 12,742 children under 5. ITN ownership was 56% of households; 50% of women and 41.2% of children slept under ITNs. Higher utilization in rural areas and North West/North East regions (p<0.001), and lower wealth quintiles (p<0.001). Younger children <12 months and women with no/primary education had higher ITN use; wealthier and more educated groups had lower use.

articles.headline: Rainfall predicts malaria incidence with 1-month lag in Uganda
articles.title_original: Climate variability and malaria incidence trends in Yumbe District, West Nile Sub-region of Uganda (2017-2021).
articles.authors: Ninsiima LR, Musiitwa R, Nanyunja Z, Muleme J, Maasaba C, Anule T, Musoke D
articles.details: Time-series analysis of 2,066,711 malaria cases in Yumbe district, Uganda (2017-2021) examined associations with climate variables. Rainfall was positively associated with malaria incidence at 1-month lag (β=0.38, p<0.05) and strongly preceded malaria cases by 1 month (r=0.759, p<0.001). Minimum temperature was inversely associated (β=-0.29, p<0.05). Maximum temperature showed no significant effect. Results indicate rainfall and minimum temperature drive seasonal transmission patterns.

articles.headline: Malaria test-and-treat guidelines poorly implemented in Nigerian hospitals
articles.title_original: Quality of inpatient test-and-treat malaria case-management in public and private hospitals in Kano State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Getachew D, Ogbulafor N, Shekarau E, Musa B, Yusuf A, Haruna S, Oladosu OO, Oresanya O, Maxwell K, Zurovac D
articles.details: Quality assessment of 18 hospitals (public and private) in Kano State, Nigeria reviewing 2,814 suspected malaria admissions and 211 malaria slides. Composite test-and-treat performance was 39.3% (children 45.7% vs adults 26.5%; public 39.8% vs private 30.8%). Among suspected cases, 73.7% were tested and 90.2% of severe malaria cases received artesunate. Only 30% of artesunate-treated patients were prescribed subsequent ACT. Routine microscopy had 93.2% sensitivity but only 42.5% specificity.

articles.headline: Global survey: pfdhfr most prevalent P. falciparum resistance marker
articles.title_original: The Global Landscape of Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Markers, 2005-2025: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Habarugira F, Batamuriza J, Ndahimana R, Minega JN, Dieng MM, Mutsaka-Makuvaza MJ, Daba TM, Idaghdour Y, Mutesa L
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 261 studies from 64 countries (2005-2025) examined P. falciparum drug-resistance markers. Pooled prevalence: pfdhfr 85.7%, pfcrt 78.0%, pfdhps 73.7%, pfmdr1 60.5%, pfk13 45.0%. Rising pfk13 prevalence since 2012 indicates emerging artemisinin resistance. Persistent pfdhfr/pfdhps mutations reflect ongoing sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine selective pressure. Authors conclude integrating molecular surveillance into national malaria programs is essential for guiding treatment strategies and supporting elimination progress.

articles.headline: Erratum to transcriptome profiling of liver-stage Plasmodium parasites
articles.title_original: Correction: Transcriptome profiling of intrahepatocytic Plasmodium and their host hepatocytes based on the infection phase and the zonation of the liver.
articles.authors: Mo Z, Chen Y, Qin Y, Song J
articles.details: Correction notice to previously published article (DOI: 10.3389/fgene.2025.1548487). No substantive findings presented in this erratum. The original article addressed transcriptome profiling of intrahepatocytic Plasmodium parasites and their host hepatocytes.

articles.headline: qTRACE platform reveals dormancy-driven artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: High throughput quantitative tracking of Plasmodium falciparum clonal blood stage parasite growth and applications for antimalarial drug discovery.
articles.authors: Cheng Z, Ru L, Hoang A, Wang M, Ganjikunta S, Maher SP, Kyle DE
articles.details: Developed qTRACE (Quantitative Tracking After Chemotherapy Exposure), a high-throughput platform with AI for single-parasite resolution tracking of P. falciparum growth and drug effects. Found that up to 50% of viable parasites after artemisinin exposure recover directly from drug-induced dormancy. Next-generation qTRACE integrated with deep learning confirmed viability of dormant ring stages and demonstrated that recovery rates from artemisinin-induced dormancy differ between artemisinin-susceptible and -resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Geography and occupation shape Cambodian Plasmodium falciparum genetic relatedness
articles.title_original: Genetic relatedness of Cambodian Plasmodium falciparum isolates is driven by geography and occupation.
articles.authors: Rowley E, Guo B, Wojnarski M, Spring MD, Sriwichai S, Vesely BA, Silva JC, Waters NC, Thaloengsok S, Saingam P, Chaisatit C, Lertsethtakarn P, Sok S, Prom S, Chaudhury S, Lon C, Lek D, Saunders D, O'Connor TD, Takala-Harrison S
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 107 P. falciparum isolates from military personnel and farmers in Cambodia (2014-2016). Mean identity-by-descent (IBD) sharing was 40%, with significantly greater relatedness in Oddar Meanchey than Kratie province (p<2.2e-16). Isolates from different occupations shared 7.2% less genome IBD than farmer pairs. Highly related clusters (>90% IBD) were associated with occupation and K13/PfCRT mutations, suggesting occupation-specific transmission sources.

articles.headline: Spatial modeling identifies Anhui Province malaria re-transmission risk zones
articles.title_original: Assessing the risk of malaria re-transmission in Anhui Province, China: an integration of spatiotemporal scan statistics and ecological niche modeling.
articles.authors: Liu B, Zhang T, Xue J, Huo S, He H, Tan X, Li W, Li S
articles.details: Spatial-temporal analysis of imported malaria cases (2017-2023) and vector surveillance in Anhui Province using ecological niche modeling. Ensemble model showed strong predictive performance (AUC 0.943, TSS 0.759). Anopheles sinensis distribution was mainly affected by annual vegetation index, night-light brightness, and precipitation. Central region identified as highest risk for malaria re-transmission.

articles.headline: Pediatric malaria causes high rates of long-term neurological sequelae
articles.title_original: Malaria in Children is Associated with a High Rate of Neurological Sequelae and Need for Rehabilitative Therapies in Germany.
articles.authors: Adolph JE, Pentek C, Rink L, Held CJ, Della Marina A, Gangfuß A, Kölbel H, Dziobaka J, Rath PM, Verhasselt HL, Felderhoff-Müser U, Dohna-Schwake C, Goretzki SC
articles.details: Retrospective study of 11 pediatric patients (median age 9.5 years) with malaria, 91% P. falciparum, 72.7% with severe malaria. Despite guideline-appropriate treatment with artesunate (64%), 82% had residual neurological symptoms and 44% had neurocognitive deficits. Three patients developed epilepsies; three had cytotoxic lesions of the corpus callosum on MRI. At 12 months, 77.8% showed clinical improvement.

articles.headline: Out-of-pocket payments impair malaria treatment access in developing countries
articles.title_original: Implications of out-of-pocket payments for the control of malaria in developing countries: A narrative review.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Obeagu GU
articles.details: Narrative review of out-of-pocket payment implications for malaria treatment in developing countries. Out-of-pocket payments significantly hinder timely treatment access, particularly affecting low-income and rural households. Identified delayed treatment-seeking, underutilization of preventive measures, and suboptimal diagnostics use as major challenges. Alternative financing mechanisms including subsidies, community insurance, and donor-funded programs highlighted as solutions to reduce barriers and improve equitable malaria control.

articles.headline: Type 2 diabetes comorbidity increases parasite load and vaccine antigen responses
articles.title_original: Malaria-diabetes comorbidity is linked to higher parasitaemia and enhanced IgG response to malaria vaccine candidate antigens.
articles.authors: Agyare G, Courtin D, Asamoah Sakyi S, Nakotey GK, Adjeley Frempong N, Barnie PA, Tchum SK, Victor Nuvor S, Amoani B
articles.details: Hospital-based case-control study (144 participants: 40 with diabetes, 25 with comorbid malaria-diabetes, 41 malaria only, 38 controls) from Ghana. Malaria-diabetes comorbidity group had significantly higher parasitaemia (1702 vs 932, p=0.02). IgG responses markedly elevated to GLURP-R2 (1.60-fold, p=0.01), GLURP-R0 (1.43-fold, p=0.03), and MSP1 (1.42-fold, p<0.05), while responses to MSP3, AMA-1, and CSP showed no significant differences.

articles.headline: Seasonal mass drug administration in Burkina Faso interrupts dry-season transmission
articles.title_original: Impacts of Seasonal Mass Drug Administration in School-Aged Children on Chronic Malaria Infections in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Paré IGL, Da DF, Guigma F, Bazié T, Somé BM, Djègbè NDC, Cissé F, Ouédraogo K, Somé AF, Kaboré J, Churcher TS, Dabiré RK
articles.details: Seasonal parasitological surveys of school-aged children in Burkina Faso comparing uninfected cohort (no treatment) and parasitemic cohort treated with artemether-lumefantrine. Ninety-three percent of uninfected children remained parasite-free; 91% of treated children remained negative during follow-up. Persistent infections remained stable at 5-9% in both cohorts. Drug-resistance markers showed low mutation frequencies, demonstrating malaria transmission interruption during dry season.

articles.headline: Low R21/MATRIX vaccine awareness but high acceptability among Nigerian caregivers
articles.title_original: R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine: awareness, perception, acceptability, and willingness to pay among caregivers of under-five children in endemic communities in Lagos, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Agboola P, Precious DN, Adebisi YA, Kingsley OO, Olaniyi P, Ezekiel A, Ode OP, Aderounmu B, Olusogo AP, Opeyemi FI, Adeyemi-Benson OS
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 372 caregivers of children under five in Lagos, Nigeria. Vaccine awareness was low (9.7%), but positive perceptions were high (98.1%), with 76.6% indicating they would "definitely" vaccinate their child. Willingness to pay reported by 71.2% of respondents, with 51.9% willing to pay ₦1,001-₦5,000 per dose. Higher household income and guardianship status significantly increased likelihood of willingness to pay.

articles.headline: Malaria service readiness low in Ethiopian antenatal care facilities
articles.title_original: Malaria service readiness and associated factors among health facilities that provide antenatal care services in Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study using generalised estimating equation analysis.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Asemahagn MA, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Azage M
articles.details: Cross-sectional facility-based study of 1156 public and private health facilities providing antenatal care in Ethiopia. Only 15.7% (95% CI 13.8-18.0) demonstrated full malaria service readiness, while 14.3% had no key components. Governance factors were strong predictors: client suggestion boxes (AOR=1.60), recent report submission (AOR=2.79), and monthly staff meetings (AOR=1.64) were associated with higher readiness. Substantial regional variation observed across Ethiopia.

articles.headline: Urban Ethiopian towns show marked heterogeneity in malaria seroprevalence
articles.title_original: Heterogeneity of malaria transmission in urban settings in Ethiopia: A seroprevalence and risk factor analysis.
articles.authors: Teka H, Fekadu S, Ejigu LA, Sisay S, Abdo M, Demisse M, Mamo H, Drakeley C, Byrne I, Medhin G, Golassa L, Gadisa E, Tadesse FG
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 912 individuals from three Ethiopian towns (January-March 2020). Malaria prevalence was low: 0.78% by RDT, 4% by qPCR (1.7% P. falciparum, 2.3% P. vivax). Seroprevalence showed marked heterogeneity: Awash Sebat Kilo highest (36.5% P. falciparum, 28% P. vivax recent markers), Adama lowest (2.8% each). Proximity to breeding sites (OR=2.8, p=0.037) and age >15 years (OR=4.0, p<0.001) were significant risk factors.

articles.headline: Climate thresholds (25°C, 37mm rainfall) optimally predict malaria incidence
articles.title_original: Integrating causal inference and machine learning to quantify climate-malaria relationships: Evidence of temperature and rainfall thresholds from Colombian municipalities.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez JD
articles.details: Ecological observational study of 969 Colombian municipalities (2007-2023) analyzing 1,075,112 malaria cases using targeted maximum likelihood estimation. Identified non-linear exposure-response curves showing optimal temperature of 25°C and approximately 37 mm of rainfall for highest malaria incidence. Analysis revealed residual confounding bias (p<0.05), but E-values indicated low to moderate tolerance to unmeasured confounders (1.14-1.48). Supports integration of rainfall and temperature thresholds into early-warning systems.

articles.headline: Multi-country analysis reveals health system barriers to GMS malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Strengthening the health systems at national level for malaria elimination in the Greater Mekong Subregion countries: a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Htike W, Oo WH, Bennett CM, Agius PA, Barry AE, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: Multi-country qualitative study in Greater Mekong Subregion with 39 stakeholder interviews including policymakers, Ministry of Health staff, implementing partners, and technical agencies. Thematic analysis aligned with WHO health system building blocks identified requirements for elimination: strong political commitment, targeted interventions for high-risk groups, reliable forecasting and supply chains, skilled workforce, and quality assurance. Major barriers included lack of targeted national policies, incomplete private sector reporting, workforce shortage, supply chain constraints, declining international funding, and poor compliance to regulations.

articles.headline: P. vivax CyRPA protein shows vaccine potential across diverse populations
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, natural selection, and immunological features of the Plasmodium vivax CyRPA protein: Implications for vaccine development.
articles.authors: Garzón-Ospina D, Buitrago SP, Cepeda-Riaño N, Castro-Cavadía CJ, Yasnot-Acosta MF
articles.details: Analysis of 950 pvcyrpa sequences including 42 new Colombian isolates revealed high genetic diversity shaped by natural selection and recombination. Identified B- and T-cell epitopes with strong HLA binding affinities that overlap with regions eliciting natural immune responses in infected individuals. Immune simulation predicted robust humoral memory profile. Conserved C-terminal region identified as strong, broadly reactive vaccine candidate for Plasmodium vivax.

articles.headline: Molecular mechanisms regulating Plasmodium gametocytogenesis reviewed
articles.title_original: Molecular mechanisms involved in Plasmodium gametocytogenesis.
articles.authors: Scovino AM, Oliveira-da-Silva RS, Almeida-da-Silva J, Mendes KD, da Silva-Junior EB, Decote-Ricardo D, Freire-de-Lima L, Freire-de-Lima CG, Totino PRR, Morrot A
articles.details: This review consolidates recent advances in understanding the complex molecular mechanisms regulating gametocytogenesis in Plasmodium falciparum. Sexual differentiation is modulated by environmental stressors like lysophosphatidylcholine depletion and orchestrated through a regulatory network including the AP2-G transcription factor, epigenetic mechanisms (H3K9me3, HP1, GDV1), and post-transcriptional regulation via RNA-binding proteins (PfPuf1, PfPuf2) and epitranscriptomic modifications (m5C and m6A methylation). Understanding these pathways is essential for identifying novel therapeutic targets and developing transmission-blocking vaccines.

articles.headline: Zanzibar malaria epidemiology shows significant non-falciparum species
articles.title_original: Understanding the Epidemiology of Malaria in Zanzibar Through Molecular and Serological Analysis of Samples Collected During Reactive Case Detection.
articles.authors: Goel V, Hassan W, Murphy C, Choloi BB, Ali M, Mohamed B, Zacharia A, Dominick MC, Thwai K, Mohammed S, Shija SJ, Bailey JA, Björkman A, Ngasala BE, Rogier E, Juliano JJ, Lin JT
articles.details: Among 1618 participants surveyed in 35 reactive case detection activities in Unguja island (May 2022–January 2023), serological exposure was 35.3% for any malaria species: 29.8% P. falciparum, 5.8% P. ovale, 5.9% P. malariae, and 5.9% P. vivax. Active infections detected by PCR were 6.0%, predominantly P. falciparum (4.6%). Travel to mainland Tanzania was the dominant risk factor for seropositivity across all four species, with geographic distribution of non-falciparum exposure showing relatively higher seroprevalences in rural districts.

articles.headline: Proline transporter PfApiAT2 essential for P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.
articles.authors: Wirth D, Khushu M, Kissel RC, Kauffman J, Taccheri C, Singh N, Summers R, Plant L, Catteruccia F, Bopp S
articles.details: PfApiAT2, a proline-specific amino acid transporter, is essential for early oocyst development in Anopheles gambiae. Halofuginone-resistant PfApiAT2-mutant parasites formed stunted oocysts severely defective in sporozoite production, while PfApiAT2-knockout parasites underwent complete block in sporogony with oocysts stalling and degenerating. Growth defect was rescued by nutrient supplementation to the mosquito vector, revealing that P. falciparum has critical nutritional dependency on its mosquito vector.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid-resistance mutation associates with transmission-blocking Microsporidia
articles.title_original: The L1014F Kdr mutation is associated with a higher prevalence and load of the Plasmodium-blocking symbiont Microsporidia MB In Anopheles Gambiae s.l. In Benin.
articles.authors: Sovi A, Ahouandjinou MJ, Aïkpon R, Totongnon GS, Yovogan B, Syme T, Dembélé A, Koukpo CZ, Sagbohan H, Ossè R, Agbo-Ola A, Gnanguenon V, Toffodji U, Megido RC, Hougbe SZ, Zoungbédji DM, Konkon AK, Cook J, Padonou GG, Protopopoff N, Akogbéto MC, Messenger LA, Adoha CJ
articles.details: Among 1040 Anopheles gambiae complex mosquitoes from 60 villages in Benin, Microsporidia MB prevalence was 1.6% in An. coluzzii and 2.2% in An. gambiae s.s., with zero co-occurrence in P. falciparum-infected mosquitoes (2% sporozoite rate overall). The L1014F kdr resistance allele was associated with higher Microsporidia MB prevalence and load: absent in kdr-SS but present with mean DNA load of 23.23 ng/µl in kdr-RR versus 10.13 ng/µl in kdr-RS mosquitoes. Elevation and soil contributed 78% and 20% respectively to Microsporidia MB habitat suitability.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology and control strategies across Africa reviewed
articles.title_original: Malaria: a review on its current epidemiological status and management strategies.
articles.authors: Okello G, Aucamp M
articles.details: This comprehensive review examines malaria epidemiology and management strategies globally, with focus on Africa where P. falciparum causes the highest mortality. Between 2000 and 2022, the WHO African Region achieved a 40% reduction in malaria incidence and 60% decline in mortality through expanded insecticide-treated nets, indoor residual spraying, rapid diagnostic tests, intermittent preventive treatment, artemisinin-based combination therapies, and malaria vaccines for children under 5. Key emerging challenges include insecticide and antimalarial drug resistance, climate change, and outdoor transmission, requiring multi-faceted strategies including seasonal malaria chemoprevention scale-up and vaccines such as RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M.

articles.headline: CYP6P3-E205D mutation drives pyrethroid resistance in An. gambiae
articles.title_original: The E205D mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid insecticide resistance in the African malaria mosquito vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Kengne-Ouafo JA, Kouamo MFM, Muhammad A, Tepa A, Ntadoun S, Mugenzi LMJ, Tekoh T, Hearn J, Tchouakui M, Wondji M, Ibrahim SS, Wondji CS
articles.details: A P450 gene haplotype containing an E205D point mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid insecticide resistance in Anopheles gambiae. In vitro metabolism assays showed the 205D variant had 3.7-fold higher catalytic efficiency for permethrin than the E205 variant. In field mosquito populations, the CYP6P3-E205D variant correlated strongly with pyrethroid resistance (odds ratio 26.4, P<0.0001), and the homozygous resistance genotype showed higher survival on PermaNet 3.0 bed nets (odds ratio 6.1, P=0.011). The 205D variant predominates in West and Central Africa but is less abundant or absent in East and South Africa.

articles.headline: Malaria and dengue transmission study in Indonesia's new capital
articles.title_original: A study protocol for a baseline community-based cross-sectional study to investigate malaria and dengue transmission dynamics in a development area of new capital city, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Pradana A, Oktaviani M, Prameswari H, Supriyanto D, Waluyo P, Ndoen E, Rosadi D, Lubis I, Suwarti S, Subekti D, Kusumaningrum T, Carroll M, Djaafara B, de Alwis R, Mishra S, Drakeley C, Leder K, Lechner A, Fornace K, Elyazar I, Surendra H
articles.details: This community-based cross-sectional study protocol will investigate malaria and dengue transmission in Ibu Kota Nusantara (IKN) in East Kalimantan and surrounding areas. Approximately 2000 individuals aged >1 year will be enrolled with finger-prick blood samples for serological analysis using multiplex bead-based assays for malaria species (including Plasmodium knowlesi) and dengue virus serotypes, along with malaria rapid diagnostic tests. Statistical and geostatistical models will assess seroprevalence, spatial patterns, and risk factors of exposure to malaria and dengue in this area experiencing land-use changes and population movements.

articles.headline: Severe P. vivax malaria with pulmonary involvement in returning traveler
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium vivax With Pulmonary Involvement: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Valido T, Goncalves A, Sanches M, Costa Silva T, Pereira C
articles.details: A 46-year-old woman presented with severe P. vivax malaria manifesting as high parasitemia (50%, 8645 parasites/µL), severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, and pulmonary involvement (ground-glass opacification on chest imaging), requiring ICU admission and high-flow oxygen therapy. Treatment with artemether-lumefantrine and doxycycline (due to unavailability of IV artesunate) resulted in progressive improvement with parasitemia clearance by day 3 and successful weaning from supplemental oxygen. This case demonstrates the potential for P. vivax to cause severe malaria with pulmonary complications.

articles.headline: P. vivax population genetics during transmission decline and rebound
articles.title_original: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Mehra S, Razook Z, Lautu-Gumal D, Nate E, Lee S, Kattenberg JH, Koepfli C, Kazura J, Ome-Kaius M, Laman M, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Barry AE
articles.details: Analysis of 336 P. vivax isolates from serial surveys in East Sepik (2005, 2012, 2016) and Madang Province (2006, 2010, 2014) in Papua New Guinea using genome-wide SNP barcodes revealed contrasting population patterns. In Madang, complexity of infection and genetic diversity varied with prevalence, with possible population bottleneck at low transmission followed by rapid recovery with resurgence; in East Sepik, impact was less dramatic with ongoing transmission of multiple residual lineages. P. vivax decline was accompanied by increased genetic differentiation between provinces, which reduced with resurgence, suggesting changes in parasite migration patterns.

articles.headline: P. fragile co-infection causes GI mucosal disruption in SIV macaques
articles.title_original: Gastrointestinal Mucosal Disruptions During ART-Treated SIV/Plasmodium fragile Co-Infection.
articles.authors: Nemphos SM, Green HC, Prusak JE, Fell SL, Midkiff C, Rodgers A, Perret J, Goff K, Miller J, Varnado M, Didier K, Valencia N, Moström MJ, Tatum C, Barnes MB, Krzykwa CE, Rowe LA, Allers C, Grasperge B, De Paris K, Maness NJ, Kaur A, Londono-Renteria B, Blair RV, Manuzak JA
articles.details: In six adult male rhesus macaques inoculated with SIVmac239 and treated with antiretroviral therapy (ART starting week 8), subsequent Plasmodium fragile co-infection (inoculated week 12, followed through week 20) was associated with gastrointestinal mucosal immune disruption. Duodenal NET-forming granulocyte frequencies were significantly positively associated with peripheral SIV burden following P. fragile co-infection, though not associated with peripheral parasitemia or CD4+ T cell frequencies. The study demonstrates that P. fragile co-infection during ART-treated SIV could cause mucosal disruptions contributing to persistent SIV replication despite ART.

articles.headline: SR1 splicing factor interacts with rhoptry proteins in P. berghei
articles.title_original: Identification of serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1-interacting proteins in Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Niikura M, Koyama Y, Fukuda Y, Kato K, Fukutomi T
articles.details: Immunoprecipitation coupled to mass spectrometry identified proteins interacting with PbSR1 (serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1) in Plasmodium berghei. PbSR1::GFP localized more strongly to nuclei than cytoplasm and interacted with nuclear proteins including RNA-binding proteins and small nuclear ribonucleoproteins, as well as cytoplasmic rhoptry proteins RhopH1 (Clag3), RhopH2, and RhopH3. Live-cell fluorescence imaging demonstrated co-localization of PbSR1 and RhopH2 in trophozoites and gametocytes but not in mature schizonts and merozoites.

articles.headline: Transfluthrin spatial repellent trial for malaria control in Uganda
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the protective efficacy of a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent product to reduce malaria prevalence in Uganda: study protocol for a cluster-randomised double-blinded control trial-the Mossie-GO trial.
articles.authors: Dennehy J, Dyall W, Jenkins A, Bradley J, Sserwanga A, Kigozi R, Bwanika JB, Nuwa A, Mawejje H, Turner MA, Wallace R, Lyle FGA, Hiscox A, Livesey AK, Logan JG, Achan J, Jones RT
articles.details: The Mossie-GO trial is a double-blinded, placebo-controlled cluster randomized trial evaluating the protective efficacy of Mossie-GO™, a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent device, in reducing malaria prevalence in children ≤5 years in rural and peri-urban Uganda. The trial randomizes 56 clusters (1:1 ratio to intervention or placebo) with 100 children enrolled at baseline and 60 children ≤5 years sampled in each cluster at 6 and 12 months. Secondary objectives include measuring impact on entomological correlates of transmission, determining user acceptance, and quantifying transfluthrin air concentration.

articles.headline: SIMPLseq: sensitive P. falciparum genotyping with contamination detection
articles.title_original: SIMPLseq: a high-sensitivity Plasmodium falciparum genotyping and PCR contamination tracking tool.
articles.authors: Schwabl P, Amaya-Romero JE, Kelley KA, Manrique P, Murphy SC, Crompton PD, Neafsey DE
articles.details: A 6-locus P. falciparum PCR amplicon sequencing panel (SIMPLseq) was optimized for low-parasitemia samples and integrated contamination detection via well-specific inline barcodes. Testing on mock samples and malaria-infected dried blood spots from Mali showed SIMPLseq achieved 100% average locus detection at ≥0.5 parasites/μl, ≥50% at 0.25-0.125 parasites/μl, zero false positives at 1% limit across 25 replicates, and correctly identified all 24 deliberately introduced contaminations plus 39 unintentional contaminations in 1420 samples. The method extends the genomic epidemiology toolkit for recurrent infection classification, polyclonality estimation, and genotypic infection endpoints in intervention trials.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus distribution and transmission role in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Updating Anopheles funestus group members in Burkina Faso: species distribution and contribution to malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Zongo ON, Toé I, Kientega M, Yemien SOG, Sawadogo G, Kaboré H, Agolinou AAM, Traore N, Kiendrebeogo E, Millogo AA, Niang A, Namountougou M, Lanfrancotti A, Nolan T, Maiga H, Diabaté A
articles.details: October-December 2022 entomological survey across 67 sites in Burkina Faso collected 617 An. funestus group members from eight regions; An. funestus s.s. was 95% (589/617), mostly from Hauts-Bassins (30%) and Sud-Ouest (55%), with 3.57% hybrids (22/617). Zoophilic behavior was prevalent at 64.3% of blood sources. P. falciparum infection was detected in 5.76% (33/572) of An. funestus s.l., with 5.6% (32/572) in s.s. and 0.174% (1/572) in rivulorum-like.

articles.headline: Neuroprotective drugs as adjunct therapy for experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Evaluation of clinically approved neuroprotective drugs as adjunct therapies in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Joshi P, Shaham SH, Shivahare R, Yadav K, Sharma A, Parul, Shukla S, Tripathi R
articles.details: Six clinically-approved drugs (topiramate, rasagiline, vinpocetine, cilostazol, piracetam, rivastigmine) were screened for antimalarial and anti-CM activity. In vitro, vinpocetine, cilostazol, and rivastigmine inhibited P. falciparum growth without toxicity. In P. berghei ANKA-infected mice, early oral cilostazol, piracetam, or rivastigmine delayed CM symptom onset and significantly improved survival versus untreated controls. When initiated after first neurological signs, only rivastigmine combined with α/β-arteether markedly improved outcomes including parasite clearance and extended survival compared to either drug alone.

articles.headline: CM and SMA associated with long-term cognitive impairment in children
articles.title_original: Cerebral Malaria and Severe Malarial Anemia, but not Other Forms of Severe Malaria, Are Associated With Long-Term Cognitive Impairment.
articles.authors: Bangirana P, Bond C, Namazzi R, Datta D, Mellencamp KA, Opoka RO, John CC
articles.details: Prospective cohort study of 600 Ugandan children aged 6 months-4 years with severe malaria evaluated cognition 12 months post-discharge. CM was associated with lower cognition scores than community controls (mean difference -0.66, 95% CI: -1.12 to -0.21, P=.001), with 10.6% (5/47) showing cognitive impairment versus 1.9% (2/104) controls (P=.003). SMA showed similar pattern (score difference -0.71, 95% CI: -1.05 to -0.37, P<.001; 12.1% [17/140] impairment vs 1.9% controls, P=.018). Other severe malaria forms did not differ significantly from controls.

articles.headline: Not all severe malaria forms impair long-term cognition
articles.title_original: Not All Forms of Severe Malaria Impair Long-term Cognition-More Characterization, More Questions.
articles.authors: Seydel KB, Ray STJ
articles.details: Commentary on differential cognitive outcomes following severe malaria, noting that certain phenotypes (cerebral malaria and severe malarial anemia) are associated with long-term cognitive impairment while others are not, and calling for more research to characterize these effects.

articles.headline: Malaria patients' symptom experiences in South Africa
articles.title_original: Experiences of people diagnosed with malaria regarding signs and symptoms at a selected village in the Vhembe District, Limpopo province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Mudzweda AD, Luhalima T
articles.details: Qualitative study using semi-structured interviews exploring experiences of people diagnosed with malaria in Mhinga Village, Vhembe District, Limpopo province, South Africa. Identified two subthemes: experiences with signs and symptoms and circumstances prompting immediate consultation. Participants experienced different symptoms according to body systems, and certain circumstances drove them to seek medical consultation immediately.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic inequality in malaria outcomes in Southeastern Tanzania
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic health equity with malaria burden: quantifying slope (Relative) index of inequity in malaria infection outcomes in the Southeastern-coastal Tanzania.
articles.authors: Liu L, Lu S, Ding W, Wang Z, Abdulla S, Lv S, Li S, Chaki PP, Zhou XN, Mlacha YP, Wang D
articles.details: Analysis of baseline survey data from China-Tanzania Demonstration Project on Malaria Control (2019) in three Southeastern Tanzania districts. Higher socioeconomic status was associated with lower malaria infection risk (OR=0.9975, 95% CI: 0.9972-0.9978), lower treatment costs (coefficient=-3.13, P<0.05), and fewer work/education days off (coefficient=-0.0017, P<0.05). Rufiji district showed most significant socioeconomic equality in infection risk (SII=-12.62%, 95% CI: -17.19% to -8.06%; RII=1.28, 95% CI: 1.12-1.55) and treatment cost (RII=1.30, 95% CI: 1.09-1.63).

articles.headline: Cortical edema patterns in pediatric cerebral malaria on MRI
articles.title_original: Peri-Rolandic and Occipital Sparing Cortical Edema: A Prevalent MRI Finding in Pediatric Patients with Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Vidal L, Zandifar A, Kim JDU, Tierradentro-García LO, Heng S, Meneses M, Small D, Chetcuti K, O'Brien N, Postels D, Taylor T, Goyal MS
articles.details: Retrospective review of admission brain MRIs from 190 Malawian children with clinical cerebral malaria (2013-2019). Cortical edema was identified in 163 (85.8%) MRIs. The predominant pattern was diffuse cortical involvement with relative sparing of occipital and peri-Rolandic areas: 63.2% (103/163) had this pattern, 22.7% (37/163) had occipital sparing only, and 14.1% (23/163) had generalized edema without focal sparing. Presence of occipital and peri-Rolandic sparing inversely correlated with brain volume score (β=-0.26, P<.001) and outcomes (OR: 0.3; 95% CI: 0.1-0.6; P=.002).

articles.headline: LLINs effect on malaria transmission dynamics in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Modelling the effect of long-lasting insecticidal nets on malaria transmission dynamics in Kebbi State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Olasupo II, Imoudu M, Abidemi A, Daniel DO, Osikoya SA, Mogbojuri OA, Nwana AO, Oniyelu DO, Olorunfemi RD, Olagbami OS, Agboola DO, Ikediashi SI, Adedeji A, Ikuseka O, Fadairo M, Odewale O, Onwuka IG, Hansinon A, Bakare DA, White LJ, Chitnis N, Oresanya O, Okoronkwo C, Nelson E, Kosoko S, Ogban GI
articles.details: Deterministic compartmental model of malaria transmission in Kebbi State, Nigeria using routine confirmed malaria case data from January 2015 to May 2024. Model fitted to surveillance data showed that if 80% sustained LLIN usage had been maintained since 2015, approximately 5.2 million cases would have been averted (97.98% reduction). Forward projections predict that if 80% usage is achieved and sustained, about 3 million cases would be averted by May 2029 (78.93% incidence reduction).

articles.headline: PfRIPR genetic diversity in high transmission Senegal region
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the malaria vaccine candidate PfRIPR in a high transmission region of Senegal.
articles.authors: Nair M, Geering G, Agarwal A, Li R, Qiao Y, Xiao Q, Pouye MN, Thiam LG, Ba A, Hagadorn KA, Cisse A, Guerra N, Tawaldemedhen Y, Mangou K, Moore AJ, Diallo F, Sene SD, Sadio BD, Zhang E, Shapiro L, Patel SD, Mbengue A, Vigan-Womas I, Sheng Z, Bei AK
articles.details: Deep amplicon sequencing identified RH5-interacting protein (RIPR) mutations in 89 P. falciparum clinical isolates from Kédougou, Senegal. Non-synonymous SNPs were identified in 71.9% (64/89) samples, with 26 total SNPs including 15 novel ones. Of 16 SNPs modeled on crystal structures, 7 were predicted to destabilize PfRIPR and 2 to increase stability. Three SNPs (Q737K, T738K, V840L) were identified in EGF5-8 domains where neutralizing antibodies bind, relevant to RIPR vaccine development in Phase 1a clinical trials.

articles.headline: P. falciparum invasion preferences and Duffy negativity expansion
articles.title_original: Invasion preferences suggest a possible role for Plasmodium falciparum parasites in the expansion of Duffy negativity in West and Central Africa.
articles.authors: Manneh B, Introini V, Reed J, Rotariu M, Antrobus R, Cicuta P, Weekes MP, Penman BS, Rayner JC
articles.details: In vitro flow cytometry and microscopy studies investigated P. falciparum RBC invasion preferences. P. falciparum showed consistent preference for Duffy-positive RBCs; biophysical properties and surface protein expression varied between Duffy phenotypes. Evolutionary-epidemiological modeling parameterized with in vitro invasion data showed that observed P. falciparum invasion inhibition in Duffy-negative RBCs leads to FYBES allele frequency increasing at low but decreasing at high transmission levels. Findings suggest DARC may be involved in P. falciparum invasion and, together with P. vivax, may explain Duffy negativity distribution in sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: Spatial inequalities in malaria burden among Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 years in Nigeria: a subnational analysis, 2010-2019.
articles.authors: Oso TA, Okesanya OJ, Adebayo UO, Ayelaagbe OB, Ahmed MM, Obadeyi KB, Eshun G, Saliu OM, Lucero-Prisno DE 3rd
articles.details: Analysis of IHME estimates using WHO Health Equity Assessment Toolkit for malaria incidence and mortality among Nigerian children aged 0-4 years, 2010-2019. National incidence declined from 103 to 74.5 cases per 1000 (27.7% reduction); mortality fell from 477.3 to 237.6 per 100,000 (50.2% reduction). Northern states (Zamfara, Kano, Katsina) had highest baseline burden in 2010 (>150 per 1000) and remained most affected by 2019, while southern states had consistently lower burdens. Subnational inequality narrowed, with incidence coefficient of variation declining from 46.3% to 28.1%.

articles.headline: Faculty enrichment program for applied malaria modeling
articles.title_original: Building contextually-relevant training programs for scientific development: process and lessons learned in implementing two iterations of a faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling.
articles.authors: Onyango L, Ouédraogo-Ametchie G, Stahlfeld A, Holden TM, Richter R, Runge M, Toh KB, Agorinya I, Mensah B, Gerardin J
articles.details: Two cohorts participated in a 4-month faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling with components including technical skills development, communication skills, and domain knowledge on malaria epidemiology. Participants valued growth in technical expertise and research skills but faced challenges with cross-disciplinary learning and balancing program components. Program instructors adapted structures and teaching approaches based on continuous feedback to address participant needs.

articles.headline: Pooled prevalence of G6PDd in malaria patients
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency among malaria patients in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Dessie G, Getnet M, Getahun AB, Mengstie TA, Asefa T, Enyew EF, Yewodiaw TK, Endale HT, Endalew HL
articles.details: Meta-analysis of G6PDd prevalence in malaria patients using PubMed, Cochrane, and Hinari databases. Overall pooled prevalence was 6% (95% CI 0.04-0.08), with highest prevalence of 23% (95% CI 0.06-0.41) and lowest of 1% (95% CI 0.00-0.03). Majority of G6PDd cases were associated with A376G and A376T mutant variants.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence and WASH factors in Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Household characteristics, water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) and malaria prevalence among children aged 6-59 months in Ghana: an analysis of the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.authors: Klu D, Alhassan A, Vidzro ES, Aberese-Ako M
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 3,255 households with children aged 6-59 months from the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey using RDT testing. Malaria prevalence was 3.7%; ITN ownership was high at 78.9% but only 51.5% of children slept under ITNs. Multivariate analysis found anaemic children had higher odds of malaria (aOR 2.03, 95% CI 1.36-3.04), children from wealthier households had lower odds (aOR 0.15, 95% CI 0.03-0.71), and outside toilets were associated with higher malaria odds (aOR 16.64, 95% CI 2.06-134.57).

articles.headline: Provider distrust of negative malaria RDT results in DRC
articles.title_original: Positive tests are all alike, every negative test is negative in its own way: lack of confidence in negative malaria rapid diagnostic tests in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Sugg KN, Mpata FW, Humes MD, Aliango Marachto D, Rajan R, Winch PJ
articles.details: Qualitative study of 64 semi-structured interviews with staff at 16 primary health facilities in Kasai Central and Haut Katanga provinces, DRC. Healthcare providers were significantly less likely to trust negative RDT results than positive results, often explaining negative tests as false negatives due to RDT inaccuracy. Higher reported malaria cases in the national Health Management Information System compared to an automated Deki reader database, particularly during the dry season.

articles.headline: Case of post-malaria neurological syndrome and ADEM
articles.title_original: Post malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis: a case highlighting an ongoing debate.
articles.authors: Rodrigues BA, Magriço M, Krupka D, Serrazina F
articles.details: Case report highlighting the debate surrounding post-malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis.

articles.headline: Apicoplast development linked to nuclear cell cycle in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Independent nuclear and organellar mechanisms determine apicoplast fate in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Shahar M, Qasem A, Shamay E, Tissawak A, Maron Y, Florentin A
articles.details: Live-imaging analysis tracked apicoplast organelle development during P. falciparum's 48-hour intraerythrocytic life cycle, identifying four distinct morphological stages correlating with nuclear replication. A critical "Crown" morphology stage was required for nucleus-apicoplast attachment and proper organelle segregation into daughter cells. Inhibition of nuclear DNA replication blocked apicoplast biogenesis, while inhibition of apicoplast genome replication disrupted the Crown stage and organelle segregation.

articles.headline: Malaria surveillance and elimination trends in Saudi Arabia
articles.title_original: Malaria: Examining Persistence at the Margins of Endemicity over 15 Years in Saudi Arabia.
articles.authors: Alruwaili Y
articles.details: Analysis of national surveillance data spanning 2010 to 2024 reported by Saudi Arabia's Ministry of Health, examining 52,627 malaria cases. Malaria burden initially concentrated in seven endemic regions, subsequently restricted to Aseer and Jazan, with P. falciparum and P. vivax/ovale infections predominating. From 2021 onward, no indigenous transmission was recorded; most infections were classified as imported. ARIMA models forecast stable national and regional malaria trends through 2030.

articles.headline: Airport malaria cluster with imported vectors in Libya
articles.title_original: Airport Malaria Cluster in Certified Malaria-Free Country, Libya, 2024.
articles.authors: Alarbi AM, Elhaddad AB, Almehdawi NM, Saadawi WK, Aqeehal H, Elalem M
articles.details: Autochthonous cluster of 4 Plasmodium falciparum cases with 1 fatal outcome identified near Benina International Airport in Benghazi, Libya. Epidemiologic and entomologic investigation ruled out local transmission, indicating airport malaria caused by imported vectors.

articles.headline: Spatial-temporal vector habitat and biting probability modeling
articles.title_original: Spatial-temporal coupling of malaria vector habitat suitability and biting probability.
articles.authors: Aduvukha GR, Abdel-Rahman EM, Mutanga O, Odindi J, Tonnang HEZ
articles.details: Species distribution modeling and fuzzy logic rule-based techniques integrated remotely sensed data, climate, topography, and Anopheles vector presence data to model vector habitat suitability and biting probability from 2000 to 2018. Two model scenarios were evaluated; validation with 25 An. gambiae complex biting observations from 2017-2021 yielded mean accuracy of 91%. Models showed reduced biting probability with increased bed net usage and correlated with reduced P. falciparum prevalence due to increased intervention measures.

articles.headline: Malaria and diabetes co-morbidity mechanisms in Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Diabetes Mellitus and Malaria Co-Existence as Deadly Burden in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Review of Clinical Outcomes and Molecular Interactions.
articles.authors: Erhabor OG, Abdullahi AD, Sani A, Gabdo MB, Saad RS, Abubakar M, Isah MB, Simelane MBC, Umar IA, Ibrahim MA
articles.details: Review of clinical studies and molecular interactions underlying diabetes mellitus and malaria co-morbidity in malaria-endemic areas. Malaria-related inflammation, oxidative stress, and immune activation disrupt insulin signaling and beta-cell function, resulting in increased hyperglycemia in diabetics. Conversely, chronic hyperglycemia damages the endothelium, increases oxidative stress, and impairs immunity, facilitating parasite survival. TNF-α and insulin signaling pathways identified as pivotal links orchestrating inflammatory responses contributing to both disease pathogenesis and metabolic dysregulation.

articles.headline: Benzoxaborole compounds inhibit P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase
articles.title_original: Benzoxaboroles Are Inhibitors of the η-Class Carbonic Anhydrase From Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Bonardi A, Wein S, Mustafa M, Cerdan R, de Luca V, Capasso C, Gratteri P, Supuran CT, Nocentini A, Winum JY
articles.details: In vitro evaluation of benzoxaborole compounds 13, 14, and 7 tested for Plasmodium falciparum carbonic anhydrase (PfaCA) inhibition and antiplasmodial activity against P. falciparum 3D7 strain. Compounds 13 and 14 were the most potent PfaCA inhibitors (KI = 0.45 and 0.43 µM, respectively), while compound 7 (ethyl ester) demonstrated the strongest antiplasmodial activity (IC50 = 2.5 µM). Molecular docking studies supported favorable binding of active derivatives within the PfaCA active site.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Karen State, Myanmar during COVID-19 and civil conflict
articles.title_original: Re-emergence of malaria in Karen State, Myanmar: has the battle of Burma been lost?
articles.authors: Pateekhum C, Thu AM, Lwin KM, Nyaw SB, Tun SW, Daniel S, Khin A, Aung WL, Kain K, Imwong M, Day NP, White NJ, Nosten FH
articles.details: Observational study analyzed malariometric and genomic data from eastern Karen State, Myanmar, comparing 2020 and 2024 from clinics serving an estimated 350,000 population. P. falciparum cases increased 12-fold and P. vivax cases increased 3-fold, with greatest resurgence in northern Karen State coinciding with reduced access to diagnosis and treatment. Transmission risk ratios increased for P. falciparum (RR = 1.72, 95% CI 1.68-1.76) and P. vivax (RR = 1.82, 95% CI 1.80-1.84), with service disruption from COVID-19 pandemic and insecurity contributing to the resurgence.

articles.headline: Malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria.
articles.details: No abstract provided; based on title only.

articles.headline: Sporozoite vaccine efficacy declines due to parasite diversity and host context
articles.title_original: The endemic dilemma: why whole sporozoite malaria vaccine efficacy falls in the field.
articles.authors: Hassert M, Harty JT
articles.details: Review examining why whole sporozoite vaccines (WSVs) show reduced efficacy in endemic field settings compared to controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) studies. Proposes antigen mismatch hypothesis: limited genetic diversity in vaccines fails to capture field parasite diversity, reducing immune recognition. Proposes host-context hypothesis: genetics, infection history, microbiome, and nutrition influence vaccine outcomes. Authors suggest future vaccine development requires integration of parasite genomics and systems immunology with animal models reflecting endemic conditions.

articles.headline: Mixed Plasmodium ovale and falciparum co-infections show differential clinical profiles
articles.title_original: Comparison of routine clinical profiles of patients with imported P. falciparum malaria and co-infection with Plasmodium ovale wallikeri or Plasmodium ovale curtisi.
articles.authors: Balsa-Vázquez J, Rubio-Muñoz JM, Duvignaud A, Alcedo S, de Ayala AP, Salas-Coronas J, Elía-López M, Cattaneo P, Arévalo-Cañas C, García-Bujalance S, Ruiz-Giardín JM, Norman FF, Rojo-Marcos G
articles.details: Retrospective multicentre cohort study comparing 28 P. ovale wallikeri/P. falciparum and 11 P. ovale curtisi/P. falciparum co-infections with 117 P. falciparum mono-infections. P. ovale wallikeri co-infections showed higher total leukocyte counts (median 6.25 vs 4.85 × 10⁹/L; p=0.010) and lower parasitemia (median 0.6% vs 1.0%; p=0.025). P. ovale curtisi co-infections had lower ALT (18.0 vs 27.3 IU/L; p=0.022) and more frequent thrombocytopenia (100% vs 51%; p=0.003). Findings suggest species-specific interactions in mixed infections.

articles.headline: Males show delayed blood-stage parasitaemia onset in controlled malaria infection
articles.title_original: Sex-based differences in Plasmodium infection in the control groups of controlled human malaria infection trials in malaria-naive populations in the USA and the Netherlands: a pooled analysis.
articles.authors: Duncombe CJ, Hergott DEB, Staubus W, Balke-Buijs M, Kublin JG, Duffy PE, Healy SA, Talley A, Jackson L, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Sauerwein RW, Roestenberg M, Murphy SC
articles.details: Pooled analysis of 102 malaria-naive participants (48 female, 54 male) across 13 CHMI studies in USA and Netherlands. Mean time to first detectable parasitaemia was longer in males (7.59 days) versus females (7.17 days). Adjusted analysis showed time to positivity occurred 8% later in males (time ratio 1.08 [1.03–1.16]). Males significantly more likely to have detectable infection after day 7 (35% vs 13%), with adjusted odds 5.20 times higher. Suggests sex-specific host-pathogen interactions in liver-stage dynamics.

articles.headline: Thermal profiling reveals dynamic protein interactions in Plasmodium falciparum
articles.title_original: MAP-X unveils the shapeshifting interactome of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li Q, Chen Q
articles.details: Researchers introduced MAP-X (meltome-assisted profiling of protein complexes), a thermal profiling method combined with machine learning, to map protein complex dynamics in intact P. falciparum parasites. In situ approach reveals highly dynamic interactome, identifying novel complexes and proteins with multiple functions ('moonlighting' proteins). Shifts understanding from static protein lists to temporal networks of parasite protein interactions.

articles.headline: Commercial qPCR assay shows superior sensitivity for Plasmodium vivax diagnosis
articles.title_original: Promising diagnostic performance of a commercial qPCR assay for Plasmodium vivax detection: Contributing to malaria control and elimination.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Sampaio MP, Almeida MNG, A Silva A, Montoia G, Storti-Melo LM, Baptista ARS, Di Santi SM, Machado RLD
articles.details: Study evaluated commercial qPCR assay for P. vivax detection using 167 samples. Assay demonstrated greater sensitivity than microscopy, detecting three additional positive cases. Showed high specificity with no cross-reactivity. Results support qPCR as reliable tool for malaria diagnosis and surveillance.

articles.headline: PCR detects more malaria cases than microscopy with clinical biomarker correlations
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic and host biomarker assessment of plasmodium infections in malaria-endemic Peshawar, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Khan MA, Fayaz S, Saeed M, Murad R, Murad A, Khan RU, Naz S, Perillo A, Alhidary IA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 2500 suspected patients in District Peshawar, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa during peak malaria transmission months, comparing microscopy and PCR. PCR detected significantly more cases than microscopy (p=0.001), with microscopy showing moderate sensitivity (κ=0.692) and perfect specificity (AUC=0.823). Malaria-positive individuals showed hematological abnormalities with reduced hemoglobin and altered leukocytes, elevated liver enzymes, and parasite density correlating with hemoglobin and platelet counts.

articles.headline: Beta-thalassemia and the missing malaria landscape
articles.title_original: Beta-thalassemia and a missing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Elmakhzen B, El Makhzen N, Abriel H
articles.details: No abstract provided; based on title only.

articles.headline: Wolbachia-based malaria control: mechanisms and field application pathway
articles.title_original: Wolbachia for malaria control.
articles.authors: Zhang X, Dimopoulos G
articles.details: Review of Wolbachia-based approaches for malaria vector control. Discusses experimental systems for Wolbachia-Anopheles interactions and challenges in generating stable Wolbachia transinfected Anopheles gambiae strains. Studies show Wolbachia-induced Plasmodium suppression is feasible in both transiently and stably infected Anopheles. Reviews mechanisms of Plasmodium suppression based on insights from Wolbachia-Aedes-arborvirus systems and outlines challenges and opportunities for translating findings to field application.

articles.headline: Cool roofs and screening reduce indoor malaria vectors and heat
articles.title_original: Housing modifications for heat adaptation, thermal comfort and malaria vector control in rural African settlements.
articles.authors: Abong'o B, Kwaro D, Bange T, Moshi V, Simwero J, Otima J, Mendt S, Ochomo E, Maggioni MA
articles.details: Randomized pilot field study with 40 houses allocated to four arms: cool-roof, cross-ventilation, mat-ceiling, or control. Cool-roof houses had lowest indoor Heat Index (daytime -3.3°C, p<0.001; nighttime -2.4°C, p<0.01). Screening of doors, windows, and eaves reduced female Anopheles funestus mosquitoes by 77% and Culex mosquitoes by 58% compared to control. Eighty-five percent of households expressed willingness to invest in housing interventions.

articles.headline: MSRP1 protein marks sexually committed parasites with distinct proteome signatures
articles.title_original: Defining the Proteome of Sexually Committed Parasites in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Venugopal K, Achcar F, Wolski WE, Nanni P, Lemgruber Soares L, Wright GJ, Marti M
articles.details: Study identified MSRP1 as highly specific marker for sexual commitment in P. falciparum gametocyte development. Researchers generated and validated FACS-based isolation of sexually versus asexually committed parasites, then analyzed their proteomes. Proteomics revealed distinct protein signatures between populations, including merozoite surface proteins, indicating functional differences. Study provides methodology for systematic characterization of parasites at this critical life-cycle stage.

articles.headline: Delayed hemolytic anemia after artemether-lumefantrine in immunosuppressed patient
articles.title_original: Delayed hemolytic anemia after artemether-lumefantrine in Plasmodium falciparum malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Usta O, Güllü D, Alhan Ö, Ergönül Ö
articles.details: Case report of 50-year-old male with severe P. falciparum malaria (parasitemia >10%) developing delayed hemolytic anemia (DHA) eight days after extended oral artemether-lumefantrine treatment. Patient had underlying immunosuppression (rituximab therapy 10 months prior) and hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory findings showed reticulocytosis and positive direct Coombs test, suggesting immune-mediated component. Case highlights DHA can occur after oral artemether-lumefantrine and underscores need for post-treatment hemoglobin monitoring (2–4 weeks) in high-risk patients.

articles.headline: TIRAP rs8177374 variant protects against severe malaria
articles.title_original: Immunomodulatory effects of the TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 (S180L) variant: Meta-Analytic evidence from severe and Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: González-Castillo NI, Prado-Rodríguez SG, Iñiguez-Mosqueda O, Íñiguez-Gutiérrez L, Silva-Jara JM, Díaz-Zaragoza M, Graciano-Machuca O
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 10 studies (4,103 cases, 2,460 controls) found T allele protective against severe malaria (OR=0.51, 95% CI 0.40–0.65, p<0.001) but associated with increased P. falciparum infection risk (OR=1.47, 95% CI 1.09–1.98, p=0.012). Results suggest TIRAP/MAL has a modulatory role in malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance widespread in Tanzanian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Insecticide Resistance Management in Malaria Vectors in Tanzania Mainland (2015-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Nkya TE
articles.details: Scoping review of 10 studies (2015–2024) found pyrethroid resistance driven by kdr mutations and metabolic mechanisms. DDT resistance common; carbamate resistance in approximately 50% of studies. Organophosphates (pirimiphos-methyl) remained effective; new insecticides (clothianidin, chlorfenapyr) showed high efficacy without detected resistance.

articles.headline: Immune markers differentiate bacterial from malaria fever
articles.title_original: Profiling of circulating immune factors and host-derived extracellular vesicles in response to bacterial or malaria acute fever.
articles.authors: Tiberti N, Bertoli G, Mazzi C, Vezzelli E, Zanzoni S, Alladio F, Bisoffi Z, Tamarozzi F, Piubelli C, Buonfrate D, Gobbi FG
articles.details: Retrospective study of 52 bacterial and 73 malaria febrile patients. Bacterial infection showed elevated endothelial activation markers (CD29+, CD62P+, CD9+ EVs) and MMP-2/9. Malaria showed elevated antigen recognition and cytokine storm markers (CD8+, CD81+, HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs, IFNγ, CXCL10, TNFα). HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs distinguished infections (AUC 0.87).

articles.headline: Fat and vitamin A reduce malaria in young children
articles.title_original: Associations Between Climate-Sensitive Nutrients, Clinical Malaria, and Anaemia Among Young Children in Rural Burkina Faso: An Analysis of Baseline Data From a Cluster-Randomised Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Kurniawan AL, Borga MV, Zerbo F, Abidha CA, Gonnet A, Sorgho R, Coulibaly B, Sié A, Danquah I
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 452 children (median 17 months) in Burkina Faso showed 25.0% with clinical malaria, 88.5% with anemia. Fat and vitamin A dietary pattern inversely associated with clinical malaria (OR quintile 3 vs 1: 0.50, 95% CI 0.26–0.96) but not anemia. Fiber and micronutrient pattern showed no associations.

articles.headline: Mixed P. falciparum and P. vivax infections detected by PCR
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic approach for mixed Plasmodium infection among acute febrile patients: rare incidence from Eastern Uttar Pradesh.
articles.authors: Bhardwaj P, Agrawal A, Tiwari S, Kumar B, Kumar N, Singh R, Joshi HS, Dwivedi GR
articles.details: Study of acute febrile patients in Eastern Uttar Pradesh identified 4 RDT-positive cases. PCR revealed 3 of 4 had mixed P. falciparum and P. vivax infections; 1 had P. falciparum alone. Mixed infections associated with life-threatening complications including concurrent varicella-zoster, Japanese encephalitis, and scrub typhus.

articles.headline: Malaria and typhoid co-infection prevalent with antibiotic resistance
articles.title_original: Malaria and salmonella typhi co- infection and antibiotic susceptibility in febrile patients at the Buea Regional Hospital.
articles.authors: Ebini-Ako TA, Tanih NF, Djakissam W
articles.details: Cross-sectional hospital study of 152 febrile patients at Buea Regional Hospital (March–May 2024) found malaria prevalence 52.0% (79/152) and typhoid 37.5% (57/152). Among 112 confirmed cases, 22.3% (25) had both infections. Vomiting associated with typhoid (p=0.008). All S. typhi isolates resistant to sulfamethoxazole.

articles.headline: TACT pharmacokinetics show no drug-drug interaction
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of artemether-lumefantrine plus amodiaquine in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Ding J, Hoglund RM, van der Pluijm RW, Callery JJ, Peto TJ, Tripura R, Das S, Châu NH, Promnarate C, Mukaka M, Dysoley L, Fanello C, Onyamboko MA, Anvikar AR, Mayxay M, Smithuis F, von Seidlein L, Dhorda M, Amaratunga C, Faiz MA, Nghia HDT, White NJ, Day NPJ, Dondorp AM, Tarning J
articles.details: Population PK analysis from 2 RCTs (2015–2020) in Africa and Southeast Asia: 443 receiving artemether-lumefantrine alone, 442 with amodiaquine. Characterized Cmax and AUC for artemether, dihydroartemisinin, lumefantrine, amodiaquine, and metabolites. Geometric mean ratios for DDI effects fell within 0.80–1.25 range, indicating minimal clinical DDI.

articles.headline: Historical study of malaria fever therapy in psychiatry
articles.title_original: In the shadow of the "psychopharmacological revolution": Malaria fever, insulin coma, cardiazol and electroconvulsive therapy at the Vienna Psychiatric University Clinic, 1951-1969.
articles.authors: Heiss G
articles.details: 2-year research on therapeutic practices at Vienna Psychiatric University Clinic (1951–1969) analyzed 14,919 patient records. Examined application of 4 invasive somatic treatments: electroconvulsive therapy, cardiazol-induced convulsion, insulin coma, and malaria fever therapy. Study analyzed professional publications from Austria, Switzerland, Germany, France, and UK during transition to pharmacological treatments.

articles.headline: MAM01 monoclonal antibody well-tolerated in phase 1 trial
articles.title_original: Human monoclonal antibody MAM01 for protection against malaria in adults in the USA: a first-in-human, phase 1, dose-escalation, double-blind, placebo-controlled, adaptive trial.
articles.authors: Lyke KE, Berry AA, Laurens MB, Winkler J, Joshi S, Koudjra AR, Butler L, Billingsley PF, Pascini T, Patil A, Sim BKL, Fitzgerald G, Riegel J, Andrews K, Levi M, Anderson AB, Wells CD, Liu H, Huleatt J, Miller RS
articles.details: Phase 1 dose-escalation trial at University of Maryland (Aug 2023–Dec 2024) with 37 participants assigned to MAM01 or placebo. Well tolerated with no treatment-related serious adverse events. After controlled human malaria infection with P. falciparum NF54, 6 of 6 control and 18 of 22 MAM01 participants developed parasitemia.

articles.headline: Commentary on monoclonal antibodies for malaria
articles.title_original: A new monoclonal in the arMAMentarium against malaria.
articles.authors: Lorenz FR, McCall MBB
articles.details: Editorial commentary on monoclonal antibody approaches to malaria treatment and prevention.

articles.headline: Postmalaria labyrinthitis ossificans: rare complication
articles.title_original: Postmalaria Labyrinthitis Ossificans: A Rare Complication of Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Batista FS, Couto R, Pinto D, Matias AM, Neves L, Castelão F
articles.details: Case report describing labyrinthitis ossificans as a rare complication of Plasmodium infection.

articles.headline: Rational vaccine design targets erythrocyte invasion
articles.title_original: Erythrocyte invasion in malaria: from molecular mechanisms to rational vaccines.
articles.authors: Alam N, Farrell B, Jamwal A, Higgins MK
articles.details: Review of erythrocyte invasion mechanisms in malaria and vaccine development. Discusses temporally organized molecular processes including PCRCR complex membrane bridging. Structural studies of human antibodies from vaccination or natural infection revealed neutralizing epitopes and antibody potentiation mechanisms. First rationally designed blood-stage malaria vaccine immunogen now entering clinical trials.

articles.headline: Congenital malaria prevalence in Nigeria: systematic review and meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Congenital malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Odeniran PO, Paul-Odeniran KF, Ademola IO
articles.details: Systematic review of 35 studies (9,503 neonates, 2000–2024) using PRISMA. Pooled prevalence of congenital malaria 12.7% (95% CI 7.3–19.2). Maternal parasitaemia 25.5%, placental 20.3%, baby peripheral 12.9%, cord blood 11.1%. Average parasite count 56.6/μL. Neonates with both malaria and septicaemia 4.8% (3.4–6.7%).

articles.headline: Plasmodium sporozoite gliding motility modulated by surface adhesin TLP
articles.title_original: Traction force generation in Plasmodium sporozoites is modulated by a surface adhesin.
articles.authors: Ripp J, Probst D, Singer M, Schwarz US, Frischknecht F
articles.details: Traction force microscopy study of motile sporozoites. Both wild-type and tlp(−) sporozoites show distinct foci of force generation, but tlp(−) sporozoites generated lower forces overall. TLP is an important element of the force-generating machinery during sporozoite gliding motility.

articles.headline: Long-term renal and neurological sequelae after severe imported falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Term Sequelae After Severe Malaria: A Retrospective Study.
articles.authors: Cardona F, Héritier L, Cortaredona S, L'Ollivier C
articles.details: Retrospective cohort of 57 adults with severe P. falciparum malaria, all receiving intravenous artesunate. At day 28, 6/41 (14.6%) had sequelae (4 renal, 1 neurological, 1 both). At remote follow-up, 6/33 (18.2%) had sequelae (2 renal, 3 neurological, 1 both). At one year, 8/14 had persistent symptoms (fatigue, cognitive complaints). Sequelae associated with neurological failure, severe metabolic acidosis, and baseline renal impairment.

articles.headline: Malaria-associated acute kidney injury: case series of imported P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury in a Low-Endemic Region: A Case Series of Five Imported Plasmodium falciparum Infections.
