articles.title_original: From song to stories: assessing the impact of exposure to arts-based community engagement tools in shaping knowledge, attitudes, and acceptability toward the Sterile Insect Technique for malaria control in South Africa.
articles.authors: Manana PN, Jewett S, Munhenga G
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 614 participants in South Africa assessed impact of arts-based community engagement on knowledge and acceptance of the Sterile Insect Technique for malaria control. Among 26.2% exposed to engagement tools, participants were more likely to correctly identify female mosquito blood-feeding (95.0% vs 85.8%, p=0.008) and support SIT releases (98.1% vs 89.4%, p=0.003).

articles.headline: Automated XN-31 analyzer achieves 98.4% sensitivity for vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Performance evaluation of the automated haematology analyzer XN-31 for malaria diagnosis in Plasmodium vivax-dominant regions of Thailand.
articles.authors: Kawaguchi M, Komaki-Yasuda K, Nakatsu M, Kerdsakundee N, Hamana M, Tougan T, Kamei M, Konishi A, Krudsood S, Kano S
articles.details: From November 2019 to June 2022, 349 suspected malaria patients at Hospital for Tropical Medicine were analyzed with XN-31 automated haematology analyzer, microscopy, RDTs, and PCR. Among 125 microscopy-positive samples (103 P. vivax, 17 P. falciparum, 3 P. knowlesi, 1 P. ovale, 1 P. malariae), XN-31 achieved 98.4% sensitivity versus 88.0% for RDT. All XN-31-positive samples were confirmed by PCR with 100% accuracy in species identification. XN-31 MI-RBC% values correlated strongly with microscopy parasitemia (r=0.903).

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax shows reduced diversity in Duffy-negative Sudanese hosts
articles.title_original: Highly conserved Plasmodium vivax genomes in Duffy-negative individuals from Sudan.
articles.authors: Schroeder RE, Ahmed S, Ford A, Elfaki M, Hamad SO, Elfaki TM, Mohamed S, Manko E, Clark TG, Campino S, Hamid MMA, Lo E
articles.details: Genome sequencing of P. vivax from Duffy-positive and Duffy-negative Sudanese individuals revealed Duffy-positive individuals had significantly higher parasitemia and much greater parasite genome diversity across all 14 chromosomes and 44 erythrocyte binding gene candidates. Merozoite surface protein family genes accounted for much of the genetic diversity. Many erythrocyte binding gene candidates showed both positive and negative selection pressure. Amino acid changes in DBP, RBP genes, and TRAg38 binding regions could affect erythrocyte binding affinity and antigenic conformation.

articles.headline: Correction to machine learning gametocyte imaging study
articles.title_original: Correction: Machine learning-based phenotypic imaging to characterise the targetable biology of Plasmodium falciparum male gametocytes for the development of transmission-blocking antimalarials.
articles.authors: Tsebriy O, Khomiak A, Miguel-Blanco C, Sparkes PC, Gioli M, Santelli M, Whitley E, Gamo FJ, Delves MJ
articles.details: This is a correction notice to a previously published article (DOI: 10.1371/journal.ppat.1011711).

articles.headline: Selection-linked integration enables PfEMP1 functional studies and endotyping
articles.title_original: A system for functional studies of the major virulence factor of malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Cronshagen J, Allweier J, Mesén-Ramírez JP, Stäcker J, Vaaben AV, Ramón-Zamorano G, Naranjo-Prado I, Graser M, López-Barona P, Ofori S, Jansen PWTC, Hornebeck J, Kieferle F, Murk A, Martin E, Castro-Peña C, Bártfai R, Lavstsen T, Bruchhaus I, Spielmann T
articles.details: Researchers developed selection-linked integration (SLI and SLI2) systems to generate P. falciparum parasites expressing uniform PfEMP1 variants and allow genome editing at the expressed locus. System was successfully transferred from 3D7 to IT4 strain, producing parasites with effective receptor-binding capacity. Using these systems, researchers studied PfEMP1 trafficking, generated cell lines binding common endothelial receptors, surveyed protein environment surrounding functional PfEMP1, and identified new proteins required for PfEMP1-mediated sequestration.

articles.headline: Drug-induced hepatitis with cholestasis following antimalarial treatment
articles.title_original: Deciphering Hepatic Dilemmas: A Case of Acute Hepatitis Following Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Lovrien S, Quist E, Maita Coronel D, Singal AK, Xie C
articles.details: Case report of a 55-year-old African woman treated for P. falciparum malaria in Cameroon who presented with jaundice and transaminitis. Liver biopsy showed cholestatic hepatitis. Diagnosis was complicated by only 1 of 4 follow-up blood smears positive for P. falciparum. After more than 17 months of follow-up, liver enzymes and bilirubin returned to baseline without further antimalarial treatment. Findings were attributed to drug-induced liver injury from antimalarial medications.

articles.headline: Novel mini-dbPCR-NALFIA achieves 96.5% sensitivity for P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of the miniature direct-on-blood PCR nucleic acid lateral flow immunoassay (mini-dbPCR-NALFIA) for the detection of Plasmodium falciparum in high seasonal malaria transmission setting in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Kiemde F, Rouamba T, Some DY, Kabore B, Valia D, Natama HM, Bere AW, Mens PF, Tinto H, Schallig HDFH
articles.details: Phase 3 diagnostic accuracy study conducted over one year in Burkina Faso screened 438 febrile patients with PfHRP2-based RDT, microscopy, and investigational dbPCR-NALFIA test, using qPCR as reference. Malaria prevalence by qPCR was 65.5% (287/438). dbPCR-NALFIA demonstrated 96.5% sensitivity and 98.0% specificity, outperforming RDT specificity (80.8%) and microscopy sensitivity (90.2%). dbPCR-NALFIA detected 67.9% of submicroscopic qPCR-positive samples missed by microscopy. Agreement with qPCR was highest for dbPCR-NALFIA (Cohen's kappa=0.90) compared to microscopy (kappa=0.80) and RDT (kappa=0.71).

articles.headline: Good malaria knowledge but low risk perception among gold miners
articles.title_original: An assessment of knowledge, practices, and malaria parasitaemia among artisanal gold miners and community members in Mudzi, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari MJ
articles.details: Mixed-methods cross-sectional study in Mudzi, Zimbabwe recruited 128 artisanal gold miners (AGMs) and 113 community members (CMs). Responses showed good knowledge of malaria cause and transmission but revealed low risk perception and misconceptions in both groups. No significant differences in education, knowledge, and perceptions between AGMs and CMs. AGMs predominantly lived in open without protective measures. Malaria prevalence across groups was less than 1%. Authors conclude high knowledge is not translating to preventive behavior; innovative health education needed to increase risk perception.

articles.headline: PDGFRA inhibitor GSK190937 kills malaria parasites via hemozoin
articles.title_original: Repositioning a Human Kinase Platelet-Derived Growth Factor Receptor Alpha Type II Inhibitor for Malaria and Inhibition of Hemozoin Formation.
articles.authors: Mmonwa MM, Audu O, Maepa K, Dziwornu GA, Govender P, Tshaka L, Burrows J, Taylor D, Masike K, Njoroge M, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Chibale K
articles.details: Type II PDGFRA human kinase inhibitor GSK190937 from the Kinase Chemogenomics Set demonstrated antiplasmodium activity against asexual blood stage parasites (PfNF54 IC50=0.22 μM). Medicinal chemistry optimization generated compounds 20, 23, and 29 with improved microsomal metabolic stability and asexual blood stage antiplasmodium activity. Mechanism of action studies showed this series inhibits hemozoin formation, killing late-stage trophozoites.

articles.headline: Helminth coinfection does not drive malaria parasite virulence evolution
articles.title_original: Experimental evolution of Plasmodium yoelii in single and helminth-coinfected mice.
articles.authors: Dusuel A, Bourbon L, Groetz E, Rialland M, Roche B, Faivre B, Sorci G
articles.details: Experimental evolution study serially passaged P. yoelii lines in single-infected (SI) hosts or hosts previously infected with Heligmosomoides polygyrus nematode (COI-lines). After 5 and 7 passages, serial passages increased parasitemia and virulence due to competitive advantage of fast-replicating genotypes, but SI-lines and COI-lines showed relatively similar replication rates and virulence. When COI-lines were tested in single infection (mismatched environment), they had slower early replication compared to matched-environment trials. Authors found limited evidence for virulence divergence between SI and COI-lines.

articles.headline: P. vivax dominates imported Iranian malaria; P. falciparum drives severity
articles.title_original: Epidemiological and clinical profile of malaria patients in Isfahan Province, Iran: a retrospective analysis from 2009 to 2025.
articles.authors: Lesani S, Boozhmehrani MJ, Golban M, Ghayour Najafabadi Z
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 569 microscopically confirmed malaria cases in Isfahan Province from January 2009 to January 2025. Incidence declined sharply after 2009 but fluctuated subsequently with resurgence in 2024. Cases were 96.1% male, 53.6% aged 15-24 years, with 80.8% Afghan nationals; imported cases represented 80.8% of burden. P. vivax accounted for 88.0% of cases while P. falciparum (4.6%) was strongly associated with severe disease (adjusted odds ratio 22.6; 95% CI 1.24-410.8; p=0.035). Seasonal peaks occurred in spring and summer.

articles.headline: Water, sanitation access strongly predict lower malaria incidence
articles.title_original: Investigating the relationship between malaria incidence and public health infrastructure in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Shin J
articles.details: Country-level analysis using Kruskal-Wallis test, Dunn's post hoc comparisons, and machine learning models assessed relationships between malaria incidence and access to clean water, sanitation, and vitamin A supplementation. Significant differences in malaria incidence were found across water and sanitation access groups, with lowest access consistently exhibiting highest incidence. Cliff's delta indicated large effect sizes. Tree-based machine learning models showed moderate predictive performance, outperforming logistic regression. Authors conclude clean water and adequate sanitation essential for malaria control.

articles.headline: Insect-specific viruses do not suppress Plasmodium sporozoite development
articles.title_original: Systemic infection with insect-specific viruses does not affect Plasmodium sporozoite formation in Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Schinkel M, Overheul GJ, Ploeger E, van Gemert GJ, Junglen S, Miesen P, Bousema T, van Rij RP
articles.details: Researchers tested a panel of 15 insect-specific viruses for effects on P. falciparum development in Anopheles mosquitoes. Six viruses (Flock House, Wallerfield, Agua Salud alphavirus, Herbert herbevirus, Gouléako goukovirus, and invertebrate iridescent virus 6) efficiently replicated in Anopheles gambiae cell lines. In vivo coinfections of P. falciparum with four efficiently replicating viruses (excluding high-mortality Flock House and poor-replicating Wallerfield) showed sporozoite formation and Plasmodium infection rates did not differ between virus-infected mosquitoes and non-infected controls.

articles.headline: Framework for assessing malaria surveillance capacity post-elimination
articles.title_original: Building a tool to assess malaria surveillance and response capacity in malaria post-elimination contexts: a modified and dual-weighted Delphi approach.
articles.authors: Lu G, Xu E, Cao Y, Chai L, Liao Z, Wang J, Sha T, Wang Y, Müller O, Zhao J, Zhu G, Cao J
articles.details: Study developed a standardized framework through systematic reviews, expert panels, and a two-round Delphi process involving 30 experts. The framework comprises 34 indicators across six domains weighted using analytic hierarchy process and entropy methods. The proportion of confirmed cases with completed epidemiological investigations and submitted reports had the highest indicator weight (0.1153), followed by interval between first medical visit and diagnosis (0.1131). The framework aims to help countries sustain elimination and support eradication efforts.

articles.headline: P. falciparum cytochrome c-2 exhibits anomalously low redox potential
articles.title_original: A Divergent Cytochrome c in Malaria Parasites with an Anomalously Low Redox Potential.
articles.authors: Loveridge KM, Olsen OD, Scherer SR, Espino-Sanchez TJ, Wienkers H, Hill CP, Kay MS, Sigala PA
articles.details: Researchers identified an unrecognized lineage of cytochrome c homologs in Plasmodium falciparum. Pfcyt c-2 has a pentacoordinate heme and redox potential of −278 mV, over 500 mV lower than canonical cytochrome c (+250 mV) and lower than any known natural monoheme c-type cytochrome. Despite this anomalous redox potential, Pfcyt c-2 displayed canonical thermostability and low-level peroxidase activity, revealing a new clade of eukaryotic cytochrome c variants with divergent biochemical properties.

articles.headline: Malaria rapid diagnostic test shows 99% agreement with microscopy
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and diagnostic performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests compared to microscopy among febrile patients at Pawe General Hospital, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Legesse F, Berhane N
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 422 febrile patients found overall malaria prevalence of 27.0% by microscopy, with P. falciparum (84.2%), P. vivax (7.9%), and mixed infections (7.9%). The Bioline™ mRDT showed 99.1% sensitivity, 99.3% specificity, 98.3% positive predictive value, 99.7% negative predictive value, and near-perfect agreement with microscopy (κ=0.98). Male sex (aOR 1.88, 95% CI 1.16–3.04) and household size ≥5 members (aOR 2.19, 95% CI 1.36–3.54) were independent risk factors for malaria infection.

articles.headline: Bradford method for R21 malaria vaccine protein quantification validated
articles.title_original: Systematic Development and Validation of a Bradford-Based Protein Quantification Method for Novel Multi-Dose R21 Malaria Vaccine Formulated with 2-Phenoxy Ethanol (2-PE).
articles.authors: Jena R, Ranade D, Chaudhari P, Salunke A, Mahamuni A, Gairola S
articles.details: Study evaluated protein quantification methods (Micro-BCA, BCA, Bradford assays) for the R21 malaria vaccine formulated with Matrix-M1 adjuvant and 2-phenoxy ethanol. The Bradford assay was selected and validated per ICH Q2(R2) guidelines, achieving a limit of quantitation of 2 µg/mL with comparable performance to ELISA. The method demonstrated sensitivity to detect antigen adsorption to vial surfaces and showed robust in vivo immunogenicity in release and stability studies, providing a rapid, high-throughput solution for quality control.

articles.headline: Malaria incidence drops from 23.8 to 0.7 per 1000 in Cambodia
articles.title_original: Assessing progress and challenges towards malaria elimination in Kampong Speu, Cambodia: analysis of Plasmodium vivax and mixed infections, 2019-2023.
articles.authors: Ly K, Ung S, Roces MC, Lek D, Ly P
articles.details: Epidemiological analysis of malaria cases and radical cure treatment outcomes over a five-year period using national surveillance data found annual parasite incidence declined from 23.8 per 1000 at-risk individuals to 0.7 per 1000, while radical cure treatment completion rates improved from 78% to 98%. Males aged 15–49 years, particularly forest-goers, remained the highest-risk group. Study recommends enhanced prevention, increased screening, and ensuring 100% radical cure treatment for eligible cases in high-risk populations.

articles.headline: RDT procedural errors impair performance at low parasitaemia levels
articles.title_original: The effects of introduced procedural errors on malaria rapid diagnostic test performance in a laboratory setting.
articles.authors: Wilson S, Ah Y, Aidoo M
articles.details: Study tested six RDT products against cultured P. falciparum at various parasitaemia levels for compliance with manufacturer instructions. Increasing sample volume beyond 5 µL impaired performance, causing 83.3% invalid results in 4 of 6 products. The shortest incubation period (5 min) led to most deviations from baseline; longer periods showed better agreement. Insufficient buffer volumes caused invalid outcomes in >50% of products. Higher parasitaemia (1,000/µL) showed best tolerance to errors, with three products achieving 100% agreement with baseline regardless of buffer or incubation variations.

articles.headline: Housing modification costs $50–116 per house; equity gaps in willingness
articles.title_original: Housing modification to prevent malaria in Uganda: an analysis of costs, willingness to pay, and equity.
articles.authors: Snyman K, Ochieng W, Gonahasa S, Aber J, Katureebe A, Staedke SG, Kamya MR, Westercamp N, Pitt C
articles.details: Economic analysis alongside a cluster-randomized trial of approximately 4,000 homes evaluated screening and eave tube interventions. Screening cost $116 per house ($4.22/person annualized); eave tubes cost $50 ($3.03 annualized). Installation costs were higher in wealthiest vs poorest quintiles (screening: $151 vs $69; eave tubes: $95 vs $31). While >75% willing to pay something, values represented only a fraction of costs, with larger gaps between wealthy and poor households (screening 12% vs 7% willingness-to-pay; eave tubes 18% vs 14%).

articles.headline: Low P. falciparum prevalence but high drug-resistance mutations in PLWH
articles.title_original: Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum infections among people living with HIV under antiretroviral and co-trimoxazole chemoprophylaxis in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Djontu JC, Baina MT, Ntabi JDM, Mapanguy CCM, Batchi-Bouyou AL, Elenga VA, Kouikani FY, Ndala ACM, Mouanga AM, Ntoumi F
articles.details: Health facility-based cross-sectional study of 683 people living with HIV found 1.2% malaria infection by microscopy and 9.2% submicroscopic infections by PCR. P. falciparum was dominant (98.6%) with mean multiplicity of infection 2.1 and 68.1% polyclonal. High prevalences of drug-resistance mutations were detected: Pfdhfr 51I (78.2%), 59R (84.2%), 108N (84.0%); Pfdhps 437G (72.0%); Pfcrt 76T (46.9%); Pfmdr1 184F (42.1%). No artemisinin partial-resistance mutations were found.

articles.headline: IFN-γ and IL-4 signature correlates with malaria vaccine protection
articles.title_original: IFN-γ and IL-4 correlate with protection induced by an attenuated malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: Robinson TM, Bolton JS, Duncan EH, Bergmann-Leitner ES, Mura M
articles.details: Study used controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) after immunization with radiation-attenuated P. falciparum sporozoites delivered via mosquito bites to identify immune correlates of protection. Analysis of circulating mononuclear cells and PBMC responses revealed that baseline immune responses (IFN-γ secretion, HLA-DRhigh expression) and vaccine-induced responses (IL-4 secretion, CD57− γδ T cell frequency) correlated with protection from malaria infection.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria 15.3% in adults; rural areas burden higher
articles.title_original: Burden and determinants of asymptomatic malaria among adults living in urban and rural areas in Gabon in 2023: a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, M'Bondoukwé NP, Ndong Ngomo JM, Kono HN, Pongui Ngondza B, Ada Mengome M, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Moussavou Mabika DA, Mihindou CJ, Moutongo R, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Bouyou-Akotet MK
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of adults without malaria symptoms found overall P. falciparum prevalence of 15.3%, with higher burden in rural areas (22.4%) versus urban (4.1%, p<0.01). Independent risk factors included rural residence (aOR 7.1, 95% CI 4.4–9.8), being a worker (aOR 5.2, 95% CI 3.4–7.8), and attending school (p<0.01). ITN use was low (32.7%) and not significantly protective in multivariate analysis. Substantial asymptomatic malaria burden in rural areas highlights need to broaden control strategies to include adult populations.

articles.headline: Integrated malaria control in Madagascar: vector and community approaches
articles.title_original: Integrated Malaria Control in Madagascar: From Vector Dynamics to Community Interventions.
articles.authors: Ramboarina S, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Rasolofo V, Solofoharivelo MC
articles.details: Study examines integrated approaches to malaria control combining vector dynamics assessment with community-level interventions.

articles.headline: Transfusion malaria seroprevalence 6.8% among at-risk blood donors
articles.title_original: Prevention of Transfusion-Transmitted Malaria and Chagas Disease in Non-Endemic Countries: An 8-Year Study of Seroprevalence Among Donors at Risk in Tuscany (Central Italy).
articles.authors: Mangano VD, Pinto B, Marotta R, Galli L, Moscato GA, Lupetti A, Bruschi F
articles.details: Seroprevalence screening of blood donors at risk detected 6.8% anti-Plasmodium seropositivity (95% CI 6.1–7.7%), with higher rates in donors from Sub-Saharan Africa (42.9%, 95% CI 36.1–49.9%) and Southeast Asia (10.6%, 95% CI 6.7–16.4%). Seroprevalence varied by assay (Euroimmun ELISA 4.8% vs DRG ELISA 8.2%). Among 20 seropositive donors tested with LAMP, none had detectable Plasmodium DNA, and high seroreversion was observed after one year.

articles.headline: K13 artemisinin resistance mutations expanding rapidly in East Africa
articles.title_original: Mapping the prevalence of molecular markers of Plasmodium falciparum artemisinin partial resistance in Africa: a spatial-temporal modelling study.
articles.authors: Young NW, Meier-Scherling CPG, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Tollefson GA, Connelly SV, Marglous J, Gyuricza IG, Carey-Ewend K, Kyong-Shin R, Popkin-Hall ZR, Zeleke AJ, Ishengoma DS, Fola AA, Simkin A, Niaré K, Parr JB, Conrad M, Okell LC, Ruybal-Pesántez S, Watson OJ, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Verity R
articles.details: Systematic review of 182,071 genotyped samples from 3,806 molecular surveys (2014-2025) using spatial-temporal modeling. Found distinct k13 mutations (561H in Rwanda, 675V in Uganda, 622I in Ethiopia-Eritrea) driving artemisinin partial resistance. K13 prevalence increased 1.81% yearly in Uganda and 3.49% yearly in Rwanda, reaching 26.36% and 39.44% respectively by 2024. Partner drug markers mdr1 86Y declining but crt 76T remains prevalent.

articles.headline: DDT and malathion resistance widespread in Indian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal trends of insecticide resistance in Indian malaria vectors (2000-2024): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mullick R, Yadav CP, Singhal R
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 51 studies across India. Prevalence of DDT resistance in almost all states with Anopheles culicifacies showing very low susceptibility. Deltamethrin resistance major in Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh with 89% mortality in A. culicifacies, 93% in A. stephensi, and 100% in A. fluviatilis. A. culicifacies and A. stephensi highly resistant to malathion; north-eastern states remained mostly susceptible.

articles.headline: COVID-19 minimally impacted malaria indicators in Guinea
articles.title_original: The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on HIV, malaria, tuberculosis and childhood vaccination services in Guinea: an interrupted time series analysis.
articles.authors: Diallo A, Sidikiba S, Cissé AS, Camara M, Kaba M, Diallo BD, Carlos-Bolumbu M, Sako FB, Delamou A
articles.details: Interrupted time series analysis of national surveillance data (January 2018–December 2022) using Poisson segmented regression. In-patient malaria cases showed 0.8% monthly decline during pandemic (IRR 0.992, 95% CI 0.986–0.998). Overall, malaria indicators were generally maintained, whereas HIV, TB, and vaccination indicators were heavily impacted.

articles.headline: Vector metabolic state and microbiota determine P. vivax infection outcomes
articles.title_original: Metabolic reprogramming and gut microbiota ecology drive divergent Plasmodium vivax infection outcomes in Anopheles darlingi.
articles.authors: Carlos BC, Voges K, Affonso PHA, Jaye A, Tong Rios C, Tinoco-Nunes B, Alonso DP, Cai JA, MacCallum RM, Moreno M, Vlachou D, Souza-Neto JA, Christophides GK
articles.details: Experimental infections of Anopheles darlingi with field-derived P. vivax gametocytemic blood produced low and high oocyst burden phenotypes. Low-infection mosquitoes mounted sustained metabolic response (detoxification, redox regulation, aromatic amino acid catabolism, purine depletion) with reduced bacterial diversity and enrichment of Enterobacteriales and Pseudomonadales. High-infection mosquitoes showed limited metabolic reprogramming and Flavobacteriales expansion.

articles.headline: Microbiota composition associates with P. falciparum in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Microbiota diversity of Anopheles gambiae in Bankeng, southern Cameroon, and its association with Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Sandeu MM, Maffo Tatsinkou CG, Dada N, Kouhoué Feukam F, Njiokou F, Hughes GL, Wondji CS
articles.details: 16S rRNA sequencing of 120 naturally infected or uninfected Anopheles gambiae (60 each) identified 99 bacterial taxa; 97 shared between groups. Only Acetobacteraceae and Enterococcus were unique to uninfected mosquitoes. Differential abundance revealed 14 genera more abundant in uninfected mosquitoes and 2 in infected. Higher bacterial diversity observed in uninfected during wet season and infected during dry season.

articles.headline: Serratia AS1 persists in mosquitoes and habitats for paratransgenesis
articles.title_original: Environmental persistence and transmission dynamics of Serratia AS1 in mosquito habitats: advancing paratransgenesis for malaria control.
articles.authors: Kamel Urmia H, Koosha M, Yakhchali B, Moosa-Kazemi SH, Oshaghi MA
articles.details: Semi-field evaluation of Serratia AS1-mCherry in Anopheles stephensi habitats demonstrated successful colonization of mosquito midguts and ovaries. Bacterium persisted in larval water 14 days and remained stable on sugar-soaked cotton pads 4–6 days. Transmission routes included transstadial, venereal, and vertical transmission plus adult acquisition from larval habitats, establishing feasibility for field paratransgenesis malaria control.

articles.headline: Severe P. falciparum malaria complicated by symmetrical peripheral gangrene
articles.title_original: Symmetrical lower extremity gangrene in a patient with severe malaria: a case report and literature review.
articles.authors: Aman ST, Belay EH, Jirata TD, Hussen SH, Isa SF, Abagojam AH, Kebede ML, Bezabih KT, Tukeni KN
articles.details: Case report of 14-year-old with severe P. falciparum malaria presenting with fever, vomiting, and diarrhea. Despite prompt antimalarial therapy, developed symmetrical peripheral gangrene of lower limbs with preserved vital signs and organ function. Highlights importance of early recognition of this rare complication of severe malaria.

articles.headline: P. falciparum genomics needs evolutionary baseline model for drug resistance
articles.title_original: Towards an evolutionary baseline model of Plasmodium falciparum for population-genomic inference.
articles.authors: Henry CM, Marsh JI, Daigle A, Crescenzi J, Lin JT, Bailey JA, Johri P
articles.details: Perspective on population genomics of P. falciparum using tens of thousands of genomes from MalariaGEN. Multiple drug-resistance alleles identified at intermediate and high frequency in Asian and African populations. Argues that baseline evolutionary model accounting for simultaneous selective pressures and demographic history is needed to improve understanding of resistance evolution and identify new drug-resistance candidates.

articles.headline: CSP vaccine waning linked to dysfunctional memory B cell responses
articles.title_original: Dysfunctional memory B cell responses to the protective repeat region of the Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein are associated with waning humoral immunity.
articles.authors: McDougal CE, Mkindi CG, Rodda LB, Lucarelli CV, Langowski MD, King NP, Jongo S, Daubenberger C, Pepper M
articles.details: Cross-species analysis of B cell responses to circumsporozoite protein (CSP) repeat region following infection and vaccination. CSP-based vaccines display limited durability in malaria-naïve individuals and poorer efficacy in malaria-experienced individuals. Identified two main factors impairing optimal memory B cell differentiation: infection-induced germinal center inhibition and skewed differentiation due to CSP's repetitive nature.

articles.headline: Biliverdin reductase activity essential for malaria protection via bilirubin
articles.title_original: Biliverdin Reductase Catalytic Activity Is Essential for Malaria Resistance.
articles.authors: Mesquita M, Figueiredo A, Rastogi ST, Ramos S, Pagnotta S, Carlos AR, Cardoso S, Nóvoa A, Mallo M, Paul B, Jentho E, Soares MP
articles.details: Generated BVRA mutant mice (G17A and E97A) with reduced enzymatic activity in the NAD(P)H-binding domain and reductase motif. Both mutants succumbed to non-lethal malaria infection in wild-type mice. Quantified circulating unconjugated bilirubin showing dose-response relationship: mutant strains failed to reach protective threshold. Establishes antimalarial effect relies on BVRA catalytic activity.

articles.headline: Rosette formation reduces parasite adhesion in malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Rosette margination in blood flow during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Dasanna AK, Papagrigorakes M, Lanzer M, Schwarz US, Gompper G, Fedosov DA
articles.details: Combined hydrodynamic simulations and microfluidic experiments investigated rosette (infected RBC + healthy RBCs) margination in blood flow. Rosette margination significantly weaker than single infected RBCs across varying flow rates and binding strengths. Deformability and dynamics of rosette clusters primarily responsible for poor margination. Suggests rosette formation functions to maintain infected RBCs in circulation rather than enhance cytoadhesion to endothelium.

articles.headline: High-dose primaquine superior to standard dose for P. vivax radical cure
articles.title_original: Safety, tolerability, and efficacy of high versus low-dose, short versus long-course daily primaquine for the radical cure of uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria in children under 15 years of age: an open-label, non-inferiority, randomized controlled trial (CHILDPRIM).
articles.authors: Pacheco ALO, Omena AG, Baía-da-Silva DC, Jardim TAP, Silva DCB, Lopes APB, Barbosa LRA, Souza IG, Araujo RF, Nogueira LOS, Vale SCN, Melo GC, Cordeiro JSM, Bassat Q, Sampaio VS, Lima VDS, Martinez-Espinosa FE, Mourão MPG, Monteiro WM, Alecrim MGC, Brito-Sousa JD, Lacerda MVG
articles.details: Open-label RCT of 100 children (<15 years) with uncomplicated P. vivax in the Brazilian Amazon comparing three primaquine regimens: 3.5 mg/kg over 7 days (standard), 7.0 mg/kg over 14 days, and 7.0 mg/kg over 7 days. Kaplan-Meier 180-day recurrence-free rates: 50% (standard), 82.3% (high-dose long), 79.4% (high-dose short), p=0.0065. Higher doses caused more adverse events but no serious AEs.

articles.headline: Positive selection and duplication of P. vivax Duffy-binding protein gene
articles.title_original: Complete gene sequence diversity, amplification, and natural selection of the Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein locus.
articles.authors: Putaporntip C, Kuamsab N, Tia T, Takashima Y, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Sequence analysis of 232 P. vivax isolates from Thailand revealed Pvdbp duplication in 9.48% (22/232) of isolates, characterized by identical or microheterogamous copies. Positive selection occurred in both the 5' region and PvdbpII domain, with 26 positively selected codons identified. Duplication may enhance immune evasion through increased gene dosage, and positive selection is likely driven by host immune pressure.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi gametocyte carriage high among malaria patients in Malaysia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi gametocyte carriage and load among malaria patients at Kapit Hospital in Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo.
articles.authors: Theivindran D, Hu TH, Mohamad DSA, Rosli N, Hii KC, Singh B, Siner A
articles.details: In 295 P. knowlesi-infected patients at Kapit Hospital, Sarawak, 67.5% (199/295) carried viable gametocytes. Gametocyte load correlated positively with parasitemia (ρ = 0.32, p = 0.01), though no significant association with illness duration (p = 0.7). Elevated gametocyte levels were observed in some patients (13 with 10,001-100,000 and 12 with >100,000 pks25 transcript copies/µL), suggesting potential for human-to-human transmission.

articles.headline: Dam proximity increases P. falciparum prevalence in Burkina Faso villages
articles.title_original: Epidemiological profile and environmental determinants of Plasmodium falciparum infection in villages surrounding the Soum Agropole, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ouare N, Sorgho H, Hansson H, Rouamba T, Ilboudo H, Derra K, Kaboré B, Tahita MC, Rouamba E, Alifrangis M, Zida A, Magnussen P, Tinto H
articles.details: Household surveys of 13,786 participants showed asymptomatic carriage of 24.5% and clinical cases of 19.5%. Twelve-month malaria prevalence was 39.6% (95%CI 38.79-40.42), highest near Soum dam (42.1%; 95%CI 40.7-43.47) and lowest far from dam (33.2%; 95%CI 31.21-35.32). Ages 5-19 had highest prevalence (43.6%-69.4%). Monthly rainfall was associated with decreased infection risk (OR = 0.77, 95%CI 0.66-0.9).

articles.headline: Wnt pathway inhibition improves P. falciparum liver stage development in vitro
articles.title_original: Inhibition of Wnt signaling in primary human hepatocytes promotes Plasmodium falciparum liver stage development.
articles.authors: Kanyal A, van Gemert GJ, Wu H, van der Starre A, de Wilt JHW, Bousema T, Sauerwein RW, Bártfai R, Yang ASP
articles.details: Primary human hepatocytes cultured with Wnt signaling inhibition showed improved size and maturity of P. falciparum liver stage schizonts while retaining metabolic activity and epithelial characteristics. Transcriptomic analysis identified host signaling pathways contributing to hepatocyte dedifferentiation and parasite permissiveness. Wnt pathway inhibition significantly improved liver stage development, providing insights for therapeutic strategy development.

articles.headline: Low vaccine awareness and multiple barriers to RTS,S/AS01 uptake in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Awareness, perception and influences on uptake of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine among caregivers for children under 5 years in South West region, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Anye JC, Niba LL, Njimanted O, Fang EE, Itoe BT, Fung EG, Ewi-Kang HG, Kimbi HK
articles.details: Qualitative study of 20 caregivers of children aged 11-30 months in urban and rural Cameroon. Urban caregivers had high awareness but limited understanding; rural caregivers showed uneven awareness but strong trust in health workers. Barriers included communication gaps, misinformation, gender dynamics, long waiting times, and distance to health facilities. Enhanced community engagement and improved service delivery recommended.

articles.headline: MRI reveals cytotoxic edema in fulminant P. falciparum cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Radiological findings in fulminant cerebral malaria: a rare neuroimaging presentation.
articles.authors: Nayak B, Mohanty S, Das S, Panda S, Mishra SB
articles.details: Case of a 54-year-old woman with P. falciparum presenting with fever, seizures, shock, and multiorgan failure. MRI showed bilateral T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in basal ganglia, thalami, centrum semiovale, and periventricular white matter with diffusion restriction suggesting cytotoxic edema. Despite artesunate treatment and organ support, patient died from refractory multiorgan failure. MRI findings correlate with disease severity and poor prognosis in fulminant cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Serratia bacteria identified in Anopheles mosquitoes in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Molecular characterization of potential Plasmodium-Blocking Serratia spp. bacteria in field-caught malaria mosquito in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traoré H, Gnambani EJ, Hien DFS, Yao RK, Konkobo M, Sodré AF, Somda MB, Ouedraogo AS, Diabaté A, Bilgo E
articles.details: Molecular analysis of Anopheles mosquitoes from three field sites in Burkina Faso identified Serratia spp. bacteria with overall prevalence of 13.3%. Phylogenetic analysis showed these strains were highly similar but distinct from Serratia strains in neighboring countries. One A. coluzzii mosquito was co-infected with Serratia and P. falciparum. Findings support presence of Serratia in wild mosquitoes and its potential for malaria control.

articles.headline: China's malaria elimination demonstrates One Health vector-borne disease governance
articles.title_original: China's malaria elimination: One Health lessons for vector-borne disease governance.
articles.authors: Liu J, Liu Y, Cheng G
articles.details: Perspective article interpreting China's 2021 WHO certification as malaria-free, achieved through seven decades of adaptive surveillance, evolving strategies, and community engagement. Reviews regional case studies from Hainan Province, Huai River Basin, and Yunnan Province. Identifies four pillars of success: integrated surveillance, regional cooperation, ecological intervention, and technological innovation for sustainable vector control governance.

articles.headline: Geohelminth-malaria co-infection low but concentrated in third trimester
articles.title_original: Geohelminthiasis and Malaria Co-Infection in Pregnant Women in Kinshasa: Case of Lisungi and Lukunga Hospitals in Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Dianzenza C, Tshiongo JK, Kuseke L, Muswele F, Kasereka G, Mangungulu D, Laken EK, Tevuzula VM, Kayentao K, Mavoko HM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 336 pregnant women in Kinshasa found geohelminth-malaria co-infection in 5.7% and geohelminthiasis alone in 7.7%. Ascaris lumbricoides was most frequent (6.3%), followed by Trichuris trichiura (1.5%) and Ancylostoma duodenale (0.3%). Third trimester was associated with increased risk of co-infection and geohelminthiasis (ORa = 5.8; 95%CI 1.36-9.23; p = 0.0077). No significant association with maternal anemia.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial pH changes mark artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum parasites
articles.title_original: Fluorescence-based bioassay for the detection of changes in mitochondrial physiology during artemisinin-induced growth arrest of blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Whangviboonkij N, Kulkeaw K, Imwong M, Kobpornchai P
articles.details: Fluorescence bioassay using Mtphagy dye monitored mitochondrial physiology during dihydroartemisinin (DHA) exposure. DHA caused increased fluorescence (decreased pH) in early ring-form parasites. Inhibition of mitochondrial fission and PI3K-mediated pathway significantly reduced Mtphagy-positive parasites and was associated with delayed recrudescence. Targeting mitochondrial dynamics may offer strategy for identifying and overcoming artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: Sudan healthcare workers show critical knowledge gaps about malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Preliminary insights into knowledge and attitudes toward malaria vaccination among Sudanese healthcare workers: an exploratory cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Hamza DAA, Suliman OKB, Sam AAK, Abdelrahman AOO, Elemam TAA, Fadul EMAM
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 222 Sudanese healthcare workers (mean age 31.5 years, 66.7% female). Only 9% demonstrated adequate knowledge (≥70%) of malaria vaccination and 21.6% displayed positive attitudes (≥70%). While 66.2% heard of malaria vaccines, 71.2% were unaware of WHO recommendations and 76.6% did not know the target parasite. Negative attitudes reported by 78.4%, particularly regarding safety and affordability.

articles.headline: Brazilian intervention increased malaria detection in Yanomami indigenous lands
articles.title_original: What impact did the Brazilian Federal Government's intervention have on malaria cases in the Yanomami Indigenous lands?
articles.authors: de Aguiar Barros J, Granja F, Siqueira PEL, da Silva Epifânio I, Coelho RR, Ferreira-da-Cruz MF
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 2021-2024 malaria data from Roraima using Brazil's Health Information System. After federal intervention in 2023, indigenous malaria cases increased significantly (52,210 to 65,004; p = 0.0005), while hospitalizations decreased (787 to 638; p = 0.0304), and deaths remained steady (111; p = 0.9089). P. vivax was dominant species. Post-intervention, case detection increased for all species with age distribution shift toward vulnerable groups (0-19, 60+ years). Exported cases to other states declined sharply (Maranhão -95.5%, Pará -86.8%, Amazonas -71.5%).

articles.headline: Anopheles species distribution varies across Burkina Faso climatic zones
articles.title_original: Spatial Distribution and Biodiversity of Anopheles Mosquito Species Across Climatic Zones in Burkina Faso: Implications for Malaria Vector Control.
articles.authors: Zongo ON, Kiendrebeogo E, Sow BBD, Kientega M, Toé I, Sanou R, Yemien SOG, Sawadogo G, Kaboré H, Agolinou A, Traore N, Epopa PS, Millogo AA, Niang A, Namountougou M, Maiga H, Diabaté A
articles.details: Indoor resting mosquitoes were collected from 67 health districts across 13 regions, identifying 30,521 Anopheles specimens. An. gambiae s.l. dominated overall (94.4%). Molecular analysis of 2,026 An. gambiae s.l. specimens revealed heterogeneity by zone: An. coluzzii predominated in Sahelian (74.9%) and Sudano-Sahelian (71.2%) zones, while An. gambiae s.s. was most frequent in the Soudanian zone (53.8%). Biodiversity decreased from humid southern to arid northern areas, highlighting the need for locally adapted malaria vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Periostin modulates phagocytosis of infected red blood cells
articles.title_original: Effect of periostin (OSF-2) on phagocytosis of Plasmodium-infected erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Chin JY, Mohammed-Syafiei MN, Lim YJ, Chong GX, Suhaimi MN, Phong ZY, Ng YL, Lau YL, Sam IC, Rénia L, Lee WC
articles.details: Using human monocytic THP-1 cells and P. falciparum and P. knowlesi isolates, researchers examined how periostin (OSF-2), a host inflammation-related protein, affects phagocytosis of infected erythrocytes. OSF-2 exerted a dual role: when parasite rosetting was prevented, OSF-2 increased CD36-dependent phagocytosis; however, when rosetting occurred (with uninfected RBCs added), OSF-2 reduced phagocytosis rates. These findings highlight complex parasite-host interactions influencing infection pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers detected in P. falciparum from western Kenya
articles.title_original: Comparative molecular surveillance of polymorphisms in chloroquine resistance transporter, multidrug resistance 1, and Kelch 13 genes associated with antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Bungei J, Ouma C, Zhong D, Oyweri J, Lee MC, Zhou G, Atieli H, Githure J, Wang C, Yan G
articles.details: A cross-sectional study screened P. falciparum isolates from malaria patients in two western Kenya regions for SNPs in Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, and Pfk13 genes. Overall mutation frequencies included Pfcrt K76T (5.4%), Pfmdr1 Y184F (48.2%), and Pfk13 A675V (12.8%). The Kisii region showed significantly higher prevalence of Pfcrt M74I/N75E/K76T alleles (11.5%) and Pfk13 A675V (27.5%) compared to Kisumu (p < 0.05), while rare Pfk13 S522C and R561H mutations emerged in Kisumu. These findings support continued molecular surveillance given artemether-lumefantrine as Kenya's first-line treatment.

articles.headline: RDT self-testing is feasible and acceptable in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Feasibility of self-testing for acute malaria using rapid diagnostic tests in three peri-urban sub-Saharan African community settings.
articles.authors: Brumwell A, Bemer M, Lauff A, Morton JF, Thomas KK, Moe CA, Cooper S, Humphries H, Sharma A, Pollard D, Kwena Z, Sang N, Winters A, van Heerden A, Bukusi E, Rech D, Drain P
articles.details: A feasibility study evaluated RDT self-testing in 100 households each in Kenya (225 participants), South Africa (80), and Zambia (163). Participants interpreted RDTs with 100% sensitivity and 99.7% specificity compared to trained staff interpretation. Over 96% of participants across all three sites felt confident using RDTs for specimen collection, test operation, and result interpretation. Perceived comfort for future self-testing was high in Kenya (92%) and Zambia (86%), and moderate in South Africa (66%).

articles.headline: Biometric fingerprinting improves patient identification in malaria clinic
articles.title_original: Enhancing unique identification of patients with malaria using biometric fingerprinting in Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Fransisca L, Kenangalem E, Ubra RR, Ley B, Price RN, Poespoprodjo JR, Douglas NM
articles.details: A 16-month before-and-after study at a clinic in Papua, Indonesia evaluated biometric fingerprinting to improve patient identification. Among 7,471 malaria patients, the proportion who could be linked to a pre-existing malaria card increased from 44.4% in the pre-intervention phase to 48.9% after fingerprinting implementation (95% CI 1.4–7.7%). Among fingerprinted patients, 55.8% were successfully linked, representing an 11.4% improvement over baseline (95% CI 8.1–14.8%). Refusal rates were very low (0.26–0.39%), though unreadable fingerprints in children posed implementation challenges.

articles.headline: High P. falciparum genetic diversity in Busia and Kilifi
articles.title_original: Characterizing Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity and complexity of infections in clinical malaria infections in Western and Coastal Kenya using the poly-alpha microsatellite marker.
articles.authors: Abade NA, Mgawe C, Ndwiga L, Kimenyi KM, Ochola-Oyier LI
articles.details: Genotyping of P. falciparum isolates from 93 participants in a 2016 therapeutic efficacy study in Busia and 92 from a 2020 malaria monitoring study in Kilifi using the poly-α microsatellite marker revealed high genetic diversity in both regions. Busia infections were predominantly polyclonal (80%) with mean complexity of infection (COI) 2.9, while Kilifi was mostly monoclonal (52.5%) with mean COI 1.7 (p < 0.0001). Both regions showed similar expected heterozygosity (Busia 0.92, Kilifi 0.90), with Busia having more effective alleles (10.8 vs 9.3). These findings support poly-α utility as a molecular correction tool in efficacy studies.

articles.headline: Optimized whole genome amplification for faster malaria diagnostics
articles.title_original: Enhancing whole genome DNA amplification of Plasmodium falciparum for advanced molecular surveillance in malaria control.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, de Jesus MCS, Phelan JE, Thorpe J, Ward D, Batra R, Patel A, Nolder D, Chiodini P, Sutherland CJ, Edgeworth J, Clark TG, Campino S
articles.details: A study optimized selective whole genome amplification (SWGA) protocol for P. falciparum clinical samples to improve sequencing yield for genomic surveillance. Using 13 clinical whole blood samples from returning travelers, researchers tested varying SWGA incubation durations and a new EquiPhi29 protocol. The optimized 3-hour SWGA protocol achieved >63% P. falciparum genome coverage with >90% SNP concordance across conditions and reduced reagent costs by almost 60%. Drug resistance profiles remained consistent across tested conditions, supporting faster, more cost-effective malaria genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Deep learning achieves 98% accuracy for blood smear malaria detection
articles.title_original: Trustworthy deep learning for malaria diagnosis using explainable artificial intelligence.
articles.authors: Parveen R, Qui B, Song W, Al-Kahtani N, Jamjoom MM, Mostafa SM, Sultan N, Fatima J
articles.details: Four CNNs (MobileNetV2, VGG19, InceptionV3, ResNet18) were evaluated on blood smear images, with ResNet18 achieving F1-score 96.33%. Two advanced architectures, Xception and Inception-ResNetV2, were fine-tuned on 27,090 Kaggle malaria images, attaining ~98% accuracy on validation and test sets. Model robustness was confirmed on independent Harvard Dataverse thick smear images (97–98% accuracy despite varied staining and imaging conditions). Three explainable AI techniques (Grad-CAM, LIME, SHAP) enhanced interpretability and clinical trust by revealing morphological/staining artifacts contributing to misclassifications.

articles.headline: Parasite metabolism drives rRNA gene silencing in human host
articles.title_original: A metabolism-chromatin axis promotes differential ribosomal RNA transcription in the human malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Couble JE, Vignolini T, Doré G, Mussgnug S, Lombard B, Richard M, Loew D, Büttner M, Dueñas-Sánchez R, Poschet G, Bryant JM, Baumgarten S
articles.details: In P. falciparum, researchers characterized epigenetic regulation of rRNA transcription, finding that rDNA silencing relies on aerobic glycolysis, the parasite's energy source in the human host. Disruption of NAD+ regeneration during lactate fermentation promoted rDNA de-repression. The sirtuin histone deacetylase Sir2a mediates the link between fluctuating NAD+ levels and rDNA transcriptional activity. rDNA activation appears coupled to metabolic state transition during host-to-vector transmission, from aerobic glycolysis to mitochondrial respiration.

articles.headline: Gut microbiome composition predicts parasitemia risk in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Distinct gastrointestinal microbial signatures predict parasite levels in controlled Plasmodium infections in both rhesus macaques and humans.
articles.authors: Gustin AT, Broedlow CA, Hager K, Coronado E, Wangari S, Iwayama N, Ahrens CY, Garrison WD, Guerriero KA, De Paris K, Londono-Renteria B, Gale M Jr, Klatt NR, Kublin JG, Manuzak JA
articles.details: Using 16S rRNA sequencing, researchers characterized pre-infection gut microbiome composition in rhesus macaques infected with P. fragile and humans in a controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) trial (NCT04072302) originally designed to test KAF156 efficacy. Distinct pre-infection microbial signatures predicted increased risk for above-median parasitemia in both RMs and CHMI participants infected with P. falciparum. A Bifidobacterium feature set accurately stratified parasitemia risk and could serve as a biomarker panel for prevention efforts. The microbiome-parasite dynamic in RMs mirrored that in humans, supporting future microbiome-targeting interventions.

articles.headline: Histone lactylation serves as metabolic sensor in malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Histone lactylation: A new epigenetic mark in the malaria parasite Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Jabre I, Andoh NE, Naldoni J, Gregory W, Mbye H, Yoon CE, Cunnington AJ, Georgiadou A, Blagborough AM, Merrick CJ
articles.details: Researchers characterized histone lactylation as a novel epigenetic mark in four Plasmodium species (P. falciparum, P. knowlesi, P. yoelii, P. berghei). Histones were increasingly lactylated in response to elevated lactate levels, making this mark a metabolic sensor for severe malaria-associated hyperlactataemia. Mass spectrometry revealed lactylation of multiple lysines on parasite histones and non-histone chromatin proteins, with P. falciparum showing greater lactylation than P. knowlesi, suggesting species-specific epigenetic evolution. In P. yoelii, hyperlactataemia correlated with transcriptomic programmes indicating metabolic dormancy.

articles.headline: Dengue virus detected in Niger River Basin febrile patients
articles.title_original: Dengue virus infection amongst malaria and typhoid fever suspected acute febrile patients in the Niger river basin of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okpanachi RO, Omatola CA, Abraham JO, Okolo MO, Conco-Biyela T, Onoja AB, Adamu AM
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 200 febrile patients in Nigeria's Niger River Basin detected dengue virus (19.0% anti-DENV seropositive: 13.5% IgG, 4.0% IgM) alongside endemic malaria (52%) and typhoid (62.5%). Mono-infections were dengue 13.5%, malaria 18.5%, and typhoid 29%, with 29% having dual malaria-typhoid co-infection and 4% triple infection. Risk factors for dengue included age 41–50 years, farming occupation, being married, and environmental factors such as uncovered water containers and stagnant water. Symptoms significantly associated with active dengue included back pain and nausea.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence and prevention gaps in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of malaria among pregnant women in Maraki Health Center, North West, Gondar, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Dagnaw M, Indracanti M, Jan A, Ayalew J
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 271 pregnant women found 13.4% malaria prevalence. Key risk factors included not using ITN (AOR=3.2; 95% CI: 1.4-7.5), not receiving IPTp-SP (AOR=2.8; 95% CI: 1.1-6.9), fewer than four ANC visits (AOR=4.6; 95% CI: 1.9-11.2), living near stagnant water (AOR=2.5; 95% CI: 1.1-5.8), and attending ANC in rainy season (AOR=1.9; 95% CI: 1.0-3.7).

articles.headline: Protocol for sorting MSP1-specific B cells in malaria
articles.title_original: Protocol to identify and sort antigen-specific B cells from Plasmodium-infected mice.
articles.authors: Calôba C, Minns AM, Lindner SE, Vijay R
articles.details: Protocol describes identification and sorting of antigen-specific B cells binding MSP-1-19 antigen in rodent malaria models, including steps for splenic single-cell suspension preparation, B cell enrichment, and live-cell staining for downstream applications such as single-cell RNA, V(D)J, and ATAC sequencing.

articles.headline: Long-read sequencing characterizes P. falciparum antigenic variants
articles.title_original: Protocol for genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum antigens using amplicon-based PacBio long-read sequencing.
articles.authors: Plaza DF
articles.details: Protocol identifies and classifies structural variants of msp1, msp2, glurp, and csp in P. falciparum using amplicon-based long-read sequencing with PCR barcoding, circular consensus sequencing, and Galaxy-based analysis. Approach resolves full-length sequences for antigenic clones, enabling measurement of infection complexity, phylogenetic construction, and vaccine design support.

articles.headline: Forest-data integration increases malaria surveillance detection threefold
articles.title_original: Integrating forest data and health facility surveys to optimise risk-based malaria surveillance in the Philippines.
articles.authors: Fornace KM, Reyes RA, Macalinao MLM, Lim JS, Bareng APN, Luchavez JS, Hafalla JCR, Espino FEJ, Matthiopoulos J, Drakeley CJ
articles.details: Health facility surveys in a forested Philippine district integrated environmental data with RDT, microscopy, and molecular malaria detection. Risk-based surveillance incorporating landscape data increased probability of detecting malaria foci over threefold compared to routine surveillance and was more cost-effective, demonstrating the value of environmental data in optimizing disease surveillance.

articles.headline: Correction to immune response and evasion article
articles.title_original: Correction to "Immune Response and Evasion Mechanisms of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites".
articles.details: Correction to article DOI: 10.1155/2018/6529681.

articles.headline: Hemozoin causes neuronal injury in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Hemozoin induces neuronal injury primarily characterized by axon rupture and mitochondrial damage in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Li T, Dang D, Shen Y, Wang J, Huang Y, Zhu Q, Wang Y, Yang C, Li G, Sun J, Zhang A, Li P, Liang J, Zhao Y
articles.details: In C57BL/6J mice infected with P. berghei ANKA, cerebral cortex showed significant hemozoin deposition and reduced NeuN expression. In vitro, purified hemozoin induced neuronal damage including dendritic loss and axon rupture, mitochondrial dysfunction (reduced membrane potential and ATP, elevated ROS), and upregulated nNOS activity. Study provides evidence that hemozoin directly causes neuronal injury through internalization and oxidative stress mechanisms.

articles.headline: Drug resistance surveillance reveals regional patterns in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance genes in Nigeria: a systematic review and roadmap to malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Bamikole OJ, Fayehun AF, Uthman YA, Salako IA, Adedeji BA, Olufeagba MB, Amodu OK
articles.details: Systematic review of 26 studies (2010-2024) examining Pfcrt, Pfmdr, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, and Pfk13 genes found high prevalence of chloroquine and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance mutations, particularly in Pfcrt and Pfdhfr. Artemisinin resistance mutations (Pfk13) were less common. Regional disparities observed with generally lower Pfcrt prevalence in northern Nigeria compared to the south.

articles.headline: 2025 malaria funding cuts threaten control gains in Africa
articles.title_original: African leaders and regional institutions need to take the bull by the horns: a perspective on the impact of the 2025 funding cuts to malaria programmes.
articles.authors: Osoro CB, Hill J
articles.details: Significant funding decreases in 2025 from US government and other donors disrupted key malaria control activities including bed net distribution, seasonal chemoprevention campaigns, and indicator surveys. Africa carries 94% of global malaria burden (263 million cases in 2023). African governments must increase domestic funding and establish public-private partnerships to sustain malaria control progress and prevent resurgence.

articles.headline: Spatial mapping identifies high-risk urban malaria areas in Ghana
articles.title_original: Fine-scale spatial mapping of urban malaria prevalence for microstratification in an urban area of Ghana.
articles.authors: Oppong SK, Dosoo DK, Peprah NY, Adu GA, Mohammed W, Rozier J, Kayan K, McPhail M, Amratia P, Alene KA, Asante KP, Gething PW, Malm KL
articles.details: Geospatial analysis of malaria prevalence from 12,371 surveyed children aged 6 months-10 years in Greater Accra Region combined environmental, climatic, and urban covariates to predict risk. Predicted prevalence ranged from 0-49%, with satellite-derived data on vegetation indices predictive of malaria risk. Maps quantifying probability of prevalence exceeding 10% identified high-risk districts for targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Molecular surveillance detects hidden submicroscopic malaria infections
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic parasitaemia in a malaria pre-elimination setting in the Putumayo area of the Ecuadorian Amazon basin.
articles.authors: van der Ende J, Dávila MV, Menting S, Sáenz FE, Hanscheid T, Schallig HDFH, Grobusch MP
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 293 asymptomatic individuals during 2023 rainy season using RDT, microscopy, and qPCR found 0/293 infections by RDT, 3 P. vivax (1.0%; 95% CI: 0.2-2.4%) by microscopy, and 5 (1.7%; 95% CI: 0.6-3.9%) by qPCR with very low parasite densities (mean Cq 32.6). Molecular tools revealed submicroscopic infections missed by RDTs and microscopy.

articles.headline: P. falciparum-infected RBCs impair megakaryocytic differentiation
articles.title_original: Interactions between Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells and their extracellular vesicles with megakaryocytes: implications for platelet-like particle formation.
articles.authors: Nunthanasup N, Palasuwan A, Palasuwan D, Combes V
articles.details: MEG-01 cells differentiated with PMA were co-cultured with P. falciparum-infected RBCs, normal RBCs, or their extracellular vesicles. Infected RBCs transiently adhered to megakaryocytes, suppressed megakaryocytic marker expression, downregulated NOTCH3, and altered apoptosis genes. Platelet-like particle production increased but clotting activity was impaired. Infected RBCs and vesicles increased IL-8, RANTES, and MCP-1.

articles.headline: Declining malaria correlates with reduced endemic Burkitt's lymphoma
articles.title_original: A "Familiar Foe Revisited": examining the relationship between endemic Burkitt's lymphoma and changing malaria admissions in the coastal region of Kenya.
articles.authors: Mwaniki MK, Mohammed S, Kariuki N, Wamalwa DC, Mutisya F, Newton CR
articles.details: Analysis of pediatric hospital data over three decades (1990-2020) identified 95 Burkitt's lymphoma cases (75.8% male). Cumulative incidence declined: 93.1 per 100,000 (1990-1999), 130.3 (2000-2009), 10.2 (2010-2020; p<0.0001). Median P. falciparum parasite density declined from 13,966 to 7,224 (p=0.0021). Correlation between BL and P. falciparum infection was 0.53 (p=0.0024), indicating strong positive association.

articles.headline: Liver-stage malaria models: advances and research gaps
articles.title_original: Experimental models of liver-stage malaria: Progress, gaps, and challenges.
articles.authors: Nitaramorn N, Kulkeaw K, Imwong M
articles.details: Review article examining experimental platforms for studying Plasmodium parasites in the liver, including in vitro models and emerging organoid technology. Discusses strengths and limitations of current approaches and identifies critical gaps in intrahepatic parasite growth representation. Highlights how organoids can improve drug development and prevention strategy evaluation.

articles.headline: Plant extract fraction shows potent antimalarial activity in mice
articles.title_original: n-Butanol Fraction of Dioscorea dumetorum Tuber Aqueous Extract Exhibited Potent Antimalarial Activity in Plasmodium berghei-infected Mice.
articles.authors: Okeme U, Yakubu MT
articles.details: The n-butanol fraction (nBF) and column chromatography sub-fraction (CCF1) from Dioscorea dumetorum tubers demonstrated 49.13% and 40.80% inhibition respectively of Plasmodium berghei parasite growth in a murine model. CCF1 contained 11 compounds with allantoin and protodioscin being most abundant, suggesting potential for novel antimalarial drug development from plant sources.

articles.headline: Treatment-seeking barriers persist despite free malaria care access
articles.title_original: Predictors and characteristics of malaria treatment-seeking in Malawi: a longitudinal cohort study.
articles.authors: Wan C, Katsabola H, Sixpence A, Mangani C, Matengeni A, Taylor TE, Wilson ML, Valim C, Mathanga DP, Laufer MK, Cohee LM, Buchwald AG
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study of 962 participants in rural Malawi (7,293 visits) found 82% sought formal-sector malaria treatment; longer walking distance (OR=0.28 for >90 min vs <30 min) and higher education (OR=0.41 for secondary vs none) associated with reduced formal-sector treatment-seeking. PCD visit rate was 70 per 100 person-years, significantly lower among older children, males, and households farther from facilities.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic malaria infections high in Peruvian Amazon indigenous communities
articles.title_original: Hidden burden: submicroscopic Plasmodium spp. infections in indigenous populations of the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Saavedra-Samillán M, López-Ugarte LC, Sandoval-Bances J, Rojas LM, Udhayakumar V, Valdivia HO, Sanchez JF, Chenet SM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 926 indigenous residents found 17.0% Plasmodium-positive (157/926) with 8.5% submicroscopic infections (79/926); P. vivax most frequently detected. Asymptomatic individuals had 2.32-fold higher submicroscopic prevalence (95% CI 1.63-3.29). Household animal presence associated with 36% reduced prevalence (aPR=0.64, 95% CI 0.48-0.85); Caterpiza community showed 2.34-fold higher prevalence than Alianza Progreso (95% CI 1.68-3.24).

articles.headline: Anxiety prevalent among caregivers of children with severe malaria
articles.title_original: Prevalence of anxiety and associated factors among guardians of children admitted with severe malaria at Thyolo District Hospital in Malawi.
articles.authors: Chidzere N, Munthali C, Chapweteka G, Munthali T, Kumwenda P, Chikasema B, Mkwinda E, Chironda G
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 187 guardians of children with severe malaria hospitalization found mild anxiety most common (79.7%), followed by moderate (10.7%) and minimal (9.6%), with no severe anxiety cases. Longer hospital stays (p<0.001) and repeated admissions (p<0.001) were significantly associated with increased anxiety severity among caregivers.

articles.headline: Microtubule acetylation not essential for rodent malaria parasite survival
articles.title_original: Luminal acetylation of microtubules is not essential for Plasmodium berghei and Toxoplasma gondii survival.
articles.authors: Kumar T, Röver K, Stortz JF, Binder AM, Spreng B, Konert M, Meissner M, Frischknecht F, Jimenez-Ruiz E
articles.details: Genetic mutant studies examined role of lysine 40 acetylation in microtubules of Plasmodium berghei and Toxoplasma gondii. Q40K mutation in P. berghei α1-tubulin and α-acetyltransferase deletion in T. gondii had no impact on parasite development, growth, or infectivity, indicating luminal K40 acetylation is not essential for microtubule function.

articles.headline: Low uptake of breast cancer screening in Tanzanian women
articles.title_original: Factors associated with the uptake of clinical breast examination for early detection of breast cancer: interaction analysis of the 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Nyamhanga T, Luoga P, Paulo H, Wangwe P, Adam J, Eustace R
articles.details: Secondary analysis of Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey 2022 and Malaria Indicator Survey examined clinical breast examination (CBE) uptake among women aged 15-49. Overall CBE uptake was very low (5%); women with low income, unemployment, and lack of health insurance had significantly lower odds of screening. Interactions observed between age and residence, and between age and education.

articles.headline: Severe malaria with multiple WHO complications: fatal case report
articles.title_original: We Still Have to Fear Malaria: A Case Report of Severe Malaria With Almost all the Listed WHO Complications in a Patient Living in a Sub-Saharan Endemic Area.
articles.authors: Simeni Njonnou SR, Tsiazok Dongmo M, Djilo Kouonang LO, Tsafack Tapondjou B, Ndembe Djeni E, Pierre Choukem S
articles.details: Case report of 67-year-old woman in Sub-Saharan endemic area with severe malaria and multiple WHO-listed complications including cerebral involvement, hemolytic anemia, severe hypoglycemia, status epilepticus, respiratory failure, hepatocellular failure, acute kidney injury with hyperkalemia (6.24 mmol/L), digital ischemia, and pressure ulcers. Patient died from sepsis despite initial improvements in consciousness and metabolic correction.

articles.headline: Low genetic diversity in P. falciparum purine transporters supports targeting
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum equilibrative nucleoside transporters PfENT1 and PfENT4: Implications for purine-based antimalarial drug development.
articles.authors: Tashie W, de Koning HP, Duah-Quashie NO, Quashie NB
articles.details: Analysis of >7,000 P. falciparum isolates examined genetic diversity in equilibrative nucleoside transporters PfENT1 and PfENT4. Missense variant frequencies were 4.5% (PfENT1) and 6.2% (PfENT4); common variants included F36S, V129I, L133F in PfENT1 and I188F, N209I, A260T, S383A in PfENT4. Both genes showed high conservation with low genetic diversity, supporting suitability as drug targets.

articles.headline: Transmission dynamics shifted toward co-transmission in western Kenya over decades
articles.title_original: Assessing the impact of temporal changes in transmission on Plasmodium falciparum strains in Asembo, western Kenya (1996-2017) using within-host metrics via 24-SNP barcodes.
articles.authors: Vestal G, Zhou Z, Sergent S, Sheth M, Lee J, Otieno K, Kariuki S, Hill A, Ter Kuile FO, Lindblade KA, Slutsker L, Hamel MJ, Desai M, Gimnig JE, Samuels AM, Vigfusson Y, Shi YP
articles.details: Longitudinal study using 24-SNP barcode genotyping of P. falciparum from surveys spanning 1996-2017 in western Kenya examined transmission dynamics. Multiplicity of infection (MOI) decreased from 4.32 (1996) to 3.34 (2012) then increased to 3.49 (2017); within-host strain relatedness inversely correlated with MOI. Temporal patterns suggested shift toward co-transmission 2001-2012, returning to superinfection-dominant dynamics by 2017.

articles.headline: Dual-AI nets sustain mosquito control better than pyrethroid-only
articles.title_original: Three-year trends in malaria transmission parameters under deployment of Interceptor®G1, Interceptor®G2 and PermaNet®3.0 insecticide-treated bed nets in South-Western Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Tapsoba M, Guelbeogo WM, Sanou A, Zongo S, Gogue C, Debe S, Arnett K, Davis K, Shannon J, Digre P, Mwesigwa J, Tynuv K, Fornadel C, N'Falé S, Robertson M, Challenger JD, Tougri G, Gansané A, Ranson H, Olivier G, Wagman J
articles.details: Longitudinal entomological study (August 2019-February 2022) in three Burkina Faso districts evaluated three ITN types with 73,101 female Anopheles collected (>59% An. gambiae s.l.). Banfora district with pyrethroid-chlorfenapyr Interceptor G2 nets showed 88.7% mosquito density decline sustained over 3 years; pyrethroid-only district showed initial decline then year-3 rebound; pyrethroid-PBO district maintained stable densities.

articles.headline: Malaria incidence declined 96% but persists in specific hotspots
articles.title_original: Unraveling the spatiotemporal clustering of malaria incidence and modeling a decade of epidemiological data in Jimma Zone, Oromia, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Biruksew A, Demeke A, Birhanu Z, Golassa L, Sisay AL, Kemal J, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Analysis of decade of malaria surveillance (2012-2021) from 15 Jimma Zone districts screened 1,718,471 individuals identifying 359,655 confirmed cases (20.93%); P. falciparum 52.71%, P. vivax 43.62%, mixed infections 3.67%. Malaria incidence decreased 96.13% with API falling from 87.97 per 1,000 population in 2012 to 3.40 in 2021. ARIMA modeling predicted stable incidence 2022-2026 with seasonal fluctuations; specific areas remained high-risk.

articles.headline: Is Cabo Verde truly malaria-free?
articles.title_original: "Cabo Verde: is it really malaria-free?".
articles.authors: Diogo R, Carvalho BT, Mendonça L, da Cunha MR, Pedro DM, Paramés F, Marques T, Paulo SE, Cristino JM, Pereira ÁA
articles.details: This article questions whether Cabo Verde has achieved malaria-free status.

articles.headline: Delayed diagnosis and severe malaria outcomes in Vietnam
articles.title_original: Delayed diagnosis and severe outcomes in imported malaria in Vietnam highlighting systematic gaps in detection.
articles.authors: Nguyen TTT, Do CD, Nguyen BHT, Pham HTT, Nong VM, Van Vu G, Doan TT
articles.details: This article addresses imported malaria in Vietnam, examining the relationship between delayed diagnosis, severe outcomes, and systematic gaps in malaria detection.

articles.headline: Room-temperature stable LAMP for rapid knowlesi malaria detection
articles.title_original: Rapid Detection of Knowlesi Malaria Using Room-Temperature Stable and Ready-to-Use Colorimetric Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification Reagents.
articles.authors: Lai MY, Lau YL
articles.details: A dehydrated colorimetric loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostic tool was developed for detecting P. knowlesi malaria. The assay was tested on 75 clinical blood samples from Malaysian hospitals and achieved 100% sensitivity (95% CI: 92.1–100.0%) and 100% specificity (95% CI: 88.4–100.0%) compared to nested PCR. Dehydrated LAMP reagents remained stable at room temperature for up to 15 days, offering a practical diagnostic option for resource-limited settings where knowlesi malaria is endemic.

articles.headline: Home tablet-based neurorehabilitation for cerebral malaria survivors
articles.title_original: A Pilot Feasibility Study of a Home Tablet-Based Neurorehabilitation Program and Serial Brain Vital Sign Monitoring for Survivors of Pediatric Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Macoun SJ, Silverman JM, Brunet PR, Lewis JM, Kafoteka E, Ziba T, Kirby ED, Ighalo J, D'Arcy RCN, O'Brien N
articles.details: A pilot feasibility study evaluated a 6-month in-home tablet-based neurorehabilitation program (Dino Island) and serial brain vital sign monitoring using event-related potentials (ERPs) in children aged 3–12 years who survived cerebral malaria. Both programs demonstrated feasibility with 95% retention rate, 100% positive family ratings, and 60% of children showing positive behavioral changes per parent reports. Brain vital sign monitoring showed adequate data acquisition for most participants and positive affordability indicators, supporting further development of cognitive recovery interventions in malaria-endemic regions.

articles.headline: Deep learning models for automated malaria parasite detection
articles.title_original: Malaria Parasite Cell Classification Using Transfer Learning with State-of-the-Art CNN Architectures.
articles.authors: Laghari AA, Muhammad W, Memon ML, Hussain A, Kumar A
articles.details: This study used transfer learning with pretrained convolutional neural network (CNN) models to automate malaria parasite detection and classification from microscopic blood smear images. Eight models were evaluated: VGG16, VGG19, Inception-v3, ResNet-18, ResNet-34, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, and Xception. ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 achieved ~89% accuracy, Xception ~88%, and VGG-16 ~80%. Fine-tuning on extensive labeled datasets of parasitized and uninfected cells showed that ResNet-50, ResNet-101, and Xception demonstrated balanced performance with higher accuracy, while VGG-16 exhibited higher precision but lower recall for parasitized cells.

articles.headline: Next-generation insecticide nets: efficacy from trials to real-world
articles.title_original: Cascades of effectiveness of next-generation insecticide-treated nets against malaria, from entomological trials to real-life conditions.
articles.authors: Champagne C, Lemant J, Assenga A, Kibondo UA, Lekundayo RG, Mbuba E, Moore J, Muganga JB, Ntabaliba WS, Odufuwa OG, Swai JK, Alexa M, Goers R, Golumbeanu M, Chitnis N, Ross A, N'Guessan R, Moore S, Pothin E
articles.details: A mathematical model quantified the effectiveness cascade of two next-generation insecticide-treated nets (ITNs)—Interceptor®G2 (chlorfenapyr-pyrethroid) and Olyset® Plus (piperonyl butoxide-pyrethroid)—from entomological efficacy to population-level impact. The model was parameterized with entomological data and validated against randomized controlled trial results. Beyond entomological factors, operational factors including functional survival, ITN use, and in-bed exposure critically impact overall effectiveness and vary by ITN type. Results from Tanzania can be extended to other settings using a dashboard for context-specific product selection.

articles.headline: No artemisinin-resistance mutations in P. falciparum in Iran
articles.title_original: Absence of Artemisinin Resistance-Associated Mutations in the Plasmodium Falciparum Kelch 13 (K13) Propeller Domain Gene Following Widespread Use of Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapy in Iran.
articles.authors: Mohammadi S, Forouzesh F, Mehrizi AA, Shafi ZSM, Pakdel JD, Raeisi A, Pirahmadi S
articles.details: A surveillance study assessed prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 (pfk13) mutations linked to artemisinin resistance in Iranian isolates, 18 years after nationwide introduction of artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs). Finger-prick blood samples (n=60) from patients with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria during 2022–2023 were sequenced via nested PCR. Of successfully sequenced samples (n=50), 98% (49/50) carried the wild-type allele; one carried a novel nonsynonymous R513C mutation (not validated as resistance-associated). No validated pfk13 mutations indicative of artemisinin resistance were detected, suggesting artemisinin remains effective for treating P. falciparum malaria in this region.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA antibodies in pregnant Brazilian women infected with malaria
articles.title_original: VAR2CSA-reactive IgG in Brazilian women exposed to Plasmodium falciparum or P. vivax infection during pregnancy.
articles.authors: Dos Santos MI, Dombrowski JG, Hoffmann-Veltung H, Quintana MdP, Marinho CRF, Hviid L, Lopez-Perez M
articles.details: A study measured VAR2CSA-reactive IgG levels and Fc-afucosylation in plasma from pregnant Brazilian women at delivery using recombinant VAR2CSA expressed in different cell systems and native antigen, plus IgG specific for GLURP (P. falciparum) and PvDBP (P. vivax). VAR2CSA IgG expressed in Chinese hamster ovary cells (FV2CHO) was restricted to P. falciparum-exposed women with lower levels than insect-cell protein. PvDBP-specific IgG was higher in P. vivax-exposed women but did not correlate with VAR2CSA IgG. Data caution against using recombinant insect-cell VAR2CSA as an exposure marker and do not support PvDBP cross-reactivity with VAR2CSA in South America.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi Hsp90 protein structure analysis for drug targeting
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi Heat Shock Protein 90s: In Silico Analysis Reveals Unique Druggable Structural Features.
articles.authors: Daniyan MO, Singh H, Blatch GL
articles.details: An in silico study identified the predicted structure, function, and druggability of Plasmodium knowlesi heat shock protein 90s (PkHsp90s) using protein sequence/structural analyses, phylogenetics, and docking studies. Four isoforms were identified (cytosol, endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondrion, apicoplast) with key structural differences from human Hsp90s. The cytosolic PkHsp90 showed a predicted straight glycine-rich helix loop (GHL) motif forming a plasmodial-specific hydrophobic extension. Virtual screening identified three compounds (ZINC22007970, ZINC724661072, ZINC724661078) predicted to bind strongly to the GHL pocket of PkHsp90 with weak or no binding to human Hsp90s, supporting rational drug design for zoonotic malaria.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria species circulation in West Africa
articles.title_original: Circulation of non-falciparum malaria species in West Africa: A literature review from 2000 to 2024.
articles.authors: Garba MN, Moustapha LM, Diallo MA, Diongue K, Seck MC, Doutchi M, Ndiaye M, Ibrahim ML, Ndiaye D, Badiane AS
