articles.details: A literature review of 31 articles on non-falciparum malaria in West Africa (2000–2024) identified species prevalence from multiple studies: P. falciparum in 93.5% (n=29), P. malariae in 80.6% (n=25), P. ovale in 77.4% (n=24), and P. vivax in 35.5% (n=11) of reviewed papers. Diagnostic methods included RDTs in 54.8% of studies (70.6% PfHRP2-based, 29.4% Pf/Pan-based). The review emphasizes the need for more sensitive diagnostic techniques and modified control strategies to address asymptomatic Plasmodium infections in the region.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology and clinical features in Pernambuco, Brazil
articles.title_original: Clinical and epidemiological profiles of confirmed malaria cases in the state of Pernambuco, Brazil, from 2001 to 2022.
articles.authors: Guedes DL, Guimarães HMR, Padilha MCG, Pinto MCP, Silva MRND, Andrade EVO
articles.details: A descriptive observational study of 350 malaria cases in Pernambuco (2001–2022) found most patients were men (75.1%), Mixed-race (51.1%), median age 32 years. Travel and construction activities were primary exposure sources; most infections originated in Brazil (52%) or Angola (33%). Median diagnostic delay was 10 days for Indigenous individuals, 5 days for Black, 4 days for Mixed-race and White individuals. P. vivax and P. falciparum predominated at 49.7% and 47.5% respectively. Cases showed significant seasonal pattern with higher incidence in the first half of the year (p=0.0382). Multivariate analysis indicated complex factors beyond ethnicity and education drive diagnostic delays.

articles.headline: Free healthcare policy reduces malaria in Burkina Faso children
articles.title_original: Evaluating the effects of the free healthcare policy on clinical visits and malaria among children in Burkina Faso: a modeling study of past trends and future forecasts.
articles.authors: Ouédraogo AL, Offosse MJ, Zhang J, Toé S, Yaméogo P
articles.details: A modeling study evaluated Burkina Faso's 2016 free healthcare policy using data from 344,935 clinical visits across 199,664 children in 192 villages. Clinical visits increased 82% from 1.1 to 2.0 per child (2015–2018, p<0.001), with higher access within 5 km of facilities (2.2 vs 1.7 visits, p<0.001). Malaria prevalence decreased from 98% to 80% (p<0.001) and severe malaria from 6% to 2.2% (p<0.001). Bayesian forecasts predicted visits reaching 2.75 by 2020, malaria prevalence declining to 64%, and severe malaria to 1.02%, demonstrating transformative effects of removing financial barriers on malaria outcomes.

articles.headline: Red blood cells source affects P. falciparum growth variability
articles.title_original: Improving reproducibility of Plasmodium falciparum culture: a large-language-model-driven literature review and a parasite growth variability assessment of donor red blood cells.
articles.authors: Mahomed SM, Hanscheid T, Almeida Y, Grobusch MP, Rebelo M
articles.details: LLM-assisted systematic review of 963 articles (2000–2025) on RBC sourcing plus experimental analysis with P. falciparum strain 3D7 and RBCs from 69 malaria-naïve volunteers. Among 963 reviewed studies, 61.1% didn't specify donor origin, 72.6% omitted blood group info, only 6.0% reported anticoagulant. Parasite growth after 96 h varied 6.4-fold (range 3.0–19.3); low-growth volunteers had lower hemoglobin (12.3 vs 14.8 g/dL, p=0.03) and more anemia history (p=0.01). Authors propose STRuBeC reporting standard to improve reproducibility.

articles.headline: Machine learning reveals mitochondrial NATs in severe vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Integrative transcriptomic and machine learning approaches to decipher mitochondrial gene regulation in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Roy P, Aggarwal Y, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: Transcriptomic profiling integrated with machine learning decoded mitochondrial sense and antisense transcripts (NATs) in severe P. vivax malaria. NATs emerged as potent regulators tightly linked to mitochondrial pathways and translational machinery, reflecting parasite's strategy to fine-tune mitochondrial function under severe disease. Authors position mitochondrial NATs as potential antimalarial drug targets while noting findings are preliminary insights from limited clinical cohort requiring validation in larger populations.

articles.headline: Abbott RDT shows poor sensitivity in vivax malaria outbreak
articles.title_original: The underperforming Abbott-Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test: a whiter shade of pale - where the truth is not plain to see.
articles.authors: Thein KS, Soe K, Tun SKS, Kuipers R, Janssen S, Tarning J, Htun Y, Day NP, Kyaw TT, Dondorp AM, White NJ, Smithuis FM
articles.details: During P. vivax outbreak in Myanmar, Abbott-Bioline Ag P.f/P.v RDT compared with microscopy in 187 febrile persons. RDT detected 11 of 46 P. vivax cases confirmed by microscopy (sensitivity 0.24, 95% CI 0.12–0.36; specificity 1.0). Among 28 patients with ≥200/µL parasites (WHO detection limit), sensitivity 0.39 (95% CI 0.21–0.57). RDT colour bands often absent or very faint on ImageJ analysis. Authors conclude Abbott RDT is insensitive vs microscopy, causing dangerous treatment delays.

articles.headline: IgE antibodies show potential against malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Biophysical and functional characterization of IgE interactions with a malaria antigen.
articles.authors: Adam AI, Sutton BJ, McDonnell JM
articles.details: Study investigated IgE variant (CIS43ε) of neutralizing antibody binding to P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP). Surface plasmon resonance showed biphasic binding with higher affinity for core Peptide 21 than NANP repeats. Multimeric Peptide 21 induced significant degranulation of FcεRI-expressing cells, peaking at trimeric state. Full-length PfCSP triggered greater degranulation than tetrameric Peptide 21, highlighting importance of native antigen structure and multivalent interactions.

articles.headline: Aurora kinase inhibitors show multistage activity against falciparum
articles.title_original: Targeting Aurora Kinases as Essential Cell-Cycle Regulators to Deliver Multi-Stage Antimalarials Against Plasmodium Falciparum.
articles.authors: Langeveld H, Maepa K, Maree M, Thibaud JL, Salomane N, Bridgwater R, Famodimu MT, Godoy LC, Pasaje CFA, Boonyalai N, de Souza ML, Fong J, Rabie T, van der Watt M, Theart RP, Ghidelli-Disse S, Niles JC, Lee MCS, Winzeler EA, Delves MJ, Chibale K, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Birkholtz LM
articles.details: Study evaluated human Aurora kinase inhibitors targeting P. falciparum Aurora-related kinase (Ark) family members. Several AurB inhibitors exhibited multistage potency (<250 nM) against asexual blood stages, liver schizonts, and male gametes with >1000-fold selectivity. Hesperadin, TAE684, and AT83 were most potent compounds. PfArk1 identified as principal vulnerable target; inhibition prevents mitotic completion, causing unsegregated multi-lobed nuclei via aberrant microtubule organization.

articles.headline: PfARMA N-terminal region dominates antibody response but lacks protection
articles.title_original: The N-terminal region of malaria vaccine candidate Plasmodium falciparum asparagine-rich merozoite antigen is immunodominant and targeted by polyreactive antibodies.
articles.authors: Garza R, Marchioni JM, Honeycutt JD, Hurlburt NK, Torres C, Garcia A, Loranc E, Yemington E, Towers D, Ssewanyana I, Pancera M, Lavinder JJ, Jagannathan P, Greenhouse B, Bol S, Bunnik EM
articles.details: Study analyzed B cell and antibody responses to PfARMA vaccine candidate using samples from P. falciparum-exposed individuals. IgM+ B cells dominated response to intrinsically disordered region 1 (IDR1); human monoclonal antibodies to IDR1 were non-neutralizing or weakly neutralizing. PfARMA-specific plasma IgM and IgG predominately targeted IDR1 and increased with P. falciparum exposure. Antibody responses did not correlate with protection in age/exposure-matched children; ~30% of unexposed individuals also had polyreactive IgG to IDR1.

articles.headline: Gold miners achieve 82% accuracy with malaria self-test RDT
articles.title_original: Practicability of a malaria rapid diagnostic self-test among artisanal gold miners in French Guiana: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Le-Querriou R, Lambert Y, Petit-Sinturel M, Plessis L, Bardon T, Carboni C, Suarez-Mutis M, Vreden S, Muller J, Bordalo-Miller J, Sanna A, Douine M
articles.details: Cross-sectional study evaluated Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.f/P.v RDT practicability among artisanal small-scale gold mining workers in Oiapoque, Brazil (May–June 2024). 40 participants (median age 37, 27.5% no formal education) underwent Malakit training. Following training: knowledge 83.9%, practice 84.6% without assistance (100% with guidance), interpretation 77.2%; overall practicability score 81.9%. All RDTs yielded valid results; correct interpretation rates 96.0% for positive strips, 70.0% for negative, 65.8% for invalid strips.

articles.headline: Anopheles seasonality impacts species diversity in south Odisha
articles.title_original: Seasonal dynamics and species diversity of Anopheles mosquitoes in malaria endemic districts of Southern Odisha India.
articles.authors: Baig MM, Koppula DT, Panigrahi DK, Vijayakumar B, Choudhary D, Acharya P, Patnaik M, Gunasekaran K, Kumar A, Rahi M, Shriram AN
articles.details: Entomological survey March 2021–February 2023 across 11 districts in southern Odisha collected 10,807 Anopheles mosquitoes indoors/outdoors, revealing 18 species. An. subpictus predominant in summer 2021 (42.99%) and rainy season (46.60%); An. culicifacies (primary vector) present at 31.58% (rainy 2021) and 38.35% (rainy 2022); An. varuna peaked in winter 2021–2022 (35.56%) and 2022–2023 (25.18%). Ganjam district showed highest species diversity (1.26–2.2). Seasonal variation significantly impacted diversity (p<0.001) more than district-level factors.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria prevalence 3.4% in Ethiopian schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic Plasmodium infection and predictors among schoolchildren in Bahir Dar Zuria District, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Muhye A, Abate MA, Ayehu A, Dejen P
articles.details: School-based cross-sectional study March–May 2024 of 416 schoolchildren in Bahir Dar Zuria District, Northwest Ethiopia. Asymptomatic Plasmodium infection prevalence 3.4% (95% CI 1.7–6.3); P. falciparum 64.3% of cases. Predictors by multilevel logistic regression: age (AOR 3.53, p=0.001), gender (AOR 2.87, p=0.021), stagnant water near household (AOR 3.76, p=0.031), never used LLINs (AOR 2.90, p=0.007), malaria history past year (AOR 2.23, p=0.047). Asymptomatic children identified as significant transmission reservoirs requiring active case detection and improved LLIN coverage.

articles.headline: Malaria vectors distributed across Colombian ecoregions below 500m
articles.title_original: Ecoregional and altitudinal distribution of the principal malaria vectors in Colombia.
articles.authors: Carrasquilla MC, González R, Pineda SR, Ocampo CB, Montoya-Lerma J, Pareja-Loaiza P, Ahumada ML
articles.details: Database study of principal malaria vectors in Colombia using diverse sources 1929–2023 compiled 2,371 records for An. albimanus, An. darlingi, An. nuneztovari s.s., An. neivai s.l., and An. calderoni. An. albimanus (Atlantic/Pacific coasts) and An. neivai s.l. (Pacific) highly represented in coastal regions; An. darlingi, An. nuneztovari s.s., An. calderoni mainly inland. An. darlingi highly associated with forests. Vectors predominantly distributed below 500 m elevation; An. albimanus and An. calderoni also present >1,000 m. An. albimanus and An. neivai s.l. show elevation increase through time.

articles.headline: Age and comorbidities predict malaria mortality in South Sudan
articles.title_original: A Cox Proportional Hazards Model Approach to Identifying Malaria Risk Factors in Children in South Sudan.
articles.authors: Jumi LGL, Mohmmed AOA
articles.details: Hospital-based retrospective study at Al Sabah Children's Hospital, Juba, South Sudan, January–December 2021 of 6,410 children (ages 1 month–15 years) with malaria (56.08% male, 4.73% mortality). Cox proportional hazards model: age increased death hazard (HR 1.0223, 95% CI 1.0158–1.0280); body weight decreased hazard (HR 0.8513, 95% CI 0.8146–0.8896); treatment with quinine vs artesunate reduced risk (HR 0.7107, 95% CI 0.5501–0.9183); absence of comorbidity reduced risk (HR 0.5530, 95% CI 0.4273–0.7157).

articles.headline: Daylight-adjusted surveillance needed for Mozambique malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Malaria Mosquito Host-Seeking Activity Times in Manhiça District, Rural Mozambique, and the Need to Better Match Entomological Surveillance Strategies to Daylight Cycles.
articles.authors: Jobe NB, Máquina M, Opiyo MA, Martí-Soler H, Malheia A, Marrenjo D, Cuamba N, Pino D, Saúte F, Paaijmans KP
articles.details: Study assessed daytime (06:00–18:00) host-seeking activity of malaria mosquitoes indoors/outdoors in two villages, Manhiça district, Mozambique. Most abundant species was An. tenebrosus; daytime host-seeking observed in only one An. ziemanni specimen collected between 16:00–18:00 (after sunset at 17:06 that day). Analysis of sunrise/sunset times across African longitudes/latitudes shows seasonal variation exceeds 2 hours. Authors conclude entomological surveillance strategies must adjust to actual daylight cycles and human behaviors to collect actionable malaria control data.

articles.headline: Hospital malaria trends show P. falciparum dominance, low mortality
articles.title_original: Malaria Trends, Burden, Seasonal Variation, and Interventions in Western Tigray, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Kassahun GB, Berhe AM, Brhanu MA, Aregawi BB
articles.details: A hospital-based retrospective study in Western Tigray, Ethiopia (2011-2019) analyzed 36,438 microscopically confirmed malaria cases. P. falciparum was predominant (22,621 cases, 62.1%). Hospital mortality was 50 cases (0.14%), with the highest deaths in those ≥15 years (38 cases, 0.10%). Cases showed significant fluctuation over time (p=0.001), though mortality trend was not significant (p=0.62). The study highlights malaria as a persistent health problem in Ethiopia.

articles.headline: Persistent malaria transmission in Meghalaya despite low prevalence
articles.title_original: Malaria risk, treatment-seeking, and prevention in Northeastern India: clinic-based surveillance of persistent transmission.
articles.authors: Lyngdoh P, Sarkar R, Wilson ML, Lyngdoh I, Kessler A, Carlton JM, Albert S
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study across five primary health centers in Meghalaya, northeastern India (2018-2021) tested 1,031 individuals with malaria-like symptoms. Prevalence was 4.4% (45/1,031), with P. falciparum comprising 60%. Regional variation: 1.8% in Khasi Hills to 12.3% in Garo Hills. Risk factors included prior malaria within 12 months (OR=9.2, p<0.001) and outdoor activities during peak mosquito hours (bedtime: OR=2.0, p=0.023; early morning: OR=3.1, p=0.010).

articles.headline: Dried blood spots preserve malaria diagnostics for four months
articles.title_original: The reliability of malaria rapid diagnostic test kit for detecting P. falciparum PfHRP2 in dried blood spot samples preserved under different conditions and durations.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Apekey J, Boatey CA, Banono E, Adu JO, Boakye AA
articles.details: A laboratory study evaluated PfHRP2 detection in dried blood spots (DBS) made from 60 blood samples under three storage conditions (room temperature 25°C, 6°C, -60°C) over 150 days. PfHRP2 was consistently detected from day 30 to 120 regardless of storage conditions. After 150 days, positivity rates declined to 90% (25°C), 88.3% (6°C), and 80% (-60°C, p=0.236). Room temperature proved cost-effective for preserving PfHRP2 for up to 120 days.

articles.headline: P. simium reference genome reveals zoonotic malaria diversity
articles.title_original: Novel zoonotic cases of Plasmodium simium from São Paulo with a reference genome of the Brazilian strain.
articles.authors: Manko E, Billows N, Higgins M, Ward D, Ibrahim A, Campino S, Di Santi SM, Clark TG
articles.details: Oxford Nanopore and Illumina sequencing generated a high-quality reference genome for P. simium (21.8 Mb) from a Brazilian strain. Comparative genomic analysis of 38 P. simium samples revealed divergence from P. vivax, including mitochondrial mutations and deletions in reticulocyte-invasion genes (RBP2a, DBP) and variants in blood-stage antigens (msp3.1, msp3.2). Analysis indicated unique signatures of selection and host adaptation, advancing understanding of genomic diversity in this zoonotic parasite.

articles.headline: Intradermal malaria vaccination induces regulatory immune responses
articles.title_original: Intradermal immunization with Plasmodium berghei late-arresting genetically attenuated sporozoites induces PD-L1 expression on regulatory macrophages and dendritic cells.
articles.authors: van Schuijlenburg R, Naar CM, de Bes-Roeleveld HM, Chevalley-Maurel S, Zigó LA, Houlder EL, Lambooij JM, Baalbergen E, Roestenberg M, Franke-Fayard B
articles.details: A P. berghei rodent model compared intradermal (ID) versus intravenous (IV) delivery of late-arresting genetically attenuated sporozoite (GA2) vaccine. ID immunization resulted in lower frequencies of tissue-resident memory CD8+ T cells in spleen (1.3%), lungs (8.1%, p=0.005), and liver (2.5%), with reduced activation markers. Critically, liver cytotoxic functions were impaired: Granzyme B (p<0.0001) and perforin (p<0.0001) were significantly decreased. ID vaccination induced a regulatory myeloid phenotype with high PD-L1 expression, suggesting systemic immunosuppression.

articles.headline: Monthly DP+primaquine superior to reactive screening for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Evaluating malaria elimination strategies among military forces in Cambodia: a multi-arm clinical trial comparing monthly prophylaxis and focused screening and treatment.
articles.authors: Wojnarski M, Chaudhury S, Boonchan T, Bun R, Chann S, Gosi P, Soveasna K, Song S, Buathong N, Ittiverakul M, Sriwichai S, Arsanok M, Kuntawunginn W, Saingam P, Chaisatit C, Ponlawat A, Fansiri T, Vanachayangkul P, Jaichapor B, Sinoun M, Chuor CM, Kheangheng T, So M, Wanja E, Davidson S, Spring M, Rekol H, Dysoley L, Saly K, Livezey JR, Lin JT, Smith PL, Satharath P, Manning JE, Sok S, Saunders DL
articles.details: A multi-arm clinical trial in Cambodia enrolled 1,050 military volunteers comparing monthly malaria prophylaxis (MMP) versus focused screening and treatment (FSAT) over six months. MMP consisted of three monthly three-day doses of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine plus 22.5 mg weekly primaquine for 12 weeks. P. falciparum positivity was reduced 90% in MMP cohorts (10% to 1%, ARR 9%), though 32% recrudesced and required rescue treatment. In FSAT cohorts, P. falciparum declined 66% (7.6% to 2.7%, ARR 4.9%). P. vivax dropped from 9% to 0% at three months in MMP but rebounded to 6.7% at six months, while FSAT showed no significant reduction. The 22.5 mg weekly primaquine regimen was safe, including in G6PD-deficient individuals (15% of cohort).

articles.headline: Plasmepsin V validated as antimalarial drug target
articles.title_original: Deconvolution of the On-Target Activity of Plasmepsin V Peptidomimetics in Plasmodium falciparum Parasites.
articles.authors: Su W, Nguyen W, Siddiqui G, Dziekan JM, Marapana D, Penington JS, Mehra S, Razook Z, McCann K, Ngo A, Jarman KE, Barry AE, Papenfuss AT, Gilson PR, Creek DJ, Cowman AF, Sleebs BE, Dans MG
articles.details: In vitro study employed chemoproteomic approaches to demonstrate that plasmepsin V (PMV) peptidomimetic inhibitors directly engage their target in P. falciparum parasites. Whole-genome sequencing of parasites with reduced sensitivity to peptidomimetics identified a single nucleotide variant in the pmv gene, which was validated using reverse genetics to confirm its role in mediating resistance. These findings provide evidence that PMV is a viable antimalarial drug target and that peptidomimetics exert activity through direct PMV inhibition.

articles.headline: Humanized P450 mice improve antimalarial drug metabolism prediction
articles.title_original: Humanization of Drug Metabolism in the Plasmodium berghei Mouse Model for Antimalarial Drug Discovery.
articles.authors: MacLeod AK, Merino C, Viera-Morilla S, Frame L, Tavendale A, Duncan C, Ferguson L, Pinto EG, Simeons FRC, Gomez-Jimenez V, García AB, Riley J, Shishikura Y, Martínez-Martínez MS, Wolf CR, Read KD
articles.details: A preclinical study characterized the '8HUM' mouse line, extensively genetically humanized for cytochrome P450 enzymes and their transcriptional regulators, for antimalarial drug discovery. P. falciparum infection could not be established in 8HUM/Rag2-/- mice despite engraftment of human erythrocytes, but P. berghei infection was successful. Approved antimalarials were more metabolically stable in 8HUM than wild-type mice, and major species differences between humans and mice in metabolism of quinine derivatives were eliminated. The 8HUM P. berghei model thus provides improved translational accuracy for early antimalarial drug discovery.

articles.headline: Plant essential oils show promise as mosquito repellents
articles.title_original: Spatial Exposure Responses of Malaria Vectors to Eucalyptus grandis (W. Hill ex Maiden) and Cymbopogon citratus (DC.) Stapf Essential Oils.
articles.authors: Kaddumukasa MA, Mutekanga NM, Kula F, Batume C, Kato AB
articles.details: A laboratory study examined spatial responses of female An. gambiae (Kisumu strain) to essential oils from Eucalyptus grandis and Cymbopogon citratus using Y-tube olfactometry. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry analysis identified main constituents: L-α terpineol and Eucalyptol (Eucalyptus) and Lavandulol methyl ether and citral (Cymbopogon). Results demonstrated mosquito repellency, suggesting potential for development as botanical spatial repellents for vector control.

articles.headline: P. vivax manipulates mosquito sterol metabolism to enhance transmission
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax infection-driven modulation of sterol carrier protein reveals a metabolic link to reproductive physiology in Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Kumari S, Yadav P, Sankhala N, Rani J, Sharma G, Chauhan C, Tevatiya S, Dixit R
articles.details: A molecular study demonstrated that Plasmodium vivax infection modulates sterol carrier protein (SCP) expression in Anopheles stephensi mosquitoes, with upregulation in midgut and salivary glands but suppression in hemocytes. SCP transports blood-derived sterols essential for mosquito reproduction. RNAi-mediated knockdown of SCP significantly reduced mosquito fecundity, highlighting its role in reproductive physiology. The parasite-driven modulation of SCP may facilitate parasite growth and transmission.

articles.headline: Splenic CD103+ T cells show limited activation during malaria infection
articles.title_original: Characteristics of CD103(+)CD8(+) T cells in the spleen of Plasmodium yoelii NSM-infected mice.
articles.authors: Pan X, Shi F, Tang S, Liu M, Pan L, Liang G, Li L, Xie H, Zhao S, Huang J
articles.details: A murine P. yoelii NSM infection model used single-cell RNA sequencing to characterize CD103+CD8+ T cells in the spleen. These cells were present in naïve mice but rapidly declined in frequency and absolute number following infection. CD103+CD8+ T cells exhibited higher CD62L expression and lower CD44, CD69, and TIGIT levels compared to CD103-CD8+ T cells, and rarely produced IFN-γ or Granzyme B upon stimulation. Transcriptomic analysis revealed enrichment of 'cytoplasmic translation' and 'ribosome biosynthesis' pathways, consistent with a pre-activation state. Bioinformatics and reporter assays suggested LEF1 transcription factor regulates Itgae expression.

articles.headline: Malaria remains Uganda's leading febrile illness despite 4% decline
articles.title_original: Spatial and Temporal Distribution of Febrile Illnesses in Uganda: Case Studies of Brucellosis, Malaria and Typhoid Fever (2016-2023).
articles.authors: Aceng FL, Arinaitwe ES, Muruta A, Deza-Cruz I, Ekiri AB
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of national disease surveillance data (2016-2023) examined spatial and temporal distribution of malaria, typhoid, and brucellosis in Uganda. Over the eight-year period, malaria cases totaled 110,134,705 with prevalence declining 4% (43,316 to 29,271 per 100,000). The northern region showed the highest malaria prevalence while central region had the lowest. Malaria remained the dominant febrile illness compared to typhoid (1,572,162 cases) and brucellosis (361,563 cases), which each showed 2% and 8% declines respectively.

articles.headline: PMC acceptability influenced by perceived effectiveness and health worker attitudes
articles.title_original: Investigating factors enabling the acceptability of perennial malaria chemoprevention implementation in Osun State, Nigeria: evidence from a qualitative process evaluation.
articles.authors: Ekholuenetale M, Aransiola JO, Ujuju C, Ogbulafor N, Oroge O, Garba RA, Rahman SA, Maxwell K, Awosanya RA, Adekola MA, Ilesanmi BB, Ajayi A, Ogundun OA, Oresanya O, Tibenderana JK
articles.details: Qualitative study using focus groups and key informant interviews with caregivers, community leaders, fathers, and health workers regarding perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) acceptability in Osun State, Nigeria. Acceptability was positively influenced by perceived effectiveness, child-friendliness, free services, and delivery through the expanded programme on immunization (EPI) platform. Negatively influenced by lack of transportation funds, fear of side effects, and health worker attitudes. Religious leaders accepted PMC as not contradicting their faith.

articles.headline: High malaria vaccine acceptance despite limited prior awareness in Sudan
articles.title_original: Awareness and acceptance of the malaria vaccine among caregivers attending primary health care centres in Sudan: a mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Hamid A, Elsir R, Abbas E, Ibrahim N, Ahmed F, Mohamed S, Adam M, Abdalla R, Abuzaid M, Alameen F, Ahmed M, Bakhit A, Abdalla E
articles.details: Cross-sectional mixed-methods study of 159 caregivers in Sudan (November 2024–January 2025). Vaccine acceptance was high at 86.2% despite only 11.3% prior awareness. Acceptance was significantly associated with perceived malaria severity (p<0.001), caregivers' own vaccination history (p=0.034), and children's complete immunization (p=0.047). Qualitatively, vaccine safety concerns and healthcare system skepticism were main hesitancy drivers.

articles.headline: Behavioral ITN interventions critical for countries to reach malaria elimination targets
articles.title_original: Insecticide-treated net use and elimination of malaria in Sub-Saharan African countries: assessing the Global Technical Strategy using an evolutionary game approach.
articles.authors: Laxmi, Oraby T, Tyshenko MG, Danquah I, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: Behavior-incidence model and statistical analysis of malaria case reports and insecticide-treated net (ITN) usage data from Sub-Saharan African countries. Countries classified by transmission rates, mosquito prevalence, and socioeconomic conditions. Some countries can meet Global Technical Strategy 2025/2030 targets through ongoing ITN efforts; others require enhanced social awareness campaigns, economic assistance, and improvements in ITN efficacy to achieve malaria elimination.

articles.headline: P. falciparum incidence fell 93.9%; geostatistical model identifies persistent high-risk villages
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal trends in P. falciparum malaria and identification of high-risk villages in Eastern Myanmar: an 8-year observational study.
articles.authors: Rae JD, Parker DM, Maude RJ, Thu AM, Devine A, Nosten FH, Simpson JA
articles.details: Geostatistical model analysis of routine surveillance data (2014–2021) from 507 village-based malaria posts in Hpapun Township, Myanmar. P. falciparum incidence decreased 93.9% between 2014–2019. Intervention villages had significantly higher pre-intervention incidence (incidence rate ratio 4.72, 95% CI 4.56–4.90) and lower post-intervention incidence (IRR 0.26, 95% CI 0.24–0.27). In 2021, 12 high-risk villages were identified, with 8 persistently high-risk (≥90th percentile for ≥6 months).

articles.headline: RDT performance varies widely due to HRP2 polymorphism and antigen persistence
articles.title_original: Performance and challenges of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in endemic regions of Africa.
articles.authors: Ojeniyi FD, Ayoola AO, Ibitoye O, Opaleye OO, Olowe OA, Ehigie LO, Thomas BN, Ojurongbe O
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis pooling data from 48 studies evaluating rapid diagnostic test (RDT) diagnostic performance across sub-Saharan Africa. Results revealed considerable variability influenced by antigen persistence, cross-reactivity, and genetic polymorphism in the HRP2 gene, leading to false positives and negatives. Findings emphasize need for region-specific diagnostic strategies and advanced tools capable of detecting low-level parasitemia and differentiating between Plasmodium species.

articles.headline: kelch13 R622I widespread in Ethiopia; emerging link with HRP2-RDT failure
articles.title_original: High prevalence and emerging positive association of kelch13 R622I and HRP2-based RDT negativity in Plasmodium falciparum in northern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Zeleke AJ, Fola AA, Tollefson GA, Niaré K, Leonetti A, Taropawala O, Marglous J, Crudale R, Brhane BG, Assefa A, Kiyuka P, Parr JB, Hailu A, Tegegne MA, Bailey JA
articles.details: Genomic surveillance of 903 P. falciparum infections in northwestern Ethiopia using molecular inversion probe panels targeting drug-resistance mutations. R622I mutation found in 44.3% overall (52% in Gondar Zuria vs 35% in Tach Armachiho; p<0.001). HRP2-based RDT negativity prevalent at 39.3% overall (48.7% vs 27.6%; p<0.001). Co-occurrence of R622I and HRP2-RDT negativity in 22% of samples, significantly higher in R622I mutants than wild-type (48.3% vs 30.7%; p<0.05). C580Y detected at 0.4%, first report in Horn of Africa.

articles.headline: An. funestus replacement drove malaria resurgence in Tororo; much higher transmission risk
articles.title_original: Disentangling the roles of different vector species during a malaria resurgence in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: McClure M, Oruni A, Arinaitwe E, Musiime A, Kyagamba P, Otto G, Adiga J, Rwatooro JA, Kilama M, Krezanoski P, Briggs J, Rosenthal PJ, Nankabirwa JI, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: Cohort study of 422 children in 94 households across Tororo and Busia Districts, Uganda (2021–2023) with passive and monthly active P. falciparum surveillance by qPCR and biweekly mosquito collections. Malaria resurgence in Tororo was temporally associated with replacement of An. gambiae by An. funestus following indoor residual spraying formulation changes. Each doubling of annualized household entomologic inoculation rate (aEIR) increased P. falciparum hazard by 29% (adjusted HR 1.29, 95% CI 1.25–1.33), primarily driven by An. funestus (HR 1.29) versus An. gambiae (HR 1.04).

articles.headline: High pyrethroid/DDT resistance widespread in Ghana; L1014F mutations prevalent
articles.title_original: Trends and patterns of insecticide susceptibility of Anopheles gambiae between 2015 and 2020: implications for malaria vector control interventions in Ghana.
articles.authors: Asafu-Adjaye A, Chabi J, Malm K, Akrofi OO, Coleman S, Dery DB, Yihdego Y, Frempong KK, Pi-Bansa S, Asigbaase MA, Pwalia R, Joannides J, Sakyi KY, Akuoko OK, Osei JHN, Athinya DK, Baffoe-Wilmot A, Bart-Plange C, Boakye DA, Dadzie SK
articles.details: Annual WHO insecticide susceptibility tube assays on Anopheles gambiae from 30 sentinel sites in Ghana (2015–2020) using seven insecticides across four classes. High resistance to pyrethroids and DDT detected in all sites with decreasing susceptibility over the period. PCR detection of kdr-west (L1014F) and acetylcholinesterase (ace-1 G119S) mutations showed L1014F significantly more prevalent with site variations. Widespread resistance confirmed to deltamethrin, permethrin, DDT, malathion, pirimiphos-methyl, bendiocarb, and propoxur.

articles.headline: Diagnostic delay increased severe malaria risk; 24-hour diagnosis prevented complications
articles.title_original: Diagnostic delay inducing severe imported falciparum malaria: a time-sensitivity comparison of two imported cases during COVID-19 co-circulation.
articles.authors: Gong QH, Wan SB
articles.details: Case reports of two male patients with imported P. falciparum malaria in Shanghai, China (2021). Case 1 experienced 96-hour diagnostic delay and developed severe falciparum malaria with delirium, thrombocytopenia (33×10⁹/L), and acute kidney injury (creatinine 278 μmol/L) requiring intensive care. Case 2 was diagnosed within 24 hours via rapid diagnostic test and microscopy, received immediate artesunate treatment, and did not develop severe complications.

articles.headline: High SP resistance markers in Nigeria; no artemisinin-resistance mutations detected
articles.title_original: Molecular epidemiology of Plasmodium falciparum drug resistance and vaccine targets in high-transmission settings in Africa.
articles.authors: Aliyu IA, Bala JA, Musa BM, Amole TG, Ahmed AB, Kovalenko G, Umar A, Garba Y, Gwarzo SA, Mansur A, Hidaya MG, Aliyu MH, Galadanci HS
articles.details: Oxford Nanopore amplicon sequencing of five drug-resistance genes (crt, dhfr, dhps, mdr1, k13) and vaccine-target csp in P. falciparum isolates from northern Nigeria. High prevalence of dhfr-IRNI (90%) and dhps-SGKAA (45%) markers of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) resistance; no dhfr/dhps quintuple/sextuple mutations associated with SP-IPTp failure detected. Pfcrt-76T persisted at ~25%; no WHO-validated Pfk13 mutations for artemisinin resistance detected, though novel nonsynonymous variants identified. Geographic analysis showed localized clustering of resistance haplotypes in Kano and Yobe.

articles.headline: Blood group antigens modulate parasite invasion and disease severity
articles.title_original: Blood group antigens and malaria susceptibility.
articles.authors: Liu FF, Liang J, Gao M, Li K
articles.details: Review article synthesizing evidence on how erythrocyte surface molecules (ABO, Duffy, MNS, Gerbich, Knops, Diego blood group antigens) shape P. falciparum parasite invasion, cytoadherence, immune evasion, and disease severity. Discusses molecular mechanisms affecting malaria susceptibility, population heterogeneity in parasite-host adaptations and counter-adaptations, and clinical implications for risk stratification, transfusion safety, and vaccine design.

articles.headline: Healthcare-associated P. falciparum malaria from blood-borne transmission; no further spread
articles.title_original: Case Report and Epidemiological Investigation of Healthcare-associated Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Transmission in Westchester County, New York-2023.
articles.authors: White JL, Southwick K, Madison-Antenucci S, Piedmonte NP, Gayen S, Clemons B, Quinn M, Barrett K, Backenson PB, Antwi M, Slavinski S, Lee E, Huang A, Smith K, Guzzardi E, Hunter JC, McKinney KL, Mace KE, Ridpath AD, Williams S, Barratt JLN, Jacobson D, Pierre-Louis E, Bajic M, Kelley J, McElroy PD, Raphael BH, Bulbin A
articles.details: Case report and epidemiological investigation of 65-year-old resident of Westchester County, New York diagnosed with P. falciparum on October 2, 2023 with no recent travel history. Extensive epidemiological investigation and genetic analysis implicated blood-borne transmission in a healthcare setting from a concurrently hospitalized traveler. The mechanism remained unknown but was likely due to a lapse in infection control. No mosquito-transmitted or additional healthcare-associated cases were identified.

articles.headline: Suriname malaria elimination strategy adaptable to Amazon regions
articles.title_original: Is malaria elimination in the Amazon rainforest feasible? The case of Suriname.
articles.authors: Vreden S, Eersel M, Bretas G, Cairo H, van Eer E, Adhin M
articles.details: This opinion paper summarizes a malaria elimination approach implemented in both stable and nonstable communities within Suriname, comprising robust surveillance, maximized access to early diagnosis and treatment, retreatment strategies, and widespread insecticide-treated net distribution supported by sustained community engagement. The authors believe similar strategies could be effectively adapted elsewhere in Amazon regions.

articles.headline: Burundi malaria prevention perceptions guide multifaceted control approach
articles.title_original: Understanding perceptions of malaria control measures in Burundi: a qualitative descriptive study using a socio-ecological approach.
articles.authors: Niyonsaba T, Ndayisaba R, Dumont C, Muhoza C, Lampaert E, Alfred AD, Pellecchia U, Uwimana F, Bucumi A, Irabaheta N, Marisasi D, Bigirimana I, Ndayiragije S, Rwimo A, Nishemezwe D, Nimubona D, Niyomwungere L, Ndihokubwayo F, Iradukunda O, Irambona E, Nicobaharaye C, Sindayikengera J, Van Bortel W, Colombe S, Meudec M
articles.details: A qualitative descriptive study conducted with 60 individual interviews and 28 focus group discussions of farmers explored perceptions of long-lasting insecticidal nets, indoor residual spraying, intermittent preventive treatment during pregnancy, and environmental management across three interconnected levels: intrapersonal (perceived needs and adverse effects), organizational (information dissemination and healthcare facility conditions), and policy/environmental factors. The authors conclude effective malaria prevention requires integrating communication, contextual adaptation, adverse-effect management, local priorities, and socio-economic considerations.

articles.headline: Engineered mosquitoes block malaria strain development
articles.title_original: Genetically engineered mosquitoes block development of circulating malaria strains.
articles.details: Title indicates research on genetically engineered mosquitoes that block development of malaria strains. No additional details available.

articles.headline: Plasmodium inositol polyphosphate multikinase identified as antimalarial drug target
articles.title_original: Unveiling the Plasmodium inositol (pyro)phosphate pathway: Highlighting inositol polyphosphate multikinase as a novel therapeutic target for malaria.
articles.authors: Obuobi A, Quashie NB, Duah-Quashie NO, Sayers JR
articles.details: This exploratory study used in silico and molecular approaches to investigate the Plasmodium falciparum inositol phosphate pathway as a potential source for novel antimalarial drugs with different mechanisms of action to address drug resistance. Experimental validation across laboratory strains and a clinical isolate confirmed the p.Pro375Gln substitution in IPMK1, while the authors identified P. falciparum inositol polyphosphate multikinase (IPMK) as a promising drug target due to its unique sequence and structural characteristics.

articles.headline: Angola's RDT limitations require microscopy and molecular diagnostics
articles.title_original: The risks of exclusive reliance on rapid diagnostic tests for malaria in Angola.
articles.authors: Chitumba HO
articles.details: This commentary discusses malaria diagnosis in Angola, noting that reliance on rapid diagnostic tests limits species identification and parasite quantification. The authors highlight the need to strengthen microscopy capacity and integrate molecular diagnostic methods to improve clinical management, surveillance capacity, and detection of emerging drug resistance.

articles.headline: Transcription-mediated amplification screening prevented transfusion malaria in Florida
articles.title_original: Heightened Nontesting Risk Mitigation Blood Donor Screening with Retrospective Transcription-Mediated Amplification Testing during a 2023 Autochthonous Florida Malaria Cluster.
articles.authors: Sullivan DJ, Lanteri MC, Bres V, Hanhan M, Pagan M, Rey A, Thomas K, Reik RA
articles.details: In response to an autochthonous Plasmodium vivax outbreak affecting seven individuals, the local blood center conducted retrospective transcription-mediated amplification nucleic acid testing on 436 donor samples (258 from Sarasota zip codes, 178 from Miami zip codes) collected July–October, with a limit of detection of 2–7 infected erythrocytes per milliliter. All donor samples tested nonreactive, and no transfusion-transmitted malaria cases were reported.

articles.headline: Adjuvants enhance antibody responses to nanoparticle malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: A Comparative Evaluation of Adjuvants for Enhancing Antibody Responses to a Nanoparticle-Based Malaria Vaccine.
articles.authors: Nepal Y, Francian A, Roberts BT, Khan R, Wondra G, Demierre A, Apel F, Chackerian B
articles.details: This study evaluated multiple adjuvant classes to enhance immunogenicity of a virus-like particle-based vaccine targeting Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (CSP) epitopes. Five formulations significantly increased anti-CSP IgG titers, with several also enhancing antibody durability, identifying promising adjuvants that enhance both the magnitude and longevity of vaccine-induced immunity.

articles.headline: P. falciparum microhaplotypes estimate malaria prevalence but not incidence
articles.title_original: Accuracy of Plasmodium falciparum genetic data for estimating parasite prevalence and malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiyaga S, Mbabazi M, Katairo T, Kabbale KD, Asua V, Nsengimaana B, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Mwubaha C, Nakasaanya J, Watyekele E, Ayitewala A, Tukwasibwe S, Mulondo J, Nsobya SL, Agaba B, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Kamya MR, Kateete DP, Nabende JN, Jjingo D, Mboowa G, Batte C, Ssewanyana I, Aranda-Díaz A, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ, Conrad M, Greenhouse B, Briggs J
articles.details: A study analyzed dried blood spots from 3,563 symptomatic P. falciparum cases collected across 26 sentinel health facilities in Uganda during 2023. Amplicon deep sequencing of 165 polyallelic microhaplotypes revealed marked geographic heterogeneity: parasite prevalence ranged from 5.0% to 49.23% and incidence from 91–1,062 and 33–1,667 cases per 1,000 person-years. Complexity of infection (COI) and effective COI (eCOI) were strongly positively associated with prevalence (nested grouped cross-validation r=0.79), while between-host relatedness predicted incidence (r=0.37), indicating microhaplotype-based diversity metrics reliably reflect spatial prevalence variation but have limited ability to predict incidence.

articles.headline: Plasmodium NEK4 kinase regulates meiosis and zygote development
articles.title_original: A divergent Plasmodium NEK4 acts as a key regulator driving the early events of meiosis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Hair M, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Brady D, Pasquarello C, Bottrill A, Bhanvadia S, Neal A, Tromer EC, Le Roch KG, Hainard A, Holder AA, Vaughan S, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: This study identified Plasmodium berghei NEK4, a divergent NIMA-related kinase, as a key regulator coupling meiotic initiation with zygote morphogenesis. Using ultrastructure expansion microscopy, NEK4 was shown to accumulate at the microtubule-organizing center and apical polar complex shortly after fertilization. Deletion of nek4 resulted in complete developmental arrest with blocked MTOC duplication, microtubule formation, chromatin condensation, nuclear migration, and cell polarity establishment, with transcriptomic and phosphoproteomic analyses revealing collapsed regulatory networks governing meiosis and cytoskeletal organization.

articles.headline: Fractional-order model captures malaria transmission memory and routes
articles.title_original: Analytical and data-driven fractional-order malaria transmission model with vector and non-vector pathways.
articles.authors: Ahman QO, Okpara PA, Agbata BC, Senewo EO, Didigwu NE
articles.details: The authors developed a Caputo fractional-order malaria model integrating both vector and non-vector transmission pathways while embedding memory effects. Analytical properties including positivity, boundedness, and disease-free equilibrium were derived, with the Adams–Bashforth–Moulton predictor–corrector scheme used for numerical simulation. Numerical experiments showed that decreasing the fractional order substantially modifies dynamics by delaying epidemic peaks, prolonging oscillatory persistence, and amplifying long-term infection memory; parameter estimation using weekly malaria incidence data from Nigeria validated the model's ability to reproduce outbreak patterns.

articles.headline: Nigeria RDT meta-analysis shows suboptimal diagnostic performance
articles.title_original: Performance of rapid diagnostic tests for malaria diagnosis in Nigeria: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Ismail M, Mukhtar A, Odetokun IA, Ibrahim MA, Salman AA, Bawa-Sani HG, Nass SS, Goni BW, Nuhu A, Zhang X, Usman A, Isah MB
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 studies evaluated the diagnostic performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) compared to microscopy in Nigeria. The pooled sensitivity was 69.7% (95% CI 67.7–71.6%), specificity was 81.5% (95% CI 79.9–83.1%), and diagnostic odds ratio was 21.87 (95% CI 7.45–64.21). The authors concluded that RDTs in Nigeria show suboptimal sensitivity and specificity compared with WHO benchmarks, and while RDTs remain practical due to accessibility, integrated diagnostic algorithms combining RDTs with microscopy or molecular methods are needed.

articles.headline: Machine learning designs multiepitope PfCSP vaccine targeting L9
articles.title_original: Machine learning enables de novo multiepitope design of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein to target trimeric L9 antibody.
articles.authors: Nelson JAD, Garfinkle SE, Lin ZJ, Park J, Kim AJ, Bayruns K, McCanna ME, Konrath KM, Agostino CJ, Kulp DW, Odom John AR, Pallesen J
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: The authors developed MESODID, a machine-learning-driven structural antigen design pipeline, to create antigens presenting multiple copies of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) minor repeat epitopes capable of binding the protective monoclonal antibody L9. Cryo-EM structure of their top-performing design, M-TIM, at 3.6 Å resolution demonstrated successful orientation of three L9-bound epitope copies, effectively recapitulating critical homotypic antibody interactions. This approach has broad applicability for designing antigens incorporating repeated epitopes in challenging vaccine targets.

articles.headline: Nucleoside phosphonates as potential antimalarial inhibitors
articles.title_original: Acyclic Nucleoside Phosphonates As Potential Inhibitors of Plasmodium Falciparum Egress.
articles.authors: Cheviet T, Wein S, Lionne C, Badji Y, Venturini N, Cerdan R, Peyrottes S
articles.details: A study evaluated acyclic nucleoside phosphonates as novel antimalarial agents targeting parasite development. Two series of compounds incorporating structural modifications on purine scaffolds or acyclic chains were synthesized and tested against chloroquine-sensitive P. falciparum 3D7 strains. Several compounds demonstrated in vitro activity with IC50 values ranging from 11.3 to 38.8 µM; molecular docking revealed crucial interactions supporting biological data. The authors conclude this family represents promising antimalarial scaffolds warranting further optimization.

articles.headline: Environmental mapping of malaria risk in Ghana's Oti Region
articles.title_original: Mapping Malaria Susceptibility in the Oti Region of Ghana Using Remote Sensing and GIS: A Retrospective Observational Study.
articles.authors: Okyere G, Abakah A, Kwarteng A, Kwarteng EVS
articles.details: A retrospective observational study mapped malaria susceptibility across Oti Region using remote sensing and GIS. Malaria incidence was analyzed using spatial statistics; environmental factors were assessed with Boosted Regression Tree, Generalized Additive Model, and support vector machine approaches. Krachi East (91,376 cases) and Nkwanta South (73,592 cases) were identified as persistent hotspots over 3 years. Key environmental factors were elevation (27.60% feature importance), land surface temperature 22–32°C (25.48%), vegetation index (23.12%), and distance to rivers (20.00%), with conclusions supporting targeted control efforts addressing these environmental risk factors.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles gambiae in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Pyrethroid Resistance in Anopheles gambiae s.l.: A Focus on Permethrin and Deltamethrin for Malaria Vector Control.
articles.authors: Adesoye OA, Awoniyi AM, Adeogun AO
articles.details: A study assessed susceptibility of An. gambiae s.l. to permethrin and deltamethrin in Zuba, Nigeria, using CDC bottle bioassays. Permethrin at standard dose (21.5 µg/bottle) induced 84.4% mortality at 30 min (P=0.031) versus 72% at 15.0 µg/bottle, with complete mortality after 35 min. Deltamethrin at standard dose (12.5 µg/bottle) caused 76% mortality at 30 min (P=0.04) and 100% after 40 min. No mortality occurred at sub-lethal doses (≤0.8 µg/bottle) after 24 h (P=0.061). The authors conclude these findings highlight resistance levels and underscore the need for routine surveillance and tailored control strategies.

articles.headline: Smartphone location data identifies malaria transmission hotspots
articles.title_original: Smartphone-based retrospective analysis for malaria hotspot detection.
articles.authors: da Silva BM, Giddaluru J, Cardozo LE, Martins FM, Antolini APR, Bargieri DY, de Lacerda MVG, Monteiro WM, Sampaio VS, Nakaya HI
articles.details: A study leveraged anonymized location history from over 200 malaria-infected individuals via Google Takeout to identify transmission hotspots using a tool called Sickness Positioning System with density-based clustering. Field validation confirmed two previously unrecognized mosquito breeding sites as active transmission sources. Compared to Brazil's federal surveillance system (which updates annually), the system provided more precise and timely spatial assessment. The authors conclude this approach offers a cost-effective, objective, and timely method for identifying hotspots and facilitating targeted public health interventions.

articles.headline: Primaquine safety for P. vivax radical cure in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Haematologic responses to primaquine used for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax in public health facilities in Ethiopia: a prospective observational study.
articles.authors: Shifraw T, Abdelmenan S, Zewde A, Worku A, Abera A, Mohammed H, Etana K, Keating J, Chibsa S, Girma S, Teka H, Mumba P, Murphy M, Karmarkar E, Price RN, Hwang J, Berhane Y
articles.details: A prospective observational study (May 2019–June 2021) enrolled 879 patients with P. vivax and mixed malaria at four health centers in Ethiopia to assess safety of low-dose primaquine (0.25 mg/kg for 14 days) without prior G6PD testing. On day 3, median fractional haemoglobin fall was −0.9% (range −51.9 to 61.3); nine patients had drops >25%, two with levels below 8 g/dL. By day 13, 91.9% achieved haematological recovery; three female patients (1.0% of 298 tested with SD Biosensor) had intermediate G6PD deficiency. No serious adverse events occurred. Authors conclude the regimen was well tolerated with few mild adverse events, supporting Ethiopia's policy without prior testing if adverse-event monitoring systems are in place.

articles.headline: Vector behavior and human activity drive malaria transmission in Kenya highlands
articles.title_original: Human and vector behavioural determinants of malaria transmission in the Nandi highlands, western Kenya.
articles.authors: Machani MG, Sunkara A, Awandu SS, Ombok M, Ouko A, Abel L, Turnbull LB, Rodriguez I, Kim J, Clemens JC, Ayodo G, Ochomo EO, John CC, Lobo NF
articles.details: A study examined human-mosquito interactions in three ecological settings in Nandi highlands, Kenya, using human landing catches indoors/outdoors 18:00–06:00 over five nights in ten houses per site during long (May 2018) and short (October 2018) rainy seasons, with concurrent hourly human behavior observations. High Anopheles diversity was observed with site-specific dominance: An. arabiensis (Kipsamoite), An. christyi (Kebulonik), and novel An. spp. 14 (multiple sites). Outdoor exposure peaked 18:00–21:00 h; indoor exposure peaked 19:00–01:00 h coinciding with low LLIN use. LLINs prevented 24.5% (long rains) and 44.9% (short rains) of bites in Kipsamoite, 24.6–37% in Kebulonik, and 35.8% in Kapsisywa. Authors conclude human-vector interactions are shaped by temporal and spatial behavior, with highest biting for unprotected sleepers indoors and early-evening outdoor exposure, revealing residual transmission gaps despite net use.

articles.headline: Climate change and solar geoengineering reshape malaria in South Asia
articles.title_original: Malaria transmission dynamics under climate change and solar geoengineering in South Asia: a GLENS-based assessment.
articles.authors: Hussain A, Shoaib M, Latif M
articles.details: Using the VECTRI malaria model, this study simulated transmission dynamics 2020–2097 across Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Iran, India, Nepal, and Pakistan under GLENS-SAI solar geoengineering scenario (stabilizing 2020 temperatures) versus RCP 8.5 unmitigated warming. GLENS-SAI reduced malaria transmission intensity across South Asia with significant EIR declines in India, Nepal, Bangladesh, northern Pakistan, southern Iran, and the Afghanistan-Pakistan border. However, localized EIR increases were projected in southeastern Pakistan, western Afghanistan, north-central and eastern Iran, and northern Nepal driven by SAI-induced temperature and precipitation changes. The annual transmission cycle shortened in amplitude and duration across endemic areas. Authors conclude GLENS-SAI may reduce transmission across much of South Asia but localized increases highlight need for region-specific strategies.

articles.headline: Glycogen synthase kinase inhibitors target P. falciparum metabolism
articles.title_original: In silico investigation of novel Plasmodium Falciparum glycogen synthase kinase (pfGSk3β) inhibitors for the treatment of malaria infection.
articles.authors: Adebambo KF, Otify S
articles.details: An in silico study screened 53 GSK-3β inhibitors from the CHEMBL database via molecular docking, 100 ns molecular dynamics simulations, and MMPBSA analysis to identify potential antimalarials targeting pfGSK3β. Three compounds were identified as candidates: S20 (CHEMBLID 1910196), S39 (CHEMBLID 2321945), and S56 (CHEMBLID 2321951). Compound S56 showed superior performance to control ligand S1 with binding affinities of −5.64 versus −7.1 kcal/mol and lower RMSD (1.0 nm versus 4.5 nm) across 100 ns MD. MMPBSA analysis revealed comparable total energies: S56 at −14.45 kJ/mol and S1 at −13.03 kJ/mol. An in silico toxicity assessment indicated possible toxicity of repurposed compounds. Authors propose S20, S39, and S56 as potential anti-malaria drug candidates.

articles.headline: COVID-19 pandemic disrupted malaria control in Brazil
articles.title_original: Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on malaria transmission in the state of Pará, Brazil.
articles.authors: de Sá CAF, Sardinha DM, de Paula Souza E Guimarães RJ, Vallinoto ACR, Dos Santos EJM, Viana GMR, do Socorro Souza Chaves T
articles.details: An observational, descriptive, analytical study analyzed secondary malaria case data from state of Pará, Brazil, 2018–2023 stratified by pre-pandemic (2018–2019), critical period (2020–2021), and less critical period (2022–2023). Total cases: 170,245 (pre-pandemic: 79,125; critical: 44,830; less critical: 47,502). Cases among miners increased from 17.2% to 58%; P. falciparum cases rose from 2.6% to 11.3%. Disease seasonality was maintained at pre-COVID levels; peaks occurred August–November across 2018–2023. Authors attribute case reduction during critical period to disease control program actions, and the increase to resumed social interaction and internal mobility facilitated by COVID-19 vaccination.

articles.headline: CD44 cross-linking promotes P. falciparum red blood cell invasion
articles.title_original: CD44 cross-linking promotes Plasmodium falciparum invasion.
articles.authors: Kongsomboonvech AK, Scally SW, Le Guen Y, Valissery P, Salinas ND, Cowman AF, Tolia NH, Egan ES
articles.details: A molecular study identified CD44 as a host factor for P. falciparum invasion through forward genetic screening. Anti-CD44 monoclonal antibody BRIC 222 significantly promoted invasion through CD44 cross-linking, inducing phosphorylation changes in RBC cytoskeletal proteins and increasing accessibility of surface proteins including invasion receptor Basigin. Parasite ligand EBA-175, which interacts with CD44, similarly enhanced invasion and induced membrane changes. Both BRIC 222 and EBA-175 increased binding of the PfRH5/PCRCR complex to Basigin. Authors propose that CD44 cross-linking, potentially by EBA-175, coordinates and enhances ligand-receptor interactions and promotes host cytoskeleton signaling, making RBCs more permissive to invasion.

articles.headline: Genomic toolkit for malaria vector surveillance in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: A genomic toolkit for surveillance and elimination of the principal malaria vectors in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Dennis TPW, Vantaux A, Kobylinski KC, Alam MS, Witkowski B, Al-Amin HM, Lobo NF, Wilson AJ, Drury E, Goncalves S, Harding N, Clarkson CS, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Kwiatkowski D, Miles A, Donnelly MJ, St Laurent B
articles.details: This study released Adir1.0, a curated catalog of 540 An. dirus whole-genome sequences with cloud-based analysis via malariagen-data-python API, and updated Amin1.0 API for An. minimus. An. dirus and An. minimus are principal vectors in Southeast Asia inhabiting diverse ecological niches with variable insecticide resistance phenotypes. First exploratory population genomic analysis of An. dirus from Bangladesh, Thailand, and Cambodia revealed population structure, candidate physiological resistance regions, and genomic structural variation. These resources provide a foundation for genomic surveillance studies to inform vector control and malaria elimination strategies in Southeast Asia.

articles.headline: Ivermectin's role in malaria control
articles.title_original: Ivermectin's role in malaria control: the simplicity and the complexity.
articles.authors: Schwartz E, Orshan L, Schlagenhauf P
articles.details: No abstract provided; unable to extract specific study design, population, intervention, or quantitative results from title alone.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-derived formula modulates immune response in murine malaria
articles.title_original: Proteomics-driven screening of artemisinin-based combination ratios and mechanistic insights into Plasmodium berghei infection in mice.
articles.authors: Hao L, Sun J, Li J, Li Z, Yu Z, Huang H, Liu G, Shen Z, Huo H, Yuan Q, Li H, Huang L
articles.details: Screened antimalarial compound ratios in murine Plasmodium berghei model. Optimized formula comprising arteannuin B, artemisinic acid, and scopoletin. Results showed the formula strengthened host defense by modulating phagocytic activity in splenic macrophages, dendritic cells, and natural killer cells via Fcγ receptor-mediated pathways.

articles.headline: Host defense peptides target Plasmodium parasites and modulate immunity
articles.title_original: Host defense peptides in malaria infection: their contributions, significance and constraints.
articles.authors: Alfaki DA, Elbasheir MM
articles.details: Review of host defense peptides (HDPs)—defensins, cathelicidins, NK-2 peptide, platelet factor 4, macrophage inflammatory protein-3α, and hepcidin—in malaria defense. Describes direct antimicrobial activity against Plasmodium, immunomodulation of host responses, and roles at various parasite development stages. Addresses current limitations in understanding HDPs' full implications in malaria infection.

articles.headline: Northern Ghana malaria control strategies review and evidence gaps
articles.title_original: Malaria control in Northern Ghana: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Zankawah MM, Issakah MA, Yakubu E, Tiwaa-Boateng NA, Baatiema L, Moomin A, Banchani JS, Chimdi IE, Edusei AK, Ordinioha B
articles.details: Scoping review of 38 studies from Northern Ghana (2013–2023) examining malaria control interventions. Studies concentrated in Upper East region (44.7%) and focused on case management (28.9%), intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (23.7%), and long-lasting insecticidal nets (15.8%). Most studied pregnant women (19%), children (14%), and malaria patients (14.3%). Malaria vaccine research was least common (2.6%).

articles.headline: Inconsistent neonatal malaria treatment across case reports and series
articles.title_original: Management of malaria in newborns: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Sultanli A, Beck JS, Kremp M, Sondermann H, Bonitz M, Honkpehedji YJ, Remppis J, Adegnika AA, Esen M, Bélard S
articles.details: Systematic review of 26 publications (22 case reports, 3 case series, 1 cross-sectional) on antimalarial treatment in 40 neonates with confirmed malaria. Median age at symptom onset 16 days, diagnosis 19 days. Plasmodium falciparum 45%, P. vivax 43%. For P. falciparum: only 28% received artemisinin-based therapy; 56% received oral amodiaquine or chloroquine monotherapy. P. vivax mostly treated with oral chloroquine (64%). All survived with parasitaemia clearance in 2–7 days.

articles.headline: Vector control options for outdoor malaria transmission reduction
articles.title_original: Outdoor biting by malaria vectors: what are the options for intervention?
articles.authors: Braack L, Macdonald MB
articles.details: Literature review of intervention classes to reduce outdoor malaria vector biting in Asia-Pacific region. Evaluated approaches: attractive targeted sugar baits, endectocides, outdoor residual spraying, fogging, mass trapping, genetic modification, larval source management, repellents, raised platforms, and zooprophylaxy. Larval Source Management effective in some contexts (WHO-endorsed Supplemental Method). Spatial repellents received WHO recommendation for indoor use. Topical repellents effective short-term. Endectocides offer potential in focal locations. Gene drive a longer-term possibility.

articles.headline: Cambodia surveillance system strong performance despite elimination challenge
articles.title_original: Key findings and recommendations from assessment of Cambodia's national malaria surveillance system.
articles.authors: Sovannaroth S, Kugler AM, Ngor P, Brindle H, Chhun B, Filip E, Hem V, Khy V, Matoy RJJ, Ou V, Ran S, Ringwald P, Sok K, Yoem R, Zhang Z, Rekol H
articles.details: WHO Malaria Surveillance Assessment Toolkit evaluation of Cambodia's system using 2021–2023 data across 68 indicators in four domains. Performance: 82% (14/19) indicators met. Context/Infrastructure: 77% (8/13). Technical/Process: 79% (4/7). Behavior: 100% (3/3). Strengths: improved completeness and timeliness of reporting and case investigation. Key gaps: revising case investigation, data cleaning/verification, system upgrades for unique patient identifiers and interoperability. Cambodia reported 1,384 cases in 2023, aiming for elimination by 2025.

articles.headline: Alpha-gal antibodies associated with malaria protection in endemic regions
articles.title_original: Naturally acquired immune responses to alpha-gal in malaria endemic settings and pre-clinical efficacy testing with R21/MM.
articles.authors: Mukhopadhyay ES, Bellamy DG, Tinto H, Hamaluba M, Truby A, Salman AM, Hill AVS
articles.details: Assessed anti-α-gal IgG and IgM antibodies in UK sporozoite-challenged volunteers, Kenyan adults, and Burkina Faso infants. UK volunteers showed no significant antibody increase at 90 days post-challenge. Burkina Faso infants showed increased anti-α-gal IgM and IgG over time, with malaria protection correlating to higher IgM antibody levels (and possibly IgG). BSA-linked α-gal with Matrix-M adjuvant in mice elicited IgG/IgM response but failed to protect against sporozoite challenge.

articles.headline: Insecticide-treated nets remain critical but progress requires new approaches
articles.title_original: Advances in population-based interventions to control falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Glossop SE, Peto TJ, Adhikari B
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Review of population-level Plasmodium falciparum malaria prevention strategies. Insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) made the most substantial contribution to mortality reduction but face challenges with suboptimal distribution/access/use and emerging insecticide resistance. Chemoprevention protects tens of millions primarily in sub-Saharan Africa but threatened by drug resistance. Highlights R21/Matrix-M vaccine with greater efficacy, lower cost, and scalable production compared to RTS,S/AS01. Discusses field trials of monoclonal antibodies for seasonal chemoprophylaxis and potential of gene drives for vector eradication.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi causes submicroscopic infections and potential severe malaria
articles.title_original: Submicroscopic Burden of Zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi Malaria on Mursala Island and Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Transmission in Mainland North Sumatra, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Lubis IND, Permatasari R, Siahaan L, Cahyadi RAD, Nainggolan IRA, Syafutri RD, Sinambela MN, Jauharah S, Lestari A, Theodora M, Prameswari H, Piera KA, Barber BE, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ
articles.details: Molecular diagnostics study using ultrasensitive RT-PCR to detect malaria in febrile patients across three mainland districts and Mursala Island, North Sumatra. Among 64 Mursala Island participants: 10.9% had P. knowlesi including 5 agricultural workers and 2 with severe WHO-defined anemia; 3.1% had P. vivax. All were negative by microscopy and rapid diagnostic tests. Among 947 mainland participants: 26% had confirmed Plasmodium species (17.5% P. vivax, 7.5% P. falciparum) with 30% submicroscopic. No P. knowlesi detected in mainland. P. knowlesi transmission low but may cause severe malaria.

articles.headline: RDT diagnostic accuracy varies by transmission intensity and demographics
articles.title_original: Varying Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Accuracy by Regional Transmission Level and Demographics in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Wiener D, Seth MD, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Popkin-Hall ZR, Giesbrecht D, Bakari C, Lyimo B, Pereus D, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Sheahan TP, Parr JB, Bailey JA, Ishengoma DS, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Cross-sectional diagnostic accuracy study in Tanzania (2021) comparing three national standard malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs) and one research mRDT against qPCR reference. Found differences in test performance by age, sex, and regional malaria transmission intensity. Research mRDT underperformed with poor sensitivity across transmission strata. Results indicate transmission intensity affects mRDT agreement and diagnostic performance.

articles.headline: Metabolite profiling identifies uridine as potential malaria treatment supplement
articles.title_original: Temporal multi-tissue profiling reveals metabolites linked to immune response and malaria parasitemia control.
articles.authors: Teklemichael AA, Wu J, Bohrnsen E, Xu F, Stadler RV, Obiorah T, Brandes NT, LaCroix IS, Bosio CM, Schwarz B, Su XZ
articles.details: Experimental study infecting C57BL/6n mice with four P. yoelii strains and P. berghei ANKA. Blood, spleen, and liver samples collected days 4 and 6 post-infection; metabolites measured by LC-MS/MS. Tissue- and strain-specific metabolic alterations in glycolysis intermediates, amino acids, and nucleotides correlated with cytokine levels and parasitemia. Glycolysis intermediates negatively correlated with parasitemia. Uridine supplementation significantly reduced parasitemia and MCP-1 in P. berghei-infected mice and reduced P. falciparum parasitemia in vitro.

articles.headline: South Korea capital region malaria resurgence linked to post-COVID activities
articles.title_original: Individual Malaria Case and Case Cluster Status in the Capital Region of the Republic of Korea, 2024.
articles.authors: Ko M, Seo YJ, Kim H, Hwang K
articles.details: Epidemiological analysis of malaria in South Korea's capital region (2022–2024). In 2024, 620 cases reported (94.1% of national cases), representing 78.7% increase from 2022 but slight decrease from 2023. Identified 47 case clusters (10.6% in military units) concentrated near North Korean border in northwestern Gyeonggi, Ganghwa-gun, and Incheon. Primary risk factors: residence (57.8%), work (12.9%), military service in high-risk areas (12.9%), outdoor leisure. Post-COVID rise linked to resumption of social activities and enhanced surveillance.

articles.headline: Barriers to multiple first-line malaria therapies in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Barriers and facilitators to the adoption of multiple first-line therapies for management of uncomplicated malaria in Tanzania: a multi-method qualitative study.
articles.authors: Ntissi HH, Itaeli A, Sangeda RZ, Bwire GM
articles.details: Multi-method qualitative study with desk review of 2020-2024 national data and 12 semi-structured interviews examining adoption of multiple first-line therapies (MFT) for uncomplicated malaria in Tanzania. Malaria cases in Tanzania declined from 8.9 million to 7.2 million (2020-2024) before resurgence to 8.1 million in 2023; children under five represented 34% of cases and 46% of deaths, with case incidence declining significantly (p=0.011) but mortality unchanged (p=0.802). Artemether-lumefantrine comprised 73.5% of antimalarial imports versus 2.6% for dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. Key barriers identified: high cost of alternatives, limited provider training, and weak supply chains. Authors conclude MFT is promising strategy but implementation limited by financial constraints and import dependence.

articles.headline: Imported malaria surveillance reveals transmission drivers in Pemba
articles.title_original: Epidemiological and entomological analysis of imported malaria in Pemba Island, Zanzibar: a study from 2020-2022.
articles.authors: Ali AO, Ali AM, Mwema MF, Mlacha YP
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of surveillance data 2020-2022 in Pemba Island, Zanzibar, identified 2,646 total malaria cases of which 1,411 (53.3%) were imported, primarily from mainland Tanzania regions: Tanga (61%), Dar es Salaam (12%), Coastal Region (10%). Imported cases significantly higher in males (57.3%, p<0.0001) and adults over 18 years (68%, p<0.0001). Importation odds significantly higher in 2021 (OR=3.58, p<0.001) and 2022 (OR=5.62, p<0.001) relative to 2020. Human biting rate associated with imported cases (84.81-unit increase per HBR unit increase, 95% CI 13.29-156.32, p=0.021). Authors conclude imported malaria remains persistent barrier to elimination.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine uptake critically low in Somalia
articles.title_original: Factors associated with intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp-SP) use for malaria during pregnancy in Somalia: a multilevel analysis of the 2020 demographic and health survey.
articles.authors: Ali AO, Abdillahi HA, Nadarajah S, Muse AH, Hared YA
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 7,124 women aged 15-49 from 2020 Somalia Demographic and Health Survey found overall reported use of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP/Fansidar) during pregnancy only 6.3%, with 69% reporting no antenatal care visits, 22% reporting 1-3 visits, and 9% reporting 4+ visits. Paradoxically, women with 4+ ANC visits had significantly lower odds of reported SP use (AOR=0.38, 95% CI 0.25-0.58) compared to those with no visits. Nulliparous women had lower odds of SP use (AOR=0.6, 95% CI 0.4-0.9); nomadic women higher odds versus rural women (AOR=3.7, 95% CI 2.2-6.4). Community-level factors explained 7.4% of variance (ICC=7.4%). Authors conclude coverage falls critically short of WHO goals, fundamentally driven by low ANC attendance.

articles.headline: Machine learning improves malaria prediction in IDP settings
articles.title_original: From risk factors to predictive modelling: applying machine learning to childhood malaria surveillance in resource-limited settings.
articles.authors: Ashaolu JO, Akanji TS, Ayansola VI, Olawale-Succes OO, Sunday AJ, Some SYM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 693 under-5 children in Nigerian internally displaced persons camps found malaria prevalence of 68.5%. Four machine learning models evaluated: logistic regression, decision tree, random forest (RF), and gradient boosting machine. Random Forest achieved best performance (AUC=0.892) and identified caregiver occupation and residential camp as most significant predictors. Key risk factors: caregiver with no education (aOR=3.23, p=0.026); female caregivers protective (aOR=0.53, p=0.024). Wide knowledge-practice gap observed with 60.3% of caregivers having prevention knowledge but only 2% using bed nets. Authors conclude ML integration into surveillance enables precision public health interventions.

articles.headline: Indonesia and PNG coordinate malaria response on shared island
articles.title_original: Two Nations, One Front: Indonesia and Papua New Guinea forge a One-Island approach to fight persistent malaria on New Guinea.
articles.authors: Prameswari HD, Kisomb J, Mapira P, Keven JB, Susanto H, Isa R, Supriyanto D, Garjito TA, Syafruddin D, Ndoen E, Iswahyudi, Firmansyah, Permana F, Chin N, Lobo NF, Walker ED, Laihad FJ, Das S, Hawley WA, Elyazar IR
articles.details: Title indicates Indonesia and Papua New Guinea are forging collaborative "One-Island approach" to malaria control on New Guinea. No abstract available to provide detailed study design, sample size, or quantitative results.

articles.headline: Malaria-dengue spatiotemporal patterns in Peruvian region
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal co-distribution and time lagged cross correlation of malaria and dengue in Loreto, Peru.
articles.authors: Carrasco-Escobar G, Carcamo PM, Kaplan SR, Quispe JM, McCord GC, Benmarhnia T
articles.details: Time-lagged cross-correlation analysis of district-level dengue and malaria incidence in Loreto region, Peru, 2000-2021 identified growing number of districts reporting both diseases over time. Maximum TLCC coefficients varied in magnitude and direction between districts. In Northwest, malaria increases often preceded dengue increases; in Northeast, malaria increases preceded dengue decreases. Spatial correlation found between TLCC coefficients in some Northwest regions, suggesting geographic characteristics influence disease associations. Authors conclude identification of districts with strong associations can inform targeted integrated vector-control interventions.

articles.headline: Novel Plasmodium falciparum GluRS inhibitors identified via screening
articles.title_original: Rational design of novel Plasmodium falciparum glutamyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors for the development of next-generation antimalarial drugs.
articles.authors: Nofal W
articles.details: Virtual screening of 1,830 compounds from medicinal fungi secondary metabolites library using PyRx identified two lead compounds: MSID000152 (binding affinity -10.4 kcal/mol, mean RMSD 3.94 Å) and MSID000974 (binding affinity -10.1 kcal/mol, mean RMSD 2.35 Å) as Plasmodium falciparum glutamyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors. Molecular dynamics simulations (100 ns) confirmed structural stability. MSID000152 showed favorable MMPB/GBSA binding free energy. Reverse genetic studies suggest Rpt3 essential during P. berghei erythrocytic cycle. Authors identify both compounds as promising antimalarial leads warranting further in vitro and in vivo investigation.

articles.headline: Placental malaria: 25% prevalence drives adverse perinatal outcomes
articles.title_original: Prevalence, predictors and adverse perinatal outcomes of placental malaria in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Alemayehu A, Kiya GT, Berhanu S, Zeynudin A, Beyene J, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 178 observational studies (2000-2024) involving 93,809 parturient women from 23 countries found overall pooled placental malaria prevalence of 25.1% (95% CI 22.0-28.3), varying by detection method: RDT 16.9%, microscopy 19.1%, PCR 24.8%, histopathology 34.8%. Key predictors: younger age (OR=2.50, 95% CI 1.89-3.12), primigravidae (OR=2.65, 95% CI 2.12-3.18), not using IPTp (OR=1.98, 95% CI 1.42-2.54), not using ANC (OR=7.43, 95% CI 2.47-12.40), HIV infection (OR=2.67, 95% CI 1.22-4.62). Placental malaria associated with maternal anemia (OR=2.18, 95% CI 1.73-2.63) and low birth weight (OR=1.71, 95% CI 1.26-2.16). Authors conclude multidimensional interventions targeting identified predictors are needed.

articles.headline: Shift to artemisinin combinations for P. vivax elimination in India
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax elimination from India may need therapeutic efficacy studies - Informed shift to artemisinin-based treatment.
articles.authors: Kathpalia M, Kojom Foko LP, Rawat P, Chaturvedi R, Rahi M, Sharma A
articles.details: Review synthesizing clinical, molecular, and pharmacological evidence for transition from chloroquine to artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) in P. vivax treatment. ACTs demonstrate superior efficacy including rapid parasite clearance, improved tolerability, effectiveness against mixed-species infections, and reduced relapse when combined with primaquine compared to chloroquine. WHO recommends changing first-line treatment if total failure rate exceeds 10%. Authors note India has sparse therapeutic efficacy studies coverage and limited molecular surveillance for resistance markers. Recommend expansion of therapeutic efficacy studies and adoption of ACT-based regimens to sustain elimination momentum.

articles.headline: P. berghei Rpt3 enhances protein phosphatase 1 activity
articles.title_original: Characterization of the Regulatory AAA-ATPase Subunit Rpt3 in Plasmodium berghei as an Activator of Protein Phosphatase 1.
articles.authors: Lainé C, De Witte C, Martoriati A, Farce A, Metatla I, Guerrera IC, Cailliau K, Khalife J, Pierrot C
articles.details: Study characterizes Plasmodium berghei proteasome AAA-ATPase regulatory subunit Rpt3 demonstrating binding to and enhancement of Protein Phosphatase 1 catalytic subunit (PP1c) activity in vitro and in Xenopus oocyte heterologous model. PbRpt3 RVxF motifs involved in binding and regulatory function; ATP-binding capacity contributes to phosphatase regulatory activity. Reverse genetic studies indicate PbRpt3 essential during P. berghei erythrocytic cycle.
