articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness of intermittent preventive therapy and insecticide-treated bed nets for malaria prevention among pregnant women in Jos North, Plateau State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoro LN, Zoakah AI, Bupwatda P, Anyamene EL, Bulus NG, Kumbak FD, Sabano MC, Mukisa KA, Gmanyami JM, Amoakoh MY, Isiko I
articles.details: Cross-sectional cost-effectiveness analysis in 1,000 pregnant women from five facilities in Jos North, Plateau State, Nigeria (2019–2020). Compared intermittent preventive therapy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP) alone vs. combined IPTp-SP/insecticide-treated nets (ITNs). Combined intervention averted 15.63 DALYs (disability-adjusted life years) with incremental cost of US$100.19, yielding ICER of US$2.12 per DALY averted. At willingness-to-pay threshold US$150/DALY, combined strategy had ~60% probability of cost-effectiveness.

articles.headline: RDT misrecording rates and determinants across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Characteristics of healthcare workers and health facilities associated with inaccurate recording of malaria rapid diagnostic test results: a multi-country study.
articles.authors: Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ngufor C, Yavo W, Konate-Toure A, Ahogni I, Ssewante N, Orya E, Akpiroroh E, Okoro O, Agaba B, Kpemasse A, Agnon J, Tanoh AM, Affoukou C, Opigo J, Ntadom G, Okagbue H, Eugene EC, Aponte JJ, Hilton E, Galles N, Soebiyanto R, Cooper S, Okoronkwo C, Humes M, Griffith K, Lindblade KA, Sampson S
articles.details: Multi-country observational study in 64 facilities across four countries (June–December 2023). Among >100,000 rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) performed by 499 healthcare workers: 5.1–7.3% misrecorded as positive, 0.7–3.7% as negative. Overall agreement with external panel ranged 90.2% to 94.3%. Misrecording influenced by healthcare worker knowledge, attitudes, education, facility cadre, and systemic factors. Improving surveillance requires behavior and facility-level interventions.

articles.headline: Forest pack acceptability for malaria elimination in Cambodia
articles.title_original: Evaluating the acceptability and feasibility of new mosquito bite prevention tools in a "forest pack" to support malaria elimination in Cambodia.
articles.authors: McIver DJ, Doum D, Men C, Neukom J, Lobo NF, Richardson JH, Macdonald M, Shete PB, Sovannaroth S, Tatarsky A
articles.details: Pilot study in two high-burden operational districts targeting forest-exposed populations. 28,000 forest packs distributed over four rounds, containing volatile pyrethroid spatial repellent, topical repellent, and insecticide-treated clothing. 2,935 participants from 18 villages enrolled. Reported acceptability high: 96% for spatial repellent, 93% for topical repellent, 100% for treated clothing. Distribution reached 85–102% of targets; coverage of treated clothing very low (1–40%). Products deemed appropriate by implementers; further epidemiological impact assessment needed.

articles.headline: Duffy binding protein gene amplification in Amazonian P. vivax
articles.title_original: The pattern of gene amplification of members of the Plasmodium vivax erythrocyte binding-like proteins family across the Amazon rainforest.
articles.authors: Guedes KS, Fernandes GM, Sanchez BAM, Ntumngia FB, Adams JH, Kano FS, Fontes CJF, de Sousa TN, Carvalho LH
articles.details: Analyzed 191 P. vivax isolates from three Amazon regions for copy number variation in dbp and ebp2 genes. Duffy binding protein (DBP) amplification: 25% of isolates in western region (up to 8 copies), 2–9% in southwestern/eastern regions (2–3 copies). Erythrocyte binding protein 2 (EBP2) amplification less frequent: 15% in western, 1–4% in other regions. All individuals DARC-positive; no association between gene amplifications and DARC genotypes. Findings warrant further investigation of biological and immunological significance.

articles.headline: Mosquito repellent awareness high despite low consistent use
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes, practices and determinants of mosquito repellent use for malaria prevention in Nangabo Ward, Wakiso District, Uganda.
articles.authors: Kemigisha G, Musoke D, Walekhwa AW, Yeka A
articles.details: Cross-sectional mixed-methods study in Nangabo Ward, Wakiso District, Uganda among 185 household heads. Awareness of repellents 63.8%, positive attitudes 59.5%, but consistent use only 8.2%; 44.9% reported occasional use. Only 38.4% understood repellents safe for children <5 years; 35.7% for pregnant women. Barriers: cost (36.8%), lack of awareness of regulations (91.9%). Multivariable analysis: age ≥60 associated with non-use (aOR 2.50, 95% CI 1.43–4.38); tertiary education protective (aOR 0.42, 95% CI 0.22–0.79).

articles.headline: Larviciding reduces Anopheles densities across transmission strata
articles.title_original: A large-scale mosquito larviciding in Tanga Region, Tanzania, reduced mosquito densities to varying degrees across malaria transmission risk strata.
articles.authors: Gavana T, Kailembo D, Kasagama E, Lilolime NS, Mwamlima TG, Mwalimu CD, Bernard J, Yoram B, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Kisoka N, Chaki P, Lengeler C
articles.details: Pilot project in Tanga Region (June 2022 to April 2024) conducting six rounds of mosquito breeding habitat treatment with Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis and Bacillus sphaericus. 120 villages (60 intervention, 60 control) across high-, moderate-, and low-risk strata. Late-stage Anopheles larval density reductions (incidence rate ratios): 0.20 (95% CI 0.12–0.35) in high-risk, 0.51 (95% CI 0.26–1.01) in moderate-risk, 0.40 (95% CI 0.24–0.68) in low-risk areas. Adult Anopheles gambiae s.l. reduced in moderate/low-risk strata; no significant A. funestus reduction.

articles.headline: Iso-mukaadial acetate disrupts P. falciparum metabolism and DNA replication
articles.title_original: The impact of Iso-mukaadial acetate on Plasmodium falciparum transcriptional gene regulation.
articles.authors: Salomane N, Mafuna T, Allie F, Mahlangu JS, van Zyl RL, Pooe O, Simelane M
articles.details: RNA sequencing of P. falciparum NF54 strain exposed to iso-mukaadial acetate (IMA) from Warburgia salutaris. IMA and chloroquine both upregulated adhesion genes (PfEMP1, RIFIN, STEVOR). IMA downregulated glycolytic pathway enzymes (GAPDH, G6PD) and pentose phosphate pathway; chloroquine specifically downregulated DNA replication licensing (MCM3) and helicase. IMA proposed to impair energy metabolism and antioxidant defenses through glycolysis suppression while also affecting DNA replication machinery. Effects differ from chloroquine's primary DNA replication targeting.

articles.headline: Essential apicoplast fission adaptor protein identified in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: An essential adaptor for apicoplast fission and inheritance in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Blauwkamp JA, Rajaram K, Staggers SR, Harrigan O, Doud EH, Xu W, Ke H, Prigge ST, Sun SY, Absalon S
articles.details: Study identified PfAnchor, a novel P. falciparum protein that regulates apicoplast fission during blood-stage parasite division. Using Ultrastructure Expansion Microscopy, researchers showed that PfAnchor localizes to the apicoplast throughout the asexual blood-stage. Conditional depletion of PfAnchor disrupted apicoplast fission, leading to incomplete cytokinesis and parasite death. The protein interacts with dynamin-like GTPase PfDyn2, making it the first apicoplast-specific dynamin adaptor protein identified.

articles.headline: Mefloquine resistance undermines triple artemisinin-based combination therapy efficacy
articles.title_original: Tolerance of Plasmodium falciparum mefloquine-resistant clinical isolates to mefloquine-piperaquine with implications for triple artemisinin-based combination therapies.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Cosson A, Mairet Khedim M, Khim N, Kloeung N, Ke S, Srun S, Eam R, Khean C, Kul C, Adoux L, Popovici J, Leang R, Ringwald P, Ariey F, Coppée R, Witkowski B
articles.details: Study assessed mefloquine-piperaquine (MQ-PPQ) triple artemisinin-based combination therapy using Cambodian clinical isolates with distinct resistance profiles. MQ-resistant strains showed significant cross-tolerance to the MQ-PPQ combination, while piperaquine-resistant and -sensitive strains remained susceptible. Under four months of repeated MQ-PPQ pressure, parasites rapidly acquired MQ-PPQ tolerance driven by pfmdr1 amplification. Mechanistic analysis revealed MQ inhibits piperaquine accumulation in a dose-dependent manner, compromising combination efficacy.

articles.headline: Community engagement accelerates malaria elimination in forest-dwelling populations
articles.title_original: Early and sustained community engagement to reach unreached populations for malaria elimination in Lao People's Democratic Republic.
articles.authors: Roy SR, Banouvong V, Hoban E, Khamlome B, Chindavongsa K, Inthirath I, Inthivong S, Sibounheuang K, Akavong K, Huong TTG, Yadav RPH, Ringwald P, Kelley JF, Xangsayarath P, Shortus MS, Reyburn R
articles.details: In 2022, the Lao People's Democratic Republic implemented "Accelerator Strategies" targeting remote ethnic communities with high-risk behaviors, including forest goers and farmers on forest fringes. Interventions included targeted drug administration and intermittent preventive treatment as chemoprevention among individuals at increased risk irrespective of infectious status. Three key enablers for success were identified: service delivery and community engagement by community members themselves; advocacy and political commitment from local leaders; and people-centered services with granular local data on risk behaviors and geographic mapping. Early and sustained community engagement resulted in high intervention coverage and decreased malaria burden.

articles.headline: Dengue and malaria mortality surveillance reveals gaps in Ecuador's hospital monitoring
articles.title_original: Dengue and malaria mortality in Ecuador: A population analysis of hospital deaths across 24 provinces, 2015-2023.
articles.authors: Acosta-España JD, Morales-Gualotuña M, Pazmiño-Villaseñor J, Dueñas-Espín I
articles.details: Cross-sectional ecological study analyzed hospital discharge data from Ecuador's 24 provinces from 2015 to 2023. Dengue accounted for 125 hospital deaths concentrated in Amazonian and coastal provinces. Malaria resulted in five adult deaths total (3 in 2019, 1 in 2021, and 1 in 2023), with Plasmodium falciparum predominating. The highest malaria mortality rate occurred in Napo province at 16.3 per million in 2021. Both diseases revealed fragmented mortality patterns with persistent underreporting in endemic zones.

articles.headline: Gestational malaria impairs physical and environmental health in endemic Colombia region
articles.title_original: Health-related quality of life among pregnant women exposed to malaria in an endemic region of Colombia.
articles.authors: Cardona-Arias JA, Higuita-Gutierrez LF
articles.details: Mixed-methods study with 400 pregnant women in Colombia (6% with current gestational malaria history, 44% with lifetime malaria exposure). Health-related quality of life measured by WHOQOL-BREF showed psychological health domain highest (median 78), while physical health (median 43), environmental, and social domains scored lower. Multivariate analysis revealed gestational malaria, comorbidities, and rural residence were associated with poorer physical and environmental health. Qualitative analysis with 30 women showed malaria impact was socially normalized and underrecognized as health determinant.

articles.headline: Malaria discourse on Sina Weibo shows event-driven patterns and geographic disparities
articles.title_original: Event-driven dynamics, regional disparities, and netizen engagement: characterizing and exploring malaria discourse on Sina Weibo for enhanced health communication.
articles.authors: Wang N, Zhao X, Li C, Mao Y
articles.details: Analysis of 81,010 malaria-related posts from Sina Weibo (January 2019–August 2024) found attention is event-driven, with peaks around China's health assistance to Africa and public health incidents, with malaria-day posts accounting for approximately 20% of annual total. Negative events sustained engagement approximately 1.5 times longer than positive events. Africa-related discussions constituted over 60% of region-specific posts annually, while Asia and Americas discussions remained fragmented. Official health accounts post infrequently; personal accounts emphasized treatment and health narratives.

articles.headline: IRS household visits identify pregnant women for malaria prevention interventions
articles.title_original: Indoor residual spraying household visits as a platform for engaging pregnant women in malaria prevention on Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea.
articles.authors: Galick DS, Eyono JNM, Rivas MR, Phiri WP, Guerra CA, García GA
articles.details: Implementation study in Equatorial Guinea used routine annual indoor residual spraying (IRS) to identify pregnant women (589 identified from 1,567 households visited) and conduct follow-up visits assessing malaria knowledge and intervention uptake. High awareness of long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (76.9% cited as prevention method) but low awareness of intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (34.5% cited benefits, 37.1% aware of availability). Regular LLIN use was 54.8% (using every night) while IPTp uptake was 35.2% on track for three doses. Ordinal regression showed LLIN use associated with malaria prevention awareness and IPTp uptake associated with dose-number awareness.

articles.headline: Seasonal migration drives high P. falciparum genetic connectivity between highland and lowland
articles.title_original: High connectivity and low differentiation of Plasmodium falciparum parasite populations in a setting with high seasonal migration.
articles.authors: Esayas E, Louie W, Routledge I, Murphy M, Demeke NN, De Amaral F, Aranda-Díaz A, Greenhouse B, Kassa FA, Nigusse T, Assefa M, Ashine T, Getachew A, Ntuku H, Golassa L, Gadisa E, Bennett A, Smith JL
articles.details: Study examined P. falciparum genetic diversity in Ethiopia using multiplexed amplicon sequencing of dried blood spots from highland health facilities and lowland agricultural worksites. Lowland populations (seasonal workers and residents) had higher complexity of infection and polyclonal rates (mean COI 2.62, 62%, n=581) versus highland residents (mean COI 2.00, 40%, n=599). Similar expected heterozygosity observed (He≈0.4). P. falciparum infections showed high genetic connectivity between regions with extensive parasite sharing, including 27 related clusters in highland cases and 10 in seasonal workers, highlighting seasonal agricultural migration's role in sustaining transmission.

articles.headline: Methylene blue reverses cerebral malaria brain damage; identifies blood biomarkers
articles.title_original: Methylene blue treatment of fatal cerebral malaria and identification of potential blood biomarkers.
articles.authors: Hang JW, Leong YW, Narang V, Sunyakumthorn P, Im-Erbsin R, Foo S, Lum J, Lee B, Brown AE, Rénia L, Turner GDH, Wassmer SC, Lombardini ED, Russell B, Malleret B
articles.details: Study used Plasmodium coatneyi-infected rhesus macaques to model human cerebral malaria (CM). Methylene blue demonstrated efficacy in reversing detrimental infection effects on the brainstem. Differential gene expression analysis comparing P. coatneyi-infected macaque brainstem with P. coatneyi-infected macaque blood and P. falciparum-infected human blood transcriptomic datasets identified nine genes associated with CM severity. Most identified genes expressed in neutrophils, indicating potential as blood biomarkers for diagnosing P. falciparum-induced fatal CM.

articles.headline: Ruxolitinib adjunctive therapy reduces inflammation and enhances malaria immunity
articles.title_original: Adjunctive ruxolitinib attenuates inflammation and enhances immunity in volunteers experimentally infected with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Webster R, Oyong DA, Llewellyn S, Abd-Rahman AN, Potter AJ, Mukhiya R, Sahai N, Leelasena I, Ujvary E, Mathison S, Andrew DW, Bukali L, de Labastida Rivera F, Engel J, Soon MSF, Frame T, Hamelink J, Nalubega M, Dooley NL, Loughland JR, Nguyen T, Rosenberg-Hasson Y, Maysel-Auslender S, Sigal N, Foygel K, Gower J, Peters J, Woo R, Amante F, Wells TNC, Chalon S, Moehrle JJ, McCarthy JS, Birrell GW, Edstein MD, Leipold M, Obermoser G, Maecker H, Engwerda CR, Barber BE, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial in 20 malaria-naïve volunteers inoculated with blood-stage P. falciparum, randomized on day 8 to artemether-lumefantrine with ruxolitinib (JAK 1/2 inhibitor) or placebo, with second inoculation 90 days later. Ruxolitinib was safe and well tolerated; attenuated inflammatory responses to initial infection with reduced posttreatment C-reactive protein and disease severity markers (angiopoietin-2, intercellular adhesion molecule-1). Enhanced immune memory after second infection with elevated human leukocyte antigen-DRA and 4-1BB, consistent with increased T cell activation.

articles.headline: AI applications improve malaria diagnosis and outbreak prediction across multiple modalities
articles.title_original: The role of artificial intelligence (AI) in the elimination of malaria: a systematic literature review.
articles.authors: Kumar SM, Nath SN, Vani HC, Shrinivasa BM, Ahmed N, Tomer V, Bankoti K, Negi G, Ali SSB, Jaiswal MK, Warkade V
articles.details: Systematic literature review of 62 research articles published 2014–2025 examined AI applications in malaria diagnosis, treatment optimization, and outbreak prediction. Main findings indicate AI models achieved better diagnostic accuracies and significantly decreased diagnostic time and human error in image-based parasite detection and predictive analytics. Major limitations identified include poor data interoperability, limited rural infrastructure, and gaps in healthcare worker training. Review analyzes AI techniques globally adaptable for use in India and highlights areas requiring future research.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria parasites circulate in southern Senegal febrile patient populations
articles.title_original: Common Molecular Detection of the Neglected Human Malaria Parasite Among Febrile Patients in Southern Regions in Senegal.
articles.authors: Sambe BS, Diaw SOM, Diagne A, Diatta AS, Diatta HAM, Sarr I, Sané R, Guigma PYB, Senghor B, Diouf B, Sembene PM, Vigan-Womas I, Niang M
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 1,990 febrile patients in four southern Senegal regions (Kedougou, Kolda, Tambacounda, Ziguinchor) during 2020–2022 transmission seasons tested for Plasmodium infections using qPCR and species-specific nested PCR. Plasmodium positivity rate was 73.43%, with 67.92% unique species infections and 32.08% co-infections by two or three species. Study documented circulation of all non-falciparum species (P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae) in three regions; non-detection in Ziguinchor; first evidence of non-falciparum infections in Kolda and Tambacounda; and first report of P. ovale in Ziguinchor.

articles.headline: High malaria-helminth co-infection prevalence associated with anemia in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Malaria and Soil-Transmitted Helminth Co-Infection and Its Association With Anemia Among Pregnant Women Visiting Mizan-Tepi University Teaching Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Duguma T, Assefa S, Tesfaye T, Alemayehu B
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 332 pregnant women at a teaching hospital in Ethiopia. Malaria, soil-transmitted helminths, and co-infection prevalences were 24.4%, 33.7%, and 12.7%, respectively. Overall anemia prevalence was 57.5%, with anemia more common in malaria-only (18.4%) and helminth-only (26.2%) infections but less common in co-infection (9.6%). Authors conclude that malaria and STH infections showed strong statistical association with anemia.

articles.headline: Symptom-based score optimizes malaria detection in resource-limited outpatient settings
articles.title_original: Syndromic presentation by patients with malaria and respiratory symptoms in a malaria-endemic urban-and-peri-urban setting.
articles.authors: Muwonge H, Akugizibwe R, Bbosa G, Damani AM, Nakidde B, Mugahi R, Byakika-Kibwika P
articles.details: Among 332 adults at outpatient clinics, 16.0% tested positive for malaria by RDT and microscopy. Fever and cough were strongest independent predictors. A four-point symptom score (fever, cough, nasal symptoms, sore throat) achieved 98.1% sensitivity and 47.3% specificity at threshold of ≥2 symptoms, with malaria positivity rising from 0.8% with one symptom to 100% with four. Authors conclude incorporating this score into triage could optimize malaria testing in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Community SP delivery in pregnancy acceptable but hindered by supply shortages
articles.title_original: Initiating community intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal: qualitative evaluation of stakeholder acceptability and experience.
articles.authors: Ndoye T, Majeres Lugand M, Diallo AM, Sakho S, Pouye MM, Dieng K, Faye SLB
articles.details: Qualitative study using 111 interviews and 16 focus groups exploring stakeholder perspectives on community-delivered intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (c-IPTp) for pregnant women in Senegal. Healthcare providers found c-IPTp acceptable and beneficial, reducing workload and improving access in remote areas. Key barriers included SP and pregnancy test stockouts and concerns about adverse events. Authors conclude ensuring supplies, incentivizing community health workers, and securing consistent funding are crucial to expand c-IPTp uptake.

articles.headline: Temporal shifts in Pfcrt and Pfk13 mutations track parasite resistance in Kenya
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, population structure, and drug resistance of Plasmodium falciparum in Kenya: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Nyataya JN, Mutsaers M, Masakhwe CA, Laigong AC, Kattenberg JH, Rosanas-Urgell A, Waitumbi JN
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 58 studies from Kenya (2002-2023) assessing P. falciparum genetic diversity and drug resistance. Expected heterozygosity was high (0.79-0.96 across regions) with multiclonal infections (34-80% prevalence, mean MOI 2-4.8). Pfcrt haplotype CVIET declined from 96% (1999) to 1% (2017), while CVMNK increased from 6.8% (1998) to 98.8% (2017). Temporal increases in Pfdhfr-51I, Pfdhfr-59R, Pfdhps-437G, and Pfdhps-540E were detected; Pfk13 mutations (R539T, N458Y, R561H, A675V, V568G) emerged in Lake Basin and Highlands (2018-2024).

articles.headline: Malaria burden declined but resurging incidence threatens 2050 targets
articles.title_original: Global, regional, national burden, trends and health inequality of neglected tropical diseases and malaria from 1990 to 2021.
articles.authors: Feng L, Mo G, Liu Z, Liu Q
articles.details: Secondary analysis of GBD 2021 data assessing global malaria and NTD burden from 1990-2021. Global age-standardized incidence rate increased 0.59% while prevalence, death rate, and DALY rate declined by 58.05%, 25%, and 32.36%, respectively. Western Sub-Saharan Africa bore highest burden; children under 5 had highest incidence and death rates. Projections to 2050 show continued prevalence decline but significant increases in incidence, death rate, and DALY rate with sustained gender and regional gaps.

articles.headline: CNN achieves 92.4% accuracy detecting Plasmodium stages from blood smears
articles.title_original: Mobile phone-based plasmodium parasites stage detection from Giemsa stained blood smear by convolutional neural networks.
articles.authors: Bedir H, Arslan MÖ, Akıner MM, Öztürk M, Uygun ZO
articles.details: Study developed a convolutional neural network pipeline for automated detection and staging of malaria parasites from Giemsa-stained blood smear images across four classes: Ring Form, Trophozoite, Schizont, and Uninfected RBCs. CNN achieved overall classification accuracy of 92.4% with precision, recall, and F1-scores exceeding 0.90 across all classes (mean accuracy 92.4%±2.1%, precision 93.1%±1.8%, recall 92.8%±1.9%). Schizont stage had highest accuracy (94.7%) while Ring Form was lower (91.2%). Authors conclude this approach offers scalability and adaptability for deployment in resource-constrained diagnostic workflows.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Ethiopia marked by 11-fold P. vivax surge
articles.title_original: Resurgence of malaria in the Amhara Region, Ethiopia (2014-2024): trends, spatial expansion, and control challenges.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Asemahagn MA, Nigussie TZ, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Retrospective surveillance analysis of 11 years (2014-2024) from 166 districts covering 7,710,733 malaria cases and 162 deaths. A trend inflection in late 2018 marked shift from decline (APC -13.2%) to resurgence (APC +12.6%). Annual Parasite Incidence rose from 10.9 per 1000 (2018) to 74.8 per 1000 (2024). Plasmodium vivax contribution surged from 25% to 43%, with vivax incidence rising 11-fold (2.7 to 32.4 per 1000). Test positivity rate increased from 10% to >50% during peak months; 83% of districts experienced resurgence with hotspots expanding into urban and highland areas.

articles.headline: Comprehensive surveillance essential for preventing malaria reintroduction in elimination zones
articles.title_original: Imported malaria: a silent and forgotten threat in malaria free zones?
articles.authors: Lusiyana N, Ahmad RA, Elyazar I, Murhandarwati EEH
articles.details: Systematic review of 149 articles (24 selected for analysis) examining strategies to prevent malaria reintroduction in elimination zones. Studies implemented imported case surveillance (case detection in 21 articles, case management in 4), with supporting measures including mass drug administration (3), chemoprophylaxis (3), vector surveillance (6), vector control (7), and cross-border initiatives (3). Reintroduction events were driven by imported cases, cross-border population movements, and competent vectors. Authors conclude case-based surveillance alone is insufficient; comprehensive strategies must be complemented with additional interventions and context-specific activities for sustained elimination.

articles.headline: Low asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence in pregnant women in Somalia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of asymptomatic malaria and associated factors among pregnant women in Mogadishu, Somalia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Abdi MY, Mohamud FA, Abdullahi SA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 171 asymptomatic pregnant women at a hospital in Somalia (February-March 2025). Asymptomatic Plasmodium infection prevalence was 2.9%, with P. falciparum the only species detected in all positive cases. No significant associations were found between infection and sociodemographic, obstetric, or malaria-prevention factors. However, younger age (OR 0.24; 95% CI 0.03-2.23), urban residence (OR 0.12; 95% CI 0.012-1.32), and ITN utilization (OR 0.06; 95% CI 0.002-1.82) were associated with lower odds of infection.

articles.headline: Eschweilenol C from Terminalia plants shows potent anti-malaria activity
articles.title_original: Eschweilenol C and subfractions from Terminalia plants disrupt haemoglobin metabolism, inhibiting Plasmodium falciparum growth at rings and trophozoite stages.
articles.authors: Tali MBT, Mbouna CDJ, Zehra A, Wouamba SCN, Mouthe GPK, Kamche AY, Dize D, Kouam SF, Kamdem BP, Tchouankeu JC, Khan F, Boyom FF
articles.details: In vitro study evaluated eschweilenol C (isolated from Terminalia plants) and subfractions against P. falciparum strains (Dd2, 3D7, Dd2-GNF156). Eschweilenol C had IC50 values of 490.74 nM (PfDd2) and 379.20 nM (Pf3D7); subfractions showed IC50 0.15-5.73 µg/ml. Top hits (Ti03, Ti04, eschweilenol C) demonstrated IC50 <1 µg/ml with good selectivity (SI>10) and no loss of efficacy against mutant resistant strain. These compounds reduced haemozoin production and showed killing activity on ring and trophozoite stages through binding to PfM1AAP and PfM17LAP.

articles.headline: Malaria-anaemia co-occurrence affects 6.4% of Ghanaian children under five
articles.title_original: The double burden: co-occurrence of malaria and anaemia in children under five in Ghana - a multilevel mixed-effects logistic regression analysis.
articles.authors: Karikari JK, Bedu-Addo K, Hassan YSA, Konadu E, Addae M, Amponsah OKO, Opoku DA
articles.details: Analysis of 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey data assessing prevalence and risk factors for co-occurrence of malaria and anaemia in children under five. Prevalence of the double burden was 6.4% (95% CI 5.5-7.5). Significant risk factors included being fifth-born (aOR 3.92; 95% CI 1.60-9.62) and recent fever (aOR 2.49; 95% CI 1.46-4.24). Protective factors included higher household wealth (aOR 0.31-0.51) and elderly household heads aged ≥60 years (aOR 0.33; 95% CI 0.14-0.79). Authors conclude integrated interventions are essential to address this major health concern.

articles.headline: Reversible paralysis in pregnancy from malaria-schistosomiasis co-infection in Malawi
articles.title_original: Reversible paralysis in pregnancy, thinking beyond malaria: a case report.
articles.authors: Sous M, Boti M, Mose B, Sous W
articles.details: Case report of a 17-year-old pregnant woman in Malawi presenting with fever, headache, and abdominal pain, diagnosed with malaria and started on artemisinin-based therapy. Twelve days later she developed progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness and neurogenic bladder despite intravenous artesunate treatment. Laboratory studies revealed both malaria parasites on blood film and schistosomiasis ova in urine. Treatment with prednisone followed by praziquantel resulted in resolution of paralysis, with patient ambulatory at discharge 3 weeks later.

articles.headline: PI3K inhibition reverses artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: PfPI3K inhibition reversed artemisinin resistance by increasing ROS and ubiquitinated protein levels in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shi N, Wang R, Yu T, Yang J, Chen L, Li Q, Li Y, Zhang S
articles.details: In vitro study showing idelalisib (PfPI3K inhibitor) combined with dihydroartemisinin (DHA) exhibits synergistic antimalarial effects and reverses artemisinin resistance. Mechanism involves PI3P level decline, ROS accumulation, increased ubiquitinated protein levels, downregulation of molecular chaperone genes, and increased eIF2α phosphorylation. Results indicate targeting PfPI3K is a successful antimalarial strategy for controlling artemisinin resistance development.

articles.headline: Clinical malaria induces IL-10-skewed T-cell response in Kenyan children
articles.title_original: CD4 and CD8 T-cell response is dominated by IL-10-secreting cells in children with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Ariera BO, Guyah B, Onditi I, Waomba K, Koech E, Sabourin KR, Samayoa-Reyes G, Rochford R, Ogolla S
articles.details: Flow cytometry study of 10 Kenyan children with acute P. falciparum malaria and 10 healthy community controls following stimulation with EBV and CMV peptides. Measured T-cell activation markers and cytokine secretion. Found shift toward IL-10 secretion in both CD4 and CD8 T cells during acute malaria and at 4-week post-recovery, suggesting malaria-induced systemic immune suppression.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury drives 2.87× increased mortality in childhood severe malaria
articles.title_original: Identification of acute kidney injury in African children with severe malaria: a multinational individual participant data meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Bond C, Batte A, Afolayan F, Anstey N, Bassat Q, Bejon P, Berkley JA, Evans RDR, Goldstein SL, Hawkes MT, Ibrahim O, Imani P, John CC, Kain KC, Liepmann C, Marsh K, Namazzi R, Nakuya M, O'Brien N, Oluwadare LP, Opoka RO, Sserunkuuma J, Wynkoop H, Conroy AL
articles.details: Individual participant data meta-analysis of 18 studies across 8 countries including 13,528 African children (3 months–16 years) with P. falciparum. Acute kidney injury (KDIGO criteria) present in 47%; associated with 2.87-fold increased odds of mortality (95% CI 2.28–3.61) with monotonic increase across AKI stages. WHO-defined AKI threshold (>3 mg/dL creatinine) identified only 3.4% of cases, missing 93% compared to KDIGO criteria.

articles.headline: NEK4 kinase drives Plasmodium meiosis and zygote morphogenesis
articles.title_original: A divergent Plasmodium NEK4 acts as a key regulator driving the early events of meiosis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Hair M, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Brady D, Pasquarello C, Bottrill A, Bhanvadia S, Neal A, Tromer EC, Le Roch KG, Hainard A, Holder AA, Vaughan S, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: Microscopy and transcriptomic study in P. berghei showing NEK4 accumulates at microtubule-organizing center (MTOC) and apical polar complex shortly after fertilization. Deletion of nek4 blocks MTOC duplication, microtubule formation, chromatin condensation, nuclear migration, and cell polarity establishment. Transcriptomic and phosphoproteomic analyses reveal NEK4 regulates expression of HOP1, REC8, and AP2-O.

articles.headline: Locally transmitted P. falciparum malaria in non-endemic Lebanon
articles.title_original: Locally Acquired Plasmodium falciparum Malaria in a Non-endemic Region of Lebanon: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Hashem S, Mahfouz T, Berry A, Salameh H, Tarhini M
articles.details: Case report of 70-year-old Lebanese female with no travel history presenting with fever, chills, headache, nausea, and abdominal pain. Laboratory findings included anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia; peripheral blood smear showed intraerythrocytic ring forms; rapid diagnostic test confirmed P. falciparum. Treated with intravenous artesunate followed by oral artemether-lumefantrine with full recovery. Absence of conventional exposure routes (travel, transfusion, transplant) presents significant diagnostic and public health challenge.

articles.headline: Computational screening identifies potent novel PfMDR1 inhibitors against drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: In-silico identification of antimalarial compounds targeting PfMDR1 of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Preetha TL, Yadav V, Subbarao N
articles.details: In silico molecular docking study screening compounds against PfMDR1 (PDB ID: 8JWI) using GLIDE and GOLD docking software. Top-ranking compound achieved GLIDE docking score of -12.169 kcal/mol vs. benchmark -5.435 kcal/mol. Molecular dynamics simulations showed stable binding; MM/PBSA binding free energy calculations yielded -130.971 ± 14.283 kJ/mol, substantially higher than benchmark antimalarial drugs, supporting superior inhibition of P. falciparum drug-resistance transporter PfMDR1.

articles.headline: Clothianidin IRS reduces malaria entomological inoculation rate 4-fold in Côte d'Ivoire
articles.title_original: Entomological impact of three years of clothianidin-based indoor residual spraying in two high malaria endemic districts in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Chabi J, Edi CVA, Coleman S, Gbalegba GCN, Ouattara AF, Broudje BRN, Kouame RMA, Yokoly FN, Soro D, Konan LY, N'Tamon RN, Tia E, Yapo RM, Konan GK, Tea AS, Akre MA, Kpan DSM, Tanoh AM, Cissé NG, Flatley C, Kouassi BL
articles.details: Pre- and post-IRS entomological surveillance in Sakassou and Nassian (IRS) vs. Beoumi and Dabakala (control) districts, April 2019–December 2022, using human landing catches and pyrethrum spray catches. Primary vectors: Anopheles coluzzii and A. gambiae. Human-biting rate declined 60% by third IRS round (IRR=0.40, p=0.002); sporozoite infection odds decreased 49% after first two rounds (OR=0.51). Entomological inoculation rate declined 4-fold from 3.92 to 1.25 ib/p/n; control areas declined minimally.

articles.headline: Severe malaria treatment guideline adherence only 33% in Northern Uganda hospitals
articles.title_original: Adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines in Northern Uganda: a mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Akello Z, Oloro J, Mukanyangezi MF, Asingizwe D
articles.details: Mixed-methods study reviewing 384 patient records from three Northern Uganda hospitals and conducting 9 key informant interviews with healthcare workers. Overall adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines: 33.07%, with highest non-adherence for intravenous followed by oral medications (38.3%) and treatment of malaria-confirmed cases only (25%). Medical doctors more likely to adhere (adjusted IRR 1.7, p=0.035). Barriers included knowledge gaps and resource constraints; facilitators included training and institutional support.

articles.headline: Hybrid chemoprevention improves malaria prevention 2-3× compared to perennial chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Rethinking a hybrid malaria chemoprevention delivery strategy for children in sub-perennial settings: a modelling study integrating age- and seasonally-targeted delivery.
articles.authors: Sen S, Schellenberg D, Penny MA
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study using OpenMalaria simulating hybrid malaria chemoprevention (HMC) combining perennial chemoprevention with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) added during rainy season in children 3–24 months (HMC) and 3–36 months (HMC+). Across prevalence 5–70%, HMC provided 2.1 (IQR 1.6–2.6) fold increase in clinical burden averted and 2.0 (IQR 0.6–3.4) in severe cases; HMC+ provided 2.9 (IQR 2.2–3.6) and 3.3 (IQR 0.8–5.8), respectively. Protective efficacy: HMC 31.8% clinical, 16.1% severe; HMC+ 44.9% clinical, 26.4% severe.

articles.headline: Secondary anopheline vectors sustain malaria transmission in northeastern India
articles.title_original: Evidence of secondary anopheline vectors in sustaining malaria transmission in Kokrajhar District, Assam, Northeastern India.
articles.authors: Singh K, Mohanty A, Malla WA, Ranjha R, Gam J, Ali R, Bharti PK, Anvikar AR, Singh H
articles.details: Entomological survey in Kokrajhar District, Assam collecting 1,794 female mosquitoes via CDC light traps, indoor resting collection, and pyrethrum sprays. Region has both Plasmodium vivax and P. falciparum transmission; An. minimus and An. baimaii are traditional primary vectors. An. maculatus showed highest Plasmodium positivity (4%, 5/80), followed by An. minimus (4.8%, 1/21) and An. kochi (4.6%, 1/22). Despite long-lasting insecticidal nets and indoor residual spraying reducing primary vectors, secondary anopheline species may sustain transmission.

articles.headline: Malaria diagnostic testing scale-up over 15 years merits celebration
articles.title_original: A reason to celebrate in a time of uncertainty. Response to: Linblade et al. "Assessing the accuracy of the recording and reporting of malaria rapid diagnostic test results in four African countries-methods and key results".
articles.authors: Barat LM
articles.details: Commentary response to Linblade et al.'s publication on accuracy of malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) recording and reporting in four African countries. Commends the study and notes findings demonstrate success of country and global efforts to scale up malaria diagnostic testing over the past 15 years. When viewed in historical context of malaria diagnosis and treatment, these findings warrant celebration of progress achieved in diagnostic scaling.

articles.headline: UA2239 blocks malaria parasite egress by inhibiting cGMP synthesis
articles.title_original: An acyclic nucleoside phosphonate effectively blocks the egress of the malaria parasite by inhibiting the synthesis of cyclic GMP.
articles.authors: Ali M, Dura R, Guery MA, Colard-Itté E, Cheviet T, Robresco L, Berry L, Lionne C, Lavazec C, Claessens A, Peyrottes S, Wengelnik K, Wein S, Cerdan R
articles.details: In vitro study of UA2239, an acyclic nucleoside phosphonate with guanine nucleobase, showing rapid and irreversible inhibition of Plasmodium parasites by blocking merozoite and gamete egress from infected erythrocytes. UA2239 disrupts cGMP-dependent egress pathway by decreasing parasite cGMP levels, with P. falciparum guanylyl cyclase α as primary target. Parasites develop resistance through mutations in downstream effectors, not the target itself, suggesting a unique mechanism of action with potential dual-stage antimalarial activity.

articles.headline: Father-dependent decisions delay severe malaria treatment in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Influence of Sociodemographic Factors and Household Decision-Making on the Delayed Presentation of Childhood Severe Malaria at a Secondary Facility in North-Central Nigeria.
articles.authors: Issa A, Lawal AF, Folorunsho M, Ibrahim OR
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 146 children admitted with severe malaria. 69.2% (101/146) presented >24 hours after symptom onset; failure to recognize illness (44.6%) and lack of funds (38.6%) were common reasons for delay. Socioeconomic class independently predicted delayed presentation (AOR 3.05, 95% CI 1.10–8.48). Waiting for father's decision was associated with presentation timing.

articles.headline: Migratory birds and bright plumage linked to haemosporidian infection risk
articles.title_original: Host Traits and Avian Malaria Parasites: Understanding the Intrinsic Connections.
articles.authors: Pinzón-Cárdenas PA, González-Quevedo C, Rúa-Uribe GL, Pérez-Tris J, Rivera-Gutiérrez HF
articles.details: Phylogenetic comparative analysis of 115 bird species from Colombia. Migratory species, larger body size, and colorful plumage were associated with higher haemosporidian infection probability. Habitat specialization was negatively associated with infection risk. Results suggest increased vector exposure explains observed associations.

articles.headline: Male-drive female-sterile system for genetic control of Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Author Correction: A male-drive female-sterile system for the self-limited control of the malaria mosquito Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Strampelli A, Willis K, Gulliford HR, Gribble M, Fasulo B, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: Author correction to previously published work on a genetic control system for malaria mosquito vector.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection damages mosquito salivary gland protein composition
articles.title_original: High-resolution proteomics unveils salivary gland disruption and saliva-hemolymph protein exchange in Plasmodium-infected mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Alves E Silva TL, Kanatani S, Barletta Ferreira AB, Schwartz CL, Liffner B, Talyuli OAC, Olivas J, Nagata BM, Pala ZR, Pascini T, Alves DA, Zhao M, Suzuki M, Dorner LP, Frischknecht F, Coppens I, Barillas-Mury C, Absalon S, Ribeiro JMC, Sinnis P, Vega-Rodriguez J
articles.details: Proteomics study of Anopheles gambiae salivary glands infected with P. berghei or P. falciparum. Sporozoite invasion causes structural damage; hemolymph proteins diffuse into glands while saliva proteins diffuse into circulation. Immune proteins reduced in saliva from infected mosquitoes with similar protein profiles between parasite species, suggesting conserved sporozoite-salivary gland interactions.

articles.headline: Novartis antimalarial Ganaplacide shows clinical trial success
articles.title_original: 'A sigh of relief': New malaria drug succeeds in clinical trial as existing treatments falter.
articles.authors: Kupferschmidt K
articles.details: Ganaplacide, developed by Novartis, succeeded in clinical trials, addressing concerns about antimalarial drug resistance.

articles.headline: Low-density malaria infections persist in Malaysia's malaria-free areas
articles.title_original: Persistent low-density infection in areas at risk of malaria reintroduction in Malaysia.
articles.authors: Jiram AI, Hisam S, Abdul Hamid MH, Jelip J, Noordin R, Musa KI, Ogu Salim N, Md Yusuf NA, Amir NF, Rasyidina A, Ismail NP, Hazwani Aziz DI, Abdullah NH, Kep A, Hussin Z, Ismail A
articles.details: Community-based survey of 3,322 asymptomatic residents in 23 localities across Sabah, Perak, Kelantan, and Johor from June–October 2020. nPCR detected 1.86% (62/3,322) low-density infections undetected by microscopy: P. malariae (40.3%), P. vivax (29.0%), P. knowlesi (24.2%), P. falciparum (1.6%), P. cynomolgi (1.6%), and mixed P. vivax/P. knowlesi (3.2%). Most cases from Sabah and Orang Asli settlement in Perak, with 68% adults.

articles.headline: Plasmodium detected in 24.4% of acute febrile illness patients in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Plasmodium spp. Detection Among Acute Febrile Illness Patients in Two Regions of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Quiner CA, Ephraim AZ, Kwaghe V, Erameh C, Samuels J, Courtney LP, Kim JH, Edeawe OI, Vongdip N, Ejike OJ, Odia I, Asman K, Chebu P, Agbukor J, Matthew OD, Orok V, Owolagba F, Okhiria B, Ogbaini-Emovon E, Odion WM, Obagho BA, Fayomade R, Adams J, Oga EA
articles.details: Study of AFI patients at two tertiary hospitals (August 2023–September 2024) screened for 25 pathogens using multi-pathogen assay. Plasmodium spp. detected in 293/1,199 (24.4%); associated with larger household size (COR 3.70, 95% CI 1.12–12.28), healthcare worker status (COR 3.39, CI 1.22–9.41), children aged 11–14 (COR 1.86, CI 1.15–3.02), and enrollment from FCT (COR 1.30, CI 1.00–1.69). Coinfections present in 37.2% (109) of Plasmodium-positive cases.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccines largely cost-effective in African settings
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness and budget impact of malaria, measles, and meningitis vaccines in Africa: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Anosike C, Ojiakor IM, Etiaba EI, Uguru NP, Ezenduka CC, Onwujekwe O
articles.details: Scoping review of 41 cost-effectiveness studies (2004–2024) on malaria, measles, and meningitis vaccines in Africa. Cost per fully vaccinated child for malaria: US$4.20–$52.35. Cost per DALY averted for malaria: US$22.63–$3,314.01. Budget impact of malaria vaccine introduction in 41 African countries approximately US$185 million. Most vaccines more cost-effective than no intervention.

articles.headline: Invasive An. stephensi and native vectors co-occur in Ethiopian urban habitats
articles.title_original: Co-occurrence of native and invasive malaria vectors in anthropogenic habitats in Metehara, Ethiopia: Opportunities for urban malaria control.
articles.authors: Ashine T, Kochora A, Assefa M, Negash N, Gidisa B, Hailemeskel E, Alemayehu M, Assefa C, Ebabu M, Mamo Ayana G, Nigusse T, Aberra Kassa F, Esayas E, Eyasu A, Chali S, Epstein A, Jaiteh F, Bradley J, Doumbe-Belisse P, Reynolds AM, Massebo F, Wilson AL, Weetman D, Donnelly MJ, Gadisa E
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Bioecological assessment in Metehara, Ethiopia (November 2023–October 2024) surveyed 767 breeding habitats (98.3% anthropogenic). Of 417 habitats with Anopheles immatures, one-third contained both An. stephensi and An. arabiensis. Adult collections (n=2,078): An. arabiensis 63.7% (1,323), An. pharoensis 26.4% (549). An. arabiensis showed seasonal variation (LR=46.96, P<0.01) while An. stephensi remained stable. Sporozoite rates: An. pharoensis 4.2% (23/548), An. arabiensis 0.4% (5/1,321), An. stephensi 0%.

articles.headline: Ultrasensitive ELISA detects P. falciparum 10-fold lower than current tests
articles.title_original: Ultrasensitive ELISA for Accurate Detection of Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Kobayashi Y, Okita K, Chen PK, Hasunuma M, Tsuneki T, Hayakawa ESH, Yoshimura T, Ito E
articles.details: Developed ultrasensitive protein detection system combining ELISA and thio-NAD cycling for PfHRP2 and pLDH markers. Detects recombinant proteins at 0.782 pg/mL (PfHRP2) and 1.33 pg/mL (pLDH). In human whole blood, detects 0.5 parasites/μL for both markers. Sensitivity approximately 10-fold greater than latest ultrasensitive antigen kits. Successfully detects pfhrp2-gene deletion parasites using pLDH.

articles.headline: Guillain-Barré Syndrome following Plasmodium vivax malaria in Ethiopian patient
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome Following Malaria: A Rare Case Report From Rural Ethiopia and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Abdela HA, Kimo NH, Woyimo TG
articles.details: Case report of 21-year-old male from rural Ethiopia developing GBS following P. vivax malaria. Presented with ascending paralysis, areflexia, dysphagia, dysarthria, and autonomic dysfunction. CSF analysis showed albumin-cytologic dissociation. Treated with intravenous immunoglobulin and supportive care; achieved full recovery after 8 weeks.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA-engineered CAR-T cells target chondroitin sulfate A-positive tumors
articles.title_original: Protein catenation potentiates antitumor activity of malaria VAR2CSA navigation CAR-T cells in a mouse model of hematological malignancies.
articles.authors: Hu W, Tao Z, Guo W, Ding W, Huang S, Li J, Peng H, Zeng L, Chen Z, Qiu R, Kang Z, Yao Y, Chen X
articles.details: Developed CAR-T cell system using malaria protein VAR2CSA as targeting module for pl-CSA+ tumors. Created rVAR2 catenane (AXVB) via orthogonal screening with enhanced thermostability and target affinity compared to rVAR2 monomer. In vitro and in vivo, AXVB-CAR-T cells eliminated CD19+ and CD19- pl-CSA+ tumor cells and prolonged survival in tumor-bearing mice.

articles.headline: ITN use among pregnant women in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence and factors associated with insecticide-treated net use among pregnant women in malaria high-burden countries in sub-Saharan Africa: a multilevel mixed-effects analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Musinguzi B, Legason ID, Opoke R, Agaba BB, Kananura RM, Mwangi A
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2017-2021 Malaria Indicator Survey data from 7 sub-Saharan African countries in 4950 pregnant women aged 15-49. Pooled prevalence of insecticide-treated net use was 63.8% (95% CI 61.8-65.9), ranging from 49.2% in Ghana to 90.5% in Niger. Primary education and 1-2 prior births were associated with higher ITN use (aOR 1.36 and 1.24, respectively). Regional variation accounted for 32% of within-country variation in use, suggesting localized interventions tailored to regional disparities are needed.

articles.headline: Artemisinin biosynthesis and resistance management for malaria
articles.title_original: Safeguarding artemisinin: integrating bioproduction innovation and resistance management for malaria eradication.
articles.authors: Farmanpour Kalalagh K, Rezaei A, Hazrati S, Nicola S, Moradi S, Davarpanah Dizaj S, Tariverdizadeh N, Beyraghdar Kashkooli A
articles.details: Review consolidating challenges in artemisinin production and Plasmodium falciparum resistance. Covers biotechnological advances in artemisinin biosynthesis via metabolic engineering, constraints in Artemisia annua cultivation, and molecular mechanisms of artemisinin resistance including pfk13 mutations. Current artemisinin-based combination therapies and emerging triple and quadruple ACTs are evaluated. The review proposes a sustainable malaria control roadmap integrating production innovation and resistance management.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance in Anopheles gambiae in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and malaria transmission indicators in Anopheles gambiae s.l. in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso: implications for vector control strategies.
articles.authors: Amara MF, Namountougou M, Konaté H, Kouadio KWU, Yaméogo KB, Kagoné ST, Diabaté A, Gnankine O
articles.details: WHO-standard bioassay study of Anopheles from 6 neighborhoods in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso; 622 specimens genotyped. High resistance to DDT and pyrethroids with knockdown resistance mutations (L995F and L995S); susceptibility to bendiocarb and pirimiphos-methyl. Anopheles arabiensis dominated (90.8%), with sporozoite infection rates of 45% in An. coluzzii, 27.4% in An. arabiensis, and 16.2% in An. gambiae s.s. Overall entomological inoculation rate was 10.6 infectious bites per person, with An. arabiensis responsible for 91.2% of transmission.

articles.headline: Knockdown resistance in malaria vectors: systematic review
articles.title_original: Knockdown resistance (kdr) and organochlorine insecticide resistance in malaria vectors: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Abbasi E, Daliri S
articles.details: Systematic review of 5 studies examining knockdown resistance (kdr) mutations in 4 Anopheles species (An. albimanus, An. darlingi, An. dirus, An. punctipennis) through June 2024. No kdr mutations at codons 1010, 1013, or 1014 in the VGSC gene were identified in any species. Organochlorine resistance was attributed to metabolic mechanisms including elevated mixed-function oxidases and non-specific esterases. Metabolic and behavioral resistance mechanisms, rather than target-site insensitivity, are responsible for organochlorine resistance in these vectors.

articles.headline: Modeling malaria control with pyrethroid resistance in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of different intervention packages for malaria control under varying intensities of pyrethroid resistance.
articles.authors: Gervas HE, Mayengo MM, Mlacha YP, Ngowo HS, Okumu FO, Selvaraj P
articles.details: Compartmental transmission model incorporating Anopheles funestus and An. arabiensis with varying pyrethroid resistance levels. At 50% pyrethroid resistance, 71% ITN coverage combined with 50% IRS or 32% biolarvicide reduces effective reproduction number (Re) below 1. At >75% resistance, vector control alone cannot achieve Re<1. Integrating 80% ITN coverage, ≥40% vaccine coverage (RTS,S/AS01 or R21/Matrix-M), and 80% treatment with artemisinin-based combination therapy reduces infectious individuals to <20% and Re below 1 even under high resistance.

articles.headline: Mobile surveillance and vector control integration: scoping review
articles.title_original: Mobile app-based malaria surveillance and vector control integration: a scoping review of evidence from endemic settings.
articles.authors: Okereke PU, Okereke OW, Ogunyinka BO, Aluele SI, Tiamiyu RA, Afolabi MA, Umeh CV, Nwibo EU, Ibekwe SN, Ogbu AC, Okwor EO, Onwurah CC, Ogamba N
articles.details: Scoping review of 20 studies (2010-2024) on mobile health platforms for malaria surveillance linked to vector control. Mobile systems (MCBR Myanmar, SOCH and MCS India, MRRS Zambia) significantly improved reporting timeliness and enabled precise vector control targeting via GIS hotspot identification. Community platforms like FeverTracker enhanced case detection. Key barriers included poor internet access, low digital literacy, and limited national policy integration. Success requires interoperability, community participation, and long-term policy support within national health systems.

articles.headline: P. falciparum genetic diversity using msp genes in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of Plasmodium falciparum using merozoite surface proteins 1 and 2 (msp-1 and msp-2) genes in Oveng and Mintom, South region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nanssong-Vomo CT, Feufack-Donfack LB, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Ibrahima-Ibrahima, Kiam CB, Youmsi Fotso GJ, Kemeni MN, Fotso BT, Abate L, Eboumbou C, Ayong L, Mbida-Mbida JA, Nsango SE
articles.details: Genotyping study of 315 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Oveng and Mintom, South region of Cameroon using msp1 (KI, MAD20, RO33) and msp2 (FC27, 3D7) markers. msp1 identified 12 alleles with KI 87.8%, MAD20 75.2%, and RO33 74.9% prevalence; msp2 identified 28 alleles with FC27 100% and 3D7 98.4% prevalence. Expected heterozygosity was 0.83 (msp1) and 0.98 (msp2). Mean multiplicity of infection was significantly higher for msp2 (4.85) than msp1 (2.40, p<0.05), with 99% polyclonal infections.

articles.headline: Intermittent preventive malaria treatment in school-aged children (Tanzania)
articles.title_original: Implementation of the intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in school-aged children (IPTsc) in moderate and high endemic areas in Tanzania: a policy brief.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Baraka V, Minja DTR, Chiduo M, Mmbando BP, Seth MD, Lusasi A, Chacky F, Aaron S, David A, Mohamed A, Lazaro S, Molteni F, Bastiaens H, Van Geertruyden JP, Lusingu JPA
articles.details: Policy brief on IPTsc implementation in Tanzania following clinical trial and implementation research. School-aged children (5-15 years) harbor parasites asymptomatically up to 70%, contributing to 13-50% school absenteeism and 61% anemia. Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine delivered through school-based systems was safe, cost-effective, and acceptable to communities, with significant malaria prevalence reductions. IPTsc is an effective complementary control strategy for endemic areas with moderate to high transmission.

articles.headline: Rituximab increases malaria risk in adult lymphoma patients
articles.title_original: Rituximab is associated with an increased risk of malaria among adult lymphoma patients in Malawi: a prospective observational cohort.
articles.authors: Eastburg L, Makhijani SA, Forconi CS, Moore D, Kharabora O, Kasonkanji E, Kampani C, Chikasema M, Roush SM, Kimani SM, Fedoriw Y, Juliano JJ, Moormann AM, Gopal S, Painschab MS
articles.details: Prospective observational cohort of 96 adult diffuse large B-cell lymphoma patients in Malawi (2013-2019) comparing rituximab plus chemotherapy (R-CHOP, n=37) to chemotherapy alone (CHOP, n=59). Symptomatic malaria occurred in 24% of R-CHOP recipients (9/37) versus 8% of CHOP recipients (5/59, OR 3.5, 95% CI 1.1-12, p=0.039). Among Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2-positive cases, antigen levels were significantly higher in R-CHOP recipients (3.6 vs 0.92 ng/mL, p=0.011). Rituximab-induced B-cell deficiency may increase symptomatic malaria risk.

articles.headline: Barriers to RTS,S vaccine uptake and delivery: MVIP insights
articles.title_original: Recommendations to mitigate barriers to uptake and delivery of a four-dose malaria vaccine schedule: insights from the MVIP's qualitative evidence.
articles.authors: Price J, Collymore Y, Bawa JT, Mkisi RE, Buell C, Jalang'o RE, Chisema M, Amponsa-Achiano K, Gordon WS
articles.details: Qualitative study from Malaria Vaccine Pilot (MVIP) in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi (2019-2023) examining barriers to RTS,S/AS01E vaccine uptake and four-dose schedule completion. Despite good vaccine acceptance, many children missed doses due to overlapping uptake and delivery challenges. The complexity of integrating a four-dose schedule into crowded routine immunization programs and difficulty tracking doses per child emerged as key barriers. Recommendations include developing guidance and tools to address schedule complexity and support vaccine expansion.

articles.headline: Five-decade malaria trends in Rajasthan, India: spatio-temporal analysis
articles.title_original: Echoes of resilience: spatio-temporal analysis of five-decade battle against malaria in Rajasthan, India.
articles.authors: Singh H, Singh S, Saroha P, Singh K, Sharma RS, Sharma RP, Gupta J, Gupta SK
articles.details: Spatio-temporal analysis of malaria in 4 ecogeographical regions of Rajasthan (Desert, Aravalli, Gangetic plain, Hadoti Plateau), 1975-2023. Malaria peaked in 1976 and 1996 with sharp post-2000 decline. Median API ranged from 1 (Gangetic plain) to 1.93 (Hadoti Plateau). Rainfall positively correlated with API in Desert (r=0.39, p=0.006) and Gangetic Plain (r=0.35, p=0.014) regions. Anopheles stephensi and An. culicifacies predominated, resistant to organochlorines/organophosphates but susceptible to pyrethroids, with Aravalli showing highest P. falciparum prevalence.

articles.headline: Vaccine awareness, acceptability, willingness to pay in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Awareness, acceptability, and willingness to pay for the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine: a cross-sectional study among pregnant women and nursing mothers in Enugu State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Dim OF, Chima UE, Iloabuchi FC, Anene-Okeke CG, Nwachuya CA, Onyehalu JC, Umeh AU, Agbo CA, Agbo JE, Isah A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 310 pregnant women and nursing mothers in Enugu State, Nigeria (mean age 22-33 years; 58.1% with child ≤3 years). R21/Matrix-M vaccine awareness was low (15.5%), but acceptability was high (94.2%). Willingness to pay was declared by 79.7% (247), with median WTP of US$0.38. Age (p=0.005) and health insurance (p=0.021) were significantly associated with WTP; health insurance predicted lower WTP (OR 0.417, 95% CI 0.19-0.92). Increased awareness campaigns could substantially improve vaccine coverage.

articles.headline: mRNA malaria vaccine candidates show sterilizing immunity in mice
articles.title_original: mRNA delivery of circumsporozoite protein epitope-based malaria vaccines induces protection in a mouse model.
articles.authors: Wu NR, Beutler N, Hu X, Skog PD, Liguori A, Flores-Garcia Y, Maiorino L, Terada S, Lu D, Lai YC, Ndihokubwayo J, Schiffner T, Cottrell CA, Eskandarzadeh S, Alavi N, Kubitz M, Phelps N, Tingle R, Hodges S, Youhanna JE, Amirzehni S, Irvine DJ, Himansu S, Zavala F, Rogers TF, Burton DR, Schief WR
articles.details: Mouse model tested self-assembling nanoparticle immunogens displaying different PfCSP epitope regions. mRNA-delivered immunogens displaying both junctional and NANP repeat epitopes were most effective, exhibiting 99% reduction in liver burden and sterilizing immunity from parasitemia in some mice. Authors note mRNA immunogens represent promising candidates for rapid translation to human challenge studies and could be combined with T cell vaccines.

articles.headline: PBO-treated nets show greater but non-significant malaria protection
articles.title_original: Do Piperonyl Butoxide Long-Lasting Insecticide Treated Nets Provide Additional Protection Against Malaria Infections Compared with Conventional Nets in an Operational Setting in Western Kenya?
articles.authors: Rascón-García K, Lapp Z, Markwalter CF, Kimachas E, Abel L, Obala A, Taylor SM, O'Meara WP, Mangeni JN
articles.details: Cohort study of 768 participants in Bungoma County, Western Kenya (June 2017–December 2023) compared piperonyl butoxide (PBO)-treated long-lasting insecticidal nets with conventional LLINs. PBO LLINs provided greater protection against malaria at individual level (odds ratio 0.70; 95% CI 0.47–1.03), though findings were not statistically significant. Authors conclude that in areas with documented pyrethroid resistance, PBO-treated nets should be considered for scaled-up scenarios despite additional cost.

articles.headline: Diagnostic delays and healthcare access drive malaria mortality in Colombia
articles.title_original: Inequitable Access to Healthcare and the Malaria Burden in Colombia: A Value-Based Policy Approach for Sustainable Elimination.
articles.authors: Olivera MJ, Magali Caro H, Ricardo Gómez J
articles.details: Longitudinal ecological study across Colombian municipalities (2010–2023) examined associations between healthcare access, diagnostic timeliness, and malaria outcomes. Diagnostic delay >48 hours (aOR 4.1; 95% CI 2.24–7.11) and very low healthcare access (aOR 3.5; 95% CI 1.94–6.37) were strongest mortality predictors; travel time >2 hours (aOR 2.8; 95% CI 1.62–5.15) and age <5 years (aOR 1.9; 95% CI 1.28–3.32) were independently associated with poor outcomes. Authors conclude persistent inequities in diagnostic timeliness and healthcare availability are barriers to elimination.

articles.headline: Pregnant women with vivax malaria show high recurrence and genetic diversity
articles.title_original: Parasite genotypes and host immune mediators in primary and recurrent episodes of vivax Malaria in Colombia.
articles.authors: Duque-Isaza YY, Carmona-Fonseca J, Arango-Flórez EM
articles.journal: Rev Peru Med Exp Salud Publica
articles.details: Two cohorts (41 pregnant women, 46 non-pregnant individuals) with uncomplicated vivax malaria were followed for 120 days in Cordoba, Colombia and genotyped using microsatellite markers (Pv3.27, Pv3.502, Pv1.501) and immune mediator expression. Recurrence frequency was significantly higher in pregnant women (41.6%) than non-pregnant individuals (8.7%), with higher parasite genetic diversity in pregnant women; recurrences were genetically homologous in only 23% of pregnant and 33% of non-pregnant cases. Authors conclude pregnant women have high vivax malaria recurrence with genetic variation that may affect protective immunity development and neonatal outcomes.

articles.headline: Indigenous communities perceive malaria as Western disease with spiritual dimensions
articles.title_original: Between their own and the western: the experience of Emberá communities in Chocó with Malaria.
articles.authors: Duque-Ortiz C, Osorno-Sánchez S, Gutierrez-Asprilla SD, Garcia-Tirado MJ, Nieto-Betancurt J, Guzman-Sánchez LF, Montoya-Cedula S, Pérez-Villa M, Botero-Palacio LE, Vera-Marin C, Hernández-Sarmiento JM
articles.journal: Rev Peru Med Exp Salud Publica
articles.details: Ethnographic study (October 2022–November 2023) conducted in six Emberá indigenous communities in Chocó, Colombia with 250 hours of participant observation and 25 interviews. Communities perceived malaria as a "Western disease" transmitted by mosquitoes, with the Jaibaná (traditional healer) playing a fundamental role in care by addressing spiritual conditions alongside Western treatment. Authors conclude incorporation of ancestral practices into health care is recommended, though currently done empirically based on public demand.

articles.headline: Antibody responses to merozoite proteins differ in severe vs uncomplicated malaria
articles.title_original: Antibody responses to polymorphic Plasmodium falciparum merozoite antigens in Malawian children with severe and uncomplicated malaria.
articles.authors: Dobaño C, Rogerson SJ, Cavanagh DR, Taylor TE, McBride JS
articles.details: Case-control study of 269 pediatric patients in Blantyre, Malawi with cerebral malaria (CM), severe malarial anaemia (SMA), or uncomplicated malaria (UM) examined IgG responses to polymorphic MSP-1 and MSP-2 antigens and concordance with infecting parasite genotypes. Lower IgG response to MSP-2 type B (FC27) correlated with CM, while lower response to MSP-2 type A (IC1/3D7) correlated with SMA. Discordant antibody-genotype responses were associated with neurological sequelae after CM compared to full recovery. Authors conclude antigenic diversity of blood-stage antigens could contribute to immune escape and malaria severity.

articles.headline: Weather-driven model predicts Zambian malaria outbreaks four months ahead
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreak prediction at the sub-district level in Zambia using remote sensing satellite data.
articles.authors: Ippolito MM, Kvit A, Xu T, Nande A, Bond C, Matoba J, Lubinda M, Shields T, Thuma P, Shiff C, Curriero FC, Moss WJ, Truelove S
articles.details: Forecasting study using 15 years of weekly health facility data from Choma District, Zambia (2010–2024) developed negative binomial regression model predicting seasonal malaria incidence from remote sensing temperature and rainfall. Mean nighttime temperature (November–January) and mean daily rainfall (December) were optimal predictors; cases increased with higher nighttime temperature (β=2.1; 95% CI 0.7–3.5; p=0.003) and greater precipitation (β=23.5; 95% CI –0.7 to 47.8; p=0.057). Model reproduced the 2020 outbreak within 4% accuracy (2367 observed vs 2467 predicted cases; 95% PI 1105–3828) based on weather conditions available four months prior.

articles.headline: Log-transformed polynomial regression forecasts malaria across Indian states
articles.title_original: Forecasting Malaria in Indian States: A Time Series Approach with R Shiny Integration.
articles.authors: Ghosh SK, Ananthakumar U, Chougale PD, Somaraj AB
articles.details: Time series forecasting study comparing multiple modeling approaches (polynomial regression, log-transformed polynomial regression, ARIMA) across eight Indian states (Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Odisha, Tripura, Uttar Pradesh) to predict malaria incidence. Log-transformed polynomial regression model consistently outperformed other methods across all states based on RMSE and MAPE metrics, with rolling forecast validation confirming superior predictive capability. An interactive R Shiny tool was developed to enable real-time malaria forecasting and decision-making support for researchers and public health officials.

articles.headline: Parasite genotypes differ between cerebral malaria and severe anemia
articles.title_original: Genotypic and antigenic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum merozoite proteins in Malawian children with severe and uncomplicated malaria.
articles.authors: Dobaño C, Rogerson SJ, Taylor TE, McBride JS
articles.details: Genetic characterization of P. falciparum from 261 pediatric patients in Malawi with cerebral malaria (n=93), severe malarial anaemia (n=50), both (n=26), or uncomplicated malaria (n=92) using MSP-1 and MSP-2 polymorphisms. Patients with severe malarial anaemia had significantly greater parasite genetic diversity (2.70±1.20) than cerebral malaria patients (2.25±0.89; p<0.01); MSP-2 FC27-type parasites were associated with SMA (66%) while IC1-type parasites were associated with CM (61.5%; p<0.025). Authors conclude patients with different malaria presentations could be distinguished partly by parasite merozoite genotypes, suggesting parasite antigenic variability influences malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Combined LLIN and treatment most cost-effective malaria control strategy
articles.title_original: Optimal control analysis of malaria infection in children under 5 years and pregnant women under the influence of temperature.
articles.authors: Robert PK, Irunde JI, Mtunya AP
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study of malaria transmission dynamics in children under 5 and pregnant women analyzed three control strategies: treatment of infected individuals, long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs), and indoor residual spraying (IRS). Treatment of infected individuals was most effective alone; when combined, LLINs plus treatment emerged as the most effective strategy, with all three controls together yielding no significant additional improvement over LLINs and treatment. Authors conclude combining LLINs with treatment of infected individuals provides the most cost-effective and efficient strategy for managing malaria transmission in high-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Deep learning with image segmentation detects malaria in blood smears
articles.title_original: Optimized CNN framework for malaria detection using Otsu thresholding-based image segmentation.
articles.authors: Singh R, Prabha C, Abdulla S
articles.details: Deep learning study using 43,400 blood smear images (70:30 training-test split) developed an optimized convolutional neural network (CNN) with Otsu-based thresholding segmentation for malaria detection. Baseline 12-layer CNN achieved 95% accuracy, improving to 97% with EfficientNet-B7 hybrid architecture and further to 97.96% with Otsu segmentation (Dice coefficient 0.848, Jaccard Index 0.738). Five-fold cross-validation confirmed robustness with accuracies of 94.8%, 96.9%, and 97.8%, demonstrating that segmentation-driven deep learning can develop reliable, scalable, cost-effective malaria diagnostic tools.

articles.headline: WHO value profile guides next-generation malaria vaccine and mAb development
articles.title_original: Value profile for Malaria vaccines and monoclonal antibodies.
articles.authors: Birkett AJ, Ansah E, Gordon S, Gyapong M, Hall S, Kelly SL, Laurens M, Penny MA, Shirey M, Slutsker L, Sparrow E, Ethelston S, Westercamp N, Wu L, Hamel MJ
articles.details: Vaccine value profile developed by WHO/Gavi working group assessing current and pipeline malaria vaccines and prophylactic monoclonal antibodies (mAbs). Two WHO-recommended vaccines introduced since 2021 showed 13% decline in all-cause mortality and 22% reduction in severe malaria hospitalizations in pilot introduction programs across sub-Saharan Africa. Authors discuss alignment with WHO's 2022 preferred product characteristics for next-generation vaccines and new guidance for mAb prophylaxis, noting potential to extend from prevention in young African children to population-level transmission-breaking interventions.

articles.headline: P5 ATPase structural features in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Homology Modeling of Type-P5 ATPases from the Malaria Parasite: Insight into Their Functions and Evolution, and Implications About the Effect and Role of Intrinsically Disordered Protein Structure.
articles.authors: Wiser MF
articles.details: Homology modeling study characterized haemosporidian type-P5 ATPases using yeast Spf1 and human ATP13A2 as structural templates. Malaria parasites possess both P5A and P5B subtypes with distinct features including an additional α-helix in P5A and intrinsically disordered loops. P5A likely functions as an ER transmembrane dislocase, while P5B has undergone lineage-specific functional divergence, suggesting parasite-specific adaptations of these ion transporters.

articles.headline: Essential oil and 6-ishwarone inhibit P. vivax in vitro
articles.title_original: First Evidence of Anti-Plasmodium vivax (Plasmodiidae): Activity of the Essential Oil and 6-Ishwarone Isolated from Piper alatipetiolatum Yunck. (Piperaceae).
articles.authors: Ramos GQ, de Azevedo RG, de Oliveira AC, da Costa MLL, da Silva FMA, Sá ISC, Melo GC, Pinto Lopes SC, Pontes GS, Nunomura SM, Nunomura RCS, Roque RA
articles.details: In vitro evaluation of Piper alatipetiolatum essential oil and its major constituent 6-ishwarone against P. vivax showed dose-dependent inhibition with IC50 values of 9.25 µg/mL and 3.93 µg/mL, respectively. Cell viability remained high (94.7–98.3%) at 24 hours, demonstrating selectivity toward the parasite. Molecular docking indicated selective binding of 6-ishwarone to dihydrofolate reductase (Ki = 2.27 µM) as the primary target, with favorable in silico ADMET properties supporting potential for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Subnational epidemiological stratification guides malaria interventions
articles.title_original: Epidemiological stratification and sub-national tailoring of malaria interventions in Liberia.
articles.authors: Koko VS, Hinneh DL, Kumeh O, Washington T, Alade JO, Duokie DS, Wesseh CS, Bawo L, Davis T, Zawolo J, Konwloh P, Jackson D, Kollah TC, Jones L, Janafo AS, Jeuronlon MK, Galatas B, Temu EA
articles.details: District-level malaria risk stratification in Liberia using 2022 Malaria Indicator Survey data and routine facility records analyzed parasite prevalence (PfPR) across 98 health districts. Median PfPR was 29% (SD 4.8%, range 17–37%), with 84 districts classified as moderate transmission and 14 as high transmission. The stratification informed targeting of eight key interventions and identified data gaps, establishing a framework for continuous data-driven decision-making and resource prioritization for malaria burden reduction.

articles.headline: Automated reports synthesize primaquine dosing effects for P. vivax
articles.title_original: Automated reporting of primaquine dose efficacy, tolerability and safety for Plasmodium vivax malaria using a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Edler P, Rajasekhar M, Price DJ, Adam I, Awab GR, Barber BE, Brasil LW, Chamma-Siqueira NN, Chu CS, Cui L, Daher A, Gomes MDSM, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Grigg MJ, Karunajeewa H, Lacerda MVG, Ladeia-Andrade S, Leslie T, Ley B, Lidia K, Llanos-Cuentas A, Longley RJ, Manning L, Mekonnen DA, Monteiro WM, Moore BR, Nosten F, Pasaribu AP, Pereira DB, Poespoprodjo JR, Pukrittayakamee S, Rijal KR, Saravu K, Siqueira AM, Sutanto I, Taylor WRJ, Thanh PV, Thriemer K, Vieira JLF, White NJ, Yeshiwondim AK, Zuluaga-Idarraga LM, Guerin PJ, Simpson JA, Price RN, Commons RJ
articles.details: Living systematic review compiled 9,270 individual patient records from 41 published studies of daily primaquine regimens for uncomplicated P. vivax malaria (data through January 2025). An open-access R Shiny application was developed to generate automated reports assessing location-specific effects of primaquine mg/kg dose on antirelapse efficacy, hematological safety, and gastrointestinal tolerability. The reports provide accessible evidence to inform national malaria control policies for P. vivax treatment optimization and policy decisions in vivax-endemic regions.

articles.headline: Malaria documentation and reporting severely limited in retail pharmacies
articles.title_original: Exploring documentation and reporting practices in malaria tracking: a mixed-methods study among formal drug retail shops in Hohoe Municipal, Ghana.
articles.authors: Loglo V, Gmayinaam VU, Ampomah MA, Boateng P, Ansah EK
articles.details: Mixed-methods study of 57 formal drug retail facilities in Hohoe Municipality, Ghana (9 community pharmacies, 48 over-the-counter medicine sellers) found that 51 (89.5%) lacked any documentation and reporting tools, with only 5 (10.5%) using paper-based methods. Among 70.2% counter medicine assistants (median age 26 years), 61.4% favored digital reporting but faced barriers including resource constraints and inadequate training. Significant differences existed between pharmacy types (p = 0.004), highlighting critical gaps in malaria surveillance infrastructure in the formal private sector.

articles.headline: Blighia sapida extract shows antimalarial and hepatoprotective activity
articles.title_original: Antimalarial, Antioxidant and Hepatoprotective Activities of Ethanol Extract of Blighia sapida K.D. Koenig (Sapindaceae) in Plasmodium berghei-infected Mice.
articles.authors: Akinyemi DO, Olorunfemi AB, Akinola O, Abiodun OO, Afolabi OK, Thomas BN, Gbotosho GO, Ojurongbe O
articles.details: In vivo study in Swiss mice inoculated with chloroquine-resistant P. berghei tested Blighia sapida ethanolic extract at 50–800 mg/kg using Peters 4-day suppressive and Rane's curative tests, with piperaquine/dihydroartemisinin 18/4 mg/kg as positive control. Maximum suppressive activity was 77.02% and curative activity 71.94% at 800 mg/kg with no toxicity up to 5000 mg/kg. Hepatoprotective effects were significant across doses, with 400 mg/kg showing strongest reduction in serum AST (84.93 vs 133.88 U/L) and ALT (68.48 vs 93.92 U/L) versus negative control.

articles.headline: Plant smoke potently kills malaria vectors in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of ethnomedicinal plants' smoke knockdown and repellent efficacy against pre-bedtime biting malaria vectors in Mazowe district, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Nyasvisvo DS, Sande S, Sithole R, Nhiwatiwa T
articles.details: Field study in Mazowe district, Zimbabwe evaluated knockdown and repellent efficacy of smoke from three native plants against malaria vectors.
