Vitex payos and Peltophorum africanum both achieved 100% knockdown of An. gambiae s.l. within 150 minutes with kdT50 of 37.8 and 44.8 minutes respectively and 24-hour mortality of 91.7% and 80%; Adansonia digitata showed slower knockdown (kdT90 146.9 minutes, mortality 71.6%). In field huts, V. payos reduced An. funestus s.l. biting by 86% (p = 0.008) and P. africanum by 73% (p = 0.028), suggesting potential to complement insecticide-treated nets.

articles.headline: Novel β-carbolines active against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Characterization of β-Carboline Derivatives Reveals a High Barrier to Resistance and Potent Activity against Ring-Stage and DHA-Induced Dormant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Haney RS, Butler JH, Wardlaw LA, Merino EF, Mendiola V, Cooper CA, Mathew J, Tumwebaze PK, Rosenthal PJ, Cooper RA, Kyle DE, Rizopoulos Z, Baud D, Brand S, Totrov M, Carlier PR, Cassera MB
articles.details: Characterization of β-carboline compounds (PRC1584, PRC1697) demonstrated high resistance barrier with potent in vitro activity against fresh Ugandan P. falciparum isolates, targeting both actively proliferating ring stages and dihydroartemisinin-induced dormant stages. PfKelch13-C580Y mutation was associated with increased susceptibility to PRC1584; conversely, PfKelch13-R549T and Pfcoronin-R100 K-E107V mutations showed no effect. The differential resistance profile suggests a novel mechanism of action and therapeutic potential for future artemisinin combination therapies targeting resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Histone lysine methyltransferases regulate P. falciparum epigenome
articles.title_original: Yet to SET: Plasmodium falciparum Histone Lysine Methyltransferases.
articles.authors: Shah D, Karmodiya K
articles.details: Review of epigenetic regulation in P. falciparum, focusing on histone lysine methyltransferases (SET proteins). The parasite's AT-rich genome and limited heterochromatin necessitate epigenetic control for survival and virulence. P. falciparum histones undergo diverse post-translational modifications with parasite-specific epigenetic signatures distinct from the host. While histone acetylation has been extensively studied, the role of histone lysine methyltransferases remains understudied, representing promising targets for novel antimalarial interventions with distinct mechanisms of action.

articles.headline: Platelet TGF-β1 drives cerebral malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Platelet-Derived Transforming Growth Factor-β1: A New Hope for Cerebral Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Liu S, Guo B, Song L, Chen G, Zhang T, Du Y
articles.details: Mechanistic review identified platelets as pivotal in cerebral malaria pathogenesis through platelet-derived transforming growth factor (TGF)-β1, which induces endothelial cell apoptosis, microangiopathy, and blood-brain barrier compromise. Reduced TGF-β1 concentrations in tissue and peripheral circulation correlate with cerebral malaria severity. The paper proposes targeting TGF-β1 using exosomes as natural drug carriers for brain-specific delivery as a novel therapeutic approach to prevent cerebral edema and inflammation in this fatal neurological complication.

articles.headline: Cell-permeable nucleotide analogs halt P. falciparum DNA replication
articles.title_original: Membrane-Permeable 5-Fluorodeoxyuridine Triphosphate Derivatives Inhibit the Proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nikolova V, Linnemannstöns K, Bendel ME, Machado M, Ganter B, Budimir P, Vogts M, Fischer C, Ganter M, Meier C, Dobbelstein M
articles.details: Study evaluated cell-permeable 5-fluorodeoxyuridine triphosphate derivatives (cpFdUTP) against P. falciparum in infected human red blood cells. cpFdUTP rapidly and potently inhibited parasite proliferation, arresting development at the trophozoite-to-schizont transition through DNA replication stalling. While cpFdUTP impaired human cell growth, concurrent thymidine or cell-permeable deoxythymidine triphosphate (cpdTTP) supplementation selectively rescued human cells while maintaining parasite inhibition, identifying a potential therapeutic window for cpFdUTP combination therapy.

articles.headline: AsSGU identified as transmission-blocking vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Cloning, expression and purification of AsSGU protein and its immunogenic response analysis as target candidate for transmission blocking vaccine against malaria.
articles.authors: Singh H, Kirar M, Janjoter S, Dahiya N, Yadav M, Sehrawat N
articles.details: Study cloned and expressed recombinant AsSGU (Anopheles stephensi Secretory Glycoconjugate), a GPI-anchored midgut protein critical for Plasmodium ookinete invasion and transmission. The protein is highly conserved across Anopheles, Culex, and Aedes species. Recombinant protein was expressed in E. coli BL21(DE3) at 0.2 mg/L yield and characterized for immunogenic properties. Rabbit immunization demonstrated immunological responses, supporting AsSGU as a potential transmission-blocking vaccine candidate based on its confirmed role in parasite transmission through the mosquito.

articles.headline: Meteorological factors drive malaria incidence in Cameroon children
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal dynamics of malaria incidence in children under five years of age and correlation with meteorological variability in the North and Far North regions of Cameroon between 2015-2022.
articles.authors: Mouliom A, Ilorme F, Ateba JM, Ekoyol G, Fotso J, Kidwang JP, Tchopwe J, Djele S, Dzika JO, Essangui Same E, Ayong L, Ali IM, Eboumbou Moukoko EC
articles.details: This spatiotemporal analysis examined malaria incidence in children <5 years in Cameroon's North and Far North regions (2015-2022), correlating cases with meteorological variables using distributed lag nonlinear models. Maximum incidence was 190.6 per 1000 person-years (North) and 243.8 per 1000 (Far North). Rainfall >1 mm/day increased transmission risk 1.2-fold (North) to 1.4-fold (Far North); 70% humidity increased risk 2-fold (North) to 3.7-fold (Far North). Authors conclude meteorological factors strongly predict malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Pfsa1 and PfCRT K76T drive genetic sweeps in Mali parasites
articles.title_original: Genome-wide SNP genotyping of Plasmodium falciparum isolates across Mali reveals major impacts of Pfsa1 and PfCRT K76T selections on parasite populations.
articles.authors: Niaré K, Dara A, Sissoko S, Crudale R, Koné A, Doucouré H, Sanogo V, Cissoko M, Sissoko MS, Tékété M, Koné A, Diarra B, Dembélé M, Koné B, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Djimdé AA
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 458 P. falciparum isolates from Mali (2023) using molecular inversion probes identified selection signals around pfcrt and pfacs8 (Pfsa1). Polygenomic infections ranged 13.0–44.0% by region. Compared to wild-type parasites, those carrying Pfsa1 showed higher prevalence of PfCRT K76T (55.8% vs 26.6%, P<0.005) and sickle cell trait (8.4% vs 1.3%). A major haplotype encompassed 80% of K76T mutants. Authors conclude parasite populations show clear selective sweeps linked to drug pressure and host genetics.

articles.headline: DNA repair pathways maintain P. falciparum chromosome structure
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum DNA repair dynamics reveal unique roles for TLS polymerases and PfRad51 in genome diversification.
articles.authors: Vishwanatha A, Zhang X, Liu YJ, Leung A, Herring M, Visone J, Chan A, Calhoun S, Deitsch K, Kirkman L
articles.details: Using genetic knockout studies of PfRad51 and TLS polymerases (PfPolζ, PfRev1), this study revealed differential DNA repair mechanisms across P. falciparum chromosomes. PfRad51 was essential for homologous recombination in the conserved core genome, while subtelomeric regions (containing var genes) employed a Rad51-independent, TLS-independent pathway. The authors propose these differential repair responses maintain the unique chromosomal structure defining P. falciparum biology.

articles.headline: Reactive malaria surveillance fidelity varies by region
articles.title_original: Implementation and fidelity of reactive surveillance and response strategies for malaria elimination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Aye Tun N, Thu KM, Cutts JC, Htike W, Htwe EP, Oo MC, Zaw AK, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 69 studies examined implementation fidelity of reactive surveillance and response strategies across malaria elimination settings. The '1-3-7' strategy achieved high fidelity in China (100% case investigation completeness, 97% timeliness). In contrast, Africa and Greater Mekong Subregion showed lower fidelity: timeliness of case investigation was 56% (95% CI 26–83%) and 57% (95% CI 37–77%), respectively. Authors conclude implementation fidelity varies significantly by region and health system capacity.

articles.headline: Malaria and NTDs burden higher in migrant populations
articles.title_original: The burden, clinical outcomes and risk factors related to neglected tropical diseases and malaria in migrant populations in the Middle East and North Africa: a systematic review and meta-analyses.
articles.authors: Elafef E, Maatoug T, Evangelidou S, Marti-Soler H, Adam A, Arisha AH, Hilali M, Hargreaves S, Bani I, Seedat F, Requena-Méndez A
articles.details: This meta-analysis of 16 malaria and 39 NTD studies examined disease burden in migrants across 5–11 Middle East and North Africa countries. In Sudan, malaria incidence among internally displaced persons was 6.8/1000 and among refugees 2.72/1000 (refugees <5 years: 7.3/1000). Malaria hospitalization rate was 25.8%, case fatality 0.2–1.5%. Compared to non-migrants, migrants showed higher prevalence of hookworm (1.8% vs 0.03%) and dengue (26% vs 3.5%). Authors conclude migrants are disproportionately affected by malaria and NTDs.

articles.headline: Digestive vacuole phases reveal heme detoxification dynamics
articles.title_original: Heme processing in the malaria parasite, Plasmodium falciparum: a time-dependent basal-level analysis.
articles.authors: Garnie LF, Egan TJ, Wicht KJ
articles.details: This study examined digestive vacuole (DV) physiology in P. falciparum using microscopy and immunological techniques. In chloroquine-sensitive NF54 parasites, DV development followed three phases: lag-type growth (20–28 h), rapid growth (28–40 h), and plateau (40–48 h), each with distinct characteristics regarding heme uptake, plasmepsin abundance, and hemozoin levels. Key differences were observed between CQ-sensitive NF54 and CQ-resistant Dd2 parasites. The study provides mechanistic insights into hemozoin formation, a validated antimalarial drug target.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria complicated by sickle cell disease in adolescent
articles.title_original: Protracted Febrile Illness and Coma in an Adolescent with Sickle Cell Disease and Malaria.
articles.authors: Judd L, Bevot A, Lalremruata A, Flesche C, Mayer A, Mezger M, Heideking K, Bélard S, Remppis J
articles.details: Case report of a 15-year-old with sickle cell disease presenting with severe hemolysis, fever, and neuropsychiatric symptoms (5 weeks duration) after travel to West Africa. Cerebral malaria was eventually diagnosed and treated with artesunate, leading to prompt recovery. However, another hemolysis episode occurred 12 days after artesunate treatment. The case illustrates the complex interplay between malaria, SCD, and autoimmune hemolytic anemia, emphasizing the importance of timely malaria testing in vulnerable travelers.

articles.headline: Metabolomics identifies P. knowlesi drug targets and pathways
articles.title_original: Mass spectrometry-based metabolomics uncovers distinct metabolic signatures and potential therapeutic targets in Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Uthailak N, Chotirat S, Anatjitsupha A, Thongyod W, Tipthara P, Sattabongkot J, Tarning J, Nguitragool W, Reamtong O
articles.details: This metabolomic study examined three intraerythrocytic stages of P. knowlesi (ring, trophozoite, schizont) using mass spectrometry. Analysis revealed distinct metabolic profiles, with ring-stage parasites showing unique metabolic characteristics. Glycerophospholipid metabolism and sphingolipid biosynthesis emerged as common pathways across all stages, while phosphatidylserine synthesis was ring-stage specific. The authors identified CDP-diacylglycerol-inositol 3-phosphatidyltransferase as a promising therapeutic target. Findings enhance understanding of P. knowlesi biology and support drug development.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention appropriateness in East Africa
articles.title_original: Is Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention with Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine/Amodiaquine Appropriate in East Africa?
articles.authors: Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: No abstract available; based on title, this article questions whether sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine is appropriate for seasonal malaria chemoprevention in East Africa.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine outperforms SPAQ for SMC in Uganda
articles.title_original: Pharmacometric evaluation of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine- amodiaquine and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine seasonal malaria chemoprevention in northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Bonnington C, Nuwa A, Theiss-Nyland K, Kajubi R, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa J, Ebong C, Nabakooza J, Opigo J, Salandindi D, Odongo M, Sararat C, Watson JA, Suwannasin K, Proux S, Koesukwiwat U, Tarning J, Imwong M, Tibenderana J, Nosten FH, White NJ
articles.details: This prospective 28-day pharmacometric study compared seasonal malaria chemoprevention regimens in 1250 children in Northern Uganda. Baseline parasitemia prevalence was 46% (P. falciparum 85%, P. ovale 25%). Against breakthrough parasitemia, protective efficacy was 56% for sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine (95% CI 35–70%) and 84% for dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (95% CI 77–89%). Against clinical malaria, efficacy was 60% (SPAQ) vs 86% (DP). All 260 P. falciparum isolates carried K76 mutations with high Pfdhfr/dhps resistance. Authors conclude DP was superior to SPAQ for SMC in this high-resistance setting.

articles.headline: Proteasome inhibitors show promise for malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Optimization of Species-Selective Reversible Proteasome Inhibitors for the Treatment of Malaria.
articles.authors: Gahalawat S, Ray S, Zhang X, Deng X, Han Y, Chen Z, Lawong A, Shackleford DM, Katneni K, Chen G, Li P, Ng A, Zhong L, Hu M, McInerney M, Wang W, Saunders J, Collins D, Jayaseelan J, Noack CL, C Maity B, De N, Laleu B, Campbell SF, Phillips MA, Charman SA, Ready JM
articles.details: This medicinal chemistry study optimized species-selective reversible inhibitors of the P. falciparum 20S proteasome. Lead compounds based on a carboxypiperidine scaffold demonstrated nanomolar potency against blood-stage P. falciparum parasites with selective inhibition of the parasite proteasome over the human enzyme. Cryo-EM confirmed binding at the β5 subunit. In vivo pharmacokinetic studies identified promising lead candidates. Authors conclude proteasome inhibition represents a viable strategy for novel antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Malaria-typhoid coinfection prevalent in Cameroon febrile patients
articles.title_original: Malaria and Typhoid Fever Coinfection Among Febrile Patients Attending a Health Facility in Yaoundé, Cameroon: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Netongo PM, Tchoutang AM, Eric MB, Tepa A, Nzuno MC, Kamdem SD
articles.details: This cross-sectional study of 288 febrile patients in Yaoundé, Cameroon (June–August 2023) examined malaria and typhoid fever using microscopy, serology, and antigen detection. Malaria prevalence was 44.79% (129/288), typhoid 37.15% (107/288), and coinfection 19.79% (57/288). Coinfection was significantly associated with fever (p=0.008) and profuse sweating (p=0.05). Risk factors included water sources and environmental conditions. Authors conclude malaria was the leading febrile cause with notable coinfection prevalence.

articles.headline: β-Dystroglycan cleavage disrupts blood-brain barrier in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: β-Dystroglycan Downregulation and Astrocytic Alterations: A Possible Role in Blood-Brain Barrier Disruption During Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Karri S, Babu PP
articles.details: In an experimental cerebral malaria model, researchers measured blood-brain barrier integrity at 5 days post-infection (early stage) and 7 days post-infection (terminal stage). They found increased Evans blue dye extravasation, FITC-dextran leakage, and brain edema at terminal stage compared with artemether-treated animals. Loss of β-dystroglycan, altered astrocyte morphology, and reduced zonula occludens-1 and collagen IV indicated that β-DG cleavage by matrix metalloproteinase-9 disrupts astrocytic connection with vasculature, leading to edema.

articles.headline: PbIMC1j protein regulates ookinete morphology and subpellicular microtubule organization
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei IMC1j interacts with δ-tubulin to orchestrate subpellicular microtubule organization in ookinetes.
articles.authors: He G, Feng Y, He D, Hu Y, Wang D, Wang S, Sun X, Shi S, Yang T, Hu X, Huang Y, Cui L, Cao Y, Zhu X
articles.details: In Plasmodium berghei, the IMC1j protein interacts with δ-tubulin to organize subpellicular microtubules. Gene knockout of PbIMC1j led to defects in asexual stage fitness and virulence, altered ookinete morphology, and impaired motility and infectivity in ookinetes and sporozoites. The C-terminal coiled-coil domain is required for targeting to the inner membrane complex and interaction with δ-tubulin and the IMC protein ISC1.

articles.headline: RON6 protein maintains Plasmodium sporozoite infectivity and virulence
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei rhoptry neck protein 6 maintains parasite infectivity and virulence.
articles.authors: Narahari V, Patri S, Satpute MD, Vemuganti GK, Mishra DK, Kolli SK, Arun Kumar K
articles.details: In Plasmodium berghei, RON6-depleted parasites failed to maintain infectivity and virulence, resulting in prolonged mouse survival. The mutants induced chronic malaria and hyper-reactive malarial splenomegaly, characterized by decreased B and T lymphocytes and loss of lymphoid follicles. RON6 localizes to the sporozoite membrane with an extracellular C-terminal domain and is required for invasion of red blood cells, hepatocytes, and liver stage development.

articles.headline: Extensive ribosomal RNA degradation occurs during P. falciparum gametocytogenesis
articles.title_original: Degradation of ribosomal RNA during Plasmodium falciparum gametocytogenesis.
articles.authors: Grünebast J, Singhal R, Bromley R, Kanatani S, Watson K, Olson S, Dumetz F, Pascini TV, Tripathi A, Dunning Hotopp JC, Sinnis P, Llinás M, Serre D
articles.details: Using Oxford Nanopore direct RNA sequencing of synchronized parasites, A-type ribosomal RNAs predominated during asexual development and gametocytogenesis, while S-type rRNAs gradually increased in mosquito stages starting 3 days post-infection. Extensive rRNA degradation occurred during gametocytogenesis, starting in stage II gametocytes and continuing through final stages. This degradation may be linked to translational repression and metabolic quiescence in stage V gametocytes, similar to stress responses in bacteria and eukaryotes.

articles.headline: CRISPR gene-drive generates target-site mutations at consistent rates in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Mutant allele formation and inheritance during Cas9/guide RNA-mediated gene drive in a population modification mosquito strain for human malaria control.
articles.authors: Carballar-Lejarazú R, Pham TB, Tushar T, James AA
articles.details: In Anopheles gambiae carrying a TP13-based Cas9/guide RNA gene-drive system, drive-system-mediated target-site mutagenesis occurred at average rates of 0.08% per target gene per generation in paternal lineages, 0.33% in maternal testcross lineages, and 0.21% overall. Mutations originating in germlines represented 47% of total inherited mutations, with the remainder from paternal and maternal effects inherited through parental lineages. These empirically derived rates inform realistic gene-drive modeling.

articles.headline: Complement inhibition may reduce malarial anemia by blocking C3b opsonization
articles.title_original: Inhibition of Complement Will Substantially Reduce the Anemia of Malaria.
articles.authors: Taylor RP, Lindorfer MA
articles.details: Review evidence that complement, particularly C3b opsonization, drives elimination of non-parasitized erythrocytes during malaria: for every Plasmodium falciparum or P. vivax-infected red blood cell destroyed, at least 10 non-parasitized red blood cells are cleared. Complement activation occurs from lysed parasitized erythrocyte breakdown products (hemin/hematin, digestive vacuoles, hemozoin) and causes both childhood malarial anemia and post-artesunate delayed hemolysis. The authors propose testing FDA-approved C3 complement inhibitors as therapy for malarial anemia.

articles.headline: Sporozoite movement is shaped by skin microenvironment biophysical and biochemical factors
articles.title_original: Influence of biophysical and biochemical cues on Plasmodium sporozoite dynamics in skin tissue.
articles.authors: Gopakumar N, Chen H, Messenger LA, Park S
articles.details: This review examines how Plasmodium sporozoite motility during skin-stage infection is affected by extracellular matrix composition, tissue stiffness, surface topography, temperature, and humidity. Sporozoites must navigate the dermal microenvironment to reach blood vessels for successful infection. The review identifies significant gaps in understanding how age-related, inflammation-induced, or mechanical stress-induced skin changes influence sporozoite behavior, highlighting the need for deeper insight into underlying signaling pathways and novel diagnostic approaches.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention reduced uncomplicated malaria incidence 50% in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Impact of seasonal malaria chemoprevention: a plausibility evaluation of routine data from health facilities in three implementing states in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Ikechukwu EC, Okereke E, Ogunmola O, Chukwumerije J, Emeto D, Salifu E, Balogun A, Oguoma C, Shekarau E, Ogbulafor N, Cassidy E, Rassi C, Oresanya O, Nnaji CA
articles.details: A pre-post evaluation of seasonal malaria chemoprevention implementation in 36 health facilities across three Nigerian states (Kogi, Oyo, Federal Capital Territory) targeting children aged 3-59 months. Mean uncomplicated malaria incidence was 20 cases per 1000 children in 2021 and 9 cases per 1000 in 2022. Malaria incidence was 50% lower (95% CI: 39-60) in the SMC period compared with pre-SMC, though impact varied by location with greatest reduction in Oyo state and no evidence of impact in Kogi state.

articles.headline: Uganda's '24,2 Hours Initiative' improves timely malaria case management and care continuity
articles.title_original: The 24, 2 hours initiative: a game changer in malaria mortality reduction.
articles.authors: Ranjbar M, Katureebe C, Musanhu CC, Maureen A, Nabakooza J, Rukundo GB, Maiteki C, Mpimbaza A, Opigo J
articles.details: Uganda launched the '24,2 Hours Initiative' to improve malaria case management timing: uncomplicated cases treated within 24 hours, severe cases receiving pre-referral or injectable treatment within 2 hours of facility arrival, and post-discharge malaria services within 24 hours of discharge. The initiative emphasizes patient-centered approaches, continuity of care across pre-referral and post-discharge settings, and free community-based malaria case management to improve affordability and equity in primary health care access.

articles.headline: EFFORT trial: tafenoquine and high-dose primaquine effectively prevent P. vivax relapse
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of tafenoquine and primaquine for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax: a meeting report from dissemination of results of the EFFORT trial to stakeholders in Pakistan.
articles.authors: Qurashi B, Zaidi SE, Mushtaque M, Faisal A, Haroon Z, Agha B, Hammal N, Hameed A, Raza A, Ali U, Khan A, Jabbar FA, Adam ZR, Mehmood T, Price RN, Ghanchi N, Thriemer K, Beg MA
articles.details: In Pakistan, where Plasmodium vivax causes approximately 85% of confirmed malaria cases, the EFFORT clinical trial across four endemic countries evaluated single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg) and 7-day high-dose primaquine (7 mg/kg total dose) versus the standard 14-day low-dose primaquine (3.5 mg/kg). Both tafenoquine and high-dose primaquine were well tolerated and effective in preventing relapse. In Pakistan specifically, high-dose primaquine showed modest but consistent added benefit over the standard regimen, consistent with other South Asian countries, while tafenoquine demonstrated benefits beyond 2024 WHO guideline recommendations.

articles.headline: PCPI trial evaluates sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine efficacy against K540E dhps mutation in Zambia
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance and protection from Plasmodium falciparum infection (PCPI): a two-arm, parallel, double-blinded, placebo-controlled, randomised trial of presumptive sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine versus artesunate monotherapy among asymptomatic children 3-5 years of age in Zambia.
articles.authors: Martinez-Vega R, Chaponda M, Mousa A, Gwasupika J, Chaponda EB, Daka V, Mwanza S, Chipunga M, Beshir KB, Chopo-Pizarro A, Kaur H, Okell L, Hansson H, Hocke EF, Alifrangis M, Gosling R, Roper C, Sutherland C, Chico RM
articles.details: A double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized trial in Zambia comparing presumptive sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (n=400) and artesunate monotherapy (n=200) in asymptomatic children aged 3-5 years to evaluate the effect of the K540E dhps gene mutation on SP chemoprevention efficacy. Primary outcomes include time-to-parasite clearance among SP-positive children through day 63, mean duration of SP protection against infection, and duration of symptom-free status. Dried blood spots and thick smear slides were collected through day 63 for qPCR and genotyping using next-generation sequencing.

articles.headline: High prevalence of Pfdhps mutations A437G and A581G in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Polymorphism of the dihydropteroate synthase resistance gene of Plasmodium falciparum in three hospitals in Mbouda, Western Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nadia NAC, Cedric Y, Line GAP, Pacome TK, Armand GNG, Anaud NKJ, Esther DD, Edna GDM, Yval KT, Mbida M, Fuyou Z, Hu H
articles.details: In 296 samples from three hospitals in Mbouda, Cameroon, P. falciparum prevalence was 25.34% and Pfdhps resistance gene prevalence was 74.66%. The A437G mutation occurred in 56% of samples and A581G in 64%. Mutant alleles showed higher frequencies than wild-type (57.14% vs 42.85% for A437G; 91.66% vs 8.33% for A581G). The age group 6-35 years carried the majority of resistance alleles, with females (77.8%) showing higher prevalence than males (71.8%).

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine awareness and acceptance vary widely in Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Public awareness and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Ekhator E, Essien VG, Afolabi OE, Okundaye MI, Adewonbi T
articles.details: Systematic review of 33 studies (2021-March 2025) on vaccine awareness and acceptance in Sub-Saharan Africa. Awareness ranged from 7.26% (Democratic Republic of Congo) to 95.6% (Ghana); acceptance ranged from 10.3% to 100%, generally higher in Nigeria and Ghana. Education and occupation were consistently associated with greater awareness and acceptance; healthcare workers were the main information source.

articles.headline: Community trust and engagement shape ivermectin mass drug administration participation
articles.title_original: Community Experiences and Perceptions of the Broad One Health Endectocide-Based Malaria Intervention in Africa Trial of Ivermectin Mass Drug Administration: A Longitudinal Qualitative Study in Kwale County, Kenya.
articles.authors: Wangari W, Onyango T, Kazungu K, Nuru K, Kasiwa L, Chaccour C, Rabinovich NR, Mwangangi J, Maia M, Jones C
articles.details: Longitudinal qualitative study of the BOHEMIA ivermectin MDA trial in Kwale County, Kenya, including 22 in-depth interviews and 15 focus group discussions across villages. Trust in implementing institution and previous positive experiences fostered participation, while previous negative MDA experiences reduced involvement. MDA was widely perceived as effective in reducing mosquitoes and malaria in both trial arms.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine maintains efficacy against P. falciparum and P. vivax
articles.title_original: Treatment Efficacy of Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine for Uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Timika, Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Kambuaya NN, Ramli MSS, Candrawati F, Kenangalem E, Prayoga P, Puspitasari AM, Noviyanti R, Trianty L, Indrawanti R, Simatupang M, Ubra RR, Hill J, Hafidz F, Poespoprodjo JR
articles.details: Treatment efficacy study in Timika, Papua recruiting 75 P. falciparum and 75 P. vivax patients receiving supervised dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine for 3 days with day-42 follow-up. Cumulative risk of same-species recurrence was 1.5% (95% CI 0-7.9%, PCR-adjusted) for P. falciparum and 5.2% (95% CI 1.1-14.1%, unadjusted) for P. vivax. No kelch13 mutations or increased plasmepsin 2-3 copy numbers were detected, with no evidence of high treatment failure or resistant parasite selection.

articles.headline: Plasmodium actin-like proteins essential for successful parasite transmission
articles.title_original: Plasmodium actin-like proteins are essential for DNA segregation during male gametogenesis and malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Varshney A, Pandey E, Nirdosh, Mishra S
articles.details: Study of actin-like proteins (Alp5a, Alp5b) in P. berghei showing these proteins are essential for DNA segregation during male gametogenesis. While individually dispensable during blood stage, Alp5a and Alp5b knockouts produced significantly smaller oocysts that failed to mature. Defects in male gamete integrity and impaired DNA segregation prevented successful malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Pfs230:Pfs48/45 structure informs transmission-blocking vaccine design
articles.title_original: Cryo-EM structure of endogenous Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex with six antibodies reveals mechanisms of malaria transmission-blocking activity.
articles.authors: Bekkering ET, Yoo R, Hailemariam S, Heide F, Ivanochko D, Jackman M, Proellochs NI, Stoter R, van Gemert GJ, Maeda A, Yuguchi T, Wanders OT, van Daalen RC, Inklaar MR, Andrade CM, Jansen PWTC, Vermeulen M, Bousema T, Takashima E, Rubinstein JL, Kooij TWA, Jore MM, Julien JP
articles.details: Cryo-EM structure of the endogenous Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex bound to six transmission-blocking antibodies revealed multiple functional domains and conserved epitopes. The structure identified mechanisms of complement-dependent activity of Pfs230-targeting antibodies, informing development of next-generation Plasmodium falciparum transmission-blocking interventions.

articles.headline: Malaria trial researchers lack post-trial access plans in Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Post-trial access practices in conducted clinical trials for Malaria, Tuberculosis, and Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) across Sub-Saharan African countries: A quantitative study.
articles.authors: Seralegne Y, Wangamati CK, Bernabe RC, Mdala I, Zewdie M, Adane HT
articles.details: Survey of 37 principal investigators and trial coordinators from malaria, TB, and NTD clinical trials conducted in Sub-Saharan Africa (2008-2019). Findings showed 43% of respondents did not provide post-trial access (PTA) plans for trial interventions, with an overall lack of formalized PTA policies, though 70.3% expressed need for PTA training.

articles.headline: Severe malaria mortality in Senegalese children linked to respiratory distress
articles.title_original: Characteristics and mortality factors of severe malaria in children and teenager aged from 5 to 15 years in Thiès region, Senegal.
articles.authors: Lawson ATD, Sidibé M, Mahmoud MA, Bahada BHB, Diop SA
articles.details: Retrospective study of 109 children aged 5-15 hospitalized for severe malaria at 4 referral hospitals in Thiès, Senegal (July 2019-June 2024). Common presentations included severe anemia (33%), thrombocytopenia (61.62%), prostration (27%), and convulsions (21.10%). Mortality (4.5%) was significantly associated with respiratory distress and ≥2 WHO severity criteria; 90% had favorable outcomes with 5.5% experiencing neurological sequelae.

articles.headline: Multigravidae recognize distinct VAR2CSA epitopes for placental malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Identification of Plasmodium falciparum VAR2CSA peptides differentially recognized by IgG of multigravidae through epitope excision.
articles.authors: Misal SA, Renn JP, Morrison RD, Cowles MV, Mahamar A, Attaher O, Dicko A, Duffy PE, Fried M
articles.details: Study using epitope excision and quantitative proteomics to identify VAR2CSA epitopes differentially recognized by IgG of multigravidae compared to primigravidae. Multigravidae differentially recognized ten conserved VAR2CSA epitopes (Log2 fold change >1, p<0.05), while most epitopes were similarly recognized, with implications for VAR2CSA subunit vaccine design.

articles.headline: IMC55 protein not required for Plasmodium blood-stage development
articles.title_original: The inner membrane complex protein, IMC55, is dispensable for intra-erythrocytic development of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Vick GW, Fierro MA, Park L, Brooks C, Vasant M
articles.details: Study of inner membrane complex protein IMC55 in P. falciparum using conditional expression systems. Despite transposon screening predictions of essentiality, inducible expression revealed no defect in replicative fitness during intra-erythrocytic asexual replication, indicating IMC55 function is either dispensable or redundant for blood-stage development.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence, parasitemia, and anemia vary by ecological zone in Ghana
articles.title_original: Clinical malaria in three ecological zones in the Volta and Oti Regions of Ghana.
articles.authors: Axame WK, Dinko B, Kweku M, Binka FN
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 459 participants across three ecological zones (Ho forest, Keta coastal, Nkwanta savannah). Overall malaria prevalence was 15.2% by RDT and 9.7% by microscopy; geometric mean parasite density differed significantly (Keta 11,715.6 vs Nkwanta 7,616.4 vs Ho 5,880.1 parasites/µL; p=0.0003). Anemia prevalence was 79.3% overall with zone-specific differences supporting need for ecology-specific interventions.

articles.headline: Strong but imperfect RDT recording accuracy in Uganda health facilities
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording of malaria rapid diagnostic test results in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ssewante N, Namuganga JF, Katahoire A, Musoke J, Mutesi N, Humes M, Griffith K, Aponte JJ, Soebiyanto R, Cooper S, Agaba B, Opigo J, Lindblade KA, Mpimbaza A
articles.details: Prospective study of RDT interpretation accuracy in 16 public health facilities across Uganda (June-November 2023). Of 37,137 RDTs analyzed, overall agreement between healthcare workers and external panel was strong (κ=0.81, 95% CI 0.76-0.85). HCWs misrecorded results as positive (7.2%) more than negative (1.9%), with higher accuracy in Busoga (κ=0.86) compared to Lango (κ=0.78).

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence and mortality fluctuate across localities in Sudan
articles.title_original: Epidemiological trends of malaria in Sennar State, Sudan.
articles.authors: Abubakr M, Mohamed NS, Sami H, Sabon K, AbdElbagi H, Osman OF, Ahmed A
articles.details: Descriptive cross-sectional survey of 2,027 households (8,372 individuals) in Sennar State, Sudan (2018-2020). Malaria prevalence rose from 2.8% (2018) to 4.8% (2019) then fell to 3.3% (2020); malaria-related deaths increased from 12 (both 2018 and 2019) to 22 (2020). Significant locality-specific variations were observed with Abu Hujar showing highest prevalence.

articles.headline: Copper-dependent cell death pathway exacerbates cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Cuproptosis-driven astrocyte reactivity exacerbates experimental cerebral malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Hou X, Mo X, Zhang X, Wang Q, Chen X, Lai C, Gao J, Wu L, Liu W, He J, Zeng X, Yin H, Deng Z, Liu T, Ye M, Liu Z, Jin X, Song J, Wang J, Huang B
articles.details: Using a P. berghei ANKA-induced experimental cerebral malaria model in mice, researchers tested pharmacological modulation with copper ionophore disulfiram (DSF) versus copper chelator tetrathiomolybdate (TTM). DSF administration exacerbated disease progression through amplified parasitemia, aggravated blood-brain barrier permeability, cerebral edema, and neuroinflammatory responses, while TTM counteracted these pathological manifestations, including suppression of astrocyte reactivity. The authors conclude that cuproptosis exacerbates cerebral malaria pathogenesis by promoting astrocyte reactivity, positioning copper homeostasis modulation as a potential therapeutic strategy.

articles.headline: Training improves LAMP diagnostic skills in resource-limited settings
articles.title_original: Evaluation of laboratory workers competency on loop mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) technology for malaria diagnostics: a technology transfer assessment study (LAMP-TTA) during a multi-site clinical trial.
articles.authors: Tegegne B, Kamaliddin C, Pillai DR
articles.details: Fifteen laboratory technicians enrolled in a loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) competency assessment study, with 14 reporting no previous LAMP experience. Post-training, self-assessed technical knowledge median rating increased by 4 points (median 5/5, range 4/5-5/5) and LAMP chemistry knowledge increased by 9 points (median 10/10, range 6/10-10/10). At follow-up visits, all participants reported confidence running LAMP reactions, with raw results interpretation median scores of 10/10. The authors conclude that adequate training, support, and evaluation are essential for successful technology transfer programs.

articles.headline: Malaria messages increase bed net use among women and children
articles.title_original: Effect of exposure to malaria messages on insecticide-treated net use among women and under-five children in Uganda: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Agbi DM, Iha DT, Agasa LO, Mwangi A
articles.details: Using Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey 2018-19 data, propensity score analysis on 8,868 women and 6,915 children found 69.3% [95% CI: 67.2-71.2] of women and 71.8% [95% CI: 69.1-74.4] of children slept under an insecticide-treated bed net the preceding night. Exposure to malaria messages had a significant positive effect on net use, with Average Treatment Effect of the Treated = 5.1% [95% CI: 1.5-8.9%] for women and 4.3% [95% CI: 1.0-7.6%] for children. The authors conclude that behavioral change communications are an effective way of increasing bed net use and may reduce malaria burden.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic infections and multiple vectors drive residual malaria
articles.title_original: Submicroscopic infections and two malaria vectors are major contributors to residual malaria in an Amazonian village in Peru.
articles.authors: Guzmán-Guzmán M, Saavedra MP, Rodriguez P, Acosta C, Gomez J, Valdivia HO, Sanchez JF, Vittet M, Manrique E, Herrera-Varela M, Morales J, Bickersmith SA, Vinetz JM, Gamboa D, Conn JE
articles.details: In a year-long study in Amazonian Peru, 353 inhabitants enrolled in monthly longitudinal cohort study and population screening revealed a 17:1 ratio of submicroscopic to microscopic malaria infections detected by qPCR. Despite high mosquito net usage, residents with outdoor occupations had significantly higher P. vivax infection rates (35% versus 21%, p = 0.012). Plasmodium-infected Nyssorhynchus darlingi were detected across all 5 study landscapes; a putative secondary vector, Anopheles near costai G1, was infected with P. vivax and P. malariae, reported for the first time in Peru. The authors conclude that submicroscopic infections and two malaria vectors are major contributors to residual malaria.

articles.headline: Bayesian method improves falciparum treatment failure classification
articles.title_original: Probabilistic classification of late treatment failure in uncomplicated falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Mehra S, Taylor AR, Imwong M, White NJ, Watson JA
articles.details: Development of PfRecur software to classify recrudescence versus reinfection in uncomplicated falciparum malaria using Bayesian posterior probabilities addresses limitations of standard match-counting methods that have unacceptably high false-discovery rates in polyclonal infections common in high-transmission settings. The model appropriately accounts for multiple comparisons in polyclonal infections and demonstrates potential utility using data from a recent study in Angola. The authors conclude that PfRecur resolves complex polyclonal P. falciparum infections and provides accurate treatment failure rate estimation.

articles.headline: Non-pyrethroid IRS dramatically reduces malaria in Zambia district
articles.title_original: Impact of malaria vector control interventions implemented in Luangwa District southeastern of Zambia: A 13-year observational time series analysis of malaria trends.
articles.authors: Chinula D, Hamainza B, Chiwaula J, Mumba K, Zulu R, Ndhlovu K, Kiware S, Reed T, Killeen GF
articles.details: In Luangwa District, a 13-year observational study (2009-2021) using HMIS data and generalized linear mixed models assessed effects of long-lasting insecticidal nets and indoor residual spraying (IRS) with pirimiphos-methyl, deltamethrin, and other formulations. IRS with pirimiphos-methyl (micro-encapsulated formulation) reduced inpatient admissions for severe malaria by 72% (RR 0.28, 95% CI 0.19-0.98, p=0.0019) for ≤3 months post-spray and reduced total confirmed cases by 75% (RR 0.25, 95% CI 0.01-0.67, p=0.0017). Overall, >90% fewer inpatient admissions and >80% fewer cases occurred by study end, largely following IRS scale-up in late 2014 (RR 0.36 per year, p=0.0001). The authors conclude that IRS with durable non-pyrethroid insecticides and improved diagnostic/treatment access were clearly associated with substantial malaria reductions.

articles.headline: Community engagement essential for sustainable malaria control
articles.title_original: Experiences of Community Members and Health Workers Regarding Malaria Control Programmes in the Rural Ingwavuma Community, uMkhanyakude District Municipality, KwaZulu-Natal Province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Thembakazi MT, Herbert C, Nelisiwe K
articles.details: Qualitative study in a rural South African community using 8 in-depth interviews with healthcare workers and 8 focus group discussions with 88 community members explored experiences with malaria control programs. Community members viewed malaria control activities positively, particularly health education, indoor residual spraying, and vector surveillance; however, healthcare workers identified challenges including illegal cross-border population movement. Some participants reported negative experiences with indoor residual spraying and social protests occasionally disrupted spraying activities. The authors conclude that community-based malaria control measures have been instrumental in preventing malaria, with continued community engagement essential to maintain program success.

articles.headline: Malaria microscopy infrastructure can diagnose other skin diseases
articles.title_original: Leveraging malaria microscopy infrastructure to diagnose common and neglected skin diseases using direct microscopy in Sumba, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Siregar GO, Harianja M, Smith DJ, Ataupah MR, Laiskodat RD, Bøgh C, Soebono H, Grijsen ML
articles.details: This study explores leveraging existing malaria microscopy infrastructure in Sumba, Indonesia to diagnose common and neglected skin diseases using direct microscopy.

articles.headline: Ivermectin trial efficacy inconclusive due to operational challenges
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to reduce malaria transmission- safety and efficacy results from the BOHEMIA cluster randomized trial in Mozambique.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Nicolas P, Martinho S, Mundaca H, Elobolobo E, Ruiz-Castillo P, Houana A, Montaña J, Mbanze J, Casellas A, Macucha A, Mael M, Soares A, Kiuru C, Gutierrez AS, Imputiua S, Constantino L, Vegove V, Cole G, Duthaler U, Ribes M, Mutepa V, Brew J, Munguambe H, Stanulovic S, Xerinda A, Materula F, Gorski N, Wanjiku C, Furnival-Adams J, Túnez L, Sam L, Collins L, Xia K, Hammann F, Rudd M, Rist C, Jones C, Maia M, Candrinho B, Rabinovich NR, Saute F
articles.details: The BOHEMIA cluster-randomized trial in Mopeia, Zambezia province (>70% coverage of IRS and LLINs) randomized clusters to receive either monthly 400 mcg/kg ivermectin to humans for three months, ivermectin to humans plus injectable ivermectin to pigs and cattle, or albendazole (control). Originally planned for 53 clusters per arm, cyclone flooding resulted in retention of 100 clusters with 22,724 participants (study March-October 2022). Six months after first treatment, malaria infection incidence was comparable between arms (adjusted IRR 0.99 [95% CI 0.86-1.14, p>0.9] for human-only vs control; 1.05 [95% CI 0.87-1.27, p=0.6] for human+livestock vs control). The authors conclude that operational challenges rendered efficacy data inconclusive, though safety data showed no significant difference in adverse events (IRR 1.29, 95% CI 0.62-2.68).

articles.headline: School-based malaria prevention with piperaquine highly effective
articles.title_original: Implementation and effectiveness of intermittent preventive treatment in school aged children using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine to reduce malaria burden: an implementation research of a cluster randomised trial in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Mmbando B, Seth MD, Baraka V, Challe DP, Francis F, Mhina AD, Liheluka E, Minja DTR, Chiduo M, Mtove G, Mandara C, Gesase S, Segeja MD, Kamugisha M, Hayuma PM, Mbwana JR, Sebukoto H, Malabeja A, Tupa JB, Ngede SJ, Lusasi A, Chacky F, David A, Thawer SG, Mohamed A, Aaron S, Lazaro S, Molteni F, Nkayamba A, Bastiaens H, Van Geertruyden JP, Lusingu JPA
articles.details: Cluster-randomized trial in Tanzania delivered three annual rounds of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine to school-aged children (5-15 years) via schoolteachers, covering 127 schools with >73,000 schoolchildren. In effectiveness evaluation with 1,971 children receiving DP and 1,781 controls, DP reduced clinical malaria incidence by 41% overall [95% CI: 31-49; p<0.001] and 54% in high-endemic strata [95% CI: 44.2-62.6; p<0.001], with parasite prevalence decreasing by 81% overall [95% CI: 56.3-100; p<0.001]. IPTsc coverage reached 77-80% across three rounds, and DP was well tolerated. The authors conclude that large-scale IPTsc implementation achieved impact comparable to randomized trials and was operationally feasible and well-accepted.

articles.headline: P450 metabolic enzymes enable malaria vector insecticide resistance
articles.title_original: Investigating the potential role of metabolic resistance genes in conferring cross-resistance to pyrethroids and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon pollutants in the major malaria vector Anopheles coluzzii.
articles.authors: Muhammad A, Ibrahim SS, Ismail HM, Irving H, Paine MJI, Wondji CS
articles.details: In vitro study evaluated microsomal fractions from pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles coluzzii and recombinant P450 enzymes for metabolism of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (fluorene, fluoranthene, naphthalene). Resistant An. coluzzii microsomes significantly depleted fluorene (73%±0.5, p=0.001) and fluoranthene (43%±2.2); steady-state kinetics for fluorene showed Km 58.69 µM±20.47 and turnover 37.016 min⁻¹±3.67. CYP6P3 and CYP6Z3 showed significant fluorene metabolism (47.9%±2.3 and 52.8%±0.8, p=0.001), while CYP6Z2 and CYP6Z3 metabolized fluoranthene (50.4%±4.9 and 60.3%±5.3, p<0.003); no naphthalene metabolism was observed. The authors conclude that P450s from malaria vectors can metabolize environmental pollutants, potentially selecting resistance-conferring P450s in field populations.

articles.headline: High MSP2 genetic diversity indicates intense P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of MSP2 and its implications for Plasmodium falciparum resistance in N'Djamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Abel D, Christelle Nadia NA, Cedric Y, Pacome TK, Bismarck N, Ngaryedji T, Merveille TT, Hervé MM, Deborah DK, Hu H, Michel N
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 302 patients from four health facilities in N'Djamena, Chad examined P. falciparum prevalence (40.72%) and MSP2 gene polymorphism using nested PCR and phylogenetic analysis. Of 129 positive samples analyzed, 101 (78.21%) carried the MSP2 gene with 12 different alleles identified, yielding genetic diversity of 9.37%. Multiplicity of infection was 1.26, with 73.26% monoclonal, 25.74% double clonal, and 1% triple clonal infections; phylogenetic analysis revealed 11 distinct clades across study sites. The authors conclude that significantly high genetic diversity and multiplicity of infections indicate high transmission intensity and a critical malaria transmission situation.

articles.headline: RDT recording errors increase misdiagnosis risk in Nigerian clinics
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording and reporting of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Atobatele S, Sampson S, Orya E, Okoro O, Akpiroroh E, Okagbue H, Gab-Deedam SD, Bademosi O, Atu G, Lindblade KA, Mukhtar AM, Ayodeji O, Eugene EC, Aponte JJ, Galles N, Humes M, Griffith K, Cooper S, Ogbulafor N, Okoronkwo C, Ntadom G
articles.details: Evaluation of 18,319 RDTs from 16 health facilities in Nigeria; 6.2% misrecorded as positive (positive predictive value 87.2%, 95% CI 86.4–87.8%) and 3.7% as negative (negative predictive value 92.9%). Overall agreement 90.2% (95% CI 89.7–90.6%); kappa 0.80 (95% CI 0.75–0.85) with substantial variation across facilities (range 0.59–0.92). Faint test lines (8.8%) associated with increased likelihood of misrecording as negative.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge high in Gambia's Upper River Region despite burden
articles.title_original: Evaluation of knowledge, perception, and attitude of malaria burden in the Upper River region of The Gambia.
articles.authors: Okafor UA, Yaffa S, D'Alessandro U, Ojeh VN, Filho WL
articles.details: Survey of 381 household heads in Upper River Region across seven districts; participants demonstrated 81% knowledge index of basic malaria concepts. Over 2011–2023, malaria cases fell 87% in ages >5 years and 45% in under-5s; mortality decreased 98% and 89% respectively. High understanding of malaria concepts influenced positive prevention attitudes, though implementation varied across districts.

articles.headline: Quality antenatal care increases uptake of malaria prevention in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Association between adequacy of antenatal care and uptake of intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy among women in Ghana.
articles.authors: Bayuo FB, Antwi EO, Adu-Boahen N, Effah EA, Appiah SK, Opoku HA, Efiong MB, Acheampomaa G, Senyah MM, Bofa A, Afoakwah G, Haile ZT
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 3,692 pregnant women from 2022 Ghana DHS; 61.3% achieved ≥3 doses of IPTp-SP. Women with ≥8 ANC visits: OR 1.50 (95% CI 1.18–1.90, p=0.001); first ANC visit in first trimester: OR 1.74 (95% CI 1.44–2.09, p<0.001); receipt of all ANC components: OR 1.62 (95% CI 1.29–2.03, p<0.001). Adequate ANC independently associated with higher IPTp-SP uptake.

articles.headline: Child health and environment directly drive malaria risk in Uganda
articles.title_original: Structural equation modelling (SEM) for malaria prevalence and risk factors in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kakaire G, Chumoh EC, Salyungu M, Kerich G, Too RK, Kosgei M
articles.details: Structural equation modeling analysis of 2018–2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey with latent constructs (socioeconomic status, environment, maternal health, child health). Child Health had strongest positive association with malaria status (β=0.22, p<0.001); Environment showed marginal negative association (β=−0.36, p=0.056). Model fit adequate (CFI 0.786, RMSEA 0.064). Framework identifies child-level health and environmental exposures as priority intervention targets.

articles.headline: Human movement patterns shape optimal malaria elimination zones in Bangladesh
articles.title_original: Identifying malaria elimination strategies in the presence of human movement in Bangladesh.
articles.authors: Mahmud AS, Chang MC, Citron DT, Engø-Monsen K, Sayeed AA, Zaman SI, Uddin D, Rahman MM, Rahaman M, Islam MN, Maude RJ, Buckee CO, Chang HH
articles.details: Metapopulation malaria model of Chittagong Hill Tracts parameterized by human mobility and epidemiological data. Substantial heterogeneity in transmission intensity across districts; estimated basic reproduction number varies greatly despite similar observed incidence. Vector control most effective in areas with both high transmission intensity and high connectivity to western populated districts. Local elimination in low–intermediate incidence areas alone has moderate overall impact.

articles.headline: Pfs230D12 domain shows potent transmission-blocking vaccine activity in mice
articles.title_original: Pfs230 domain 12 is a potent malaria transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.
articles.authors: Inklaar MR, de Jong RM, Da DF, Hubregtse LL, Meijer M, Teelen K, Bekkering ET, Grievink S, van de Vegte-Bolmer M, van Gemert GJ, Stoter R, Nagaoka H, Tsuboi T, Takashima E, Spruijt CG, Vermeulen M, Dabire RK, Arinaitwe E, Cohuet A, Bousema T, Jore MM
articles.details: Eight Pfs230 single-domain fragments produced and immunogenicity assessed in mice. D12-specific antibodies demonstrated strong functional transmission-reducing activity in membrane feeding assays, complement-dependent. D12 antisera reduced mosquito transmission of parasites from naturally infected carriers. D12 antigen recognized by sera from naturally exposed individuals with age-related antibody increases. Identified as promising transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Malaria stratification identifies 55 districts for targeted control in Mali
articles.title_original: Geo-epidemiological risk stratification to select malaria interventions, case of Mali.
articles.authors: Cissoko M, Magassa M, Cissé IA, Coulibaly S, Samaké DS, Sanogo V, Koné M, Thiam S, Ballo T, Diarra S, Fomba S, Koné A, Gaudart J, Sagara I
articles.details: Risk stratification of 75 Health Districts (2018–2022 data) per WHO Elimination Framework; 54 HDs classified in highest and moderate transmission categories (84% of population, ~18 million). Tailored intervention packages: 61 HDs for LLIN mass distribution, 57 for seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC), 67 for IPTp, 19 for vaccination. Stratification enabled geo-epidemiological prioritization of control interventions.

articles.headline: US babesiosis cases exceed malaria but receive vastly less research support
articles.title_original: Babesiosis and Malaria in the United States: Epidemiology, Research Funding, Medical Progress, & Recommendations for Improvement.
articles.authors: Jajosky RP, Li W, Jajosky AN, Jajosky PG, Stowell SR
articles.details: Analysis of CDC surveillance data (2018–2022) and NIH funding; 9,799 confirmed babesiosis cases vs 7,722 malaria cases with babesiosis exceeding malaria in 4 of 5 years. Deaths from babesiosis (70) exceeded malaria (32). NIH 2024 funding: ~$4 million for babesiosis vs ~$169 million for malaria. Authors conclude babesiosis qualifies as rare and orphan disease warranting increased federal support.

articles.headline: Ensemble deep learning achieves 97.93% accuracy in malaria detection
articles.title_original: An optimized transfer learning approach integrating deep convolutional feature extractors for malaria parasite classification in erythrocyte microscopy.
articles.authors: Reddy CKK, Anisha PR, Almushharaf A, Talla R, Baili J, Cho Y, Nam Y
articles.details: Ensemble learning model integrating VGG16, ResNet50V2, DenseNet201, and VGG19 transfer learning architectures with data augmentation. Test accuracy 97.93% (F1-score 0.9793, precision 0.9793), outperforming standalone models (Custom CNN 97.20%, VGG16 97.65%, CNN-SVM 82.47%). Effectively classified parasitized and uninfected erythrocyte microscopy images. Scalable automated solution reduces manual microscopy workload in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Cryo-EM reveals ten ribosomal states in malaria parasite translation
articles.title_original: Seeing is believing-Plasmodium falciparum translation in action.
articles.authors: Erath J, Pavlovic Djuranovic S
articles.details: Cryoelectron tomography and microscopy of Plasmodium falciparum ribosomes within infected erythrocytes; ten ribosomal states resolved with five previously unseen in eukaryotes. Comprehensive characterization of parasite translation elongation cycle. Visualization shows how antimalarial cabamiquine disrupts parasite elongation. Advances mechanistic understanding of parasite-specific translation dynamics.

articles.headline: Thrombocytopenia prevalence 27.4% in malaria; younger age key risk factor
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with severe thrombocytopenia among hospitalized patients with malaria attending Kigeme District Hospital, Southern Province, Rwanda: cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ndatumuremyi J, Kagimbangabo JMV, Nshimiyimana I, Gaspard B, Sendegeya JP, Nizeyimana V, Byukusenge C, Nsanzabera C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 124 hospitalized malaria patients (December 2023–November 2024) at Kigeme District Hospital, Rwanda; severe thrombocytopenia prevalence 27.4%. Independent risk factors: age ≤5 years (adjusted OR 5.17, 95% CI 1.67–16.26, p=0.004), Plasmodium species (AOR 3.4, 95% CI 2.41–8.3, p=0.04), positive C-reactive protein (AOR 3.45, p=0.045), jaundice (AOR 2.88, p=0.045), epistaxis (AOR 1.84, p=0.042), high serum urea (AOR 1.73, p=0.001). Cerebral malaria protective (AOR 0.19, p=0.028).

articles.headline: Optimized artemether-lumefantrine dosing achieves 95-100% cure in infants under 5 kg
articles.title_original: Pharmacokinetics, safety and efficacy of an optimized dose of artemether-lumefantrine in the treatment of acute uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in neonates and infants of less than 5 kg body weight: a multicentre, open-label, single-arm phase 2/3 study (CALINA).
articles.authors: Wounounou G, Tiono AB, Ogutu B, Manyando C, Sagara I, Schneitter S, Bassat Q, Gaaloul ME, Marrast AC, Demin I, Winnips C, Risterucci C, Hugot S, Hofstetter G, Qian Z, Su G, Zhang J, Renner KC, Cousin M, Venishetty VK, Sayyed S, Gandhi P, Kabore B
articles.details: Multicentre phase 2/3 study of new formulation 5 mg artemether:60 mg lumefantrine given twice daily for 3 days in patients <5 kg with Plasmodium falciparum malaria; Cohort 1 (n=22, >28 days) and Cohort 2 (n=6, 1–28 days). Artemether Cmax comparable to historical data in 5–<15 kg children (Cohort 1: 68.0 ng/mL, 90% CI 45.1–103). Day 29 PCR-corrected adequate clinical and parasitological response 95.5% and 100%; well-tolerated with normal developmental assessments.

articles.headline: Climate change drives increased malaria incidence in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria in Chiredzi District, Zimbabwe: emerging evidence and pathways towards malaria prevention.
articles.authors: Musakwa W, Selamolela NB, Ndlovu A, Zanamwe C, Dhlandhlara B, Mandinyenya B
articles.details: Study in Chiredzi District analyzed weather data, malaria incidence (2010-2022), and focus group discussions on climate change perceptions. Found positive relationship between climate change and malaria incidence (P=0.0479), with increases in rainfall, temperature, and extreme weather events. Effect attributed partly to socioeconomic factors like poverty and migration. Authors emphasize need for forecasting malaria based on climate and socioeconomic conditions for prevention programs.

articles.headline: SMC reduces uncomplicated, severe malaria for weeks post-administration
articles.title_original: Trends in uncomplicated and severe malaria following seasonal malaria chemoprevention administration in Nouna, Burkina Faso: a quasi-experimental pre-post study.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: Quasi-experimental pre-post study of 285 villages in Burkina Faso using surveillance data. SMC administration associated with uncomplicated malaria rate of 8.5 per 1000 person-weeks and severe malaria rate of 0.31 per 1000 person-weeks in administration weeks. Post-SMC reductions: uncomplicated malaria 41%, 34%, 22% in weeks 1–3; severe malaria 47%, 47%, 34% in weeks 1–3. Protection limited to immediate weeks post-administration, suggesting need for extended rounds in longer transmission seasons.

articles.headline: COVID-19 pandemic caused severe decline in malaria healthcare utilization
articles.title_original: Malaria healthcare service utilization before, during, and 1 year into the COVID-19 pandemic in 20 sub-Saharan African countries using Generalized Estimating Equation.
articles.authors: Asnake AA, Eshun G, Osborne A, Sarfo M
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 4,726 adults (aged ≥16) across 20 sub-Saharan African countries using Generalized Estimating Equations. One year into pandemic, malaria testing declined from 71.77% to 59.12%, healthcare provider visits for malaria symptoms dropped from 80.61% to 1.25%, and treatment receipt fell from 97.97% to 15.23%. Participants were 45% less likely to be tested (AOR=0.55, 95% CI 0.48–0.63), 99.7% less likely to visit providers (AOR=0.003, 95% CI 0.002–0.005), and 99.6% less likely to receive treatment (AOR=0.004, 95% CI 0.002–0.006).

articles.headline: Education and awareness shape malaria vaccine uptake in mothers
articles.title_original: Untangling choices: how awareness and socio-economic status shape malaria vaccination decisions among Ghanaian women.
articles.authors: Ansah RK, Tackie S, Asosega KA, Tawiah K, Danquah BA, Appiah ST, Twum RA, Omane-Adjepong M
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 3,004 women from 2019 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey found 93.54% intended to vaccinate vs. 6.45% did not. Among pro-vaccine respondents, 72.10% had formal education and 40.10% were vaccine-aware; among anti-vaccine, 78.35% were educated and 28.87% were aware. Rural educated women showed modest positive association (1.62% increase in motivation), while urban educated women showed negative social effect (−4.06%). Education, vaccine awareness, and social context jointly shape vaccination decisions.

articles.headline: Health facility malaria messages increase mosquito net use
articles.title_original: A comparative analysis of the predictive influence of access to malaria messages on utilization of mosquito nets by under-five children in West Africa.
articles.authors: Ajaero ID, Nwachukwu CA, Oramah CP, Ajaero CK
articles.details: Analysis of post-2020 Malaria Indicator Surveys from Liberia, Mali, Niger, Nigeria with nationally representative women samples (aged 15–49). U5 net usage ranged from 47% (Nigeria) to 86% (Niger), with exposure to malaria information from health facilities significantly predicting net use. Regression analysis showed increased odds of U5 net use in Liberia (OR=1.31), Mali (OR=9.90), Niger (OR=3.67), but reduced odds in Nigeria (OR=0.31). Health worker-based malaria campaigns recommended, particularly in regions with low net use.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium exposure alters Culex pipiens microbiota composition
articles.title_original: Effects of avian Plasmodium exposure on the microbiota of Culex pipiens.
articles.authors: Garrigós M, García-Ruiz O, Enkvist CR, García-López MJ, Moreno-Indias I, Ruiz-López MJ, Veiga J, Figuerola J, Videvall E, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: 16S rRNA metabarcoding study of Culex pipiens exposed to Plasmodium relictum-infected house sparrows vs. uninfected birds. Identified 2,006 ASVs dominated by Wolbachia; no significant differences in alpha/beta diversity. Exposed mosquitoes showed higher relative abundance of Bacteroidaceae/Bacteroides and Rikenellaceae families. Pathway enrichment analysis revealed bacterial community enriched in biosynthesis and metabolism pathways in exposed mosquitoes.

articles.headline: Correction to EEG features in cerebral malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Correction to: Electroencephalogram Features Distinguish Cases of Cerebral Malaria Among Malawian Children With Fever and Coma.
articles.details: Correction published to prior study on electroencephalogram features distinguishing cerebral malaria cases among Malawian children with fever and coma.

articles.headline: EEG features distinguish cerebral malaria from other coma causes
articles.title_original: Electroencephalogram Features Distinguish Cases of Cerebral Malaria Among Malawian Children With Fever and Coma.
articles.authors: Andrews A, Harrar DB, Zelleke T, Zhang J, Zhang B, Barber J, Dasan R, Amoah N, Findorff O, Krieger S, Barrett E, O'Brien N, Fuller CE, Moxon CA, Taylor TE, Griffiths MJ, Ray STJ, Postels DG
articles.details: Retrospective case-control study at Queen Elizabeth Central Hospital, Malawi (2013–2021) comparing EEG recordings of 203 children with cerebral malaria vs. 87 with nonmalarial febrile coma (bacterial meningitis, viral encephalitis, unknown). Machine learning analysis showed EEG features distinguish malarial from nonmalarial coma: qualitative interpretation AUROC=0.85, quantitative AUROC=0.86, combined AUROC=0.90. Cerebral malaria associated with higher voltage, slower frequency, more sleep elements, less variability.

articles.headline: Venezuelan migration significantly predicts malaria in Trinidad and Tobago
articles.title_original: Assessing the Potential for a Malaria Outbreak in Trinidad and Tobago Due to Migrant Populations From Guyana and Venezuela for the Period 2009-2019.
articles.authors: Narinesingh-Motie R, Ivey M, Edwards J, Edwards RJ
articles.details: Census analysis of 211 malaria cases (2009–2019); mean age 35 years, 74% male. Common parasites: P. vivax (68.2%) and P. falciparum (22.3%), with 91.9% imported (Guyana 39.7%, Venezuela 38.7%, Sub-Saharan Africa 15.5%). Linear regression showed moderate correlation between Venezuelan arrivals and malaria incidence (r=0.642, R²=0.412, p=0.033). Population movement from high malaria-risk areas significantly predicts transmission and potential outbreak risk.

articles.headline: Antimalarial drugs show differential mitochondrial effects in mice
articles.title_original: Comparative effects of antimalarial drugs on oxidative phosphorylation, mitochondrial dynamics and mitophagy in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Olanlokun JO, Yahaya OO, Babarinde CO, Oloke OM, Steenkamp P, Prinsloo G
articles.details: Experimental study in 35 Swiss mice infected with chloroquine-resistant P. berghei (ANKA strain), treated orally with 10 mg/kg each of four drugs: amodiaquine artesunate (AA), artemether-lumefantrine (AL), sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), and artesunate (ART). ART opened mPT pore (7-fold) and enhanced FoF1 ATPase (P<0.01); SP and AA induced mitochondrial lipid peroxidation (P<0.01) and silenced mitochondrial complex expressions. Varying effects on mitochondrial fission/fusion and mitophagy markers observed.

articles.headline: Environmental barriers shape An. coluzzii gene flow and resistance
articles.title_original: Genomic population structure and insecticide resistance mechanisms in the malaria vector An. coluzzii across contrasting bioclimatic zones in West Africa.
articles.authors: Amoako EK, Bennett KL, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Debrah I, Morang'a CM, Binaansim S, Asoala VA, Ariana C, Malm KL, Awandare G, Miles A, Clarkson CS, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: Genomic study of An. coluzzii across West African bioclimatic zones using whole-genome SNPs and inversion karyotypes. Found restricted gene flow between northern savannah and southern forested regions. Ghana case study showed marked differences in insecticide resistance profiles: northern regions displayed greater target-site pyrethroid and metabolic cross-resistance; coastal Greater Accra had distinct mechanisms possibly from urban agricultural activity. Environmental conditions restrict gene flow and impact geographical distribution of molecular resistance.

articles.headline: Novel 4-quinolones show broad-stage antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the Activity of 4‑Quinolones against Multi-Life Stages of Plasmodium spp.
articles.authors: Annunciato Y, da Silva EM, Araújo JE, Martinez LDN, Santana S, Workneh EA, S Alvarez LC, Magalhães ML, Andrade AO, da Costa WJ, Campolina G, Nascimento Santana M, Barbosa CS, Peres EPML, Moura CS, Dos Santos NAC, Bastos ADS, Barros RRM, Gazarini ML, Medeiros JF, Teles CBG, Costa FTM, Alves AC, Pereira DB, Pinto J, Cravo PVL, Guido RVC, Prudêncio M, Araujo MDS, Corrêa AG, Cassiano GC, Aguiar ACC
articles.details: Novel 4-quinolone compounds tested for multistage antimalarial activity against asexual intraerythrocytic, liver, and transmission-blocking Plasmodium stages. Compound 1 (natural 2-substituted-4-quinolone) and five derivatives (2–6) displayed significant activity against sexual stages in ex vivo and in vivo assays and effectively prevented hepatic cell infections. Compound 1 showed highest activity against both sexual and asexual stages. Compounds warrant further development as potential new antimalarials.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum drug-resistance SNPs in Southwest Cameroon
articles.title_original: Targeted Sequencing Identifies SNPs Associated with Antimalarial Drug Resistance and the CSP Vaccine Antigen in Plasmodium falciparum from Southwest Cameroon.
articles.authors: Efeti MT, Fankem SN, Diallo MT, Lahngong MS, Acha NL, Shey RA, Demeyer K, Souopgui J, Ghogomu SM, Njemini R
articles.details: Targeted Oxford Nanopore sequencing of 72 blood samples from Southwest Cameroon identified high prevalence of pfdhfr mutations (98.6% N51I, C59R; 97.7% S108N), pfmdr1 Y184F (76.1%), and pfdhps mutations (54.2% S436A, 38.9% A581G). No WHO-validated artemisinin resistance markers were found, though K189T (63.4%) and R255K (4.2%) variants were detected. Findings support continued artemisinin-based combination therapy use.

articles.headline: Seasonal travel patterns and rainfall drive malaria importation to Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Integrating mobility, travel survey, and malaria case data to understand drivers of malaria importation to Zanzibar, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Muller JG, Dewalt K, Goel V, Ali M, Hassan W, Mohamed B, Choloi BB, Msolo D, Zacharia A, Nwange M, Kane R, Bisanzio D, Emch M, Björkman A, Reithinger R, Shija SJ, Ngasala BE, Juliano JJ, Lin JT
articles.details: Among 3,875 malaria cases presenting to Unguja health facilities, 1,172 reported mainland Tanzania travel within 30 days (283/511 [55%] in 2022, 889/3,348 [27%] in 2023). Children and females with mainland travel had higher odds of malaria (aOR 3.2, 95% CI 2.2–4.8 and aOR 1.5, 95% CI 1.3–1.8, respectively). Travel to Tanga (20%), Dar es Salaam (20%), and Morogoro (15%) was most common. Imported cases correlated with rainfall at mainland destinations.

articles.headline: ITN textile, age, and poverty associated with net utilization in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Factors associated with insecticide-treated mosquito nets utilization for malaria prevention in Burkina Faso: finding from cross-sectional household survey.
articles.authors: Badolo H, Hien H, Hien AS, Somda SMA, Bazié H, Bacyé FY, Diallo CO, Tierno A, Guira M, Meda N
articles.details: A cross-sectional household survey in Burkina Faso identified factors influencing ITN use. Polyester net textiles increased utilization (OR 1.23, 95% CI 1.13–1.33), while older nets (≥12 months, OR 0.52, 95% CI 0.401–0.675) and excessive ITN possession (OR 0.66, 95% CI 0.597–0.74) decreased use. Wealthier households showed lower ITN utilization (very rich, OR 0.557, 95% CI 0.484–0.641). Health education strengthening is recommended.

articles.headline: Ferulenol inhibits asexual and sexual stages of Plasmodium falciparum
articles.title_original: Disruption of mitochondrial function in blood and sexual stages of Plasmodium falciparum by ferulenol.
articles.authors: Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Nguitragool W, Sattabongkot J, Kangwanrangsan N
articles.details: In vitro studies of ferulenol on P. falciparum life stages showed that the highest dose inhibited asexual blood-stage proliferation by 88%, early-stage gametocyte development by 82%, and stage V gametocyte maturation by ~90%. Male and female gamete formation were inhibited by 81% and 27%, respectively. These findings position ferulenol as a dual-activity antimalarial targeting both asexual parasites and transmission stages.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance management plans inadequately funded in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Managing insecticide resistance in malaria vectors in Africa: case studies from Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire and Tanzania.
articles.authors: Tabue RN, Gbalegba CGN, Mwalimu CD, Tungu PK, Fodjo BK, Elanga-Ndille E, Menze B, Mosha J, Wolie RZ, Clarke SE, Lines J, Abeku TA
articles.details: A mixed-methods evaluation of insecticide resistance management plans in Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Tanzania found that while plans aligned with national malaria strategies and international guidelines, implementation was hindered by inadequate domestic funding and heavy external donor reliance. Plans for Côte d'Ivoire and Tanzania were outdated; sentinel site coverage for resistance monitoring remained limited. Recommended improvements include increased domestic financing and regular plan updates.

articles.headline: IL-2 levels show no consistent association with Plasmodium infection
articles.title_original: Association between interleukin-2 cytokine levels and Plasmodium infections: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Kwankaew P, Kotepui KU, Anabire NG, Wilairatana P, Kotepui M
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 30 studies found no significant difference in IL-2 levels between individuals with malaria and uninfected controls (P = 0.25, SMD 4.56, 95% CI −3.16–12.29, 1074 participants). Similarly, no significant IL-2 differences were detected between severe and non-severe malaria cases (P = 0.57, SMD 0.37, 95% CI −0.91–1.67, 694 participants). Geographic region significantly influenced IL-2 expression patterns, but substantial heterogeneity limits interpretability.

articles.headline: Pediatric CNS malaria shows prolonged dysregulation of coagulation biomarkers
articles.title_original: Prolonged dysregulation of gene expression and biomarkers of coagulation, fibrinolysis, and endothelium in pediatric central nervous system malaria.
articles.authors: Dent AE, Rosa BA, Dobbs KR, Ogolla S, Midem D, Embury P, Skomorovska-Prokvolit Y, Hannon ER, Koech E, Schmaier AH, Otieno W, Mitreva M, Kazura JW
articles.details: A Kenyan longitudinal study of 37 children with CNS malaria, 20 with uncomplicated malaria, and 12 with non-malaria illness found prolonged dysregulation of coagulation, fibrinolysis, and endothelial biomarkers persisting 6 weeks post-recovery (normalized in uncomplicated malaria and non-malaria illness). Gene expression profiling showed CNS-specific upregulation of IL/cytokine signaling and unique downregulation of hemostasis pathways at 6 weeks. Biomarkers correlated with high plasma cell-free DNA.

articles.headline: Wealth index, ITN use, travel history predict urban malaria in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Modeling urban malaria infection in Anopheles stephensi hotspot area in Eastern Ethiopia: application of Structural Equation Modeling.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Abiy E, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: A facility-based cross-sectional study (n=329) in Dire Dawa, Ethiopia identified risk factors for urban malaria infection using structural equation modeling. Wealth index had negative effects on ITN utilization (β=−0.66, p<0.001) and malaria knowledge (β=−0.63, p<0.001). Travel history outside the city increased malaria infection risk (β=0.969, p=0.01). Attitude positively affected ITN use (β=0.16, p=0.049), with indirect pathways through ITN utilization significantly influencing infection risk.

articles.headline: Nanoparticles deliver sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine for malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Advancing malaria treatment with magnetic and mesoporous silica nanoparticles loaded with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine: a review.
articles.authors: Egza TF, Kebede EM, Perumal RD
articles.details: This review examines magnetic and mesoporous silica nanoparticles as drug delivery platforms for sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), addressing reduced bioavailability and resistance challenges. In vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated improved pharmacokinetics, reduced toxicity, and enhanced antimalarial efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains. The approach enables sustained drug release and potential codelivery of synergistic drugs. Challenges including scalability, biocompatibility, and regulatory requirements must be addressed for clinical translation.

articles.headline: Novel food vacuole transporter mutations contribute to artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Role of novel mutations in food vacuole transporters beyond K13-mediated artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Junaid IT, Panda A, Deshmukh A, Sardar R, Narwal M, Agrawal P, Prakash N, Akhtar A, Dey AK, Singh SS, Mridha S, Mochi J, Parveen S, Kumar M, Nagar R, Gaur N, Gupta D, Mohmmed A, Kaur I, Karmodiya K, Malhotra P
articles.details: Proteome analysis and SNP database analysis of 2,517 samples identified six mutations in four transporter genes (PfCRT, PfNT1, PfCTR2, PfMDR2) coassociated with PfKelch13 polymorphisms (P < 0.0001). Yeast complementation with mutant PfNT1 (F394L) and PfMFR5 variants (S278T, Y570F) conferred dihydroartemisinin resistance phenotypes. These findings identify novel mechanisms beyond K13-mediated resistance and unexplored contributions of multiple transporters to artemisinin resistance evolution.

articles.headline: ASHAs effective in malaria case management but underutilized as first care
articles.title_original: Implementation Fidelity of Accredited Social Health Activist (ASHA) Workers in Malaria Case Management in a High-Burden District of North India.
articles.authors: Selvaraju E, Goel PK, Gour N, Kumar A
