articles.title_original: Olfactory gene dynamics in invasive Indian and non-invasive African malaria vectors at the crossroads of development, infection and resistance.
articles.authors: Sharma A, Dixit B, Goyal B, Harit R, Kumar J, Biswas S, Goswami R, Pandey KC, Emami SN, Chakraborti S
articles.details: Genome-wide analysis of olfactory genes in four malaria vectors revealed significantly higher numbers of odorant-binding proteins (OBPs), odorant receptors (ORs), and ionotropic receptors (IRs) in African species (A. gambiae, A. funestus) compared to Indian species (A. stephensi, A. culicifacies). Pyrethroid-resistant A. stephensi larvae exhibited elevated chemosensory protein (SAP2 and SAP3) expression, suggesting roles in insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: Sub-Saharan Africa malaria seasonality dataset assembled
articles.title_original: A malaria seasonality dataset for sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Sanna F, Keddie SH, Boyhan T, Dzianach PA, McPhail M, Seitz J, Nguyen T, Redpath A, Chikolwa T, Browne AJ, Lubinda J, Saddler A, Hafsia S, Jayaseelen R, Baggen HS, Rozier JA, Symons TL, Harris J, Connor S, Vargas C, Whittaker C, Nguyen M, Gething PW, Weiss DJ
articles.details: Published literature review and surveillance compilation assembling geolocated timeseries of malaria seasonality in sub-Saharan Africa since 2000. Dataset includes prevalence, incidence, mortality, and entomological timeseries, plus qualitative seasonality descriptions. Uses three assembly approaches: natural language processing of literature, routine surveillance extraction, and entomological dataset cross-referencing.

articles.headline: Malaria treatment adherence poor in Rwanda despite knowledge
articles.title_original: Understanding Malaria Treatment Adherence in Rwanda: Implications for Artemisinin Resistance.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Jordan A, Saramba E, Wernsman Young N, Iradukunda PG, Karema C, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Banek K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 406 febrile patients at six Rwanda health facilities (December 2023–February 2024). Among 180 who received malaria treatment in the last 6 months, only 46% (n=83/180) completed the appropriate 3-day course; 37% (n=66/180) stopped within 2 days; 11% (n=19/180) went over 3 days. Malaria knowledge was high (81% correctly identified symptoms), but poor adherence exacerbates artemisinin-resistance risk.

articles.headline: Anopheles mascarensis review: vector in Madagascar and Comoros
articles.title_original: Anopheles mascarensis, a Malaria Vector Endemic to Madagascar and the Comoros Archipelago: A Review.
articles.authors: Goupeyou-Youmsi J, Tantely LM, Andrianinarivomanana TM, Girod R, Bourgouin C
articles.details: Review of Anopheles mascarensis, endemic to Madagascar and the Comoros Archipelago. An. mascarensis is the main malaria vector in east and southeast Madagascar, secondary vector elsewhere. Analysis of published data and unpublished geographical distribution records suggests possible sibling species complex with differences between east-coast and Central Highlands populations along climatic/biogeographic gradients.

articles.headline: var diversity and parasite census size rebound after interventions
articles.title_original: Measuring changes in Plasmodium falciparum census population size in response to sequential malaria control interventions.
articles.authors: Tiedje KE, Zhan Q, Ruybal-Pésantez S, Tonkin-Hill G, He Q, Tan MH, Argyropoulos DC, Deed S, Ghansah A, Bangre O, Oduro AR, Koram KA, Pascual M, Day KP
articles.details: Tracked Plasmodium falciparum parasite population size using multiplicity of infection (MOIvar) derived from var gene DBLα diversity across ~2000 individuals in northern Ghana, 2012–2017. Following indoor residual spraying that reduced transmission >90% and prevalence ~40-50%, var diversity and population size decreased significantly but rebounded 32 months after IRS discontinuation and seasonal malaria chemoprevention introduction, except in 1-5-year-old children targeted by SMC.

articles.headline: Red-fluorescent probe enables malaria diagnosis by microscopy and flow
articles.title_original: Advanced Clinical Diagnosis of Malaria Using a Simple Red-Emissive Smart Fluorescent Probe.
articles.authors: Ye Y, Zhang S, Chen G, Liu Z, Chen X, Luo R, Zhang Z, Zhao X, Lian J, Yang Z, Samanta S, Yang Z, Xu K
articles.details: DNA-specific fluorescent probe MR-1 (hemicyanine dye) designed for malaria parasite detection with detection limit ~20/μL by microscopy. Flow cytometric analysis with MR-1 enabled precise detection and classification of various malaria parasites and differentiation of malaria from Babesia-parasite infection. Excellent cell permeability and nuclear localization demonstrated.

articles.headline: Rwanda: high artemisinin and antifolate resistance, emerging kelch13
articles.title_original: Genomic Surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Markers Between October 2021 and June 2023 in Kigali, Rwanda.
articles.authors: Fankem SN, Mbonimpa JB, Kalimba EM, Diallo MT, Teke ME, Souopgui J
articles.details: Genomic surveillance of 250 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Kigali (October 2021–June 2023). Resistance mutations highly prevalent: pfcrt 76T (26%), pfmdr1 184F (72.8%), pfdhfr/pfdhps in all isolates (IRNI-SAEAA 45.6%, IRNI-SAEGA 24.8%). Pfkelch13 mutations in 50.4%, including validated R561H (25.6%), A675V, and candidate P441L. Novel haplotype K189T + R561H (24.8%) identified for first time in Rwanda.

articles.headline: Anopheles gambiae G3 draft genome assembled from 1975 strain
articles.title_original: Whole-Genome Shotgun Sequencing and Assembly of Anopheles gambiae G3, the Host of Malaria Parasite Plasmodium sp.
articles.authors: English E, Gulick C, Jane A, Kim M, Kpodonu N, Lee A, Lopez RKS, Patel K, Rajaram A, Unver L, Rankin AE, Ghazal SM
articles.details: Draft genome sequence and assembly of Anopheles gambiae strain G3 (isolated from Gambiae in 1975): 216.3 Mbp, ~82,000 scaffolds, 53X coverage, 44.5% G+C content.

articles.headline: Ethiopia malaria surveillance: 999,687 confirmed cases 2017–2021
articles.title_original: Retrospective analysis of malaria surveillance data in the former Southern Nations, Nationalities, and peoples' region, Ethiopia (2017-2021).
articles.authors: Dana DD, Ango TS, Getachew S, Deressa W
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of malaria surveillance in former SNNPR, Ethiopia (2017–2021). Among 6,292,147 suspected cases tested, 999,687 (15.89%) confirmed positive: 679,787 (68%) P. falciparum, 319,900 (32%) P. vivax; 16,023 (1.6%) clinically diagnosed without confirmation. Average 206,942 cases annually (12 per 1,000 population). South Omo reported highest burden (22%), followed by Gamo (19%) and Wolaita (17%).

articles.headline: Male Drive Female Sterile gene drive for Anopheles gambiae control
articles.title_original: A male-drive female-sterile system for the self-limited control of the malaria mosquito Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Strampelli A, Willis K, Gulliford HR, Gribble M, Fasulo B, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: Self-limiting genetic strategy (MDFS) designed to cause dominant female sterility while maintaining male fertility, allowing super-Mendelian transmission of female-sterility trait in Anopheles gambiae. Laboratory studies show repeated MDFS releases can eliminate caged populations; modeling suggests high effectiveness for wild-population suppression as self-limiting alternative to unrestricted gene drives.

articles.headline: PfATP2 flippase influences drug sensitivity in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: PfATP2 is a flippase on the Plasmodium falciparum surface that is important for growth and influences parasite sensitivity to antiplasmodial compounds.
articles.authors: Qiu D, England E, Lehane AM
articles.details: PfATP2, a phospholipid flippase on the Plasmodium falciparum parasite surface, is important for growth and influences sensitivity to antiplasmodial compounds MMV007224, MMV665852, and MMV665794. Parasites overexpressing PfATP2 show reduced sensitivity to these compounds; knockdown parasites become hypersensitive. PfATP2 expression correlates with phosphatidylserine internalisation rate, consistent with ATP-dependent phosphatidylserine uptake function.

articles.headline: Novel LDH-based RDTs detect P. vivax and P. falciparum effectively
articles.title_original: First field evaluation of novel LDH- and HRP2-based rapid tests for Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum malaria diagnosis.
articles.authors: Oduma CO, Lulu B, Ewnetu Y, Lown LA, Kayode TA, Hawaria D, Koepfli C
articles.details: Field evaluation of four rapid diagnostic tests in southern Ethiopia among 708 patients. At densities >20 parasites/µL by qPCR, all RDTs achieved sensitivities >96% for P. falciparum (vs. 63% by health-center microscopy) and >92% for P. vivax (vs. 72% microscopy). Specificity >99% for P. falciparum and >98% for P. vivax. Novel BIOCREDIT RDTs with combined HRP2-LDH and separate-line Bioline RDT showed high performance. Of 53 P. falciparum typed, only 2 carried hrp2/hrp3 deletions, both detected by all LDH-based tests.

articles.headline: Kenya: malaria prevalence 22.72% in under-five children
articles.title_original: Individual and community level factors of malaria among under-five children in Kenya: Based on the Kenya Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.authors: Lakew G, Tareke AA, Yigzaw ZA, Getachew D, Getachew E, Yirsaw AN
articles.details: Multilevel analysis of 3,146 under-five children from Kenya Demographic and Health Survey 2022. Malaria prevalence 22.72% (95% CI: 21.29–24.22%). Protective factors: maternal age 25–34 years (AOR 0.75), richest wealth (AOR 0.48), treated bed net (AOR 0.39), breastfeeding (AOR 0.66). Risk factors: community-level poverty (AOR 1.82). No bed net use was protective (AOR 0.49, suggesting measurement/recall issue in data).

articles.headline: Temperature and rainfall increase malaria risk in African children
articles.title_original: Associations of temperature and precipitation with malaria in children under 5: A multi-country study in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Chombo S, Bachwenkizi J, Omary H, Paulo HA, Luoga P, Zacharia A, Mbishi JV, Andrew JD, Addo IY
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 15,009 children under 5 in 6 Sub-Saharan African countries found overall malaria prevalence of 25.9% (Nigeria 38.1%, Burundi 38.0%). One-degree Celsius temperature rise increased malaria risk 1.77-fold (95% CI 1.297–2.414, p<0.001). Children in regions with annual precipitation 250–500 mm faced 72% higher risk than those in areas >500 mm (95% CI 1.331–2.212, p<0.001). Climate data incorporation recommended for malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: MSP1 vaccine well-tolerated in malaria pre-exposed African adults
articles.title_original: Full-length merozoite surface protein 1 formulated with GLA-SE adjuvant in malaria pre-exposed adults: a randomised, controlled, double-blind, parallel-group, single-centre Phase Ib trial.
articles.authors: Kahatano AE, Mpina M, Vanobberghen F, Hassan O, Urasa N, Sasamalo I, Mswata S, Paris D, Gajewski S, Saxena M, Fürle K, Kiehl V, Böhnlein E, Aschenbrenner A, Lanzer M, Thomson-Luque R, Olotu A, Daubenberger C
articles.details: Phase Ib randomized controlled trial of 40 participants in Tanzania (August 2023–April 2024) comparing SUM-101 vaccine (150 μg full-length MSP1 with GLA-SE adjuvant, 3 monthly doses) vs rabies vaccine control. 37/40 (93%) completed all vaccinations. Within 7 days, 10/20 (50%) in vaccine and 13/20 (65%) in control reported mild-to-moderate solicited adverse events. No severe adverse events or serious adverse events reported. SUM-101 elicited robust MSP1-specific IgM and IgG antibodies with peak responses on days 56 and 84.

articles.headline: Corrigendum: Malaria epidemics in India and climatic conditions
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Malaria epidemics in India: Role of climatic condition and control measures" [Sci. Total Environ. Volume 712, 10 April 2020, 136368].
articles.authors: Baghbanzadeh M, Kumar D, Yavasoglu SI, Manning S, Hanafi-Bojd AA, Ghasemzadeh H, Sikder IU, Kumar D, Murmu N, Haque U
articles.details: This is a correction to previously published article on malaria epidemics in India and the role of climatic conditions and control measures.

articles.headline: Anti-GLURP and anti-Rh5.1 antibodies protect against malaria
articles.title_original: A Multiplex Serological Assay to Evaluate the Antibody Responses to a Set of Plasmodium falciparum Antigens and Their Protective Role Against Malaria in Children Aged 1.5 to 12 Years Living in a Highly Seasonal Malaria Transmission Area of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ezinmegnon S, Nébié I, Marlais T, Ouattara D, Diarra A, Patterson C, Tetteh K, Ouédraogo A, Drakeley C, Tiono AB, Sirima SB
articles.details: Multiplex serological assay of P. falciparum antigens in 1.5–12-year-old children from Burkina Faso examined 16 antigens by age and infection status. IgG to AMA1, CSP, and MSP2 CH150 showed age-positive correlation. Anti-GLURP R2 and anti-Rh5.1 antibodies associated with reduced malaria risk (adjusted IRR 0.52 and 0.40, respectively). Antibody combination of AMA1, GLURP R2, and Etramp5 Ag1 showed best predictive performance (AUC 0.70).

articles.headline: TCF25 regulates gametocyte development and ribosome biogenesis
articles.title_original: Transcription factor 25 modulates gametocytogenesis and ribosome biogenesis in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Wu J, Zhang Z, Ngim C, Yang W, Li P, Wang F, Ye J, Li B, Tian B, Zhang Q, Shang X
articles.details: Study of tcf25 knockout P. falciparum parasites revealed transcriptomic dysregulation: 168 genes downregulated and 24 upregulated during ring stage; 53 downregulated and 4 upregulated during schizont stage. ChIP-seq identified 44 TCF25-binding target genes including rDNA. TCF25 deficiency increased rRNA expression, particularly 28S rRNA component of 60S ribosomal subunit. TCF25 plays crucial role in gametocytogenesis via ap2-g pathway and directly controls A-type rRNA expression.

articles.headline: PfEBA-175 shows high antigenic variation across Tanzania villages
articles.title_original: Micro-geographic variation in antigenic diversity of PfEBA-175 region II in asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum infections in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Syahada JH, Lee WJ, Jun H, Louis JM, Fitriana F, Muh F, Lu F, Ahmed MA, Na S, Chun W, Park WS, Jeon BY, Han ET, Todd J, Manjurano A, Kidima W, Mazigo E, Lee SJ, Han JH
articles.details: Genetic and antigenic analysis of PfEBA-175 region II from 172 asymptomatic P. falciparum isolates in Tanzania revealed high nucleotide diversity (π=0.00359±0.00012) with adaptive evolution driven by immune pressure (dN-dS=3.15, p<0.001). Recombinant RII protein showed 84.8% average seropositivity with micro-geographic variation (95.4% in Rwantaba to 50.0% in Bunyambo). Antibody response correlated with age (ρ=0.333, p<0.001). Despite substantial polymorphism, glycan-binding residues remain conserved.

articles.headline: Rice intensification systems may impact African malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Climate change adapted rice production: does the system of rice intensification impact malaria vector ecology?
articles.authors: Hardy H, Hopkins RJ, Mnyone L, Hawkes FM
articles.details: Review article discussing relationship between rice cultivation and malaria transmission in Africa. Contemporary research reveals association between enhanced malaria vector densities from irrigated rice fields and intensified transmission. System of Rice Intensification (SRI), employed to enhance yields while reducing water inputs, fundamentally alters rice-growing environment and may significantly impact ecology of dominant African malaria vector species, with consequences for local transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Implementation barriers for post-discharge malaria prevention in Malawi
articles.title_original: Assessing stakeholder perceptions and contextual factors in implementing post-discharge malaria chemoprevention in children with severe anaemia in Malawi.
articles.authors: Ndambo MK, Hoyt J, Nyirongo E, Phiri KS, Hill J, Manda-Taylor L
articles.details: Qualitative study in Malawi (May 2024) assessing stakeholder perceptions of post-discharge malaria chemoprevention (PDMC) for children with severe anemia; purposive sampling of 31 participants. Severe anemia identified as leading cause of hospitalization and mortality in Malawian children <5. Identified knowledge gaps among health workers and caregiver barriers including traditional beliefs, financial constraints, and logistical challenges. Key implementation needs: operational planning, medication supply, targeted health worker training, community engagement, and supply chain strengthening.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine clears fever faster than piperaquine in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance of two commonly administered artemisinin-based combination therapies in under-five children with uncomplicated malaria in a comprehensive healthcare facility in South-Western Nigeria: a randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Ahmed AA, Bello IS, Adewole TO, Olowookere SA, Oyegbade OO, Ogundokun AO, Olajubu TO, Funwei RI, Ojurongbe O, Adepoju DA, Bello AB, Adegboyega AA, Anjorin AO, Anjorin AO, Akinola OO, Adewumi KM, Omenugha MK, Salami OT, Familusi AO, Akande EO
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial in Southwestern Nigeria (July–December 2020) of 122 children aged 6–59 months with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria comparing artemether-lumefantrine (AL, n=61) vs dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHAPQ, n=61). Mean fever clearance faster with AL (46.16±3.31 h vs 48.83±8.95 h, p=0.032). Parasite clearance time similar (61.30±23.75 h vs 63.97±23.42 h, p=0.541). Day 42 PCR-uncorrected cure rates: AL 94.7%, DHAPQ 92.9% (p=0.679); both 100% after PCR correction.

articles.headline: Explainable AI diagnoses malaria from blood smears with 95.61% accuracy
articles.title_original: Ensemble-Based Early Detection of Malaria via Explainable ViT-CNN Feature Fusion and SHAP.
articles.authors: Özdemir EY, Koç C, Küçük K, Özyurt F
articles.details: Hybrid machine-learning model combining Vision Transformer (ViT) and EfficientNet CNN for automated malaria diagnosis from microscopic blood smear images. Features ranked by importance using SHAP method; ensemble trained with CatBoost, XGBoost, and Logistic Regression. ViT_Large_16 and EfficientNetB0 achieved 95.61% accuracy with 1.6-second processing time, approximately 204-fold faster than traditional approaches. Vision Transformer models best captured complex details in blood smears.

articles.headline: Correction: Anticoagulant therapy in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Correction: Anticoagulant therapy and altered tissue factor expression protect against experimental placental and cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Andrew AK, Bracken TC, Nazario-Maldonado N, Russ BN, Gonzalvo KT, Brainard BM, Mackman N, Moore JM
articles.details: This is a correction notice to previously published article DOI: 10.1371/journal.ppat.1013259.

articles.headline: Malaria alters glucose metabolism and amino acid catabolism in rats
articles.title_original: Application of a Non-Targeted Metabolomics Study in Plasmodium berghei-Infected Rats: Towards Unravelling Metabolic Alterations During Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Mbuli ZN, Ndlovu IS, Masola B, Mukaratirwa S
articles.details: Untargeted metabolomics study in Plasmodium berghei-infected male Sprague Dawley rats compared to uninfected controls using GC-TOF-MS. Infected rats showed elevated urea and reduced 1,5-anhydroglucitol, D-(+)-Talose, and arachidonic acid. Pathway enrichment revealed upregulation of glucose-alanine cycle, alpha-linolenic acid metabolism, and linoleic acid metabolism. Glucose-alanine cycle upregulation suggests increased gluconeogenesis responding to parasite-induced glucose depletion. Elevated urea indicates enhanced amino acid catabolism.

articles.headline: Temperature-malaria association systematic review 1959-2019
articles.title_original: Association between temperature and malaria (1959-2019): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Murugan Y, Shrivastav V
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 studies from Africa and Asia (1959-2019). Mean temperature showed moderate positive correlation with malaria incidence (r=0.417, 95% CI 0.098-0.735, p=0.01); minimum temperature showed strongest association (r=0.454, 95% CI 0.358-0.549, p<0.001). Maximum temperature showed weaker correlation (r=0.356, 95% CI 0.263-0.450). Authors conclude temperature, especially minimum temperature, significantly influences malaria transmission and should be integrated into surveillance systems.

articles.headline: Bold ideas for changing malaria landscape
articles.title_original: Innovation under pressure: bold ideas for a changing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Epstein A, Tangena JA
articles.details: No abstract available; article title suggests focus on innovative approaches to malaria control.

articles.headline: Diagnostic challenges in post-artesunate delayed haemolysis
articles.title_original: The usual suspect-malaria? Diagnostic challenges in post-artesunate delayed haemolysis: a clinical laboratory perspective through a case report.
articles.authors: Molnar E, Cabral PM, Portal F, Guimarães AR, Santos L, Guimarães JT
articles.details: Mini review examining case of travel-associated malaria and post-artesunate delayed haemolysis (PADH), a rare but potentially severe complication following artemisinin treatment. Article focuses on diagnostic challenges from clinical laboratory perspective in distinguishing PADH from malaria manifestations.

articles.headline: Insecticide-treated net utilization among African children
articles.title_original: Utilization of insecticide-treated nets for malaria prevention among children in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Basiru A, Alozieuwa UB, Aboh MI, Usman UY, Nwaefulu ON, Okeke OP, Akinsolu FT, Sobande OO
articles.details: Systematic review of 30 studies including 76,045 children, 50 households, and 1,009 caregivers (2013-2023). Pooled ITN utilization prevalence was 69.5% (95% CI 64.5-74.5%), with regional variation: Central Africa 85.4%, Southern Africa 80.7%, Eastern Africa 69.2%, West Africa 67.0% (I²=100%, p=0.001). Authors conclude ITN utilization among African children is inadequate and varies significantly by region.

articles.headline: Low validated kelch13 mutations in South African elimination
articles.title_original: Very Low Prevalence of Validated kelch13 Mutations and Absence of Histidine-Rich Protein 2/3 Double Gene Deletions in South African Malaria-Eliminating Districts (2022-2024).
articles.authors: Raman J, Mabona M, Nyawo Q, Mangena B, Kok G, Mathaba L, Malatje G, Lauterbach SB, Makhanthisa TI, Gwarinda H, Letinić BD, Ndadza A, Raswiswi E, Shandukani M, Baloyi E, Fekema Z, Ismail A, Featherston J, Greenhouse B, Smith JL, Aranda-Diaz A
articles.details: 4,471 samples from KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga collected Jan 2022-Aug 2024 screened for resistance markers. Validated artemisinin partial resistance was rare: P574L detected in 2 samples, P553L in 1 sample. Most prevalent mutations were A578S (30/999) and I494V (13/951). Parasites carried crtK76 (99.8%) and mdr1N86 (99.0%), suggesting chloroquine susceptibility; no hrp2/3 deletions detected. Authors conclude recommended treatments and diagnostics remain effective.

articles.headline: SumiShield 50WG maintains residual efficacy in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Efficacy of the Insecticide Formulation SumiShield® 50WG for Malaria Vector Control in Experimental Huts in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Randrenjarison R, Randriamanga J, Girod R
articles.details: Experimental hut study in Madagascar over 9 months post-spray tested SumiShield 50WG (clothianidin neonicotinoid) against wild anophelines and An. arabiensis lab strain. Mortality in wild mosquitoes remained above 80% WHO threshold for 8 months, not achieved by month 9. An. arabiensis showed similar pattern. Authors conclude 8-month residual efficacy sufficient for malaria transmission seasons in most endemic areas.

articles.headline: Rapid bio-efficacy loss in Madagascar ITNs distributed 2018
articles.title_original: Early Bio-Efficacy Loss of Nets Mass Distributed for Malaria Vector Control in Madagascar in 2018: Implications for Malaria Prevention.
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Randrenjarison R, Raharinjatovo J, Swamidoss I, Mapp C, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Bowen A, Belemvire A, Zohdy S, Poyer S, Girod R
articles.details: DawaPlus 2.0 and PermaNet 2.0 ITNs distributed in Madagascar 2018 showed rapid efficacy decline. At distribution: mosquito mortality 86.4% (DawaPlus) and 83.6% (PermaNet), exceeding 80% WHO threshold. By 12-36 months: mortality dropped to <56% for all districts. Deltamethrin residue fell below expected ranges (DawaPlus <66 mg/m², PermaNet <36 mg/m²). Authors conclude ITNs lost bio-efficacy within 1 year despite 3-year claims.

articles.headline: Secondary vectors sustain malaria transmission Madagascar 2018
articles.title_original: Malaria Outbreak in Farafangana District, Southeast Madagascar, 2018: Are Secondary Vectors a Threat to Current Malaria Control Approaches?
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Harimanana A, Randrianaivo F, Randrenjarison R, Raherinjafy R, Rakotondramanga JM, Zohdy S, Razakamiadana S, Kapesa L, Andriamananjara MN, Baril L, Randremanana R, Randrianasolo L, Dentinger CM, Girod R
articles.details: Outbreak investigation in Farafangana District, Madagascar, 2018, following ITN and IRS implementation. Of 1,488 Anopheles collected, 25 (1.7%) carried P. falciparum sporozoites; 24 found outdoors with 54.2% being An. coustani. High exophagy rates (59.8-100%) occurred in most species except An. funestus (<47%). Of 226 individuals tested, 71 (31.4%) were RDT-positive; 61 (85.9%) asymptomatic, mostly children. Authors conclude outdoor-biting vectors and asymptomatic infections sustained transmission.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria persists months in sub-Saharan migrants
articles.title_original: Burden of asymptomatic malaria in adult sub-Saharan migrants attending an outpatient clinic in Rome from February 2024 to January 2025.
articles.authors: Faraglia F, Vita S, Kontogiannis D, Bartoli TA, Rosati S, Bevilacqua N, Maffongelli G, Corpolongo A, Bartolini B, Vulcano A, D'Abramo A, Fontana C, Nicastri E
articles.details: 87 asymptomatic adult sub-Saharan migrants screened Feb 2024-Jan 2025 in Rome. Malaria prevalence 6% (all male cases). LAMP detected Plasmodium in 5 cases (6.0%), confirmed by RT-PCR; RDT detected only 2 (2.3%). Species included P. falciparum, P. malariae, P. ovale, and mixed infection. Longest time since arrival was 181 days; median interval since departure exceeded 600 days. Authors conclude asymptomatic malaria persists for months and is underdetected by RDTs.

articles.headline: var gene hierarchy enables P. falciparum immune evasion
articles.title_original: Hierarchical switching pattern in antigenic variation provides survival advantage for malaria parasites under variable host immunity.
articles.authors: Iragavarapu GP, Varsha HJ, Vembar SS, Ghosh B
articles.details: Theoretical Markov chain modeling combined with single-cell RNA-seq, RT-qPCR, and RNA-seq analysis of ~60-member var gene family showed hierarchical switching pattern. Hierarchical PfEMP1 variant switching optimizes parasite survival under variable host immune pressure. Variants efficiently cleared by immune system maintained stably at higher population frequency; slowly-cleared variants suppressed. Analysis showed stable variants have greater IgM binding affinities. Authors propose hierarchical switching provides mechanistic advantage for parasite persistence.

articles.headline: Optimized PI4K inhibitors reduce parasitemia in malaria
articles.title_original: Optimization of 2,8-Diaryl-1,5-naphthyridines as Plasmodium falciparum Phosphatidylinositol 4-Kinase Inhibitors with Improved ADME Profiles and In Vivo Efficacy.
articles.authors: Dziwornu GA, Seanego D, Fienberg S, Sypu VS, Salomane N, Krugmann L, Taylor D, Masike K, Njoroge M, Boonyalai N, Lee MCS, Godoy LC, Pasaje CF, Niles JC, Basarab GS, Coulson LB, Ghorpade SR, Chibale K
articles.details: Structure-activity optimization of 2,8-diaryl-1,5-naphthyridine phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase inhibitors identified compound 27 with improved properties. In humanized NOD-scid IL-2Rγnull mouse model of P. falciparum malaria, compound 27 at 4×50 mg/kg once daily reduced parasitemia 91%. Compound showed no hERG inhibition or mammalian cytotoxicity. Authors identify compound 27 as promising antimalarial candidate with favorable pharmacokinetics.

articles.headline: Amodiachins active against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Discovery of Amodiachins, a Novel Class of 4-Aminoquinoline Antimalarials Active against Multidrug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Handford MJ, Li Y, Riscoe T, Liebman KM, Zhang X, Kelly JX, Riscoe MK
articles.details: Structure-activity optimization study of amodiaquine analogs (amodiachins) against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant P. falciparum. Lead compound 43 (ADC-028) showed nanomolar activity against both strains, minimal cytotoxicity, and microsomal stability (t1/2=48.2 min). In Plasmodium yoelli murine model: nonrecrudescence dose 16 mg/kg/day; single-dose cure at 50 mg/kg. Pharmacokinetics showed half-life 84 hours, oral bioavailability 76%. Authors propose compound 43 for development as resistance-overcoming antimalarial.

articles.headline: Marinoquinoline compound 19 inhibits P. falciparum protease activity
articles.title_original: Fluorescent Marinoquinoline Derivative as Inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum: SAR Analysis, Mode of Action and In Vivo Studies.
articles.authors: Santos Barbosa P, Souza GE, Maluf SEC, Bonatto V, Moura CS, Mendes GR, Valdes TA, Annunciato Y, Rossetto BDS, Ventura PDS, Ortin GGD, da Silva W, Icimoto MY, Ferreira ADS, Cruz FC, Teles CBG, Pereira DB, Cassiano GC, Santana S, Prudêncio M, Barbosa CS, Moura IMR, Giampauli RM, De Sousa IL, Rocco SA, Gazarini ML, Correia CRD, Aguiar ACC, Guido RVC
articles.details: Twenty fluorescent marinoquinoline analogues were developed, with compound 19 achieving IC50 = 0.28 μM against P. falciparum 3D7 in vitro and EC50 = 0.53 μM against P. vivax ex vivo. A 3 × 50 mg/kg oral dose reduced parasitemia by 96% in P. berghei-infected mice. Compound 19 accumulates in infected erythrocytes and inhibits Plasmodium protease activity.

articles.headline: Transcription factor PfAP2-06B regulates erythrocyte invasion in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: An ApiAP2 Family Transcriptional Factor PfAP2-06B Regulates Erythrocyte Invasion Indirectly in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shi Q, Wan K, Gong Y, Pang J, Liu Y, Tang J, Zhang Q, Cao J, Shen L
articles.details: Conditional knockdown of transcription factor PfAP2-06B in P. falciparum resulted in defective asexual growth and impaired erythrocyte invasion. RNA-seq analysis revealed PfAP2-06B modulates invasion-related genes during schizont stage, while single-cell RNA-seq showed its role in stochastic variation of invasion gene expression. These findings highlight PfAP2-06B as a potential target for novel malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: PKCθ inhibition prevents CD8+ T cell-mediated cerebral malaria in mice
articles.title_original: Inhibition of PKCθ Abrogates CD8(+) T Cell-Mediated Neurotoxicity in Murine Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Albrecht-Schgör K, Stefan VE, Steinlechner M, Humer D, Siegmund K, Peer S, Gruber T, Überegger M, Zur Nedden S, Baier-Bitterlich G, Lackner P, Schmutzhard E, Thuille N, Klepsch V, Baier G
articles.details: PKCθ-deficient mice on C57BL/6N background infected with P. berghei ANKA showed significantly improved survival without alterations in blood or brain parasite burden. Loss of PKCθ shifted CD8+ T cell differentiation toward central memory rather than effector memory phenotypes, reducing sequestration in cerebral microvasculature and preventing neurovascular damage and neurological signs. These results suggest PKCθ inhibitors may protect against cerebral malaria pathology.

articles.headline: Global burden and vaccine advances for intracellular parasitic infections
articles.title_original: Intracellular Parasitic Infections Caused by Plasmodium falciparum, Leishmania spp., Toxoplasma gondii, Echinococcus multilocularis, Among Key Pathogens: Global Burden, Transmission Dynamics, and Vaccine Advances-A Narrative Review with Contextual Insights from Armenia.
articles.authors: Sargsyan T, Stepanyan L, Tsaturyan A, Palumbo R, Vicidomini C, Roviello GN
articles.details: Narrative review examining intracellular parasitic diseases including P. falciparum, Leishmania, Toxoplasma, and Echinococcus, discussing parasite classification, immune evasion mechanisms, diagnostic challenges, and vaccine developments. The review emphasizes a 'One Health' approach integrating human, animal, and environmental health and uses Armenia as a case study highlighting challenges in diagnostics, surveillance systems, and research infrastructure in affected regions.

articles.headline: Gametocytogenesis induction: methods from stress to genome editing
articles.title_original: Induction of gametocytogenesis in human malaria parasites: from stress to genome editing.
articles.authors: Subramaniam R, Chiew ZY, Zuhaidi NDB, Lau YL, Cheong FW
articles.details: Mini-review summarizing gametocytogenesis induction methods in human Plasmodium species, covering conventional environmental stressors and modern genome-editing approaches for modifying sexual conversion genes. While progress has been made in both approaches, scalability remains challenging, especially for non-falciparum species. The review concludes that integrating both approaches is crucial for developing efficient gametocytogenesis induction methods to support malaria control.

articles.headline: Mosquito metabolic pathways support avian Plasmodium parasite development
articles.title_original: Avian malaria and the overlooked metabolic pathways underlying mosquito-Plasmodium interactions.
articles.authors: Garcia-Longoria L, Berthomieu A, Hellgren O, Rivero A
articles.details: Review of avian Plasmodium transmission examining metabolic pathways in mosquito vectors beyond immune responses. Transcriptomic analysis identified differential expression of pathways essential for parasite development, including genes for parasite energetic needs, digestive enzymes for midgut barrier traversal, and salivary enzymes for blood uptake. Findings suggest the parasite modulates these metabolic pathways to enhance and prolong transmission.

articles.headline: LLIN ownership reduces malaria infection in middle-SES households
articles.title_original: Long-lasting insecticidal net ownership and malaria infection by socio-economic status: a cross-sectional household study in an area along Lake Victoria, Kenya.
articles.authors: Iwashita H, Nagi S, Bahati F, Kagaya W, Larson PS, Kongere J, Kanoi BN, Hayasaka R, Sugishita T, Gitaka J, Kaneko A
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey in Suba North Sub-County, Lake Victoria region, Kenya (June–September 2021) assessed LLIN effectiveness stratified by socioeconomic status. In middle SES households, sufficient LLIN ownership was significantly associated with lower malaria infection (OR 0.32, 95% CI 0.12–0.92). Combined low and middle SES households showed significant protection with sufficient LLIN ownership (OR 0.37, 95% CI 0.17–0.87).

articles.headline: Malaria RDT cassettes remain stable for one month, enabling verification
articles.title_original: Are malaria rapid diagnostic test results stable over time to support verification of surveillance data?
articles.authors: Ngufor C, Lindblade KA, Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ahogni I, Ssewante N, Akpiroroh E, Kpemasse A, Okoro O, Agaba B, Cooper S, Griffith K, Humes M
articles.details: Prospective study in 48 health facilities in Benin, Nigeria, and Uganda (June–September 2023) evaluated RDT result stability using AI-powered imaging at baseline, one week, and one month. Of 45,155 RDTs meeting criteria, 95.1% of initially positive and 95.3% of initially negative results remained unchanged at one month (PPV 96.3%, NPV 93.8%). Most result changes occurred within the first week, with faint lines showing higher variability.

articles.headline: PfPX2 protein binds phosphoinositides in parasite secretory pathways
articles.title_original: Characterization of a phosphoinositide-binding protein containing a PHOX homology domain in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Roucheray S, Stastny D, Kaur H, Tahotna D, Dacks JB, Griac P, Richard D
articles.details: Characterization of P. falciparum protein PfPX2, a phosphoinositide-binding protein with a PX domain. Lipid binding assays demonstrated PfPX2 binds all phosphoinositide species with preference for PI3P, PI5P, and PI(3,5)P2. Immunofluorescence showed PfPX2 localizes to Golgi apparatus and micronemes in developing schizonts. Proximity labeling identified potential interactors including PfSortilin, clathrin heavy chain, and PfDyn1, suggesting involvement in vesicular trafficking.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing distinguishes P. falciparum recrudescence from new infection
articles.title_original: Rapid multiplexed nanopore amplicon sequencing to distinguish Plasmodium falciparum recrudescence from new infection in antimalarial drug trials.
articles.authors: Holzschuh A, Lerch A, Nsanzabana C
articles.details: Nanopore AmpSeq assay targeting six microhaplotypes distinguishes recrudescence from new infections in antimalarial drug trials. The assay achieved 98% intra-assay and 97% inter-assay reproducibility, high sensitivity detecting minority clones at 1:100 ratios, and <0.01% false-positive rate. Across 20 paired patient samples, the assay distinguished recrudescence from new infections in 17/20 cases (85%), enabling rapid corrected drug efficacy estimates.

articles.headline: Correction: ESCRT-III machinery in Plasmodium falciparum secretion
articles.title_original: Correction: The ESCRT-III machinery participates in the production of extracellular vesicles and protein export during Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Avalos-Padilla Y, Georgiev VN, Lantero E, Pujals S, Verhoef R, Borgheti-Cardoso LN, Albertazzi L, Dimova R, Fernàndez-Busquets X
articles.details: Correction to article DOI: 10.1371/journal.ppat.1009455 regarding ESCRT-III machinery's role in extracellular vesicle and protein production during P. falciparum infection.

articles.headline: Spatiotemporal analysis of November 2023–March 2024 malaria epidemic in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: The November-2023-March-2024 malaria epidemic in Zanzibar: a spatiotemporal epidemiological analysis.
articles.authors: Kooiman F, Ali MH, Alifrangis M, Shija SJ, Hassan WS, Schiøler KL, Saleh F
articles.details: Epidemiological analysis of 23,569 confirmed malaria cases during an outbreak in Zanzibar (November 2023–March 2024) identified risk factors and transmission dynamics. Males, individuals aged 15–35, urban residents, and those not sleeping under insecticide-treated nets had higher malaria risk. One significant spatial-temporal cluster identified in urban southwest of Unguja; weekly cases were significantly associated with average weekly temperature (8-week lag).

articles.headline: Antimalarial drugs bind Plasmodium serpentine receptor PfSR10
articles.title_original: Identification of antimalarial drugs and bradykinin as ligands of the Plasmodium membrane protein PfSR10.
articles.authors: Jiang D, Wen X, Han JF, Cheng QX, Zhang R, Zheng Y, Zheng K, Huang SH, Chen JY, Li SW, Yang ZS, Han B, Tie L, Yang F, Zhang PJ, Xiao P, Lin H, Yu X, Sun JP
articles.details: This study characterized the mechanism of action for four antimalarial drugs (chloroquine, dihydroartemisinin, piperaquine-tetraphosphate tetrahydrate, and primaquine diphosphate) using biochemical reconstitution, cryo-EM analysis, and mutagenesis studies. The researchers found that these antimalarials act as agonists of the Plasmodium membrane protein PfSR10, which also binds the host peptide bradykinin. The identification of PfSR10 as a dual-function receptor for both antimalarial drugs and host peptides provides insights into drug action mechanisms and host-parasite communication in malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Cellular aging biomarkers in Plasmodium-host interactions
articles.title_original: Critical Understanding of the Influence of Cellular Aging Biomarkers on Host-Parasite Relationships Serving as a Key Platform for Malaria Eradication.
articles.authors: Anzaku DO, Afolabi IS
articles.details: This comprehensive review examines the interplay between Plasmodium parasites (especially P. falciparum) and host cellular aging processes, integrating infection mechanisms, immune responses, and parasite evasion strategies. The authors emphasize how understanding cellular aging biomarkers during malaria infection could provide novel insights into disease pathophysiology and support parasite eradication efforts. The review underscores the critical importance of understanding temporal and spatial dimensions of host-parasite adaptation for forecasting parasite evolution and spread.

articles.headline: DDT and pyrethroid pesticide contamination in Brazilian Amazon soils
articles.title_original: Potential Risks to Human Health Caused by the Use of Pesticides in Soils of Three Municipalities Impacted by Localized Malaria in the Brazilian Amazon.
articles.authors: Santos LFD, Deus RJA, Sucupira IMC, Brasil DDSB, Mendes RA
articles.details: This cross-sectional study analyzed DDT and alphacypermethrin concentrations in soils from three municipalities in the Brazilian Amazon (Mazagão, Porto Velho, and Cantá) using gas chromatography. Detected DDT and metabolites, with highest concentrations in Cantá (average 2.694 mg/kg), and alphacypermethrin in Porto Velho (average 0.364 mg/kg), reflecting historical malaria control efforts and LLIN use. The incremental lifetime cancer risk (ILCR) reached 2.93 × 10⁻³ for DDT and 1.17 × 10⁻¹ for alphacypermethrin, with a maximum non-carcinogenic hazard index of 336.61, indicating potential health risks to humans.

articles.headline: Parvovirus B19 coinfection complicates falciparum malaria recovery
articles.title_original: Transient Pure Red Cell Aplasia in a Case of Plasmodium falciparum and Parvovirus B19 Coinfection.
articles.authors: John CV, Basu A, Bose D, Ray Y
articles.details: This case report describes a 22-year-old patient from West Bengal, India with Plasmodium falciparum infection treated with intravenous artesunate followed by artemether-lumefantrine. Despite parasitological clearance, the patient developed persistent hypoproliferative anemia; bone marrow examination revealed pure red cell aplasia with giant pronormoblasts, and B19V quantitative PCR testing showed 2.1 × 10³ international units/mL. The coinfection with human parvovirus B19 was identified as causing infection-related autoimmunity and dyserythropoiesis. Complete recovery occurred after IV immunoglobulin and prednisone therapy.

articles.headline: Long-amplicon sequencing detects full-length artemisinin resistance genes
articles.title_original: Multiplex long-amplicon sequencing for comprehensive molecular surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum resistance to artemisinin and partner drugs in artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs).
articles.authors: Li F, Zhang J, Xu S, Wang Y, Mao Z, Zhu G, Liu Y, Cao J
articles.details: This study developed and validated a multiplex long-amplicon PCR panel for detecting drug-resistance mutations across six P. falciparum genes: Pfk13, Pfcoronin, Pfap2μ, and Pfubp1 (artemisinin resistance markers), and Pfmdr1 and Pfcrt (partner drug markers). Analytical sensitivity was 50 parasites/μL for dried blood spots and 5 parasites/μL for venous blood, achieving 100% target coverage. Cost per sample was $15.60, with optimized sequencing requirements (0.25GB for DBS at 55× mean depth; 0.5GB for venous blood at 33× depth with >89% coverage uniformity), enabling scalable surveillance of multidrug-resistant P. falciparum in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Financial incentives trial to increase malaria testing in Kenya
articles.title_original: The second-round evaluation of financial incentives on malaria prevalence in the Lake Victoria Basin, Kenya: updated study protocol for a cluster-randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Matsumoto T, Nagashima M, Kagaya W, Okomo G, Kongere J, Oginga J, Opiyo V, Gitaka J, Kaneko A
articles.details: This cluster-randomized controlled trial protocol describes a second-round evaluation in Suba South Sub-county in Homa Bay County, Kenya, re-randomizing 92 clusters to conditional cash transfer, lottery incentive scheme, or control arms. Each intervention includes updated malaria education and financial incentives tied to negative malaria test results. Primary outcomes are changes in malaria prevalence, long-lasting insecticide-treated net usage, and malaria knowledge/perception, assessed at 3 and 6 months post-intervention via rapid diagnostic tests, microscopy, and PCR.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology and control response in Pakistan, 2020-2024
articles.title_original: Fighting malaria in Shangla District, Pakistan: insight into epidemiology, risk factors, and strategic control measures (2020-2024).
articles.authors: Ullah I, Baig RM, Riaz A, Mukhtar M, Khan MA, Zaib A, Muhammad N, Khan S, Ali A, Ahmad A, Del Angel Nunez RM
articles.details: This retrospective epidemiological study analyzed 130,401 laboratory-confirmed malaria cases from 572,696 febrile individuals screened in Shangla District, Pakistan, 2020-2024. The Annual Parasite Incidence (API) peaked at 61 per 1,000 population in 2023, declining to 46/1,000 in 2024 following targeted interventions. Plasmodium vivax comprised 95% of cases, P. falciparum 4.6%, and mixed infections 0.4%, with seasonal peaks in July-August (P. vivax) and October-November (P. falciparum). Major risk factors included poor primaquine adherence (only 14% completion) and absence of G6PD screening; the 2024 decline corresponded with deployment of 78 diagnostic/treatment centers, expanded indoor residual spraying, LLIN distribution, and health education.

articles.headline: Caregiver knowledge linked to better malaria prevention in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Malaria knowledge and preventive practices among caregivers of under-five children in Southwest Nigeria.
articles.authors: Adeleke TO, Akinlosotu MA, Samson TK, Adeleke OV, Ajala DE, Olasinde Y, Oluwasanya AN, Esan DT
articles.details: This cross-sectional hospital-based study surveyed 797 caregivers of children under five in Southwest Nigeria. Of these, 689 caregivers (86.4%) demonstrated good knowledge of malaria, with caregivers from Ondo and Ogun States showing significantly higher odds of good knowledge compared to Osun State. Good malaria knowledge was significantly associated with better prevention practices (odds ratio = 2.347, 95% CI = 1.251–4.406, p = 0.008), highlighting the importance of targeted public health education for malaria control.

articles.headline: Harmala alkaloids show promise as antimalarial candidates in silico
articles.title_original: Computational insights into harmala alkaloids as novel antimalarial candidates against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Dipto KZ, Shariar R, Saha CK, Huzaifa A, Robin TB, Patil RB, Alam MT, Rafi MIH, Faruk MAZ, Moin AT, Zinnah KMA, Hasanuzzaman M, Islam T
articles.details: This computational study examined harmala alkaloids (harmine and harmaline) from plants (Peganum harmala) and microbes (Delftia tsuruhatensis TC1) as antimalarial agents against P. falciparum. Using in silico methodologies including ADME analysis, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulation, researchers identified these compounds as potential inhibitors of protein kinase 4 (PK4), an essential parasite enzyme. The compounds demonstrated lower toxicity profiles while effectively inhibiting blood-stage parasite growth and transmission, warranting further in vitro and in vivo validation.

articles.headline: Lightweight deep learning network automates malaria parasite detection
articles.title_original: DANet a lightweight dilated attention network for malaria parasite detection.
articles.authors: Ali A, Pal R, Dey I, Cuevas E, Perez-Cisneros M, Sarkar R
articles.details: This study developed DANet, a lightweight Dilated Attention Network with approximately 2.3 million parameters for automated malaria parasite detection from blood smear images. Tested on the NIH Malaria Dataset comprising 27,558 images (19,290 training, 2,756 validation, 5,512 test), DANet achieved an F1-score of 97.86%, accuracy of 97.95%, and area under curve-precision recall of 0.98. The model's efficiency enables deployment on edge devices such as the Raspberry Pi 4, providing a practical solution for automated malaria diagnosis in resource-constrained settings.

articles.headline: Female caregivers and trust increase RTS,S malaria vaccine uptake
articles.title_original: Caregivers' practices and factors associated with malaria vaccine uptake among under-five children in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon: A community based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Abiache IM, Nsobinenyui D, Ukah CE, Kumenyuy YL, Ngeha NC, Wefuan R, Dang SZ, Ndip NE, Feguem MP, Nsagha DS
articles.details: This community-based cross-sectional study of 410 caregivers in Tiko Health District, Cameroon conducted March–April 2025 assessed factors associated with uptake of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine in children aged 0–5 years. Only 32.2% of children (n=132) had received the vaccine, with 72.0% of vaccinated children completing recommended doses. Multivariable logistic regression showed significantly higher vaccine uptake odds for female caregivers (aOR 4.16, 95% CI 1.47–11.75), caregivers in health professions (aOR 2.87), biological parents (aOR 11.44), and those with household income 89–179 USD (aOR 2.76). Trust in health workers (aOR 6.12) and healthcare provider information (aOR 7.60) were strong predictors; prior malaria infection in children reduced uptake (aOR 0.31, 95% CI 0.18–0.54).

articles.headline: Ceiling-mounted nets reduce indoor Anopheles density in Peru
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ceiling-mounted mosquito nets for malaria vector control in a Peruvian Amazon riverine community: A stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial.
articles.authors: Quispe AM, Álvarez-Antonio C, Gutierrez Rodriguez F, Carey C, Rodríguez-Ferrucci H, Inti C, Kim ES
articles.details: This stepped-wedge cluster-randomized trial evaluated ceiling-mounted mosquito nets as a vector control intervention in 69 households from three clusters in the Llanchama community (San Juan Bautista district, Loreto) in the Peruvian Amazon from March to December 2024. Renovated households with ceiling-mounted nets showed a 55% reduction in indoor Anopheles counts (95% CI 33%–74%, p=0.004), 60% decrease in indoor bites per person per night (95% CI 27%–78%, p=0.003), and 61% reduction in indoor bites per person per hour (95% CI 15%–83%, p=0.018) compared to non-renovated households. Peridomestic mosquito counts did not differ significantly.

articles.headline: Tfh1-CCR7+ and Tfh2 cells drive antibody-mediated malaria immunity
articles.title_original: Tfh2 and a subset of Tfh1 cells associate with antibody-mediated immunity to malaria.
articles.authors: Soon MS, Oyong DA, Dooley NL, Mukhiya R, Pava Z, Andrew DW, Loughland JR, McCarthy JS, Chan JA, Beeson JG, Engwerda CR, Haque A, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Single-cell RNA and VDJ sequencing of peripheral blood Tfh cells from healthy subjects before and during controlled human malaria infection identified two Tfh1 subsets distinguished by CCR7 expression. Tfh1-CCR7+ and Tfh2 cells clonally expanded during infection and positively associated with protective antibody responses, while Tfh1-CCR7- cells did not, revealing functionally distinct subsets in antibody-mediated immunity.

articles.headline: Nanozyme immunoassay detects transmission-competent malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Malaria Parasite Transmission Competency Using a Nanozyme-Based Immunodiagnostic Targeting Female Gamete Antigen Release.
articles.authors: Haghighi T, Najer A, Broto M, Dahalan FA, Churchyard A, Yahiya S, Famodimu MT, Tunnicliff M, Abdelwahed A, Tachibana M, Ishino T, Aniweh Y, Awandare GA, Mahamar A, Vanheer LN, Bousema T, Drakeley C, Dicko A, Stone W, Baum J, Stevens MM
articles.details: A nanozyme-based immunoassay targeting PfG377 release during gametogenesis was developed to identify transmission-competent individuals. In patient samples from a transmission-focused trial, primaquine-treated individuals showed significantly lower PfG377 signals 2 days post-treatment, confirming the assay's ability to detect transmission-blocking efficacy and guide elimination strategies.

articles.headline: DP superior to SP for IPTp in pregnancy; SP selects resistance
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kizza J, Katairo T, Kakuru A, Nsengimaana B, Esilu T, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Gerlovina I, Hathaway N, Briggs J, Tukwasibwe S, Kiwuwa SM, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Randomized trial in pregnant women in Uganda compared monthly SP (sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine), DP (dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine), and DP+SP for intermittent preventive treatment. SP had poor efficacy with 57.8% cumulative recurrent parasitemia vs 4.1% for DP and 3.9% for DP+SP. SP treatment selected for increased resistance mutations (PfDHFR I164L and PfDHPS A581G combined prevalence rising from 24.9% to 35.2%), while DP/DP+SP showed minimal selection.

articles.headline: Correction to publication on malaria-associated acute kidney injury
articles.title_original: Correction: Simião et al. Involvement of Inflammatory Cytokines, Renal NaPi-IIa Cotransporter, and TRAIL Induced-Apoptosis in Experimental Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury. Pathogens 2024, 13, 376.
articles.authors: Simião GM, Parreira KS, Klein SG, Ferreira FB, Freitas FS, Silva EFD, Silva NM, Silva MVD, Lima WR
articles.details: Correction notice to Simião et al. publication. No substantive details provided in abstract.

articles.headline: Zanzibaris lack knowledge of acquired immunity despite 70 years interventions
articles.title_original: Reconsidering rebound malaria: Zanzibari voices on acquired immunity, risk, and history.
articles.authors: Graboyes M, Lefevre D, Meta J, Minu-Sepehr A
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in Zanzibar with 98 interviews and 301 survey participants assessed understanding of acquired immunity and rebound malaria. Lay public showed little to no familiarity with these concepts despite 70 years of international malaria work. Brief five-to-ten minute visual education followed by conversation changed understanding and was valued by participants, highlighting need for better public disclosure of risks and benefits.

articles.headline: Simple strategy to cut malaria infection rates in babies
articles.title_original: A simple strategy to cut malaria infection rates in babies.
articles.authors: O'Leary K
articles.details: Title only; full abstract not provided.

articles.headline: AI-guided pharmacogenetic modeling optimizes antimalarial dosing for Africa
articles.title_original: Artificial intelligence coupled to pharmacometrics modelling to tailor malaria and tuberculosis treatment in Africa.
articles.authors: Turon G, Mulubwa M, Montaner A, Njoroge M, Chibale K, Duran-Frigola M
articles.details: Pipeline coupling machine learning with physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) and non-linear mixed effects (NLME) modeling identifies drug-gene pairs with pharmacogenetic interest and proposes dose adjustments for malaria and tuberculosis treatments in African populations. Analysis addresses historical underrepresentation of African pharmacogenetic data to optimize therapeutic outcomes.

articles.headline: Low awareness but high acceptance of gene-drive mosquitoes in Uganda
articles.title_original: "Have Scientists Done Enough?" Community Perspectives on Gene Drive-Modified Mosquitoes for Malaria Control in Northern Uganda: A Mixed-Methods Study.
articles.authors: Madraa G, Musoke P, Nantale R, Bongomin F
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in Northern Uganda with 301 survey participants and 22 interviews assessed awareness and acceptance of gene-drive modified mosquitoes. Only 19.9% were aware of this technology, but 64.1% (95% CI: 58.4-69.5) reported acceptance. Independent predictors of acceptance included favourable attitude (aPR 1.25, p=0.013), high subjective norm (aPR 1.28, p=0.004), and high perceived behavioural control (aPR 1.23, p=0.013).

articles.headline: Deep learning improves protein annotation across Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: PlasmoFP: leveraging deep learning to predict protein function of uncharacterized proteins across the malaria parasite genus.
articles.authors: Srivastava HR, Berenberg D, Qassab O, Wang Z, Bonneau R, Carlton JM
articles.details: PlasmoFP deep learning models trained on phylogenetically relevant SAR supergroup proteins predict function of uncharacterized Plasmodium proteins. Across 19 Plasmodium species, the approach reduced unannotated proteins without Gene Ontology terms from 15-59% to 3-28% and increased fully annotated proteins from 7-42% to 36-68%, advancing parasite functional genomics research.

articles.headline: Plasmodium and Haemoproteus infections prevalent in wild birds across Great Britain
articles.title_original: The lineage diversity, spatiotemporal distribution and pathological significance of Plasmodium and Haemoproteus spp. infection of wild birds in Great Britain.
articles.authors: Heaver JP, John SK, Seilern-Macpherson K, Spiro S, Wilkinson V, Cunningham AA, Lawson B
articles.details: PCR screening of 857 wild birds over 15 years found 13.5% infected with avian haemosporidians: 8.9% (n=76) with Plasmodium and 4.7% (n=40) with Haemoproteus. Highest Plasmodium infection rates in Paridae (36.3%) and Turdidae (34.5%, including 51/148 blackbirds). Thirty AHP lineages detected including 23 novel to Great Britain. Histopathology of 13 Plasmodium-positive blackbirds revealed exoerythrocytic parasites or malaria-consistent lesions in 9 birds.

articles.headline: Age-structured model shows vaccination can achieve malaria eradication
articles.title_original: Age-structured vaccination strategies in mathematical modeling of malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Haringo AT, Obsu LL, Bushu FK
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Age-structured mathematical model of malaria transmission with vaccination examined stability of malaria-free and endemic equilibria in relation to effective reproduction number (Re). Malaria-free equilibrium is globally asymptotically stable when Re<1, while endemic equilibrium is stable when Re>1. Sensitivity analysis identified key parameters for prioritization in malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: HRP2/3 gene deletions prevalent in Ethiopian P. falciparum, affecting RDT validity
articles.title_original: High Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum HRP2/3 Gene Deletions in Ethiopia: Implications for Malaria Diagnosis and Treatment-A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Gebrie H, Abere A, Gashaw A, Gebremeskele BT, Bukayaw YA, Bayable A, Mekuriaw BY, Yimer EG, Demeke AD
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 studies in Ethiopia found high overall prevalence of PfHRP2/3 gene deletions: 35.64% (95% CI: 21.43-49.85). Specifically, PfHRP2 deletion prevalence was 8.48%, PfHRP3 was 23.74%, and concurrent deletions were 8.14%. These deletions compromise reliability of HRP2/3-based rapid diagnostic tests and warrant phasing out of HRP2/3 RDTs from national diagnostic algorithms.

articles.headline: Platelet distribution width predicts malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Predicting malaria in sudanese pregnant women: reliability of complete blood count.
articles.authors: Dura MC, Gürsoy B, Aktürk H, Aslan Ö, Hergüner M, Ezirmik E
articles.details: Retrospective study of 174 pregnant women (78 with malaria, 96 controls) comparing hematological parameters. Platelet distribution width was significantly lower in malaria group (p<0.05) and was the only parameter remaining significant in multivariate analysis with diagnostic accuracy AUC=0.857. Authors conclude platelet distribution width may serve as reliable marker for malaria in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Intermittent larviciding shows no impact on malaria incidence
articles.title_original: Large-scale intermittent larviciding intervention and associations with key malaria epidemiological parameters in Tanga Region, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Kailembo D, Gavana T, Kasagama E, Bernard J, Molteni F, Kisoka N, Yoram B, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Dismas C, Ross A, Chaki P, Lengeler C
articles.details: Large-scale study in Tanga Region covering >1.1 million population from June 2022 to April 2024 using Bacillus thuringiensis var. israelensis and Bacillus sphaericus (8 weeks per round, 3 rounds/year). No significant association with clinical malaria incidence: IRR 1.16 (95% CI 0.98–1.37) in high stratum, 0.86 (95% CI 0.72–1.02) in moderate, 0.96 (95% CI 0.76–1.22) in low stratum. Authors conclude intermittent larviciding showed no strong evidence of association with malaria epidemiological indicators.

articles.headline: Ivermectin dosing and diet affect mosquito killing efficiency
articles.title_original: Optimizing endectocide and ectocide compound evaluation in Anopheles malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Kobylinski KC, Hongsuwong T, Khemrattrakool P, Kaewkhao N, Kullasakboonsri R, Phanphoowong T, Sriwichai P, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Jittamala P, Tarning J
articles.details: Three experiments on Anopheles dirus and A. minimus assessing ivermectin efficacy. Multivitamin diet substantially increased LC50 compared to sucrose diet. Ivermectin mortality response was substantially increased when ingested in blood meal vs plasma meal for A. dirus (inverse for A. minimus). LC-MS/MS showed approximately 20% loss of ivermectin concentration in mosquito midgut compared to blood meal. Authors conclude mix diet minimizes control mortality and ivermectin may be partially excreted during blood meal.

articles.headline: Integrated vector-parasite strategy proposes framework for sustained control
articles.title_original: Integrated vector and parasite management for malaria control in high-endemic areas: pummel and pin strategy to prevent malaria setbacks.
articles.authors: Ranjbar M, Opigo J
articles.details: Viewpoint article discussing malaria control challenges in high-endemic areas of sub-Saharan Africa where endemicity levels rebound or stagnate despite interventions. Proposes Integrated Vector and Parasite Management (IVPM) with "Pummel and Pin" strategy to reduce both parasite reservoir and vectorial capacity. No quantitative results provided. Authors recommend operational research to assess feasibility and cost-effectiveness of IVPM.

articles.headline: Nigerian physicians show knowledge gaps on RTS,S and R21 vaccines
articles.title_original: Physicians' knowledge of, and willingness to encourage caregivers to partake in the RTS S/AS01 and R21 matrix M malaria vaccines roll out - a national survey in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oboh MA, Adeleke OT, Adeleke OV, Awotunde TA
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of Nigerian physicians on RTS,S/AS01 and R21 Matrix M vaccines. Results: 68.9% knew about malaria vaccines while 29.9% did not know; 64% correctly stated age 5 years for vaccine administration; majority willing to recommend vaccines to caregivers. Authors conclude many Nigerian physicians lack knowledge about malaria vaccination and need educational interventions to address concerns about efficacy, safety, dosing, affordability, and availability.

articles.headline: Fractional-order modeling reveals memory effects in malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Analysis of fractional-order model for the transmission dynamics of malaria via Caputo-Fabrizio and Atangana-Baleanu operators.
articles.authors: Agbata BC, Dervishi R, Gümüş M, Smerat A, Mbah GCE
articles.details: Theoretical study proposing fractional-order mathematical model of malaria transmission dynamics using three types of non-integer order differential operators (Caputo, Caputo-Fabrizio with exponential decay, Atangana-Baleanu with Mittag-Leffler kernel). Numerical simulations showed increasing fractional order slows disease spread, reduces peak infections, and prolongs outbreak duration. Authors conclude fractional-order models capture memory-dependent dynamics more accurately than classical models and recommend integration into malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: P. falciparum dominates imported malaria cases in Saudi Arabia
articles.title_original: Malaria in travelers and local populations: Comprehensive study of incidence patterns and origin-based classification in Saudi Arabia.
articles.authors: Alharbi B, Ahmed M
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of malaria cases in Saudi Arabia from January 2022 to December 2023 across 13 regions. Among 1,453,451 febrile cases, 0.7% (10,779) tested positive. In 2022, 0.9% (6,460) of 688,629 examined were positive; in 2023, 0.6% (4,319) of 764,822 were positive. Jazan exhibited highest incidence (59%) followed by Makkah (20%, P=0.046). P. falciparum was most common at 63.5%, followed by P. vivax–P. ovale (33%), P. malariae (0.5%), and mixed infections (3%). Authors conclude imported malaria was predominant type with P. falciparum as leading species.

articles.headline: Hybrid ML-time series models forecast malaria incidence accurately
articles.title_original: Hybrid models combining trend and seasonality components with machine learning algorithms provide accurate forecasting of malaria incidence.
articles.authors: Hussain SSA, Bedi S, Yadav CP, Mohanty AK, Mahatme K, Tyagi S, Krishnan NMA, Kota SH, Sharma A
articles.details: Comparative study of forecasting models using climate and malaria data from Goa, India (2010–2019). Tested 3 machine-learning models (SVM, Random Forest, XGB), 3 time-series models (ARIMA, SARIMA, SARIMAX), and 3 hybrid models (RF-ARMA, SVM-ARMA, XGB-ARMA). Machine-learning models showed lower accuracy (RMSE 13–37) but high precision; time-series models higher accuracy (RMSE 5–41) but lower precision. Hybrid models achieved both accuracy and precision (RMSE 0.5–15). Authors conclude hybrid models incorporating time-series components provide superior forecasting for malaria elimination planning.

articles.headline: Novel vinyl-DNADetect probes enable live malaria parasite imaging
articles.title_original: Vinyl-DNADetect Probes to Assess DNA Proliferation in Plasmodium Parasites using Inverse-Electron-Demand Diels-Alder Click Reactions.
articles.authors: Braun AC, Fisher GM, Hilko DH, Macdonald JR, Thomas S, Andrews KT, Poulsen SA
articles.details: Technical development of vinyl-2'-deoxyuridine (VdU)-based chemical probes for detecting DNA synthesis in Plasmodium parasites. Overcomes limitation of standard EdU probe which requires thymidine kinase (absent in Plasmodium). Novel probes use catalyst-free inverse-electron-demand Diels-Alder (IEDDA) click reaction, enabling live-cell imaging without fixation. Demonstrated high-efficiency labeling in P. falciparum using flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy, including first dual orthogonal pulse-chase labeling with VdU and EdU probes.

articles.headline: An. darlingi dominates malaria transmission in Brazilian Amazon Yanomami
articles.title_original: Bionomics and malaria infection rates in anopheline species in Yanomami villages of the Brazilian Amazon Forest.
articles.authors: Sánchez-Ribas J, Silva-do-Nascimento TF, Pereira-Ribeiro C, Santos-Neves MSA, Barnwell JW, Lourenço-de-Oliveira R, Oliveira-Ferreira J
articles.details: Longitudinal entomological survey of Yanomami villages in Brazilian Amazon from March 2013 to March 2015 collecting 1,962 adult Anopheles from 7 species. An. darlingi was predominant in three villages (52.5–87.9% of collections) with infection rates up to 2.1% for P. vivax and P. falciparum. Nightly human biting rates showed An. darlingi as primary vector across villages; in Taibrapa 2, An. nuneztovari exhibited 359 bites/night extradomestically. Outdoor entomological inoculation rate ranged 49.3–79.3 in three villages. Authors conclude village-specific vector control strategies needed.

articles.headline: Age, housing, and bed nets drive locally-acquired malaria in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Determinants of locally acquired malaria infections in Zanzibar: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Fakih BS, Seidahmed O, Holzschuh A, Abdul R, Irema I, Shija SJ, Aliy SM, Ali A, Fink G, Yukich J, Hetzel MW
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in five Zanzibar districts (May 2017–October 2018) of 17,891 individuals, 15,151 without recent travel. Among 7,286 tested by qPCR, 2.6% (95% CI 2–3%) were positive. Children aged 5–15 years had higher odds of infection (aOR=3.5, 95% CI 2.2–5.7) compared to older age groups. Bed nets protective (aOR=0.5, 95% CI 0.3–0.9), while open windows increased odds (aOR=2.1, 95% CI 1.4–3.3). Living in areas with moderate wetness increased odds (aOR=3.4, 95% CI 2.0–5.8). Authors recommend promoting bed net uptake and housing improvements.

articles.headline: Parasite clag2 gene amplification enables survival under nutrient stress
articles.title_original: Nutrient stress dramatically increases malaria parasite clag2 copy number to increase host cell permeability and enable pathogen survival.
articles.authors: Potchen NB, Silva TM, Kalia I, Lack JB, Desai SA
articles.details: Study of nutrient-stress adaptation in P. falciparum CLAG3-knockout parasites. CLAG3 knockout caused incomplete reduction in plasmodial surface anion channel (PSAC) activity and inability to expand under physiologically relevant nutrient-limiting conditions. In vitro selection obtained mutant capable of expansion; whole-genome sequencing revealed dramatically increased clag2 copy number without other changes. Quantitative PCR and transfection confirmed increased clag2 gene product. Authors propose CLAG2 directly forms nutrient channels and reveal substantial genome plasticity in Plasmodium.

articles.headline: Hypnozoite metabolites identified as diagnostic markers
articles.title_original: In vitro cultured malaria hypnozoites leave a footprint of specific metabolites.
articles.authors: Pasini EM, Hakimi H, Chaiyawong N, Klop O, Zeeman AM, Nieuwenhuis I, van der Werff N, Kihara K, Kami K, Kocken CHM, Kaneko O
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study combined in vitro Plasmodium cynomolgi hypnozoite cultures (a proxy for P. vivax) with sensitive metabolomics to identify specific hypnozoite-related metabolites in culture supernatant. The authors propose these metabolites could enable development of rapid diagnostic tests for hypnozoite infection to identify P. vivax carriers and enable targeted primaquine treatment while avoiding unnecessary drug exposure in non-infected individuals.

articles.headline: DR Congo consolidates malaria control gains amid elimination challenges
articles.title_original: Malaria in the Democratic Republic of Congo: how support programs consolidate elimination gains and protect at-risk populations from recrudescence.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Mufungizi I, Rasheed S, Tague C, Shams Z, Shah RJ, Wilczyńska W, Siddiqui HT, Atif MH, Tufail Z, Qadri M, Alhassan A, Scott GY, Wei CR, Mukherjee A, Muco Mugisha MJ, Bisimwa C, Bulondo D, Muhoza B, Langat AK
articles.details: Review of malaria burden in DR Congo, focusing on pregnant women and children under 5. Discusses innovations (genomic surveillance, RDTs, mHealth, vaccines RTS,S and R21, LLINs) and barriers (limited healthcare access, inadequate funding, insecticide resistance, surveillance gaps, donor dependence). Emphasizes need for sustainable financing, policy reinforcement, community engagement, and climate adaptation for elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Memory Tregs switch to protective helpers during malaria reinfection
articles.title_original: Memory Regulatory T Cells Reprogram into Protective Tfh-like Effectors in Recurrent Malaria.
articles.authors: Charles-Chess NAE, Ruberto AA, Bowers C, Obeng-Adjei N, Hansen MR, Renuka Prasad DB, Traore B, Klonowski KD, Crompton PD, Kurup SP
articles.details: Longitudinal studies in humans and mice showed memory regulatory T cells (mTregs) undergo antigen-driven expansion and epigenetic reprogramming during Plasmodium reinfection. These cells transition from immunosuppressive Foxp3+ phenotype to Bcl6+ follicular T helper (Tfh)-like effectors that enhance germinal center responses and antibody generation, facilitating parasite control.

articles.headline: TEP1 immune mechanisms compared in malaria and schistosomiasis vectors
articles.title_original: Comparative immunological roles of TEP1 in Anopheles gambiae and Biomphalaria glabrata: implications for malaria and schistosomiasis control.
articles.authors: Li H, Feng Y, Qian Y, Jiang W, Zhu Y, Xu J, Li X, Fei X, Wang R, Shao Y, Du L, Zhang X, Chen K
articles.details: Review comparing thioester-containing protein (TEP) activation and functional roles in malaria vector Anopheles gambiae and schistosomiasis vector Biomphalaria glabrata. Discusses TEP-mediated immune pathways (opsonization, reactive oxygen species generation, phagocytosis, melanization), cofactor interactions (LRIM1/APL1C, BgFREPs), and evolutionary conservation informing vector-control strategies.

articles.headline: Molecular markers distinguish imported from local malaria in Honduras
articles.title_original: Molecular Characterization of Plasmodium Species to Strengthen Malaria Surveillance in Migrant Populations in Honduras.
articles.authors: Godoy A, Euceda K, Pinto A, Valdivia HO, Chaver L, Ardon G, Fontecha G
articles.details: Study analyzed 14 molecular markers (6 in P. falciparum, 8 in P. vivax) from local and migrant samples in Honduras. P. falciparum isolates carried wild-type pfcrt; polymorphisms in pfmdr1, pfama1, pfglurp, and pfs47 distinguished migrant from local samples. P. vivax showed three novel VK210 haplotypes and the first VK247 variant in Honduras in migrants, plus novel haplotypes in pvmsp1, pvmsp3α, pvmsp3β, pvs47, and pvs48/45.

articles.headline: Symptom recognition and insurance enable timely child malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Determinants of timely malaria treatment among under-five children attending public health facilities in Kisumu East sub-county, Kenya: a health facility-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ochieng G, Silumbwe A, Sampa M, Maritim P, Zulu JM, Kato J, Okungu J, Jacobs C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 434 caregivers in Kenya (April-May 2023) examined determinants of timely (<24-hour) malaria treatment in under-five children. Caregivers' ability to recognize symptoms (AOR=2.9, p=0.006), belief in appropriate antimalarials (AOR=6.9, p<0.0001), and health insurance (AOR=2.1, p=0.005) were associated with timely treatment. Conversely, prior herbalist visits (AOR=0.1, p<0.0001) and fear of drug side effects (AOR=0.5, p=0.013) reduced odds of timely treatment.

articles.headline: SLDPQ safety confirmed: no impairment of folate recovery in children
articles.title_original: Plasma folate dynamics in Plasmodium falciparum-infected African children treated with artemisinin combination therapy and single low-dose primaquine or placebo.
articles.authors: Ajayi S, Onyamboko MA, Olupot-Olupot P, Ayuen DS, Chimjinda N, Taya C, Williams TN, Uyoga S, Maitland K, Fanello C, Day NPJ, Mukaka M, Taylor WRJ
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial of 408 children (6 months-11 years) in Uganda and Congo received artemether-lumefantrine or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine with SLDPQ (0.25 mg/kg) or placebo. Mean baseline folate concentrations were 10.83 ng/ml (SLDPQ) vs 10.92 (placebo), declined 1.65 ng/ml initially, then rose to 11.04 ng/ml by day 28. SLDPQ and G6PD status did not significantly affect folate trajectories, supporting safe use of SLDPQ without requiring G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Abbott-Bioline RDT shows critically low sensitivity for malaria detection
articles.title_original: Investigating the Abbott-Bioline™ malaria antigen Pf/Pv rapid diagnostic test.
articles.authors: Aung MTT, Thu AM, Phyo AP, Aung WL, Pateekhum C, Proux S, Yotyingaphiram W, Archusuksan L, Wongaeka M, White NJ, Day NP, Nosten F
articles.details: Field evaluation on Thailand-Myanmar border (October 2024-January 2025) comparing Abbott Bioline, First Response RDTs, and microscopy found Bioline had sensitivity 0.18 and specificity 0.99 for P. falciparum versus First Response 0.89 and 0.93. For P. vivax, Bioline sensitivity was 0.44 versus 0.59 for First Response. Laboratory testing showed Bioline detected only 45% (9/20) of P. falciparum and 74% (37/50) of P. vivax cases versus 90.3% and 84% for First Response, respectively.

articles.headline: Artesunate-amodiaquine efficacy sustained in Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artesunate-amodiaquine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children aged 2-11 years in the Asante Akim North Municipality, Ghana: a single-arm open trial.
articles.authors: Hanson H, Marfo KS, Osei-Mensa J, Jaeger A, Sarpong N, Tsali DN, Martey A, Gyimah AK, Placca E, Oppong M, Foli G, Almoustapha NS, Amuasi J, May J, Maïga Ascofaré O
articles.details: Prospective single-arm trial in Ghana (July 2018-June 2019) enrolled 173 children aged 2-11 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria treated with artesunate-amodiaquine. All achieved parasite clearance by day 3; adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) at day 28 was 92.07% by intention-to-treat and 100% by Kaplan-Meier analysis with no treatment failures. No pfk13 mutations linked to artemisinin resistance detected; pfmdr1 NFD haplotype present in 27.1% of isolates and pfcrt K76T in 1.9%.

articles.headline: Plasmodium male gametocytes show unique microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition
articles.title_original: A basal body microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition in Plasmodium male gametogenesis.
articles.authors: Yang S, Ma S, Yuan C, Li Z, Ji F, Yao L, Cui H, Guo Q, Yuan J
articles.details: Study using iterative ultrastructure expansion microscopy and cryo-electron tomography resolved microtubule organization in basal bodies of rodent malaria parasite P. yoelii male gametes.
