The parasite exhibited a microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition distinct from the canonical triplet-to-doublet seen in other systems. δ-Tubulin and ε-Tubulin were expressed in male gametocytes and localized at the proximal B-tubule, regulating axoneme assembly during male gametogenesis.

articles.headline: Novel serological biomarkers show diagnostic promise for malaria
articles.title_original: Evaluation of GDH and HDP as novel serological biomarkers for Plasmodium falciparum malaria diagnosis across varying parasitemia and transmission settings in India.
articles.authors: Ahmad A, Nema S, Arora K, Sikka R, Singh A, Krishna S, Singh MP, Verma AK, Nitika N, Anvikar AR, Gupta H, Bharti PK
articles.details: Study of 928 participants (886 P. falciparum-positive) evaluated antibody responses to novel biomarkers glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) and heam detoxification protein (HDP) compared to conventional antigens (HRP2, LDH, aldolase) using ELISA. All peptides showed high diagnostic accuracy (ROC AUC >96%) for P. falciparum detection. GDH2 and HRP2 effectively distinguished parasitemia levels; GDH2, HDP2, and LDH showed promise for distinguishing endemicity levels, though further refinement needed.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 polymorphism shapes age-dependent P. vivax infection dynamics
articles.title_original: How CYP2D6 Polymorphism Modulates the Community-Wide Risk of Plasmodium vivax Infection: A Panel Study in Amazonian Brazil.
articles.authors: Puça MCSB, Naziazeno IM, Santos VCFD, Rodrigues PT, Calil PR, Ladeia WA, Gil JP, Ferreira MU, Nobrega De Sousa T
articles.details: Community-based panel study of ~1300 individuals in Amazonian Brazil genotyped for CYP2D6 and assessed repeatedly for P. vivax using molecular diagnosis over 4-year follow-up. Children with impaired CYP2D6 activity showed higher infection frequency than those with normal enzyme activity; this pattern reversed in adolescence with increased prevalence in those having normal enzyme activity (p=0.0008). Adults with impaired CYP2D6 had lower parasite densities than younger individuals with normal enzyme activity (p=0.0383).

articles.headline: Malaria doesn't impair COVID-19 vaccine antibody response in HIV+ adults
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Parasitemia Does Not Diminish Neutralizing Antibody Responses After mRNA COVID-19 Booster Vaccination in HIV-infected Adults.
articles.authors: Samandari T, Achola M, Hutter JN, Mboya G, Otieno W, Kee JJ, Huang Y, Aponte JJ, Ockenhouse CF, Lee CK, Polakowski L, Yacovone M, Tapley A, Dadabhai S, Mkhize NN, Kaldine H, Bhebhe S, Moore PL, Hural J, Garrett N, Kublin JG
articles.details: Evaluated neutralizing antibody responses in asymptomatic HIV-infected adults with and without P. falciparum parasitemia who received mRNA-1273 booster vaccines. ID50 geometric mean titers exceeded 22,000 in both parasitemic and nonparasitemic participants 1 month after boosting. COVID-19 mRNA vaccine antibody responses remain unimpaired by concurrent asymptomatic parasitemia.

articles.headline: IgG3 allele IGHG3*30 protects against Plasmodium infection in PNG children
articles.title_original: Association of a Novel IgG3 Allele With Malaria in Children From the Sepik Region of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Saeed M, Aitken EH, Naung MT, Bourke C, Wu KW, Longley RJ, Chung AW, Damelang T, Kiniboro B, Mueller I, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study of 203 children aged 1-3 years from East Sepik, Papua New Guinea. IGHG3*30 allele carriers had significantly fewer Plasmodium spp infections (β = -1.736, 95% CI -3.39 to -0.079, p = 0.038), with strongest effect for P. vivax asymptomatic infections (β = -1.06, 95% CI -2.01 to -0.12, p = 0.028). IGHG3*30 carriers also showed lower IgG levels to P. vivax vaccine candidate proteins.

articles.headline: Malaria-induced neutrophil shift linked to Burkitt lymphoma susceptibility
articles.title_original: The Impact of Malaria-Induced Neutrophil Subset Shift and a Link to Burkitt Lymphoma.
articles.authors: Akinyi S, Tonui RK, Maina TK, Agwati E, Oduor CI, Njuguna FM, Keitany KK, Chepsiror D, Ayieko C, Moormann A, Kinyua AW, Forconi CS
articles.details: Flow cytometry analysis of neutrophil subsets in malaria-exposed and malaria-naive children and children with Burkitt lymphoma from Western Kenya. Malaria-exposed children exhibited increased frequencies of aged neutrophil subsets with reduced mature subset frequencies. Strong positive correlation (rs = 0.7, p < 0.0001) in immature neutrophils between malaria-exposed healthy and BL children, suggesting malaria-driven expansion may promote permissive environment for BL.

articles.headline: Apicoplast DNA polymerase structure balances replication and lesion bypass
articles.title_original: Structural basis of multitasking by the apicoplast DNA polymerase from Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kumari A, Enache T, Craggs TD, Pata JD, Lahiri I
articles.details: Cryoelectron microscopy structures of P. falciparum apicoplast DNA polymerase bound to undamaged DNA and nucleotide substrates in five pre-chemistry conformational states. apPol accommodates nascent base pairs with fingers in open configuration, facilitating lesion bypass activity. Nascent base pair checkpoint preferentially selects Watson-Crick pairs, essential for accurate replicative synthesis.

articles.headline: Focal MDA reduces P. vivax transmission in low-transmission Peru communities
articles.title_original: FocaL mass drug administration for Plasmodium vivax malaria elimination (FLAME): study protocol for an open-label cluster randomized controlled trial in Peru.
articles.authors: Fine SR, Soto Calle V, Altamirano Quiroz A, Rodriquez Ferruci H, Manrique P, Wu X, Carrasco Escobar G, Benjamin-Chung J, Bennett A, Auburn S, Price RN, Greenhouse B, Baird JK, Domingo GJ, Roh ME, Rosas A, Llanos-Cuentas A, Hsiang MS
articles.details: 3-year cluster randomized controlled trial (FLAME) in Loreto, Peru with 30 low-transmission communities (API < 250 cases/1000 population) randomized 1:1 to focal MDA versus control. High-risk individuals within 200 m of P. vivax case receive three MDA cycles over 2 years: 25 mg/kg chloroquine plus 300 mg tafenoquine (age ≥16) or 0.5 mg/kg/day primaquine × 7 days (age <16). Primary outcome is cumulative incidence symptomatic P. vivax malaria, powered to detect 68% relative reduction.

articles.headline: Health worker density and institutional quality reduce malaria burden in SSA
articles.title_original: Institutional quality, aid flows, and malaria burden: a geospatial analysis of sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Namubiru C
articles.details: Geospatial analysis of 38 sub-Saharan African countries (2010-2022) using spatial econometric models to assess relationships between institutional quality, development aid, and malaria burden (cases and deaths per 1,000 population). Increased health worker density, enhanced institutional effectiveness, and higher aid levels positively associated with malaria burden reduction. Malaria burden demonstrated significant spatial autocorrelation annually with limited spillover effects from neighboring countries.

articles.headline: ITN non-use and pharmacy distance associated with 26% malaria prevalence
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria infection and associated factors among children under five years attending health facilities in the Nandom Municipality, Upper West Region, Ghana.
articles.authors: Boadu S, Azornu CK, Axame W, Bonsu EO, Wumnaya R, Yidana S, Abanga WA, Sarpomaa Asante L, Adjei MR, Kubio C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 404 children with febrile illness in 6 health facilities (July 2024). Malaria prevalence 26% by microscopy (CI 21.9-30.5%). Children not using insecticide-treated bed nets were 2.01 times more likely to test positive (aOR 2.01, 95% CI 1.02-3.93). Children with >30-minute travel time to pharmacy had higher odds of malaria (aOR 2.19, 95% CI 1.08-4.45).

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine coverage 69-91% for dose 1 in three African countries
articles.title_original: POST introduction evaluation (PIE) of the malaria vaccine introduced in three pilot countries (Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi) in 2021.
articles.authors: Walldorf JA, Mayers GF, Amponsa-Achiano K, Jalang'o R, Chisema M, Khalayi L, Njoroge J, Haji A, Tweneboah PO, Sally ET, Mategula D, Mhone B, Zimba B, Chirwa E, Mohammed NT, Adjei MR, Sillah J, Pellaux-Furrer E, Magafu MGMD, Okine RNA
articles.details: WHO post-introduction evaluation of RTS,S malaria vaccine in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi assessed at least 1 year after introduction. Reported median coverage at 24-30 months: dose 1 69-91%, dose 2 62-82%, dose 3 58-81%. Best practices included early subnational involvement, health worker training, community engagement, defaulter tracing, and ongoing supervisory visits. Vaccine successfully integrated into routine childhood immunization programs with comprehensive planning and social mobilization.

articles.headline: Supply chain gaps and delayed IPT adoption hinder pregnant women care
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers of malaria prevention and treatment services to pregnant women in Ethiopia: a multi-level health system analysis, 2025.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Yenesew MA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Asemahagn MA, Enbiale W
articles.details: Qualitative study of 49 multi-level stakeholders in Ethiopia exploring facilitators and barriers to malaria prevention and treatment for pregnant women. Facilitators included prioritized bed net distribution, NGO partnerships, increased antenatal care time, and expanded diagnostics. Barriers: chloroquine shortages, fragmented service delivery, staff shortages, policy gaps including absence of targeted interventions and delayed IPT adoption, and inadequate surveillance data.

articles.headline: Correction: Anopheles species associated with malaria in Colombia gold-mining areas
articles.title_original: Correction: Anopheles species associated with malaria transmission in gold-mining areas of northwest Colombia.
articles.authors: Rojas MA, Ahumada ML, Velasco-Pareja MC, Chaparro P, Yasnot-Acosta MF, Orjuela LI
articles.details: Correction notice for article on Anopheles species and malaria transmission in gold-mining areas of northwest Colombia. Specific corrections and findings not detailed in title.

articles.headline: Vitamin E and iron deficiency strongly associated with malaria infection
articles.title_original: Associations between undernutrition and malaria infection: a case-control study from Rwanda.
articles.authors: Uwimana A, Alexiou H, Mutoni JD, Robert A, Rujeni N, Cani PD, Coutelier JP, Mutesa L, Everard A
articles.details: Case-control study in Rwanda (November 2021-December 2023) comparing malaria cases (positive blood smear) to malaria-free household controls in 9 endemic districts. Risk of vitamin E deficiency (aOR 7.46, 95% CI 4.43-12.58, p < 0.001) and iron deficiency (aOR 1.80, 95% CI 1.11-2.93, p = 0.017) strongly associated with malaria. Age, female sex, and selenium deficiency were inversely associated with malaria.

articles.headline: Testing and treatment high but patient tracking poor for malaria
articles.title_original: Adherence to the test, treat and track malaria policy among selected health facilities in Ghana: the clients' perspective.
articles.authors: Kyei C, Tawiah C, Agbokey F, Anim JA, Tivura M, Afari-Asiedu S, Kyeremateng CK, Kornu FM, Oppong J, Zuuri W, Nyampong I, Agana VA, Awimba PI, Ankonu AN, Awine E, Welega P, Gyaase S, Tetteh RJ, Mayers P, Harrison SBE, Asante KP
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (November 2019) in 30 health facilities across 3 epidemiological zones in Ghana; 590 patients interviewed. Testing and treatment adherence high across all zones. Patient tracking adherence very low. Southern zone facilities 2.87 times more likely to adhere to test, treat, track policy compared to middle zone (aOR 2.87, 95% CI 1.7-4.8, p < 0.001). Males less likely to return for review or home visit.

articles.headline: Malaria transmission determinants in Indian districts: ensemble model insights
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria transmission in Indian districts in 2018: insights from ensemble models.
articles.authors: Sam AK, Khatoon A, Phuleria HC
articles.details: District-wise spatial analysis using Random Forest and Poisson regression models identified clusters of annual parasite incidence (API) and annual falciparum incidence (AFI) in central and northeastern Indian districts, with additional hotspots in northern regions. Socio-economic factors including water inaccessibility, lack of mobile phones, and mother's education significantly influenced transmission. Study recommends clusters of high-burden districts as strategic targets for interventions promoting socio-economic development and education.

articles.headline: EBA175 antibodies persist despite mass malaria testing and treatment
articles.title_original: Increased Plasmodium falciparum EBA175RIII-V antibody titres despite a 13-month malaria mass testing, treatment, and tracking intervention in a high malaria endemic community in Ghana.
articles.authors: Tey PK, Brashear A, Kwapong SS, Acquah FK, Bredu DG, Busayomi A, Anang SF, Abban BC, Nagaoka H, Takashima E, Obiri-Yeboah D, Cui L, Amoah LE
articles.details: Quarterly mass testing, treatment, and tracking (MTTT) intervention over 13 months in high-transmission Ghana involving 314 participants. Infection prevalence (PCR) decreased from 56.2% at baseline to 46.8% at study end; 67.5% were infected at least once. Overall anti-PfEBA175RIII-V IgG antibody titers fluctuated but did not significantly decrease; infected individuals had significantly higher titers than uninfected. MTTT intervention showed no significant effect on naturally acquired immunity related to this antigen.

articles.headline: Elevated ESR associated with severe malaria: meta-analysis findings
articles.title_original: A systematic review and meta-analysis of Plasmodium infections and erythrocyte sedimentation rate in relation to disease severity.
articles.authors: Kotepui M, Suwannatrai AT, Kotepui KU, Mahittikorn A, Masangkay FR, Thinkhamrop K, Wangdi K, Wilairatana P
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 19 studies comparing erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) in malaria versus non-malarial controls and severe versus non-severe malaria. Malaria patients had significantly higher ESR than controls (standardized mean difference 3.0037, P=0.0171). Severe malaria cases had higher ESR than non-severe (SMD 2.2556, P=0.0014). High between-study heterogeneity (I²>96%) and publication bias were detected, limiting confidence in associations despite ESR's potential as a supportive diagnostic marker.

articles.headline: Correction: CRP as bacterial infection and malaria marker study
articles.title_original: Correction: Evaluation of CRP as a marker for bacterial infection and malaria in febrile children at the Douala Gyneco-Obstetric and Pediatric Hospital.
articles.authors: PLOS One Editors
articles.details: Correction notice to prior publication on CRP evaluation as a marker for bacterial infection and malaria in febrile children.

articles.headline: Pyronaridine-artesunate effective for uncomplicated falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Pyronaridine-artesunate for treating uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Fox T, Otieno JA, Pryce J, Hine P
articles.details: Cochrane review of 15 studies (14 RCTs, 7295 participants) comparing pyronaridine-artesunate (PY-AS) to alternative artemisinin-based combination therapies for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Versus artemether-lumefantrine, PY-AS showed superior efficacy: PCR-adjusted treatment failure at day 28 (RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.19-0.85, moderate certainty); unadjusted failures at day 28 (RR 0.27, 95% CI 0.14-0.52) and day 42 (RR 0.61, 95% CI 0.46-0.82). Similar advantages found versus artesunate-amodiaquine.

articles.headline: CD8 T cells efficiently find Plasmodium via liver sinusoid constraints
articles.title_original: Liver sinusoid constraints make random search of CD8 T cells for Plasmodium parasites efficient.
articles.authors: Zenkov VS, Cockburn IA, Lin R, Griffith WN, Langston MA, Ganusov VV
articles.details: Mathematical modeling and imaging study of CD8 T cell search for Plasmodium sporozoites in liver sinusoids. Graph-based simulations of imaged sinusoids showed that <10^5 CD8 T cells per liver, moving randomly without attraction at experimentally observed speeds, can find infection within 24 hours—well below the lifespan of the Plasmodium liver stage. Results suggest tissue-constrained movement increases search efficiency, contradicting prior assumptions that T cell attraction is necessary for finding infections in tissues.

articles.headline: Immune-metabolic responses differ between Plasmodium falciparum and vivax
articles.title_original: Systems Biological Analysis of Immune-Metabolic Host Responses to Distinct Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Lima DV, Guimarães TP, Almeida ACG, Teixeira AJ, Monteiro WM, Melo GC, Gardinassi LG
articles.details: Multi-omics analysis integrating blood flow cytometry, transcriptomics, and metabolomics from simian models (P. coatneyi, P. cynomolgi) and human malaria (P. falciparum, P. vivax). Identified conserved immune-metabolic responses across species: dendritic cell dynamics, CD8+ effector memory cells, HMOX1 and coagulation gene upregulation, complement activation, kynurenine and hormone metabolites, and tyrosine metabolism activity. Chemokine and T cell differentiation gene modules were predominantly associated with P. cynomolgi and P. vivax, suggesting significant parasite-species-specific host responses.

articles.headline: Molecular methods reliably detect and identify Plasmodium in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Detection of Plasmodium in Anopheles spp (Diptera: Nematocera: Culicidae) using molecular biology techniques: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Fonseca EDC, David EDS, Santos KCD, Oliveira FP, Barbosa LMC, Komninakis SV, Souto RNP
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Systematic review of molecular biology techniques for detecting Plasmodium in Anopheles mosquitoes. Analysis of 12 studies (from 484 initially retrieved) using molecular methods to identify parasite species in mosquito samples. Findings demonstrate that molecular biology provides accurate and reliable species identification, contributing to effective epidemiological response and surveillance monitoring of Plasmodium spread in endemic areas despite challenges in applying molecular techniques to mosquito samples.

articles.headline: Malaria transmission shift toward elimination in Brazilian Amazon
articles.title_original: Changes in the malaria transmission profile in a low-endemic area of the Brazilian Amazon: a cause for concern.
articles.authors: Oliveira-Neto FM, Albuquerque HG, Silva ARD, Gonçalves EDGDR, Suárez-Mutis MC
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Descriptive retrospective analysis of 83,517 malaria cases in Maranhao state, Brazil (2003-2022). Annual parasite incidence recently considered low-risk in most municipalities. Epidemiological profile shifted from predominantly autochthonous (67.8%) to increasing imported cases from abroad and other Brazilian states. Proportion of P. falciparum declined significantly from 17.7% (2003) to 6.01% (2022). Most cases in males aged 15-45 years, increasing from 59.1% to 70.5%, raising concerns about transmission reintroduction near elimination.

articles.headline: Mathematical models predict recurrent malaria infection patterns in cohorts
articles.title_original: How do recurrent malaria infections occur in clinical cohorts: a mathematical modelling study to support study planning.
articles.authors: Krumkamp R, Rautman LH, Maiga-Ascofaré O, May J, Lorenz E
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study developing five compartmental models representing different malaria transmission scenarios in naïve cohorts: constant infection risk, treatment-mediated protection, vaccination effects, heterogeneous transmission, and seasonal variation. Results show transmission scenarios strongly affect recurrent infection patterns; vaccination substantially reduces first and recurring infections, while heterogeneous transmission leads to high recurrence in high-risk subpopulations. Study provides equations for estimating expected recurrent events in longitudinal studies.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminths significantly reduce hemoglobin in Cameroon
articles.title_original: The impact of malaria and intestinal helminths on haemoglobin concentrations among armed forces of the North West and South West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Che SB, Ntonifor HN, Tofel KH, Jugha VT, Wanyu BY, Taiwe GS
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 812 male armed forces in Cameroon. Plasmodium overall prevalence 13.6% (P. falciparum 86.6% of cases, mostly mild malaria; P. vivax also identified). Intestinal parasites in 22.8%, including Ascaris lumbricoides, Trichuris trichiura, hookworms, Schistosoma mansoni, and S. intercalatum. Malaria, Trichuris trichiura, and Schistosoma mansoni significantly affected mean hemoglobin concentration. Study emphasizes that asymptomatic carriers serve as transmission agents, necessitating government efforts to eradicate or limit transmission.

articles.headline: Wing morphometrics distinguish malaria vector species in Siberia
articles.title_original: Unraveling Wing Shape Variation in Malaria Mosquitoes from the Arctic Edge: A Geometric Morphometric Study in Western Siberia.
articles.authors: Calderon X, Artemov G, Burlak VA, Alexeeva S, Hernández-P R, Suazo MJ, Pérez LM, Benítez HA, Correa M
articles.details: Geometric morphometric analysis of wing venation in Anopheles from Western Siberia (An. messeae, An. daciae, An. beklemishevi, and hybrids). Procrustes ANOVA, principal component analysis, and canonical variate analysis revealed significant shape and size differences among species; hybrids showed intermediate morphologies. Landmarks on radial and medial veins provided most reliable species separation. No static allometry detected; wing shape differences not explained by size. Findings support wing morphometrics combined with molecular identification for vector surveillance.

articles.headline: Occurrence and Distribution of Three Low Molecular Weight PAHs in Caño La Malaria, Cucharillas Marsh (Cataño, Puerto Rico): Spatial and Seasonal Variability, Sources, and Ecological Risk
articles.title_original: Occurrence and Distribution of Three Low Molecular Weight PAHs in Caño La Malaria, Cucharillas Marsh (Cataño, Puerto Rico): Spatial and Seasonal Variability, Sources, and Ecological Risk.
articles.authors: Berríos-Rolón PJ, Márquez F, Cotto MC
articles.details: Occurrence and Distribution of Three Low Molecular Weight PAHs in Caño La Malaria, Cucharillas Marsh (Cataño, Puerto Rico): Spatial and Seasonal Variability, Sources, and Ecological Risk

articles.headline: Plasmodium-helminth coinfection increases parasitemia and anemia in Ethiopian patients
articles.title_original: Integrated assessment of malaria parasite load, anemia, and associated factors in patients with and without soil-transmitted helminthes coinfection in Southern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye B, Mitiku A, Kasse A, Getu E, Gemechu T, Misganaw T, Degefa T
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 220 patients in Dilla zuria, Southern Ethiopia (110 monoinfected, 110 coinfected with soil-transmitted helminths) found coinfected patients had higher mean parasitemia (40,093.54 parasites/µl). Hookworm and Trichuris trichiura positively associated with parasitemia; hemoglobin levels significantly lower in coinfected patients with hookworm (p < 0.001) and Trichuris trichiura (p = 0.038). Integrating deworming into malaria case management may mitigate anemia.

articles.headline: Environmental and socioeconomic factors predict malaria transmission in West Africa
articles.title_original: Environmental and socio-economic determinants of malaria transmission in West Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Gbaguidi GJ, Topanou N, Filho WL, Begedou K, Ketoh GK
articles.details: Systematic review of 68 articles identified environmental and socioeconomic determinants of malaria transmission in West Africa. Environmental factors including air temperature, rainfall, relative humidity, and vegetation were identified as most common predictors. Socioeconomic determinants including education level, place of residence, housing structure, poverty, and quality of information were identified as key factors influencing malaria transmission risk.

articles.headline: Adaptive cycling of multiple antimalarials: feasible health systems strategy in Kenya
articles.title_original: Health system challenges and facilitators associated with adaptive cycling deployment of multiple first-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria: a pilot study in a malaria-endemic region of Kenya.
articles.authors: Cole A, Chege T, Aman R, Githuka G, Muga R, Aspinall A, Kokwaro G
articles.details: Pilot study (June 2020–June 2022) comparing adaptive cycling of four artemisinin-based combinations (AL, DHA-PIP, ASAQ, PYR-ART) in Homa Bay implementation site versus Migori control in western Kenya. Cost per patient treated was USD 3. Migori control showed 12.5 percentage points higher malaria positivity (23.3% vs 10.8%). Main challenges: drug supply logistics, human resource constraints, and diagnostic kit stock-outs. Authors conclude adaptive cycling of multiple first-line treatments is feasible with adequate planning.

articles.headline: Climate change projected to decrease malaria transmission in most of India
articles.title_original: Changing climate and its impacts on the dynamics of future malaria transmission over certain endemic regions in India.
articles.authors: Bal PK, Kumar DS, Parihar RS, Saini A
articles.details: VECTRI model predicted malaria transmission dynamics over four climatological zones of India under SSP5-8.5 scenario with CMIP6 climate data. By end of century (2080s) compared to baseline (1985-2014), most regions showed 10–30% decrease in EIR with temperature increase of 4–5°C and rainfall increase of 10–40%. Few exceptions in western and peninsular regions showed increased EIR. Decreases controlled by temperature and rainfall during monsoon seasons.

articles.headline: LAMP-based test detects asymptomatic P. falciparum with 95% sensitivity
articles.title_original: Sensitive near point-of-care detection of asymptomatic and submicroscopic Plasmodium falciparum infections in African endemic countries.
articles.authors: Rakotomalala Robinson D, Pennisi I, Cavuto ML, Kiemde F, Chamai M, Some DY, Quigley E, Malpartida-Cardenas K, Ndiath MO, Correa S, Darboe B, Stewart LB, Georgiou P, Baldeh M, Tinto H, Cunnington AJ, Erhart A, D'Alessandro U, Rodriguez-Manzano J
articles.details: LAMP-based diagnostic platform evaluated on 672 capillary blood samples from The Gambia and Burkina Faso, with 146 P. falciparum positives confirmed by qPCR. Achieved 95.2% sensitivity (95% CI: 90.4–98.1) and 96.8% specificity (95% CI: 94.9–98.0). Detected 94.9% (130/137) of asymptomatic infections and 95.3% (41/43) of submicroscopic cases (<16 parasites/µL), outperforming expert microscopy and rapid diagnostic tests.

articles.headline: Malaria coinfection present in acute febrile illness patients in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Detection of dengue, malaria, and additional causes of acute febrile illness: The need for expanded testing, Bayelsa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akanbi DO, Abaye BB, Averhoff F, Berg MG, Orf GS, Lawan KM, Beckett GA, Omotoso AB, Mata M, Cloherty GA, Chabuka L, de Oliveira T, Mac-Fisi KW, Walter A, Mark II, Edeki G, Sinayoko A, Nasrullah M, Justina BI, Balogun MS
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 443 acute febrile illness patients at four hospitals in Bayelsa State, Nigeria (20 May–15 June 2022). Malaria prevalence was 42.4% (n = 188) by thick smear microscopy. Acute DENV seroprevalence was 14.5% (n = 64), with 6.5% (n = 29) coinfected with both acute DENV and malaria. Multiple viral pathogens identified by NGS in unknown cases. Authors highlight need for routine malaria testing in acute febrile illness patients.

articles.headline: Low P. vivax and P. ovale prevalence in Cameroon highlands
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of ovale, vivax and falciparum malaria in the highlands of Cameroon: An integrated community survey of human infection and vector abundance.
articles.authors: White SJ, Tchuenkam VPK, Mbouh M, Gaither C, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Kiam BC, Popkin-Hall ZR, Sadler JM, Carey-Ewend K, Hand E, Ngum MN, Ngomsi YN, Bailey JA, Kaunda DB, Mafo LK, Lemogo GN, Dinka CL, Nsani CA, Noubom M, Goel V, Ibrahima I, Onguene JC, Lin FC, Lin JT, Nsango SE, Dinglasan RR, Juliano JJ, Ali IM
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 3,661 participants in Dschang, Cameroon identified: P. vivax 0.1%, P. ovale spp. 0.64%, P. falciparum 8.1% by qPCR. P. ovale spp. coinfected with P. falciparum in 23.1% of ovale cases. Gametocytemia uncommon in qPCR-positive ovale infections despite being common in falciparum. P. vivax and P. ovale spp. concentrated in Penka-Michel and Baleveng with higher Anopheles abundance. Children and those with recent domestic travel had higher odds of P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 41.2% in febrile patients in northwest Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and associated factors among febrile patients in selected healthcare facilities of Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Dagnew A, Yeshanew S, Nibret E, Atenafu G, Kendie FA, Assefa M, Habtamu A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 420 febrile patients in Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia (April–November 2023) found 41.2% malaria prevalence (95% CI: 0.365–0.459), with P. falciparum at 64.8%. Risk factors included lack of formal education (AOR = 2.452, p = 0.047), proximity to breeding sites (AOR = 43.224, p = 0.015), and awareness gaps (AOR = 1.384, p = 0.044). Authors recommend strengthening education, housing improvements, and sustained access to diagnosis and treatment.

articles.headline: Glutathione S-transferase inhibition as novel antimalarial drug target
articles.title_original: Targeting phase II detoxification enzyme inhibition in Plasmodium for malaria treatment.
articles.authors: Onisuru O, Achilonu I
articles.details: Review of Plasmodium glutathione S-transferase (GST) as a potential novel antimalarial drug target. GST plays a critical role in the parasite's xenobiotic metabolism and detoxification of toxic molecules. The authors propose that inhibition of GST in P. falciparum and P. vivax, particularly through phase II biotransformation/detoxification processes, represents a promising avenue for antimalarial drug discovery against multidrug-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Limited genetic diversity of PfCelTOS vaccine candidate in Vietnam
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of cell-traversal protein for ookinetes and sporozoites (CelTOS) in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Vietnam.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Lê HG, Kang JM, Minh Trinh NT, Cho M, Goo YK, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Genetic analysis of pfceltos sequences from 366 Vietnamese P. falciparum isolates from three endemic provinces (Dak Lak, Gia Lai, Phu Yen) collected 2018–2022. Identified 29 polymorphic amino acid changes, with four substitutions (K100N, S104L, F117V, N119K) commonly detected. Overall low genetic diversity; Dak Lak pfceltos showed greater diversity and different population structure. Limited genetic diversity and conservation of biologically significant amino acids support PfCelTOS as a vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: TLR7-STAT3 pathway regulates macrophage responses to Plasmodium infection
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection regulates M1 macrophage polarization and function through TLR7/STAT3 signaling pathway.
articles.authors: Mo L, Jiang Z, Hong C, Guo J, Qiu G, Wu Q, Deng G, Chen Y, Lu Y, Zhou H, Liu Q, Luo X, Zhao Y, Qi Y, Li Q, Ma X, Yang Q
articles.details: Experimental study in P. yoelii-infected mice showed infection triggered M1 macrophage polarization and enhanced phagocytosis via TLR7 upregulation. TLR7 agonist R848 alleviated infection. STAT3 inhibitors attenuated TLR7 effects, indicating TLR7-STAT3 pathway regulates macrophage polarization. Macrophage depletion increased parasitemia and weakened T and B cell responses. Macrophages are essential for innate and adaptive immunity against Plasmodium infection.

articles.headline: ZY19489 resistance paradoxically restores piperaquine susceptibility
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.authors: Okombo J, Ndiaye T, Qahash T, Moura IMR, Gil-Iturbe E, Hagenah LM, Bridgford JL, Bonatto V, Ward KE, Yeo T, Narwal SK, Orta LV, Anderson I, Dhingra SK, Pasaje CFA, Park H, Kim J, Guido RVC, Angulo-Barturen I, Niles JC, Mancia F, Uhlemann AC, Mok S, Quick M, Winzeler EA, Leroy D, Llinás M, Thathy V, Fidock DA
articles.details: Mechanistic study identified novel PfCRT mutation mediating low-grade resistance to ZY19489 (triaminopyrimidine in Phase IIb trial) with substantial parasite fitness cost. Parasites resistant to ZY19489 lost chloroquine resistance and became hypersusceptible to piperaquine. All three agents interfered with hemoglobin catabolism; ZY19489 blocked mutant PfCRT-mediated efflux and reduced intracellular hemoglobin-derived peptides while increasing pyrimidine deoxynucleotides, creating an evolutionary trap that restores piperaquine efficacy.

articles.headline: AI decision support boosts Sudan physician antimalarial protocol adherence
articles.title_original: Enhancing Physicians' Adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol Using AI as an Intervention Tool.
articles.authors: Muhammed A, Ibrahim S, Mohammed AAY, Mohamed Ahmed AK, Idris MYM, Yousif MMO, Omer ITA, Rababah A, Sherfeldin Mohammed HE, Ahmed SAE, Osman Ahmed Osman M, Musa Mohamed ZH, Mohamed Ibrahim LA, Musa Hussain EY, Ali HKM, Mohamed Mustafa FA, Eltahir SME, Elhag MES, Kanan Ahmed AA
articles.details: Two-cycle clinical audit of 50 physicians in Sudan (July–September 2025) evaluated AI-based clinical decision-support system for 2023 malaria protocol. Protocol recognition increased from 38% to 100% (risk difference +62%, p<0.001); IV/IM artesunate awareness from 53% to 98% (RD +45%, p<0.001); severe malaria attitudes from 15% to 91% (RD +76%, p<0.001). Mean compliance increased from 50.7% to 96.8% (p<0.001). Residual gaps in microscopy reporting and G6PD testing persisted.

articles.headline: Computational vaccine design achieves 87% global HLA population coverage
articles.title_original: Immunoinformatics-Based Multi-Epitope Vaccine Design Against P. falciparum-Causing Malaria: A Computational Approach.
articles.authors: Larbi AA, Mensah C, Korankye R, Darkwah AA, Agyemang EB, Ayikpah IY, Kwarteng A
articles.details: Immunoinformatics study designed multi-epitope vaccine against P. falciparum using B cell, Tc cell, and Th cell epitopes from five conserved parasite antigens. Predicted vaccine construct covers estimated 87.07% of world population based on HLA data, demonstrated appropriate physicochemical properties, showed strong stable TLR4 interactions in molecular dynamics simulations, and predicted long-lasting memory B and CD4+ T cell responses in immune simulations. Experimental validation is required.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions cause HRP2-RDT failures in Malawi—first report
articles.title_original: First report of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions compromising HRP2-based malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Malawi.
articles.authors: Louis JM, Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Syahada JH, Fitriana F, Muh F, Chun W, Park WS, Lee SJ, Na S, Lu F, Han ET, Han JH
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1582 participants at referral hospitals in Mzuzu (n=1186) and Lilongwe (n=396), Malawi (December 2020–June 2021) analyzed 391 P. falciparum samples for pfhrp2/pfhrp3 deletions. Malaria prevalence was 22.9% in Mzuzu (97.1% symptomatic) and 45.2% in Lilongwe (94.2% asymptomatic). RDT sensitivity 78.5% vs microscopy 64.8%; RDT specificity 93.6% vs microscopy 95.4%. Dual pfhrp2/3 deletions found in 15.0% Lilongwe and 10.4% Mzuzu isolates; all dual-deleted samples were RDT false-negative but microscopy/qPCR positive.

articles.headline: Urban schoolchildren bear highest malaria burden despite control efforts
articles.title_original: Malaria among schoolchildren in urban, peri-urban and rural settings in Eastern Ethiopia: repeated cross section study.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Abiy E, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Repeated cross-sectional study in Eastern Ethiopia (November 2023 and May 2024) of primary schoolchildren found malaria prevalence 5.1% (95% CI 3.84–6.7) during high transmission and 2.24% (95% CI 1.48–3.37) during low transmission seasons. Prevalence was highest in urban settings (7.3% high transmission) compared to peri-urban (4.97%) and rural areas (2.9%, p=0.007). P. falciparum dominated (83% high, 59% low transmission). Non-ITN use (AOR 2.2, 95% CI 1.2–3.99) and presence of under-five children (AOR 1.9, 95% CI 1.1–3.38) predicted infection during high transmission season.

articles.headline: Chrysosplenetin reverses artemisinin resistance via multiple pathway mechanisms
articles.title_original: Chrysosplenetin acts as a homeostasis stabilizer with dual-function in shattering Plasmodium berghei K173 resistance to artemisinin driven by both ABC transporters and heme-ROS/GSH axis.
articles.authors: Ji H, Huang Y, Tian J, Bo R, Heng X, Yang J, Huang Y, Wang X, Tan Q, Gao H, Chen L, Chen J
articles.details: Preclinical study in WT and Mdr1a-knockout mice infected with artemisinin-resistant P. berghei K173 compared artemisinin monotherapy with artemisinin-chrysosplenetin combination. Combination therapy achieved 100% survival and ~20% parasitemia. Chrysosplenetin reversed resistance through dual mechanisms: disrupting ABC transporter function and modulating ROS/GSH balance (fold changes 1.02–10.18), hemoglobin catabolism, and signaling pathways (PI3K/AKT-mTOR, MAPK). Effects were partially independent of P-gp functionality.

articles.headline: Malaria narratives reveal socioeconomic devastation beyond clinical burden
articles.title_original: Malaria in their words: thematic analysis of online narratives from Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Zhang SH, Nwankwoala O, Jakazi CS, D'Amiano AJ, Kabuya JB, Kapungu K, Laker M, Ippolito MM
articles.details: Qualitative thematic analysis of 160 online narratives from five Sub-Saharan African countries (Kenya n=121, Uganda n=33) explored lived experiences of malaria. Vulnerability contexts included poor housing and occupational hazards (agriculture, pre-dawn labor). Consequences encompassed severe financial burden from treatment forcing healthcare–livelihood tradeoffs, household triage decisions among multiple infected family members, lost workdays and closed businesses, school dropout from financial constraints, and lasting disabilities (vision loss, mobility impairments, cognitive challenges) leading to social exclusion and diminished long-term well-being.

articles.headline: Indigenous beliefs and spiritual practices shape malaria prevention behavior
articles.title_original: Indigenous knowledge systems and malaria prevention in Zimbabwe: a market basket analysis.
articles.authors: Chapoterera B, Marume A, Midzi N, Naidoo K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 480 adults in Manicaland Province, Zimbabwe found 36.7% reported malaria in previous 3 months and 54.0% of families affected. Indigenous knowledge sourced primarily from older people (27.7%), informal discussions (7.7%), and observations (5.6%). Association rule mining identified strong inverse relationship between prophet visits and traditional healer visits (support 0.66, confidence 0.91, lift 1.08). Health-seeking behavior reflected complex interactions between spiritual, traditional, and biomedical systems.

articles.headline: Correction: Survey on knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) of malaria prevention and control among Chinese expatriates in South Sudan
articles.title_original: Correction: Survey on knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) of malaria prevention and control among Chinese expatriates in South Sudan.
articles.authors: Su Q, Yu Y, Chen H, Gao Y, Li J
articles.details: Correction: Survey on knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) of malaria prevention and control among Chinese expatriates in South Sudan

articles.headline: CRCT sample size guidance for evaluating vector suppression interventions
articles.title_original: Requirements for designing cluster randomised control trials to detect suppression of malaria vector population densities.
articles.authors: Hancock PA, Hui TJ, Epopa PS, Milogo A, McKemey AR, Yao FA, Diabaté A, Burt A
articles.details: Statistical simulation study informed by 5-year vector density time series from four villages in western Burkina Faso provided cluster randomized trial (CRCT) design guidance for detecting mosquito population suppression. For 50% suppression effect, 21–22 clusters needed when targeting all Anopheles gambiae complex species (90% power), 24–26 for An. coluzzii alone, and 60–66 for An. gambiae alone. For 90% suppression effect, 9–10 clusters sufficient regardless of target species. Sample size varied with monitoring intensity and sampling frequency.

articles.headline: Severe malaria recrudescence despite guideline-based artemisinin treatment
articles.title_original: Recrudescent severe malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum following treatment in a Taiwanese patient: a case report.
articles.authors: Tseng YC, Cheng MY, Lin PB, Hsu SF, Hsu JJ, Shen YC, Ho MW, Hsiao CT, Hsueh PR
articles.details: Case report of a 66-year-old Taiwanese traveler presenting with severe P. falciparum malaria (parasitemia 20%) and multi-organ failure (acute kidney injury, liver failure, thrombocytopenia, respiratory distress) following return from Central Africa. Initial IV artesunate and oral artemether-lumefantrine achieved parasite clearance but recrudescence occurred 4 weeks later; revised treatment with artesunate-doxycycline-clindamycin followed by artemether-lumefantrine achieved clearance within 2 weeks without pfk13 mutations.

articles.headline: Healthcare workers lack knowledge about R21 malaria vaccine prior to rollout
articles.title_original: General knowledge and attitude of healthcare professionals and infrastructural readiness prior to implementation of malaria vaccine in selected hospitals in Tororo district, Uganda: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Asio L, Besigye I, Puleh SS, Nazziwa N, Ocan M
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 211 healthcare professionals at two hospitals in Tororo district, Uganda (prior to R21 vaccine rollout April/May 2025) found 70% (147/211, 95% CI 63–76) held unfavorable attitudes toward malaria vaccine with concerns about affordability, accessibility, and effectiveness (mean attitude score 3.30, SD 0.489). Knowledge was very low: 99% (208/211, 95% CI 96–100) demonstrated poor knowledge and only 1% (3/211, 95% CI 0.5–4.4) good knowledge. Limited infrastructure preparedness documented for vaccine implementation.

articles.headline: 30% of Ghanaian herbal antimalarials showed potent P. falciparum activity
articles.title_original: The efficacy of selected Ghanaian herbal antimalarials against laboratory strains and clinical isolates of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Yeboah SN, Ansong M, Clotworthy D, Domie PE, Chirawurah JD, Awiaga CK, Mensah C, Sakyi ME, Adika E, Morang'a CM, Awandare GA, Aniweh Y, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: Twenty commercial herbal formulations were tested in vitro against five laboratory and two clinical P. falciparum strains. Six formulations (30%) demonstrated potent activity with IC50 values below 50 μg/ml, with three showing activity at the trophozoite stage and others at varied intraerythrocytic stages. Most herbal preparations showed poor anti-plasmodial activity, highlighting the need for improved regulatory testing.

articles.headline: Opsonic phagocytosis levels increase clinical malaria risk in older children
articles.title_original: A Novel Phagocytosis Assay Reveals the Association between Elevated Opsonic Phagocytosis Levels and an Increased Risk of Clinical Malaria in a Low-Malaria Transmission Area.
articles.authors: Odhiambo EO, Mellencamp KA, Holla P, Ayodo G, John CC
articles.details: A novel opsonic phagocytosis (OP) assay was applied to 634 individuals (317 cases, 317 controls, 1:1 matched by age/village) from a low-transmission Kenyan area. OP responses were rare before age 15 but associated with increased malaria risk in 5-14-year-olds (aOR 3.32, 95% CI 1.13–9.77) and ≥15-year-olds (aOR 4.85, 95% CI 1.02–23.00), suggesting OP marks exposure rather than protection in low-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Parasites optimizing transmission by sensing two within-host cues
articles.title_original: Malaria parasites can optimize transmission investment by sensing two within-host cues.
articles.authors: Wang A, Greischar MA, Mideo N
articles.details: Theoretical modeling investigated what environmental cues Plasmodium chabaudi parasites should sense to maximize transmission investment during infection. Sensing two cues—log-transformed infected and uninfected red blood cell abundance—enabled parasites to accurately track infection progression, achieve terminal investment, and recover fitness of optimal time-varying strategy, though this increased virulence and reduced robustness to environmental fluctuations.

articles.headline: Oxidative stress mechanisms in malaria and typhoid fever
articles.title_original: Oxidative stress in malaria and typhoid fever: A scoping review of the pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic implications.
articles.authors: Netongo PM, Eric MB, Kamdem SD, Tchoutang AM, Mbacham WF
articles.details: A scoping review examined oxidative stress in malaria and typhoid fever, including co-infections, based on studies from January 2000 to May 2024. Both diseases elevate reactive oxygen species production; endogenous antioxidant responses are activated but often insufficient to neutralize ROS. Antimalarial and antibacterial drugs influence oxidative-antioxidant dynamics, suggesting adjunctive therapies targeting this balance may improve treatment.

articles.headline: Anopheles fluviatilis misidentification masks actual vector species in Northeast India
articles.title_original: Diagnostic puzzle of Anopheles species using morphological vs. molecular approach: a case study in Tripura, a hyperendemic malaria state, Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sihag AK, Rajkonwar J, Gogoi P, Vezhavendan S, Chellappan S, Satpute SS, Malakar P, Debnath S, Baruah K, Kaur H, Subbarao SK, Bhattacharyya DR, Bhowmick IP
articles.details: During a 2019-2024 longitudinal entomological study in Tripura, morphological methods identified nine An. fluviatilis and 500+ An. minimus, but molecular sequencing confirmed the morphologically identified An. fluviatilis were actually An. minimus with three new haplotypes, likely due to hypermelanization obscuring key diagnostic features. Correct species identification is crucial for appropriate vector control policies.

articles.headline: Permethrin-treated baby wraps reduce childhood malaria incidence by 66%
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria.
articles.authors: Boyce RM, Shook-Sa BE, Ndizeye R, Baguma E, Giandomenico D, Cassidy CA, Eshun S, Siedner MJ, Staedke SG, Ntaro M, Juliano JJ, Reyes R, Mulogo EM
articles.details: In a double-blind RCT of 400 mother-child pairs in Uganda (children 6-18 months, 24-week follow-up), permethrin-treated cloth wraps retreated every 4 weeks plus pyrethroid ITNs reduced clinical malaria incidence to 0.73 per 100 person-weeks versus 2.14 in controls receiving sham-treated wraps plus ITNs (IRR 0.34, 95% CI 0.23–0.51, p<0.001). Rash occurred more often in the intervention group (8.5% vs 6.0%).

articles.headline: Nonparametric temporal models for recurrent malaria infection in vaccine trials
articles.title_original: Generalized nonparametric temporal modeling of recurrent events with application to a malaria vaccine trial.
articles.authors: Heng F, Sun Y, Xu J, Gilbert PB
articles.details: A generalized nonparametric temporal model was developed for analyzing intensity processes with multiple time scales, applicable to recurrent-event data from vaccine trials. The method was applied to MAL-094/MAL-095 malaria vaccine trial data to investigate how infection risk changes over time and how prior malaria infection or vaccination modifies future infection risk, providing new insights into vaccine protective effects.

articles.headline: Sustained low SP-resistance mutations support continued PMC in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Molecular Markers of Resistance to Antimalarial Drugs Three Years After Perennial Malaria Chemoprevention in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Chen H, Owusu-Kyei K, Fombah AE, Williams J, García-Fernández C, Rovira-Vallbona E, Bofill A, Quintó L, Figueroa-Romero A, Mac-Abdul F, Samai M, Mayor A, Menéndez C
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 300 P. falciparum-positive children aged 2-5 years in Sierra Leone (June-August 2021) assessed resistance markers after perennial malaria chemoprevention. Pfdhfr triple mutant prevalence was 99.5%, while pfdhfr/dhps quintuple mutant prevalence was only 4.6%, with no sextuple mutants or validated artemisinin-resistance pfK13 markers detected. The sustained low quintuple-mutant prevalence supports continued use of SP for IPTp and PMC expansion.

articles.headline: Genetic diversity of circumsporozoite protein in Vietnam P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of circumsporozoite surface protein in Vietnam Plasmodium falciparum isolates.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Lê HG, Kang JM, Trinh NTM, Van Khanh C, Cho M, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Ninety-five pfcsp gene sequences were obtained from P. falciparum-infected individuals in five endemic provinces in Central Vietnam (2018-2022) and compared to 836 global sequences. Both N- and C-terminal non-repeating regions showed limited genetic diversity, though notable polymorphisms including A98G and a 19-amino-acid insertion occurred in the N-terminal region. The central repeat region displayed high polymorphism due to varying peptide repeat motifs.

articles.headline: Complete mitochondrial genome of Plasmodium pitheci from Bornean orang-utans
articles.title_original: The mitochondrial genome sequence of the Bornean orang-utan (Pongo pygmaeus) malaria parasite, Plasmodium pitheci.
articles.authors: Dharmayanthi AB, Pratama R, Sánchez KL, Nurillah A, Fadiah F, Komara, Nugraha RTP, Saputra S, Nurkanto A, Fahroni A, Irwanto A, Syafruddin D, Adnan IA, Saragih JR, Coutrier FN, Setiadi W, Lobo NF
articles.details: The complete 5,871 bp mitochondrial genome sequence of Plasmodium pitheci, a malaria parasite of Bornean orang-utans, was determined and analyzed via Bayesian phylogenetics. The sequence confirms that previously identified Plasmodium sp. VM, VS, and Pongo clades A and B correspond to P. pitheci, providing insights into geographic distribution and evolutionary relationships with other non-human primate Plasmodium species and implications for zoonotic malaria surveillance.

articles.headline: Green-synthesized MgO nanoparticles show antiplasmodial activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Antimalarial potential of Matricaria chamomilla-derived MgO nanoparticles against Plasmodium falciparum strains: an experimental study.
articles.authors: Farzaneh Z, Hanifian H, Nateghpour M, Hasanpour G, Raeisi A, Shabani M, Farivar L, Khezri A, Dehdast SA, Shahsavari S
articles.details: Green-synthesized magnesium oxide nanoparticles (30-80 nm) derived from Matricaria chamomilla were evaluated in vitro against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) and -resistant (K1) P. falciparum strains. IC50 values were 0.19-0.22 mg/mL against 3D7 and 0.41-0.45 mg/mL against K1 strains depending on solvent ratio used in synthesis. Results support further investigation of MgO nanoparticles as potential antimalarial agents.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy test positivity declining in Ghana's Savannah Region
articles.title_original: Trend of malaria test positivity rate among pregnant women, Savannah Region, Ghana, 2018-2022.
articles.authors: Abesig WJ, Nindow JAC, Bagonluri AC, Sarfo AK, Issahaku GR, Odikro MA, Akowuah G, Bandoh DA, Kenu E, Kubio C
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of malaria in pregnancy (MiP) data from Ghana's Savannah Region (2018-2022) found an overall testing rate of 90.8% with 54.5% test positivity. The MiP test positivity rate declined from 60.4% in 2018 to 51.6% in 2022, though Bole District remained highest at 70.1% with 40.5% of all regional MiP cases. Pregnant women under 20 years had the highest test positivity rate at 59.3%.

articles.headline: Microbes may mediate rhythmic parasite-mosquito transmission dynamics
articles.title_original: Do microbes mediate rhythmic malaria parasite-mosquito vector interactions?
articles.authors: Riithi N, Mooney JP, Reece SE
articles.details: This integrative review proposes that mosquito gut microbiota mediate rhythmicity in vector susceptibility and parasite development through modulation of immunity and metabolism and direct parasite interactions. Given changes in mosquito biting behaviors, understanding how time-of-day transmission timing influences malaria spread is proposed as urgent for transmission control. No empirical results are presented.

articles.headline: Droplet digital PCR detects asymptomatic malaria in Thailand and Sudan
articles.title_original: High-sensitivity detection of asymptomatic malaria in high-risk Thai populations using droplet digital PCR.
articles.authors: Kramyoo M, Boonpeng K, Janchoo C, Siriwattanakul K, Phankhod K, Vongthanom N, Kritprayoch P, Yokanit W, Srisutham S
articles.details: Researchers compared droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) with microscopy for detecting Plasmodium infections in 403 individuals from endemic Thailand regions and 270 Thai workers returning from Sudan. ddPCR detected malaria in 0.25% of Tak samples and 7.41% of Sudan samples, with superior sensitivity for low-parasitemia infections compared to microscopy (which detected only 2.59% P. falciparum in Sudan samples). ddPCR is proposed as a valuable tool for surveillance of asymptomatic parasite reservoirs.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle artesunate improves parasite clearance in mouse malaria
articles.title_original: Effects of artesunate-loaded nanoparticles on Plasmodium berghei treatment in a mouse model.
articles.authors: Wen H, Li Y, Luo J, Zhu W, Hide G, Feng X, Lun ZR, Lai DH
articles.details: Researchers evaluated artesunate, dihydroartemisinin, and artemisinin encapsulated in zinc-based metal-organic framework nanoparticles against P. berghei in a heavy-parasite-load mouse model. Three injections of 16 mg/kg artesunate nanoparticles over 24 hours significantly improved parasite clearance and prevented hyperparasitaemia (≥5%), whereas the same dose over 12-hour intervals only delayed it. A 4-injection regime showed 10 mg/kg dihydroartemisinin and artemisinin nanoparticles demonstrated superior clearance versus non-encapsulated controls.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance and biting patterns sustain malaria in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Residual malaria transmission in Western Burkina Faso: Vector Behavior, insecticide resistance, and the efficacy limits of next-generation LLINs.
articles.authors: Kouadio KWU, Amara MF, Soma DD, Dabiré RK, Diabaté A, Gnankiné O, Namountougou M
articles.details: An entomological surveillance in two western Burkina Faso districts (August-October 2023) documented seven Anopheles species, with An. gambiae s.l. complex predominant (An. coluzzii and An. arabiensis) showing early and late biting behaviors. Entomological inoculation rates were 0.875, 0.437, and 0.063 infectious bites/person/month in August, September, and October, respectively. Kdr-west and kdr-east resistance mutations were detected across gambiae complex members. Despite widespread LLINs and IRS, substantial residual transmission persisted.

articles.headline: Novel triazole compounds show potent activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: 1,2,4-Triazole amides: Design, synthesis, characterisation, in vitro and in silico evaluation against blood stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Tom AA, Rajendran V, Thottasseri AA, Gopan G, Mani M, Kannan T
articles.details: Researchers synthesized and evaluated 30 triazole amide derivatives against P. falciparum 3D7 in vitro using SYBR Green assay. Meta-substituted derivatives showed strongest activity, with compounds 17 (3,5-dimethyl), 11 (3-Br), and 7 (3-Cl) displaying IC50 values of 0.87, 1.27, and 2.17 μg/mL respectively against wild-type, and 1.55-5.70 μg/mL against chloroquine-resistant INDO strain. These compounds demonstrated no hemolytic or cytotoxic effects and showed favorable docking with Pf-dihydrofolate reductase.

articles.headline: Anti-CSP antibodies protect against Plasmodium sporozoites via tissue mechanisms
articles.title_original: Tissue-dependent protection mechanisms of antibodies targeting Plasmodium sporozoites.
articles.authors: Aguirre-Botero MC, Amino R
articles.details: This review explores how antibodies targeting the circumsporozoite protein (CSP) of Plasmodium sporozoites confer protection against infection. Anti-CSP antibodies binding the central repetitive region form the foundation of CSP-based vaccine protection documented in both animal models and humans. Understanding tissue-specific protection mechanisms is identified as critical for improving malaria vaccines and antibody-based prophylaxis, though complete mechanisms remain incompletely characterized.

articles.headline: Kelch13 mutations emerge in East Africa, resistance markers widespread
articles.title_original: Mapping Plasmodium falciparum mutations in Africa: A critical review of emerging drug resistance and implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Kagame J, Iradukunda PG, Siddig EE, Tessema SK, Tegegne MA, Mazaba ML, Fallah M, Ngamije D, Harelimana JD, Muvunyi CM
articles.details: A critical review of P. falciparum genetic diversity and drug resistance in Africa (2019-2024) documented validated kelch13 mutations mainly in East Africa—Eritrea (R622I, 68%) and Uganda (R561H, 52%)—with emerging mutations in Rwanda (C469F, 36%), Uganda (C469F 59%, P441L 69%), and Tanzania (P441L 20%). Resistance markers (Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps) were prevalent in eastern (30-38%) and western Africa (44-55%). Contributing factors included drug pressure (23-35%), drug misuse (10-12.5%), and cross-border population movements (10%).

articles.headline: New malaria vector Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis detected in Venezuela
articles.title_original: First detection of Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis (Diptera: Culicidae) in southern Venezuela and its infection with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Conn JE, Bickersmith SA, Moreno JE, Sallum MAM, Sanchez V, Moncada N, Grillet ME
articles.details: Researchers identified 88 specimens of Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis (previously known only from Brazil) in southern Venezuela using COI barcode sequencing. One mosquito was found infected with P. falciparum (1.14% infection rate) by real-time PCR, detected across multiple seasons in peridomestic and forest fringe habitats with anthropophilic and early/late evening biting behaviors. This detection suggests the species has been misidentified elsewhere and may represent an underappreciated malaria vector in the Neotropics.

articles.headline: Captopril reduces liver damage in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Captopril reduces liver cytotoxicity in a murine model of Plasmodium chabaudi infection.
articles.authors: Ventura PDS, de Souza DV, Aguiar ACC, Carvalho CPF, Ribeiro DA, Nagaoka MR, Gazarini ML
articles.details: Researchers conducted histopathological analysis of livers from mice infected with P. chabaudi and treated with chloroquine, captopril (ACE inhibitor), or losartan (AT1 antagonist). Chloroquine alone or combined with vasodilators reduced histopathological changes including sinusoid congestion, leukocyte infiltration, hemozoin deposition, and portal inflammation. Captopril treatment notably reduced cytotoxicity and improved hepatic alterations, suggesting vasodilators may have protective effects against malaria-induced liver damage.

articles.headline: Routine health data can monitor malaria intervention effectiveness
articles.title_original: Use of Routine Health Data to Monitor Malaria Intervention Effectiveness: A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Reithinger R, Bisanzio D, Cushnie A, Craig J
articles.details: A scoping review analyzed 49 studies (from 957 PubMed records) published in the past decade examining malaria intervention effectiveness using routine health management information systems data. Studies varied across publication year, geography, outcome measures, target populations, interventions, HMIS platforms, and analytical approaches (modelling and non-modelling methods). Routine HMIS data can serve as a valuable complement or alternative to surveys and mathematical modeling for regularly assessing whether malaria interventions remain effective and help countries progress toward national strategic goals.

articles.headline: Primaquine accelerates gametocyte clearance with artemether-lumefantrine
articles.title_original: Efficacy and gametocyte carriage in Plasmodium falciparum cases treated with artemether-lumefantrine alone versus with single-dose primaquine in Ethiopia: A randomized controlled study.
articles.authors: Kumsa T, Jima B, Tamiru G, Getu R, Melaku M, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: A randomized controlled trial in Ethiopia compared artemether-lumefantrine (AL) alone versus with single-dose primaquine (PQ) in 106 symptomatic P. falciparum cases over 28 days. By microscopy, both groups cleared asexual parasites by day 2; by day 7 PCR, 5.8% (3/52) of AL+PQ versus 18.5% (10/54) of AL-only cases showed parasite DNA (P=0.09). PCR-uncorrected cure rates were 100% (AL) and 98.1% (AL+PQ); recrudescence was 2.8%. Gametocyte clearance occurred by day 7 with AL+PQ versus day 14 with AL alone.

articles.headline: miRNA profiles differ between lethal and non-lethal malaria infections
articles.title_original: Differential expression of miRNAs dictates the susceptibility/resistance during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Kataria P, Chaudhary A, Thakur RS, Sharma I, Kalkal M, Chauhan R, Malla WA, Singh K, Das J
articles.details: A comparative miRNA expression analysis of splenocytes from mice infected with lethal (Py17XL) versus non-lethal (Py17XNL) P. yoelii strains (day 13 post-infection) identified 171 and 220 differentially expressed miRNAs versus uninfected controls, with 79 commonly dysregulated and 53-86 strain-specific miRNAs. Lethal infection elevated miR-6992-5p, miR-704, miR-7676-3p, and miR-1933-3p, while non-lethal infection upregulated miR-875-3p, miR-30a-3p, miR-499-5p, and miR-212-3p. Pathway enrichment indicated these miRNAs target immune genes (MAPK, mTOR, FoxO, Th-17, TGF-β signaling).

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers in SMC regimens, Mali 2019-2020
articles.title_original: Frequencies of molecular markers of drug resistance in the context of two different Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) treatment regimens in the Koulikoro health district, Mali.
articles.authors: Traore B, Kane F, Toure M, Jørgensen MHM, Sanogo D, Keita S, Keita M, Sogoba N, Shaw-Saliba K, Diakite M, Shaffer JG, Alifrangis M, Hansson H, Doumbia S
articles.details: Study analyzed 564 P. falciparum samples (218 in 2019, 346 in 2020) from Mali using NGS and qPCR to track resistance markers under two SMC treatment regimens (SP + AQ vs DHA + PQ). Pfdhfr 51I-59R-108N haplotype was highly prevalent (>93% both years); Pfdhps single mutant 437G represented ~60% in both arms. In 2020, combined Pfdhfr/Pfdhps haplotypes (IRN/IAGKAS and IRN/VAGKGS) were detected at 9.6% and 1.2% respectively. Pfcrt wild-type increased significantly from 39% (2019) to 52% (2020, P=0.01). Authors highlight emerging resistance markers including IRNI/IAGKAS quintuple mutant and Pfdhps-431V and PfK13-Y493H variants, underscoring need for continued surveillance.

articles.headline: RSL3 synergizes with pyrimethamine to inhibit P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Synergistic activity of RSL3 and Pyrimethamine to inhibit the proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nikolova V, Linnemannstöns K, Zahoruiko A, Ganter M, Lüder CG, Dobbelstein M
articles.details: In vitro study demonstrated synergistic activity between RSL3 (ferroptosis inducer) and pyrimethamine (DHFR inhibitor) against P. falciparum in red blood cells. Similar synergy observed with cycloguanil but not other DHFR inhibitors. Ferrostatin-1 antagonist did not rescue parasite growth, suggesting mechanism distinct from canonical ferroptosis. Authors propose RSL3 and related compounds as promising adjuvants to enhance antimalarial efficacy of pyrimethamine and potentially overcome drug resistance.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Africa: confronting challenges
articles.title_original: Malaria resurgence in Africa: confronting the challenges.
articles.authors: Ntoumi F, Aklillu E, Asogun D, Ansumana R, Mfinanga S, Yeboah-Manu D, Nachega JB, Zumla A
articles.details: Title only; no abstract provided.

articles.headline: Malaria hotspots in North East India via geospatial analysis
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria vulnerable zones in North East Region of India: Spatiotemporal assessment through geographical information system and self-organizing maps.
articles.authors: Mopuri R, Kadiri MR, Bhimala KR, Mutheneni SR
articles.details: Spatiotemporal analysis of reported malaria cases 2011-2019 across North East Region (NER) of India using geospatial techniques (Local Moran's I, Getis-Ord Gi* statistics, Self-Organizing Maps, GIS). Overall malaria incidence decreased consistently 2011-2019. Results revealed significant and persistent malaria hotspots predominantly in Meghalaya, Mizoram, and Tripura. Authors conclude targeted control interventions in these areas are critical for achieving elimination goals.

articles.headline: GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis drives cerebral malaria neuroinflammation
articles.title_original: GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis promotes the progression of neuroinflammation in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Wang J, Zhu Q, Tong G, Yu K, Liang J, Huang Y, Wang Y, Yang C, Li T, Liang W, Li G, Liu X, Sun J, Li Y, Shen Y, Zhao Y
articles.details: Experimental study in murine cerebral malaria (CM) model showed astrocytes exhibit robust IFN-γ response during CM, facilitating CD8+ T cell recruitment and antigen presentation. Key finding: brain-infiltrated CD8+ T cells trigger GSDME-dependent pyroptosis in astrocytes, amplifying neuroinflammation and exacerbating neuronal injury. Genetic ablation of Gsdme or pharmacological GSDME inhibition by mannose significantly attenuated brain inflammation and damage. Authors identify GSDME as novel therapeutic target for mitigating CM.

articles.headline: WHO-based standard operating protocol for malaria management
articles.title_original: Standard operating protocol for malaria management.
articles.authors: Pannu AK
articles.details: Evidence-based clinical guideline emphasizing early diagnosis via parasitological confirmation (peripheral smear or rapid diagnostic test) before treatment, except in suspected severe cases. Mainstay treatment: oral artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated malaria; intravenous artesunate for severe cases including pregnant women (all trimesters) and children. Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase testing not required for single-dose gametocytocidal primaquine but essential prior to radical cure with primaquine or tafenoquine.

articles.headline: Antiplasmodial activity of Wrightia species from Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Phytochemical analysis and evaluation of antiplasmodial and cytotoxic activities of Wrightia dubia and Wrightia pubescens traditionally used for malaria treatment in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Abd Karim HA, Ilmi H, Widyawaruyanti A, Ismail NH, Zohdi RM, Osman CP
articles.details: Phytochemical analysis of two plant species traditionally used for malaria in Southeast Asia. Of ten plant extracts screened, three dichloromethane extracts (W. dubia wood, W. dubia twig, W. pubescens twig) showed significant antiplasmodial activity with IC50 values 1.89 ± 0.06 μg/mL, 6.38 ± 0.04 μg/mL, and 1.79 ± 0.03 μg/mL respectively. LC-MS identified 38 compounds; isolated compound tryptanthrin demonstrated activity against P. falciparum 3D7 and Dd2 strains (IC50 2.50 ± 0.05 μM and 3.14 ± 0.023 μM). All active extracts and compounds were non-toxic (CC50 > 10 μM) and non-hemolytic. Authors recommend further in vivo evaluation of tryptanthrin as antimalarial drug candidate.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in German ED: 24 patients, severe and uncomplicated
articles.title_original: The role of emergency departments in the treatment of imported severe and uncomplicated malaria - a retrospective monocentric observational study in a German university hospital.
articles.authors: Wörnle M
articles.details: Retrospective monocentric study at LMU University Hospital Munich ED (Jan 2019–Dec 2023) of 24 adult patients with imported malaria: 22 with P. falciparum, 1 with P. ovale, 1 with P. vivax. 19 patients had uncomplicated malaria; 5 developed severe malaria. 20 patients required inpatient treatment (15 uncomplicated, 5 severe). No fatalities during hospital stay. Authors conclude EDs play crucial role in malaria diagnosis and management in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: CD47 regulates NK cell recruitment in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: CD47-mediated selective accumulation of mature Ly49h+ NK cells to the brain associates with the pathogenesis of cerebral malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Oakley MS, Malla P, Hayashi CTH, Majam V, Chorazeczewski JK, Okoth WA, Meredith S, KuKuruga M, Akue A, Rotstein DS, Kumar S
articles.details: Study compared P. berghei ANKA (lethal murine malaria) infection in wild-type vs CD47-knockout C57BL/6 mice. Absence of CD47 conferred partial but significant resistance to experimental cerebral malaria (ECM, log-rank P < 0.001), associated with diminished brain sequestration of NK cells, CD4+ T cells, and CD8+ T cells, and lower circulating interleukin-10 and MIP1β. Critical finding: CD47 mediates maturation and activation of brain-sequestered Ly49h+ NK cells (an activation receptor). Authors establish CD47 as previously undefined regulator of NK cell-mediated ECM pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Brain injury pathogenesis in pediatric cerebral malaria: mechanisms review
articles.title_original: Pathogenesis of brain injury in cerebral malaria: emerging mechanisms and implications for intervention.
articles.authors: Datta D, John CC
articles.details: Literature review of recent advances in pediatric cerebral malaria (CM) brain injury mechanisms. Key findings: blood-brain barrier (BBB) impairment occurs via local hypoxemia, ischemia, and endothelial activation after sequestration of infected erythrocytes; free heme release induces oxidative stress. TNF-α, reactive oxygen species, CD8+ T cells, and parasite toxins cross impaired BBB to activate CNS immune responses and cause direct neuronal cytotoxicity. Acute kidney injury and hyperuricemia may worsen BBB impairment and cerebral edema. Authors note cytotoxic/vasogenic edema, abnormal blood flow, and increased microvascular hemoglobin are present but their relationship to brain injury not yet fully defined.

articles.headline: p-Cymene + quinine combination achieves 100% survival in malaria mice
articles.title_original: Therapeutic Effects of Isopropyltoluene (p-Cymene) Alone and in Combination with Quinine Against Malaria Infection Through Modulation of Inflammation and Oxidative Stress.
articles.authors: Shater AF
articles.details: Experimental study in 108 BALB/c mice infected with 1 × 10⁶ P. berghei-infected erythrocytes, treated orally for 4 days. Combination of p-cymene (CM, 10 mg/kg) + quinine (Qu, 10 mg/kg) achieved 100% survival rate and 100% parasite growth suppression (p < 0.001). Combination reduced tissue malondialdehyde and nitric oxide, upregulated superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase (>fourfold), upregulated IL-10 (>fourfold), and downregulated TNF-α (<1.3-fold) and IL-1β (<1.4-fold). Authors conclude combination therapy modulates oxidative stress and inflammation but note clinical trials needed to validate toxicity and efficacy.

articles.headline: Malaria-on-chip predicts in vivo pharmacokinetics for drug development
articles.title_original: Translation of a Human-Based Malaria-on-a-Chip Phenotypic Disease Model for In Vivo Applications.
articles.authors: Rupar MJ, Hanson HM, Botlick BL, Sriram N, Rogers S, Zuniga J, Liu Z, Trimmer SJ, Ciurca JM, Long CJ, McAleer CW, Schmidt S, Favuzza P, Lowe P, Gobeau N, Hickman JJ
articles.details: Organ-on-chip model incorporating human liver, spleen, and endothelium with P. falciparum-infected blood, maintained 7 days in serum-free medium, supporting all intraerythrocytic life stages. Chloroquine, lumefantrine, or artesunate delivered as monotherapies to 3D7 or W2-infected systems showed dose-dependent parasite clearance in both strains. In 3D7 model, chloroquine and lumefantrine caused recrudescence; artesunate did not. In W2 model, chloroquine and lumefantrine stabilized parasitemia by day 7; artesunate further reduced parasitemia. Population dynamics PK/PD modeling predicted human in vivo parameters for efficacy and off-target toxicity from in vitro results.

articles.headline: Quercetin inhibits Plasmodium falciparum heat shock proteins
articles.title_original: Comparative Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum Small Heat Shock Proteins and Their Inhibition by Quercetin (3,3',4',5,7-Pentahydroxyflavone).
articles.authors: Timothy FM, Zininga T
articles.details: P. falciparum encodes three small heat shock proteins (PfHsp20a, b, c) with conserved α-crystallin domains. Quercetin disrupted structure and function in concentration-dependent manner, with IC50 values of 5.4 μM (Nf54 strain) and 7.8 μM (Dd2 strain) for growth inhibition in vitro. PfHsp20c was most sensitive to quercetin inhibition. Authors propose small heat shock proteins as potential novel antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: Exosomes in malaria pathogenesis and therapeutic targeting
articles.title_original: Extracellular vesicles in malaria pathogenesis and Therapy: Emerging insights and future perspectives.
articles.authors: Abida, Kamal M, Alqurashi AA, Alhuthli SM, Alharbi MA, Alghamdi BJ, Alsharari SH, Khobrani MA, Almakrami JA, Albishri JO, Alshammari AK, Imran M
articles.details: This review discusses the pivotal role of exosomes (small extracellular vesicles) in malaria pathogenesis and immune modulation. Exosomes carry bioactive molecules influencing innate and adaptive immunity and mediate complex host-pathogen interactions. Authors discuss exosome biogenesis, molecular cargo, functional roles in disease progression, and potential as therapeutic targets and biomarkers, emphasizing importance of 3D cell culture systems and in vivo models.

articles.headline: Creating a specific definition of severe malaria in travelers
articles.title_original: Severe malaria in travellers-time to create a specific definition.
articles.authors: Barkati S, Libman M, Schwartz E
articles.details: This article addresses the need to establish a more precise clinical definition of severe malaria among international travelers, noting the lack of currently standardized diagnostic criteria.

articles.headline: Omics approaches reveal insecticide resistance mechanisms in African vectors
articles.title_original: Impact of Omics Studies on Understanding Insecticide Resistance Mechanisms in Sub-Saharan Malaria Vectors: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Orogun YG, Ogunlana OO, Taiwo DI, Fadare OA
articles.details: This systematic review analyzed 22 studies (published 2016-2025) using omics approaches (genomics, transcriptomics, multi-omics) to understand insecticide resistance in Anopheles vectors in sub-Saharan Africa. Key resistance mechanisms included metabolic resistance, target-site mutations, and cuticular changes. Multi-omics studies revealed novel markers: CYP450 reductase, UDP-glycosyltransferases, and salivary gland proteins. Authors conclude omics has enhanced understanding of vector resistance, providing insights for molecular diagnostics and region-specific control strategies.

articles.headline: Apicoplast fission protein identified as antimalarial drug target
articles.title_original: An Essential Adaptor for Apicoplast Fission and Inheritance in Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Blauwkamp J, Rajaram K, Staggers SR, Harrigan O, Doud EH, Xu W, Ke H, Prigge ST, Sun SY, Absalon S
articles.details: This study identifies PfAnchor (Pf3D7_0613600) as an essential adaptor protein regulating P. falciparum apicoplast fission. Using Ultrastructure Expansion Microscopy, conditional depletion of PfAnchor disrupted apicoplast fission, causing incomplete cytokinesis and parasite death. The protein interacts with dynamin-like GTPase PfDyn2, establishing PfAnchor as the first apicoplast-specific dynamin adaptor protein and potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Dengue may trigger Plasmodium vivax relapse in travelers
articles.title_original: A Case of Intercurrent Dengue and Probable Relapsing Plasmodium vivax Malaria in a Returned Traveler to India: Case Report and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Kavanoor Sridhar K, Buskandar F, Dhaliwal M, Calloo GV, Boggild AK
articles.details: This case report describes a returned traveler with concurrent dengue and P. vivax infection. Clinical and epidemiological features suggest dengue may have triggered relapse of P. vivax malaria. Authors review literature on co-occurrence of dengue and malaria in travelers and discuss evidence for dengue as a precipitant of relapsing malaria.

articles.headline: Antenatal care surveillance reliably monitors malaria prevalence in Africa
articles.title_original: Antenatal care surveillance for monitoring malaria prevalence and intervention coverage: a multicountry analysis.
articles.authors: Munsey A, Digre P, Hicks J, Wagman J, Robertson M, Alao M, Ogouyemi Hounto A, Gansane A, Debe S, Candrinho B, Uhomoibhi P, Okoko OO, Lemwayi R, Aron S, Kangale CC, Kabamba BM, Miller J, Walker P, Gutman J
