articles.details: This multicountry analysis in six sub-Saharan countries (Benin, Burkina Faso, Mozambique, Nigeria, Tanzania, Zambia) compared malaria prevalence (RDT) among pregnant women at first antenatal care (ANC1) visits versus children <5 from household surveys. District-level ANC1 prevalence and child prevalence strongly correlated (Spearman r=0.79, 95% CI 0.65-0.88, p<0.001), with ANC1 data predictive of child prevalence (LOOCV mean absolute error 6.5%). Authors conclude ANC1 surveillance provides useful monitoring of malaria prevalence and prevention intervention coverage.

articles.headline: Mosquito nutrition alters biting time, impacting malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Biting time of day in malaria mosquitoes is modulated by nutritional status.
articles.authors: Oke CE, Rund SSC, Machani MG, Sabtiu ARM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Afrane YA, Reece SE
articles.details: This semi-field study with Anopheles gambiae s.l. evaluated how nutritional status affects biting behavior. Mosquitoes with low nutritional resources (0.5% sucrose) were 2-3 fold more likely to host-seek overall and 5-10 fold more likely to seek hosts at earlier times compared to well-nourished mosquitoes (10% sucrose ± additional blood meal). Well-resourced mosquitoes predominantly sought hosts in the second half of night. Authors conclude mosquito nutrition drives plasticity in biting time, contributing to residual malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Low pfhrp2 deletions support RDT performance in Angola
articles.title_original: Analysis of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions, and the structure and variability of PfHRP2 and PfHRP3 proteins: implications for the performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Cubal, Angola.
articles.authors: Mediavilla A, Molina-de la Fuente I, Martínez-Vallejo P, Francisco K, Martins JF, Febrer-Sendra B, Maturana CR, Silgado A, Nindia A, Goterris L, Martínez-Campreciós J, Oliveira-Souto I, Molina I, Berzosa P, Sulleiro E
articles.details: This study of P. falciparum isolates from Cubal, Angola (May-July 2022) found deletions in 7.29% (pfhrp2 exon 1-2) and 11.46% (pfhrp3 exon 1-2), with no double deletions. Epitopes 3A4 and C1-13 were present in all samples at high frequency (15 and 18 times per isolate respectively). Protein analysis revealed significant variability in histidine repeats between samples. Authors conclude low deletion prevalence supports PfHRP2-RDT suitability for diagnosis, though continued monitoring remains essential.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence increased in rural Tanzania communities 2021-2023
articles.title_original: Temporal and spatial trends of the prevalence of infections caused by Plasmodium parasites among rural community members in three regions with varying transmission intensities in Mainland Tanzania.
articles.authors: Challe DP, Petro DA, Francis F, Seth MD, Madebe RA, Mandai SS, Budodo R, Kisambale AJ, Chacha GA, Moshi R, Mbwambo RB, Pereus D, Bakari C, Mbata D, Lyimo B, Kanyankole GK, Aaron S, Mbwambo D, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Kapologwe N, Mandara CI, Makene VW, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: This community survey of 8,166 individuals ≥6 years in three rural Tanzania regions (2021-2023) found Plasmodium prevalence of 23.2% (n=1,896), with significant yearly variation: 22.9% (2021), 20.6% (2022), 26.9% (2023; p<0.001). School children (5-<15 years) and males had higher prevalence (p<0.001). Prevalence among those with fever 48h prior increased from 40.1% (2021) to 58.6% (2023). Higher prevalence associated with no bed net use, traditional housing, and low socioeconomic status (p≤0.003).

articles.headline: High acceptance of shortened P. vivax radical cure in Cambodia
articles.title_original: Perceptions of shorter radical cure Plasmodium vivax treatment regimens and their implementation: a qualitative study among stakeholders in Cambodia.
articles.authors: Cassidy-Seyoum SA, Chheng K, Chanpheakdey P, Meershoek A, Hsiang MS, von Seidlein L, Adhikari B, Tripura R, Ley B, Price RN, Lek D, Engel N, Thriemer K
articles.details: This qualitative study of 142 stakeholders (policymakers, healthcare providers, patients) in Cambodia explored perceptions of 7-day high-dose primaquine and single-dose tafenoquine for P. vivax radical cure. Healthcare providers valued shorter regimens for reduced treatment burden and improved effectiveness. Patients valued faster recovery and reduced opportunity costs. Patients accepted increased adverse events if treatment duration was shorter; policymakers required confidence-building through evidence. Authors conclude acceptance was high in this pre-elimination setting with likely uptake if evidence generation and adverse-event monitoring occur.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 22.5% across sub-Saharan Africa with spatial clustering
articles.title_original: Malaria prevalence and its determinants across 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a spatial and geographically weighted regression analysis.
articles.authors: Yitageasu G, Worede EA, Alemu EA, Tigabie M, Birhanu A, Angelo AA, Aweke MN, Demoze L
articles.details: This cross-sectional analysis of Demographic and Health Survey data from 19 sub-Saharan African countries (126,424 households, 11,594 clusters) found overall malaria prevalence of 22.47% (95% CI 22.24%-22.70%). Significant spatial clustering was identified (Global Moran's I=0.159, Z=239.1, p<0.001), with 34 spatial clusters detected. Higher prevalence associated with rural residence, rudimentary housing, younger household heads, poverty, lack of bed nets, and larger household size (6-12 members). Lower risk in richest households and female-headed households.

articles.headline: Education, gender, and occupation predict malaria risk behaviors
articles.title_original: Estimating malaria risk behaviours and their determinants among at-risk populations in a pre-elimination setting, Sisaket Province, Thailand-Cambodia border.
articles.authors: Saeung M, Aung PL, Jupatanakul N, Manguin S, Chareonviriyaphap T, Phuanukoonnon S
articles.details: Survey of 402 at-risk individuals found 51.5% with good knowledge, 64.7% with good attitudes, and 58.2% with good practices regarding malaria. Poor attitudes were more likely in those with primary education (aOR=2.2, 95% CI 1.2-4.0) or low socioeconomic status (aOR=1.8, 95% CI 1.1-3.0). Risk behaviors were more frequent in males (aOR=2.8, 95% CI 1.2-6.8) and those with poor practices (aOR=3.5, 95% CI 1.6-8.1), suggesting targeted behavior change communication is needed.

articles.headline: Young, primigravid women vulnerable to malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Barriers to malaria prevention services in pregnancy: a multi-methods study in rural western Uganda.
articles.authors: Clubine Horowitz RA, Ngelese H, Baguma E, Nyangoma G, Shem B, Ntaro M, Mulogo EM, Boyce RM
articles.details: Screening of 1,644 pregnant women at first antenatal visits found 225 (13.5%) tested positive for P. falciparum, with primigravid status and younger maternal age associated with positivity. Qualitative interviews identified barriers to malaria prevention including delayed pregnancy testing, fear of chemoprevention, financial constraints, transportation issues, and low bed net use. Decentralizing antenatal services further into communities may improve prevention access.

articles.headline: AI algorithm reads malaria rapid diagnostic tests with 96.8% accuracy
articles.title_original: Evaluating the performance of an artificial intelligence-based electronic reader for malaria rapid diagnostic tests across Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria and Uganda.
articles.authors: Lindblade KA, Ngufor C, Yavo W, Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ssewante N, Akpiroroh E, Konaté-Toure A, Ahogni I, Kpemasse A, Tanoh AM, Ntadom G, Opigo J, Zobrist S, Griffith K, Humes M
articles.details: The HealthPulse smartphone application, using artificial intelligence computer vision, was evaluated against expert panel interpretation of 106,877 RDT images. The algorithm achieved 96.8% accuracy (95% CI 96.7%, 96.9%) and F1 score of 96.6 for positive and negative results, but lower performance for invalid (recall 84.8%) and uninterpretable images (recall 0.8%). Performance varied by RDT product, presence of faint lines, and image quality, suggesting further refinement is needed.

articles.headline: Community participation in mosquito surveillance without monetary incentives
articles.title_original: "Fighting against malaria is everyone's concern": a randomized control trial assessing the role of incentives for encouraging local communities to record and upload mosquito sounds using the MozzWear application.
articles.authors: Mponzi WP, Dam R, Msaky D, Mwalugelo Y, Sinka M, Kiskin I, Herreros-Moya E, Roberts S, Willis K, Kaindoa EW
articles.details: Randomized trial evaluated whether airtime incentives increased community participation in mosquito acoustic surveillance using HumBug sensors and MozzWear application over four months. Control group without incentives performed comparably to the incentive group in terms of nights recorded and data uploaded. Community members expressed strong intrinsic motivation to participate in malaria surveillance research, suggesting community engagement may not require financial incentives.

articles.headline: Urban irrigated farming harbors highest densities of malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Larval habitat diversity, physicochemical characteristics and their effect on the larval density of malaria vectors in the city of Accra, Ghana.
articles.authors: Sabtiu ARM, Hinne IA, Sraku IK, Halou DK, Doe RT, Attah SK, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
articles.details: Surveys of 727 potential mosquito habitats identified 475 (65.34%) positive for Anopheles larvae, with irrigated urban farming sites showing the highest An. gambiae s.l. abundance (27.24% of 22,919 larvae), especially during rainy season (77.01%). Anopheles coluzzii was the predominant vector species (54.4%, 368/677), and invasive Anopheles stephensi was detected. Polluted habitats had distinct physicochemical characteristics including higher ammonium, conductivity, and total dissolved solids.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing improves CYP2D6 genotyping for P. vivax treatment
articles.title_original: Enhancing CYP2D6 genotyping with nanopore sequencing to address allele diversity P. vivax malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Wongsurawat T, Jenjaroenpun P, Suwannasin K, Wankaew N, Sanguansakpakdee N, Amornpornviwat T, Dondorp AM, Day NPJ, Nosten F, White NJ, Imwong M
articles.details: Nanopore sequencing with long-read amplicon technology was optimized to characterize CYP2D6 alleles in 90 individuals to improve identification of patients who would benefit from primaquine therapy for P. vivax malaria. The method identified complex structural variants and a previously unidentified allele, providing superior resolution compared to conventional assays. Nanopore sequencing proved particularly valuable for identifying local genetic variability in malaria-endemic regions and supporting policy decision-making.

articles.headline: Epstein-Barr virus reactivation alters malaria immune responses in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Impact of epstein-barr virus reactivation on cytokine levels in pregnant women with malaria in the west region of cameroon.
articles.authors: Chatue IAD, Nyegue MA, Kamdem SD, Taya-Fokou JB, Djiyou ABD, Tchoupe EB, Tepa A, Neba RF, Junaid IT, Malhotra P, Netongo PM
articles.details: Analysis of 220 pregnant women found those with reactivated EBV and severe malaria had significantly lower IL-6 levels (29.86 pg/ml vs 55.83 pg/ml, p=0.02) and higher TGF-β (17.6 pg/ml vs 6.1 pg/ml, p=0.01) compared to those with latent EBV. A strong negative correlation was observed between IL-10 levels and parasitemia in women with reactivated EBV (r=-0.9762, p=0.0004). EBV reactivation appears to impair inflammatory control of parasitemia and potentially exacerbate malaria severity.

articles.headline: Age drives differential inflammatory responses to malaria infection
articles.title_original: Age is an intrinsic driver of inflammatory responses to malaria.
articles.authors: Loughland JR, Dooley NL, Pava Z, SheelaNair A, Andrew DW, Tipping P, Bourke P, Engwerda CR, Lopez JA, Piera KA, William T, Barber BE, Grigg MJ, Anstey NM, Minigo G, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Comparison of non-immune children and adults showed age-associated differences in inflammatory responses to malaria parasites. Adults exhibited increased inflammatory chemokines (CCL2, CCL3, CXCL8, CXCL9), C-reactive protein (CRP), and indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO) that associated with symptoms. In contrast, children's responses were characterized by elevated IL-10 and enriched IL-10 signaling pathways, suggesting age-dependent cellular mechanisms drive more severe malaria responses in adults.

articles.headline: Cytophilic antibodies control parasite growth better than noncytophilic
articles.title_original: IgM, IgG, and IgG Subclass Antibody Responses to Plasmodium falciparum Proteins in Naïve, Malaria-Vaccinated and Semi-Immune Volunteers after Controlled Human Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Gómez-Pérez GP, Vidal M, Campo JJ, Moncunill G, Jimenez A, Vázquez-Santiago M, Ruiz-Olalla G, Sanz H, Ayestaran A, Angov E, Dutta S, Chitnis C, Chauhan V, James ER, Billingsley PF, Sim BKL, Kremsner PG, Hoffman SL, Lell B, Mordmüller B, Dobaño C
articles.details: Three volunteer groups (malaria-naïve n=22, PfSPZ-CVac vaccinated n=27, semi-immune Africans n=20) received controlled human malaria infection with 3,200 P. falciparum sporozoites. PfSPZ-CVac-immunized individuals exhibited higher IgM and IgG to pre-erythrocytic antigens (CSP and SSP-2/TRAP). Cytophilic IgG1 and IgG3 antibodies to blood-stage antigens were positively associated with longer pre-patent periods, while noncytophilic IgG2 and IgG4 were higher in semi-immune individuals and may impair vaccine efficacy.

articles.headline: Malaria fever therapy reveals P. vivax incubation and fever periodicity
articles.title_original: Clinical and Parasitological Characteristics of Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Malaria-Naïve Patients: A Review of Malaria Fever Therapy in Patients with Schizophrenia and Neurosyphilis during the 1950s and 1960s in Vienna, Austria.
articles.authors: Wolff S, Kainz RM, Reimold D, Lagler H, Mischlinger J, Ramharter M
articles.details: Historical analysis of 322 patients receiving induced P. vivax for treating schizophrenia or neurosyphilis documented fever characteristics following intravenous inoculation of 4-8 mL blood. First fever peaks appeared approximately 7 days post-inoculation, with temperatures increasing over sequential paroxysms and afebrile intervals shortening from 41 to 31 hours. After a mean of 5-6 fever peaks, all patients received antimalarial therapy, establishing the extra-hepatic incubation period as approximately 7 days.

articles.headline: Transfluthrin spatial emanator provides protection against resistant mosquitoes
articles.title_original: The BiteBarrier transfluthrin emanator demonstrates significant protection against susceptible and resistant malaria and arbovirus vectors in semi-field trials in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Maasayi MS, Swai JK, Muganga JB, Moore J, Stevenson JC, Coleman M, Lobo NF, Moore SJ, Tambwe MM
articles.details: Semi-field trials of BiteBarrier, a transfluthrin-based spatial emanator, evaluated protection against five mosquito species including pyrethroid-susceptible and pyrethroid-resistant vectors over 8 weeks. The emanator provided >93% protection against mosquito bites indoors (95% CI 92-93) and 80% outdoors (95% CI 78-81), with substantial mortality (47% indoors, 26% outdoors). Protection was maintained throughout the 8-week aging period regardless of mosquito species or insecticide resistance status.

articles.headline: Epigenetic pathways regulate P. vivax hypnozoite persistence in liver
articles.title_original: A drug repurposing approach reveals targetable epigenetic pathways in Plasmodium vivax hypnozoites.
articles.authors: Maher SP, Bakowski MA, Vantaux A, Flannery EL, Andolina C, Gupta M, Antonova-Koch Y, Argomaniz M, Cabrera-Mora M, Campo B, Chao AT, Chatterjee AK, Cheng WT, Chuenchob V, Cooper CA, Cottier K, Galinski MR, Harupa-Chung A, Ji H, Joseph SB, Lenz T, Lonardi S, Matheson J, Mikolajczak SA, Moeller T, Orban A, Padín-Irizarry V, Pan K, Péneau J, Prudhomme J, Roesch C, Ruberto A, Sabnis SS, Saney CL, Sattabongkot J, Sereshki S, Suriyakan S, Ubalee R, Wang Y, Wasisakun P, Yin J, Popovici J, McNamara CW, Joyner C, Nosten FH, Witkowski B, Le Roch KG, Kyle DE
articles.details: Drug screening of the ReFRAME library and epigenetic inhibitors identified DNA methyltransferase inhibitors and histone modification inhibitors as selectively active against P. vivax hypnozoites. Bisulfite sequencing demonstrated that liver-stage parasites have methylated DNA with methylation affecting gene expression. The acetyl-CoA synthetase inhibitor MMV019721, which affects histone acetylation, was validated as active against hypnozoites, suggesting epigenetic mechanisms may be targeted for improved radical cure therapeutics.

articles.headline: Microsporidia MB undergoes sporogeny in mosquito germline tissues
articles.title_original: Discovery of sporogeny in the malaria transmission-blocking symbiont of Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Herren JK
articles.details: Research visualized Microsporidia MB sporogeny within developing oocytes in mosquito germline tissue, revealing octosporogony and suggesting both vertical and horizontal transmission routes. Identification of spores with hallmark microsporidian structures supports environmental dissemination via egg deposition. These findings advance Microsporidia MB's feasibility as a transmission-blocking symbiont for sustainable malaria control.

articles.headline: PfRIPR vaccine candidate shows genetic diversity in Senegal isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity of the Malaria Vaccine Candidate PfRIPR in a High Transmission Region of Senegal.
articles.authors: Nair M, Geering G, Agarwal A, Li R, Qiao Y, Xiao Q, Pouye MN, Thiam LG, Ba A, Hagadorn KA, Cisse A, Guerra N, Tawaldemedhen Y, Mangou K, Moore AJ, Diallo F, Sene SD, Sadio BD, Zhang E, Shapiro L, Patel SD, Mbengue A, Vigan-Womas I, Sheng Z, Bei AK
articles.details: Deep amplicon sequencing identified 26 non-synonymous SNPs in the RIPR vaccine candidate across 89 P. falciparum isolates from Kédougou, Senegal, with mutations in 64/89 (71.9%) samples and 15 novel variants. Seven mutations predicted destabilized RIPR while 2 increased stability. Three SNPs (Q737K, T738K, V840L) occurred in EGF5-8 domains where neutralizing antibodies bind.

articles.headline: Stakeholder perspectives on domestic funding for malaria control services
articles.title_original: Stakeholders' perspectives on funding malaria, HIV/AIDS and tuberculosis services in Tanzania through domestic resources mobilisation: a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Ngadaya FD, Isangula K, Kimambo H, Soka G, Kitinya C, Philbert D, Mwenda L, Kagaruki GB, Kimaro G, Ngadaya E, Kahwa A, Mfinanga S
articles.details: Qualitative study of 76 stakeholders in Tanzania explored alternative domestic funding approaches for malaria, HIV/AIDS, and TB services. Proposed strategies included universal health insurance, reducing service exemptions, disease-specific funds, and product taxation. Implementation challenges included poor awareness, conflicting political promises, and accountability issues.

articles.headline: Seasonal R21 mass vaccination trial to assess transmission reduction impact
articles.title_original: Seasonal mass vaccination with R21/Matrix-M for malaria elimination (SERVAL): protocol of the cluster randomised trial.
articles.authors: Dabira ED, Natama HM, Jaiteh F, Grietens KP, Bocoum FY, Ndiath MO, Mohammed N, Gibba B, Hill AVS, Ghani A, Erhart A, Tinto H, D'Alessandro U
articles.details: Cluster-randomized trial in Eastern Gambia (low-moderate transmission) and Central Burkina Faso (intense transmission) enrolling 30 and 24 villages respectively. Eligible residents in intervention villages received R21 vaccinations in three-monthly rounds prior to transmission season (May-July 2024), with booster in June 2025. Primary outcome was malaria prevalence at peak transmission (November 2024); secondary outcomes included safety, clinical malaria incidence, and vaccination coverage.

articles.headline: Correction to human-to-mosquito P. knowlesi transmission article
articles.title_original: Correction: Human-to-Anopheles dirus mosquito transmission of the anthropozoonotic malaria parasite, Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Kumpitak C, Duangmanee A, Thongyod W, Rachaphaew N, Suansomjit C, Manopwisedjaroen K, Aung PL, Imad HA, Cui L, Sattabongkot J, Nguitragool W, Bantuchai S
articles.details: Correction notice with no substantive research details provided beyond the title reference.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminths co-infection in pregnant women in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria and soil transmitted helminth infection and associated factors among pregnant women attending public health facilities in Dilla town, South Ethiopia: cross sectional study design.
articles.authors: Tefera S, Bogale A, Kassie A, Abreham A, Bayih A, Ali MM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 331 pregnant women at antenatal facilities in Dilla town, Southern Ethiopia found STH prevalence of 60/331 (18.1%), malaria of 29/331 (8.8%), and co-infection of 19/331 (5.7%). P. vivax and P. falciparum comprised 16/331 (4.8%) and 13/331 (4.0%) respectively. Ascaris lumbricoides was predominant helminth (45/331, 13.6%), followed by hookworms (8/331, 2.4%).

articles.headline: Azithromycin mortality effect varies with seasonal malaria chemoprevention
articles.title_original: The role of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in the effect of azithromycin on child mortality: A secondary analysis of the CHAT cluster randomized clinical trial.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, O'Brien KS, Hsiang MS, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: Secondary analysis of CHAT trial in 341 communities in Nouna District, Burkina Faso examined azithromycin effect with seasonal malaria chemoprevention. Mortality rate ratio for azithromycin vs placebo was 0.77 (95% CI: 0.60-0.98) during SMC season and 0.89 (95% CI: 0.68-1.15) during non-SMC season. In clusters with <80% SMC coverage, effect was 0.73 (95% CI: 0.56-0.96); with ≥80% coverage, 1.0 (95% CI: 0.59-1.69).

articles.headline: Deep learning with MobileNetV2 achieves high accuracy for P. vivax detection
articles.title_original: Leveraging MobileNetV2 and deep learning innovation for high accuracy Plasmodium Vivax detection in blood smears.
articles.authors: Prathap VM, Yadav S, Qidwai T
articles.details: YOLOv3 model with modified MobileNetv2 backbone and Transformed Convolution Layer employed for automated Plasmodium vivax detection in blood smear images. Model achieved accuracy of 1.00, precision 0.98, recall 0.98, F1 score 0.97, and mean average precision (mAP) 0.90, providing improved diagnostic approach over traditional microscopy.

articles.headline: Alstonia boonei extract reduces parasitemia and glucose in infected mice
articles.title_original: Alstonia boonei stem bark aqueous extract ameliorates elevated parasitemia levels, normalises blood glucose, modulates inflammatory biomarkers and enhances antioxidant status in Plasmodium berghei-infected/diabetic mice.
articles.authors: Agunloye OM, Akintoye OF, Oboh G
articles.details: Study evaluated Alstonia boonei stem bark aqueous extract in P. berghei-infected diabetic mice across untreated, chloroquine-metformin treated, and extract-treated (40, 80 mg/kg) groups. Extract produced dose-dependent reduction in fasting blood glucose and parasitemia, enhanced antioxidant status (SOD, catalase, GSH), and reduced C-reactive protein, with effects at 80 mg/kg comparable to standard drug treatment.

articles.headline: Water, sanitation, hygiene, malaria factors predict diarrhea outcomes
articles.title_original: Association of Diarrhea Outcomes with Drinking Water Factors, Sanitation, Hygiene, and Malaria Practices in the Population of Béré, Chad.
articles.authors: Boutrin MC, Andersen M, Gately Z, McLarty C, Santos E
articles.journal: Int J Environ Res Public Health
articles.details: Survey of 484 respondents in Béré, Chad examined diarrhea outcomes and associated factors. Drinking water treatment, transport, storage (p < 0.001), malaria factors (p < 0.001), and sanitation/hygiene practices (p < 0.001) were significant predictors of diarrhea in adults and children. Similar factors including health advice source were predictive of child diarrhea outcomes.

articles.headline: Tetanus with cerebral malaria comorbidity in unvaccinated pediatric patient
articles.title_original: Generalized Tetanus Associated With Malaria and Probable Meningitis in a 4-Year-Old Patient: A Case Report in Limbe Regional Hospital, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchuisseu Ngapjang CD, Fotso C, Gobina R, Lingo PA, Shumbang Tchumukong QJ, Kouam BB, Effila M, Berinyuy Nyuydzefon E, Vincent Siysi V
articles.details: Case report of 4-year-old unvaccinated male presenting with febrile seizures, neck stiffness, and vomiting, diagnosed with tetanus and cerebral malaria, with probable bacterial meningitis as differential. Patient was empirically treated with anti-tetanus toxoid, antibiotics, and antimalarials. Despite limited ICU supportive care, patient improved within 48 hours and was discharged after 5 additional hospital days.

articles.headline: CHMI alters B cell subsets based on malaria immunity status
articles.title_original: Effect of controlled human Plasmodium falciparum infection on B cell subsets in individuals with different levels of malaria immunity.
articles.authors: Requena P, Gómez-Pérez GP, McCall MBB, Barrios D, Aguilar R, Fernández-Morata J, Vidal M, Campo JJ, Sanchez C, Yazdabankhsh M, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Kremsner P, Lell B, Mordmüller B, Dobaño C, Moncunill G
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection in malaria-naïve, vaccinated, and semi-immune individuals showed different B cell responses. Semi-immune subjects had increased frequencies of immature, active naive, and active atypical memory B cells compared to naïve individuals. Vaccination altered CHMI effects: positive association with resting classical MBCs (β = 0.190, 95% CI 0.011-0.368) and active naïve-PD1+ (β = 0.637, 95% CI 0.058-1.217); negative with CD1c+ resting atypical MBCs (β = -0.328, 95% CI -0.621 to -0.032).

articles.headline: Health center sicker patients with severe malaria in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Treatment-Seeking for Children with Suspected Severe Malaria Attending Community Health Workers and Primary Health Centres in Adamawa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Lee TT, Omoluabi E, Ayodeji K, Yusuf O, Okon C, Brunner NC, Delvento G, Signorell A, Lambiris MJ, Kwiatkowski M, Burri C, Lengeler C, Emedo E, Cheshi F, Fashanu C, Wiwa O, Hetzel MW
articles.details: Enrollment study of 589 children <5 years with fever and danger signs in Adamawa State. Compared 314 children attending community health workers (CORPs) to 275 at primary health centres (PHCs). PHC attendees were more likely urban, had ≥4 clinical danger signs (50% vs 39%, p=0.02) and CNS involvement (90% vs 74%, p<0.01). Case fatality was 6% at CORPs vs 19% at PHCs, attributed to sicker children seeking PHC care rather than better treatment capacity.

articles.headline: Climate change expanding malaria transmission range in PNG
articles.title_original: Climate Change Is Expected to Expand Malaria Transmission Range and Population at Risk in Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Karl S, Skinner EB, McEwen S, Keven J, Kisomb J, Robinson LJ, Laman M
articles.details: Analysis of malaria transmission suitability in Papua New Guinea from 1960–2019 using satellite temperature data and climate models. Population living in suitable transmission areas increased from 58% to 61% (249,125 additional people). Under conservative climate models, this is projected to reach 74% by 2040 (2.8 million additional people). Total population requiring malaria control expected to double to 13.4 million by 2040.

articles.headline: Education and internet access boost malaria vaccine awareness in Ghana
articles.title_original: Determinants of Malaria Vaccine Awareness Among Women in Ghana: Findings From the 2022 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Sarfo M, Larbi-Ampofo AK, Lawal O, Bonnah SK, Eshun G
articles.details: Analysis of 38,459 women aged 15–49 years from 2022 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey. Malaria vaccine awareness was higher among women with tertiary education (AOR=2.89, 95% CI: 2.50–3.34), internet use in past 12 months (AOR=1.85, 95% CI: 1.74–1.97), and those in Greater Accra (AOR=1.75, 95% CI: 1.54–2.00). Awareness increased with age, peaking at 45–49 years (AOR=2.03, 95% CI: 1.60–2.57).

articles.headline: Malaria burden in vulnerable populations, Ethiopia umbrella review
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria infection among most vulnerable populations in Ethiopia: an umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
articles.authors: Demeke A, Kassa G, Gebrie H, Tefera S, Getu E, Dube SJ, Bayable A, Kassie A, Muleta B, Eba WW, Gebremeskele BT, Demeke AD
articles.details: Umbrella review of six systematic reviews and meta-analyses of malaria prevalence in vulnerable populations (children, pregnant women, migrants) in Ethiopia. Overall pooled prevalence was 10.10% (95% CI: 0.36–19.85). Non-use of insecticide-treated nets (OR=5.22, 95% CI: 3.93–6.94) and proximity to stagnant water (OR=4.21, 95% CI: 2.75–6.46) were independently associated with increased malaria risk.

articles.headline: CYP6P9a/b cytochrome P450 drives cross-resistance in malaria vectors
articles.title_original: The duplicated cytochrome P450 CYP6P9a/b confers cross-resistance to a mitochondrial complex I inhibitor in the African malaria vector Anopheles funestus.
articles.authors: Tekoh TA, Mugenzi LMJ, Menze B, Tchapga W, Wondji M, Tchouakui M, Small G, Wondji CS
articles.details: Bioassay and molecular study showing CYP6P9a/b genes in Anopheles funestus confer cross-resistance to pyrethroid and novel mitochondrial complex I inhibitor (Sherlock). Field strains from Cameroon were susceptible; FUMOZ-R strain showed moderate resistance. Individuals carrying one CYP6P9a_R allele had significantly higher odds of surviving Sherlock: CDC bioassay 1×dose (OR=5.3, CI=2.7–9.8, p<0.0001), 5×dose (OR=18.6, CI=7.8–46.4, p<0.0001); cone bioassay (OR=5.1, CI=2.7–9.8); tunnel tests (OR=6.6, CI=3.4–12.6, p<0.0001).

articles.headline: Treatment delay prevalence and determinants in under-five malaria, Horn of Africa
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of delay in seeking malaria treatment among under five children in the Horn of Africa: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Agimas MC, Aweke MN, Mengistu B, Baffa LD, Fentie EA, Shewarega ES, Belew AK, Muhammad EA
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of malaria treatment delay in children <5 years. Pooled prevalence of delayed treatment was 48% (95% CI: 34%–63%). Risk factors: history of child death (OR=2.5, 95% CI: 1.73–3.59), distance >3000 m (OR=2.59, 95% CI: 2.03–3.3), drug side effects (OR=2.94, 95% CI: 1.86–4.67), high transport costs (OR=4.39, 95% CI: 2.49–7.76). Protective factors: formal education (OR=0.69, 95% CI: 0.49–0.96), middle income (OR=0.42, 95% CI: 0.28–0.63).

articles.headline: Household vegetation linked to malaria vector abundance and infection in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Housing and household characteristics associated with malaria vectors abundance and clinical malaria incidence in a semi-urban and a rural area of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Nikiema M, Dambach P, Kabore J, Some SYM, Ouedraogo A, Barry Y, Boudo V, Chabi BAM, Sie A, Badolo A, Gneme A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 355 houses in semi-urban and rural Nouna health district sampled July–October 2021. Total 22,863 mosquitoes collected, primarily Anopheles coluzzii, A. gambiae, and A. arabiensis. Vegetation around houses positively associated with mosquito abundance (RR=2.5, CI=1.43–4.15, p<0.001), vector infection (RR=2.5, CI=1.74–3.33, p<0.001), and human malaria cases (RR=1.4, CI=1.03–2.0, p=0.048). Presence of children <5 years (RR=1.52, CI=1.25–1.84, p<0.001) and female householder (RR=1.23, CI=1.02–2.78, p<0.001) associated with malaria cases.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection alters skin flora and increases mosquito attraction
articles.title_original: [Skin flora, odor and malaria transmission].
articles.authors: Song X, Wang J
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Review discussing how Plasmodium infection changes host body odor and skin flora, increasing attractiveness to female Anopheles mosquitoes. Recent studies in mice found elevated ethylbenzene and other volatile substances following infection. Mechanisms remain incompletely clear but offer potential targets for novel malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: P. vivax RBP1a ligand binds multiple erythrocyte receptors for reticulocyte invasion
articles.title_original: The reticulocyte restriction: invasion ligand RBP1a of Plasmodium vivax targets human TfR1, prohibitin-2, and basigin.
articles.authors: Molina-Franky J, Röth D, Ararat-Sarria M, Patarroyo MA, Kalkum M
articles.details: In vitro study establishing JK-1 and BEL-A erythroid cell lines as effective P. vivax invasion surrogates. Membrane proteomics and proximity labeling identified PvRBP1a ligand interactions with transferrin receptor TfR1 (CD71), basigin (BSG), and novel co-receptor prohibitin-2 (PHB2). Additional candidates included CD98hc, CAT-1, ATB(0), CD36, integrin beta-1, and CNNM3. First report of PHB2 as P. vivax co-receptor.

articles.headline: Plasmodium ARK1 kinase drives atypical spindle assembly and mitotic progression
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.authors: Tewari R, Nagar A, Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Abel S, Pashley S, Mishra A, Eze A, Rea E, Brady D, Bottrill A, Vaughan S, Roch KL, Guttery D, Holder A, Tromer E, Sharma P
articles.details: Functional study of Plasmodium Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) during schizogony and gametogony. ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle formation, kinetochore segregation, nuclear division, and cytokinesis in both stages, affecting parasite transmission. Interactome analysis revealed ARK1 as catalytic core of divergent chromosomal passenger complex (CPC) containing two inner centromere proteins (INCENPs) but lacking Survivin and Borealin, a non-canonical architecture arising early in Apicomplexa.

articles.headline: Cytokines TNF-α, IFN-γ, TGF-β1 associate with parasite density in malaria patients
articles.title_original: Assessment of Cytokines TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma, TGF-beta-1, and IL-10 in Malaria Patients of the River Nile State , Sudan: A critical study of immune response.
articles.authors: Hamad MNM, Albadrani GM, Ghazwani AA, Abdelmola A, Suliman RS, Mahjaf GM, Fahmy SA, Badi S, Yassin HM, Othman GA, Altaher TA, Noor SK
articles.details: Ninety malaria-positive participants in River Nile State, Sudan. TNF-α, IFN-γ, and TGF-β1 levels significantly associated with parasite density (p<0.05), with positive correlations (r=0.42, 0.38, 0.51; p<0.01). TGF-β1 significantly elevated in P. vivax infections. IL-10 higher in P. falciparum than P. vivax cases (560.0 vs 415.6 pg/mL, p=0.019). IL-10 not significantly associated with parasite density.

articles.headline: Clinical immunity to P. vivax develops after single controlled infection, species-specific
articles.title_original: Development of clinical immunity to Plasmodium vivax following repeat controlled human malaria infection.
articles.authors: Hou MM, Harding AC, Barber NM, Kundu P, Bach FA, Salkeld J, Themistocleous Y, Greenwood NM, Cho JS, Barrett JR, Nugent FL, Rawlinson TA, Hodgson SH, Khozoee B, Mac Lochlainn DJ, Cowan RE, Poulton ID, Baker M, Kingham L, Mitton CH, Platt A, Lopez Ramon R, Ramos Lopez F, Thomas M, Skinner K, Quinkert D, Pipini D, Lias AM, Bardelli M, Edwards NJ, Donnellan FR, Biswas S, Rayner JC, Nielsen CM, Silk SE, Draper SJ, Nahrendorf W, Spence PJ, Minassian AM
articles.details: Multi-cohort repeat controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) study: 19 participants primary P. vivax CHMI, 12 secondary homologous, 2 tertiary; 6 underwent heterologous P. falciparum rechallenge. Clinical immunity to P. vivax developed rapidly after first CHMI, protecting against fever and laboratory abnormalities through attenuation of inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, coagulation and endothelium activation—independent of parasite clearance. Immunity was species-specific: P. vivax immunity provided no protection against P. falciparum CHMI.

articles.headline: Supply gaps and vector control lapses drive malaria surge in Uganda's Elgon
articles.title_original: From Endemic to Epidemic: A Qualitative District-Level Assessment of an Increase in Malaria Cases in the Elgon Region of Uganda.
articles.authors: Fuller B, Ssekitoleko R, Kyozira C, Makumbi I, Bakainaga A, Moore CC, Kiirya HI
articles.details: A qualitative assessment of 10 districts in Uganda's high-burden Elgon region identified critical prevention and response gaps. Malaria incidence increased from 206 to 271 cases per 1,000 population between 2012 and 2022. Key findings included inadequate insecticide-treated nets in 70% of districts, absent indoor residual spraying in 30%, limited rapid diagnostic tests in 80%, and a mean antimalarial resupply interval of 117 days. Main themes extracted were lapsed vector control strategies, insufficient logistical support, and limited chemotherapy availability.

articles.headline: Standardized reporting framework for malaria molecular surveillance genomics
articles.title_original: Toward Setting Minimum and Optimal Data to Report for Malaria Molecular Surveillance with Targeted Sequencing: The "What" and "Why".
articles.authors: Juliano JJ, Meier-Scherling CPG, Wernsman Young N, Tollefson GA, Connelly SV, Parr JB, Conrad MD, Sadler JM, Hennelly CM, Assefa A, Okell L, Fola AA, Niaré K, Marglous J, Carey-Ewend K, Futila Kyong-Shin R, Gerdes Gyuricza I, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Ruybal-Pesántez S, Watson OJ, Verity R, Bailey JA
articles.details: This article proposes minimum and optimal data standards for reporting malaria molecular surveillance (MMS) to improve validation and comparability of genomic findings. It addresses challenges such as artemisinin partial resistance and deletions in Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 genes. The authors recommend reporting individual infection-level polymorphism or microhaplotype data while adhering to local regulatory practices and preventing data colonialism. Standardized reporting practices are essential for advancing global public health responses.

articles.headline: Climate warming may reduce malaria transmission in high-temperature Colombian regions
articles.title_original: Stochastic treatment regimes in climate-health research: Reassessing malaria risk under warming scenarios in Colombia.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez JD
articles.details: Using causal machine learning on malaria cases across 100 high-incidence Colombian municipalities (2007–2023), the study identified an optimal temperature range of 15–23.5°C for malaria transmission, with transmission declining at higher temperatures. The Average Treatment Effect (ATE) on excess malaria cases was −0.007 for a 0.5°C temperature increase and −0.063 for a 2.0°C increase. Findings suggest that further warming could constrain transmission intensity in already-warm regions, supporting need for locally-tailored public health strategies.

articles.headline: Ellagic acid from Mitragyna inermis shows potent cross-activity against resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: A bio-guided investigation of Mitragyna inermis (Willd) O. Kuntze (Rubiaceae) unveils natural product isolates with potent cross-activity against sensitive and multidrug resistant Plasmodium falciparum strains in vitro.
articles.authors: Hassan S, Osei Bobbie S, Tchatat Tali MB, Dize D, Kamche Youbi A, Lawane AI, Kemgne Madiesse E, Mahamat Alio H, Baker B, Fekam Boyom F
articles.details: Bio-guided screening of Mitragyna inermis stem bark and twig extracts identified ellagic acid (IC50 0.84–1.62 μg/mL) with potent cross-activity against drug-susceptible (Pf3D7) and multidrug-resistant P. falciparum strains (PfDd2, PfINDO, PfW2, PfK1). Methanolic and ethanol/water extracts showed highly potent antiplasmodial activity (IC50 < 3 μg/mL) with acceptable selectivity index (> 10) against Vero cells. Ellagic acid exhibited favorable pharmacokinetic properties including good oral bioavailability and non-CYP450 inhibition. Four additional compounds (5–8) showed IC50 values of 0.21–5.38 μg/mL against multiple parasite strains.

articles.headline: Herbal extracts reverse testicular oxidative damage from P. berghei infection in mice
articles.title_original: Bitter leaf (Vernonia amygdalina) and siam weed (Chromolaena odorata) aqueous extracts alleviate testicular damage induced by Plasmodium berghei in male mice via modulation of oxidative stress pathways.
articles.authors: Oyeleye IS, Bolarinde AA, Ademiluyi AO, Oboh G, Ojo OR
articles.details: In 64 male mice inoculated with chloroquine-sensitive Plasmodium berghei NK65, treatment with bitter leaf (Vernonia amygdalina) or Siam weed (Chromolaena odorata) aqueous extracts at 100–400 mg/kg for 5 days significantly (p < 0.05) suppressed parasitemia and reduced testicular oxidative stress markers (ROS and TBARS) in a dose-dependent manner. Treated mice showed increased antioxidant levels and inhibited enzyme activities compared to infected, untreated controls. Herbal extracts reversed oxidative damage to testes through modulation of oxidative stress pathways.

articles.headline: Genetic variants in olfactory genes linked to host preference in An. minimus
articles.title_original: Genetic variation in olfactory pathways associated with host-seeking behavior in natural populations of Anopheles minimus, a primary malaria vector in western Thailand.
articles.authors: Pusawang K, Zhong D, Sriwichai P, Samung Y, Saeung A, Aupalee K, Somboon P, Junkum A, Wongpalee SP, Saingamsook J, Sattabongkot J, Cui L, Yan G
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of wild Anopheles minimus females collected in Tha Song Yang district, Tak province, Thailand (July 2019–November 2020) identified genetic variants associated with host-seeking behavior. Among 504 An. minimus specimens (25% of total samples), sequencing revealed 143 unique cox1 haplotypes and 12.66 million variants genome-wide. After filtering, 2,629–4,369 significant non-synonymous SNPs were identified across host-preference groups (FDR-adjusted p < 0.001) with strong enrichment for olfaction-related genes. Candidate genes included AMIN001807, AMIN001339, AMIN003886, AMIN000912, AMIN003926, AMIN011060, AMIN002342, and AMIN015480.

articles.headline: ARIMA time series models outperform FB-Prophet for Uganda's regional malaria incidence prediction
articles.title_original: Comparison of ARIMA and FB-Prophet time series models for the prediction of national and regional malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.authors: Fuller B, Ssekitoleko R, Kyozira C, Colston JM, Makumbi I, Bakainaga A, Moore CC, Kiirya HI
articles.details: Using DHIS2 surveillance data from 2020–2023, ARIMA and FB-Prophet time series models were compared for predicting national and regional malaria incidence in Uganda. Malaria incidence increased from 200.5 cases per 1,000 persons annually in 2021 to 265.4 per 1,000 in 2022, with highest burden in West Nile, Acholi, and Busoga regions. ARIMA demonstrated superior performance with mean regional MAE 0.007, MAPE 31.2%, and RMSE 0.01, compared to FB-Prophet (MAE 0.01, MAPE 47.8%, RMSE 0.01). ARIMA outperformed FB-Prophet at the national level and in 14 of 15 regions.

articles.headline: Human genetic variations conferring malaria resistance: a review
articles.title_original: Human genetic variations conferring resistance to malaria.
articles.authors: Zhang X, Wu J, Peng Y, Luo L, Zhang L, Huang X, Chen G, Li Y, Yi H
articles.details: This review synthesizes knowledge of genetic variations associated with malaria resistance, including hemoglobinopathies (sickle cell trait, thalassemia), glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, blood group polymorphisms, and genetic variants related to inflammation and immune regulation. The prevalence of these genetic adaptations varies widely across geographic regions, reflecting historical malaria burden in those areas. The authors note that precise protective mechanisms remain incompletely understood and interactions between genetic factors and environmental influences add complexity. Comprehensive understanding of these variations is crucial for advancing malaria epidemiology, diagnostics, and targeted prevention strategies.

articles.headline: 36% of An. sinensis carry kdr mutations; 78% carry ace-1 resistance mutations in China
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and the role of target-site insensitivity mutations among malaria vectors in China: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: He Z, Wang D, Kou Y, Liu Y, Zhao D, Yang C, Zhou R, Zhang H, Deng Y
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 30 articles reporting 30,065 Anopheles sinensis (2000–2024) found pooled insecticide mortality of 61% (95% CI: 53–68). Mortality varied by insecticide: fenitrothion 82%, malathion 81%, propoxur 69%, permethrin 61%, cyfluthrin 59%, lambda-cyhalothrin 56%, deltamethrin 47%, DDT 49%, beta-cypermethrin 48%, and beta-cyfluthrin 28%. Knockdown resistance (kdr) frequency was 36% (13% homozygous, 13% heterozygous, 74% susceptible), while acetylcholinesterase-1 (ace-1) mutation frequency was 78% (42% homozygous, 25% heterozygous, 33% susceptible). An. sinensis demonstrated resistance to pyrethroids, organochlorines, carbamates, and organophosphates.

articles.headline: Education increases malaria awareness yet paradoxically decreases net use in rural Nigeria
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and educational influences on malaria prevention and treatment behaviours in rural Nigeria.
articles.authors: Alao JO, Olowoshile OP, James TA, Okezie CC, Adebayo ZP, Ogbonna SC, Oyelayo EA
articles.details: A cross-sectional analysis of 2021 Malaria Indicator Survey data examined relationships between socioeconomic factors and malaria control behaviors in rural Nigeria across three populations: households (n=5,021), women aged 15–49 years (n=9,546), and treatment-seekers (n=893). Insecticide-treated net ownership was near-universal (97.7%) but usage was 70.3%, with low-income households showing 3.6× higher ITN use odds (95% CI: 3.07–4.29) than wealthy households. Education strongly predicted malaria prevention awareness (p < 0.001), yet ITN use paradoxically decreased with increasing education. Media exposure did not significantly influence choice of healthcare service for malaria treatment.

articles.headline: Adjuvants and boosting overcome subdominance in P. vivax Duffy binding protein vaccine epitopes
articles.title_original: Adjuvants and MHCII modulate the immunogenicity of subdominant epitopes in Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein.
articles.authors: Vinals DF, Hoque MR, Oludada OE, Jansen EB, Mitran CJ, Enterina JR, Macauley MS, Hawkes MT, Yanow SK
articles.details: The study investigated how immunization strategies affect immunogenicity of subdominant regions within Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein (DBP). Antigens with mutations in immunodominant epitopes combined with adjuvants GLA-SE or alum successfully overcame subdominance, though MHC class II restriction largely negated these effects. Boosting proved essential, altering the epitope hierarchy and enabling recruitment of germinal center B cells specific to subdominant epitopes. These findings provide insights for designing subunit vaccines targeting conserved subdominant epitopes rather than immunodominant ones.

articles.headline: Suriname achieves malaria elimination in Amazon region
articles.title_original: Suriname: first malaria-free country in the Amazon region.
articles.authors: Das M
articles.details: Suriname has achieved malaria elimination, becoming the first country in the Amazon region to attain malaria-free status.

articles.headline: Blood donor screening reveals malaria prevalence and seasonal patterns in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Blood donors as a sentinel population for real-time malaria surveillance using the Sysmex XN-31: a one-year review from the National Blood Transfusion Centre in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Sawadogo S, Hwandih T, Saker J, Coetzer TL, Paszkowska A, Kompaoré SCB, Münster M
articles.details: Prospective study screening consecutive blood donations (Aug 2023-Aug 2024) using Sysmex XN-31 automated analyzer at two regional centers in Burkina Faso. Malaria prevalence 5.91% (3164/53575); key predictors included age ≤30 years (OR 2.85, p<0.001), male sex (OR 1.47, p<0.001), and rural residency (OR 2.40, p<0.001). Strong seasonal variability mirroring rainfall with 3-month lag demonstrated potential for real-time surveillance using blood donor screening.

articles.headline: REL2 pathway regulates Anopheles midgut bacteria and affects malaria parasite prevalence
articles.title_original: The major role of the REL2/NF-κB pathway in the regulation of midgut bacterial homeostasis in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Zaković S, Rivera GE, Gomaid R, Martinez CG, Kappler C, Marois E, Levashina EA
articles.details: Study of tissue-specific immune responses in Anopheles gambiae females during blood feeding and P. falciparum infection. REL2 regulates antimicrobial peptide genes and shapes midgut bacterial composition; loss of REL2 causes dysbiosis with Serratia overgrowth and mosquito lethality. Serratia-induced dysbiosis reduces P. falciparum prevalence in surviving mosquitoes, highlighting complex interactions between mosquito immunity, gut microbiota, and malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Genome assembly and characterization of Anopheles stephensi malaria vector
articles.title_original: Whole genome Shotgun Sequence of Anopheles stephensi, The Host of Malaria parasite, Plasmodium sp.
articles.authors: Farah J, Jang J, Madhi K, Phan J, Pratt E, Sankrit A, Sharma NL, Suchak A, Thomas DW, Ghazal SM
articles.details: Whole-genome shotgun sequencing of female Anopheles stephensi, a major malaria vector in Asia. Resulting genome assembly was 201 Mbp, consisting of 32,280 contigs with an N50 of 21.1 kb and GC content of 45%.

articles.headline: Editorial on drug resistance mechanisms in tuberculosis and malaria
articles.title_original: Editorial: Mechanisms driving drug resistance in tuberculosis and malaria: genetic, environmental, and evolutionary insights.
articles.authors: Kushwaha AK, Bansal A, Arora G, Kushwaha D
articles.details: Editorial discussing mechanisms driving drug resistance in tuberculosis and malaria, addressing genetic, environmental, and evolutionary insights.

articles.headline: Dual malaria infection with exflagellated P. vivax in peripheral blood
articles.title_original: Exflagellation of Plasmodium vivax and Dual Infection Diagnosed on Peripheral Blood Film: A Rare Case Report.
articles.authors: Patil BU, Ghongade P, Bhatkule M, Chavhan P
articles.details: Case report of 18-year-old male with simultaneous P. vivax (trophozoites, schizonts, exflagellated microgametes) and P. falciparum (trophozoites) detected on peripheral blood smear. Exflagellation of P. vivax is highly unusual during human-stage development and requires prompt reporting for appropriate treatment and prevention of parasite transmission.

articles.headline: P. vivax genetic diversity and population structure across Mexican malaria foci
articles.title_original: Population Dynamics of Plasmodium vivax in Mexico Determined by CSP, Pvs25, and SSU 18S rRNA S-Type Polymorphism Analyses.
articles.authors: González-Cerón L, Gómez-Pérez DJ, Santillán-Valenzuela F, Ovilla-Muñoz M, Guzmán-Bracho C, Pech-May A, Amores GR, Montoya-Pérez A, Villarreal-Treviño C
articles.details: Analysis of P. vivax parasites from five malaria foci in Mexico using circumsporozoite gene, pvs25, and 18S SSU rRNA markers. VK210 haplotypes showed greater diversity (0.860) than VK247 (0.198); two pvs25 mutations (Q87K and I130T) yielded three haplotypes and two ribosomal variants were detected. Gene and multilocus frequencies varied significantly among foci (p<0.001) with structured population (FST analysis), indicating each focus retained unique parasite reservoirs relevant for elimination surveillance.

articles.headline: Guillain-Barré Syndrome following P. falciparum malaria in adolescent male
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome Following Falciparum Malaria in an 18-Year Male Adolescent: A Rare Case Report and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Getahun GA, Arega G, Jemal MK, Woldie BG, Gudina EK
articles.details: Case report of 18-year-old male who developed Guillain-Barré Syndrome (AMSAN variant) during P. falciparum infection. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed elevated protein (172 mg/dL); patient treated with intravenous artesunate and supportive care. Weakness stabilized by day 14 with significant improvement at 1-2 months.

articles.headline: Gaps between WHO malaria burden estimates and reported cases in India
articles.title_original: Time to address the drawbacks in estimation and reporting of malaria cases in India and beyond.
articles.authors: Deora N, Singhal R, Sinha A
articles.details: Analysis of estimation-reporting ratio (ERR) for malaria cases in India from 2010-2022. ERR remained consistently high (WHO-SE Asia region >6; India >10). Private sector reporting virtually non-existent; research data showed microscopy-positivity 3-55 times higher than reported by National Malaria Control Programme, highlighting significant gaps in malaria burden quantification.

articles.headline: Parasitized erythroblasts and cerebral malaria protection in murine immunization model
articles.title_original: Visual Detection of Malaria Parasite-Parasitized Erythroblasts in Peripheral Blood via Immunization-Based Model.
articles.authors: Ito K, Tateishi YS, Imai T, Miyazaki S, Miyazaki Y, Kagaya W, Nakashima M, Sase M, Yoshioka-Takeda M, Shimokawa C, Hayashi K, Itokawa K, Komagata O, Ngo-Thanh H, Shimo A, Araki T, Annoura T, Murakami T, Hisaeda H
articles.details: Murine model study in which mice immunized with live P. yoelii 17XNL and challenged with P. berghei ANKA showed protection from cerebral malaria with prolonged survival. Parasitized erythroblasts (pEBs) appeared in circulation and were detected via Giemsa-stained smears, containing all blood-stage parasite forms. Model enables in vivo analysis of pEB biology relevant to severe malaria pathophysiology.

articles.headline: Culicidae vector ecology and blood-feeding preferences in southern Iran
articles.title_original: Culicidae vector ecology in southern Iran: Integrating Faunistics and molecular analysis of blood-feeding preferences to strengthen malaria surveillance in transition zones.
articles.authors: Kalantari M, Azizi K
articles.details: Study of Culicidae fauna and blood-feeding preferences in Shiraz, Iran (April 2023-January 2024) on 1249 collected mosquitoes using morphological and molecular methods. Dominant species were Culex pipiens (39.0%), Cx. quinquefasciatus (23.6%), and Aedes caspius (19.6%); Anopheles dthali and An. sacharovi comprised primary Anophelinae. PCR-RFLP analysis showed Anopheles primarily fed on rodents/dogs (41%) and humans (28%), while Cx. pipiens showed marked anthropophily (54% human blood meals).

articles.headline: Sociodemographic factors associated with malaria in under-five children in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic Factors Associated with Malaria Infection among Under-Five Children in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Agboola HO, Ajibola O, Lasisi AJ, Adebayo F, Aremu O, Khan S
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey data (10,352 children) using binomial logistic regression. Children's age, mother's education, wealth index, and place of residence were significant predictors of malaria RDT positivity (all p<0.05). Rural residence increased odds of malaria infection (OR 1.633, p=0.000) compared to urban areas.

articles.headline: SMC knowledge and uptake among caregivers of under-five children in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Knowledge and Uptake of Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention among Caregivers of Under-five Children in a Tertiary Hospital in Kaduna State, North-western Nigeria.
articles.authors: Tom HM, Suleiman AG, Usman S, Darma MA, Yahaya U, Wakawa MM, Hamza AS, Babandi ZS
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 234 caregivers at a tertiary hospital in Kaduna State, Nigeria. Overall SMC knowledge was 54.0% (128/234); 58.4% (137/234) received at least one SMC dose, but only 27.2% (65/234) received all four doses. Primary reasons for missed SMC were lack of knowledge (54.0%) and child absence (20.0%) during distribution. Education, occupation, ethnicity, religion, and SMC knowledge significantly associated with uptake (p<0.05).

articles.headline: Mixed P. falciparum and P. vivax in G6PD-deficient patient
articles.title_original: Navigating Challenges in Mixed Malaria Infections: A Case of Mixed Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Infection in a Patient With Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) Deficiency.
articles.authors: Lau CKS, Kek XY, Gan WF
articles.details: Case report of imported mixed malaria infection in a glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficient patient with both P. falciparum and P. vivax identified by malaria PCR. Patient improved after treatment with IV artesunate followed by oral Riamet (artemether/lumefantrine) and primaquine.

articles.headline: Plant extract inhibits malaria parasite heat shock protein
articles.title_original: Acanthospermum australe Extract Inhibits the Chaperone Activity of Plasmodium falciparum Heat Shock Protein 70-1.
articles.authors: Koza NA, Myeza NN, Hoppe H, Mosa RA, Kappo AP, Simelane MBC, Opoku AR
articles.details: Study of dichloromethane extract from Acanthospermum australe against P. falciparum strain 3D7. Extract demonstrated IC50 of 1.3 µg/mL and inhibited PfHsp70-1 chaperone activity, increasing protein aggregation and reducing ATPase activity, suggesting potential for targeting parasite Hsp70 functions.

articles.headline: Targeting mitochondrial function for antimalarial drug discovery
articles.title_original: Targeting Mitochondrial Function in Plasmodium falciparum: Insight into Antimalarial Drugs and the Emerging Role of Saccharomyces cerevisiae as a Model System.
articles.authors: Greco S, Assalve G, Lunetti P, Kayentao K, Dara A, Scaramuzzi D, Zara V, Ferramosca A
articles.details: Review of recent advances in compounds targeting mitochondrial function in P. falciparum and discussion of Saccharomyces cerevisiae as a model organism for antimalarial drug discovery. Highlights parasite mitochondrion as promising therapeutic target due to essential role throughout life cycle stages and marked structural differences from human mitochondria.

articles.headline: Supporting the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria
articles.title_original: Supporting the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria.
articles.authors: Piot P, Abubakar I
articles.details: No abstract provided.

articles.headline: New tools needed for malaria prevention in pregnancy
articles.title_original: A need for new tools for prevention of malaria in pregnancy.
articles.authors: Saito M, McGready R
articles.details: Commentary on intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP). Highlights need for better understanding of mechanisms by which IPTp-SP reduces low birthweight and novel measures to prevent malaria earlier in gestation.

articles.headline: Controlled human malaria infection studies advance vaccine development
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection studies: insights into recent advances and key immunological and ethical implementation lessons.
articles.authors: Ogwang R, Adan M, Bejon P, Kapulu MC
articles.details: Review of controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) models and their role in clinical development. CHMI models accurately predicted efficacy of malaria vaccines RTSS/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M and accelerated their development. Studies have also supported advancement of drugs and monoclonal antibodies and characterized immunity mechanisms underlying protection.

articles.headline: Persistent P. vivax malaria hotspots challenge elimination in India
articles.title_original: Hotspot districts of persistent Plasmodium vivax malaria pose a formidable challenge to malaria elimination in India.
articles.authors: Ramasamy A, Thiruvengadam K, Viswan K A, Sharma R, Nagarajan SA, Yadav CP, Sharma A, Rahi M
articles.details: No abstract provided.

articles.headline: Mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax co-infections in Duffy-negative Ethiopians
articles.title_original: High prevalence of mixed Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax co-infections in Duffy negative individuals in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Tat B, Feufack-Donfack LB, Neneyoh Yengo B, Eng V, Tefera Shenkutie T, Abebe A, Tsigie M, Tacoli C, Khim N, Feleke SM, Lo E, Popovici J
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1,154 febrile patients in three Ethiopian regions (2020-2021) found Plasmodium infection in 46%: 49% P. falciparum, 26% mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax, 23% P. vivax mono-infections. Among Duffy-negative individuals (41% of total), P. vivax mono-infections were rare (2.4%) while 25% of mixed infections were Duffy-negative. Of 155 samples tested, 78% showed PvDBP gene duplication, suggesting evolutionary pressure to overcome host barriers in Duffy-negative populations.

articles.headline: Regulatory T cells balance immunity and persistence in malaria
articles.title_original: CXCR6(+) CD127(-) Tr1 Cells Balance Immunity and Persistence in Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Nideffer J, Bach F, Strubbe S, Lopez L, Zedi M, Nankya F, Briggs J, van der Ploeg K, Musinguzi K, Kim S, Romero AG, Keya A, Camanag K, Lewis S, Abdelbasset M, Wang B, Boss A, Nansubuga E, Nankabirwa JI, Arinaitwe E, Takahashi S, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I, Kamya MR, Bacchetta R, Ssewanyana I, Haque A, Roncarolo MG, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Study in children from endemic Uganda identified CXCR6+ CD127- markers for type 1 regulatory (Tr1) cells comprising ~90% of P. falciparum antigen-specific CD4+ T cells. These cells suppressed other CD4+ T cell proliferation via IL-10. Higher Tr1 frequencies correlated with lower probability of symptoms given parasitemia but with delayed parasite clearance in untreated children, suggesting they mediate clinical immunity while facilitating parasite persistence.

articles.headline: Genomic diversity of African malaria vector Anopheles funestus
articles.title_original: Genomic diversity of the African malaria vector Anopheles funestus.
articles.authors: Boddé M, Nwezeobi J, Korlević P, Makunin A, Akone-Ella O, Barasa S, Gadji M, Hart L, Kaindoa EW, Love K, Lucas ER, Lujumba I, Máquina M, Nagi SC, Odero JO, Polo B, Sangbakembi C, Dadzie S, Koekemoer LL, Kwiatkowski D, McAlister E, Ochomo E, Okumu F, Paaijmans K, Tchouassi DP, Wondji CS, Ayala D, Durbin R, Miles A, Lawniczak MKN
articles.details: Sequencing of 656 modern (2014-2018) and 45 historic (1927-1967) Anopheles funestus specimens from 16 African countries revealed high genetic diversity with stable continental population structure. Vector control produced strong selection signals with some resistance alleles shared across populations via gene flow and others arising independently. A gene drive target conserved in Anopheles gambiae is also highly conserved in An. funestus.

articles.headline: DHA-piperaquine plus SP no benefit over single drugs for pregnant women
articles.title_original: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine plus sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine versus either drug alone for intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy: A double-blind, randomized, controlled phase 3 trial from Uganda.
articles.authors: Kakuru A, Kizza J, Aguti M, Adrama H, Ategeka J, Olwoch P, Nakalembe M, Nankabirwa JI, Opira B, Ozarslan N, Ranjit A, Dela Cruz E, Clark TD, Roh ME, Gaw SL, Jagannathan P, Rosenthal PJ, Kamya MR, Dorsey G
articles.details: Double-blind RCT of 2,757 pregnant women in Uganda comparing monthly IPTp with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PQ), or combination. Combination did not reduce composite adverse birth outcome versus DHA-PQ (30.0% vs 30.9%, RR 0.97, p=0.70) or SP (30.0% vs 26.4%, RR 1.14, p=0.10). DHA-PQ had higher adverse outcome than SP (30.9% vs 26.4%, RR 1.17, p=0.04). DHA-PQ showed 94% reduction in symptomatic malaria and 97% reduction in microscopic parasitemia versus SP.

articles.headline: Meteorological and resistance factors drive malaria resurgence in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Investigating the resurgence of malaria in Rwanda: factors contributing to rising cases amidst near-eradication success and strategies for sustained control.
articles.authors: Julius N, Diane BI
articles.details: Review analyzing malaria resurgence in Rwanda (2022-2025) showing 45.8% increase in cases by 2024 from 620,000 in 2023/24. Major contributing factors include meteorological changes widening Anopheles gambiae habitat, pyrethroid and artemisinin resistance (K13 mutations), suboptimal non-pyrethroid IRS coverage, poverty, and human mobility. March 2024 saw 87,000 cases with outbreaks at high altitudes. 2025 Strategic Plan emphasizes new drugs (DHA-piperaquine, artesunate-pyronaridine) and regional cooperation.

articles.headline: Evolution of P. falciparum parasites and resistance mechanisms
articles.title_original: Evolutionary Trajectory of Plasmodium falciparum: From Autonomous Phototroph to Dedicated Parasite.
articles.authors: Pikor D, Hurla M, Drelichowska A, Paul M
articles.details: Perspective synthesizing evolutionary co-dynamics of human host, Anopheles vectors, and P. falciparum parasites. Discusses host genetic adaptations including hemoglobinopathies and immune polymorphisms reducing disease severity; vector insecticide resistance through metabolic detoxification and target-site insensitivity; and parasite genome streamlining optimized for parasitic lifestyle with rapid mutation accumulation enabling immune escape and drug resistance. Highlights molecular arms race necessitating next-generation therapeutic and vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Young primigravida at highest risk for malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Maternal and household risk factors for malaria in pregnancy and low birthweight: a prospective cohort study from Uganda.
articles.authors: Aguti M, Kizza J, Kakuru A, Nakalembe M, Nankabirwa JI, Gaw SL, Opira B, Ssemukuye T, Ozarslan N, Ranjit A, Cruz E, Clark TD, Roh ME, Jagannathan P, Rosenthal PJ, Adrama H, Olwoch P, Mayende J, Odongo B, John A, Kamya MR, Dorsey G
articles.details: Prospective cohort study from December 2020 to July 2024 in Busia District, Uganda of 2,728 pregnant women in randomized controlled trial of intermittent preventive treatment. Overall, 38.1% had parasitemia at enrollment, 6.5% after IPTp initiation, 6.4% high-grade placental malaria, 6.8% low birth weight. Younger age (16-21 years: RR 1.62-1.72, p<0.002) and primigravida status (RR 1.86-2.40, p<0.001) strongly increased parasitemia risk. Primigravida increased low birth weight (RR 2.14, p=0.01) and high-grade placental malaria (RR 4.20, p<0.001); traditional house construction increased parasitemia (RR 1.17-1.30, p=0.002-0.02).

articles.headline: Climate change shifting malaria transmission in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Impact of Climate Change on Malaria Transmission and Management in Zimbabwe: A Scoping Review of the Literature.
articles.authors: Chivasa T, Nunu WN, Dhlamini M, Maviza A, Ndagurwa GN
articles.details: Scoping review of 22 literature sources examining climate change impacts on malaria transmission and management in Zimbabwe. Most studies demonstrated climate change shifts geographic distribution, trends, timing, and intensity of malaria transmission. However, non-climatic factors including health systems, interventions, and human/ecological factors also shape transmission dynamics. Identified gaps include lack of longitudinal data, limited localized predictive modeling, and limited focus on climate-parasite dynamics. Concludes need for climate-informed programming and climate-resilient health systems.

articles.headline: Systematic review protocol on severe malaria progression in Africa
articles.title_original: Progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria among children in settings receiving different malaria control interventions in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review protocol.
articles.authors: Okek EJ, Lutwama J, Kinengyere AA, Asio J, Awor S, Le Doare K, Musinguzi B, Sande JO, Ocan M, Kayondo J
articles.details: Protocol for systematic review and meta-analysis examining progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria in sub-Saharan African children receiving concurrent malaria control interventions. Searches will include PubMed, Web of Science, Embase, Scopus, and Google Scholar. Will conduct narrative and quantitative synthesis with subgroup analysis exploring heterogeneity and impact of different control interventions on progression to severe malaria. Full review expected for dissemination by end of December 2025.

articles.headline: Mosquito-based transmission-blocking vaccines against malaria
articles.title_original: Mosquito-based transmission-blocking vaccine candidates for malaria: progress, challenges, and innovations.
articles.authors: Dahiya N, Yadav M, Kataria D, Janjoter S, Sehrawat N
articles.details: Review of transmission-blocking vaccine candidates targeting mosquitoes and parasites. Notes RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M vaccines show lesser efficacy than needed, motivating development of more promising vaccines. Reviews mosquito-based studies using antisera-raising methods to evaluate transmission-blocking efficacy, status of TBV antigens, challenges in development, and approaches for improving blocking efficacy. Concludes mosquito-based TBVs can effectively target diverse parasites and significantly reduce vector survival, offering promising transmission-blocking strategy.

articles.headline: Strain-specific antibody responses to P. falciparum antigens
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection with NF54 and 7G8 strains elicit differential antibody responses to Plasmodium falciparum peptides.
articles.authors: Friedman-Klabanoff DJ, Jensen TL, Lyke KE, Laurens MB, Silva JC, Stucke EM, Ouattara A, Ifeonu OO, Hodges T, Moser KA, Gelber CE, Goll JB, Hoffman SL, Patel JJ, Pinapati RS, Tan JC, Deye GA, Takala-Harrison S, Travassos MA, Berry AA
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection study in 38 malaria-naive adults infected with NF54 (n=21) or 7G8 (n=17) strains, with sera tested 28 days post-infection. Designed peptide microarray with 638,817 unique peptides representing 22,655 variants of 227 proteins from 23 P. falciparum genome assemblies and 379 field isolates. While antibody response intensity was similar between groups, 7G8-infected participants showed higher breadth of responses to 17 of 20 proteins with differential responses. Six of 543 predicted epitopes showed cross-reactivity >95%; ETRAMP10.2 identified as potential vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: El Niño-Southern Oscillation affects Amazon malaria risk
articles.title_original: El Niño-Southern Oscillation and shifting malaria risks in the Amazon.
articles.authors: Laporta GZ
articles.details: Study examining relationship between El Niño-Southern Oscillation climate patterns and malaria transmission risk in Amazon region.

articles.headline: Neurovascular pathophysiology and biomarkers in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Neurovascular Pathophysiology and Emerging Biomarkers in Cerebral Malaria: An Integrative Perspective.
articles.authors: Pikor D, Hurła M, Banaszek-Hurła N, Drelichowska A, Paul M
articles.details: Review synthesizing pathophysiology of cerebral malaria in P. falciparum infection. Describes cytoadherence of parasitized erythrocytes in cerebral microvasculature causing blood-brain barrier disruption, multifactorial cerebral edema, and elevated intracranial pressure. Highlights recent high-resolution MRI findings including basal ganglia infarcts, brainstem lesions, and corpus callosum abnormalities predicting poor outcomes. Identifies circulating extracellular vesicles as mediators of microvascular inflammation and emerging diagnostic/prognostic biomarkers. Discusses neuroprotective strategies and precision medicine approaches to prevent secondary neuronal injury and long-term disability.

articles.headline: P. vivax malaria severity and risk factors in Indian patients
articles.title_original: Clinical spectrum and complications of Plasmodium vivax malaria: A retrospective study from Delhi, India.
articles.authors: Singh D, Uddin A, Bajaj K, Chaturvedi A, Garg R, Chandra K, Das A, Kohli S, Jain V
articles.details: Retrospective study of 361 patients with P. vivax malaria between June 2020 and May 2024 at tertiary hospital in South Delhi, India. 167 (46.3%) developed complications. Common findings: anemia 73.4%, thrombocytopenia 57.9%, leucocytosis. Thrombocytopenia (OR 3.20, p<0.001) and leucocytosis (OR 2.37, p<0.05) associated with severity. Elevated creatinine (OR 6.07, p=0.001) and hyperbilirubinemia (OR 3.71, p<0.001) strongly correlated with complications. Females showed higher complication rates (42% vs 32%, p=0.039). Concludes P. vivax causes severe complications in nearly half of cases.

articles.headline: Advocacy for African representation in Global Fund governance
articles.title_original: It is time to increase Africa's governmental representation on the governing board of the global fund to fight AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria.
articles.authors: Nalugala RM, Kakoma J, Bridge G, von Agris JM, Hayes Badenhorst R, Fransen L, Brown GW
articles.details: Opinion piece advocating for increased African governmental representation on the governing board of the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria.

articles.headline: Malarial retinopathy diagnostic accuracy in pediatric cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Re-evaluating malarial retinopathy to improve its diagnostic accuracy in paediatric cerebral malaria: A retrospective study.
articles.authors: Wilson KJ, Muiruri Liomba A, Seydel KB, Banda OK, Moxon CA, MacCormick IJC, Harding SP, Beare NAV, Taylor TE
articles.details: Retrospective analysis from Blantyre, Malawi autopsy study (1996-2010) of 84 children, 65 meeting WHO clinical cerebral malaria criteria. 18 (28%) lacked cerebral parasite sequestration at autopsy. Malarial retinopathy sensitivity 89.4% (95% CI 77.6%-95.6%), specificity 73.0% (95% CI 57.2%-84.8%) for predicting sequestration. Reclassifying patients with only 1-5 hemorrhages in single eye as retinopathy-negative improved specificity to 88.0% (95% CI 70.4%-96.2%) and sensitivity to 94.3% (95% CI 81.7%-98.7%). Proposes management algorithm for retinopathy use in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: HLA-G polymorphism increases severe malaria risk in fortified children
articles.title_original: HLA-G 14 bp polymorphism: Implications and its associated risk to malaria severity among iron-fortified Ghanaian infants and young children.
articles.authors: Tetteh Danquah A, Courtin D, Asamoah Sakyi S, Nakotey GK, Tchum SK, Afriyie Duah N, Lagrave A, Sewor C, Amoani B
articles.details: Secondary analysis of double-blinded cluster randomized controlled trial with 432 archival samples from children aged 6-36 months in Tain District and Wenchi Municipal, Bono Region, Ghana. Examined HLA-G 14 bp insertion/deletion polymorphism effect on malaria severity in iron-fortified children. Iron-fortified children carrying HLA-G 14bp+/- and 14bp-/- variants had elevated risk of severe malaria. HLA-G 14bp+/- variant also associated with higher anemia risk in iron-supplemented participants. Concludes genetic polymorphisms interact with iron fortification to increase severe malaria susceptibility.

articles.headline: Novel Plasmodium diversity discovered in Vietnamese passerine birds
articles.title_original: Hidden Plasmodium diversity revealed in southeastern Asian passerines using next-generation amplicon sequencing.
articles.authors: Damnjanović D, Nazarizadeh M, Nováková M, Linh NV, Tolstenkov O, Kerimov A, Bushuev A, Štefka J
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Study using next-generation sequencing of cytochrome b gene identified Plasmodium species in three passerine species in Cat-Tien National Park, Vietnam. Found two known and ten novel Plasmodium lineages, with species delimitation methods identifying five distinct operational taxonomic units. Results suggest parasite distribution is filtered by host identity and ecology rather than representing uniform opportunistic infection.

articles.headline: P. vivax malaria with recurrent relapses despite primaquine treatment
articles.title_original: Recurrent Malaria with Plasmodium vivax: A Case Report and Brief Review of the Literature.
articles.authors: Andrejkovits ÁV, Pop AV, Fejér M, Gîrbovan EC, Coșeriu RL, Vintilă C, Văsieșiu AM
articles.details: Case report of 41-year-old from Romania with P. vivax malaria acquired in Southeast Asia, initially treated with doxycycline and atovaquone-proguanil. Experienced first relapse, received primaquine as radical cure, but suffered second relapse confirmed by RT-PCR. Case highlights importance of accurate species identification and challenges in preventing P. vivax relapses.

articles.headline: Correction to study of household practices and P. falciparum in Uganda
articles.title_original: Correction: Household practices and infrastructure associated with high Plasmodium falciparum infection rates among children under five years old in Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Echodu R, Nyeko C, Okot J, Iwiru T, Nakiwu V, Aryemo F, Ajolorwot S
articles.details: Correction article regarding household practices and infrastructure associated with P. falciparum infection in children under five in Northern Uganda.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention effective in reducing incidence and mortality
articles.title_original: Status and prospects of seasonal malaria chemoprevention among children in Sahelian countries: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Djedanem M, Salé NM, Aminou EYYM, Testa J, Jambou R
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine and amodiaquine monthly) in children under five in Sahelian countries.
