articles.title_original: Drug-drug interaction between dolutegravir and artemether-lumefantrine in HIV and malaria mono- and co-infections: a pharmacogenetic analysis from Ghana.
articles.authors: Thomford NE, Kellermann T, Twum JA, Anyimadu J, Dixon C, Sappor D, Blackhurst D, Barnie PA, Ryabinina O, Nyarko SB, Biney RP, Ekor M, Kyei GB
articles.details: Pharmacogenetic study from Ghana of 261 participants with HIV and/or malaria. Parasitemia day 1: malaria monoinfection 947.34 parasites/μL, HIV-malaria coinfection 5287.36 parasites/μL; decreased to 0 by days 3-7 in both groups. Lumefantrine day-7 plasma concentration: 741.5 ng/mL in malaria monoinfection vs 426.0 ng/mL when co-administered with dolutegravir (DTG). DTG concentrations also decreased during coinfection; CYP3A5, CYP3A4, UGT1A1, and CYP2B6 variants affected drug disposition.

articles.headline: HIV-malaria research shows rising trend with focus on drug interactions
articles.title_original: Unraveling the HIV-malaria interactions: a bibliometric analysis of global research trends and emerging insights.
articles.authors: Zhang H, Chen G, Ma P, Lai Y
articles.details: Bibliometric analysis of 4,717 articles on HIV-malaria interactions published 1995-2024 using CiteSpace software. Results show fluctuating upward pattern in publication numbers. Research hotspots identified: epidemiological studies in African populations, pathogenic mechanisms of co-infection, prevention and treatment strategies, interactions between antimalarial and antiretroviral drugs, and malaria vaccine responses in HIV-infected individuals.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury in 49% of severe malaria with 3.6× mortality risk
articles.title_original: Prevalence, Associated Factors, and Prognosis of Acute Kidney Injury in Severe Malaria Among Sub-Saharan Africans.
articles.authors: Mbengue M, Cheikh Gaye A, Tshabayembi J, Fall S, Sall I, Diouf M, Keita N, Gaye I, Seydi M, Fary Ka EH, Cristiano F, Niang A
articles.details: Multicenter retrospective study (2019-2023) of 321 severe malaria patients; 158 had acute kidney injury (AKI), prevalence 49.2%. AKI presentations: anuria 64.3%, oliguria 26.8%, edema 7.14%. Independent AKI-associated factors: herbal medicine (OR 1.509, p=0.045), diabetes (OR 3.375, p=0.044), respiratory distress (OR 2.758, p=0.034), anemia protective (OR 0.407, p=0.002). AKI independently increased death risk (OR 3.584, p=0.004).

articles.headline: Artemether clears parasites faster than quinine in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Comparison of Artemether Versus Quinine for the Treatment of Cerebral Malaria in Children: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.
articles.authors: Saeed H, Tahir D, Khan MM, Faheem MT, Malik AH, Saeed S, Azeem S, Syed M, Allami A
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 7 randomized controlled trials comparing artemether (n=618) versus quinine (n=607) for cerebral malaria in children. Parasite clearance time significantly faster with artemether (mean difference -7.63 hours, 95% CI -11.06 to -4.21, P<0.0001). Fever clearance, coma clearance, and mortality showed no statistically significant differences. Artemether demonstrated mildly superior efficacy.

articles.headline: PfCSP polymorphism in P. falciparum from Congo children
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of circumsporozoite protein of Plasmodium falciparum isolates in children in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Baina MT, Djontu JC, Ntabi JDM, Lissom A, Eouasse AGK, Elenga VA, Mapanguy CCM, Mouanga AM, Ntoumi F
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 202 P. falciparum-infected children from March-October 2021. Full-length Pfcsp gene sequenced via Oxford Nanopore. Identified 30 N-terminal haplotypes (A98G most frequent at 25.6%), 50 central-repeat haplotypes, and 76 C-terminal haplotypes with high polymorphism (K317E 87.4%, N321K 83.9%; E357Q 75.9%). Authors conclude baseline data highlights genetic variation in vaccine-targeted PfCSP epitopes.

articles.headline: Climate change impacts malaria transmission in Africa review
articles.title_original: Impact of climate change on malaria transmission in Africa: A scoping review of literature.
articles.authors: Chapoterera B, Naidoo K, Marume A
articles.details: Systematic review of 90 studies (2010-2024) examining climate-malaria relationships in Africa. Findings show temperature increases accelerate mosquito vector and parasite development affecting transmission, while rainfall pattern changes affect breeding sites and malaria incidence. Authors conclude climate-responsive malaria control strategies integrating ecological data are needed for effective interventions across Africa's diverse ecosystems.

articles.headline: Garlic extract highly insecticidal against malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Insecticidal Activities of Plants Extract Against Malaria Vectors in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Assemie A, Mulu D, Getahun A, Awoke N, Muluken W, Enyew T, Gebremariam A, Aynalem Y
articles.details: Laboratory study tested ethanol extracts of neem and garlic against malaria vectors at 50-250 ppm concentrations. Garlic (A. sativum) showed highest mortality at 90.66% (250 ppm) with lowest LC50 of 39 ppm. Garlic had significant effect on Anopheles gambiae s.l. (p = 0.008687) and An. pharoensis (p = 0.02655); neem only significant against An. pharoensis (p = 0.00651). Authors conclude garlic extract more effective than neem in vector reduction.

articles.headline: Chromatin dynamics coordinate P. falciparum gene expression
articles.title_original: Chromatin state dynamics during the Plasmodium falciparum intraerythrocytic development cycle.
articles.authors: Brown AS Jr, Llinás M, Mahony S
articles.details: Study characterized chromatin accessibility, histone modifications, and transcription factor binding throughout P. falciparum's 48-hour asexual intraerythrocytic developmental cycle. Results show stable chromatin states and dynamic states linked to gene activation/repression, with transcription factors grouped by chromatin preferences. Authors demonstrate coordinated chromatin state dynamics governing parasite blood-stage development.

articles.headline: Combined IPTp and ITNs better prevent malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Do women attending antenatal clinics who use only intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp) have higher Plasmodium falciparum prevalence compared to those who used both IPTp and slept under insecticide-treated nets?
articles.authors: Madukwe JC, Ahaneku EB, Onukafor OS, Edokpayi S, Madukwe PC, Enwereji NU, Santos EM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 143 pregnant women from hospitals in Nigeria assessed IPTp and ITN usage and P. falciparum prevalence. P. falciparum detected in 45% overall; 61.2% among IPTp-only users vs 17.0% among IPTp+ITN users. Logistic regression showed IPTp+ITN users had 86% reduced odds of infection compared to IPTp alone, with reduction driven largely by ITN use. Authors conclude ITN usage is vital for reducing malaria in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Epigenetic mechanism of artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Epigenetically conferred ring-stage survival in Plasmodium falciparum against artemisinin treatment.
articles.authors: Yu X, He J, Wang C, Mu J, Chen X, Zhao Y, He X, Liu S, Sa JM, Platon L, Tang J, Si W, Tang R, Menard D, Wellems TE, Jiang C, Cao J, Zhang Q
articles.details: Systematic evaluation of histone acetyltransferases in 73 P. falciparum isolates revealed PfMYST as sole factor fully associated with ring-stage survival phenotype linked to artemisinin partial resistance. Knockdown experiments, single-cell RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, and CRISPR validation identified PfMYST-targeted genes and show PfMYST-PfK13 mechanistic interplay. PfMYST epigenetic modulation extends beyond artemisinin to amodiaquine, pyrimethamine, chloroquine, and pyronaridine.

articles.headline: Novel PI4K inhibitors show antimalarial efficacy in mouse model
articles.title_original: Novel Inhibitors of Plasmodium Phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase IIIβ with Low Propensity for Resistance: Life Cycle Stage Activity and In Vivo Efficacy in a Humanized Mouse Malaria Infection Model.
articles.authors: Dziwornu GA, Mmonwa MM, Coertzen D, Krugmann L, Salomane N, Leshabane M, Thomas J, da Rocha S, Reader J, Masike K, Njoroge M, Sevilleno N, Coyle R, Boonyalai N, Mayville E, Lee MCS, Fidock DA, Coulson LB, Woodland JG, Wicht KJ, Ghorpade SR, Birkholtz LM, Chibale K
articles.details: Novel antimalarial based on ATM kinase inhibitor AZD0156 targets phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase IIIβ, showing activity against all P. falciparum life cycle stages with lower resistance propensity and toxicity than previous PI4K inhibitors. Lead compound 18 demonstrated efficacy in NOD-scid IL-2Rγnull mouse model with ED90 of 4.6 mg/kg. Authors present PI4K as a target for antimalarial development with reduced resistance risk.

articles.headline: ARK1 regulates spindle formation in Plasmodium parasites
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.authors: Nagar A, Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Abel S, Pashley SL, Mishra A, Eze A, Rea E, Brady D, Bottrill AR, Vaughan S, Le Roch KG, Guttery DS, Holder AA, Tromer EC, Sharma P, Tewari R
articles.details: Study identified Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as critical for inner centrosome and spindle formation in Plasmodium, regulating kinetochore dynamics and mitotic progression. Conditional ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle biogenesis, kinetochore segregation, and cytokinesis in asexual and sexual stages, affecting parasite transmission. Interactome analysis revealed ARK1 as catalytic core of a divergent chromosomal passenger complex lacking canonical centromere-targeting modules. Authors propose ARK1-INCENP interface as potential multistage therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Salacia debilis compounds target P. falciparum enoyl-ACP reductase
articles.title_original: Computational elucidation of Plasmodium falciparum enoyl-ACP reductase inhibition by Salacia debilis compounds: adaptive ligand binding drives antimalarial activity.
articles.authors: Manu P, Padiga Seidu M, Adomako A, Asamoah CK, Duodu O, Britwum A, Laryea MK, Darko G, Borquaye LS
articles.details: Identified three Salacia debilis compounds (SD03, SD04, SD05) as inhibitors of P. falciparum enoyl-acyl carrier protein reductase, enzyme in parasite type II fatty acid biosynthesis absent in humans. Molecular docking and 500 ns molecular dynamics simulations revealed binding affinities of -8.50 to -8.90 kcal/mol with stable ligand-enzyme complexes matching benchmark triclosan. SD04 emerged as lead candidate based on pharmacokinetic predictions. Authors validate PfENR as target for selective antimalarial development.

articles.headline: HIV infection factors in Mozambique youth demographics
articles.title_original: Factors associated with HIV infection among adolescents and young people in Mozambique: sub-analysis of immunization, malaria and HIV/AIDS indicator survey in Mozambique-IMASIDA 2015.
articles.authors: Chicanequisso EM, Sabonete A, Sacarlal J, Rossetto ÉV, Cassimo MN, Baltazar CS
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 4,247 youth aged 15-24 from 2015 Mozambique survey. Overall HIV prevalence 6.9% (females 9.8%). Male risk: widower status (AOR 52.69, p = 0.02). Female risks: age 20-24 (AOR 2.06, p < 0.001), widowed (AOR 15.59), early cohabitation <18 years (AOR 1.71, p = 0.01), Zambézia residence (AOR 3.52, p = 0.04), higher wealth (AOR 2.20, p = 0.02). Higher education protective (AOR 0.07, p = 0.02).

articles.headline: L-DOPA reduces mosquito lifespan and malaria burden
articles.title_original: Dietary L-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (L-DOPA) augments cuticular melanization in Anopheles mosquitos reducing their lifespan and malaria burden.
articles.authors: Camacho E, Dong Y, Chrissian C, Cordero RJB, Saraiva RG, Anglero-Rodriguez Y, Smith DFQ, Jacobs E, Hartshorn I, Patiño-Medina JA, DePasquale M, Dziedzic A, Jedlicka A, Smith B, Mlambo G, Tripathi A, Broderick NA, Stark RE, Dimopoulos G, Casadevall A
articles.details: Laboratory study investigated dietary L-DOPA effects on Anopheles mosquito physiology and Plasmodium falciparum immunity. Dietary L-DOPA incorporated into mosquito melanin via non-canonical pathway, triggering enhanced immunity, increased pigmentation, and reduced lifespan. Increased melanization enhanced electromagnetic radiation absorption affecting mosquito thermoregulation. Authors suggest dietary L-DOPA as potential environmentally friendly vector control agent to reduce malaria transmission, warranting field validation.

articles.headline: Anopheles distribution and bioclimatic niches in malaria-free Paraguay
articles.title_original: Unravelling Anopheles Dynamics in a Malaria-Free Paraguay: Species Distributions, Bioclimatic Niches, and Implications for Resurgence Risks.
articles.authors: Del Puerto F, Grissetti M, Ferreira L, Franco L, Herrera L
articles.details: Entomological survey during 2016-2017 in Paraguay found Anopheles albitarsis (59.4%) and An. strodei (21.5%) most prevalent, all negative for Plasmodium spp. Bioclimatic modeling using MaxEnt identified suitable habitats in Humid Chaco, Cerrado, Atlantic Forest, and Mesopotamian savanna ecoregions, with precipitation variables (wettest month 80.4%, driest month 18.4% contribution) most relevant. An. darlingi, the region's primary malaria vector, was not found. Authors note species richness indices suggest possible cryptic species presence.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence increases in Madagascar between 2023 and 2024
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Malaria in Northern Madagascar as Exemplified by the Mampikony District.
articles.authors: Kołodziej D, Wilczyńska W, Kasprowicz D, Marchelek-Myśliwiec M, Korzeniewski K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 484 patients in 2023 and 298 in 2024 from Mampikony District used mRDTs and RT-PCR. Malaria prevalence increased from 4.5% (mRDTs) and 8.2% (RT-PCR) in 2023 to 8.4% (mRDTs) and 12.4% (RT-PCR) in 2024. Predominantly P. falciparum with P. vivax and mixed infections. RT-PCR more sensitive than mRDTs. Authors recommend more sensitive diagnostic tools and reassessment of regional treatment strategies.

articles.headline: Reduced mathematical model preserves malaria control dynamics efficiently
articles.title_original: Model reduction and analysis: A case study of a malaria control model.
articles.authors: Korsah MA, Johnston ST, Tiedje K, Day K, Walker CR, Flegg JA
articles.details: Study employed reduction techniques on comprehensive malaria control model, deriving simplified system preserving essential dynamics. Validated accuracy via Bayesian MCMC, parameter identifiability analysis, and sensitivity analysis. Reduced system exhibited similar transmission characteristics as comprehensive model while providing greater flexibility and computational efficiency. Useful tool for evaluating and implementing malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Vaccine-evolved parasites show reduced immune killing in mice
articles.title_original: Understanding the traits underlying vaccine-driven virulence evolution in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Jeong Y, Kamiya T, Mideo N
articles.details: Experimental study comparing parasites evolved in vaccinated versus sham-vaccinated mice using hierarchical Bayesian framework fitted to within-host infection dynamics. Vaccine-evolved parasites showed lower targeted immune killing and lower anemia-driven erythropoiesis. Early-infection parasite multiplication rates showed no differences between treatments. Parasite modulation of host responses rather than target antigen evolution influences disease severity.

articles.headline: Neutrophil extracellular traps drive cerebral malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: TLR9/NF-κB-mediated dendritic cell activation by neutrophil extracellular traps drives pathogenesis in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Yao S, Zhao Y, Yao C, Li Q, Sun M, Wang Q, Zheng L, Cao Y
articles.details: Murine cerebral malaria model using Plasmodium berghei ANKA showed rapid neutrophil mobilization. Neutrophil depletion protected mice from neuropathology with reduced brain CD8+ T cell infiltration and attenuated dendritic cell activation. NETs activated dendritic cells via TLR9/NF-κB signaling, upregulating costimulatory molecules and proinflammatory cytokines. Sivelestat inhibition of NETs impeded cerebral malaria onset and progression.

articles.headline: Yemen's malaria crisis implications for Gulf Cooperation Council
articles.title_original: Yemen's Malaria Crisis and its Implications for the GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council) Countries.
articles.authors: Ebrahim SH, Al-Maslamani M, Al-Hosani F, Al-Abri S, Alqahtani M, Alahmad B, Bland S, Memish Z
articles.details: No abstract available; article addresses Yemen's malaria crisis and implications for neighboring Gulf Cooperation Council countries.

articles.headline: Broadly inhibitory anti-PfEMP1 antibodies identified from malaria-exposed child
articles.title_original: Identification of broadly inhibitory anti-PfEMP1 antibodies by mass spectrometry sequencing of plasma IgG from a malaria-exposed child.
articles.authors: Turner L, Nunez de Villavicencio Diaz T, Raghavan SSR, Kana IH, Lyimo E, Reitzel C, Wang CW, Berube E, Jensen RW, Loeffler JR, Fernández-Quintero ML, Theander TG, Lusingu JPA, Le Bihan T, Han X, Minja DTR, Ward AB, Ma B, Lavstsen T
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Mass spectrometry analysis of plasma IgG from 9-year-old malaria-exposed child identified broadly reactive monoclonal antibodies against CIDRα1 domains of PfEMP1. Antibodies potently inhibited EPCR binding across CIDRα1 variants and infected erythrocyte binding to EPCR. Structural analysis revealed shared epitope overlapping EPCR binding site. Findings support broadly inhibitory antibodies develop in malaria-exposed individuals via conserved epitopes.

articles.headline: MicroRNA panel predicts cerebral malaria with high accuracy
articles.title_original: Potential of microRNAs as diagnostic markers for distinguishing malaria severity in samples from an Indian cohort.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Arora K, Bhandari S, Sikka R, Wassmer SC, Bharti PK, Gupta H
articles.details: Case-control study quantifying plasma microRNAs in cerebral malaria (n=43), severe non-cerebral malaria (n=50), uncomplicated malaria (n=79), asymptomatic malaria (n=80), and non-malarial febrile illness (n=69) using TaqMan-RT-qPCR. Three microRNAs (hsa-miR-21-5p, hsa-miR-150-5p, hsa-miR-3158-3p) significantly correlated with severe and cerebral malaria (p<0.0005). Random forest model predicted cerebral malaria with 83% sensitivity, 100% specificity, 92% balanced accuracy.

articles.headline: Triple artemisinin-combination therapies most effective against drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: Effects of recombination on multi-drug resistance evolution in Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Tran KT, Nguyen TD, Weissman DB, Li EZ, Mok S, Small-Saunders JL, Bousema T, Zupko RJ, Tran TN, Boni MF
articles.details: Transmission model incorporating 25 drug-resistance loci and recombination from multi-clonal infections and interrupted feeds. 80% to 97% of multi-drug resistant falciparum genotypes arose from single bites on multi-clonal infection hosts. Over 15-year timeframe, triple artemisinin-combination therapy (ACT) showed largest treatment failure reductions and longest delay to 1% artemisinin resistance. Multiple first-line therapy second best; ACT cycling third best.

articles.headline: Airport and autochthonous malaria in Europe, 1969-2022
articles.title_original: Airport/seaport and autochthonous malaria in Europe from 1969 to 2022: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Balzli D, Gültekin N, Stanga Z, Ülgür I, Hedrich N, Fehr J, Schlagenhauf P
articles.details: Systematic review searching PubMed, Ovid MEDLINE, EMBASE, Scopus, and CINAHL identified 68 papers describing 115 cases: 68 airport/seaport and 47 autochthonous malaria cases in Europe. Case fatality rates 13.24% for airport/seaport versus 2.13% for autochthonous malaria. France, Germany, Italy, Spain reported both types; Belgium, Luxembourg, Switzerland, UK reported airport/seaport only; Greece, Malta, Netherlands reported autochthonous only. High fatality attributed to delayed diagnosis.

articles.headline: High malaria prevalence in pregnant women in Somalia
articles.title_original: The Prevalence of Malaria Among Pregnant Women at a Tertiary Care Hospital in Somalia: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Mohamud SM, Doğan S, Hussein AI, Mohamud RYH, Orhan Z, Doğaner A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study November 2022 to January 2023 screened 398 pregnant women at tertiary hospital in Mogadishu for malaria via blood smear microscopy. 238/398 (59.8%) tested positive; 218/238 (91.6%) were P. falciparum. Second trimester (OR 0.524, p=0.044), third trimester (OR 0.442, p=0.007), and indoor residual spraying (OR 0.192, p<0.001) associated with reduced malaria odds. Authors recommend targeted education and routine screening at antenatal visits.

articles.headline: Signal-dependent exocytosis precedes parasitophorous vacuole rupture in malaria
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal dynamics of signal dependent exocytosis and parasitophorous vacuolar membrane rupture during Plasmodium falciparum egress.
articles.authors: Dedkhad W, Tran T, Fierro MA, Brooks C, Muralidharan V
articles.details: Live-cell imaging of P. falciparum using fluorescent reporters for egress-specific vesicles and parasitophorous vacuolar membrane. Time-lapse imaging showed signal-dependent exocytosis triggered 3 hours before merozoite egress. Parasitophorous vacuolar membrane ruptured at single site, rapidly expanding to release merozoites into red blood cell. Conditional PfERC mutants showed knockdown inhibited signal-dependent exocytosis. Exocytosis begins before merozoite cytokinesis; PfERC required for egress vesicle exocytosis.

articles.headline: PEXEL and non-PEXEL protein export mechanisms in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: An overview of the interconnectedness of the PEXEL and non-PEXEL mediated protein export in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Das S
articles.details: Review summarizing protein export mechanisms in P. falciparum during asexual phase in hepatocytes and red blood cells. Discusses PEXEL (Plasmodium Export Element), conserved pentameric motif in most exported proteins, and PEXEL-negative exported proteins (PNEPs) following similar trafficking routes. Covers structural diversity of characterized PNEPs and functions. Synthesizes current understanding of protein export mechanisms and discusses remaining questions in P. falciparum and related apicomplexans.

articles.headline: P. falciparum-dengue coinfection in febrile children, western Kenya
articles.title_original: Correlation and epidemiologic distribution of emerging coinfections of Plasmodium falciparum and dengue virus among febrile children in malaria-endemic zones in western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Mangeni J, Ayodo G, Menya D, Akinyi I, Oyugi B, Yongo A, Okumu F, Lwanga C, Oluoch F, Karanja S
articles.details: Prospective cohort study recruiting 380 febrile children <5 years (from 1004 screened) testing positive for malaria and/or dengue at public health facilities in Kisumu and Busia counties. P. falciparum prevalence 21.4% (215/1004), dengue 8.9% (90/1004), coinfections 7.5% (75/1004). Busia had higher P. falciparum-only prevalence (31%, 118/380) while Kisumu had higher dengue-only (16.6%, 63/380). Disease distribution varied significantly by location (χ²=19.45, p<0.001), suggesting active dengue transmission in this malaria-endemic region.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge low in rural Ethiopian communities
articles.title_original: Malaria signs, symptoms, prevention knowledge and its associated factors among rural Ethiopians.
articles.authors: Kuti KA, Zuma SM
articles.journal: Afr J Prim Health Care Fam Med
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 634 adults (age ≥18) in three malaria-endemic rural districts in Bale Zone, Ethiopia. Only 44.2% demonstrated good understanding of malaria signs, symptoms, and prevention. Many incorrectly attributed transmission to sun exposure, lack of rest, or alcohol. Education, religion, marital status, family size, and presence of children/pregnant women were associated with better malaria knowledge. Study emphasizes need for targeted health education to improve community understanding and support elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic inequality in malaria prevention uptake persists, Ghana
articles.title_original: Between-and within-socioeconomic groups temporal inequality in the uptake of malaria prevention strategies among pregnant women and under-five children in Ghana (2003-2022).
articles.authors: Okova D, Lukwa AT, Edusei M, Bodzo P, Atta-Obeng C, Chiwire P, Hongoro C
articles.details: Analysis of nationally representative data from Ghana Demographic and Health Surveys (2003, 2008, 2014, 2022) examining ITN and IPTp coverage inequalities. ITN use among children <5 increased modestly from 58.1% (2003) to 62.9% (2022); among pregnant women fluctuated but returned to 60% (2022). IPTp coverage rose markedly from 0.85% (2003) to 60% (2022). Despite improvements, substantial within-group disparities persisted, with 85% of ITN inequality among pregnant women in 2022 driven by within-socioeconomic-status differences.

articles.headline: Diabetes and metabolic syndrome increase severe P. falciparum risk
articles.title_original: Diabetes and metabolic syndrome in adults with malaria and associations with severe disease: results from two tertiary hospitals in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Awungafac G, Njonnou SRS, Noubom M, Libon LIAA, Gesu CN, Färnert A, Choukem SP, Wyss K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 289 adults with P. falciparum malaria at two tertiary hospitals in Cameroon. Of 289 patients, 120 (41.6%) had severe malaria. Diabetes was present in 21.7% (26/120) of severe vs 6.0% (10/169) non-severe cases (p<0.001). Metabolic syndrome in 32.5% (39/120) severe vs 16.3% (27/166) non-severe cases (p=0.001). In multivariable analysis, diabetes (aOR=3.24, 95% CI 1.38-7.62) and metabolic syndrome (aOR=3.18, 95% CI 1.60-6.37) were independently associated with severe malaria.

articles.headline: Anemia more prevalent in HIV-malaria coinfection than HIV alone
articles.title_original: Hematological Profiles of Adults Coinfected With HIV and Malaria Receiving Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy at Bonga Gebretsadik Shawo General Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia: A Comparative Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Demango F, Tadasa E, Kiya GT
articles.details: Hospital-based comparative cross-sectional study of 196 HIV-infected adults (98 HIV-only, 98 HIV-malaria coinfected). Anemia prevalence was 61.2% (60/98) in coinfected vs 28.6% (28/98) in HIV-only group (p<0.001). Leukopenia 43.88% (43/98) coinfected vs 34.7% (34/98) HIV-only; thrombocytopenia 30.6% (30/98) coinfected vs 20.4% (20/98) HIV-only. Viral load negatively correlated with hematological parameters in both groups; parasite density negatively correlated with red cell indices and platelets in coinfected patients.

articles.headline: Phase-based digital holography improves P. falciparum parasite detection
articles.title_original: Enhanced detection of malaria infected red blood cells through phase driven classification.
articles.authors: Kyeremah C, Paul AS, Haehn D, Duraisingh MT, Yelleswarapu CS
articles.details: Study using lensless inline digital holographic microscopy (DHM) for label-free detection of P. falciparum-infected red blood cells. Phase-based features classification detected parasitemia at 61% sensitivity vs 48% for feature-specific methods on same samples, with 98-100% specificity. Detection rate was slightly lower than Giemsa staining (90%) but offers advantage as real-time, label-free automated imaging tool for rapid malaria diagnosis.

articles.headline: Poor LLIN washing practices reduce net durability in Dominican Republic
articles.title_original: Insights from LLIN post-distribution monitoring surveys in the malaria transmission foci of the Dominican Republic: implications for quantification and distribution strategies.
articles.authors: Ventura G, Félix MYS, Bueno NT, Ströfer NM, Raposo JLC, Solís Á, Parra RGB, Montoya LF
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey of LLIN coverage and use in two Dominican Republic malaria foci (Azua, 4-6 months post-distribution; San Juan, one year post-distribution). Overall LLIN use was 32.3% (Azua) and 50.5% (San Juan); among those with LLIN access, use was 48.8% (Azua) and 75% (San Juan). Most nets remained serviceable (96.4% Azua, 88.9% San Juan), but aggressive washing (65-86%) and sun-drying (75-90%) were prevalent. Recommend improved behavioral change strategies and revisions to net quantification methods.

articles.headline: Aspartyl protease inhibition reduces P. falciparum asexual and early gametocyte
articles.title_original: Aspartyl protease inhibition interferes with Plasmodium falciparum asexual blood-stage and early gametocyte development.
articles.authors: Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Jenwithisuk R, Roobsoong W, Sattabongkot J, Pankao V, Kangwanrangsan N
articles.details: In vitro study of pepstatin A (broad-spectrum aspartyl protease inhibitor) on P. falciparum developmental stages. At 100 µM, pepstatin A inhibited asexual stage development by 47% and early-stage gametocyte development by 73%, with morphological defects (chromatin condensation, vacuolization, hemozoin clumping). Late-stage gametocytes and gamete formation were unaffected. Authors suggest aspartyl protease inhibition may reduce disease severity and transmission, though further research needed to develop clinically effective drugs.

articles.headline: Irrigated urban agriculture drives malaria transmission in Accra
articles.title_original: Urban malaria in Accra, Ghana: transmission risk and influence of irrigated vegetable farms.
articles.authors: Sabtiu ARM, Sraku IK, Owusu-Asenso CM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Doe RT, Boadu EN, Azumah JD, Eyeson NAS, Abdulai A, Amoateng G, Attah SK, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
articles.details: Cross-sectional entomology survey collecting 41,747 mosquitoes via human landing catch and Prokopack aspiration in Accra. Anopheles constituted 52.54% of host-seeking mosquitoes; Irrigated Urban Farming sites had highest An. gambiae s.l. abundance (48.63%, 10,466/21,520). Indoor An. gambiae s.l. showed higher sporozoite infectivity (4.56%, 16/351) than outdoor (3.78%, 9/238) (p=0.009). Tuba urban irrigated site had highest sporozoite infectivity (6.41%, 10/156). Indoor transmission exceeded outdoor, especially in irrigated farming areas. Targeted indoor control and habitat management in IUF areas recommended.

articles.headline: Community case management of malaria effective but limited by stockouts
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers of Community Case management of Malaria implementation in Homabay, Busia and Kakamega Counties, Kenya.
articles.authors: Lumumba S, Luciani A, Sifuma B, Kinyua D, Gikunda G, Ogutu N, Onyango J, Mukami D, Kiilu C, Kassim S, Opanga Y, Githuka G
articles.details: Mixed-methods cross-sectional study in three Kenyan counties surveying 528 households plus 4 focus groups and 20 key informant interviews. 72% of households received Community Case Management of Malaria services (Busia 75%, Homabay 72%, Kakamega 71%); 62% regarded community health volunteers as regular healthcare source, with ~85% satisfied. Facilitators included commodity availability, functional referral systems, and CHV supervision. Barriers included myths about nets, stockouts of mRDT kits and antimalarials, and inaccessible roads.

articles.headline: Malaria parasitemia 14.6% in pregnant women, Cameroon
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of malaria in pregnant women attending antenatal consultation in Dschang, West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Ebai CB, Ngoufo FN, Teh RN, Kodjo JTS, Muyang EJ, Kimbi HK
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 314 pregnant women at two health facilities in Dschang, Western Cameroon. 46 (14.6%) tested positive for malaria parasitemia; all were P. falciparum. Bushes around houses increased infection risk (OR=2.40, p=0.03). Protective factors: ceiling presence (OR=0.20, p<0.01), IPTp use (OR=0.23, p<0.01), window screens (OR=0.14, p=0.01). Geometric mean parasite density highest in second trimester (2190/µL), those with >3 gravidities (1022/µL), those with sweating (1946/µL), and LLIN users (1536/µL).

articles.headline: Indigofera oblongifolia reduces P. chabaudi-induced lung inflammation in mice
articles.title_original: Morphometric analysis and immunobiological investigation of Indigofera oblongifolia on the infected lung with Plasmodium chabaudi.
articles.authors: Murshed M, Al-Tamimi J, Ebaid H, Al-Quraishy S
articles.details: Experimental study in 50 female C57BL/6 mice comparing Indigofera oblongifolia leaf extract with silver nanoparticles (IOLE AgNPs) and chloroquine (CQ, 10 mg/kg) for treating P. chabaudi-induced lung inflammation. IOLE AgNPs significantly restored normal lung tissue architecture and reduced alveolar/septal congestion, edema, and necrosis vs infected untreated controls. Immunohistochemistry showed IOLE AgNPs reduced TNF-α and IL-6 expression more effectively than chloroquine, which showed limited efficiency with persistent histological abnormalities. Results suggest IOLE potential as antimalarial alternative.

articles.headline: Real-time malaria detection in Amazon rainforest via drone-collected eDNA qPCR
articles.title_original: Real-time malaria detection in the Amazon rainforest via drone-collected eDNA and portable qPCR.
articles.authors: Ip YCA, Montemartini L, Chang JJM, Desiderato A, Franco-Sierra ND, Geckeler C, Herrera MAG, Gregorini M, Jucker M, Kirchgeorg S, Lüthi M, Mächler E, Thostrup FB, Murari G, Mura M, Pulido-Santacruz P, Sangermano F, Schindler T, Melvad C, Mintchev S, Deiner K
articles.details: This proof-of-concept study coupled drone-based environmental DNA (eDNA) canopy swabbing with portable quantitative PCR to detect Plasmodium in the Amazon rainforest. Of 12 samples analyzed (10 canopy swabs, 2 field blanks), 1 canopy swab amplified in duplicate (Ct = 28.7 and 29.23) with a detection limit of 0.2 parasites μL⁻¹. The workflow from drone deployment to qPCR diagnostic readout averaged 1.5 h per assay without requiring cold-chain logistics, enabling landscape-level intelligence for vector surveillance in remote settings.

articles.headline: Night watchmen, construction workers show highest malaria risk in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Occupations at high risk for malaria in Zanzibar: a case-control study, may-august 2023.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Gallalee S, Mkali H, Khamis MI, Ramadhan A, Ali MH, Hassan WS, Hassan J, Makwaruzi S, Mgata S, Gulaka M, Govella NJ, Molteni F, Kitojo C, Reaves E, Ballard SB, Serbantez N, Ikonje A, Afenu M, Mkude S, Smith JL, Mohamed S, Shija SJ, Gueye CS, Gosling R
articles.details: A case-control study in Zanzibar from May–August 2023 enrolled 197 cases and 557 age- and sex-matched controls. In urban districts, night watchmen/police (OR 5.3, 95% CI 2.7–10.6), construction workers (OR 3.0, 95% CI 1.8–5.0), and farmers (OR 1.6, 95% CI 1.1–2.2) had significantly higher odds of malaria infection. Night-time activities (OR 2.8), eating meals outside (OR 2.0), and recent travel within Zanzibar (OR 3.3) were additional risk factors in urban areas, while net use was protective in rural districts (p = 0.015).

articles.headline: P47 receptor mutation marks Anopheles stephensi outbreak propensity in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Genetic surveillance of Plasmodium-Anopheles compatibility markers during Anopheles stephensi associated malaria outbreak.
articles.authors: Waymire E, Getachew D, Gunarathna I, Spear J, Lloyd G, Follis M, Kaye AA, Ali S, Yared S, Carter TE
articles.details: This genetic surveillance study analyzed P47 and P47Rec sequences in Anopheles stephensi during a 2022 malaria outbreak in Dire Dawa, Ethiopia. Of 160 mosquitoes screened, 6.21% were positive for Plasmodium falciparum DNA and 4.37% for P. vivax DNA. Analysis revealed that P. falciparum strains exhibited only the African haplotype, while a single amino acid change in P47Rec within An. stephensi could serve as a molecular marker for outbreak propensity.

articles.headline: Temperature and nutrition stress alter vector competence of Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Effects of temperature and nutrition stress on Anopheles stephensi, an Indian urban malaria vector.
articles.authors: Ghosh C, Kumar N, Mogaveerthi S, Lamba S, Ramanjini CK, Joshi SG, Chandran P, Kumar S, Swain S
articles.details: A laboratory study exposed Anopheles stephensi to low temperature (18±1°C), high temperature (35.5±1°C), or low nutrition (33 mg/100 larvae) for 8 generations. High-temperature and low-nutrition lines showed decreased fecundity, egg hatchability, developmental time, and adult longevity, developing faster (7.5–11 days vs. ~25 days for low-temperature lines). Oocyst infection rates of Plasmodium falciparum increased 1.46-fold in high-temperature lines, 1.28-fold in low-temperature lines, and 1.15-fold in low-nutrition lines compared to control.

articles.headline: DHIS2 malaria data concordance in Uganda below WHO standards
articles.title_original: Concordance of data on key malaria indicators between DHIS2 and source documents, and influencing factors at public primary health facilities in eastern Uganda: a mixed methods study.
articles.authors: Nuwasiima S, Mpimbaza A, Muteebwa L, Nagawa E, Arinaitwe E, Kiberu F, Ejalu DL, Mugerwa J, Batte C, Mukisa J, Agaba B, Mukunya D, Kalyango JN, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI
articles.details: A mixed-methods study in Mayuge district, Uganda assessed concordance between DHIS2 and facility source documents across 12 health facilities. Overall data concordance ranged from 36.7% (suspected cases, 95% CI 25.2–49.9) to 56.8% for test positivity rate (95% CI 43.9–68.8). Presence of Health Management Information System personnel (aPR 1.41, 95% CI 1.20–1.66) and timely reporting (aPR 1.15, 95% CI 1.00–1.31) improved concordance, while rapid diagnostic test stock-out (aPR 0.55, 95% CI 0.35–0.86) reduced it.

articles.headline: Temperature-dependent age-structured malaria model validated with 6-year Jimma data
articles.title_original: Age-structured malaria model with temperature-dependent dynamics and optimal control analysis within a partial differential equation framework.
articles.authors: Petros LD, Abdisa TE, Etefa DT, Menbiko DK, Gizaw AK, Simma EA, Yewhalaw D, Deressa CT
articles.details: This study presents a temperature-dependent, age-structured malaria transmission model using partial differential equations with optimal control strategies. The model was empirically validated using 6 years of infection prevalence data from the Jimma zone, achieving R² = 0.68 and adjusted R² = 0.63. Numerical simulations incorporating temperature variations and optimal controls demonstrated superior predictive accuracy compared to existing age-structured models, offering improved prediction and control strategies under varying environmental conditions.

articles.headline: Climate and vegetation increase malaria incidence in Tanzania despite ITN expansion
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of climatic and environmental variables on malaria incidence in Tanzania: Implications for achieving the WHO's 2030 Targets.
articles.authors: Lutambi AM, Emidi B, Mbuya FG, Ryoba M, Sagamiko TD, Hugo AK, Mbalawata IS
articles.details: A geostatistical Bayesian model using Demographic and Health Survey data assessed malaria incidence trends in Tanzania from 2000–2020. Mean malaria incidence decreased from 0.347 (95% CI 0.336–0.357) in 2000 to 0.118 (95% CI 0.114–0.122) in 2020, coinciding with insecticide-treated bednet coverage increasing from 0.037 (95% CI 0.036–0.039) to 0.496 (95% CI 0.476–0.517). Higher temperatures (ORs 1.06–1.13), enhanced vegetation index (ORs 5.28–6.22), and greater aridity (ORs 1.07–1.11) were associated with increased malaria incidence, indicating region-specific interventions are essential.

articles.headline: PCR confirms Plasmodium ovale imported from Côte d'Ivoire to Colombia
articles.title_original: Imported plasmodium ovale malaria from Côte d'Ivoire: a case report.
articles.authors: Plata-Puyana JD, Vanegas Beltrán JK, Martínez-Ávila MC
articles.details: This case report describes a 29-year-old male presenting with fever, chills, and myalgia upon returning to Bogotá, Colombia after a 3-month stay in Côte d'Ivoire. Initial microscopy misidentified the parasite as Plasmodium falciparum; polymerase chain reaction (PCR) confirmed P. ovale infection. The patient initially received artemether-lumefantrine empirically and was subsequently switched to chloroquine followed by primaquine (15 mg base daily for 28 days) for radical cure, which was tolerated without adverse effects.

articles.headline: Malaria patients report complicated post-discharge symptoms in South Africa
articles.title_original: Post-discharge experiences of Malaria patients in a Vhembe district village, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Luhalima TR, Mudzweda AD
articles.details: A qualitative study explored post-discharge experiences of malaria patients in Mhinga village, Vhembe district, Limpopo, South Africa using semi-structured interviews with thematic analysis. Two themes emerged describing post-discharge experiences from both clinic and hospital settings. Participants reported experiencing complicated symptoms of malaria even after obtaining treatment from both facilities, highlighting the need for enhanced surveillance, vector control measures, and public awareness campaigns.

articles.headline: IRS and SMC reduce minor Plasmodium spp. but rebound when IRS stops
articles.title_original: Longitudinal analysis of the prevalence of minor Plasmodium spp. in the reservoir of asymptomatic infections through sequential interventions in Northern Sahelian Ghana.
articles.authors: Rios-Teran CA, Tiedje KE, Bangre O, Deed SL, Argyropoulos DC, Koram KA, Oduro AR, Ansah PO, Day KP
articles.details: An interrupted time-series study in Bongo District, Ghana tracked prevalence of minor Plasmodium species (P. malariae and P. ovale) across five age-stratified surveys (~2,000 participants each) from 2012–2022 using species-specific 18S rRNA PCR. In 2015, following indoor residual spraying (IRS), P. malariae prevalence declined from 13.7% to 1.4% and P. ovale from 5.7% to 0.4%. In 2017 after IRS was discontinued and seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) was introduced, both species rebounded to 2.9% and 4.0%, representing 2-fold and 10-fold increases respectively.

articles.headline: Anopheles stephensi invasion and drug resistance threaten Ethiopia's malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Challenges to Malaria Elimination in Ethiopia by 2030: A Review.
articles.authors: Adugna T
articles.details: This review examines challenges to Ethiopia's malaria elimination efforts. Over 42 Anopheles species have been identified, with Anopheles arabiensis historically serving as the primary vector. However, an invasive Anopheles stephensi has emerged with resistance to all four insecticide classes. Plasmodium falciparum and P. vivax, which cause the majority of malaria cases, have evolved resistance to multiple antimalarial medications, and recent civil conflict has resulted in alarming increases in malaria cases in several regions.

articles.headline: Wild and colonized Anopheles darlingi show similar resting but distinct entry behavior
articles.title_original: Experimental hut resting and entrance behaviour of Anopheles darlingi from Zungarococha, a malaria endemic community in Loreto, Northern Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Castro-Llanos F, Stoops CA, Grieco JP, Escobedo-Vargas K, Lopez-Sifuentes V, Lenhart A, Stell FM, Larson RT, Achee NL, Vásquez GM
articles.details: An experimental hut study in Zungarococha, Loreto, Peru compared resting and entry behavior of wild-caught and colonized Anopheles darlingi. Human landing collection outside local homes from June 2014 to May 2015 documented bimodal biting activity with peaks at 1800 h and 2200 h. Both colonized and wild An. darlingi preferred lower door locations (29.8% and 32.7%, respectively), and wild An. darlingi house entry peaked from 2200–2300 h, providing insights into behavioral ecology relevant to malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum plasmepsin2/3 amplification: 16% prevalence, higher in Asia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum plasmepsin2/3 gene duplication in Africa and Asia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Marwa KJ, Kilonzi M, Mnkugwe RH, Baraka V, Kapesa A, Mwaiswelo R, Zinga M, Mmbando BP, Lusingu JPA, Kamugisha E
articles.details: Systematic review of 34 studies involving 4,005 P. falciparum isolates identified pfpm2/3 gene amplification in 16.0% (95% CI 8.0-26.0%) of isolates, with higher prevalence in Asia (25.0%) than Africa (8.0%). This amplification decreases susceptibility to piperaquine, causing treatment failure in dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine regimens and contributing to partial artemisinin resistance and ACT failure.

articles.headline: Sindh Pakistan: 1.28 million annual malaria cases, doubled since 2022 floods
articles.title_original: Subnational malaria burden in Sindh, Pakistan: over a decade of evidence for tailored strategies.
articles.authors: Baig N, Ali Z, Ahmed M, Jamal WZ, Rehman SU, Ahmed Z, Rahoojo RH, Jagirani JA, Usmani BA, Fatmi Z
articles.details: Analysis of 13 years (2012-2024) of primary healthcare data from Sindh, Pakistan reported 16.7 million suspected malaria cases (1.28 million annually) with a positivity rate of 12.3%. Incidence was 92 per 1000 people per annum, with six districts carrying over 53% of the burden. Marked seasonal peaks during wet and post-monsoon periods; incidence doubled following the 2022 floods.

articles.headline: Molecular docking identifies novel compounds targeting PfSUB2 and PfTRAMP
articles.title_original: Targeting invasion-associated proteins PfSUB2 and PfTRAMP in Plasmodium falciparum: identification of potential inhibitors via molecular docking.
articles.authors: Okafor EO, Bella-Omunagbe M, Elugbadebo T, Dokunmu TM, Adebiyi E
articles.details: Computational study using molecular docking screened 519 compounds against PfSUB2 and PfTRAMP invasion-associated proteins. Compounds 139,974,934 and 154,414,021 exhibited superior binding affinities compared to standard inhibitor PMSF, with compound 155,204,487 showing potential as a dual inhibitor of both targets, promising candidates for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Mathematical modeling of malaria transmission dynamics and bifurcation patterns
articles.title_original: Complex travelling wave solutions of fractional nonlinear coupled malaria model: bifurcation, chaos, and multistability.
articles.authors: Abdullah, Shakeel M, Muhammad S, Ahmadzai HG
articles.details: Mathematical study exploring malaria transmission dynamics using a novel fractional nonlinear coupled model. The analysis yielded complex traveling wave solutions including kink, anti-kink, and dark solitons, with evidence of bifurcation, quasi-periodic and chaotic patterns, and multi-stability, contributing to theoretical understanding of disease spread dynamics.

articles.headline: P. vivax: 56% prevalence, high genetic diversity, structured populations in Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: High Prevalence, Genetic Diversity and Temporal Differentiation of Plasmodium vivax in a Remote Hard-to-Reach Community from the Peruvian Amazon Region.
articles.authors: Ramirez R, Torres K, Rodríguez P, Acosta C, Guzmán-Guzmán M, Llanos-Cuentas A, Vinetz JM, Escalante AA, Gamboa D
articles.details: Longitudinal surveillance in Santa Emilia, Peru (2013–2016) genotyped 360 P. vivax samples using 16 microsatellites, revealing 56% prevalence by qRT-PCR with 44% submicroscopic infections. High genetic diversity and population differentiation persisted across years, with 40% polyclonal infections in 2013–2015 and 29% in 2016. Four temporally differentiated, structured populations with increasing admixture were detected.

articles.headline: Imported parasites hamper malaria elimination on Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea
articles.title_original: Regular Plasmodium falciparum importation onto Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea, hampers malaria elimination from the island.
articles.authors: Stabler TC, Dwivedi A, Guo B, Shrestha B, Joshi S, Rivas MR, Donfack OT, Guerra CA, García GA, Daubenberger C, Silva JC
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing from 2019 survey of Bioko Island parasites revealed higher-than-expected genetic diversity despite lower local transmission intensity, indicating substantial parasite importation. Within-host diversity and polyclonal infection rates resembled moderate transmission areas, with stronger parasite flow from Gabon than neighboring regions, suggesting cross-border transmission challenges for elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Atypical Arp2/3 complex in malaria parasites essential for transmission
articles.title_original: Discovery of an Atypical Arp2/3 Complex in Malaria Parasites Sheds New Light on Nuclear Actin.
articles.authors: Hentzschel F, Frischknecht F, Marti M
articles.details: Researchers identified an atypical Arp2/3 complex in malaria parasites consisting of five divergent subunits essential for kinetochore-spindle attachment during male gametogenesis. Disruption of this complex or actin polymerization caused defective DNA segregation and developmental arrest in mosquitoes, highlighting specialized adaptations for malaria parasite transmission.

articles.headline: hmmIBD recommended for identity-by-descent detection in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Benchmarking and optimization of methods for the detection of identity-by-descent in high-recombining Plasmodium falciparum genomes.
articles.authors: Guo B, Takala-Harrison S, O'Connor TD
articles.details: Comprehensive benchmarking of identity-by-descent (IBD) detection tools in P. falciparum using simulated and empirical MalariaGEN Pf7 data demonstrated that low marker density significantly compromises accuracy. Most IBD callers showed high false negative rates for shorter segments, but parameter optimization improved detection. hmmIBD provided the most accurate and least-biased estimates of effective population size (Ne) across transmission settings.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Gabon during COVID-19 pandemic driven by control disruptions
articles.title_original: Malaria amidst the COVID-19 pandemic in Gabon: an application of autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) models within an interrupted time series (ITS) framework to hospital-based data.
articles.authors: Roeder F, Adegnika OS, Honkpehedji YJ, Huth M, Lell B, Adegnika AA, Lopes-Rafegas I, Sicuri E
articles.details: Hospital-based data from Lambaréné, Gabon (2018–early 2023) using ARIMA interrupted time series analysis showed an initial drop (−47.32, P = 0.031) in monthly malaria diagnosis rate, followed by gradual increase (linear: 7.32, p=0.001; quadratic: −0.19, p=0.001) exceeding pre-pandemic levels. Resurgence was attributed to disruptions of malaria control activities, though maternal malaria incidence did not increase meaningfully.

articles.headline: Wildlife Plasmodium screening in Peruvian Amazon: 4.7% coati prevalence
articles.title_original: Molecular Screening of Plasmodium spp. in Free-Living Ring-Tailed Coatis (Nasua nasua) and Nine-Banded Armadillos (Dasypus novemcinctus) in the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Ulloa GM, Greenwood AD, Cornejo OE, Monteiro FOB, Santolalla ML, Mayor P
articles.details: Molecular screening of 44 ring-tailed coatis and 66 nine-banded armadillos from an Indigenous community in the Peruvian Amazon detected Plasmodium spp. DNA in 2 coatis (4.7%) via nested PCR targeting the mitochondrial cytb gene. Sequencing identified lineages matching P. vivax/P. simium and P. malariae/P. brasilianum, suggesting wildlife may serve as incidental hosts in sylvatic malaria ecology.

articles.headline: Urban malaria in sub-Saharan Africa: 23% prevalence, 4-fold risk with travel
articles.title_original: Urban malaria and population mobility in sub-Saharan Africa: systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 39 studies in sub-Saharan African urban settings reported overall pooled malaria prevalence of 23.01% (95% CI: 19.42, 26.59). Travel history was associated with 4.17-fold higher odds of infection (95% CI: 2.33, 7.46), as was owning livestock in the house (POR = 4.1, 95% CI: 1.62, 10.39), highlighting socio-economic and behavioral drivers of urban malaria.

articles.headline: Anopheles stephensi Ethiopia fully susceptible to novel insecticides and pyriproxyfen
articles.title_original: Susceptibility of the invasive malaria vector Anopheles stephensi in Ethiopia to novel chemical insecticides and insect growth regulator.
articles.authors: Abiy E, Degefa T, Balkew M, Merga H, Alemayehu E, Mitiku A, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: WHO bottle bioassays of An. stephensi from Ethiopia demonstrated full susceptibility to broflanilide and clothianidin (100% mortality at 24 hours) and chlorfenapyr (100% at 48 hours), despite previous resistance to pyrethroids, carbamates, and organophosphates. Pyriproxyfen, an insect growth regulator, achieved 100% oviposition inhibition, offering alternative vector control options.

articles.headline: Climate factors and malaria mortality in Uganda children
articles.title_original: Climate-driven malaria mortality among children in malaria-endemic areas of Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiragga A, Otieno F, Nabukeera B, Ng'etich M, Tozan Y, Kajungu D
articles.details: Time series analysis of weekly malaria mortality from the Iganga Mayuge Health Demographic Surveillance Site using distributed lag non-linear modeling. Increased mortality risk at 11-12 weeks after rainfall >646 mm and 5-11 weeks after temperatures 25.2-29.9°C. Children under 5 and aged 5-14 years showed greater sensitivity to temperature than rainfall, and male children aged 5-14 years were more vulnerable to temperature-related mortality than females in that age group.

articles.headline: Integrating vulnerability and hazard improves malaria risk mapping
articles.title_original: Integrating vulnerability and hazard in malaria risk mapping: the elimination context of Senegal.
articles.authors: Morlighem C, Nnanatu CC, Aheto JMK, Linard C
articles.details: Bayesian geostatistical models predicted malaria prevalence in children under five using 2017 Demographic and Health Survey and 2020-21 Malaria Indicator Survey data from Senegal. Models combining vulnerability and hazard factors achieved median R² of 0.64. Vulnerability-only models outperformed hazard-only models, demonstrating that societal factors including healthcare access and housing conditions are critical for malaria risk prediction in low-transmission elimination contexts.

articles.headline: Sex-dependent brain imaging findings in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Characterization of experimental cerebral malaria by volumetric MRI A comparative study across the sexes.
articles.authors: Comino Garcia Muñoz A, Marfouk O, Michel CP, Varlet I, Royer E, Perles-Barbacaru TA, Viola A
articles.details: In vivo volumetric MRI study of experimental cerebral malaria in C57BL/6J mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA. Sex-dependent differences observed in brain swelling, microhemorrhage distribution, and brain edema regionalization, with swelling-prone structures like cortex and pons common to both sexes and sex-specific alterations in colliculi and midbrain. Experimental cerebral malaria was more severe in male than female mice.

articles.headline: PBO nets higher ownership and lower malaria prevalence than pyrethroid nets
articles.title_original: Net ownership, utilization and malaria burden in the context of Piperonyl butoxide-LLINs intervention in western Kenya.
articles.authors: Oyweri J, Onyango PO, Machani MG, Bungei J, Cheruiyot S, Afrane YA, Lee MC, Zhong D, Zhou G, Githure J, Atieli H, Yan G
articles.details: Cross-sectional study comparing pyrethroid-LLIN (181 households) and PBO-LLIN (199 households) one year post-distribution in Muhoroni Sub-County. PBO-LLINs had higher ownership (96% vs 89%), usage (87.6% vs 80.2%), and lower attrition (7.6% vs 17.9%). Malaria parasite prevalence by qPCR was 29.0% in PBO-LLIN vs 34.7% in pyrethroid-LLIN group (prevalence ratio 0.84, 95% CI 0.65-1.03).

articles.headline: Understanding mosquito biology for malaria elimination strategies
articles.title_original: Know your enemy: understanding mosquito biology to advance malaria elimination in Africa.
articles.authors: Zembere K, Kalonde PK, Bickton FM
articles.details: Review article highlighting fundamental aspects of Anopheles mosquito biology including adult and juvenile life stages, intrinsic incubation period, and blood-seeking behavior relevant to vector control. Emphasizes that understanding these biological features is essential for national malaria control programs and communities to implement sustainable vector control measures and develop innovative tools for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum malaria impairs kidney function and electrolytes
articles.title_original: Evaluation of serum electrolytes and kidney function among untreated malaria (Plasmodium falciparum) patients in health centers, Konso zone, South Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Basire B, Kibret B, Kifle K, Assefa F
articles.details: Case-control study comparing 54 untreated P. falciparum malaria patients with 54 non-malaria controls (mean age ~27 years) found significant increases in serum creatinine (1.32±0.29 vs 0.92±0.32 mg/dL, p<0.05) and urea (39.8±8.34 vs 25.78±7.97 mg/dL, p<0.05), and significant decreases in sodium (132.15±3.96 vs 137.11±3.11 mmol/L) and potassium (3.44±0.40 vs 3.94±0.39 mmol/L, p<0.05) in malaria patients.

articles.headline: CUT&Tag enables low-input chromatin profiling in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: CUT&Tag and DiBioCUT&Tag enable investigation of the AT-rich epigenome of Plasmodium falciparum from low-input samples.
articles.authors: Gockel J, Ramón-Zamorano G, Kimmel J, Spielmann T, Bártfai R
articles.details: Technical methods study adapting cleavage under targets and tagmentation (CUT&Tag) and developing DiBioCUT&Tag for profiling chromatin-associated proteins in P. falciparum from low-input samples (as little as 10,000 nuclei or crude isolates). Results are reproducible and concordant with ChIP-seq data despite the AT-richness of the Plasmodium genome, providing improved tools for genome-wide chromatin profiling.

articles.headline: Mosquito nutritional status shifts biting time and host-seeking behavior
articles.title_original: Biting time of day in malaria mosquitoes is modulated by nutritional status.
articles.authors: Oke CE, Rund SSC, Machani MG, Sabtiu ARM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Afrane YA, Reece SE
articles.details: Semi-field system study of female Anopheles gambiae s.l. with varying nutritional treatments (0.5% sucrose, 10% sucrose, or 10% sucrose plus additional blood meal) found nutritionally-deprived mosquitoes were 2-3 times more likely to host seek overall and 5-10 times more likely to host seek earlier in the night compared to well-resourced mosquitoes, suggesting nutritional condition drives changes in biting behavior.

articles.headline: RDT genetic material enables improved malaria diagnosis by PCR
articles.title_original: [Use of genetic material extracted from rapid malaria diagnostic tests using PCR during the Grand Magal of Touba in Senegal].
articles.authors: Diouf C, Ouaddane I, Diatta G, Goumbala MLB, Sow A, Fam D, Faye M, Gautret P, Sokhna C
articles.details: Validation study of genetic material extracted from 2,381 rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) collected during mass gatherings in Senegal in 2022-2023, tested by qPCR for P. falciparum. Bioline™ RDTs showed 97.3% specificity, 71.1% positive predictive value, and 95.3% negative predictive value; ParaHIT tests showed 92.5% specificity, 26.9% positive predictive value, and 98.7% negative predictive value. Genetic material from RDT cassettes enables subsequent PCR diagnosis with improved sensitivity and specificity.

articles.headline: Tick-borne borreliosis identified in malaria RDTs in Senegal
articles.title_original: [Identification of tick-borne borreliosis cases by qPCR from rapid malaria diagnostic tests during the Grand Magal of Touba in Senegal].
articles.authors: Diouf C, Ouaddane I, Diatta G, Goumbala MLB, Sow A, Fam D, Faye M, Gautret P, Sokhna C
articles.details: Nucleic acid extraction and qPCR testing of 2,381 RDTs collected during mass gatherings in Senegal identified 13 cases (0.5%) of tick-borne relapsing fever borreliosis, including 2 cases with concurrent malaria-borreliosis co-infection. No samples were positive for dengue virus, Bartonella species, or Coxiella burnetii, demonstrating that malaria RDTs can serve as accessible sources for diagnosing other febrile illnesses.

articles.headline: Type 1 diabetes complications precipitated by malaria and pancreatitis
articles.title_original: Convergence of diabetic ketoacidosis, acute pancreatitis, and malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Kouame KI, Mobio PMN, Bouh JK, Konan JK, Coulibaly TK, Toure CW, Diebi LAA, Kouakou JNH, Koffi BE, Yapo PY
articles.details: Case report of a 20-year-old female presenting with diabetic ketoacidosis triggered by malaria infection and acute pancreatitis, with severe hyperglycemia (6 g/dL), fever (38.5°C), elevated pancreatic enzymes, and detectable malaria parasites. Management with insulin therapy, rehydration, and antimalarial treatment resulted in favorable outcome and blood sugar normalization after 10 days.

articles.headline: Benzo-ring modifications on MMV008138 improve stability without efficacy
articles.title_original: Benzo-ring modification on Malaria Box hit MMV008138: effects on antimalarial potency and microsomal stability.
articles.authors: Ghavami M, Li H, Liu L, Butler JH, Ding S, Butschek GJ, Haney RS, Council-Troche RM, Grams RJ, Merino EF, Davis JM, Totrov M, Cassera MB, Carlier PR
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship study of 19 benzo-ring substitutions on tetrahydro-β-carboline MMV008138 (an IspD inhibitor in the Plasmodium MEP pathway) tested against multidrug-resistant P. falciparum (Dd2 strain). Only three derivatives (20a, c, d) retained activity within 3-fold of parent compound. Analog 20c showed marked improvement in microsomal stability but failed to improve plasma exposure or oral efficacy in a mouse malaria model.

articles.headline: Imatinib shows antimalarial activity with favorable safety profile
articles.title_original: The emerging role of Imatinib in malaria management: a review of evidence and future directions.
articles.authors: Ajekiigbe VO, Anthony CS, Ogieuhi IJ, Adeola JO, Bassey PU, Gbolagade-Jonathan P, Aremu SO, Omoleke AA, Bakare IS, Babalola AV
articles.details: Review of studies through February 2025 examining imatinib (a tyrosine kinase inhibitor) for malaria treatment. Across nine studies with varying sample sizes and dosages, imatinib reduced parasite density, pyrexia, and parasite growth, showing synergism with artesunate. No adverse drug-related events were reported. Review highlights potential for pediatric and pregnant populations but emphasizes need for randomized controlled trials with larger sample sizes and combination therapy studies.

articles.headline: Artemisinin regulates pathogenic CD8+ T cells via JAK2-STAT3 pathway
articles.title_original: Single-cell profiling reveals immunoregulation of artemisinin on CD8(+)GZMB(+) T cells via JAK2-STAT3 in malaria-infected mice.
articles.authors: Chen J, Gao P, He X, Hu Y, Zhou W, Liu Y, Wang J, Liu X, Bai Y, Chen L, Wang C, Cheng G, Zhang X, Wong YK, Liao F, Xu C, Ou J, Wu Y, Zhang W, Gao Y, Tu Y, Wang J
articles.details: Single-cell RNA sequencing of 241,837 cells from murine liver, spleen, and peripheral blood during Plasmodium berghei ANKA infection and artesunate (ART) treatment. CD8+GZMB+ T lymphocytes with elevated cytokine and cytotoxic signatures drove hepatic injury. Artemisinin treatment modulated these pathogenic cells through JAK2-STAT3 signaling, reducing their frequency and inflammatory response. Study provides mechanistic insight into ART's immunoregulatory effects beyond direct parasite killing.

articles.headline: Community-engaged larviciding reduces mosquito density across Africa
articles.title_original: Assessing the role of community involvement and capacity building in larviciding applications for malaria control in Africa: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Shirima G, Masserey T, Gervas H, Chitnis N, Kiware S, Mirau S
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Scoping review of 32 studies from 13 African countries examining community involvement in larviciding for malaria control. Larviciding significantly reduced mosquito vector density and in some cases malaria incidence. Community engagement strategies included workshops and participatory meetings targeting multiple stakeholders. Implementation barriers included logistical challenges, limited awareness, and financial constraints. Authors recommend integrating technological innovations and strengthening monitoring systems to enhance program sustainability.

articles.headline: Ten-year review of imported and non-travel malaria cases in Europe
articles.title_original: Imported and non-travel related cases of malaria in Europe - a ten-year analytic retrospective review.
articles.authors: Vanitha R, Abelein F, Stoyanova KP
articles.details: Retrospective review of imported and non-travel-related malaria cases in Europe over ten years. Abstract provides no specific findings, incidence figures, or clinical outcomes from the analysis.

articles.headline: Mosquito metabolism and microbiota ecology determine P. vivax susceptibility
articles.title_original: Metabolic reprogramming and gut microbiota ecology drive divergent Plasmodium vivax infection outcomes in Anopheles darlingi.
articles.authors: Carlos BC, Voges K, de Andrade Affonso PH, Jaye A, Tong Rios C, Tinoco-Nunes B, Alonso DP, MacCallum RM, Moreno M, Vlachou D, Souza-Neto JA, Christophides GK
articles.details: Experimental infections of Anopheles darlingi with field-derived Plasmodium vivax produced two distinct infection phenotypes (low and high oocyst burdens). Transcriptomic analysis showed low-infection mosquitoes mounted early, sustained metabolic responses including detoxification, redox regulation, aromatic amino acid catabolism, and purine depletion, accompanied by reduced bacterial diversity and enrichment of Enterobacteriales and Pseudomonadales. High-infection mosquitoes displayed limited metabolic reprogramming and expanded Flavobacteriales. Low-infection outcomes consistently correlated with lowest gametocyte densities in bloodmeals.

articles.headline: High-throughput assay measures P. falciparum artemisinin resistance phenotypes
articles.title_original: Measuring growth, resistance, and recovery after artemisinin treatment of Plasmodium falciparum in a single semi-high-throughput assay.
articles.authors: Sievert MAC, Qahash T, Singh PP, Shoue DA, Checkley LA, Brenneman KV, Cassady Z, Kumar S, Li X, Nosten FH, Anderson TJC, Vaughan AM, Romero-Severson J, Ferdig MT
articles.details: Developed and validated the Growth, Resistance, and Recovery assay (GRRA) using qPCR to measure P. falciparum phenotypes in 96-well format from straight-from-blood samples. Measured parasite growth (6-96 hours), resistance at ring stage (120 hours, most consistent phenotype), and recovery (120-192 hours). Growth versus resistance showed minor but significant correlation; growth versus recovery and resistance versus recovery showed no significant correlation. Dried blood spot samples matched liquid samples, enabling ex vivo surveillance applications for monitoring artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: Household characteristics drive seasonal malaria inequity in rural Guinea
articles.title_original: Inequity in the transmission of malaria infection among children and adolescents: a cohort study in rural Guinea.
articles.authors: Toure AA, Sidibe S, Magassouba AS, Beavogui AH, Conde M, Toure AF, Sidibe T, Keita KS, Delamou A, Kouanda S
articles.details: Cohort study in rural Guinea with 1-19 year-old children followed monthly for nine months measuring blood-smear-confirmed malaria infection and household factors. Children in households with older heads (50-77 years) had higher malaria odds during the dry season (OR=3.44). Adolescents in the rainy season were more vulnerable in middle-aged-headed households (OR=15.78) and single-parent households (OR=4.52). Concentration indices showed modest pro-rich inequity among adolescents in both seasons (CI=0.059-0.085). Secondary education and older household head age provided stronger prevention benefits to wealthier households, while primary education showed pro-poor effects.

articles.headline: High community acceptability for seasonal malaria chemoprevention in South Sudan
articles.title_original: "We witnessed medicines help our children, then we became proud": experiences and acceptability of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Aweil, South Sudan.
articles.authors: Okot F, Sans MS, Viganò E, Rom A, Mubiru D, Baker K, Khan J, Julla A
articles.details: Qualitative study of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine) delivered over five monthly cycles to children aged 3-59 months in Northern Bahr el Ghazal, South Sudan. Among 97 participants, high acceptability was reported due to perceived medication effectiveness, door-to-door delivery strategy, and delivery by trusted community health workers. Challenges included environmental barriers, inadequate health worker remuneration, and difficulty reaching all eligible children. Stakeholders expressed strong desire for program continuation and expansion, emphasizing positive economic impacts.

articles.headline: Single low-dose primaquine safe, blocks transmission in African falciparum
articles.title_original: Single low-dose primaquine for malaria control in Africa: a systematic review of safety, efficacy and implementation barriers.
articles.authors: Ye T, Caspar E, Niyomwungere D, Ouedraogo A, Thiebaut L, Strubel PE, Raobela O, Nyandwi J, Rakotosaona R, Ratsimbasoa A, Sirima SB, Menard D
articles.details: Systematic review of 41 studies (15 randomized controlled trials, 26 non-trial studies) of single low-dose primaquine (0.25 mg/kg) administered with artemisinin-based combination therapies for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Africa through 2023. SLDPQ was safe and well-tolerated, including in individuals with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency and children under 5 years. Eight randomized controlled trials confirmed significant reductions in gametocyte carriage, validating transmission-blocking efficacy at the individual level. Community-level impact evidence remained limited. Key barriers to implementation included misconceptions about primaquine toxicity, absence of pediatric formulations, and operational health system challenges.

articles.headline: Household entomological measures predict malaria risk variably by transmission setting
articles.title_original: Relating household entomological measures to individual malaria risk.
articles.authors: McClure M, Arinaitwe E, Kamya MR, Rosenthal PJ, Nankabirwa J, Kilama M, Musiime A, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: Cohort study of 439 children aged 0.5-5 years in 239 households across three Ugandan districts (Jinja: low-EIR, Kanungu: intermediate-EIR, Tororo: high-EIR) from 2011-2016. Monthly vector densities and sporozoite rates were measured using CDC light traps and related to clinical malaria incidence (fever with positive thick blood smear) via Poisson generalized additive models. Across sites, average entomological inoculation rate (EIR) showed increasing relationship with malaria incidence. Within sites, Kanungu showed positive association (IRR=1.09, 95% CI 1.04-1.14) while Jinja (IRR=1.02, 95% CI 0.774-1.26) and Tororo (IRR=1.02, 95% CI 0.986-1.06) did not, suggesting site-specific transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Physician training dramatically improves Sudan malaria case management adherence
articles.title_original: Assessing Physicians' Adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol.
articles.authors: Muhammed A, Mohammed AAY, Mohammed Ali MA, Elsmani I, Elsetiha AIMM, Salih MMA, Hadeed H, Mahmoud AEM, Mohamed MMM, Hamrawi NWA, Abdalla AYH, Elkabashi A, Abdalgadir Hamdnaalla MA, Elshafie M, Abdelraouf Ahmed HA, Osman Ahmed Osman M, Abdulla OAA, Fakher Aldeen Noman FAR, Alfaki A, Hussien Hamed MO
articles.details: Two-cycle audit of physician adherence to Sudan's 2023 malaria case management protocol at Dongola Specialized Hospital. First cycle (N=42) established baseline: general protocol adherence 30.95%, correct intramuscular artesunate injection site 30.95%, second-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria in pregnancy 23.81%, first-line P. falciparum treatment 90.48%. After physician training and guideline dissemination, second cycle (N=39) showed protocol adherence increased to 89.74% (58.79% improvement), artesunate injection site accuracy to 79.49%, second-line MIP treatment to 64.10% (40.29% improvement), and intermittent preventive treatment for pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine to 92.31% (49.45% improvement).

articles.headline: Machine learning maps MDA effectiveness across Senegal's transmission zones
articles.title_original: Using transportability methods to map the local effectiveness of mass drug administration for malaria in Senegal.
articles.authors: Roh ME, Tong Y, Heitmann GB, Ba EKC, Ndiaye JL, Fogelson A, Milligan P, Seck A, Diallo A, Lo AC, Baiocchi M, Gosling R, Bennett A, Hsiang MS, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: Applied transportability models with machine learning to generalize findings from a cluster-randomized trial showing 55% malaria incidence reduction with mass drug administration (MDA) in seasonal malaria chemoprevention areas. Generalized results to 116 non-trial Senegalese Communes accounting for differences in weather, vegetation, and population density. Estimated incidence reductions of 36%-65% in 74 non-trial Communes, with larger reductions in areas with higher precipitation, denser vegetation, and lower temperatures. MDA was not effective in the post-intervention year in non-trial areas, suggesting short-lived effects.

articles.headline: Clinical mimicry between P. falciparum malaria and TTP complicates diagnosis
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium Falciparum Malaria Mimicking Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP): A Case Report From China.
articles.authors: Wang C, Kong M, Huang M, Tang N, Mo H
articles.details: Case report of 51-year-old man with P. falciparum infection presenting with fever progressing to consciousness disturbance, thrombocytopenia, severe anemia, elevated lactate, and elevated indirect bilirubin. Laboratory findings included markedly elevated von Willebrand factor with normal ADAMTS13 activity, initially suggesting TTP (PLASMIC score 7). Peripheral blood microscopy identified large amounts of P. falciparum parasitemia. Patient improved with intravenous artesunate, highlighting importance of rapid differential diagnosis between malaria and TTP given overlapping thrombotic microangiopathic anemia presentations.

articles.headline: 39% of pregnant women have concurrent malaria-anemia in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria-anemia comorbidity and its determinants among pregnant women in high- and moderate-malaria-risk countries in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Zegeye AF, Wassie M, Tamir TT, Tekeba B, Mekonen EG, Zeleke GA, Gebrehana DA
articles.details: Multilevel analysis of 50,545 pregnant women from Malaria Indicator Surveys (2016-2022) across 17 sub-Saharan African countries found pooled malaria-anemia comorbidity prevalence 39% (95% CI 29-49%). Individual-level risk factors included no formal education (OR 1.43, 95% CI 1.34-1.54), untreated bed nets (OR 1.23, 95% CI 1.16-1.30), and poor wealth index (OR 2.37, 95% CI 2.18-2.57). Community-level factors included no indoor residual spraying (OR 2.15, 95% CI 1.87-2.48), rural residence (OR 2.73, 95% CI 2.54-2.93), and residence in West (OR 8.00, 95% CI 7.47-8.57), Central (OR 6.76, 95% CI 6.03-7.57), or South (OR 18.76, 95% CI 17.3-20.4) sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemic timing varies by Togo district with seasonal lag
articles.title_original: Identifying malaria epidemic periods in Togo by health district and target group: a generalised additive model approach.
articles.authors: Thomas A, Bakai TA, Atcha-Oubou T, Tchadjobo T, Ecochard R, Rabilloud M, Voirin N
articles.details: Analysis of 5,522,650 confirmed malaria cases from Togo (2013-2017) stratified by health district and population group: children <5 years 36.6%, older children/adults 58.5%, pregnant women 4.9%. Generalized additive models identified epidemic periods with ≥1 month lag after precipitation onset. Savanes region had later epidemic onset and greater relative case increases; Maritime and Lome-commune regions had shorter or undetectable epidemic periods. Findings can inform targeted seasonal malaria chemoprevention distribution and district-level malaria risk stratification.

articles.headline: ATSB trial CHW practices aligned with standards despite gender gaps
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of the use of Community Health Workers while deploying the Attractive Targeted Sugar Bait (ATSB) for malaria control in Western Province, Zambia.
articles.authors: Ndalama F, Mulenga D, Arnzen A, Akalalambili S, Tobolo T, Maluma C, Chishya C, Saili K, Ashton RA, Miller J, Silumbe K, Chanda J, Hamainza B, Littrell M, Orange E
articles.details: Mixed-method comparison of community health worker (CHW) involvement in phase III ATSB trial in Western Zambia (2021-2023) against literature and WHO guidelines. Five best practices identified: participatory recruitment, comprehensive training, incentivization, supervision, and community support. ATSB trial largely aligned with best practices for training and incentive structures. Challenges identified included inadequate gender balance (32% female CHWs in year 2), insufficient community involvement in CHW selection, and coordination gaps between CHWs and research teams. CHW involvement was key to successful protocol implementation.

articles.headline: Malaria increases stunting risk; preventive antimalarials don't improve nutrition alone
