articles.title_original: Malaria-malnutrition interaction: prevalence, risk factors, and the impact of intermittent preventive therapy for malaria on nutritional status of school-age children in Muheza, Tanga, Tanzania - A cross-sectional survey and a randomized controlled open-label trial.
articles.authors: Hhera JJ, Makenga G, Baraka V, Francis F, Nakato S, Gesase S, Mtove G, Madebe R, Kyaruzi E, Minja DTR, Lusingu JPA, Van Geertruyden JP
articles.details: Cross-sectional baseline (Feb-April 2019) and randomized controlled trial through December 2020 in Muheza, Tanzania among children 5-15 years. Baseline prevalence: malaria 27%, stunting 21%, underweight ≤10 years 23%, thin/severely thin 28%. Stunting odds 78% higher in malaria-positive children (AOR 1.78, 95% CI 1.36-2.33, p<0.001); low socioeconomic status associated with higher underweight odds (AOR 1.50, 95% CI 1.13-2.01, p=0.006). Three-arm RCT (dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, artesunate-amodiaquine, control) with dosing at 0, 4, 8 months showed no significant difference in weight, height, or BMI change between treatment groups versus control.

articles.headline: Galectin-3 not predictive of malaria-related insulin resistance
articles.title_original: Galectin-3 did not associate with malaria-related insulin resistance in diabetic and non-diabetic respondents at a Ghanaian General Hospital.
articles.authors: Nortey E, Derkyi-Kwarteng L, Amoako-Sakyi D, Bockarie AS, Yeboah S, Acquah S
articles.details: Cross-sectional study at Tema General Hospital measuring anthropometrics, lipids, glucose, insulin, and galectin-3 in diabetic and non-diabetic adults with/without malaria. Participants with T2DM had lower parasite levels and higher systolic BP/glucose than non-diabetic counterparts. Irrespective of diabetes status, malaria was associated with higher total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and galectin-3 but lower triglycerides. Galectin-3 did not predict HOMA-IR or HOMA-B in any group. Insulin and glucose levels were significantly associated with insulin resistance (model explained 67.7-91% of variation).

articles.headline: Expanded CHW role feasible and acceptable for malaria elimination in Myanmar
articles.title_original: Acceptability, feasibility and fidelity of an expanded role for community health workers for malaria elimination in Myanmar: A mixed-method study.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Khant KM, Htwe EP, Htike W, Aye Tun N, Oo MC, Thu KM, Zaw AK, Galau NH, O'Flaherty K, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: Mixed-method evaluation of expanded community health worker (CHW) model nested in intervention trial in Myanmar, including interviews with 6 community leaders and 14 health stakeholders, 36 focus group discussions, supervision of 69 CHWs, and surveys of 643 community members. Model was acceptable to 97.4% (626/643) of community members as it addressed healthcare demand (93%, 598/643) and promoted CHW roles within regulations. CHW fidelity was high at 97.1% (67/69). Community reliance on expanded CHW services was high (98.3%, 632/643). Areas for improvement included training quality, supervision, supply chain management, referral mechanisms, and data recording/reporting.

articles.headline: Capsicum frutescens extract reduces oxidative stress in P. berghei-infected mice
articles.title_original: Evaluating the Oxidative Stress Reducing Potentials of Sub-Fractions of Capsicum Frutescens L. Aqueous Extract on Early Established Plasmodium in Mice.
articles.authors: Nwikwe DC, Ofeniforo BE, Asogwa NT, Nwonuma CO, Balogun EA
articles.details: Murine study with 204 mice (combined sexes) in 7 groups inoculated intraperitoneally with P. berghei parasitized erythrocytes. Groups received normal saline (control), chloroquine 10 mg/kg body weight, or Capsicum frutescens leaf (CFL) sub-fractions at 0.5-8.0 mg/kg body weight. On days 4 and 8 post-inoculation, CFL treatment significantly reduced malondialdehyde (MDA) lipid peroxidation (p<0.05) and dose-dependently increased antioxidant enzyme concentrations (superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione peroxidase, glutathione reductase, glutathione S-transferase). Sub-fraction B demonstrated the greatest antioxidant effect.

articles.headline: Killer immunoglobulin receptors recognize Plasmodium surface antigens RIFINs
articles.title_original: Both inhibitory and activating KIRs recognize RIFINs: a dual-edged mechanism of NK cell control in malaria.
articles.authors: Cuapio A, Ljunggren HG
articles.details: Both inhibitory and activating killer immunoglobulin-like receptors (KIRs) recognize RIFINs, variant surface antigens of Plasmodium, representing a dual-edged mechanism of NK cell control in malaria.

articles.headline: Host sphingolipid scavenging critical for Plasmodium liver stage infection
articles.title_original: Host sphingolipids support Plasmodium berghei liver stage development.
articles.authors: Schroeder EA, Colón IC, Petruzziello PE, Derbyshire ER
articles.details: Experimental study of lipid role in P. berghei liver stage development and invasion. Exogenous C16-ceramide enhanced parasite development and nuclear replication; host cell membrane sphingomyelin was essential for parasitic invasion. Live microscopy demonstrated sphingolipids were actively transported into the parasitophorous vacuole throughout liver stage. Host ceramide transporter CERT1 was enriched at the parasitophorous vacuole; genetic disruption significantly reduced P. berghei load and ceramide trafficking into the vacuole. Depletion of host salvage pathway proteins CERS3 and SPHK1 affected parasitophorous vacuole size and infection rate but not invasion.

articles.headline: Seven-day high-dose primaquine equals 14-day low-dose with better adherence
articles.title_original: Shortening the Journey to Cure: A Quasi-experimental Trial Comparing Seven-Day and 14-Day Primaquine Radical Therapy for Vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Shahab K, Salam A, Salih N, Bilal M, Jan M
articles.details: Quasi-experimental trial of 100 patients with confirmed P. vivax malaria (age 18-76) at Hayatabad Medical Complex, Peshawar, Pakistan. Group A (n=50) received primaquine 1 mg/kg/day for 7 days; Group B (n=50) received primaquine 0.5 mg/kg/day for 14 days. Disease recurrence rates significantly lower in Group A (2%) versus Group B (26%, p=0.012 by chi-square). Adherence higher in Group A (94% vs 76%, p=0.012). Both regimens well tolerated; Group A had slightly more mild gastrointestinal symptoms with no serious adverse events in either group.

articles.headline: Nanoformulations of plant compounds show promise against malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Harnessing Phytonanotechnology to Tackle Neglected Parasitic Diseases: Focus on Chagas Disease and Malaria.
articles.authors: García M, Magi MS, García MC
articles.details: Comprehensive review examining phytochemicals with demonstrated activity against Plasmodium spp. and their integration into nanotechnology-based drug delivery systems. Discusses how poor solubility, low bioavailability, and chemical instability of natural products limit clinical use, and how nanocarrier-based formulations can improve pharmacokinetic and therapeutic properties. Emphasizes potential of phytonanotechnology to overcome current limitations in antiparasitic therapy and support development of more effective and accessible treatments for malaria.

articles.headline: Vaccine acceptance among African caregivers: 82% pooled rate
articles.title_original: Community readiness and acceptance for the implementation of the malaria vaccine among caretakers of at-risk children in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Kigongo E, Puleh SS, Kabunga A, Akech SI, Ocen F, Opollo MS, Ebong M
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 studies with 25,867 caregivers of children under five in sub-Saharan Africa found pooled acceptance rate of 82% (95% CI: 73%-90%) and willingness rate of 80% (95% CI: 70%-90%). Lowest acceptance (53%) was reported in DRC. High heterogeneity (I2 > 99%) and publication bias were noted, indicating need for context-specific strategies and high-quality studies.

articles.headline: Correction to Artemisia extract antimalarial study
articles.title_original: Correction: Assessment of the in vitro activity and selectivity of Artemisia afra and Artemisia annua aqueous extracts against artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Ashraf K, Vantaux A, Marin AA, Maher SP, Franetich JF, Kloeung N, Ke S, Vo HTM, Barale JC, Mazier D, Witkowski B
articles.details: Correction notice (abstract not available).

articles.headline: LLIN deployment reduces malaria vector habitat by 1-60%
articles.title_original: Ecological drivers of malaria vector habitat and transmission over 1 year of long-lasting insecticidal net intervention in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Talbot B, Alou LPA, Koffi AA, Sih C, Dangbenon E, Zoh MG, Camara S, Assi SB, N'Guessan R, Messenger LA, Protopopoff N, Cook J, Kulkarni MA
articles.details: Cluster randomized trial using ecological niche modeling evaluated three Anopheles species and P. falciparum-infected vectors across 33 clusters at baseline and 1-year post-LLIN intervention. Minimal to considerable reductions in suitable habitat were observed (1-60% for vector species, >50% for infected vectors). Environmental factors (land cover, climate, topography, population density) had strong significant effects on vector occurrence, while intervention arm showed no significant effect.

articles.headline: Community health workers expand malaria case management to all ages
articles.title_original: An Anthropological Analysis of Acceptability and Feasibility of Expanding Community-Based Malaria Management to All Ages in Madagascar: Levers and Challenges for National Scale-Up.
articles.authors: Rabesandratra HF, Mattern C, Brazy-Nancy E, Harimanana A, Irinantenaina J, Razanadranaivo HL, Andrianambinintsoa PTD, Dentinger C, Steinhardt L, Garchitorena A
articles.details: Cluster randomized trial with qualitative assessments (87 semistructured interviews, 12 focus groups) in one Madagascar district evaluated expansion of community case management from children under 5 to all ages. Stakeholders found age-expanded mCCM acceptable and consistent with existing CHW roles, resulting in more accurate understanding of malaria in adults. However, structural challenges including cost of care and geographic barriers were not fully resolved by the intervention.

articles.headline: Proteomic analysis reveals 11-protein signature in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Systems analysis of clinical malaria reveals proteomic perturbation and innate-adaptive crosstalk linked to disease severity.
articles.authors: Lautenbach MJ, Wyss K, Yman V, Foroogh F, Satarvandi D, Mousavian Z, Sondén K, Wang J, Álvez MB, Bergström S, Nilsson P, Edfors F, Brodin P, Uhlén M, Sundling C, Färnert A
articles.details: Study analyzed 1,463 plasma proteins in adult travelers during and after acute P. falciparum malaria, identifying perturbations in over 250 proteins. Protein profiles clustered participants by disease severity, enabling identification of an 11-protein signature enriched in severe malaria. Integration with single-cell RNA-seq linked proteomic changes to immune cell communication and organ-specific responses, providing biomarkers and potential therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: Military families from endemic countries have 5-fold higher malaria risk
articles.title_original: Malaria Incidence in US Military Families Is Related to Service Member's Birthplace.
articles.authors: Helfrich AM, Lu D, Grance M, Chu X, Hickey PW
articles.details: Retrospective cohort of 281 malaria cases in Military Health System, October 2012 to September 2018. Service members and families from malaria-endemic countries (n=67) had 5 times higher malaria risk than those from nonendemic countries (n=214) (IRR 5.2, 95% CI 2.4-10.4). Those from sub-Saharan Africa had annual incidence of 42 cases per 100,000 persons, accounting for 86.6% of endemic-region cases. Only 48% sought pretravel counseling.

articles.headline: Colombian eradication campaign (1959-1969) cost $42M, prevented 568K cases
articles.title_original: Costs and impact of the Malaria Eradication Campaign in Colombia, 1957-1969.
articles.authors: Padilla-Rodríguez JC, Olivera MJ
articles.details: Economic evaluation of Campaña de Erradicación de la Malaria (1959-1969) using institutional reports. Total cost: USD $41,788,924, with 74.1% from national sources. Attack phase: 80.4% of expenditure (USD $33,603,645); 40% allocated to spraying personnel, 19% to insecticide. Average cost per household: USD $2.70. Campaign prevented estimated 568,439 malaria cases, averaging 41,965 per year, though eradication was not ultimately achieved.

articles.headline: Emerging Kelch13 mutations rise in Gabon; E433D increases 0.7% to 5.5%
articles.title_original: Clonal Transmission of Emerging Novel Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 Mutations and Increasing Complexity of Infection in Libreville, Gabon, 2021-2023.
articles.authors: Ndong Ngomo JM, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Mouity TN, Leonetti A, Mabika DM, Mihindou JC, Mbondoukwé NP, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Bouyou-Akotet MK, Niaré K
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 468 P. falciparum samples in Libreville, Gabon, 2021-2023, identified no validated K13 resistance mutations but detected 21 with mutations of unknown significance: E433D (n=14, rising from 0.7% in 2022 to 5.5% in 2023) and Q613H (n=5). E433D and Q613H showed higher identity-by-descent than wild-type (P<0.001) with clonal transmission patterns. Complexity of infection increased, suggesting intensifying transmission. MDR1 Y184F rose from 52.6% to 68.8%.

articles.headline: Mozambique surveillance shows high SP resistance, no artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Antimalarial drug resistance and population structure of Plasmodium falciparum in Mozambique using genomic surveillance at health facilities in 2021 and 2022.
articles.authors: Boene S, Rovira-Vallbona E, da Silva C, García-Ulloa M, Rafael B, Canana N, Aranda-Díaz A, Cisteró P, García-Fernández C, Tembisse D, Ndimande N, Chidimatembue A, Matambisso G, Palmer B, Chico AR, Dimene M, Saifodine A, Inácio J, da Silva M, Plucinski M, Bonnington C, Wate F, de Carvalho E, Mathe G, Pujol A, Arregui-Gallego B, Comiche K, Nhama A, Nhamussua L, Aide P, Saute F, Enosse S, Greenhouse B, Candrinho B, Mayor A
articles.details: Genomic surveillance of 1146 P. falciparum samples at health facilities (321 in 2021, 980 in 2022) using multiplex amplicon sequencing and real-time qPCR. No pfk13 artemisinin-resistance markers or pfpm2 duplications detected. Prevalence of pfdhfr/pfdhps quintuple mutants associated with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) resistance: >92.5% in 2021 and >87.8% in 2022. pfdhps-A581G rare (1.6% in 2021, 0.8% in 2022). Prevalence of pfdhps-436 and genetic complexity increased from South to North (p<0.001).

articles.headline: Mathematical model: treatment and prevention reduce malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Fractional-order model of malaria incorporating treatment and prevention strategies.
articles.authors: Agbata BC, Kovaci S, Agbebaku DF, Dervishi R, Abah E, Mbah GCE, Emadifar H, Smerat A
articles.details: Novel mathematical modeling study using fractional-order derivatives to evaluate malaria transmission dynamics incorporating treatment and prevention strategies. Established conditions for solution existence and uniqueness, assessed endemic equilibrium stability, and performed sensitivity analysis of basic reproduction number. Simulations demonstrated that improving treatment strategies and implementing preventive measures (mosquito control, timely medication) significantly reduced malaria cases, while increased mosquito contact and ineffective treatments aggravated disease impact.

articles.headline: miRNA-92a-3p targets conserved PfEMP domain for malaria therapy
articles.title_original: Exploring the Potential of miRNA-92a-3p as Lead for Sequence-Based Therapies for Malaria.
articles.authors: Prabhu SR, Paul S, Umakanth S, Hande M, Saadi AV, Gupta H, Satyamoorthy K
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of DBL domain sequences from 20 P. falciparum-infected patients and case-control study of miR-92a gene polymorphisms. Despite notable sequence variability in DBLα domain across parasite isolates, a conserved motif facilitating host miR-92a binding was consistently identified. Genetic variants in miR-92a were found in both healthy controls and malaria cases, suggesting miRNA dysregulation. Findings support implementing miR-92a as antisense strategy targeting erythrocyte membrane protein (PfEMP) variants.

articles.headline: Ferrocenyl quinoline compounds potent against drug-resistant malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Ferrocenyl Quinoline-Benzimidazole Hybrids: A Multistage Strategy to Combat Drug-Resistant Malaria.
articles.authors: Golding TM, Garnie LF, Rabie T, Reader J, Birkholtz LM, Wicht KJ, Smith GS
articles.details: In vitro evaluation of ferrocenyl-based quinoline-benzimidazole molecular hybrids against drug-sensitive NF54 and multidrug-resistant K1 P. falciparum strains revealed potent submicromolar activity. Compounds exhibited multistage antiplasmodial activity targeting both immature and mature gametocytes. Mechanistic studies indicated inhibition of hemozoin formation as key mode of action, with additional reactive oxygen species generation under oxidative conditions. Ferrocenyl-based hybrids identified as promising candidates in antiplasmodial drug development.

articles.headline: Fifteen Anopheles species identified in Sri Lanka post-elimination surveillance
articles.title_original: Seasonal abundance and climatic determinants of Anopheles vectors in Sri Lanka: its implications in the malaria post-elimination era.
articles.authors: Hewavitharane M, Ranawaka G, Saparamadu MDJS, Premaratne R, Jayasooriya HTR
articles.details: A 2-year entomological study of female Anopheles mosquitoes from five districts across Sri Lanka's dry, intermediate, and wet zones used cattle-baited traps, human landing catches, and hand collections. Researchers identified 15 Anopheles species with varying abundance and distribution. An. culicifacies showed the highest mean adult density at intermediate zone sites with peaks during the second inter-monsoon and southwest monsoon seasons (p < 0.05 for climatic factors influencing abundance). The findings indicate the intermediate zone has the highest receptivity for malaria re-establishment.

articles.headline: Pv47 genetic diversity driven by adaptation to mosquito vectors
articles.title_original: Worldwide genetic diversity of Plasmodium vivax Pv47 is consistent with natural selection by anopheline mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Molina-Cruz A, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Dwivedi A, Torres TZB, Raytselis N, Young M, Kamath N, McNinch C, Su X, Ford A, Ferreira MU, Arévalo-Herrera M, Herrera S, Lo E, Silva JC, Barillas-Mury C
articles.details: Researchers analyzed global genetic diversity of Plasmodium vivax Pv47, finding most common polymorphisms are non-synonymous and cluster in regions similar to Pfs47 in P. falciparum. Pv47 domain 2 showed excess of non-synonymous substitutions suggesting positive selection. Greatest haplotype diversity occurred in East/Southeast Asia and Oceania. A Pv47 K27E polymorphism was associated with differences in infectivity to Anopheles albimanus and Anopheles pseudopunctipennis, suggesting adaptation to different Anopheline species drives Pv47 diversity.

articles.headline: Declining lumefantrine and DHA susceptibility in Uganda 2019-2024
articles.title_original: Changes in susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs in Uganda over time: 2019-2024.
articles.authors: Okitwi M, Orena S, Tumwebaze PK, Katairo T, Taremwa Y, Byaruhanga O, Tukwasibwe S, Nsobya SL, Legac J, Bailey JA, Cooper RA, Conrad MD, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Ex vivo drug susceptibility testing of 1114 P. falciparum isolates from uncomplicated malaria cases in Uganda (2019-2024) using nine antimalarial drugs showed low-nanomolar median IC50s for chloroquine, monodesethylamodiaquine, piperaquine, pyronaridine, lumefantrine, mefloquine, and dihydroartemisinin (DHA). Over the study period, susceptibilities improved for chloroquine but decreased for lumefantrine, mefloquine, and DHA. Lumefantrine resistance was associated with PfCRT K76T, PfMDR1 N86Y, PfK13 C469Y/A675V, and PfCARL D611N mutations; DHA resistance with PfK13 C469Y/A675V and PfMDR1 Y500N mutations. These trends suggest potential loss of efficacy of leading artemisinin-based combination therapies, though clinical consequences remain uncertain.

articles.headline: Ferroportin inhibition exacerbates P. yoelii parasitemia in mice
articles.title_original: Pharmacologic Inhibition of Erythrocyte Ferroportin Expression Exacerbates Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Zeydabadinejad S, Theis BF, Park JS, Gohara AF, Vijay-Kumar M, Yeoh BS, Saha P
articles.details: In a P. yoelii mouse model, oral ferroportin inhibitor VIT-2763 treatment increased parasitemia along with elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, erythropoietin, and liver injury markers. VIT-2763 suppressed hepatic hepcidin expression while increasing hepatocyte ferroportin, but decreased RBC ferroportin protein levels. These findings reveal tissue-specific ferroportin regulation and suggest therapeutic potential in modulating the hepcidin-ferroportin axis during malaria infection.

articles.headline: Amodiaquine resistance pfmdr1 haplotype emerging in São Tomé and Príncipe
articles.title_original: Trajectory of Plasmodium falciparum Molecular Markers of Amodiaquine Resistance in São Tomé and Príncipe.
articles.authors: Té S, Das Neves E, Pellicer A, Broumou I, Zerebinski J, Santos-Reis A, Nóbrega de Sousa T, Färnert A, Santos A, Santos MJ, Gil JP, Lopes D
articles.details: Analysis of P. falciparum samples from São Tomé and Príncipe from 2020-2022 examined prevalence of the amodiaquine-resistance-associated pfmdr1 86Y/Y184/1246Y haplotype compared to historical data. Results indicate successive SNP accretion in a background of pfcrt 76T near fixation, resulting in emergence of the dominant 86Y/Y184/1246Y haplotype. The findings suggest increased vigilance for amodiaquine resistance and potential loss of artesunate-amodiaquine efficacy is warranted.

articles.headline: Hybrid capsule network achieves 100% accuracy detecting malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Hybrid Capsule Network for precise and interpretable detection of malaria parasites in blood smear images.
articles.authors: Alawfi B
articles.details: Researchers developed Hybrid CapNet, a lightweight deep learning architecture combining convolutional neural network feature extraction with dynamic capsule routing for parasite identification and life-cycle stage classification. Evaluated on four benchmark malaria datasets (MP-IDB, MP-IDB2, IML-Malaria, MD-2019), the model achieved up to 100% multiclass classification accuracy with minimal computational requirements (1.35M parameters, 0.26 GFLOPs) and consistent cross-dataset performance improvements over baseline CNN architectures. Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed biological interpretability of the model's focus on parasite regions.

articles.headline: 96% accept ivermectin MDA but only 59% complete all rounds
articles.title_original: Community acceptance of Ivermectin mass drug administration for malaria in Southern Thailand.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Rachaphaew N, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Nguitragool W, Phuanukoonnon S
articles.details: A 2022 cross-sectional study in three districts of Surat Thani Province surveyed 391 household heads about acceptance of planned ivermectin mass drug administration (MDA). While 96.4% expressed acceptance, only 59.0% of 3,137 eligible individuals completed all three MDA rounds. Forest-related workers (aOR 4.2, 95% CI 1.1-16.1) and those believing malaria elimination was achievable (aOR 9.1, 95% CI 2.8-29.9) reported higher acceptance. Main barriers to completion were absence from the village and reluctance to take the drug in later rounds.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance and climate change expanding malaria in East Africa
articles.title_original: The burden of malaria in East Africa: prevalence, risk factors, and control strategies.
articles.authors: Bashir SG, Ahmed NI, Abdullahi YB, Abdi YH, Abdi MS, Musa MK
articles.details: A systematic review of 2015-2024 data from 12 East African countries found Uganda, South Sudan, and Burundi reporting the highest malaria incidence (>250 per 1000), while artemisinin partial resistance exceeded 20% in multiple areas and pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles vectors undermined control efforts. Climate change was expanding malaria transmission into highland zones. Integrated control strategies including long-lasting insecticide-treated nets, indoor residual spraying, vaccination, and community engagement reduced severe malaria by up to 47%, though only 48% of required funding was secured.

articles.headline: Malaria-schistosomiasis co-infections linked to chronic malnutrition in Nigerian schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Malaria, urogenital schistosomiasis and co-infection and nutritional status of school children in Ondo state.
articles.authors: Ola EM, Balogun TH, Isijola RO, Ajakaye OG
articles.details: Cross-sectional screening of 185 schoolchildren from two communities in Ondo State, Nigeria found malaria prevalence of 57.1% (36/63) in Oke Igbo and 49.2% (60/122) in Ipogun; urogenital schistosomiasis prevalence of 28.6% (18/63) and 4.1% (5/122), respectively; and co-infection of 20.6% (13/63) and 3.3% (4/122), respectively. Malnutrition rates were similar between sites (~76-78%). Participants with malaria, schistosomiasis, or co-infections showed higher chronic malnutrition frequency. Males were 2.19 times more likely to have co-infections (P=0.02, CI 1.13-4.28).

articles.headline: Annona muricata extract synergizes with artesunate achieving >90% parasitemia inhibition
articles.title_original: Enhanced Antimalarial Efficacy of Annona muricata Leaf Extract Combined With Artesunate, Chloroquine, and Pyrimethamine in Plasmodium berghei-Infected ICR Mice.
articles.authors: Sarakul O, Boonhok R, Somsak V
articles.details: In P. berghei-infected ICR mice, Annona muricata leaf extract (AME) combined with artesunate (ART) or pyrimethamine (PYR) demonstrated significant synergistic antimalarial effects, achieving >90% parasitemia inhibition and prolonging mean survival to 30 days. AME alone showed dose-dependent antimalarial activity, while the AME-chloroquine combination did not exhibit synergy. These findings suggest AME combined with ART or PYR could enhance antimalarial efficacy and potentially address drug resistance issues.

articles.headline: P. vivax genomes highly conserved in Duffy-negative Sudanese individuals
articles.title_original: Highly conserved Plasmodium vivax genomes in Duffy-negative individuals from Sudan.
articles.authors: Schroeder RE, Ahmed S, Ford A, Elfaki M, Hamad SO, Ahmed TME, Mohamed S, Manko E, Clark TG, Campino S, Hamid MMA, Lo E
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of Plasmodium vivax parasites from Duffy-positive and Duffy-negative individuals in Sudan revealed that Duffy-positive individuals had significantly higher parasitemia (p=0.0000132) and much more genetically diverse P. vivax infections across all 14 chromosomes and 44 erythrocyte binding gene candidates compared to Duffy-negatives. Merozoite surface protein family genes accounted for much of the observed diversity. Multiple erythrocyte binding gene candidates showed evidence of positive and negative selection, with some containing amino acid mutations potentially affecting binding and antigenic conformation.

articles.headline: Sequential RDT algorithm with dbPCR achieves 97.9% sensitivity in diagnosis
articles.title_original: Refining malaria diagnosis in high-transmission areas: a dual-approach with rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and dbPCR-NALFIA.
articles.authors: Some DY, Kiemde F, Sorgho H, Tahita MC, Bere AW, Ouedraogo A, Banao SV, Bagre K, Djiri G, Some G, Rouamba T, Hien YE, Savadogo A, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: A diagnostic study of 438 febrile patients from two rural Burkina Faso health facilities evaluated sequential interpretation of two-step malaria rapid diagnostic tests (HRP2 and pLDH) with confirmatory direct-on-blood mini PCR–nucleic acid lateral flow immunoassay (dbPCR-NALFIA) for inconclusive results. Among conclusive cases (87.2%), sequential interpretation achieved 98.8% sensitivity and 95.3% specificity using microscopy as reference. After confirmation of undetermined cases with dbPCR-NALFIA, overall sensitivity was 97.9%, specificity 94.8%, PPV 97.2%, and NPV 96.1%, substantially improving on single HRP2 RDT performance (95.2% sensitivity, 73.2% specificity).

articles.headline: msp2 polymorphism in P. falciparum asymptomatic carriers indicates high transmission
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of msp2 in Plasmodium falciparum isolates among asymptomatic malaria infections from two ecological settings in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Abate L, Feufack-Donfack BL, Ngou CM, Bayibeki AN, Moukoko CEE, Ayong LS, Berry A, Lefèvre T, Morlais I, Nsango SE
articles.details: Genetic analysis of dried blood spots from asymptomatic carriers in two Cameroon ecological zones found malaria prevalence of 59.2% (876/1480) in Mfou and 63.4% (808/1274) in Tibati. Study identified 36 and 42 different msp2 alleles respectively, with >60% of isolates showing multiclonal infections (MOI 2.08 in Mfou vs 1.80 in Tibati). High allelic diversity and multiclonal infections reflect high transmission intensity and distinct epidemiological patterns between sites.

articles.headline: Night outdoor activities and garden access drive malaria risk in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Risk factors for contracting malaria in six wards of Mudzi District, Zimbabwe: A case control-study.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari MJ, Ndarukwa P
articles.details: Case-control study (94 cases, 91 controls) in Mudzi District identified independent risk factors for malaria: night outdoor social/religious activities (AOR 8.13; 95% CI 1.74-37.90) and having a garden (AOR 4.51; 95% CI 1.55-13.12). Protective factors included full body clothing at night (AOR 0.13; 95% CI 0.03-0.53) and sleeping in IRS-sprayed rooms (AOR 0.04; 95% CI 0.01-0.31). Despite 96.74% of cases having good malaria knowledge, behavioral factors and IRS coverage remained critical for transmission control.

articles.headline: Malaria care quality disparities favor high-income countries globally
articles.title_original: Inequalities in disease burden and care quality of neglected tropical diseases and malaria, 1990-2021: Findings from the 2021 Global Burden of Disease Study.
articles.authors: He R, Zhu W, Hui S, Yu M, Li H, Li Y, Huang P, Yu R
articles.details: Analysis of 2021 Global Burden of Disease data found that while malaria incidence increased from 1990-2021, age-standardized mortality and DALYs declined. Quality of care index (QCI) was highest in Bahrain (91.9), Qatar (91.5), and UAE (91.5), but lowest in Uganda (78.5), Niger (77.1), and Burkina Faso (75.2). QCI strongly correlated with countries' economic development, with disparities particularly affecting children under 10 years.

articles.headline: Malaria reply
articles.title_original: Malaria. Reply.
articles.authors: Daily JP, Parikh S
articles.details: No abstract provided; reply article with insufficient substantive information for meaningful summary.

articles.headline: Malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria.
articles.authors: Barkati S, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Yansouni CP
articles.details: Title only; no abstract provided.

articles.headline: Circadian rhythm does not affect malaria parasite liver-stage establishment
articles.title_original: Time-of-day of infection: impact on liver stage malaria parasites in untreated and drug-treated hosts.
articles.authors: Schneider P, O'Donnell AJ, Herbert-Mainero A, Reece SE
articles.details: Experimental study using P. berghei-infected Anopheles stephensi to infect mice during active or rest phases, with and without pyrimethamine treatment. Parasite liver burdens measured by quantitative PCR showed that while pyrimethamine reduced burdens, host time-of-day did not significantly affect liver-stage parasite establishment in either treated or untreated mice. Findings suggest host circadian rhythms are less influential than vector behavioral changes on malaria transmission.

articles.headline: P. vivax risk factors: migration, occupation, and low bed net use
articles.title_original: Slide positivity, trends, and risk factors of febrile Plasmodium vivax malaria along the Thailand-Myanmar border, 2018-2023.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Rachaphaew N, Sripoorote P, Htwe KZZ, Suk-Aum K, Kaewkungwal J, Lawpoolsri S, Cui L, Sattabongkot J
articles.details: Longitudinal surveillance of 13,347 febrile malaria-suspected patients along the Thailand-Myanmar border (January 2018-December 2023) found cumulative P. vivax slide positivity of 11.0%. Multivariable analysis identified higher risk in school-aged children (aOR 1.56; 95% CI 1.24-1.97), males (aOR 1.19; 95% CI 1.06-1.35), Myanmar nationals (aOR 2.37; 95% CI 2.01-2.80), recent travelers (aOR 2.38; 95% CI 1.94-2.92), and livestock workers (aOR 2.49; 95% CI 1.14-5.35). Consistent bed net use was protective (aOR 0.39; 95% CI 0.30-0.51).

articles.headline: Climate change increases malaria transmission suitability across Canada
articles.title_original: Current and future temperature suitability for autochthonous transmission of malaria in Canada.
articles.authors: Siebels K, Ng V, Ogden N, Schofield S, Ludwig A
articles.details: Temperature-threshold modeling applied to climate data from 2000-2023 shows rising trends in conditions suitable for P. vivax and P. falciparum transmission across Canada. Current analysis shows surges reaching 34% of the Canadian population under suitable conditions for P. falciparum and 56% for P. vivax. Projected climate models show significant variation by species, climate scenario, and model, highlighting increasing risk of autochthonous malaria transmission in Canada.

articles.headline: Anopheles gambiae biting intensity 13-fold higher at high altitude in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Diversity of anopheline species and malaria transmission dynamics in high-altitude areas of western Cameroon.
articles.authors: Kiam BC, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Mbida Mbida JA, Ibrahima I, White SJ, Tchuenkam PVK, Popkin-Hall ZR, Mbouh M, Nanssong V CT, Abate L, Onguene CJ, Tumamo Fotso B, Sadler JM, Parr JB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ, Ali IM, Dinglasan RR, Nsango SE
articles.details: Entomological survey across altitudinal sites (700-1500m) in western Cameroon (May-June 2023) identified 2,835 Anopheles mosquitoes: An. gambiae sensu lato 82.88%, An. funestus s.l. 15.92%, and others. An. gambiae s.l. showed significantly higher human-biting rates at Penka Michel (45.25 bites/human/night) versus Santchou (3.1 b/h/n) and Dschang (0.41 b/h/n) (p<0.001), with entomological inoculation rate 13 times higher at Penka Michel (1.11 vs. 0.08 infective bites/human/night). P. falciparum was dominant (62-67%) with P. malariae (31-33%) and P. ovale also detected.

articles.headline: P. falciparum egress increases BBB permeability via JAK-STAT pathway activation
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum egress disrupts endothelial junctions and activates JAK-STAT signaling in a microvascular 3D blood-brain barrier model.
articles.authors: Piatti L, Batzilla A, Nakaki F, Fleckenstein H, Korbmacher F, Long RKM, Schraivogel D, Hawkins JA, Romero-Uruñuela T, López-Gutiérrez B, Sanz Sender S, Schwab Y, Steinmetz LM, Sharpe J, Bernabeu M
articles.details: In vitro 3D blood-brain barrier model study demonstrated that P. falciparum parasite egress products increase microvascular permeability through downregulation of junctional and vascular genes, and activate the JAK-STAT pathway across all blood-brain barrier cell types. Cytoadherent schizont-stage parasites induced inter-endothelial gaps at egress sites. JAK-STAT inhibitor Ruxolitinib prevented egress-induced permeability increases, identifying a potential adjunctive therapeutic target for cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Regional malaria forecasting reveals intervention-driven transmission patterns in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Time series analysis of malaria in pregnancy, using wavelet and SARIMAX models.
articles.authors: Oniyelu DO, Folorunsho O, Adewole L, Bakare EA, Okoronkwo C, Eze N
articles.details: Time series analysis (January 2015-January 2025) of malaria in pregnancy (MIP) across Nigerian states revealed significant regional variability. Strong cross-correlations between MIP and general malaria transmission were observed in Kebbi, Niger, Yobe, and Ondo, while South-South and South-East exhibited weaker correlations likely due to intervention fluctuations. SARIMAX models captured MIP trends more effectively than ARIMA in most states. Analysis identified intervention-driven fluctuations in Cross River and Enugu, and cyclical patterns in Ondo, Niger, and Yobe.

articles.headline: Gene drive modeling identifies fitness cost as key control parameter
articles.title_original: A model-informed target product profile for population modification gene drives for malaria control.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Sánchez C HM, Marshall JM
articles.details: Eco-epidemiological modeling in Burkina Faso and Kenya examined parameter values for population modification gene drives targeting 50% clinical malaria incidence reduction for ≥3 years with <1-year time-to-impact. Fitness costs of the gene drive allele and effector gene efficacy were the most influential parameters on target outcomes. Current Anopheles homing rates are already sufficient; low functional resistance allele generation rates are preferred. Study emphasizes that key efficacy parameters can only be reliably measured in field trials.

articles.headline: Novel compounds with prophylactic activity against Plasmodium hypnozoites
articles.title_original: Identification of novel compounds with prophylactic activity against hypnozoites using a Plasmodium cynomolgi in vitro model.
articles.authors: Zeeman A-M, Vermaat L, Castellote Alvaro MI, Van der Werff NM, Glas-Nieuwenhuis IG, Harupa-Chung A, Calderón F, Gamo F-J, Fernandez Alvaro E, Kocken CH
articles.details: Screening of 568 compounds from GSK TCAMS library in vitro against Plasmodium cynomolgi liver stages identified 12 compounds with IC50 values below 2 µM against hypnozoites, with the most active displaying nanomolar activity. These compounds grouped into six chemical clusters, some with potential modes of action including cytochrome bc1 inhibition or kinase inhibition. Preselection methodology based on activity against gametocytes and liver stages proved appropriate for identifying hypnozoite-active compounds.

articles.headline: GABA receptor RDL modulates malaria mosquito auditory sensitivity
articles.title_original: The GABA(A) receptor RDL modulates the auditory sensitivity of malaria mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Ellis DA, Alampounti AC, Suppermpool A, Georgiades M, Freeman E, Bagi J, Tytheridge S, Terrazas-Duque D, Albert JT, Andrés M
articles.details: Study examined GABA signaling in Anopheles ear through the GABAA receptor RDL (resistance to Dieldrin). Picrotoxin antagonism of RDL receptors produced multilevel auditory effects: anatomical changes in ear structure, increased spontaneous antennal nerve firing, and enhanced mechanically evoked responses, with both octopamine-dependent and octopamine-independent components. Results indicate RDL controls malaria mosquito auditory sensitivity and may influence mating in natural populations.

articles.headline: Novel Onchocercidae species and P. juxtanucleare co-infection in chickens
articles.title_original: Discovery of potentially novel species of the Onchocercidae (Nematoda: Filarioidea) in Burmese fighting chickens (Gallus gallus): Genetic insights into avian filariasis and co-infection with Plasmodium juxtanucleare.
articles.authors: Narapakdeesakul D, Junsiri W, Kongtawee R, Lattisarapunt K, Taweethavonsawat P
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Microscopy and molecular diagnostics (cox1 gene targeting) in 12 Burmese fighting chickens revealed 2/12 positive for onchocercid filarioids and 6/12 for Plasmodium juxtanucleare with one co-infection. One chicken had single Onchocercidae sp. infection (unsheathed microfilariae); another had mixed infection with two distinct isolates (CH07-S1 and CH07-S2). Genetic analysis identified potentially novel onchocercid species or genus phylogenetically distinct from known filarial lineages.

articles.headline: Primaquine mass administration effectively eliminates subclinical vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Focal Primaquine Mass Administration for Eliminating Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Northern Myanmar: A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Soe MT, Soe TN, Zhao Y, Cao Y, Aung PP, Oo TL, Lawpoolsri S, Nguitragool W, Sattabongkot J, Kaewkungwal J, Kyaw MP, Parker DM, Cui L
articles.details: Cluster-randomized crossover trial of 0.25 mg/kg/day primaquine base for 14 days involving 541 participants in group 1 and 667 in group 2. Clinical P. vivax incidence declined to zero within one month and remained vivax-free for almost a year post-administration. Adjusted odds ratio for subclinical P. vivax prevalence was 0.46 (95% CI: 0.25-0.83) in round 1 and 0.15 (95% CI: 0.03-0.65) in round 2, comparing mass drug administration to control.

articles.headline: Microhaplotype sequencing distinguishes P. vivax recurrence origins
articles.title_original: Microhaplotype deep sequencing assays to capture Plasmodium vivax infection lineages.
articles.authors: Kleinecke M, Sutanto E, Rumaseb A, Hoon KS, Trimarsanto H, Osborne A, Manrique P, Peters T, Hawkes D, Benavente ED, Whitton G, Siegel SV, Pearson RD, Amato R, Rai A, Nhien NTT, Nguyen HC, Assefa A, Degaga TS, Abate DT, Rahim AG, Pasaribu AP, Sutanto I, Alam MS, Pava Z, Lopera-Mesa T, Echeverry D, William T, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Day NP, White NJ, Kwiatkowski DP, Taylor AR, Noviyanti R, Neafsey D, Price RN, Auburn S
articles.details: High-throughput amplicon sequencing assay with 93 microhaplotype markers determined identity-by-descent (IBD) between P. vivax clinical isolates in 745 global infections including 128 pairs from randomized controlled trial. IBD estimation showed low error (RMSE < 0.12). Analysis revealed lower frequency of suspected relapses or recrudescence in patients treated with primaquine compared to untreated controls, with greater impact when primaquine paired with chloroquine versus artemether-lumefantrine.

articles.headline: Tafenoquine blocks hemozoin crystal formation in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Tafenoquine Disrupts Intraerythrocytic Stages of Malaria Parasites by Inhibiting Nonclassical Pathways of β-Hematin Nucleation and Growth.
articles.authors: Lee HJ, Massih S, Abdullah M, Rimer JD, Sullivan DJ, Vekilov PG
articles.details: Tafenoquine (FDA-approved 2018) blocks crystal growth sites and suppresses nonclassical growth mode and nucleation of β-hematin crystals by impeding formation of mesoscopic hematin-rich clusters. Stage-specific inhibitory effects observed with greater ring-stage suppression when hemozoin crystals nucleate than trophozoite suppression during crystal growth. Mechanism highlights tafenoquine's ability to disrupt parasite survival via hemozoin synthesis inhibition in addition to mitochondrial function disruption.

articles.headline: Penicillium compounds inhibit P. falciparum lactate dehydrogenase
articles.title_original: Penicillium-Derived Inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum Lactate Dehydrogenase (PfLDH): A Computational Approach for Novel Antimalarial Therapy Development.
articles.authors: Sama-Ae I, Muengthongon P, Tohlaeh A, Rukhachan W, Kiattikul P, Samaeng F, Mitklin A, Rahman MA, Tedasen A, Kwankaew P, Kotepui M, Kepan A
articles.details: Computational screening of Penicillium-derived compounds as potential PfLDH inhibitors identified 42 viable candidates after drug-likeness and toxicity assessment. Penicilactone B demonstrated strongest binding affinity (-8.71 kcal/mol) and lowest inhibitory constant (414.77 nM). Molecular dynamics simulations confirmed stability of leading compounds (Penicilactone B, Penicillimide, Penicillisocoumarin A) within PfLDH binding pocket over 200 nanoseconds, with off-target predictions suggesting minimal interaction with human lactate dehydrogenase.

articles.headline: Lymphocyte profiling reveals immune dynamics in severe malaria ARDS
articles.title_original: Monitoring of deep lymphocyte phenotypes in the blood and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of patients with severe malaria-associated acute respiratory distress syndrome.
articles.authors: Gomes AMC, Adão-Serrano M, Cunha MRD, Silva JS, Sousa AE, Ribeiro JM, Fernandes SM
articles.details: Longitudinal case study of severe malaria-associated acute respiratory distress syndrome managed with venovenous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation. Despite persistent lymphopenia, dynamic shifts occurred in T cell and innate lymphoid cell populations with type 2 immune profile predominant at acute phase; decreased exhausted T-cell populations indicated recovery. Significantly different blood versus bronchoalveolar lavage fluid immune profiles demonstrated value of multiple-compartment immune monitoring for severe malaria.

articles.headline: Social determinants shape malaria vulnerability in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Malaria and determinants of health: a scoping review of malaria vulnerabilities in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Md Hanif SA, Hassan MR, Safian N, Sutan R, Alabed AAA, Rafi'i MR, Md Asari SN, Naserrudin NA, Dapari R
articles.details: Scoping review (PRISMA-ScR guidelines) examining 22 quantitative studies (2014-2024) of malaria risk and vulnerability in Southeast Asia including cross-border and migrant populations. Findings synthesized into five thematic domains: biological influences, demographic/socioeconomic parameters, built/lived environments, behavior/practices, and healthcare access/information. Conclusion: vulnerability to malaria is multifaceted and entrenched in complex interrelated factors requiring region-specific, multi-sectoral strategies with targeted interventions and equitable public health policies.

articles.headline: SSU rRNA gene variant reduces P. malariae PCR detection
articles.title_original: First report of a Plasmodium malariae SSU rRNA gene variant in Africa associated with reduced amplification by nested PCR.
articles.authors: Goto M, Yamamoto K, Komaki-Yasuda K, Kano S, Ohmagari N
articles.details: Case of 56-year-old patient with P. malariae infection (returning after 2-month stay in Sierra Leone) showed atypical nested PCR pattern with secondary band fainter than primary, opposite of expected. Sequence analysis revealed four-base deletion in SSU rRNA within the primer-binding site of species-specific reverse primer, suggesting mutations in this region impair amplification and hinder species identification. Cytochrome b gene sequencing confirmed 100% identity with P. malariae.

articles.headline: ML spatial analysis identifies malaria incidence hotspots and determinants
articles.title_original: Unraveling global malaria incidence and mortality using machine learning and artificial intelligence-driven spatial analysis.
articles.authors: Rahman MS, Shiddik MAB
articles.details: Spatial analysis and machine learning (XGBoost) with explainable and causal AI applied to malaria data from 106 countries (2000-2022). In 2022 malaria cases reached 251.75 million; peak fatalities in 2020 totaled 99,554, with highest incidence in Nigeria (1,332.99 million), Democratic Republic of Congo (623.16 million), India (319.83 million) and highest mortality in South Sudan (149,753), Zambia (143,546), Central African Republic (124,801). XGBoost achieved RMSE=0.63, r²=0.93. Key determinants identified: sanitation access, electricity availability, population growth, under-5 mortality rate.

articles.headline: Pantothenate regulates feeding and reproduction in Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Pantothenate regulates feeding and reproduction in the malaria vector Anopheles stephensi, with patterns dependent on supplementation scheme and parental nutrition.
articles.authors: Dobson ME, Kaylor HL, Pruett SL, Brady J, Savoie-Penton K, Isoe J, Debebe Y, Riehle MA, Luckhart S
articles.details: Study examined effects of vitamin B5 (pantothenate) supplementation on Anopheles stephensi via water or blood delivery. Pantothenate regulates vectorially important traits (feeding, reproduction) in concentration-, delivery-, and age-dependent patterns. Unsupplemented offspring of water-supplemented mothers exhibited increased fecundity (transgenerational effect); when pantothenate provisioned in blood, only mothers and supplemented offspring showed altered reproduction. Findings establish pantothenate's importance in A. stephensi reproduction with implications for targeting Pan-CoA biology for novel mosquito control.

articles.headline: Risk factors for gametocyte carriage in P. vivax and P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Risk factor for gametocyte carriage and gametocytemia in Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li M, Bian Y, Ruan S, Wu Z, Zhang D, Ma T, Wu Y, Liu X, Wang D, Lin J, Pan D, Cui W, Wang L, Wei H, Zhang X, Wang Q, Zeng W, Yang Z, Cao Y, Cui L, Parker DM, Zhao Y
articles.details: This retrospective microscopy study from 2011-2020 examined gametocyte prevalence in 8,240 P. vivax and 510 P. falciparum patients. For P. vivax, dry season, prior malaria infection, fever, and higher asexual parasite density were significantly associated with gametocyte carriage; gametocyte density increased with parasite density (P<0.001) but was lower in rainy season and among those with prior infection (P<0.001). For P. falciparum, younger age and prior infection associated with carriage, with higher density in dry season (P=0.0115). Polyclonal P. vivax infections had significantly higher densities than monoclonal (P<0.0001).

articles.headline: Modeling optimal pre-erythrocytic vaccine properties for childhood malaria
articles.title_original: Performance characteristics and potential public health impact of improved pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccines targeting childhood burden.
articles.authors: Malinga J, Braunack-Mayer L, Masserey T, Cavelan A, Chandramohan D, Dicko A, Ouédraogo JB, Kelly SL, Le Rutte EA, Nekkab N, Penny MA
articles.details: This modeling study used individual-based transmission models informed by trial data to optimize pre-erythrocytic vaccine (PEV) properties. A vaccine with 90% initial efficacy against infection and 6-12 month half-life, co-administered with blood-stage drug and yearly boosters, achieved 60-80% yearly incidence reduction, consistent with RTS,S and R21 trial results. Halting vaccination after 5 years sustained at least 35% incidence reduction in children <6 years at 12 months post-cessation in settings with parasite prevalence <30%. Increasing duration to 12-18 months or coverage could achieve equivalent impact with lower efficacy.

articles.headline: Correction to RT-qPCR detection of asymptomatic malaria reservoirs
articles.title_original: Correction: Detection through the use of RT-MqPCR of asymptomatic reservoirs of malaria in samples of patients from the indigenous Comarca of Guna Yala, Panama: Essential method to achieve the elimination of malaria.
articles.authors: Cáceres Carrera L, Santamaría AM, Castillo AM, Romero L, Urriola E, Torres-Cosme R, Calzada JE
articles.details: This is a correction notice to a previously published article on RT-qPCR detection of asymptomatic malaria reservoirs in Panama.

articles.headline: GSTs as molecular targets to prevent drug resistance in Southern Africa
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium glutathione S-transferases to overcome antimalaria drug resistance in Southern Africa.
articles.authors: Otun OS, Achilonu I
articles.details: This review examines the role of glutathione S-transferases (GSTs), multifunctional detoxification enzymes, in antimalarial drug resistance development and proposes GST inhibition as a novel therapeutic approach. While artemisinin resistance has not been documented in Southern Africa, Plasmodium falciparum kelch13 mutations conferring resistance have been identified in sub-Saharan Africa. GST-targeting is presented as a pre-emptive strategy to complement current control measures and protect treatment regimens.

articles.headline: Hepatitis A and P. vivax co-infection with autoimmune hepatitis
articles.title_original: Hepatitis A-Induced Fulminant Hepatic Failure Linked to Autoimmune Hepatitis in a Child Amidst Diagnostic Challenges of Dengue and Malaria.
articles.authors: Bilal M, Ajmal MU, Ali A
articles.details: A previously healthy 5-year-old presented with 4-day fever, abdominal pain, and hepatosplenomegaly with thrombocytopenia and leukopenia. Initial diagnosis of dengue hemorrhagic fever was ruled out by negative serology, but HAV IgM and Plasmodium vivax parasitemia were confirmed. She developed hepatic encephalopathy with bilateral pleural effusions. Managed conservatively with antimalarials, antibiotics, and supportive care. Persistent transaminitis prompted evaluation revealing ANA positivity (1:320) suggestive of evolving autoimmune hepatitis, treated with corticosteroids.

articles.headline: Rickettsial infection may trigger P. vivax relapse in travelers
articles.title_original: Acute Rickettsiosis Triggering Plasmodium vivax Relapse in a Returned Traveler: A Case Report and Clinical Review of Travel-Related Coinfections.
articles.authors: Bagga R, Fuller C, Shahzad K, Bado E, Joshi J, Fernando D, Hempel A, Boggild AK
articles.details: This case report describes a returned traveler from South America with confirmed rickettsial and Plasmodium vivax coinfection, with evidence suggesting the rickettsial infection may have triggered P. vivax relapse. The clinical overlap between rickettsioses and malaria presentations can mask P. vivax or P. ovale relapse when testing confirms only one pathogen, highlighting diagnostic challenges in travelers exposed to multiple tropical diseases.

articles.headline: Novel PX1 gene mutations drive antimalarial drug resistance in Uganda
articles.title_original: A novel locus associated with decreased susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to lumefantrine and dihydroartemisinin has emerged and spread in Uganda.
articles.authors: Niare K, Tafesse B, Treat M, Sadler J, Okitwi M, Orena S, Asua V, Kreutzfeld O, Legac J, Samuel NL, Yeka A, Rosenthal PJ, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Conrad MD
articles.details: This genomic study of Plasmodium falciparum whole-genome sequences identified a novel locus on chromosome 7 (px1 gene) with signatures of recent selection in Uganda. A PIN haplotype (three SNPs and two indels: L1222P, M1701I, D1705N) first appeared in 2008 and rapidly spread to >50% prevalence in northern Uganda by 2016 and eastern Uganda by 2023. PIN-carrying parasites demonstrated significantly decreased ex vivo susceptibility to lumefantrine, mefloquine, and dihydroartemisinin (reduced IC50s), independent of kelch13 mutations.

articles.headline: High IPTp uptake associated with low malaria prevalence in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Intermittent preventive treatment, malaria, HIV, hepatitis and pregnancy outcomes in Nigerian women: a cross-sectional study in two healthcare facilities.
articles.authors: Chukwuanukwu RC, Eleje GU, Menkiti FE, Ikpeze GC, Mbachu CN, Obiegbu NP, Ogbomade CC, Anyaegbu HI, Ehiaghe AF, Ezeagwuna DA, Efobi CC, Egwuatu EC, Orjinta KK, James JE, Agbanu CM, Onubuogu CU, Ezenwa OB, Okafor CD, Onah KS, Chukwuanukwu TO
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study of 134 pregnant women in Nigeria found 5.97% malaria prevalence, 3.73% HIV prevalence, and 4.47% hepatitis B prevalence, with 3.73% experiencing preeclampsia and 2.24% PROM. IPTp use was high at 73.9%, with preterm deliveries at 5.2%. The lower malaria prevalence compared to regional reports was attributed to high IPTp implementation.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium and West Nile virus in Culex mosquitoes in Spain
articles.title_original: Vector surveillance of avian Plasmodium and West Nile virus in Culex mosquitoes from Doñana, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
articles.authors: Martínez-de la Puente J, Tamayo-Quintero J, Ruiz-López MJ, Veiga J, Ruiz S, Vázquez A, Herrero L, Soriguer RC, Figuerola J
articles.details: Molecular screening of 95 mosquito pools (1,376 females) from Doñana identified West Nile virus in 5 Culex perexiguus pools and avian Plasmodium in 28 pools (17 Cx. perexiguus, 11 Cx. pipiens). Three Plasmodium lineages were detected: P. vaughani (22), P. matutinum (4), and one unidentified Plasmodium sp. Plasmodium prevalence was significantly associated with mosquito species and sampling session.

articles.headline: High prevalence of pfhrp2/3 deletion undermines RDT effectiveness in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Deletion of target gene (histidine-rich protein 2/3) for Plasmodium falciparum rapid diagnostic tests in Amhara region, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Adamu A, Alemu G, Yimer M, Tegegne B, Mekasha S
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 1,232 P. falciparum patients across 28 facilities in Ethiopia identified pfhrp2/3 gene deletions in 88.3% (158/179) of tested samples, including 51.4% dual deletions, 34.6% pfhrp3-only, and 2.2% pfhrp2-only deletions. These deletions accounted for 93.6% of 109 false-negative pfhrp2-based RDT results. Deletions were distributed across all survey domains, with regional prevalence exceeding WHO's 5% threshold.

articles.headline: Community-led Bti biolarviciding achieves 97% reduction in Anopheles
articles.title_original: Empowering communities for malaria control: Effectiveness of community-led biolarviciding using Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis in The Gambia.
articles.authors: Jassey B, Yudhastuti R, Manjang B, Touray I, Ridha MR, Diyanah KC, Indah Permatasari F
articles.details: A non-randomized controlled trial in The Gambia compared community-led versus expert-supervised Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis (Bti) application against untreated control from 2023 to 2024. By round 10, community-led and expert-supervised interventions achieved 96.8% and 98.6% reductions in larval density, 97.4% and 99.1% pupal reductions, and 96.2% and 98.8% adult mosquito reductions, respectively, with statistically significant declines evident by 2024 (p<0.001). Community participation enabled high coverage with over 85% reporting visible mosquito reduction.

articles.headline: Bayesian spatial modeling identifies malaria hotspot trajectories in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Identifying hotspots of malaria incidence and mortality for tailored interventions in Cameroon using routine data from 2011 to 2021: A Bayesian space-time variability modeling.
articles.authors: Arnold FF, Kirakoya-Samadoulougou F, Marcelin A, Jean F, Ye Y, Samadoulougou S
articles.details: A Bayesian spatiotemporal analysis of malaria routine data from 189 health districts in Cameroon (2011-2021) classified districts by spatial and temporal trends. Districts showing decreasing trends in uncomplicated, severe malaria, and deaths were 42.9%, 44.9%, and 28.6%, respectively, while 44.9%, 37.6%, and 32.8% showed increasing trends. Among these, 24.3%, 23.3%, and 21.7% of districts had high likelihood of becoming hotspots for uncomplicated malaria, severe malaria, and deaths, respectively. Authors recommend integrating spatiotemporal modeling into surveillance for targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Lycopene attenuates malaria-induced oxidative stress in mice
articles.title_original: Lycopene Mitigates Malaria-Induced Reactive Oxygen and Nitrogen Species and Oxidative Damage in Mice Brain and Lungs.
articles.authors: Varela ELP, Gomes ARQ, da Silva Barbosa Dos Santos A, Dos Santos Guimarães M, de Carvalho EP, Ferreira OO, de Oliveira MS, Iriti M, de Aguiar Andrade EH, Percário S
articles.details: Mice infected with 10^6 P. berghei RBCs were treated orally with lycopene (3.11 mg/kg/day) or NAC (62 mg/kg/day) for up to 12 days. Lycopene significantly attenuated infection-induced increases in TBARS, uric acid, and nitric oxide in brain and lung tissues compared to infected controls (p<0.0001 vs both Pb and NAC+Pb groups), normalizing them to sham levels.

articles.headline: Biannual azithromycin reduces malaria parasitemia in first three years
articles.title_original: Biannual Mass Azithromycin Distributions for Preschool Children and Malaria Parasitemia: A Secondary Analysis of the MORDOR Cluster Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Arzika AM, Abdou A, Maliki R, Lebas E, Cook C, Vanderschelden B, O'Brien KS, Cotter SY, Varnado NE, Callahan EK, Bailey RL, West SK, Rosenthal PJ, Porco TC, Lietman TM, Keenan JD
articles.details: Cluster RCT in Niger (November 2014–June 2020) with 15 communities assigned to biannual azithromycin 20 mg/kg (1,695 children, mean age 30.8 months) and 15 to placebo (3,031 children, mean age 30.6 months). At 4-year follow-up, parasitemia was not significantly lower with azithromycin (−3.3 percentage points, p=0.05). Before seasonal malaria chemoprevention introduction, azithromycin reduced parasitemia by 4.8 percentage points (p=0.02).

articles.headline: Fluctuating heat and humidity alter mosquito immunity and development
articles.title_original: Fluctuating Warm and Humid Conditions Differentially Impact Immunity and Development in the Malaria Vector Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Lemos-Silva T, De Neef E, Valgaerts Y, Simões ML
articles.details: Anopheles stephensi larvae exposed from egg to adult to elevated fluctuating temperature (+4°C) and humidity (+10% RH) versus baseline static conditions. Realistic fluctuations primed basal immune responses and accelerated development without reducing adult lifespan. Post-bacterial challenge led to immune suppression that could enhance Plasmodium susceptibility, with implications for vector competence.

articles.headline: Community education and behavior change enhance malaria control effectiveness
articles.title_original: Behavioral health interventions in malaria control: Efficacy and implementation.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Obeagu GU, Iduh MU
articles.details: Review of behavioral health interventions for malaria control, including community education, behavioral change communication, and community-based strategies. Community education enhanced awareness and promoted ITN use and early treatment-seeking. Community-based interventions reduced malaria prevalence but faced challenges including cultural barriers, resource limitations, and inadequate monitoring frameworks.

articles.headline: Larval plastic exposure alters malaria vector gut microbiota composition
articles.title_original: The Effect of Larval Exposure to Plastic Pollution on the Gut Microbiota of the Major Malaria Vector Anopheles arabiensis Patton (Diptera: Culicidae).
articles.authors: Misser S, Chen CY, Ismail A, Oliver SV
articles.details: Anopheles arabiensis larvae exposed to degraded plastic, phthalic acid, Bisphenol-A, or latex beads; adult gut microbiota analyzed. Plastic stressors altered beta-diversity (minimal effect on alpha-diversity) and increased bacteria associated with plastic degradation and pesticide metabolism. Inflammation-associated bacterial genera increased in both strains, suggesting immune modulation.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 8% in Tanzanian children ages 6-59 months
articles.title_original: Prevalence and determinants of malaria among children aged 6-59 months in Tanzania: a nationwide cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Adam J, Luoga P, Nyamhanga T, Makunenge C, Ayubu M
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 2022 Tanzania DHS/Malaria Indicator Survey including 4,923 children aged 6-59 months with malaria RDT. Prevalence 8% (95% CI 6.2–9.5%), highest in Tabora (23%). Risk factors: age 24-59 months (aOR=1.71), rural residence (aOR=6.92), lower economic status (aOR=3.08), no ITN use (aOR=2.73).

articles.headline: Climate and vector control explain malaria risk patterns in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Bayesian spatio-temporal modeling and prediction of malaria cases in Tanzania mainland (2016-2023): unveiling associations with climate and intervention factors.
articles.authors: Njotto LL, Senyoni W, Cronie O, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: Bayesian spatio-temporal analysis of 23.4 million malaria cases (≥5 years) and 17.3 million cases (<5 years) in Tanzania (2016–2023). Malaria risk decreased 11% in children <5 years and 10% in older groups. Long-lasting insecticide nets reduced risk 1.2% in children <5 and 7% in older groups. Minimum temperature, wind speed, and vegetation index associated with increased risk.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria linked to impaired growth in young children
articles.title_original: Impact of asymptomatic malaria infection on children's growth in rural Malawi.
articles.authors: Sady H, Chaima D, Hallamaa L, Ashorn U, Banda J, Mangani C, Kamwendo J, Maleta K, Ashorn P, Fan YM
articles.details: LCNI-5 trial analyzing 840 children aged 6–18 months in rural Malawi for asymptomatic P. falciparum (PCR) and growth indices. Malaria-positive children had lower weight-for-age Z-score (−1.03 vs −0.87, 95% CI −0.17 to −0.04) and weight-for-length Z-score (−0.03 vs −0.13). Length-for-age showed no association. Malaria remained associated with poorer growth after adjusting for confounders.

articles.headline: CYP2B6 genotype affects parasite clearance with artemether-lumefantrine
articles.title_original: CYP2B6*6 single nucleotide polymorphism among patients with uncomplicated malaria in Adjumani district, Uganda: Implications on efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Angwe MK, Mwebaza N, Nsobya SL, Kiguba R, Vudriko P, Dralabu S, Omali D, Tumwebaze MA, Ocan M
articles.details: 100 patients with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria treated with artemether-lumefantrine in Uganda. CYP2B6*6 variant allele frequency 0.37 (43% GG, 40% GT, 17% TT genotypes). Day 3 microscopy positivity: 14% (GG), 37.5% (GT), 17.64% (TT). Heterozygous patients had 10-fold slower parasite clearance than homozygotes (p=0.019).

articles.headline: JAK inhibition prevents lethal malaria hypoglycemia and hyperinflammation
articles.title_original: JAK/STAT inhibition protects glucocorticoid receptor knockout mice from lethal malaria-induced hypoglycemia and hyperinflammation.
articles.authors: Prenen F, Vandermosten L, Knoops S, Pollenus E, Possemiers H, Dagneau de Richecour P, Caratti G, Cawthorne C, Vettorazzi S, Cranshoff Y, Schols D, Claes S, Deroose CM, Himmelreich U, Tuckermann J, Van den Steen PE
articles.details: Glucocorticoid receptor-deleted mice infected with P. chabaudi AS developed lethal hypoglycemia and cytokine storm. Hypoglycemia resulted from liver/spleen metabolic dysfunction: increased glucose uptake, glycogen depletion, reduced gluconeogenic gene expression, and JAK/STAT overactivation. JAK1/2 inhibitor ruxolitinib significantly improved survival by preventing hypoglycemia and suppressing hyperinflammation.

articles.headline: Apicoplast transporters: novel malaria parasite drug targets
articles.title_original: The apicoplast transportome of the malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Akkerman LJ, Kooij TWA, de Vries LE
articles.details: Review of Plasmodium apicoplast transporters, an algal-origin organelle essential for IPP and CoA biosynthesis. 11 confirmed and 17 putative apicoplast transporters identified. These transporters provide substrates and export products for apicoplast metabolic pathways and represent promising novel drug targets.

articles.headline: Fighting malaria as a patient and scientist
articles.title_original: Fighting malaria as a patient and scientist.
articles.authors: Tadesse FG
articles.details: No abstract provided; title only suggests a personal essay or perspective piece on malaria experience.

articles.headline: Statistical methods for mosquito Plasmodium infection analysis
articles.title_original: When a mean can be meaningless: evaluating mosquito infections with Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Ubiaru PC, Ranford-Cartwright LC
articles.details: This methodological paper recommends generalized linear or mixed models for analyzing parasite infection levels in mosquitoes, particularly for datasets with many zero counts or low prevalence, as observed with transmission-blocking interventions. These approaches address non-normal distributions of parasite numbers across host populations.

articles.headline: ART-heme adduct biomarker for artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Pharmacokinetics and metabolism of artemisinin (ART) in Plasmodium yoelii: ART-heme adduct as a potential biomarker for its resistance.
articles.authors: Du S, Xu K, Liu Z, Xing J
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: In P. yoelii parasites, artemisinin-resistant strain showed 2.9-fold higher ED90 (lower activity) and 61.1% lower iRBC-normalized ART-heme exposure compared to wild-type. ART-heme adduct was the major metabolite and proposed as a biomarker for antimalarial activity and resistance.

articles.headline: Hematology analyzer detects malaria in routine blood tests
articles.title_original: [Pilot Project for Early Diagnosis of Malaria Infection Using a Hematology Analyzer].
articles.authors: Shin HE, Shin HI, Lee MR, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: In a pilot project across 47 medical institutions in the Republic of Korea from April to October 2024, among 7.27 million routine hematologic analyses, 1,359 cases showed infected RBCs. Of 499 referred for confirmatory testing, 239 were confirmed malaria-positive. This approach enables early detection without additional resources.

articles.headline: Plasmodium surveillance in wild primates reveals zoonotic risk
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Plasmodium spp. Infection in Neotropical Primates from Bahia and Minas Gerais, Brazil.
articles.authors: Santos LKNSS, Aquino-Teixeira SM, Bernal-Valle S, Daltro BS, Noetzold M, Silva ARC, Alvarenga DAM, Silva LB, Oliveira RS, Oliveira CH, Celestino IA, Gonçalves-Dos-Santos ME, Teixeira TJ, Sevá AP, Campos FS, Ribeiro BM, Roehe PM, Simonini-Teixeira D, Abreu FVS, Brito CFA, Albuquerque GR
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 298 blood and tissue samples from 10 primate species in Bahia and Minas Gerais found only one sample (0.3%) positive for P. brasilianum/P. malariae—the first detection in C. geoffroyi in Brazil. These findings highlight the importance of continued surveillance to identify zoonotic transmission risk.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle-based dihydroartemisinin delivery improves efficacy
articles.title_original: Glucose-functionalized redox-responsive dihydroartemisinin prodrug nanosystem for targeted malaria therapy.
articles.authors: Wang R, Yang J, Qiang J, Li Q, Wang G, Ping C, Liu K, Wang R, Zheng B, Ren G, Zhang S
articles.details: A glucose-functionalized redox-responsive nanosystem for dihydroartemisinin (D@GLU-PMs-SS) was developed to improve drug solubility and targeting. In vitro experiments showed enhanced erythrocyte uptake via glucose transporters, reduced Plasmodium GSH concentrations, and remarkable growth inhibition with maintained biosafety. The authors conclude this strategy warrants further clinical investigation.

articles.headline: Microfluidic sensor detects malaria-induced RBC deformability
articles.title_original: Measuring Asymmetric Ionic Current Waveform Through Micropores for Detecting Reduced Red Blood Cell Deformability Due to Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Yokota K, Hirano K, Kajimoto K, Hashimoto M
articles.details: A microfluidic device was developed to measure reduced RBC deformability at the single-cell level using ionic current waveforms through micropores. In vitro studies showed P. falciparum infection reduced RBC deformability, with waveforms becoming asymmetric as deformability decreased. Computer simulations suggest these parameters reliably indicate diminished deformability.

articles.headline: PfSDP antigen shows short-lived immunity with partial invasion inhibition
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Subtilisin-like Domain-Containing Protein (PfSDP), a Cross-Stage Antigen, Elicits Short-Lived Antibody Response Following Natural Infection with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kengne-Ouafo JA, Morang'a CM, Nyakoe NK, Dosoo D, Tackie R, Mutungi JK, Bah SY, Amenga-Etego LN, Urban B, Awandare GA, Dinko B, Aniweh Y
articles.details: PfSDP, a conserved cross-stage P. falciparum antigen, was immunogenic in children from endemic areas, with antibody levels increasing from acute infection to day 7 post-treatment, then gradually declining. Anti-PfSDP antibodies inhibited erythrocyte invasion by 20-60% in a dose-dependent manner. The limited invasion inhibition suggests PfSDP may have roles beyond merozoite invasion.

articles.headline: Outcrossed malaria parasites mature faster in mosquito vectors
articles.title_original: Advantages of outcrossing in Plasmodium falciparum: insights from genetic crosses using fluorescent labelled parasites.
articles.authors: Li X, Jutzeler KS, Abatiyow BA, Rezakhani N, Haile MT, Leeb AS, Patel H, Kappe SHI, Nosten F, Cheeseman IH, Ferdig MT, Anderson TJC, Vaughan AM, Kumar S
articles.details: Using fluorescently labeled NF54 and NHP4026 parasites, genetic crosses produced 8,540 oocysts from 435 mosquitoes (days 7–14 post-infection). Outcrossed oocysts matured faster than selfed oocysts, with decreasing proportions of outcrossed forms over infection time. Selfed NF54 oocysts were larger than outcrossed forms, suggesting differential transmission potential.

articles.headline: Lassa fever cohort to assess malaria coinfection in West Africa
articles.title_original: Prospective cohort study to evaluate Lassa fever incidence, symptoms and coinfection with malaria in West Africa: the Enable Lassa Research Programme ('ENABLE 1.5') - study protocol.
articles.authors: Mandi H, Asogun D, Ayodeji O, Azuogu BN, Camacho A, Fischer W, Fulton R, Grant DS, Günther S, Ithete N, Illori E, Jan K, Kagia C, Martens N, Menon S, Ndiaye A, Ndifon M, Nsaibirni R, Ntiri M, Oloo P, Okogbenin S, Penfold S, Sankawulo-Ricks M, Sillah M, Suykerbuyk P, Tornieporth N, Vielle NJ, Williams M, Wohl D, Xie F, Yimer S, Breugelmans JG
articles.details: The ENABLE 1.5 prospective cohort study will recruit 5,000 participants across five study sites (Liberia, Nigeria, Sierra Leone), stratified by age, and follow them for 12 months. Active monitoring includes symptom assessments every 2 weeks and serological testing every 3 months. The study aims to assess incidence of RT-PCR-confirmed Lassa fever disease, including malaria coinfection.

articles.headline: Abbott RDT shows high sensitivity but low specificity in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Malaria infection prevalence and diagnostic performance of the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/Pan rapid test in rural populations of Central Cameroon.
articles.authors: Missoup VO, Suh PF, Ndjebakal CN, Tagne D, Poumachu Y, Joko S, Kouamendjouo Djilla A, Njiokou F, Mbacham W, Wondji C, Ndo C
articles.details: Cross-sectional surveys in Central Cameroon (March 2022, May 2024) assessed Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/Pan RDT against microscopy and qPCR. With microscopy as reference, P. falciparum prevalence was 64.9% vs 71.7% (RDT). The RDT showed high sensitivity (93.37%) but low specificity (68.50%), with good agreement with qPCR (accuracy 0.75).

articles.headline: Zambia reports diverse kelch13 mutations with heterogeneous distribution
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Genomic Surveillance Reveals a Diversity of Kelch 13 Mutations in Zambia.
articles.authors: Aranda-Díaz A, Mwanza S, Makhanthisa TI, Lauterbach SB, De Amaral F, Chisenga M, Mangena B, Routledge I, Letinić B, Kasonde B, Chongwe G, Mwenda MC, Miller JM, Hibwato T, Jacob C, Hamainza B, Bwalya S, Chiwaula J, Matoba J, Sikaala C, Chimumbwa J, Wesolowski A, Smith JL, Raman J, Hawela M
articles.details: Genomic surveillance of 2,486 P. falciparum samples from 61 health facilities across all 10 Zambian provinces (March–July 2023) identified resistance markers: k13 P574L (0.66% adjusted national prevalence), k13 P441L (1.39%), and pfmdr1 N86 (all samples). Dihydropteroate synthetase K540E (93.26%) showed very high levels. Distribution was heterogeneous, with some facilities reporting >46% of infections with resistance markers.

articles.headline: Deep learning neural network emulates malaria transmission model
articles.title_original: Multitask deep learning for the emulation and calibration of an agent-based malaria transmission model.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Anirudh R, Selvaraj P
articles.details: A multitask deep learning neural network was trained on simulations from the EMOD agent-based malaria model to learn relationships between immunological parameters and epidemiological outcomes (age-stratified incidence and prevalence) across eight sub-Saharan African study sites. The trained emulator was used with parameter estimation techniques to calibrate the underlying model to reference data.

articles.headline: Egypt achieved malaria-free status through integrated elimination strategy
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of Egypt's malaria elimination strategies and implementation science: Pathways to achieve malaria-free status for other African countries.
articles.authors: Elendu C, Amaechi DC, Elendu RC, Saggi SK, Elendu TC, Amaechi EC, Elendu ID, Dang K, Amosu OP, Idowu OF, Oseni TS
articles.details: Implementation science retrospective study of Egypt's evidence-based malaria elimination program that achieved WHO malaria-free certification in October 2024. Interventions included insecticide-treated nets (ITNs), indoor residual spraying (IRS), antimalarial treatment, and public health education. Results in endemic regions: 92% recruitment rate, 78% population adherence to ITNs, 96% malaria incidence reduction over 15 years, 94% reduction in Anopheles mosquito density, 98% case detection within 48 hours, 91% treatment coverage; cost-effectiveness: $24 per DALY averted, $1.5 billion saved over 15 years; implementation fidelity 93%, acceptability 87%.

articles.headline: mRNA-LNP vaccine protects mice against P. vivax infection
articles.title_original: mRNA-LNP vaccine encoding the Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein is highly immunogenic and confers protection in mice.
articles.authors: Limsalakpetch A, Kum-Arb U, Yongvanitchit K, Im-Erbsin R, Ubalee R, Waters N, Vesely BA, Muramatsu H, Weissman D, Tam YK, Yoshida S, Adams J, Yadava A, Pardi N, Pichyangkul S
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study of nucleoside-modified mRNA-LNP vaccines encoding P. vivax circumsporozoite protein (PvCSP) in mice. Construct with GPI anchor induced significantly higher anti-PvCSP antibodies than construct without GPI.
