Intradermal delivery showed superior responses at lower doses than intramuscular; intravenous showed poor responses. Delayed third intramuscular dose at 5 months enhanced anti-repeat region antibodies and T cell responses, providing strong protection against sporozoite challenge. Combined MS2 L9 and Qβ L9 VLPs elicited sterilizing immunity in highest percentage of vaccinated mice.

articles.headline: CD44 cross-linking promotes P. falciparum RBC invasion
articles.title_original: CD44 cross-linking promotes Plasmodium falciparum invasion.
articles.authors: Kongsomboonvech AK, Scally SW, Le Guen Y, Valissery P, Salinas ND, Cowman AF, Tolia NH, Egan ES
articles.details: Mechanistic study identifying CD44 as host factor for P. falciparum RBC invasion through forward genetic screening. Anti-CD44 monoclonal antibody BRIC 222 significantly promoted invasion via CD44 cross-linking, which altered RBC membrane and increased accessibility of invasion receptors including Basigin. Parasite ligand EBA-175 similarly enhanced invasion and induced RBC membrane changes; both BRIC 222 and EBA-175 increased binding of essential PfRH5/PCRCR invasion complex to Basigin, coordinating downstream ligand-receptor interactions critical for P. falciparum RBC invasion.

articles.headline: P. falciparum kelch13 A578S mutation absent in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum parasites harbouring chloroquine-resistant but not artemisinin-resistant alleles in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Onchieku NM, Gesusu N, Caspar E, Karani L, Thiong'o K, Kamau L, Kiboi D, Thiebaut L, Ma L, Kimani F, Matoke-Muhia D, Ménard D
articles.details: Genomic survey of 118 blood samples from Busia County, Western Kenya using deep amplicon sequencing for antimalarial drug-resistance SNPs. Pfkelch13 mutations in 6 samples (5.1%), all harboring A578S which does not confer artemisinin resistance. Pfcrt mutant alleles in 11% (13 samples), 62% with CVIET triple mutant. 98% had dhfr mutations (N51I, C59R, or S108N). dhps mutations in 100% with 84% single A437G mutation. No Pfhrp2 deletions or Pfmdr1 mutations at codons 86 or 1246 associated with chloroquine resistance.

articles.headline: VLP vaccines targeting L9 epitope confer sterilizing immunity
articles.title_original: Virus-like particle vaccines targeting a key epitope in circumsporozoite protein provide sterilizing immunity against malaria.
articles.authors: Nepal Y, Francian A, Flores-Garcia Y, Roberts BT, David SA, Zavala F, Chackerian B
articles.details: Study of recombinant virus-like particle vaccines displaying vulnerable CSP epitope from potent monoclonal antibody L9. MS2 bacteriophage VLPs with novel adjuvant, particularly longer L9 peptide variants, elicited strong durable antibody responses, lowered malaria liver burden, and prevented infection in mice. Qβ L9 VLPs, MS2 L9 VLPs, and licensed RTS,S/AS01E vaccine all provided significant protection from liver-stage infection. Combination vaccine of MS2 L9 and Qβ L9 VLPs in distinct structural contexts elicited sterilizing immunity in highest percentage of mice.

articles.headline: EaveTubes reduce malaria vector density by 58% in Tanzania
articles.title_original: The In2Care® EaveTubes improved protection against malaria vectors in a small-scale field study at Kagera Sugar Ltd, Missenyi, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Kweka EJ, Farenhorst M, Dijk TV, Zinga M, Osinga A, Mazigo H
articles.details: Field efficacy study of In2Care EaveTubes at Kagera Sugar Ltd, Missenyi, Tanzania from October 2019 to July 2020. 350 EaveTubes installed in 100 iron sheet barracks and 35 mud houses with 2 control camps monitoring baseline mosquito densities over 9-month period. Results: 58% overall reduction in indoor anopheline densities, 75% reduction during peak season; no insecticide degradation or loss of efficacy after 6 months field use. Malaria case incidence declined in EaveTubes-treated camp (25.6% reduction) compared to baseline.

articles.headline: Hair lumefantrine measurement reflects antimalarial drug exposure weeks later
articles.title_original: Can hair concentrations of artemether-lumefantrine be used as a tool to retrospectively determine drug exposure during malaria treatment?
articles.authors: Schnyder JL, Vos-van der Meer M, van Hest RM, Mathot R, Schlagenhauf P, de Jong HK, Grobusch MP
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study collecting hair samples from 6 P. falciparum malaria patients 4 weeks post-artemether-lumefantrine (AL) treatment at Amsterdam UMC. LC-MS/MS analysis detected lumefantrine and metabolite desbutyl-lumefantrine in all samples, quantifiable in 5; artemether and dihydroartemisinin undetectable due to short half-lives. No participants developed recrudescence. Results suggest hair analysis may retrospectively assess lumefantrine exposure in recrudescent cases, potentially distinguishing inadequate drug exposure from reduced parasite susceptibility to AL.

articles.headline: ITN and IRS remain primary vector control tools in Pakistan
articles.title_original: Malaria vector control strategies in Pakistan: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Irshad HA, Jehanzeb H, Yaseen A, Saleem U, Javaid MD, Tareen HK, Mukhtar M, Silviera H, Beg MA
articles.details: Scoping review of published literature on malaria prevention and vector control in Pakistan before September 2023. Analysis of 46 articles reporting surveillance findings on vector control. WHO-recommended strategies identified: insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) 29.79%, indoor residual spraying (IRS) 29.79%, spatial spraying 12.77%, spatial/airborne repellents 4.26%, larval source management 4.26%, house modifications 4.26%. Notable provincial variation: Khyber Pakhtunkhwa emphasizes ITN (55%), Punjab emphasizes IRS (50%) as primary control method.

articles.headline: Imperfect diagnostic tests reduce statistical power of TND studies
articles.title_original: Power and sample size considerations for test-negative design with bias correction: a case study on the world first malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Alfvén T, Yoneoka D
articles.details: Monte Carlo simulation study assessing how bias correction for test misclassification affects statistical power and sample size in test-negative design (TND) studies for vaccine effectiveness evaluation. Using malaria vaccine example, simulations tested diagnostic sensitivities of 60%, 80%, 95%. With 80% sensitivity, only 3 vaccination status groups reached 80% power with 10,000 individuals; same power achieved with 6,000 individuals under perfect diagnostic test. Bias-corrected estimates approximately unbiased but showed wider confidence intervals with lower precision at lower sensitivities.

articles.headline: P. falciparum GRP170 exhibits nucleotide-independent chaperone activity
articles.title_original: Biochemical and biophysical characterization of Plasmodium falciparum glucose regulated protein 170.
articles.authors: Muzenda FL, Stofberg ML, Mthembu W, Strauss E, Zininga T
articles.details: Biochemical and biophysical characterization of P. falciparum glucose-regulated protein 170 (PfGrp170/Hsp70-y), essential ER-localized protein involved in exporting parasite proteins to host cytosol. Recombinant full-length protein expressed in E. coli showed predominantly α-helical secondary structure with Tm >55°C. ATP reduced structural stability compared to baseline. Demonstrated basal ATPase activity and suppression of heat-induced aggregation of malate dehydrogenase substrate independent of nucleotides. Molecular docking/dynamics revealed selective nucleotide binding with greater ATP responsiveness than ADP.

articles.headline: Deep learning achieves 99.24% accuracy in malaria parasite classification
articles.title_original: MalNet-DAF: Dual-Attentive Fusion Deep Learning Model for Malaria Parasite Classification.
articles.authors: Patro KK, Prakash AJ, Madarapu S, Ari S, Routray S, Mukherjee A, Mishra S
articles.details: MalNet-DAF, novel deep-learning model combining stacked convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for spatial feature extraction and bidirectional LSTM networks for temporal dependencies. Incorporates Spatial Attention Module (SAM) refining CNN features to highlight disease-affected regions and Temporal Attention Module (TAM) suppressing irrelevant signals. Trained on National Institutes of Health (NIH) public malaria dataset. Achieved 99.24% classification accuracy, outperforming traditional baseline models, demonstrating potential for real-time clinical decision-making in malaria parasite diagnosis.

articles.headline: Bangladesh achieves sufficient LLIN coverage but gaps persist in camps
articles.title_original: Assessing long-lasting insecticidal net coverage, access, and use: Insights from malaria-endemic regions and Rohingya camps in Bangladesh.
articles.authors: Hossain MS, Neogi AK, Phru CS, Ferdous NN, Hasan A, Islam S, Rahman MM, Rahaman MM, Islam MN, Das SK, Bhuiyan ATMRH, Islam MN, Islam MA, Alam MS
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey conducted May to October 2023 across 5 malaria-endemic districts in Bangladesh and 10 forcibly displaced Myanmar national (FDMN) camps in Cox's Bazar with 1,575 households surveyed. Among Bangladeshi households: 97.6% owned ≥1 LLIN, 93.2% had sufficient coverage, 96.4% slept under LLINs previous night; 95.0% of pregnant women and 98.3% of children <5 years used LLINs. Among FDMN households: 98.2% owned LLINs but only 44.3% had sufficient coverage, 65.7% used LLINs previous night.

articles.headline: Rapid fatal P. falciparum multiorgan failure in immunocompetent traveler
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium falciparum in an Immunocompetent Young Adult: Rapid Progression to Multiorgan Failure.
articles.authors: Sanclemente-Cardoza V, Payán-Salcedo HA, Estela-Zape JL
articles.details: Case report of a 21-year-old male with P. falciparum infection presenting with severe respiratory distress (O₂ saturation <75%), cardiac arrest, and rapid progression to multiorgan failure including acute respiratory distress syndrome, refractory shock, acute kidney injury with severe hyperkalemia, and consumptive thrombocytopenia. Despite intensive management with mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, methylene blue, and platelet transfusions, the patient experienced a second cardiac arrest and died, illustrating the severity and rapid progression of severe malaria in immunocompetent hosts.

articles.headline: NR2F6 deficiency protects against experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: NR2F6 as a Disease Driver and Candidate Therapeutic Target in Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Stefan VE, Klepsch V, Thuille N, Steinlechner M, Peer S, Siegmund K, Lackner P, Schmutzhard E, Albrecht-Schgör K, Baier G
articles.details: Experimental study in mice comparing Nr2f6-knockout and wild-type C57BL6/N mice infected with P. berghei ANKA (ECM model). Nr2f6 deficiency conferred significant survival benefit with less severe endotheliopathy, reduced blood-brain barrier damage, decreased sequestered parasites, and fewer cytotoxic T-lymphocytes in the brain. These findings suggest NR2F6 is a causal driver of cerebral malaria pathology and that pharmacological inhibitors of the NR2F6 pathway could protect BBB integrity and reduce CM severity.

articles.headline: Anopheles quadrimaculatus and punctipennis vectors of deer malaria
articles.title_original: Incriminating vectors of deer malaria (Plasmodium odocoilei) at a Florida deer farm.
articles.authors: Rockwell M, Wisely SM, Mathias DK, Burkett-Cadena ND
articles.details: Entomological survey at a Florida deer farm identifying mosquito vectors of P. odocoilei (deer malaria). Four Anopheles species specialized in feeding on white-tailed deer (≥75% blood meals from this host). Highest P. odocoilei infection rates in An. quadrimaculatus s.l. (4.1%), An. punctipennis (3.1%), and An. crucians s.l. (0.47%). An. quadrimaculatus and An. punctipennis met criteria for vector incrimination based on presence, host specialization (>83% WTD meals), and natural infection rates.

articles.headline: Artisanal mining, travel, water proximity drive urban malaria in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Emergence of urban malaria and the associated risk factors: a case-control study in Mutare city, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Mharakurwa S, Mbwana N, Mharakurwa T, Karumbidza T, Mathe V, Fusire P, Tangwena A, Chauke W, Mukuzunga M, Dhliwayo P, Munyati SM, Gwanzura L, Bailey JA, Moss WJ
articles.details: Case-control study in Mutare city, Zimbabwe (2022–2023) of risk factors for urban malaria transmission. In multivariate analysis, significant risk factors included artisanal mining (RR 22 [10.7–44.1], p<0.001), travel history within 2 weeks (RR 9 [5.2–14.4], p<0.001), household proximity to still water/unprotected wells (RR 16 [3.8–67.5], p<0.001), and residential locale (RR 3 [1.1–5.8], p=0.029). Male residents had 4-fold higher odds of malaria than females (OR 4 [2.0–6.5]); artisanal mining was 93% male-dominated.

articles.headline: RTS,S malaria vaccine pilot introduction reaches 75–80% coverage
articles.title_original: Subnational introduction of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine into routine immunization: experience and lessons from the three pilot countries.
articles.authors: Jalang'o R, Amponsa-Achiano K, Chisema M, Malm K, Khalayi L, Mhone B, Mohammed W, Asiedu-Bekoe F, Haji A, Njoroge J, Zimba B, Chirwa E, Tweneboah PO, Sillah J, Magafu MGMD, Bergstrom C, Goodman T, Walldorf J, Kelleher K, Pellaux-Furrer E, Hamel MJ, Adjei MR, Okine RNA
articles.details: Summary of WHO-coordinated Malaria Vaccine Implementation Programme (MVIP) pilot introduction of RTS,S/AS01E vaccine in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi. Vaccination coverage reached approximately 80% for first dose and 75% for third dose by 2023 in pilot areas. Key implementation challenges included low fourth-dose coverage (median 46% in 2023), healthcare worker misunderstanding of age eligibility, and service disruptions. Stakeholders reported positive impact including reduction in malaria hospitalizations and strengthening of national immunization programs.

articles.headline: Larval source management reduces malaria by 11–78% across sites
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous impacts for malaria control from larviciding across villages and considerations for monitoring and evaluation.
articles.authors: Sherrard-Smith E, Fillinger U, Tia JB, Winskill P, Koudou BG, Tchicaya ESF, Sanou A, Okumu F, Opiyo M, Majambere S, Hamlet A, Charles G, Lambert B, Churcher TS
articles.details: Individual-based transmission model and epidemiological trial data from Western Kenya and Côte d'Ivoire evaluating larval source management (LSM) with larvicide alongside ITNs. In Kenya, 82% (90% CI: 64–92%) reduction in An. funestus and 88% (79–94%) reduction in An. gambiae complex adult densities. Côte d'Ivoire showed 82% (56–93%) An. gambiae reduction. Transmission models predicted malaria prevalence reductions in children (6 months–10 years) of 48–72% in Kenya and 11–78% all-age clinical incidence across villages in Côte d'Ivoire, with high heterogeneity between villages.

articles.headline: Plasmepsin gene amplification reduces piperaquine sensitivity in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: The plasmepsin-piperaquine paradox persists in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Walsh B, Summers RL, Rangel GW, Hagenah LM, Mok S, Llinás M, Fidock DA, Wirth DF, Bopp S
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout study in Cambodian P. falciparum parasites examining the role of plasmepsin II and III gene amplification in dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ) resistance. Deletion of plasmepsin II and/or III increased PPQ sensitivity. Increased plasmepsin copy numbers did not significantly increase hemoglobin digestion, peptide composition, or heme production compared to wildtype, but changes in digestive vacuole pH potentially affected PPQ response, suggesting plasmepsin amplification modulates PPQ resistance through pH-dependent mechanisms.

articles.headline: P. berghei ANKA co-infection mitigates L. major cutaneous leishmaniasis
articles.title_original: Impact of co-infection with Plasmodium berghei ANKA in Leishmania major-parasitized mice on immune modulation and cutaneous leishmaniasis.
articles.authors: Ornellas-Garcia U, Freire-Antunes L, Rangel-Ferreira M, de Sousa CHG, Ribeiro-Almeida ML, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Cuervo P, Ribeiro-Gomes FL
articles.details: Co-infection study in C57BL/6 mice with L. major and P. berghei ANKA (experimental cerebral malaria model). Acute P. berghei ANKA infection reduced cutaneous leishmaniasis progression: smaller lesion size and reduced parasite burden in dermis. Co-infected mice had elevated serum TNF compared to L. major alone and reduced recruitment of activated T cells and inflammatory monocytes to L. major infection sites, limiting Leishmania growth and tissue damage.

articles.headline: Infective A. coluzzii show higher anthropophily in malaria hotspots
articles.title_original: The spatial heterogeneity of malaria transmission: An entomological investigation in a highly endemic setting of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Perugini E, Poggi C, Micocci M, Pichler V, Nelli L, Torre AD, Guelbeogo WM, Pombi M
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Entomological survey in Goden village, Burkina Faso (ITN-covered setting) examining spatial heterogeneity of malaria transmission. An. coluzzii was dominant (79% of An. gambiae s.l., n=1,492). Overall human blood index was low (45%), but sporozoite rate was high (10%) with 43% of human blood meals containing erythrocytic parasite stages. North-west area was a hotspot with higher HBI, sporozoite rate, and infected human blood meal rate; infective mosquitoes showed 1.8-fold higher anthropophily than non-infective ones.

articles.headline: SR4-02 and SR4-04 restore cognition in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Polycyclitol Derivatives Restore Long- Term Memory Via cdk5/p25 Activation of Tau Signaling in Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Simhadri PK, Rashid S, Karri S, Bhat BA, Mehta G, Babu PP
articles.details: Experimental cerebral malaria model in mice testing four polycyclic derivatives (SR4-01 to SR4-04) as adjuncts to artemether. SR4-02 and SR4-04 showed significant improvements in learning and memory (Barnes maze, T-maze, novelty recognition) compared to artemether alone and SR4-01/SR4-03 groups. Immunohistochemistry showed reduced phospho-tau (Ser396) in SR4-02/SR4-04 groups; Golgi-Cox staining revealed enhanced neuronal arborization in hippocampal CA1/CA3 and cortex. Western blot confirmed reduced Cdk5-p25-mediated tau phosphorylation in SR4-04 group.

articles.headline: Physics-informed neural networks predict malaria transmission dynamics
articles.title_original: Analysis of a mathematical model for malaria using data-driven approach.
articles.authors: Rajnarayanan A, Kumar M, Tridane A
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study developing a compartmental SIR-SI model incorporating temperature and altitude-dependent transmission functions. Novel transmission function captures environmental dependencies. Comparative framework using ANNs, RNNs, and PINNs for parameter estimation; PINNs outperformed purely data-driven approaches by embedding epidemiological dynamics. Dynamic Mode Decomposition applied to derive a data-driven transmission risk index from infection trajectory data for real-time risk assessment.

articles.headline: Bivalent malaria vaccine protects against P. falciparum and vivax
articles.title_original: Malaria bivalent viral vectored vaccine protects against Plasmodium falciparum and vivax and blocks parasite transmission.
articles.authors: Yamamoto Y, Katayama T, Fabbri C, Niwa S, Okuhara D, Iyori M, Hasyim AA, Mizukami H, Shida H, Lopes S, Yoshida S
articles.details: Bivalent viral vectored vaccine trial using vaccinia virus LC16m8Δ (m8Δ) prime and AAV1 boost encoding Pfs25-PfCSP and Pvs25-PvCSP fusion proteins. Heterologous m8Δ-AAV1 immunization provided 70% protection against both PfCSP/Pb and PvCSP/Pb transgenic sporozoites. Membrane feeding assay with P. vivax isolates from Brazilian Amazon showed 90% transmission-blocking efficacy. Bivalent vaccine outperformed monovalent combinations with immune responses maintained >7 months.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria infections contribute 30% to transmission
articles.title_original: Impact of asymptomatic infections on malaria transmission dynamics.
articles.authors: Haringo AT, Obsu LL, Bushu FK
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study of malaria transmission using next-generation matrix method to calculate basic reproduction number (R0). Asymptomatic infections contribute approximately 30% to R0 while symptomatic cases account for 70%, revealing backward bifurcation. Authors conclude integrated control strategies addressing both symptomatic and asymptomatic cases are needed for effective transmission reduction.

articles.headline: Three zoonotic Plasmodium species found in Thai macaques
articles.title_original: First molecular detection of zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi, Plasmodium cynomolgi, and Plasmodium inui in Assamese macaques from northern Thailand.
articles.authors: Wilaisri P, Kaewchot S, Rucksaken R, Jarudecha T, Hmaidee T, Wichainchot S, Thabthimsri C, Sricharern W
articles.details: Molecular survey of 133 Assamese macaques using semi-nested PCR targeting 18S rRNA gene. P. knowlesi detected in 13.53% (18/133; 95% CI: 8.23-20.56), P. cynomolgi in 9.77% (13/133; 95% CI: 5.29-16.10), and P. inui in 0.75% (1/133; 95% CI: 0.02-4.09). Overall, 24.06% (32/133; 95% CI: 17.07-32.23) contained Plasmodium spp. DNA. First molecular evidence of these zoonotic species in this macaque species.

articles.headline: Tephrosia villosa extract shows antimalarial activity in mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial activity of root extract of Tephrosia villosa L. Pers. (Fabaceae) on Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Alemu MA, Moges TA, Ketsela Zeleke T, Abebe RB, Zewdu WS
articles.details: In vivo study testing Tephrosia villosa root extract (Dichloromethane/methanol 1:1) against Plasmodium berghei-infected mice at doses 100-400 mg/kg. At 400 mg/kg dose: parasitemia suppressed 24.78% (4-day suppressive test), 52.16% (curative test), and 49.09% (prophylactic test; p < 0.001). Extract was non-toxic at 2 g/kg dose and demonstrated chemosuppressive, curative, and prophylactic activities with protective effects on packed cell volume and body weight.

articles.headline: XGBoost ML model optimizes malaria detection accuracy
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of machine learning models for malaria detection using validated synthetic data: a cost-sensitive approach with clinical domain knowledge integration.
articles.authors: Sekhar GVC, Alemu C
articles.details: Systematic comparison of five machine learning models for malaria detection using validated synthetic dataset (87% representativeness). XGBoost achieved optimal ROC-AUC of 0.956 (95% CI: 0.952-0.961) with clinical cost of 5,496, representing 2.8% improvement over Random Forest. Enhanced Bayesian Logistic Regression achieved comparable ROC-AUC of 0.954 (95% CI: 0.950-0.959). McNemar's test showed statistically significant differences between XGBoost and Random Forest (p < 0.05).

articles.headline: Integrated pipeline for transmission-blocking drug discovery
articles.title_original: An all-in-one pipeline for the in vitro discovery and in vivo testing of Plasmodium falciparum malaria transmission blocking drugs.
articles.authors: Brancucci NMB, Gumpp C, van Gemert GJ, Yu X, Passecker A, Nardella F, Thommen BT, Chambon M, Turcatti G, Halby L, Blasco B, Duffey M, Arimondo PB, Bousema T, Scherf A, Leroy D, Kooij TWA, Rottmann M, Voss TS
articles.details: Platform for transmission-blocking drug discovery combining in vitro screening and in vivo preclinical testing using engineered transgenic P. falciparum (NF54/iGP1_RE9Hulg8) parasites expressing red-shifted firefly luciferase. Develops robust assay for identifying stage V gametocytocidal compounds and establishes in vivo model using humanized NODscidIL2Rγnull mice with bioluminescence imaging to assess gametocyte killing kinetics of antimalarial reference drugs and clinical candidates.

articles.headline: Korean mosquito surveillance shows 66.6% vector density increase
articles.title_original: [Malaria Vector Surveillance in 2024].
articles.authors: Han BG, Kim HW, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: Annual surveillance at 76 sites with mosquitoes identified by species and trap index calculated. Cumulative trap index reached 120.6 in 2024, showing 66.6% increase from 2023 and 59.3% increase from 2020-2022 average. Anopheles mosquitoes tested for P. vivax; malaria-positive pools detected with minimum infection rate of 0.15, decreased from 2023 (0.21) despite increased vector density. Authors emphasize vector surveillance as core malaria elimination strategy.

articles.headline: Expanded nanopore sequencing for P. falciparum drug-resistance surveillance
articles.title_original: An expanded method for malaria parasite genetic surveillance using targeted nanopore sequencing.
articles.authors: Harrott AJR, Morang'a CM, Pearson RD, Sakyi ML, Osumanu A, Amoako EK, Bara FD, Hosmillo M, Rowe K, Aniweh Y, Awandare GA, Zeukeng F, Goodfellow I, Ariani CV, Amenga-Etego LN, Hamilton WL
articles.details: Updated nanopore-based multiplex assay (DRAG2) for P. falciparum molecular surveillance with expanded targets including drug resistance markers, merozoite surface protein 2 (msp2), and 18S ribosomal RNA for species detection. Method validated across parasitemia ranges and sample types (venous blood and dried blood spots). Quality control uses synthetic plasmids with test SNPs (known drug resistance markers) and control SNPs to signal contamination. Authors provide SOPs and describe transition toward diagnostic assay implementation.

articles.headline: Learning curves determine training sample size for ML prediction
articles.title_original: How to use learning curves to evaluate the sample size for malaria prediction models developed using machine learning algorithms.
articles.authors: Zaloumis SG, Rajasekhar M, Simpson JA
articles.details: Tutorial using learning curves to determine sample size for malaria ML prediction models. Mock study predicted artemisinin resistance from gene expression data using 1043 P. falciparum isolates (29% slow clearing with parasite clearance half-life >5h). Testing sPLSDA + SVMs and random forests on 208-sample test set: balanced error rate decreased from 50% (20-sample training set) to 14% for sPLSDA + SVMs and 22% for random forests (835-sample training set). Further increases beyond 835 samples unlikely to significantly improve accuracy.

articles.headline: Ivermectin antimalarial potential (title only)
articles.title_original: Ivermectin against Malaria - Good News in Bad Times.
articles.authors: Steketee RW
articles.details: No abstract available. Title suggests positive findings regarding ivermectin as malaria intervention.

articles.headline: Ivermectin reduces malaria incidence by 26% in children
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to Control Malaria - A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Maia M, Kariuki M, Ruiz-Castillo P, Wanjiku C, Kasiwa L, Brazeal A, Casellas A, Ngama M, Onyango T, Elobolobo E, Kazungu K, Mael M, Wangari W, Nuru K, Otuko R, Sanz A, Ringera I, Matano A, Mitora S, Ribes M, Brew J, Gorski N, Nicolas P, Stanulovic S, Omondi I, Furnival-Adams J, Túnez L, Mbarak J, Vegove V, Yaa E, Mramba S, Kibet Y, Nyambura N, Rotich C, Wanjiru S, Vura M, Wanjiku F, Sam L, Collins L, Xia K, Hammann F, Saúte F, Rudd M, Rist C, Jones C, Mwangangi J, Rabinovich NR
articles.details: Cluster-randomized trial with 84 clusters (28,932 participants) comparing monthly mass ivermectin (400 μg/kg) to albendazole (400 mg active control) for 3 months in children 5-15 years. Six-month incidence: 2.20 per child-year with ivermectin vs 2.66 per child-year with albendazole (adjusted incidence rate ratio 0.74; 95% CI 0.58-0.95; p=0.02), representing 26% lower incidence. Serious adverse events similar between groups (incidence rate ratio 0.63; 95% CI 0.21-1.91).

articles.headline: Undiagnosed malaria during emergency cesarean section
articles.title_original: Emergent Cesarean Section Due to Malaria: A Perioperative Challenge.
articles.authors: Bandarupalli T, Zbeidy R
articles.details: Case report of woman in early 30s at 39 weeks gestation with undiagnosed malaria (from travel exposure) presenting with severe fetal heart rate decelerations requiring emergency cesarean section. Anesthetic management used rapid induction with etomidate and succinylcholine to manage febrile state with malaria-associated hemodynamic instability and coagulation anomalies. Empirical antimalarial treatment initiated before laboratory confirmation.

articles.headline: Rwandan patients show poor malaria treatment adherence
articles.title_original: Understanding Malaria Treatment Adherence in Rwanda: Implications for Artemisinin Resistance.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Jordan A, Saramba E, Wernsman Young N, Iradukunda PG, Karema C, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Banek K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 406 febrile patients/caregivers at six health facilities (56% female, 80% rural) from December 2023–February 2024. Malaria knowledge high: 81% identified symptoms, 72% understood transmission, 74.6% knew control measures. Among 44.3% who received treatment in past 6 months (180/406), only 46% completed full 3-day course; 37% stopped within 2 days, 11% after 3 days. Authors conclude poor adherence despite high knowledge exacerbates antimalarial resistance risk.

articles.headline: P. vivax detected in Duffy-negative Cameroonians; PvDBP1 mutations despite RDT failures
articles.title_original: Epidemiological Insights and Duffy Binding Protein Evolution of Plasmodium vivax in Duffy-Negative Cameroonians.
articles.authors: Dieng CC, Ning RT, Kipayko C, Schroeder R, Mdluli-Berndt N, Ayukenchengamba B, Nematchoua Z, Doris S, Reward AE, Ule ISN, Kimbi HK, Lo E
articles.details: P. vivax prevalence was 11% in febrile patients and 5.5% in asymptomatic Duffy-negative individuals. PvDBP1 genetic analysis identified two prevalent mutations: I379L (73%) and E225K (61%), suggesting adaptive evolution. Standard PvLDH-based RDTs produced false-negative results even at high parasitemia, whereas qPCR demonstrated superior sensitivity. Phylogenetic analysis clustered Cameroonian isolates with Botswana strains but distinct from Ethiopian and Sudanese isolates, indicating historical migration.

articles.headline: Microglia modulate nitric oxide and pro-inflammatory cytokines in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Microglia exhibit a dynamic response, modulating inducible nitric oxide synthase expression and the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines during experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Freire-Antunes L, Ornellas-Garcia U, Rangel-Ferreira M, Ribeiro-Almeida ML, Carvalho LJM, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Ribeiro-Gomes FL
articles.details: Using flow cytometry on P. berghei ANKA-infected C57BL/6 mice, researchers documented increased Ly6C+ T cells, inflammatory monocytes, resident macrophages, and microglia. Ex vivo analysis showed increased inflammatory monocytes, resident macrophages, and microglia expressing inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS), producing interleukin-1β and TNF. Findings highlight pronounced microglia reactivity in experimental cerebral malaria and provide information on cell dynamics and immune responses in the brain.

articles.headline: Western Kenya health facilities vulnerable to climate-driven malaria and dengue epidemics
articles.title_original: Public health facility vulnerabilities, preparedness, and health outcomes for Plasmodium falciparum and dengue virus-infected children under 5 years with acute febrile illnesses in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Menya D, Mangeni J, Ayodo G, Karanja S
articles.details: A three-month prospective cohort study of 378 participants found 17 (81%) facilities could diagnose and treat malaria, while 4 (18%) could only diagnose and treat dengue. Facilities in flood-prone locations reported longer hospitalizations (>5 days) in Kisumu, while Bunyala achieved higher recovery rates of 184/189 (96.8%) compared to Kisumu's 171/188 (91.0%). Public health facilities lack preparedness for rising climate-driven infectious disease burden and require enhanced arboviral disease surveillance.

articles.headline: Gender in malaria research remains biomedical; needs qualitative and community approaches
articles.title_original: Conceptualization of gender in published malaria and gender research: a systematic descriptive review.
articles.authors: Atobrah D, Kwansa BK, Okyere-Asante PG, Kyere A, Badasu DM, Kretchy IA
articles.details: A systematic descriptive review of 57 articles on malaria and gender (1995-2024) from four databases found the majority were biomedical in nature, reducing gender analysis to sex-disaggregated data. Most studies employed quantitative approaches and were laboratory-based, focused on sub-Saharan Africa. Authors concluded more social science research using qualitative, mixed-methods, and community-based approaches is needed to extend gender analysis beyond sex-disaggregated data to domains including resource access, labor distribution, practices, norms, and decision-making power.

articles.headline: Self-medication for malaria in Uganda associated with antiparasitic stockouts and resistance risk
articles.title_original: Self-medication for malaria and associated factors in Kakumiro District, Uganda, August 2023: implications for malaria management and mortality prevention.
articles.authors: Zalwango MG, Migisha R, Agaba BB, Bulage L, Kwesiga B, Kadobera D, Ario AR
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 592 households in Kakumiro District found 368 (62%, 95% CI 58.2-65.9%) had self-medicated for malaria. Self-medication was significantly associated with household heads aged ≥35 years (APR 1.77), distance ≥5 km to health facilities (APR 3.05), and home antimalarial storage (APR 2.21); ≥6 malaria episodes in 6 months was protective (APR 0.39). Although 85% used recommended drugs, 66% received inappropriate doses. Despite under-dosing, 85% recovered without hospitalization, highlighting risks of drug resistance.

articles.headline: Tanzania: only 31% of pregnant women achieve optimal IPTp-SP preventive treatment dosing
articles.title_original: Individual and community-level correlates of optimal doses of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine for intermittent preventive treatment of malaria during pregnancy in Tanzania: a multilevel analysis of the 2022 national survey.
articles.authors: Stephano EE, Yusheng T, John TW, Kibusi SM, Yamin L, Mtoro MJ
articles.details: A multilevel analysis of 4,497 women from Tanzania's 2022 survey found 31.2% (95% CI 29.1-33.3%) achieved optimal ≥3 doses of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Uptake was higher for ages 25-34 (AOR 1.52) and 35-49 (AOR 1.47), primary education (AOR 1.34), one child (AOR 1.65), and >3 antenatal care visits (AOR 1.77). Community factors included higher ANC rates (AOR 1.49) and Northern zone residence (AOR 1.61). Critically low overall uptake necessitates integrating malaria prevention with reproductive health services.

articles.headline: Community engagement improves malaria vaccine uptake in children under five
articles.title_original: Community engagement in promoting malaria vaccine uptake for children Under-Five.
articles.authors: Adoma PO, Acquah F, Yakubu A, Cudjoe L, Achiam WKK, Agorinya IA
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 1,183 caregivers identified factors influencing community engagement in malaria vaccine promotion through random forest analysis. Perception of vaccine effectiveness, educational level, and age emerged as most relevant factors. Vaccine safety and acceptability were moderate factors that could contribute to promoting vaccine uptake. Findings suggest tailoring educational programs to address demographic variations in perceptions of vaccine efficacy can leverage community engagement to increase malaria vaccine uptake.

articles.headline: Transfusion-transmitted malaria affects 0.8-6.8% of blood recipients in Indian subcontinent regions
articles.title_original: Transfusion-transmitted Plasmodium spp. infections and safety challenges for malaria in the Indian subcontinent: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Sharma S, Sharma A
articles.details: A systematic review analyzing 122 studies involving over 6.5 million individuals found Plasmodium parasitaemia prevalence of 0-0.87% by light microscopy and 0-2.3% by rapid diagnostic tests in donors. Post-transfusion malaria cases ranged from 0.8% to 6.8% across studies, with clinical impacts from mild to severe and fatal outcomes. Additional concerns included P. malariae infections, P. vivax and P. falciparum prevalence, drug-resistant parasites, and pfhrp2 gene-deleted P. falciparum. Point-of-care nucleic acid amplification could improve blood screening.

articles.headline: Overreliance on 18S rRNA gene limits malaria molecular diagnosis sensitivity and specificity
articles.title_original: Overreliance on Plasmodium 18S rRNA gene for malaria molecular diagnosis-inferences from systematic review.
articles.authors: Deora N, Khan MR, Singh P, Zehra N, Sircar S, Pande V, Mallick PK, Sinha A
articles.details: A systematic review examining PCR targets for Plasmodium detection found 93% of studies used the 18S rRNA gene as the sole molecular target, with nested PCR most common (68%). Notably, 85% of studies using 18S rRNA and nested PCR employed the approach developed in 1993. Authors highlight that prolonged reliance on a single molecular target may be problematic, calling for validation of newer multi-copy targets with improved limits of detection, reproducibility, reduced costs, and multiplexing capability.

articles.headline: EEG distinguishes retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria from nonmalarial coma in Malawian children
articles.title_original: Using Electroencephalography to Assess Coma Etiology in Children with Retinopathy-Negative Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Takle M, Andrews A, Riggle BA, Zelleke T, Harrar D, Zhang J, Zhang B, Wilson KJ, Beare NAV, Taylor TE, Seydel KB, Ray S, Postels DG
articles.details: In 30-minute EEG recordings from Malawian children (3 months-14 years), quantitative analysis could not discriminate retinopathy-positive from retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria but successfully differentiated retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria from nonmalarial coma (AUROC 0.83). Combined qualitative and quantitative EEG interpretation improved discrimination (AUROC 0.87). EEG patterns of retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria resembled retinopathy-positive disease and differed significantly from nonmalarial coma, supporting the hypothesis that acute malarial infection remains pathophysiologically important in retinopathy-negative disease.

articles.headline: Apicoplast chaperonin complex essential for Plasmodium proteostasis and parasite survival
articles.title_original: A chaperonin complex regulates organelle proteostasis in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Tissawak A, Rosin Y, Katz Galay S, Qasem A, Shahar M, Trabelsi N, Furman-Schueler O, Johnson SM, Florentin A
articles.details: Ablation of the apicoplast CPN60 chaperonin subunit caused rapid parasite death during the first replication cycle, bypassing the delayed phenotype of apicoplast translation inhibitors. CPN60 directly interacted with the Clp proteolytic complex, manipulating its stability and controlling organellar proteostasis. Screening of bacterial CPN60 (GroEL) inhibitors revealed anti-Plasmodium activity beyond apicoplast function. These findings reveal how balanced proteolysis and refolding safeguard the apicoplast proteome and is essential for organelle biogenesis.

articles.headline: Anti-phosphatidylserine antibodies and pro-inflammatory cytokines drive thrombocytopenia in P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Thrombocytopenia in patients with Plasmodium vivax in Colombia is associated with anti-phosphatidylserine autoantibodies and IL-6, IFNγ, IL-10 and TGF-β.
articles.authors: Velasco-Pareja MC, Tovar-Acero C, Cantero Guevara ME, Rodriguez A, Rivera-Correa J, Yasnot-Acosta MF
articles.details: In P. vivax-infected patients from Colombia with thrombocytopenia, plasma IFNγ, IL-6, and IL-10 were significantly increased (P < 0.05) compared to malaria patients without thrombocytopenia. TGF-β1 was increased in malaria without thrombocytopenia (P < 0.0001). Anti-phosphatidylserine autoantibody levels were higher in thrombocytopenia patients and showed negative correlation with platelet counts. Findings suggest thrombocytopenia results from autoantibodies targeting phosphatidylserine on activated platelets, driven by pro-inflammatory cytokine imbalance with TGF-β1 potentially protective.

articles.headline: Community knowledge gaps hinder malaria prevention efforts
articles.title_original: A scoping review of community knowledge in malaria prevention and control programmes.
articles.authors: Abdul Rahim FA, Mahmud MAF, Abdul Mutalip MH, Yoep N, Aminuddin MAH, Mohd Ngesom AM
articles.details: Scoping review of 63 studies from 27 countries examining community knowledge in malaria prevention and control. Found significant regional variations in understanding of transmission and symptoms. Higher community awareness consistently linked with better preventive practices and earlier treatment-seeking. Critical knowledge gaps persist regarding environmental risk factors and vector control, highlighting need for context-specific health education.

articles.headline: Service readiness and geography drive IPTp-SP uptake disparities
articles.title_original: Bridging urban-rural disparities in malaria care during pregnancy in Senegal: evidence from household and health facility surveys.
articles.authors: Jiang Y, Liang D, Zhao J, Prasad S, Ndiop M, Thiam SA, Diallo I, Sene D, Mpembeni R, Huang J
articles.details: Analysis of Demographic and Health Surveys and Service Provision Assessments in Senegal 2012–2019 linked at regional level. Average IPTp-SP doses: 1.66 overall (urban 1.73, rural 1.63). Each 1-point increase in malaria service readiness yielded 0.251 additional doses; differential effects between urban (0.612 per unit) and rural (0.089 per unit, interaction p<0.001). Differential strategies required to bridge urban-rural disparities.

articles.headline: Correction to CRISPR gene editing system in Plasmodium berghei
articles.title_original: Correction to 'A scalable CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing system facilitates CRISPR screens in the malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei'.
articles.details: Correction to previously published paper describing a scalable CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing system for genetic screens in the malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei.

articles.headline: Novel UV traps outperform standard CDC traps for vector surveillance
articles.title_original: Assessment of BG-Pro (Biogent AG) and Silver Bullet 2.1 (Lumin8) UV-Light Traps Efficiency for Surveillance of Malaria Vectors in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Amugune BL, Tamre R, Mogaka D, Mbare O, Bukhari T, Fillinger U, Njoroge MM
articles.details: Comparison of BG-Pro and Silver Bullet 2.1 UV light traps against UV LED CDC trap tested indoors over 64 nights in western Kenya rice irrigation area. SB 2.1 UV most effective: An. gambiae RR 4.3 (95% CI 2.5–7.3), An. funestus RR 7.1 (95% CI 3.9–13.1), Culex RR 2.7 (95% CI 1.2–6.0). BG-Pro and SB 2.1 traps 3–6× more efficient than CDC trap, promising for low-density malaria settings.

articles.headline: Prior COVID-19 infection strongly predicts severe malaria and dengue
articles.title_original: COVID-19 infection and its association with severe malaria & dengue: an epidemiological study from Southern India.
articles.authors: Ommen SJ, Mithra P, T R, Kumar N, Holla R, Kulal N, Rao M, Unnikrishnan B
articles.details: Case-control study of 293 participants (mean age 36.7 ± 14.8 years) from Southern India November 2022–August 2024; 23.9% had malaria (30% severe), 76.1% had dengue (35.8% severe). Prior COVID-19 infection dramatically increased severe disease risk: dengue aOR 55.93 (95% CI 7.17–435.92, p<0.001) with 98.1% developing severe disease; malaria aOR 263.7 (95% CI 34.9–1987.4, p<0.001) with 85.7% developing severe illness.

articles.headline: mRDT accuracy varies by transmission intensity and demographics in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Varying Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Accuracy by Regional Transmission Level and Demographics in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Wiener D, Seth MD, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Popkin-Hall ZR, Giesbrecht D, Bakari C, Lyimo B, Pereus D, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Sheahan TP, Parr JB, Bailey JA, Ishengoma DS, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Cross-sectional comparison of 3 national standard mRDTs and 1 research mRDT against qPCR in Tanzania 2021. Research mRDT underperformed with poor sensitivity across transmission strata. Diagnostic accuracy differed significantly by age, gender, and regional malaria transmission rate. Transmission intensity may affect mRDT agreement and diagnostic performance.

articles.headline: Computational tool identifies conserved malaria vaccine epitopes with broad coverage
articles.title_original: Computational Prioritization of T Cell Epitopes to Overcome HLA Restriction and Antigenic Diversity in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Laurenson AJ, Pierce BG, Takala-Harrison S, Laurens MB
articles.details: TEpiNom computational tool analyzed 42 P. falciparum vaccine-candidate proteins from 18 African countries, integrating HLA allele frequencies from 24 sub-Saharan populations to identify epitopes with broad HLA coverage. Identified 2,265 MHC I and 1,992 MHC II conserved epitopes (>95% conservation, median rank <10%). Key MHC I epitopes from pre-erythrocytic antigens achieved near 100% HLA-A/B/C coverage; MHC II epitopes provided 98.5%–100% coverage per life stage.

articles.headline: Genome-wide positive selection atlas reveals vector adaptation to insecticides
articles.title_original: An atlas of positive selection in the genomes of major malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Nagi SC, Harding NJ, Lawniczak MKN, Donnelly MJ, Miles A
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 4,306 mosquitoes (An. gambiae complex and An. funestus) from 21 countries identified regions under positive selection. Confirmed intense selection at established resistance loci: Vgsc, Rdl, Ace1, Cyp6p, Cyp9k1, Gste, Coeaexf. Identified novel signals at diacylglycerol kinase (X chromosome) and Keap1. Parallel evolution observed between species. Open-source web resource supports real-time monitoring of emerging resistance variants.

articles.headline: Spatial analysis links garimpo mining and human mobility to malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Human mobility, garimpos and spatiotemporal malaria transmission in the Yanomami Indigenous Territory: a retrospective observational study.
articles.authors: Barbieri AF, Santos RO
articles.details: Retrospective observational study 2007–2023 in Brazil's Yanomami Indigenous Territory using Malaria Mobility Networks to analyze spatiotemporal transmission. Found 86% of malaria cases and 91% of population spatially concentrated. Autochthonous malaria in indigenous localities reached 90% by 2023. Garimpo-associated infection networks increased since 2019, peaking ~27% in 2022, more associated with urban residence.

articles.headline: Low adherence to treatment guidelines undermines malaria elimination efforts
articles.title_original: Evaluation of anti-malarial treatment for elimination of malaria in South West Ethiopia: a concurrent triangulation design for prompt decision.
articles.authors: Gebremichael MA, Gebremedhin T, Niguse W, Mamo G, Alemayehu B, Negeri E, Temam I, Zewude H, Bogale T, Sahile E, Pawlos E
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study March–July 2024 in Southwest Ethiopian Peoples Regional State reviewing 1,684 patient cards (mean age 20.99 ± 15.61 years). 95.96% screened by microscopy/RDT. Overall adherence to updated malaria treatment standards: 36.99% (95% CI 33.93–38.52%). Primary species: 58.31% P. falciparum, followed by P. vivax and mixed. Major barriers: drug shortage, lack of training, staff shortages, laboratory material shortages.

articles.headline: Serum endocan elevation marks severe malaria disease severity and endothelial dysfunction
articles.title_original: Preliminary assessment of serum endocan as a biomarker of disease severity in Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Nantavisai K, Puttikamonkul S, Viriyavejakul P
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 99 participants: 20 healthy controls, 36 P. vivax, 30 uncomplicated P. falciparum, 13 severe P. falciparum. Serum endocan measured by ELISA at baseline and day 7. All malaria groups higher than controls (p<0.0001); highest in severe P. falciparum (median 4.67 [IQR 2.85–7.93] ng/ml) > uncomplicated P. falciparum (3.27 [2.24–4.33] ng/ml) > P. vivax (1.85 [1.44–3.23] ng/ml). Endocan correlated with parasite density (P. vivax rs=0.4632 p=0.0066; severe P. falciparum rs=0.6264 p=0.0251) and negatively with thrombocytopenia in P. vivax (rs=–0.5523 p=0.001).

articles.headline: Rising prevalence of artemisinin-resistant k13 mutations detected in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Rising prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum Artemisinin partial resistance mutations in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Brhane BG, Fola AA, Nigussie H, Leonetti A, Kassa M, Hailgiorgis H, Wuletaw Y, Abera A, Mohammed H, Sime H, G/Tsadik A, Assefa G, Solomon H, Tasew G, Tollera G, Hailu M, Juliano JJ, Assefa A, Parr JB, Bailey JA
articles.details: Molecular analysis of 1,199 P. falciparum infections from 12 sentinel sites across 5 Ethiopian regions (2019–2023) using MIP panels and genome-wide SNPs. Most prevalent k13 R622I mutation found in 15.7% with marked regional variation. Three validated artemisinin-resistance mutations (P441L, P574L, A675V) detected in Ethiopia for first time; A675V exclusive to Gambella clinic. Polymorphisms in crt, mdr1, dhps, dhfr genes nearly fixed. 87.2% monogenomic infections; mutant parasites show high local genetic relatedness suggesting clonal transmission.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis improves cluster randomized trial sample size calculations
articles.title_original: Characterisation of between-cluster heterogeneity in malaria cluster randomised trials to inform future sample size calculations.
articles.authors: Biggs J, Challenger JD, Dee D, Elobolobo E, Chaccour C, Saute F, Staedke SG, Vilakati S, Chung JB, Hsiang MS, Dabira ED, Erhart A, D'Alessandro U, Tripura R, Peto TJ, Von Seidlein L, Mukaka M, Mosha J, Protopopoff N, Accrombessi M, Hayes R, Churcher TS, Cook J
articles.details: Meta-analysis of cluster-summary data from 24 malaria cluster randomized trials examining between-cluster heterogeneity (coefficient of variation, k). Empirical k estimates often exceeded those used in original sample size calculations, reducing study power and effect size precision. Elevated k was associated with incidence outcomes, lower endemicity settings, and uneven intervention coverage.

articles.headline: Precision sterile insect technique cost analysis for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Evaluating the cost of malaria elimination by Anopheles gambiae precision guided SIT in the Upper River region, The Gambia.
articles.authors: Gendron WAC, Raban R, Mondal A, M Sánchez C H, Smidler AL, Zilberman D, Ilboudo PGC, D'Alessandro U, Marshall JM, Akbari OS
articles.details: Cost-effectiveness analysis of precision-guided sterile insect technique (pgSIT) for Anopheles gambiae control in The Gambia's Upper River region. pgSIT working with current interventions predicted to prevent approximately 230 deaths and 48,000 sick days per year. Cost-effectiveness was 11-94 USD per DALY and 10-86 USD per infection prevented, or 0.36-3.03 USD per person annually.

articles.headline: Chk1 inhibitor shows dual-target activity against resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: The Human Chk1 Inhibitor CHIR-124 Shows Multistage Activity Against Plasmodium falciparum via Dual Inhibition of PfArk1 and Hemozoin Formation.
articles.authors: Wicht KJ, Woodland JG, Garnie LF, Langeveld H, Taylor D, Godoy LC, Pasaje CFA, de Souza ML, Siqueira-Neto JL, Ghidelli-Disse S, Lafuente-Monasterio MJ, Gamo FJ, Coertzen D, Reader J, van der Watt M, Bridgford JL, Girling G, Coyle R, Scheurer C, Wittlin S, Lee MCS, Voss TS, Winzeler EA, Fidock DA, Niles JC, Birkholtz LM, Coulson LB, Chibale K
articles.details: In vitro study of CHIR-124, a human checkpoint kinase 1 inhibitor, against Plasmodium falciparum. Active against both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant asexual blood stage parasites, with moderate activity in liver and gametocyte forms. CHIR-124 acts via dual inhibition of PfArk1 and hemozoin formation, reducing resistance generation risk compared to single-target antimalarials.

articles.headline: Blood count parameters predict malaria severity across endemic and nonendemic settings
articles.title_original: Comparative Analysis of Initial Full Blood Count Parameters in Adults Infected With Plasmodium falciparum for Classification of Disease Severity and Previous Exposure Across Endemic (Gabon) and Nonendemic (Germany) Settings.
articles.authors: von Wedel C, Matthies LC, Dierks C, Tober-Lau P, Bardtke L, Lingscheid T, Nordmann T, Jochum J, Huebl L, Tappe D, Zoller T, Pechmann K, Okwu DG, Malinga EG, Ralser M, Sander LE, Zoleko-Manego R, Ramharter M, Mombo-Ngoma G, Kurth F, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Analysis of full blood count parameters in 1392 adults with Plasmodium falciparum comparing endemic (Gabon) and non-endemic (Germany) settings. Lower platelet counts and higher neutrophil counts correlated with greater disease severity. Machine learning classifier combining parameters achieved superior discrimination (AUC 0.94 for asymptomatic vs uncomplicated, AUC 0.84 for uncomplicated vs severe) versus single parameters.

articles.headline: Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio diagnostic marker for malaria infection
articles.title_original: Diagnostic value of full blood count derived systemic inflammatory biomarkers in malaria infection.
articles.authors: Boachie J, Ahiable D, Ajabuin LA, Amissah R, Asmah-Brown A, Apalebilah S, Owusu-Poku AG, Eshun H, Adu P, Nyarkoh JK
articles.details: Case-control study of 45 malaria patients and 50 healthy controls evaluating full blood count-derived inflammatory biomarkers. Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) showed excellent diagnostic value with AUC 0.937, sensitivity 86.7%, and specificity 92.0% at threshold ≥2.12. NLR and monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio showed moderate correlation with parasite count.

articles.headline: Splenic macrophage subsets play distinct roles in malaria immunity
articles.title_original: Red pulp macrophages clear parasites, while marginal metallophilic and marginal zone macrophages support CD4(+) T cell activation during Plasmodium yoelii infection.
articles.authors: Falkenstein J, Ninnemann A, Hose M, Elwy A, Lang KS, Matuschewski K, Buer J, Westendorf AM, Hansen W
articles.details: In vivo study using transgenic mice infected with Plasmodium yoelii examining splenic macrophage subset functions. Red pulp macrophage depletion resulted in elevated parasitemia, indicating their essential role in clearing infected red blood cells. Marginal zone and marginal metallophilic macrophages contributed to CD4+ T cell activation without significantly affecting parasitemia progression.

articles.headline: Review of P. falciparum pathogenesis mechanisms and therapeutic advances
articles.title_original: Emerging Molecular Mechanisms in Malaria Pathogenesis and Novel Therapeutic Approaches: A Focus on P. falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Sanyaolu A, Marinkovic A, Prakash S, Balendra V, Shazley O, Gardellini T, Jan A, Younis K, Okorie C, Izurieta R
articles.details: Review article examining emerging molecular mechanisms in Plasmodium falciparum malaria pathogenesis, including parasite immune evasion and drug resistance development. Discusses novel therapeutic approaches including new vaccines (RTS,S and R21) and antibody-based therapies, highlighting ongoing challenges in extending immunity duration and ensuring effectiveness across diverse parasite strains.

articles.headline: Weather and ENSO events drive malaria transmission in Brazilian Amazon
articles.title_original: The impact of weather and extreme events on malaria transmission in the Brazilian Amazon: a case-crossover and population-based study.
articles.authors: Arisco NJ, Peterka C, Schwartz J, Castro MC
articles.details: Case-crossover analysis of 5,381,105 malaria cases in the Brazilian Amazon (2003-2022) examining weather and ENSO effects. Temperatures 25.64-30.85°C and precipitation >4.46 cm in the week prior increased infection risk by 9% (95% CI 8-10%) and 86% (95% CI 35-155%), respectively. ENSO events significantly influenced transmission, with El Niño and La Niña reducing cases by 2179 and 37,258, respectively.

articles.headline: Uganda's 2022 malaria epidemic: scale, drivers, and control gaps
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemics and its drivers in Uganda in 2022.
articles.authors: Aregawi MW, Maiteki C, Rek JC, Agaba B, Katureebe C, Ranjbar M, Zhang C, Kiware S, Opigo J
articles.details: Analysis of Uganda District Health Information System data 2017-2022. Sixty-four of 146 districts experienced epidemics in 2022, with 3,379,309 excess outpatient malaria cases (95% CI 1,553,714–5,339,709) and 149,789 excess inpatient cases (95% CI 66,029–235,743). Programmatic deficiencies in indoor residual spraying and integrated community case management coverage, along with minimal rainfall correlation, were identified as primary epidemic drivers.

articles.headline: Malaria care cascade gaps in Guinea and Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: The malaria care cascade and factors associated with receiving a malaria test in children under 5 years of age and pregnant women with fever: a cross-sectional household survey from three regions in Guinea and three districts in Sierra Leone in 2022.
articles.authors: Burger G, Röbl K, Fischer HT, Feddern L, Pozo-Martin F, Geurts B, Baldé H, Kaba I, Menelik-Obbarius S, Stein K, Merhi V, Weishaar H, Kallon MMM, Dumbuya K, Sellu J, Mbawah AK, Delamou A, El Bcheraoui C
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey in three regions of Guinea and three districts of Sierra Leone (May-August 2022) assessing malaria care cascade in 1377 children under 5 years and 254 pregnant women. Reported malaria testing rates varied 42-96% in children and 62-91% in pregnant women. Treatment within 2 days of fever onset ranged from 54-81% in children and 24-69% in pregnant women.

articles.headline: Placental malaria hemozoin pigment associated with preeclampsia risk
articles.title_original: The Relationship between Placental Malaria and Preeclampsia in Blantyre, Malawi.
articles.authors: Boudova S, Divala TH, Mungwira R, Mawindo P, Tomoka T, Laufer MK
articles.details: Analysis of 751 pregnancies from an intermittent preventive therapy RCT in Malawi with placental histopathology and PCR data. Placental malaria identified in 125 pregnancies (16.7%), with 105 showing hemozoin pigment indicating early pregnancy infection. Individuals with hemozoin had higher preeclampsia rates (6.7% vs 3.7%, P=0.16), though this difference was not statistically significant and study was underpowered.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccination strategy for climate-driven control disruptions
articles.title_original: Vaccination to mitigate climate-driven disruptions to malaria control in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Rice BL, Raobson E, Miharisoa S, Rebaliha M, Lewinski J, Raharinirina H, Golden CD, Vecchi GA, Wesolowski A, Grenfell B, Metcalf CJE
articles.details: Modeling study examining malaria vaccination effectiveness in mitigating interruptions to prevention and treatment programs caused by extreme weather events in Madagascar. Using data on infection rates following major tropical cyclones, the analysis quantified expected reductions in symptomatic infections across various vaccination scenarios. Vaccination demonstrated longer duration of protection compared to other control options for addressing climate-driven disruptions.

articles.headline: Malaria and anemia linked in Ethiopian school children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria, anemia and associated factors among school children in Hawassa city, Sidama, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Gena A, Asnake S, Menjetta T
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 329 children, April-June 2024. Malaria prevalence 8.5%, anemia 9.4%; 29% of anemic children were infected. Malaria-infected children had 4.98× greater odds of anemia (AOR 4.983, 95% CI 1.067-23.265). ITN use (AOR 0.024) and transmission knowledge (AOR 0.205) reduced malaria risk. Authors recommend integrated malaria prevention and nutrition programs.

articles.headline: Blood groups don't predict malaria in protected populations
articles.title_original: Impact of the ABO and Rh Blood Groups on Malaria Incidence, Recurrence, and Severity Among a Moroccan Military Contingent in the Central African Republic.
articles.authors: El Faria W, Loubadi Y, Moutmir Y, Ababou M, Hammani A, Mahtat EM, Jennane S, El Maaroufi H, Doghmi K
articles.details: Eight-month prospective study of 749 Moroccan soldiers with chemoprophylaxis. 20.8% experienced malaria (89% P. falciparum), 1.1% developed severe malaria. No significant association between ABO/Rh blood groups and malaria incidence, recurrence, or severity. Strong preventive measures appear to overshadow genetic influences on disease risk.

articles.headline: Var gene switching hub enables chronic P. falciparum infection
articles.title_original: The molecular basis of coordinated antigen switching enabling chronic infection by human malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Visone JE, Florini F, Hadjimichael E, Patel V, Deitsch KW
articles.details: Mechanistic study of PfEMP1-mediated immune evasion in P. falciparum. var2csa functions as switching hub coordinating var gene family expression via noncoding RNAs and mRNA decay pathway. Sequential expression of different PfEMP1 isoforms allows parasites to escape antibody recognition and perpetuate chronic infections.

articles.headline: Uganda malaria crisis requires vaccination and strategic action
articles.title_original: Addressing the malaria crisis among children in Uganda: the urgent need for vaccination and strategic action.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Tague C, Karegeya A, Mugenyi N, Taj J, Mufungizi I, Naaz F, Mugisha MJM, Cakirwa H, Yokolo H, Ekouo J, Makeda D, Hangi S, Mbabazi S
articles.details: Title indicates focus on addressing childhood malaria in Uganda with emphasis on vaccination and coordinated interventions. Specific study design, population, and quantitative results not available from title alone.

articles.headline: HRP2 RDTs underestimate non-falciparum malaria species
articles.title_original: Undetected Plasmodium malariae and P. ovale infections in HRP2 RDT-positive children with uncomplicated malaria in Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Kouevi AFC, Bayala IMC, Sondo P, Kaboré B, Millogo KS, Kambou SAE, Compaore EW, Nikiema M, Kazienga A, Rouamba T, Gnémé A, Tinto H
articles.details: Study of 207 HRP2 RDT-positive children <5 years, July 2021-June 2022, in Burkina Faso. Microscopy detected 4 non-falciparum cases (3 P. malariae, 1 P. ovale); nested PCR revealed additional 6 P. ovale and 5 P. malariae sub-patent infections. Mixed infections showed lower parasite densities than P. falciparum mono-infections. Authors conclude HPR2-based RDTs underestimate non-falciparum burden.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi causes different immune responses in humans and macaques
articles.title_original: Comparative immunological study of Plasmodium knowlesi infections in humans and macaques: insights into cytokine dynamics.
articles.authors: Abd Rachman Isnadi MF, Yong YK, Grigg MJ, Sipangkui S, Lee PC, Nuin NA, Tan AF, Molius P, Tuuga A, Abd Sukor JR, Rajahram G, Daim S, Chua TH
articles.details: Comparative immunological study of 30 P. knowlesi-infected humans and 35 infected macaques in Sabah. Macaques had higher parasite gDNA and mtDNA release. Humans showed IL-10 dominance positively associated with parasite gDNA; macaques showed IL-6 dominance unrelated to gDNA. Humans had higher IL-10/TNF ratios despite lower parasite gDNA, indicating greater disease severity.

articles.headline: G6PD testing enables high-dose primaquine for P. vivax cure
articles.title_original: High daily dose Short COurse PrimaquinE after G6PD testing for the radical cure of Plasmodium vivax malaria in Indonesia and Papua New Guinea: the SCOPE implementation study protocol.
articles.authors: SCOPE Study Group
articles.details: Staged implementation study (SCOPE trial) in Indonesia and PNG assessing G6PD-guided high-dose primaquine (7mg/kg total dose) for P. vivax radical cure. Stage 1: 800 patients assess safety. Stage 2: 11,410 patients evaluate feasibility and cost-effectiveness. Treatment duration by G6PD status: 7 days if normal (≥70%), 14 days if intermediate (30-70%), 8 weeks weekly if deficient (<30%).

articles.headline: Multi-model deep learning achieves 96% malaria detection accuracy
articles.title_original: Automated multi-model framework for malaria detection using deep learning and feature fusion.
articles.authors: Shahin OR, Alshammari HH, Alabdali RN, Salaheldin AM, Saleh N
articles.details: Validation study of automated framework integrating ResNet-50, VGG16, and DenseNet-201 with feature fusion, PCA dimensionality reduction, and SVM-LSTM hybrid classification on 27,558 microscopic blood smear images. Achieved 96.47% accuracy, 96.03% sensitivity, 96.90% specificity, and 96.88% precision using majority voting ensemble.

articles.headline: Maternal parasitaemia predicts congenital malaria in newborns
articles.title_original: Prevalance and risk factors of congenital malaria in Osogbo, Southwestern Nigeria.
articles.authors: Odediji SA, Olabanji SA, Olawuyi AO, Amoo KO, Amoo-Adeboye BO, Adeleke MA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 77 newborns sampled within 24 hours of birth in Nigeria. Congenital malaria prevalence 6.5%, all cases with cord blood parasitaemia. Maternal malarial parasitaemia was strong predictor (p<0.05), while intermittent prophylactic therapy, therapeutic antimalarials, and bednet use were not significantly associated (p>0.05). Authors recommend multicenter studies.

articles.headline: Nanoscale wing morphology differs between Anopheles vectors
articles.title_original: Unveiling the Nanomorphological Architecture of Malaria Vectors' Wings via Minkowski and Multifractal Formalisms.
articles.authors: Ramos GQ, Pinto LCC, Matos RS, Costa CAR, Lopes SCP, Santana RAG, Justiniano SCB, Miranda KR, da Fonseca Filho HD
articles.details: Comparative morphological analysis of Anopheles darlingi and An. aquasalis wings using atomic force microscopy, Minkowski functionals, and multifractal analysis. An. aquasalis displayed higher surface roughness and greater nanomorphological complexity (Δα ≈1.073 vs 1.009 in An. darlingi). Differences in multifractal spectra suggest species-specific flight adaptations related to vectorial behavior.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to WIMANET workshop report
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "The Wildlife Malaria Research network (WIMANET): Meeting report on the 1st WIMANET workshop" [Int. J. Parasitol: Parasites and Wildlife 25 (2024) 100989].
articles.authors: Gutiérrez-López R, Ferraguti M, Bodawatta KH, Chagas CRF, Chakarov N, Duc M, Emmenegger T, García-Longoria L, Lopes RJ, Martínez-de la Puente J, Renner SC, Santiago-Alarcon D, Sehgal RNM, Stankovic D, Marzal A, Dunn JC
articles.details: Correction notice to article DOI: 10.1016/j.ijppaw.2024.100989. No research findings presented.

articles.headline: School-age children at highest malaria risk in dam construction zone
articles.title_original: Epidemiological Profile and Risk Factors for Malaria in Rural Communities Before the Operationalization of the Singrobo-Ahouaty Dam, Southern Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Avenié TJD, Silué KD, Guindo-Coulibaly N, Koné N, Touré S, Valian KL, Kouadio KS, Bédia AMJ, Déabo BF, Diabagaté K, Nsanzabana C, Coulibaly JT
articles.details: Baseline cross-sectional survey of 915 individuals before Singrobo-Ahouaty dam construction, Côte d'Ivoire. Malaria prevalence 43.1% overall; school-age children 6-13 years showed highest prevalence (74.3%, p<0.0001), while younger children 0-5 years had highest parasite density (1218.0/µL, p<0.0001). Risk factors included informal work (ORa 1.5), raw materials sector (ORa 1.4), and farming activities. Establishes baseline for assessing dam health impacts.

articles.headline: Investing in traditional medicine research for malaria control
articles.title_original: Investing in traditional medicine: leveraging evidence and innovative research to strengthen the fight against malaria in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Onukansi FO, Anokwuru CC, Eneh SC, Odey GO, Onah DO, Sokunbi T, Innocent DC, Dozie UW, Umoke PC, Ojo TO, Ezejindu CN
articles.details: A literature review discussing traditional medicine, particularly in Nigeria, as a potential resource against malaria. Examines the historical role of traditional remedies (citing Artemisia annua's contribution to modern antimalarials), the high malaria burden in Nigeria, and the need for increased funding, scientific validation, pharmacological testing, and integration of traditional medicine into national health systems. No quantitative results presented.

articles.headline: Sudden malaria resurgence in Northern Iran 2023
articles.title_original: The resurgence of malaria in Northern Iran in 2023: a wake-up call.
articles.authors: Koohsar F, Faridnia R, Jorjani O, Hojjati MT, Yadagiri G, Noshak G, Pirzaman HT, Kalani H
articles.details: Study of 445 individuals with suspected malaria symptoms in Golestan Province, Iran (March-December 2023) identified 22 confirmed cases (4.94%). Infections comprised 77.27% (17/22) P. vivax, 18.18% (4/22) P. falciparum, and 4.54% (1/22) co-infection. Parasitemia levels: 16,029±5,060/µL for P. vivax and 105,460±102,146/µL for P. falciparum. All patients demonstrated positive response to treatment. Gametocytes observed in some P. falciparum samples, raising concern for possible local transmission.

articles.headline: Severe malaria and chronic kidney disease in children
articles.title_original: Malaria associated pathogenesis of chronic kidney disease (MAP-CKD): a prospective study of children hospitalized with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Batte A, Nakulima V, Namazzi R, Aujo JC, Starr MC, Schwaderer AL, Lintner-Rivera M, Ahumwire A, Ogwal D, Tenywa E, Oyang W, Ssenkusu JM, Bangirana P, Idro R, Kalyesubula R, Dreyer G, McCulloch M, Bassat Q, John CC, Goldstein SL, Conroy AL
articles.details: A prospective multi-site cohort study protocol in Uganda enrolling 750 children with severe malaria and 375 community controls aged 3 months to <16 years with 2-year follow-up. Study will assess short-term kidney recovery (at 1, 2, 4 months), long-term kidney disease risk (at 12, 24 months), and mechanisms of maladaptive kidney repair following severe malaria-associated acute kidney injury. Also investigates impact on morbidity, mortality, cognitive function, and brain injury via MRI.

articles.headline: Heterogeneous malaria infection rates in Mali cohort
articles.title_original: Marked heterogeneity in malaria infection rate in a Malian longitudinal cohort.
articles.authors: LaVerriere E, Johnson ZM, Shieh M, Nziza N, Alter G, Buckee CO, Crompton PD, Traore B, Tran TM, Neafsey DE
articles.details: A six-year longitudinal cohort study in Mali genotyped >13,000 blood samples from 464 participants (aged 3 months-25 years). Molecular force of infection (molFOI: number of genetically distinct parasites) varied extensively (0-55 in 2011), remained consistent within individuals across multiple seasons, and was independent of age. HbS allele associated with lower molFOI. Functional antibody signatures against CSP C-terminus and RH5 antigens correlated with low molFOI, identifying candidate immune correlates of protection.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance markers rare; HRP deletions common in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 Variants and Histidine-Rich Protein 2/3 Deletions in Central Ethiopia, 2023.
articles.authors: Tufa TB, Oliveira R, Schallenberg E, Hendry JA, Igiraneza C, Jara-Reznakova B, Eggers Y, Sill H, Kenea D, Luedde T, Feldt T, Ndoli JM, Mockenhaupt FP, van Loon W
articles.details: Study of 220 symptomatic malaria patients in central Ethiopia (2023) identified P. falciparum in 86.4%, P. vivax in 12.3%, P. ovale in 3.6%. Among 174 P. falciparum infections: validated artemisinin resistance marker pfk13 R622I in 1.1% (2/174), candidate marker P441L in 1.7% (3/174). pfhrp2 deletion in 24.0%, pfhrp3 deletion in 78.8%, dual deletions in 22.7%. pfmdr1 patterns indicated reduced lumefantrine sensitivity. High proportions of P. falciparum missed by HRP-based rapid diagnostic tests.

articles.headline: Environmental factors shape zoonotic malaria vector bionomics in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Impact of environmental factors on the bionomics of Anopheles mosquito vectors of zoonotic malaria: A narrative review.
articles.authors: Masse RS, Vythilingam I, Fornace K, Othman H, Liu X, Jaafar AJ, Shahar MK, Abdul Rahman NA, Khairul Azman AH, Jeyaprakasam NK
articles.details: A narrative review examining the role of environmental factors (temperature, humidity, elevation, precipitation, seasonality, land use) in shaping the bionomics of Anopheles mosquito vectors of non-human primate malaria (P. knowlesi, P. cynomolgi, P. inui, P. fieldi) in Southeast Asia. The review highlights how these environmental determinants influence vector abundance, survival, and transmission intensity. Emphasizes the importance of a One Health approach integrating vector surveillance, sustainable land management, and targeted public health interventions.

articles.headline: Plasmodium antigens associated with protection in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Exploring the P. falciparum antigens associated with reduced risk of malaria in pregnancy.
articles.authors: Mwai L, Musundi S, Waweru H, Nagaoka H, Limbua PG, Tsuboi T, Takashima E, Gitaka J, Kanoi BN
articles.details: Study assessing antibody responses to 698 P. falciparum recombinant antigens in Kenyan primigravida and multigravida pregnant women identified three antigens statistically significant in multigravida: PF3D7_1301800 (SURFIN 13.1), PF3D7_0424400 (SURFIN 4.2), and PF3D7_1252100 (rhoptry neck antigen 3 domain). VAR2CSA domains showed immunoreactivity (seroprevalence 42-62%) and correlated with selected antigens. Results may guide vaccine target prioritization for reducing malaria-in-pregnancy risk and poor birth outcomes in primigravida women.

articles.headline: IDO-mediated tryptophan catabolism increases regulatory T cells in P. vivax
articles.title_original: IDO1-AhR axis increases T regulatory cells in Plasmodium vivax malaria infection.
articles.authors: Dos Santos RO, Carvalho NO, de Morais TBDN, Sobrinho WBS, da Cruz MGS, de Faria ES, de Araújo FF, Magalhães VP, Lopes SCP, Malheiros A, da Mota AJ, Santiago HDC, Nogueira PA, Teixeira de Carvalho A, de Lacerda MVG, Lalwani P
articles.details: Study examined IDO-mediated tryptophan catabolism and regulatory T cells (Tregs) in P. vivax infection using in vitro PBMC stimulation with P. vivax-infected erythrocytes. Stimulation increased IDO-1 expression and kynurenine/tryptophan (KYN/TRP) ratio, correlating with increased Tregs. Patients with acute P. vivax infection exhibited elevated KYN/TRP ratios and increased Treg frequencies (positive correlation); individuals with prior infections showed lower Treg frequencies, plasma IFN-γ, and KYN/TRP ratios. Findings suggest IDO-mediated tryptophan catabolism promotes immune tolerance during P. vivax infection.

articles.headline: Artemisinin combinations remain highly efficacious in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine, artesunate-amodiaquine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Mozambique, 2022.
articles.authors: Nhama A, Chidimatembue A, Nhamussua L, Bassat Q, da Silva C, Nhacolo A, Arnaldo P, Salvador C, Cassy A, Candrinho B, Dimene M, Carvalho E, Saifodine A, Wate F, Mucavele H, Torres-Mendoza Y, Horton B, Plucinski M, Cistero P, Mayor A, Aide P
articles.details: In vivo therapeutic efficacy study in Mozambique (2022) enrolled 828 children aged 6 months-11 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria (1000-200,000 parasites/µL): artemether-lumefantrine (AL, n=462), artesunate-amodiaquine (AS-AQ, n=183), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP, n=91), artesunate-pyronaridine (AS-PY, n=92). Day 28 corrected efficacy: AL 97.63-100%, AS-AQ 100%. Day 42 corrected efficacy: DP 97.75-100%, AS-PY above WHO cut-off (≥90%). All drugs well tolerated with adverse events in <2%. AL and AS-AQ remain effective first-line treatments.

articles.headline: Disability disparities by age, gender, and wealth in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and geographical disparities in disability distribution among Tanzanian population: insights from the 2022 demographic and health survey and malaria indicator survey.
articles.authors: Luoga P, Mahiti GR, Kapinga O, Herzon B, Mwaisengela SM, Materu PA, German CJ, Kasangala AA, Mtulo GE, Kinyenje ES, Hokororo JC, Ngonyani TL, Kacholi G, Kapologwe NA, Kumalija CJ, Anasel MG, Eliakimu ES
articles.details: Secondary data analysis from 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey (58,425 individuals) found overall disability prevalence of 11%. Increased prevalence with age (APR 3.85 for ≥50 years vs 15-29 years), female gender (APR 2.67), secondary education (APR 2.58), separation/widowing (APR 2.91), and wealth (APR 2.74). Northern zone had higher prevalence (APR 2.77) than Lake zone. Authors note counterintuitive findings on wealth and education may reflect improved access to diagnosis.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance markers tracked during Ecuador malaria pre-elimination
articles.title_original: Tracking Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance markers during a malaria pre-elimination period in the Pacific coast of South America.
articles.authors: Ñacata I, Early AM, Boboy J, Neafsey DE, Sáenz FE
articles.details: Analysis of 67 P. falciparum samples from northwestern Ecuador (2019-2021) tracked molecular resistance markers and compared with 2013-2015 period. Pfcrt CVMET and Pfdhfr CICNI haplotypes became dominant (100% in Esmeraldas). Pfmdr-1 NEDFSDFY detected for first time. Pfdhps SAKAA and Pfk13 MYRIC remained wild-type (100%). A16V mutation in Pfdhfr reported for first time in Ecuador. Chloroquine and pyrimethamine resistance increased while artemisinin sensitivity maintained, indicating artemether-lumefantrine + primaquine remains effective.
